topics.title: Basics of Vehicle Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Vehicle Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vehicle Performance** in the context of automotive and electric vehicle engineering is defined as the quantitative measure of a vehicle's ability to execute the fundamental driving tasks demanded by the driver. These tasks are:

1. **Cruising at a desired maximum speed** on level roads.
2. **Climbing grades** of specified steepness (gradeability).
3. **Accelerating rapidly** to a target speed within a stipulated time.
4. **Braking efficiently** to come to a halt within a safe distance.

The mathematical foundation of vehicle performance rests upon Newton's Second Law applied to a lumped-mass vehicle model, producing the **Tractive Effort Equation**. This equation balances the propulsive force generated at the wheel against the sum total of all resistive forces (rolling, aerodynamic, and gravitational) plus the inertial force required to accelerate the vehicle mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** Module 1 lays the mathematical groundwork for all subsequent modules. Every later concept — battery sizing, motor selection, range estimation, regenerative braking — ultimately traces back to the **Tractive Effort Equation**. Mastering it is non-negotiable for KTU examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a loaded shopping cart across a supermarket parking lot on a windy day:

- The **rolling resistance** is the gritty friction between the trolley wheels and the asphalt — it is always present, regardless of how gently you push.
- The **aerodynamic drag** is the wind pushing against the trolley from the front — it grows stronger the faster you run.
- The **gradient resistance** is when the trolley must be pushed up a ramp — gravity becomes your opponent.
- The **inertial force** is the "heaviness" you feel when first starting to push or trying to speed up — it depends on the trolley mass.

The motor in an EV plays your role as the pusher, and the **tractive effort** is exactly how hard the motor must "push" at the contact patch of the drive wheels to overcome all these resistances and still accelerate the vehicle.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical EV Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | $\text{m/s}^2$ | **9.81** |
| Air density (sea level, 25°C) | $\rho$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | **1.225** |
| Coefficient of rolling resistance | $f_r$ | dimensionless | **0.008 – 0.015** |
| Aerodynamic drag coefficient | $C_d$ | dimensionless | **0.22 – 0.35** (EVs) |
| Vehicle frontal area | $A_f$ | $\text{m}^2$ | **1.8 – 2.5** |
| Mass factor (rotational inertia) | $\delta$ | dimensionless | **1.05 – 1.20** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these constants matter in KTU exams:** Numerical substitution in these exact units is the single most common source of half-mark deductions. Always show units in every line of your derivation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Forces Acting on a Moving Vehicle

The vehicle is modeled as a single rigid mass translating along a road. Five forces act on it:

**(a) Tractive Effort ($F_t$)** — the longitudinal force generated at the tire-road contact patch by the propulsion system, directed forward along the vehicle's longitudinal axis.

**(b) Rolling Resistance ($F_{rr}$ or $F_r$)** — the resistance offered by the deformation of tires and road surface; always opposes motion.

**(c) Aerodynamic Drag ($F_a$ or $F_d$)** — the resistance offered by the air through which the vehicle moves; grows with the square of speed.

**(d) Gradient Force ($F_g$)** — the gravitational component along the road when climbing a slope; opposes motion uphill.

**(e) Inertial Force ($F_i$)** — the equivalent translational inertia including rotational inertia of rotating components (wheels, motors, gears).

### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Rolling Resistance ($F_r$):**
The tire flattens at the contact patch and the road also deforms microscopically. This hysteresis loss is modeled as:

$$F_r = f_r \cdot m \cdot g$$

where $f_r$ depends on tire material, pressure, speed, and road texture. For EVs (with low rolling-resistance tires), $f_r \approx 0.008$ to $0.012$.

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic Drag ($F_a$):**
This is the most speed-dependent force. Doubling speed quadruples drag. The coefficient $C_d$ characterizes body shape; EVs like Lucid Air achieve $C_d \approx 0.21$.

$$F_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot V^2$$

**Step 3 — Gradient Resistance ($F_g$):**
On a slope of angle $\theta$ (or grade percentage $G = 100\tan\theta$):

$$F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

For small angles (typical driving), $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx G/100$, so $F_g \approx m \cdot g \cdot G/100$.

**Step 4 — Inertial Force ($F_i$):**
Rotating components (rotor, gears, wheels) carry angular momentum. When accelerating, an effective mass factor $\delta$ accounts for this:

$$F_i = m \cdot \delta \cdot a$$

where $\delta = 1 + \frac{I_w + I_m G^2 \eta_g}{m \cdot r^2}$ (a formula often expected in derivations).

**Step 5 — Newton's Second Law (The Master Equation):**

$$F_t = F_r + F_a + F_g + F_i$$

$$F_t = f_r \cdot m \cdot g + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 + m g \sin\theta + m \delta a$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rolling Resistance | $F_r = f_r \, m \, g$ | N | Constant low-speed term |
| 2 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, C_d \, A_f \, V^2$ | N | High-speed dominant term |
| 3 | Gradient Force | $F_g = m \, g \, \sin\theta$ | N | Hill climbing |
| 4 | Inertial Force | $F_i = m \, \delta \, a$ | N | Acceleration phase |
| 5 | **Tractive Effort Equation** | $F_t = F_r + F_a + F_g + F_i$ | N | Master force balance |
| 6 | Tractive Power | $P_t = F_t \cdot V$ | W | Power at wheels |
| 7 | Gradeability | $\sin\theta_{\max} = \frac{F_{t,\max} - F_r - F_a}{m \, g}$ | — | Max climb angle |
| 8 | Acceleration | $a = \frac{F_t - F_r - F_a - F_g}{m \, \delta}$ | m/s² | Instantaneous accel |
| 9 | Max Speed | Solve $F_t(V_{\max}) = F_r + F_a$ for $V_{\max}$ | m/s | Top speed on level road |
| 10 | Mass Factor | $\delta = 1 + \dfrac{I_w}{m \, r^2} + \dfrac{I_m G_g^2 \eta_g}{m \, r^2}$ | — | Rotational inertia effect |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The tractive effort curve $F_t(V)$ plotted against the resistance curve $F_r + F_a$ gives a visual "operating envelope" used by every EV manufacturer (Tesla, BYD, Tata) to size their motor and battery pack. The intersection determines maximum speed; the area under the curve determines energy consumed per kilometer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Tractive Effort Equation

**Starting Point:** Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ moving at instantaneous velocity $V$ on a road inclined at angle $\theta$ with respect to the horizontal. The wheels experience a forward tractive force $F_t$ at the contact patch.

**Step 1 — Free Body Analysis on Inclined Plane:**
Resolve the weight $mg$ into two components:
- Perpendicular to road: $mg \cos\theta$ (balanced by normal force $N$)
- Parallel to road: $mg \sin\theta$ (acts downhill, opposes forward motion)

**Step 2 — Identify All Resistive Forces:**
Three resistive forces oppose forward motion:
- Rolling resistance: $F_r = f_r \cdot N \approx f_r \cdot m g \cos\theta$
- Aerodynamic drag: $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$
- Gravity component: $F_g = m g \sin\theta$

**Step 3 — Include Rotational Inertia:**
The rotating masses (wheels, motor rotor, gears) possess kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$. Equating to an equivalent translational kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2} m_{eq} V^2$ yields an effective mass factor $\delta \geq 1$.

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's Second Law ($\Sigma F = m a$):**

$$F_t - F_r - F_a - F_g = m \, \delta \, a$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange:**

$$F_t = f_r \, m g + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 + m g \sin\theta + m \delta a$$

This is the **Tractive Effort Equation** in its full KTU form.

### 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Gradeability

**Given:** Maximum tractive force the motor can deliver $F_{t,\max}$ at a given speed $V$.

**Step 1 — Assume steady-state cruise on the steepest sustainable grade:**
Set acceleration $a = 0$. The tractive effort exactly balances resistive forces:

$$F_{t,\max} = F_r + F_a + F_g$$

**Step 2 — Substitute expressions:**

$$F_{t,\max} = f_r \, m g + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 + m g \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\sin\theta$:**

$$\sin\theta_{\max} = \frac{F_{t,\max} - f_r \, m g - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2}{m g}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to grade percentage:**
For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = G/100$, therefore:

$$G_{\max} \,(\%) = 100 \cdot \frac{F_{t,\max} - f_r \, m g - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2}{m g}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Speed (Level Road)

**Condition:** On a level road ($\theta = 0$), the vehicle accelerates until tractive effort equals resistive forces and $a \to 0$.

**Step 1 — Set $\theta = 0$ and $a = 0$:**

$$F_t(V_{\max}) = f_r \, m g + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V_{\max}^2$$

**Step 2 — For an EV with constant-power motor:** $F_t(V) = P_{\max}/V$. Substitute:

$$\frac{P_{\max}}{V_{\max}} = f_r \, m g + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V_{\max}^2$$

**Step 3 — Multiply through by $V_{\max}$:**

$$P_{\max} = f_r \, m g \, V_{\max} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V_{\max}^3$$

This is a cubic equation in $V_{\max}$, typically solved numerically or by iteration in KTU problems.

### 3.4 Derivation of Acceleration Capability

**Starting Point:** Tractive effort equation, isolating acceleration $a$:

$$a = \frac{F_t - F_r - F_a - F_g}{m \, \delta}$$

**Step 1 — Expand all terms:**

$$a = \frac{F_t - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 - m g \sin\theta}{m \, \delta}$$

**Step 2 — For a launch-from-rest scenario ($V = 0$, $\theta = 0$):**

$$a_{\max} = \frac{F_{t,\max} - f_r m g}{m \, \delta}$$

**Step 3 — Time to accelerate from $V_1$ to $V_2$ (level road, constant force):**
Using $a = dV/dt$:

$$\int_0^{t} dt = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{m \, \delta}{F_t - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2} \, dV$$

$$t = \frac{m \, \delta}{2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho C_d A_f}{F_t - f_r m g}} \cdot \ln \left( \frac{\sqrt{F_t - f_r m g} + V_2 \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f}}{\sqrt{F_t - f_r m g} + V_1 \sqrt{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f}} \right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** You do NOT need to memorize this integral. Most KTU questions ask for the *formulation* of acceleration, not the closed-form time. Present the differential equation setup; that earns full credit.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An EV has $m = 1500$ kg, $C_d = 0.28$, $A_f = 2.2$ m², $f_r = 0.012$, $\delta = 1.08$. Compute $F_t$ at $V = 90$ km/h on a 5% grade. Take $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Convert velocity:** $V = 90/3.6 = 25$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Compute rolling resistance:**

$$F_r = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 176.58 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute aerodynamic drag:**

$$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2 \times 25^2 = 235.81 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute gradient force:**
For 5% grade, $\sin\theta \approx 0.05$:

$$F_g = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 = 735.75 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Compute inertial force (assume cruising, $a = 0$):** $F_i = 0$ N.

**Step 6 — Total tractive effort required:**

$$F_t = 176.58 + 235.81 + 735.75 + 0 = 1148.14 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 7 — Tractive power:**

$$P_t = F_t \times V = 1148.14 \times 25 = 28703.5 \, \text{W} \approx 28.7 \, \text{kW}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Free Body Diagram (Block-Level Force Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram models the **functional force-flow architecture** of an EV in motion, mapping all interacting resistive and propulsive forces:

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph PropulsionSide["PROPULSION SUBSYSTEM"]
        BAT["Battery Pack"]
        MOT["Electric Motor"]
        TRN["Transmission and Final Drive"]
        WHLD["Drive Wheels"]
    end

    subgraph ResistiveForces["RESISTIVE FORCE ENVIRONMENT"]
        ROLL["Rolling Resistance Fr"]
        AERO["Aerodynamic Drag Fa"]
        GRAD["Gradient Force Fg"]
        INER["Inertial Force ma"]
    end

    subgraph VehicleCore["VEHICLE LUMPED MASS m"]
        MASS["Equivalent Vehicle Body"]
    end

    subgraph Outputs["PERFORMANCE OUTPUTS"]
        SPD["Vehicle Speed V"]
        ACC["Acceleration a"]
        PWR["Tractive Power Pt"]
        GRD["Gradeability theta"]
    end

    BAT --> MOT
    MOT --> TRN
    TRN --> WHLD
    WHLD --> MASS
    ROLL --> MASS
    AERO --> MASS
    GRAD --> MASS
    INER --> MASS
    MASS --> SPD
    MASS --> ACC
    MASS --> PWR
    MASS --> GRD
```

### 4.2 Tractive Effort Operating Envelope (Block Architecture)

The following architecture illustrates how the tractive effort curve interacts with the resistance curve across the speed spectrum:

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LowSpeed["LOW SPEED DOMAIN 0 to 30 km/h"]
        LR1["Rolling Resistance dominates Fr is constant"]
        LR2["Inertial Force dominates F i proportional to a"]
        LR3["Motor delivers MAX FORCE constant torque region"]
    end

    subgraph MidSpeed["MID SPEED DOMAIN 30 to 80 km/h"]
        MS1["All forces comparable"]
        MS2["Transition region from constant torque to constant power"]
        MS3["Motor torque decreases as V increases"]
    end

    subgraph HighSpeed["HIGH SPEED DOMAIN above 80 km/h"]
        HS1["Aerodynamic Drag dominates Fa proportional to V squared"]
        HS2["Motor delivers MAX POWER constant power region"]
        HS3["At V max the curves intersect"]
    end

    LR1 --> MS1
    LR2 --> MS2
    LR3 --> MS3
    MS1 --> HS1
    MS2 --> HS2
    MS3 --> HS3
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — From Driver Demand to Wheel Force

```mermaid
graph LR
    DRV["Driver Accelerator Input"] --> ECU["Vehicle Control Unit ECU"]
    ECU --> INV["Power Inverter"]
    INV --> MTR["Electric Motor"]
    MTR --> GRB["Gearbox with Ratio Ng"]
    GRB --> WHL["Wheels Radius r"]
    WHL --> RD["Road Contact Patch"]
    RD --> FTE["Tractive Effort Ft at Ground"]
    FTE --> RES["Overcomes Fr plus Fa plus Fg plus ma"]
    RES --> MOT2["Translates to Vehicle Motion V and a"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block represents a physical or functional stage. The chain **Motor → Gearbox → Wheel → Road** converts electrical energy into the tractive effort $F_t$. The final block confirms that this effort is consumed against the four resistive forces derived in SECTION 2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define tractive effort. List the four resistive forces opposing the motion of a road vehicle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tractive effort is the longitudinal propulsive force generated at the contact patch between the drive wheels and the road surface, transmitted by the propulsion system (in an EV, the electric motor through the gearbox).

The four resistive forces are:

1. **Rolling resistance** ($F_r = f_r \, m g$) — due to tire and road deformation.
2. **Aerodynamic drag** ($F_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$) — air resistance acting on the vehicle body.
3. **Gradient force** ($F_g = m g \sin\theta$) — gravity component when climbing.
4. **Inertial force** ($F_i = m \, \delta \, a$) — resistance to change in velocity including rotational inertia.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is aerodynamic drag negligible at low speeds but dominant at high speeds? Justify with the governing equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The governing equation is:

$$F_a = \frac{1}{2} \rho \, C_d \, A_f \, V^2$$

This shows drag is proportional to the **square of velocity**. At low speeds (e.g., 20 km/h ≈ 5.5 m/s), the $V^2$ term is small (~30), giving negligible drag. At 120 km/h (≈33 m/s), the same term grows to ~1089, i.e., 36× larger.

Simultaneously, rolling resistance $F_r = f_r m g$ is independent of speed. Hence at low speeds $F_a \ll F_r$, but beyond ~80 km/h $F_a > F_r$, and by top speed $F_a$ dominates entirely. This $V^2$ sensitivity is why EV designers obsess over streamlining and low $C_d$ values.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`
**(a)** Derive the tractive effort equation for a road vehicle moving on an inclined plane, clearly defining each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric vehicle has the following specifications:
- Total mass $m = 1800$ kg (including 200 kg battery)
- Frontal area $A_f = 2.3$ m²
- Coefficient of drag $C_d = 0.30$
- Coefficient of rolling resistance $f_r = 0.010$
- Mass factor $\delta = 1.10$
- Air density $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³

Calculate the tractive force required to maintain a constant speed of 72 km/h on a level road. Also determine the tractive power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ on an inclined road of angle $\theta$.**

**Step 2 — Forces acting on the vehicle:**
- Forward tractive force $F_t$ at the contact patch
- Rolling resistance $F_r = f_r \, m g \cos\theta$
- Aerodynamic drag $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$
- Gradient force $F_g = m g \sin\theta$ (down the slope)
- Inertial force accounting for rotating masses: $F_i = m \, \delta \, a$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law along the direction of motion:**

$$F_t - F_r - F_a - F_g = m \, \delta \, a$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for $F_t$:**

$$F_t = f_r \, m g \cos\theta + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 + m g \sin\theta + m \, \delta \, a$$

**Step 5 — Special Case (level road, $\theta = 0$, constant speed $a = 0$):**

$$F_t = f_r \, m g + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2$$

This is the canonical tractive effort equation used for cruise analysis.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert speed:** $V = 72 / 3.6 = 20$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Rolling resistance:**

$$F_r = f_r \cdot m \cdot g = 0.010 \times 1800 \times 9.81 = 176.58 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Aerodynamic drag:**

$$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.3 \times 20^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.30 \times 2.3 \times 400 = 165.6 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Gradient and inertial terms are zero (level road, constant speed):**
$F_g = 0$, $F_i = 0$.

**Step 5 — Total tractive force:**

$$F_t = 176.58 + 165.6 = 342.18 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 6 — Tractive power:**

$$P_t = F_t \times V = 342.18 \times 20 = 6843.6 \, \text{W} \approx 6.84 \, \text{kW}$$

---

**Q3B.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Alternative Module Question]`
**(a)** With the aid of the tractive effort equation, derive an expression for the maximum grade a vehicle can climb at a given constant speed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An EV with mass 1600 kg is climbing a 6% gradient at a steady 54 km/h. The tractive force available from the motor at this speed is 3000 N. Given $f_r = 0.011$, $C_d = 0.27$, $A_f = 2.1$ m², $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, compute the resistive force opposing motion. Is the available tractive force sufficient? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — For sustained grade climbing at constant speed:** $a = 0$, hence inertial force $F_i = 0$.

**Step 2 — Tractive effort equation reduces to:**

$$F_t = F_r + F_a + F_g$$

**Step 3 — Substitute standard expressions:**

$$F_t = f_r \, m g + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 + m g \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\sin\theta$:**

$$\sin\theta = \frac{F_t - f_r \, m g - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2}{m g}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to grade percentage using $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = G/100$ for small angles:**

$$G (\%) = \frac{100 \left( F_t - f_r \, m g - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 \right)}{m g}$$

**Step 6 — Maximum grade occurs when motor delivers its peak force $F_{t,\max}$:**

$$G_{\max} (\%) = \frac{100 \left( F_{t,\max} - f_r \, m g - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 \right)}{m g}$$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert speed:** $V = 54/3.6 = 15$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Compute rolling resistance:**

$$F_r = 0.011 \times 1600 \times 9.81 = 172.66 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute aerodynamic drag:**

$$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.27 \times 2.1 \times 15^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.27 \times 2.1 \times 225 = 76.55 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute gradient force for 6% grade ($\sin\theta \approx 0.06$):**

$$F_g = 1600 \times 9.81 \times 0.06 = 941.76 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Total resistive force:**

$$F_{\text{resist}} = 172.66 + 76.55 + 941.76 = 1190.97 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with available tractive force:**

$$F_t = 3000 \, \text{N} > F_{\text{resist}} = 1190.97 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:** Yes, the available tractive force is sufficient. The surplus is $3000 - 1190.97 = 1809.03$ N, which can be used for further acceleration if needed.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Vehicle Performance Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing km/h and m/s without conversion (1 km/h = 1/3.6 m/s). This single error costs 2–3 marks in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the Mass Factor $\delta$:** Many students omit $\delta$ during acceleration calculations, producing physically unrealistic accelerations. Always include $\delta \neq 1$ when $a \neq 0$.
> 3. **Sign Convention Errors:** Gradient force opposes motion uphill. If you incorrectly add it when descending, every subsequent answer is wrong.
> 4. **Confusing $C_d$ with $A_f$:** $C_d$ is dimensionless, $A_f$ has units of m². Examiners verify unit consistency — so should you.
> 5. **Using $\sin\theta$ vs $\tan\theta$ for small grades:** KTU accepts either if you state your assumption, but consistency matters within a single problem.
> 6. **Skipping the "Free Body Diagram" step:** Even a simple FBD in the answer booklet earns 1 mark in 14-mark questions.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Master Equation to Memorize:**
  $$F_t = f_r m g + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f V^2 + m g \sin\theta + m \delta a$$
- **Speed-dependence hierarchy:** Rolling resistance (constant) < Gradient (constant) < Drag ($V^2$) < Inertia ($a$).
- **$V^2$ dependency of drag** is why EVs gain range disproportionately at lower cruising speeds.
- **Top speed** is found at the intersection of $F_t(V)$ and $F_r + F_a$.
- **Maximum grade** is found at the intersection of $F_t(V)$ and $F_r + F_a + F_g$ with $a = 0$.
- **Power at wheels** is $P_t = F_t \cdot V$ — never use rated motor power directly in tractive equations; gearbox losses matter.
- **Mass factor $\delta$** accounts for rotational inertia of wheels, motor rotor, and gears — without it, EV acceleration is overestimated.
- **EV advantage:** Electric motors deliver peak torque at zero RPM, eliminating the need for multi-gear transmissions in many designs — this directly simplifies the tractive effort analysis.
- **Energy per km:** Area under the $F_t$ vs $V$ curve, divided by drivetrain efficiency, gives Wh/km — directly feeds battery sizing.
- **Key constants to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ at standard atmosphere.
- **Typical EV values:** $f_r \in [0.008, 0.015]$, $C_d \in [0.22, 0.35]$, $A_f \in [1.8, 2.5]$ m², $\delta \in [1.05, 1.20]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Power Source Characterization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Vehicle Power Source Characterization # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Vehicle Power Source Characterization** is the systematic process of quantifying and comparing the performance attributes of energy storage and conversion devices used to propel a vehicle. In the context of Electric Vehicles (EVs), this involves evaluating parameters such as **specific energy (Wh/kg)**, **specific power (W/kg)**, **energy density (Wh/L)**, **power density (W/L)**, **state of charge (SOC)**, **state of health (SOH)**, **cycle life**, **efficiency**, and **charge/discharge characteristics** of sources like batteries, fuel cells, and ultracapacitors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):** > Characterization refers to the experimental and analytical determination of a power source's response to varying load conditions (constant current, constant power, pulse load), temperature profiles, and depth-of-discharge (DOD) cycles. It forms the engineering basis for selecting the right energy source for a given vehicle class (2W, 3W, 4W, heavy-duty). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition) Think of a vehicle's power source like a **water tank connected to a tap**: - **The size of the tank** = How much **energy** it can store (driving range). - **The width of the pipe** = How much **power** it can deliver at any instant (acceleration / hill climbing). - **A small tank with a fat pipe** (e.g., **Ultracapacitor**) → Low range, but explosive acceleration (burst power). - **A huge tank with a thin pipe** (e.g., **Fuel Cell**) → Long range, but slow response. - **A balanced tank and pipe** (e.g., **Lithium-Ion Battery**) → The modern EV compromise. Characterization is simply the engineering discipline of measuring "tank size" and "pipe width" precisely for every source, so a vehicle designer can match the source to the mission (city scooter vs. long-haul truck). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Universal Benchmarks in EV Characterization:** > - **Gravimetric Energy Density** (Specific Energy) → **Wh/kg** > - **Volumetric Energy Density** → **Wh/L** > - **Gravimetric Power Density** (Specific Power) → **W/kg** > - **Volumetric Power Density** → **W/L** > - **Round-Trip Efficiency** → **85%–95%** (for Li-ion) > - **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV)** → measured in **Volts (V)** > - **Internal Resistance (R_int)** → measured in **Ohms (Ω)** or **milli-Ohms (mΩ)** ## 1.4 Why Characterization Matters for KTU / KERALA EV Industry Kerala's EV transition (Kerala State Electric Vehicle Policy 2019, updated 2022) emphasizes two-wheelers, three-wheelers (auto-rickshaws in Kochi, Trivandrum), and electric boats. Each application demands a **different Ragone plot trade-off**. Without rigorous characterization, an EV in Munnar's ghat roads would either run out of energy (low specific energy) or fail to climb gradients (low specific power). # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Key Parameters in Detail A power source is characterized along **five engineering axes**: 1. **Energy Capacity (E)** – The total usable work stored. $$E = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} V(t) \cdot I(t) \, dt \quad \text{[Wh or Joules]}$$ 2. **Power Capability (P)** – The instantaneous delivery rate. $$P = V \cdot I = \frac{V^2}{R_{int}} = I^2 \cdot R_{int} \quad \text{[W]}$$ 3. **Energy Efficiency (η)** – Round-trip losses. $$\eta_{RT} = \frac{E_{discharge}}{E_{charge}} \times 100\%$$ 4. **Cycle Life (N)** – Number of full charge/discharge cycles before capacity drops to **80%** of the rated value (industry standard end-of-life criterion). 5. **Self-Discharge Rate** – Percentage of capacity lost per month when idle. ## 2.2 The Ragone Plot (Most Critical KTU Concept) The **Ragone Plot** is the single most important visualization tool in EV power source characterization. It is a log-log plot of **Specific Power (W/kg)** on the Y-axis vs. **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** on the X-axis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading a Ragone Plot:** > - The **top-right corner** = Ideal (lots of energy + lots of power) — currently unattainable. > - The **bottom-right** = High energy, low power (Fuel Cells, Li-S). > - The **top-left** = Low energy, high power (Ultracapacitors). > - The **middle cluster** = Li-ion, NiMH (balanced choice for EVs). **Engineering Trade-Off Rule:** For any given chemistry, increasing specific power (e.g., by making electrodes thinner) typically **reduces** specific energy, and vice-versa. This is the **Power-Energy Trade-off**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Exam-relevant. Memorize the symbol, unit, and physical meaning.** | Formula | Description | Symbol / Unit | Application / Vehicle Context | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $E = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ | Stored Energy | $E$ in Wh | Total pack sizing | | $P = \dfrac{V^2}{R_{int}}$ | Peak Power Limit | $P$ in W | Determines acceleration | | $\rho_E = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Specific Energy | Wh/kg | Range estimation | | $\rho_P = \dfrac{P}{m}$ | Specific Power | W/kg | Acceleration / gradeability | | $\varepsilon_V = \dfrac{E}{V_{vol}}$ | Energy Density | Wh/L | Packaging in chassis | | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ | Round-trip efficiency | dimensionless (%) | Energy cost / km | | $SOC = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$ | State of Charge | % | BMS algorithm | | $DOD = 100 - SOC$ | Depth of Discharge | % | Cycle life prediction | | $SOH = \dfrac{C_{actual}}{C_{rated}} \times 100$ | State of Health | % | Battery replacement decision | | $P_{peak} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}}$ | Maximum Power Transfer | W | Pulse-load capability (regen) | | $\tau = R_{int} \times C$ | RC Time Constant | seconds | Response time to load | | $E_{kinetic} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Vehicle kinetic energy | J | Regen-braking recovery sizing | *(Note: The vertical bar symbol is intentionally avoided in all formulas to preserve markdown table integrity.)* ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **BMS Design:** SOC/SOH algorithms depend directly on accurate OCV vs. SOC curves derived through characterization. - **Thermal Management:** Heat generation $Q = I^2 R_{int}$ is computed from internal resistance — a key characterization output. - **Vehicle Simulation:** Tools like ADVISOR, MATLAB/Simulink Driveline require characterized source data as **input lookup tables**. - **Warranty & Safety:** Cycle life vs. DOD curves (Peukert-like behavior) are obtained from accelerated characterization tests. ## 2.5 Comparison Table of Major EV Power Sources | Source | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Specific Power (W/kg) | Cycle Life | Charge Time | Best Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Lead-Acid** | 30 – 50 | 75 – 200 | 300 – 800 | 6 – 12 h | Low-speed e-rickshaws | | **NiMH** | 60 – 120 | 200 – 1000 | 500 – 2000 | 1 – 3 h | Toyota Prius (HEV) | | **Li-ion (NMC)** | 150 – 220 | 500 – 2000 | 1000 – 3000 | 0.5 – 2 h | Modern EVs (Tata Nexon EV) | | **LiFePO4 (LFP)** | 90 – 160 | 500 – 1500 | 2000 – 5000 | 1 – 2 h | E-buses, Kerala boats | | **Ultracapacitor** | 5 – 15 | 5000 – 10000 | $10^5$ – $10^6$ | seconds | Regenerative braking boost | | **Hydrogen Fuel Cell** | 400 – 1000 (system) | 200 – 600 | N/A (stack life) | minutes | Long-haul trucks, trains | # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Power Transfer Theorem Applied to a Battery **Problem Setup (KTU Standard):** A battery has open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ and internal resistance $R_{int}$. It is connected to a variable load $R_L$. Find the load resistance value that extracts **maximum power** from the battery. ### Step 1 — Write the circuit current using Ohm's Law The current flowing in the simple loop is determined by the net EMF divided by the total loop resistance. $$I = \frac{V_{oc}}{R_{int} + R_L}$$ **Explanation:** This is straightforward series circuit analysis. The internal resistance and load resistance both impede current flow, so they add in the denominator. ### Step 2 — Express the power delivered to the load The power dissipated in the load resistor is the square of the current multiplied by the load resistance. $$P_L = I^2 \cdot R_L = \left(\frac{V_{oc}}{R_{int} + R_L}\right)^2 \cdot R_L$$ **Explanation:** Squaring the current captures the non-linear dependence of power on load, which will be critical when we differentiate. ### Step 3 — Differentiate $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$ To find the maximum, we set the derivative of power with respect to the load resistance to zero. We use the quotient rule on the rationalized form. $$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = \frac{V_{oc}^2 (R_{int} + R_L)^2 - V_{oc}^2 R_L \cdot 2(R_{int} + R_L)}{(R_{int} + R_L)^4}$$ **Explanation:** The numerator of the derivative is obtained by applying $d(u/v)/dx = (u'v - uv')/v^2$ where $u = R_L$ and $v = (R_{int} + R_L)^2$. ### Step 4 — Simplify the numerator Factor out common terms from the numerator to isolate the meaningful condition. $$V_{oc}^2 (R_{int} + R_L)\big[(R_{int} + R_L) - 2R_L\big] = 0$$ $$\Rightarrow V_{oc}^2 (R_{int} + R_L)(R_{int} - R_L) = 0$$ **Explanation:** Since $V_{oc} \neq 0$ and $R_{int} + R_L > 0$, the only meaningful solution is when the last parenthesis equals zero. ### Step 5 — Identify the maximum power condition The maximum power transfer condition occurs when the load resistance exactly equals the internal resistance. $$\boxed{R_L = R_{int}}$$ ### Step 6 — Compute the maximum deliverable power Substitute $R_L = R_{int}$ back into the power equation to find the maximum power value. $$P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2 \cdot R_{int}}{(R_{int} + R_{int})^2} = \frac{V_{oc}^2 \cdot R_{int}}{4 R_{int}^2} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}}$$ **Explanation:** This is the **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem** as applied to a real-world electrochemical source. It is critical for sizing motor controllers and DC-DC converters. ## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Energy from Vehicle Range Requirements **Problem Setup:** A vehicle of mass $M$ (including battery) must achieve a range $R$ (km) on flat terrain at constant velocity $v$. The tractive force equals the rolling resistance. Find the required battery specific energy. ### Step 1 — Energy demand per km The rolling resistance force is the product of the weight, the gravitational acceleration, and the coefficient of rolling resistance. $$F_{roll} = M g C_{rr} \quad \text{[N]}$$ The energy consumed per kilometre is the force multiplied by the distance. $$E_{per\_km} = F_{roll} \times 1000 = 1000 \, M g C_{rr} \quad \text{[J/km]}$$ **Explanation:** A force in newtons acting over one metre delivers one joule of energy. One kilometre is one thousand metres, hence the factor of 1000. ### Step 2 — Total energy required for the trip Multiply the per-kilometre energy by the desired range to get the total energy demand. $$E_{total} = 1000 \, M g C_{rr} \cdot R \quad \text{[J]}$$ ### Step 3 — Convert to Wh Divide by 3600 to convert joules to watt-hours, the standard unit for battery capacity. $$E_{total} = \frac{1000 \, M g C_{rr} \cdot R}{3600} \quad \text{[Wh]}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for required specific energy The specific energy of the battery must satisfy the condition that the available energy (specific energy times battery mass) covers the total trip demand. $$\rho_E^{req} = \frac{E_{total}}{m_{bat}} \quad \text{[Wh/kg]}$$ $$\boxed{\rho_E^{req} = \frac{1000 \, M g C_{rr} \cdot R}{3600 \, m_{bat}}}$$ **Explanation:** This equation lets a designer back-calculate the chemistry requirement. For example, a 1500 kg EV with 300 kg battery needing 300 km range on flat road typically requires $\rho_E \geq 130$ Wh/kg — which is **why Lead-Acid fails** for modern EVs. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Source vs. Application | Application | Dominant Need | Required $\rho_E$ (Wh/kg) | Required $\rho_P$ (W/kg) | Optimal Source | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Electric 2W (e.g., Kerala scooters)** | Range + Light Weight | 100 – 150 | 400 – 800 | Li-ion (NMC) | | **E-Auto (3W in Kochi)** | Range + Frequent Stops | 80 – 120 | 300 – 600 | LFP | | **E-Bus (KSRTC-style)** | Long Life + Safety | 100 – 140 | 200 – 400 | LFP / LTO | | **Electric Boat (Kerala backwaters)** | High Torque, Moderate Range | 90 – 130 | 250 – 500 | LFP | | **Long-Haul Truck** | Maximum Range | 300 – 600 | 200 – 400 | Fuel Cell + Ultracap | | **F1 / Racing EV** | Burst Power | 50 – 100 | 5000+ | Ultracapacitor bank | | **Hybrid City Car (HEV)** | Power Buffer | 60 – 100 | 1500+ | NiMH / Li-ion | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Power Source Characterization Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify Power Source Type
Battery, FC, UC] --> B[Measure Static Parameters
OCV, Capacity, Mass, Volume] B --> C[Measure Dynamic Parameters
R_int, Time Constant, Efficiency] C --> D[Conduct Pulse Tests
HPPC Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization] D --> E[Plot Ragone Curve
Specific Power vs Specific Energy] E --> F[Match to Vehicle Mission
2W, 3W, 4W, Bus] F --> G[Generate Characterization Lookup Tables
For BMS and Vehicle Simulation] G --> H[KTU Validation
Cycle Life, SOC Curves, SOH Estimation] ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - **A → B** establishes the basic identity of the source. - **B → C** transitions from static (DC) to dynamic (transient) characterization. - **D** is the **HPPC test** (FreedomCAR protocol), the gold standard in EV source testing. - **E** produces the Ragone plot which is the single most examined artifact in KTU viva voce. ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Internal Architecture of a Characterized Battery Model ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Terminal Voltage V_t] --> B[Open Circuit Voltage V_oc] A --> C[Internal Resistance R_int] A --> D[RC Parallel Network] D --> E[Diffusion Capacitance C_d] D --> F[Charge Transfer R_ct] B --> G[BMS Estimator Block] C --> G D --> G G --> H[SOC Output] G --> I[SOH Output] G --> J[Available Power Output] H --> K[Vehicle Energy Management Unit] I --> K J --> K ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - The model separates the **thermodynamic voltage** ($V_{oc}$) from the **kinetic overpotentials** ($R_{int}$ + RC network). - The BMS consumes these characterized parameters to compute SOC/SOH and feed the vehicle's energy management system. ## 4.3 Ragone Plot (Schematic Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Fuel Cell System
High Wh per kg, Low W per kg] -->|Power Energy Tradeoff| B[Li-ion NMC
Balanced Region] B -->|Power Energy Tradeoff| C[Ultracapacitor
Low Wh per kg, High W per kg] B --> D[LiFePO4
Long Life Variant] B --> E[NiMH
Legacy HEV Choice] A --> F[Li-S Battery
Future Chemistry] F --> B ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - The arrows represent the **design trade-off**: pushing one parameter up (e.g., more power) pushes the other down (less energy). - A KTU examiner may ask: *"Why is the Ultracapacitor at the top-left and the Fuel Cell at the bottom-right?"* — answer in terms of fundamental charge-storage mechanism (electrostatic double-layer vs. continuous electrochemical conversion). ## 4.4 Comparison Flowchart: Source Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start Source Selection] --> Q1{Need more than 200 km range?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Refueling time critical below 10 min?} Q2 -- Yes --> FuelCell[Hydrogen Fuel Cell System] Q2 -- No --> LiIon[High Energy Li-ion Pack] Q1 -- No --> Q3{Application needs burst power?} Q3 -- Yes --> Hybrid[Li-ion plus Ultracapacitor Hybrid] Q3 -- No --> Standard[Standard Li-ion Pack] ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style] **Define specific energy and specific power. Why are they critical in selecting a power source for an electric vehicle?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** The amount of electrical energy stored per unit mass of the source. It directly determines the **driving range** of the vehicle. (1 Mark) - **Specific Power (W/kg):** The maximum power deliverable per unit mass. It determines **acceleration, hill-climbing ability, and regen-braking acceptance**. (1 Mark) - **Criticality:** A source with high specific energy but low specific power (e.g., fuel cell) is unsuitable for a sporty EV. Conversely, an ultracapacitor with high specific power but low specific energy is unsuitable for long-range travel. The Ragone plot helps visualize this trade-off. (1 Mark) --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] **What is a Ragone plot? Explain its significance in EV power source selection.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - A **Ragone plot** is a log-log graph of **specific power (W/kg)** on the Y-axis versus **specific energy (Wh/kg)** on the X-axis, used to compare energy storage devices. (1 Mark) - Each energy source (battery type, fuel cell, ultracapacitor) occupies a distinct region on the plot. (1 Mark) - **Significance:** It allows an EV designer to instantly visualize the power-energy trade-off, select the chemistry that best matches the **vehicle mission profile** (city commuting, highway cruising, racing), and identify hybrid-source combinations (e.g., battery + ultracapacitor) that span a larger operating envelope. (1 Mark) --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Style] **(a)** With neat block diagram, explain the procedure for characterization of an electrochemical power source. List the static and dynamic parameters measured. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A lithium-ion battery cell has an open-circuit voltage of $V_{oc} = 3.7 \text{ V}$ and an internal resistance of $R_{int} = 25 \text{ m}\Omega$. Calculate (i) the maximum power it can deliver to an external load, and (ii) the load resistance at which this occurs. If a vehicle requires $5 \text{ kW}$ peak power for 10 seconds during acceleration, determine the **minimum number of such cells** (in series-parallel configuration) needed. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — Characterization Procedure (7 Marks) The characterization of an electrochemical power source follows a structured protocol: 1. **Static Characterization** — Performed at constant current and temperature. (1 Mark) - Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$) vs. State of Charge (SOC) - Rated Capacity ($C_{Ah}$) - Energy Capacity ($E = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$) - Coulombic and Energy Efficiency 2. **Dynamic Characterization** — Performed under varying load. (1 Mark) - Internal Resistance ($R_{int}$) — from DC pulse test - HPPC (Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization) — FreedomCAR protocol - AC Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) — separates $R_{ct}$, $R_{diff}$, $C_{dl}$ 3. **Environmental Characterization** — Across temperatures (typically −20 °C to +55 °C for Kerala conditions). (1 Mark) 4. **Aging / Cycle Life Characterization** — Repeated charge/discharge at various DODs. (1 Mark) 5. **Block Diagram:** (3 Marks) - A test bench with programmable electronic load → climatic chamber → data acquisition system → characterization software. - The output is a set of lookup tables: OCV(SOC), R_int(SOC, T), Efficiency(I, T), Power Capability(SOC, T). --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks) **Given Data:** - $V_{oc} = 3.7 \text{ V}$ - $R_{int} = 25 \text{ m}\Omega = 0.025 \text{ }\Omega$ - Required peak power $P_{req} = 5 \text{ kW} = 5000 \text{ W}$ - Pulse duration: 10 s (energy is small, so steady-state peak power governs sizing) **Step 1: Maximum power per cell** (using Maximum Power Transfer Theorem derived in Section 3.1) $$P_{max,cell} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}} = \frac{(3.7)^2}{4 \times 0.025} = \frac{13.69}{0.1} = 136.9 \text{ W}$$ **Step 2: Load resistance for maximum power per cell** $$R_{L,opt} = R_{int} = 0.025 \text{ }\Omega = 25 \text{ m}\Omega$$ **Step 3: Minimum number of cells (in series-parallel)** Each cell delivers **136.9 W**. To meet **5000 W**, the required number of cells in a series-parallel configuration is found by dividing total required power by power per cell. $$N_{cells} = \frac{P_{req}}{P_{max,cell}} = \frac{5000}{136.9} \approx 36.5$$ Since we cannot have a fractional cell, we round up. $$\boxed{N_{cells} = 37 \text{ cells (in appropriate series-parallel combination, e.g., 37S1P or other combination)}}$$ **Practical Note:** In real EV packs, the pack voltage (e.g., 400 V) is set first, then cells in series give that voltage, and additional parallel strings provide the current. The 37-cell answer is a *minimum* count under matched-load (max-power-transfer) operation. In actual EVs, cells operate far from this point (≈ 30% of $P_{max}$) for efficiency and longevity. --- ### Question B. [Alternative Choice for Internal Option] **(a)** Draw a Ragone plot and explain the relative positions of Lead-Acid, NiMH, Li-ion, ultracapacitor, and fuel cell. Discuss the power-energy trade-off. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An electric auto-rickshaw in Kochi has a kerb weight of 500 kg (including a 120 kg battery). The rolling resistance coefficient is $C_{rr} = 0.015$. The vehicle must travel 80 km per day on flat urban roads. Calculate the **minimum specific energy** required from the battery pack. State which battery chemistry is most suitable. **(7 Marks)** --- ### Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — Ragone Plot and Trade-off (7 Marks) 1. **Ragone plot description** (1 Mark): Log-log plot, specific power (Y) vs. specific energy (X). The enclosed area under each curve represents the operating envelope. 2. **Relative positions** (3 Marks): - **Top-Left — Ultracapacitor:** 5–15 Wh/kg, 5000–10000 W/kg. Electrostatic energy storage at the electrode-electrolyte interface, no faradaic reaction, so high power but low capacity. - **Bottom-Right — Fuel Cell:** 400–1000 Wh/kg (system), 200–600 W/kg. Continuous conversion of hydrogen, so high energy but sluggish response. - **Middle — Li-ion (NMC):** 150–220 Wh/kg, 500–2000 W/kg. The balanced choice. - **Middle-Left — NiMH:** 60–120 Wh/kg, 200–1000 W/kg. Used in legacy HEVs (e.g., Toyota Prius). - **Bottom-Left — Lead-Acid:** 30–50 Wh/kg, 75–200 W/kg. Heavy, low power, suitable only for low-speed applications. 3. **Power-Energy Trade-off** (3 Marks): For any single chemistry, optimizing the electrode thickness and porosity for high power (thinner electrodes, more surface area) reduces the active material loading, thereby reducing energy. This fundamental inverse relationship is why **hybrid energy storage systems** (battery + ultracapacitor) are often used in performance EVs to span a wider Ragone envelope. --- #### Part (b) — Auto-Rickshaw Numerical (7 Marks) **Given:** - Total mass $M = 500 \text{ kg}$ - Battery mass $m_{bat} = 120 \text{ kg}$ - Rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr} = 0.015$ - Range $R = 80 \text{ km}$ - Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Step 1: Compute the rolling resistance force** The rolling resistance force is the normal force (weight) times the rolling resistance coefficient. $$F_{roll} = M g C_{rr} = 500 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 = 73.575 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2: Compute energy required per kilometre** The energy per kilometre is the force times the distance in metres (1000 m per km). $$E_{per\_km} = F_{roll} \times 1000 = 73.575 \times 1000 = 73{,}575 \text{ J/km}$$ **Step 3: Compute total energy required for 80 km trip** Multiply the per-kilometre energy by the desired range to obtain total trip energy. $$E_{total} = 73{,}575 \times 80 = 5{,}886{,}000 \text{ J}$$ **Step 4: Convert to watt-hours** Divide by 3600 to convert joules to watt-hours. $$E_{total} = \frac{5{,}886{,}000}{3600} = 1635 \text{ Wh}$$ **Step 5: Compute the required specific energy** The required specific energy is the total energy divided by the available battery mass. $$\rho_E^{req} = \frac{E_{total}}{m_{bat}} = \frac{1635}{120} = 13.625 \text{ Wh/kg}$$ **Step 6: Real-World Adjustment (efficiency factor)** In practice, the motor-controller efficiency is about 90% and the battery round-trip efficiency is about 95%. Combined efficiency is approximately 85%. We must inflate the energy demand by this factor. $$E_{total,actual} = \frac{1635}{0.85} \approx 1923.5 \text{ Wh}$$ $$\rho_E^{req,actual} = \frac{1923.5}{120} \approx 16.03 \text{ Wh/kg}$$ **Step 7: Select the chemistry** Almost **any** modern EV battery chemistry easily exceeds 16 Wh/kg. The deciding factors for a Kochi auto are now: **cycle life, safety, cost, and Kerala's hot-humid climate**. $$\boxed{\text{Recommended Chemistry: LiFePO4 (LFP) — 90–160 Wh/kg, 2000–5000 cycles, thermally stable.}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to use the right mass** — Use *battery mass only* (120 kg) in the specific energy denominator, NOT total vehicle mass. Common error: dividing by 500 kg. > 2. **Ignoring efficiency** — Pure mechanical analysis underestimates the real energy demand. A 10–15% efficiency factor is expected by KTU examiners. > 3. **Unit confusion** — Always convert Joules to Wh by dividing by 3600. Mixing J and Wh in the final answer is a guaranteed mark deduction. > 4. **Ragone plot reading** — Students often draw axes linearly instead of logarithmically. KTU specifically tests log-log comprehension. > 5. **Formula misapplication** — Mixing up $V^2/R$ (power) with $V \times I$ (power) and then writing $V^2/R$ in series-parallel without squaring the cell count. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - ☐ **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** determines **range**; **Specific Power (W/kg)** determines **acceleration**. - ☐ **Ragone plot** is **log-log**, Specific Power (Y) vs. Specific Energy (X). Most important visualization in EV characterization. - ☐ **Lead-Acid** is obsolete for modern 4W EVs (too heavy); survives only in low-speed 3Ws. - ☐ **Li-ion (NMC)** is the dominant EV chemistry today (Tata Nexon, MG ZS EV, Hyundai Kona). - ☐ **LFP (LiFePO4)** is preferred for buses, 3-wheelers, boats (Kerala context) due to safety + cycle life. - ☐ **Ultracapacitors** store energy electrostatically; they are not batteries and offer **100,000+ cycles**. - ☐ **Fuel Cells** are *energy converters*, not storage devices — hydrogen is the fuel, the cell is the engine. - ☐ **Maximum Power Transfer** for a real source occurs when $R_L = R_{int}$, giving $P_{max} = V_{oc}^2 / (4 R_{int})$. - ☐ **State of Charge (SOC)** and **State of Health (SOH)** are the two most important BMS outputs. - ☐ **HPPC Test** (Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization) is the industry standard for dynamic source characterization. - ☐ **Power-Energy Trade-off** is fundamental — you cannot maximize both in a single chemistry. - ☐ **Hybrid Storage** (Battery + Ultracapacitor) widens the Ragone envelope for performance EVs. - ☐ **Round-trip efficiency** of Li-ion is 85–95% — much higher than ICE (25–35%) and that's a key advantage. - ☐ For KTU numericals: always check units (J ↔ Wh ↔ kWh), and always include a safety/efficiency factor. - ☐ Kerala EV Policy 2022 targets 1 million EVs by 2025 — characterization expertise is **industry-relevant** for KTU graduates.

topics.title: Transmission Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transmission Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Transmission Characteristics?

In the context of Electric Vehicles (EVs), **Transmission Characteristics** refer to the systematic description of how the propulsion system's torque, speed, and power are transmitted from the electric motor to the driving wheels of the vehicle. It encapsulates the **torque-speed envelope**, **power-speed profile**, and the **tractive effort vs. vehicle speed** relationship required at the wheel-road interface to overcome aerodynamic drag, rolling resistance, and gradient loads.

Formally, in KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, transmission characteristics define the **mapping function** between the motor's intrinsic electromagnetic output and the vehicle's dynamic load demand, mediated by the gear reduction unit and final drive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires students to understand the *propulsion system characteristics*, *motor torque-speed curves*, and how they differ from the **constant-power narrow-band operation** of Internal Combustion Engines (ICEs).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Gear Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle up a steep hill.

- On **flat roads**, you spin the pedals fast but push *lightly* — this is the **high-speed / low-torque** region.
- On **steep hills**, you shift to a low gear: your feet push *hard* (high torque) but move *slowly* — this is the **low-speed / high-torque** region.

An Electric Vehicle's transmission is essentially an *automatic*, *electronic* equivalent of this gear-shifting logic, but instead of your legs, the **electric motor** delivers the effort. The key difference is that an EV motor can do this across an *enormous* speed range — from standstill to highway speeds — **without needing a multi-speed gearbox** in most cases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** The **Constant Power Speed Range (CPSR)** is the ratio between the base speed (where maximum power is first reached) and the maximum speed of the motor. Modern EV motors achieve a **CPSR of 3 to 5**, which is a key transmission characteristic.

## 1.3 Why Transmission Characteristics Matter in EVs

Transmission characteristics determine:

1. **Acceleration capability** (determined by tractive force at low speeds).
2. **Top speed capability** (determined by available power at high speeds).
3. **Energy efficiency** (because motor operating point must stay near its peak-efficiency island).
4. **Range and battery sizing** (a mismatched transmission can waste 10–20% of battery energy).
5. **Drive feel and drivability** (smooth torque delivery, regenerative braking integration).

## 1.4 The EV Transmission Equation — A Bird's-Eye View

The complete transmission chain follows this logical flow:

$$\text{Battery (Electrical Energy)} \longrightarrow \text{Motor Controller (Inverter)} \longrightarrow \text{Electric Motor (Electromagnetic Torque } T_m\text{)} \longrightarrow \text{Gearbox } (i_g) \longrightarrow \text{Differential } (i_0) \longrightarrow \text{Wheel } (T_w, v)$$

The **gear ratio** $i_g \cdot i_0$ multiplies motor torque and divides motor speed before delivering the final **tractive effort** $F_t$ at the tire contact patch.

---

## 1.5 Comparison: EV vs ICE Transmission Characteristics

| Parameter | ICE Vehicle | Electric Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| Torque at zero speed | **Zero** (stalls) | **Maximum** (peak torque) |
| Useful speed range | Narrow (1000–6000 rpm) | Wide (0–15,000 rpm) |
| Peak power band | Narrow window | Broad constant-power region |
| Transmission gears | 5–8 forward gears | 1 (typical), 2 (performance EVs) |
| CPSR | 1.5 – 2.0 | 3.0 – 5.0 |
| Reverse gear | Mechanical | Electronic (motor direction) |

> [!TIP]
> This table is **high-yield** for KTU short-answer questions. Memorize the contrast between ICE's **zero starting torque** and EV's **maximum starting torque**.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vehicle Propulsion Force Equation (Fundamental Law)

Every vehicle, electric or otherwise, must overcome **three resistive forces** to move forward:

$$F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc}$$

Where:

- $F_t$ = **Total Tractive Effort** required at the wheels (N)
- $F_r$ = **Rolling Resistance Force** (N)
- $F_{ad}$ = **Aerodynamic Drag Force** (N)
- $F_{hc}$ = **Hill Climbing Force** (N)

### 2.1.1 Rolling Resistance Force

The tire deforms as it rolls, dissipating energy as heat.

$$F_r = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

Where:
- $\mu_{rr}$ = **Coefficient of rolling resistance** (≈ 0.008 for radial tires, **bolded as a key constant**)
- $m$ = Vehicle mass (kg)
- $g$ = Gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $\alpha$ = Road gradient angle (radians or degrees)

> [!NOTE]
> For **flat roads** ($\alpha = 0$), $\cos(\alpha) = 1$, so $F_r$ reduces to the simple form $\mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$.

### 2.1.2 Aerodynamic Drag Force

The vehicle pushes air out of its way; air pushes back.

$$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

Where:
- $\rho_a$ = **Air density** = **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level (standard atmosphere)
- $C_d$ = **Coefficient of drag** (≈ **0.25–0.30** for modern EVs like Tesla Model 3 = 0.23)
- $A_f$ = **Frontal area** of the vehicle (m²)
- $v$ = Vehicle velocity (m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that **aerodynamic drag scales with the SQUARE of velocity**. This is why EVs become *significantly* less efficient at highway speeds — a critical exam point.

### 2.1.3 Hill Climbing Force

The component of gravitational pull along an inclined road:

$$F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

For small gradients expressed as a **percentage grade** $G\%$:

$$F_{hc} \approx m \cdot g \cdot \frac{G}{100} \quad \text{(for small angles, } \sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha\text{)}$$

## 2.2 Power Required at the Wheels

Multiplying the tractive force by velocity gives the **mechanical power demand** at the road:

$$P_w = F_t \cdot v = \left(\mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + m g \sin\alpha\right) \cdot v$$

The power demand **rises parabolically with speed** due to the $v^3$ dependence of aerodynamic power loss.

## 2.3 Motor-Side Transmission Equations

The gearbox and differential establish a kinematic relationship between the motor shaft and the wheel:

$$T_w = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_g$$

$$\omega_w = \frac{\omega_m}{i_g \cdot i_0}$$

$$v = \omega_w \cdot r_w = \frac{\omega_m \cdot r_w}{i_g \cdot i_0}$$

Where:
- $T_m$ = Motor torque (N·m)
- $T_w$ = Wheel torque (N·m)
- $\omega_m$ = Motor angular speed (rad/s)
- $\omega_w$ = Wheel angular speed (rad/s)
- $i_g$ = Gearbox ratio
- $i_0$ = Final drive (differential) ratio
- $\eta_g$ = Transmission efficiency (**typically 0.95–0.97** for single-speed)
- $r_w$ = Effective wheel radius (m)

The wheel force (tractive effort) is then:

$$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r_w} = \frac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_g}{r_w}$$

## 2.4 Idealized EV Motor Torque-Speed Characteristic

The signature "**constant torque, then constant power**" curve of an EV motor is piecewise-defined:

$$
T_m(\omega) =
\begin{cases}
T_{max} & \text{for } 0 \leq \omega \leq \omega_b \quad \text{(Constant Torque Region)} \\[4pt]
\dfrac{P_{max}}{\omega} & \text{for } \omega_b < \omega \leq \omega_{max} \quad \text{(Constant Power Region)}
\end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $T_{max}$ = Maximum motor torque (N·m)
- $P_{max}$ = Maximum motor power (W or kW)
- $\omega_b$ = **Base speed** (rad/s) — the corner point
- $\omega_{max}$ = Maximum motor speed (rad/s)

The **Constant Power Speed Range (CPSR)**:

$$\text{CPSR} = \frac{\omega_{max}}{\omega_b}$$

> [!TIP]
> **CPSR is THE most important transmission characteristic in EV design.** A higher CPSR means the motor can deliver peak power across a wider speed range, often eliminating the need for a multi-speed transmission.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meanings | Units | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc}$ | Total tractive force | N | Vehicle dynamics balance |
| 2 | $F_r = \mu_{rr} \cdot m g \cos\alpha$ | Rolling resistance | N | Tire-road losses |
| 3 | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag | N | Air resistance |
| 4 | $F_{hc} = m g \sin\alpha$ | Hill climb force | N | Gradient resistance |
| 5 | $P_w = F_t \cdot v$ | Wheel power | W | Power demand |
| 6 | $T_w = T_m i_g i_0 \eta_g$ | Torque at wheel | N·m | Gearbox output |
| 7 | $v = \omega_m r_w / (i_g i_0)$ | Vehicle velocity | m/s | Kinematic relation |
| 8 | $F_t = T_m i_g i_0 \eta_g / r_w$ | Tractive effort | N | Motor-to-road |
| 9 | $\text{CPSR} = \omega_{max} / \omega_b$ | Constant power range | dimensionless | Motor suitability |
| 10 | $P_{max} = T_{max} \cdot \omega_b$ | Power equality at corner | W | Linking base speed |
| 11 | $a = (F_t - F_{resistive}) / m$ | Vehicle acceleration | m/s² | Newton's 2nd law |
| 12 | $W_{batt} = \int P_{motor} \, dt / \eta_{overall}$ | Battery energy | Wh | Range estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do NOT use the vertical pipe `\|` in any cell above.** Standard KTU notation uses $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute values. The cheat sheet above uses clean LaTeX syntax to keep markdown tables intact.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in the Field

- **EV Powertrain Sizing:** Automotive OEMs (Tesla, Tata, BYD) size their motors by first computing the **peak tractive effort** needed for 0–100 km/h acceleration, then back-calculating required $T_m$, $i_g$, and $P_{max}$.
- **Single-Speed vs. Multi-Speed Decision:** If the CPSR of the chosen motor ≥ required CPSR of the vehicle, a **single-speed reducer** suffices (cheaper, lighter). Otherwise, a 2-speed gearbox (Porsche Taycan, Audi e-tron GT) is needed.
- **Regenerative Braking Integration:** The transmission characteristic defines the maximum negative torque the motor can absorb during regen, directly impacting brake energy recovery efficiency.
- **Battery Sizing:** A well-matched transmission reduces peak motor current demand, allowing a smaller, cheaper battery pack for the same performance.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wheel Force from Motor Torque (Transmission Equation)

We will derive the fundamental relationship between motor torque and tractive effort at the tire contact patch.

**Step 1: Start with the gearbox torque amplification.**

The gearbox multiplies torque by its ratio $i_g$ but introduces frictional losses captured by $\eta_g$:

$$T_{out,gear} = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot \eta_g$$

**Step 2: The differential (final drive) further multiplies torque.**

The differential adds another ratio $i_0$:

$$T_w = T_{out,gear} \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_d$$

Assuming the differential efficiency is bundled into the total $\eta_g$:

$$T_w = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_g$$

**Step 3: Convert wheel torque to wheel force using the wheel radius.**

The wheel torque acts through the effective tire radius $r_w$ to push the vehicle:

$$F_t = \frac{T_w}{r_w}$$

**Step 4: Substitute Step 2 into Step 3.**

$$F_t = \frac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_g}{r_w}$$

**Step 5: Convert motor angular speed to vehicle velocity.**

Wheel angular speed relates to motor speed by:

$$\omega_w = \frac{\omega_m}{i_g \cdot i_0}$$

Linear velocity at the tire contact patch is:

$$v = \omega_w \cdot r_w = \frac{\omega_m \cdot r_w}{i_g \cdot i_0}$$

**Step 6: Equivalently, express the velocity in terms of motor RPM.**

Using $\omega_m = 2\pi N_m / 60$ where $N_m$ is motor RPM:

$$v = \frac{2\pi N_m \cdot r_w}{60 \cdot i_g \cdot i_0}$$

**Step 7: Power consistency check.**

Power at the motor shaft:

$$P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m$$

Power at the wheel:

$$P_w = F_t \cdot v = \frac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_g}{r_w} \cdot \frac{\omega_m \cdot r_w}{i_g \cdot i_0} = T_m \cdot \omega_m \cdot \eta_g = P_m \cdot \eta_g$$

This confirms that the **transmission efficiency $\eta_g$ is the only source of power loss** in an ideal mechanical chain — a clean check on the derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Base Speed of an EV Motor

The base speed $\omega_b$ is the rotational speed at which the motor's back-EMF equals the applied DC bus voltage under maximum power conditions.

**Step 1: Motor back-EMF equation.**

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_m$$

Where $K_e$ is the motor's back-EMF constant (V·s/rad).

**Step 2: Maximum motor terminal voltage** (limited by battery / inverter):

$$V_{max} = V_{batt}$$

**Step 3: At base speed, with maximum current, voltage drop across stator resistance is small (often neglected in idealized analysis):**

$$V_{max} \approx E_b = K_e \cdot \omega_b$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\omega_b$:**

$$\omega_b = \frac{V_{max}}{K_e}$$

**Step 5: Maximum torque at zero speed** (where back-EMF is zero) is:

$$T_{max} = K_t \cdot I_{max}$$

Where $K_t = K_e$ in SI units, and $I_{max}$ is the inverter's current limit.

**Step 6: At the corner point, $P_{max} = T_{max} \cdot \omega_b$:**

$$P_{max} = T_{max} \cdot \omega_b = K_t \cdot I_{max} \cdot \frac{V_{max}}{K_e} \approx V_{max} \cdot I_{max}$$

This is the **electrical input power** to the motor — a powerful insight: the motor's peak mechanical power is approximately the product of DC bus voltage and maximum inverter current.

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Gradeability of an EV

**Step 1: Set tractive force equal to sum of all resistances at maximum grade.**

$$F_{t,max} = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc,max}$$

**Step 2: Substitute expressions:**

$$\frac{T_{max} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_g}{r_w} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\alpha_{max} + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2 + m g \sin\alpha_{max}$$

**Step 3: At low speed (typical for hill start), neglect aerodynamic drag:**

$$\frac{T_{max} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_g}{r_w} = m g (\mu_{rr} \cos\alpha_{max} + \sin\alpha_{max})$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\alpha_{max}$ using the identity $\sin\alpha + \mu\cos\alpha = \sqrt{1+\mu^2}\sin(\alpha + \phi)$ where $\tan\phi = \mu$:**

$$\alpha_{max} = \arcsin\left(\frac{F_{t,max}}{m g \sqrt{1+\mu_{rr}^2}}\right) - \arctan(\mu_{rr})$$

> [!NOTE]
> For typical EVs with $\mu_{rr} = 0.01$, the arctangent term is small, so the maximum grade is approximately $\arcsin(F_{t,max} / (mg))$ — a useful engineering approximation.

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Full Transmission Analysis (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A passenger EV has the following specifications:
- Vehicle mass $m = 1500$ kg
- Wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m
- Frontal area $A_f = 2.2$ m²
- Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.28$
- Rolling resistance $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$
- Motor max torque $T_{max} = 320$ N·m
- Motor max power $P_{max} = 150$ kW
- Gear ratio (combined) $i = i_g \cdot i_0 = 8.5$
- Transmission efficiency $\eta_g = 0.95$
- Air density $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³

**Part (a): Find the maximum tractive effort at the wheels.**

Using the formula $F_{t,max} = T_{max} \cdot i \cdot \eta_g / r_w$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t,max} &= \frac{320 \times 8.5 \times 0.95}{0.32} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2584}{0.32} \\[4pt]
&= 8075 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b): Find the base speed of the motor.**

Using $P_{max} = T_{max} \cdot \omega_b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_b &= \frac{P_{max}}{T_{max}} = \frac{150{,}000}{320} \\[4pt]
&= 468.75 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to RPM:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_b &= \frac{468.75 \times 60}{2\pi} \\[4pt]
&= 4476 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c): Find the maximum vehicle speed at rated power.**

At maximum speed, $F_t = F_{resistive}$. Assuming flat road and neglecting rolling resistance at high speed:

$$P_{max} \cdot \eta_g = F_{ad} \cdot v_{max} = \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v_{max}^3$$

Solving for $v_{max}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{max}^3 &= \frac{2 \cdot P_{max} \cdot \eta_g}{\rho_a C_d A_f} \\[4pt]
v_{max}^3 &= \frac{2 \times 150{,}000 \times 0.95}{1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.2} \\[4pt]
v_{max}^3 &= \frac{285{,}000}{0.7546} \\[4pt]
v_{max}^3 &= 377{,}650 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^3 \\[4pt]
v_{max} &= \sqrt[3]{377{,}650} \\[4pt]
v_{max} &\approx 72.18 \text{ m/s} \approx 259.8 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In real-world KTU problems, students are expected to include rolling resistance as well. The simplified derivation above shows the **theoretical maximum**; in practice, friction and drivetrain losses reduce this by 5–10%.

**Part (d): Find the maximum grade the vehicle can climb from rest.**

From Step 3.3, at $v = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\alpha_{max} &\approx \frac{F_{t,max}}{m g} - \mu_{rr} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{8075}{1500 \times 9.81} - 0.012 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5488 - 0.012 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5368
\end{aligned}
$$

So:

$$\alpha_{max} = \arcsin(0.5368) \approx 32.5° \quad \text{(equivalent grade} \approx 63.6\% \text{)}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a *theoretical* maximum. In practice, traction limits (tire-road friction $\mu \approx 0.8$–$1.0$) cap the maximum tractive force well below $F_{t,max}$ for safety and tire wear.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Single-Speed vs. Multi-Speed

| Criterion | Single-Speed (e.g., Tesla, Nissan Leaf) | Multi-Speed (e.g., Porsche Taycan) |
|---|---|---|
| Motor CPSR required | ≥ 3.0 | 1.5 – 2.5 acceptable |
| System weight | **Lighter** (≈ 30 kg) | Heavier (≈ 60–80 kg) |
| Efficiency at low speed | High (no shifting losses) | High (gear 1 optimized) |
| Efficiency at high speed | Slightly lower (motor in field-weakening) | Higher (gear 2 optimized) |
| Cost | **Lower** | Higher |
| Complexity | Minimal (one gear set) | Synchronizers, clutch, actuator |
| Reliability | **Higher** (fewer parts) | Lower (more failure modes) |
| Acceleration | Excellent up to base speed | Excellent across all speeds |
| Top speed | Limited by motor's field-weakening capability | **Higher** for same motor |
| Application | City cars, sedans, SUVs | Sports cars, high-performance EVs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Always justify the choice of single vs. multi-speed transmission in numerical design problems by computing the **required CPSR** from the vehicle's target performance envelope and comparing it with the **available CPSR** of the proposed motor.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EV Powertrain Transmission Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the complete signal and power flow from the energy source to the road, with annotations for transmission characteristics at each interface.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Battery["BATTERY PACK"] -->|V_batt I_batt| Inverter["POWER ELECTRONIC INVERTER"]
    Inverter -->|3-phase AC| Motor["ELECTRIC MOTOR"]
    Motor -->|Torque Tm, Speed wm| Coupling["SHAFT COUPLING"]
    Coupling -->|1:ig| Gearbox["SINGLE OR MULTI-SPEED GEARBOX"]
    Gearbox -->|1:i0| Differential["DIFFERENTIAL FINAL DRIVE"]
    Differential -->|Tw, ww| HalfShaftLeft["LEFT HALF SHAFT"]
    Differential -->|Tw, ww| HalfShaftRight["RIGHT HALF SHAFT"]
    HalfShaftLeft --> WheelLeft["LEFT WHEEL"]
    HalfShaftRight --> WheelRight["RIGHT WHEEL"]
    WheelLeft --> Road["ROAD SURFACE"]
    WheelRight --> Road
    Road -->|Rolling Friction Fr| ResistiveForces["RESISTIVE FORCES"]
    Road -->|Aerodynamic Drag Fad| ResistiveForces
    Road -->|Hill Climb Fhc| ResistiveForces
    ResistiveForces -->|Ft demand| VehicleBody["VEHICLE CHASSIS"]
    VehicleBody -->|Feedback: Accelerator Pedal| ControllerUnit["VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT VCU"]
    ControllerUnit -->|PWM commands| Inverter
    Motor -.->|Resolver Encoder| ControllerUnit
```

**Annotations (read alongside the diagram):**

- **Battery → Inverter:** DC link, transmission characteristic = **Voltage/Current profile**.
- **Inverter → Motor:** Variable frequency AC, transmission characteristic = **Torque per ampere (K_t)**.
- **Motor → Coupling:** Shaft torque, characteristic = **Torque-speed curve**.
- **Coupling → Gearbox → Differential:** Mechanical reduction, characteristic = **Overall ratio $i_g i_0$ and efficiency $\eta_g$**.
- **Differential → Wheels:** Final tractive effort, characteristic = **Wheel torque and wheel speed**.

## 4.2 Transmission Characteristic Curve (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Vehicle Operating Point]) --> SpeedCheck{Speed Region}
    SpeedCheck -->|Low Speed 0 to vb| Region1["CONSTANT TORQUE REGION"]
    SpeedCheck -->|Mid Speed vb to vmax| Region2["CONSTANT POWER REGION"]
    SpeedCheck -->|Above vmax| Region3["MOTOR CUTOFF REGION"]
    Region1 --> TorqueEq["Tm = Tmax = constant"]
    Region2 --> PowerEq["Pm = Tmax x wb = constant"]
    Region3 --> Cutoff["No tractive effort available"]
    TorqueEq --> AccelCalc["Maximum acceleration available"]
    PowerEq --> HighSpeed["High speed cruise capability"]
    AccelCalc --> GradeClimb["Hill climbing capability"]
    HighSpeed --> TopSpeed["Maximum vehicle speed"]
    GradeClimb --> MatchCheck{CPSRp Motor >= CPSR Vehicle}
    TopSpeed --> MatchCheck
    MatchCheck -->|Yes| SingleGear["SINGLE SPEED TRANSMISSION SUFFICIENT"]
    MatchCheck -->|No| MultiGear["MULTI SPEED TRANSMISSION REQUIRED"]
```

## 4.3 Motor Torque-Speed-Power Transmission Envelope

```mermaid
graph LR
    AxisOrigin["0"] --> Xaxis["Motor Speed wm rad per s"]
    Xaxis --> BasePoint["Base Speed wb"]
    BasePoint --> MaxPoint["Max Speed wmax"]
    Yaxis["Torque and Power"] --> TorqueStart["Tmax peak"]
    TorqueStart --> TorqueCorner["Tmax at wb"]
    TorqueCorner --> TorqueEnd["Pmax / w"]
    TorqueEnd --> TorqueFall["Declining torque"]
    PowerFlat["Pmax constant"] --> PowerFlat
    PowerLine["Pmax = T x w"] --> PowerFlat
    PowerFlat --> PowerBase["At wb"]
    PowerFlat --> PowerMax["At wmax"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid graphs above represent **block-level functional architecture flow** rather than physical drawings, as per protocol for complex physical systems. The exact torque-speed curve in numerical form is given in Section 2.4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Driver["DRIVER INPUT ACCELERATOR PEDAL"] --> VCU["VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT"]
    VCU --> TorqueRequest["REQUESTED MOTOR TORQUE TR"]
```
