# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define the Constant Power Speed Range (CPSR) of an electric vehicle motor. Why is it considered the most important transmission characteristic in EV design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Constant Power Speed Range (CPSR) is defined as the ratio of the motor's maximum speed to its base speed:

$$\text{CPSR} = \frac{\omega_{max}}{\omega_b}$$

It is the most important transmission characteristic because:

1. **(1 mark)** It determines the motor's ability to deliver **peak power across the full vehicle speed envelope**. A higher CPSR (3–5) means the motor can maintain constant power from base speed all the way to top speed.
2. **(1 mark)** A high CPSR allows the use of a **single-speed transmission**, which is lighter, cheaper, more efficient, and more reliable than multi-speed gearboxes.
3. **(1 mark)** It directly impacts the vehicle's **acceleration and gradeability** at high speeds, where constant-torque operation alone would be insufficient.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Compare the transmission characteristics of an Electric Vehicle with that of a conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle in terms of starting torque and speed range.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ICE Vehicle | Electric Vehicle |
|---|---|---|
| Starting torque (1 mark) | **Zero** — engine cannot deliver torque at zero RPM (stalls) | **Maximum (peak)** — motor delivers $T_{max}$ from standstill |
| Useful speed range (1 mark) | Narrow: ~1000–6000 RPM (≈ 6:1) | Wide: ~0–15,000 RPM (≈ 15:1) |
| Transmission requirement (1 mark) | 5–8 forward gears + reverse | 1-speed (typical) + electronic reverse |

The key advantage of EV transmission is the ability to deliver **peak torque at zero speed** and maintain **constant power over a wide speed range**, drastically reducing mechanical complexity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (i) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(14 Marks)** An electric vehicle has the following specifications:

- Total mass (including payload) $m = 1800$ kg
- Wheel radius $r_w = 0.33$ m
- Frontal area $A_f = 2.3$ m²
- Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$
- Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr} = 0.010$
- Motor peak torque $T_{max} = 360$ N·m
- Motor peak power $P_{max} = 160$ kW
- Overall gear ratio $i = i_g \times i_0 = 9.0$
- Transmission efficiency $\eta_g = 0.94$
- Air density $\rho_a = 1.22$ kg/m³
- Acceleration of gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**
Calculate the maximum tractive effort at the wheels and the corresponding maximum acceleration of the vehicle on a flat road at a low speed of 5 m/s.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**
Determine the maximum constant gradient the vehicle can climb from rest, and comment on whether a single-speed transmission is sufficient if the target top speed is 180 km/h.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute maximum tractive effort.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t,max} &= \frac{T_{max} \cdot i \cdot \eta_g}{r_w} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{360 \times 9.0 \times 0.94}{0.33} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3045.6}{0.33} \\[4pt]
&= 9229.1 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute resistive forces at $v = 5$ m/s on flat road.**

Rolling resistance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_r &= \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(0°) \\[4pt]
&= 0.010 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times 1.0 \\[4pt]
&= 176.58 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Aerodynamic drag:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{ad} &= \frac{1}{2} \times 1.22 \times 0.30 \times 2.3 \times 5^2 \\[4pt]
&= 0.5 \times 1.22 \times 0.30 \times 2.3 \times 25 \\[4pt]
&= 10.52 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total resistance: $F_{resistive} = 176.58 + 10.52 = 187.10$ N

**Step 3: Net force and acceleration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{net} &= F_{t,max} - F_{resistive} = 9229.1 - 187.1 = 9042.0 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
a &= \frac{F_{net}}{m} = \frac{9042.0}{1800} = 5.02 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Maximum acceleration ≈ **5.02 m/s²** (≈ 0–100 km/h in approximately **5.55 seconds**).

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Maximum grade from rest.**

At $v = 0$, neglect aerodynamic drag. The condition $F_{t,max} = F_r + F_{hc}$ gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T_{max} \cdot i \cdot \eta_g}{r_w} &= m g (\mu_{rr} \cos\alpha + \sin\alpha) \\[4pt]
9229.1 &= 1800 \times 9.81 \times (0.010 \cos\alpha + \sin\alpha) \\[4pt]
9229.1 &= 17658 \times (0.010 \cos\alpha + \sin\alpha) \\[4pt]
0.5227 &= 0.010 \cos\alpha + \sin\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving numerically (e.g., via iteration or graphical method):

$$\alpha_{max} \approx 29.5° \quad \text{(equivalent grade ≈ 56.6%)}$$

**Step 2: Check single-speed sufficiency for top speed of 180 km/h.**

Target speed: $v_{max} = 180$ km/h $= 50$ m/s

Required motor speed at top speed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{m,max} &= \frac{v_{max} \cdot i}{r_w} = \frac{50 \times 9.0}{0.33} = 1363.6 \text{ rad/s} \\[4pt]
N_{m,max} &= \frac{1363.6 \times 60}{2\pi} = 13{,}021 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

Base speed of motor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_b &= \frac{P_{max}}{T_{max}} = \frac{160{,}000}{360} = 444.4 \text{ rad/s} \\[4pt]
N_b &= \frac{444.4 \times 60}{2\pi} = 4244 \text{ RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

Required CPSR:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPSR}_{req} &= \frac{\omega_{m,max}}{\omega_b} = \frac{1363.6}{444.4} \approx 3.07
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Verdict.**

A modern EV motor (e.g., PMSM) can typically achieve CPSR = 3.5–5.0 with field-weakening. Since the required CPSR (3.07) is within this range, a **single-speed transmission is sufficient**. ✅

---

### Question B — Option (ii) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(14 Marks)** With the aid of a clear transmission characteristic diagram, explain the **constant-torque** and **constant-power regions** of an EV propulsion motor. A 75 kW electric motor has a base speed of 3000 RPM and a maximum speed of 12,000 RPM. The peak torque is 240 N·m.

**Part (a) [7 marks]:**
Sketch and label the torque-speed and power-speed characteristics. Compute the CPSR and discuss the transmission implications.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:**
If this motor is installed in a vehicle with wheel radius 0.30 m and total gear ratio (gearbox × differential) of 7.5 with efficiency 0.96, calculate the maximum tractive effort and the corresponding maximum vehicle speed in km/h.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate CPSR.**

Base speed: $N_b = 3000$ RPM

Max speed: $N_{max} = 12{,}000$ RPM

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPSR} &= \frac{N_{max}}{N_b} = \frac{12{,}000}{3000} = 4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Sketch the characteristics (textual representation).**

```
Power (kW)
75 |................________________________
   |               /
   |              /
   |             /
   |            /
  0 |___________/_________________________> Speed (RPM)
   0          3000                       12000

Torque (N.m)
240 |____________________
    |                    \
    |                     \
    |                      \___
    |                          \________
  0 |__________________________________> Speed (RPM)
    0          3000                    12000
```

**Step 3: Transmission implications.**

- **(2 marks)** A CPSR of 4.0 is **excellent** and lies in the typical high-performance range. It means the motor can deliver constant 75 kW from 3000 RPM to 12,000 RPM, providing a **broad high-speed cruise region**.
- **(2 marks)** This high CPSR means a **single-speed transmission is sufficient** for most passenger car applications, eliminating the cost, weight, and efficiency losses of a multi-speed gearbox.
- **(1 mark)** In the constant-torque region (0–3000 RPM), the vehicle achieves **maximum acceleration and gradeability**; in the constant-power region (3000–12,000 RPM), the vehicle maintains **high-speed cruise and overtaking capability**.
- **(1 mark)** The wide CPSR also ensures **seamless regenerative braking** across the entire speed range, improving energy recovery.
- **(1 mark)** This characteristic makes such a motor ideal for both **city EVs** (low-speed torque) and **highway EVs** (high-speed power).

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

**Step 1: Maximum tractive effort.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{t,max} &= \frac{T_{max} \cdot i \cdot \eta_g}{r_w} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{240 \times 7.5 \times 0.96}{0.30} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1728}{0.30} \\[4pt]
&= 5760 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum vehicle speed.**

First, convert motor max speed to rad/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{max} &= \frac{2\pi N_{max}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 12{,}000}{60} = 1256.6 \text{ rad/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum vehicle speed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{max} &= \frac{\omega_{max} \cdot r_w}{i} = \frac{1256.6 \times 0.30}{7.5} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{376.99}{7.5} = 50.27 \text{ m/s} \\[4pt]
v_{max} \text{ in km/h} &= 50.27 \times 3.6 = 180.95 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Maximum tractive effort ≈ **5760 N** and maximum vehicle speed ≈ **181 km/h**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Transmission Characteristics Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s** (or vice versa). KTU examiners specifically test unit conversions. Always use $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ explicitly. **(Loses 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Neglecting transmission efficiency $\eta_g$** in tractive effort calculations. The efficiency multiplies torque but does NOT affect speed ratios. **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 3. **Confusing CPSR with power-to-weight ratio.** CPSR is dimensionless; power-to-weight has units of W/kg. **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 4. **Mixing up base speed and maximum speed.** Base speed is the corner of the torque-power curve; maximum speed is the upper limit of operation. **(Loses 1–2 marks)**
> 5. **Forgetting to include rolling resistance** in low-speed tractive effort problems. Rolling resistance is independent of speed but proportional to mass. **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 6. **Averaging aerodynamic drag.** Drag is proportional to $v^2$, so the average is NOT equal to the value at the average speed. Use integration in energy problems. **(Loses 2 marks)**
> 7. **Not justifying single-speed vs. multi-speed choice.** KTU expects a *quantitative* comparison of required CPSR vs. motor CPSR. **(Loses 1–2 marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Three forces at the wheel:** $F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc}$ — memorize the three formulas.
- ✅ **Rolling resistance:** $F_r = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\alpha$ — independent of speed.
- ✅ **Aerodynamic drag:** $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2$ — quadratic in speed.
- ✅ **Hill climb force:** $F_{hc} = m g \sin\alpha$ — zero on flat roads.
- ✅ **Power at the wheel:** $P_w = F_t \cdot v$ — equals motor power times $\eta_g$.
- ✅ **Torque transmission:** $T_w = T_m \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_g$.
- ✅ **Speed transmission:** $v = \omega_m r_w / (i_g i_0)$ — gear ratio divides speed.
- ✅ **CPSR:** $\text{CPSR} = \omega_{max} / \omega_b$ — the master metric for EV motors.
- ✅ **Base speed:** $\omega_b = P_{max} / T_{max}$.
- ✅ **EV vs. ICE:** EVs have **max torque at zero speed**; ICEs have **zero torque at zero speed** — the defining contrast.
- ✅ **Single-speed rule of thumb:** Single-speed is sufficient if required CPSR ≤ 4.0 (typical EV motor capability).
- ✅ **Top speed equation:** $v_{max} = \sqrt[3]{2 P_{max} \eta_g / (\rho_a C_d A_f)}$ (aerodynamic limit, flat road).
- ✅ **Constants to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³ (sea level), $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.008$–$0.015$.
- ✅ **Power flow direction:** Battery → Inverter → Motor → Gearbox → Differential → Wheels → Road.
- ✅ **Regenerative braking:** Works in the constant-power region; the transmission characteristic must allow negative torque without exceeding the motor's thermal limit.
- ✅ **KTU exam weightage:** Transmission characteristics typically appear in **Part A (3 marks)** as a definition or comparison, and in **Part B (7–14 marks)** as a numerical design problem.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When solving KTU transmission problems, always (1) draw a free-body diagram, (2) list all given quantities with units, (3) identify the governing equation from the cheat sheet, and (4) verify your answer's physical plausibility (e.g., top speed should be 80–250 km/h, tractive effort should be 2,000–15,000 N for passenger EVs).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Electric Vehicles: History of Electric Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Electric Vehicles: History of Electric Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Electric Vehicle? (KTU 2024 Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **Electric Vehicle (EV)** is a road vehicle whose propulsion force is derived entirely or partially from an **electric motor**, drawing energy from a rechargeable **on-board electrical energy storage system** (typically a battery, but may include supercapacitors or fuel cells), instead of being sourced solely from the combustion of a fossil fuel in an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE).

In the KTU 2024 framework, EVs are broadly classified into three primary architectural categories:

| Class | Full Form | Power Source |
|---|---|---|
| **BEV** | Battery Electric Vehicle | Battery only (100% electric) |
| **HEV** | Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Battery + ICE (no plug) |
| **PHEV** | Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle | Battery + ICE (with plug) |
| **FCEV** | Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle | Hydrogen Fuel Cell |

## 1.2 The Big Picture — A Simple Analogy for First-Time Learners

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Bucket of Water" Model**
> Imagine a traditional petrol car is like a **campfire** — you keep feeding it wood (petrol) constantly to keep it running, and it produces smoke (exhaust emissions). Now picture an Electric Vehicle as a **rechargeable torchlight**. You charge it once (like filling a bucket with water), and as long as there is energy in the battery, it produces light (motion) cleanly, silently, and with zero on-site emissions. When the bucket is empty, you simply refill it (plug in to charge).

**Why is this analogy useful?**
- It captures the **energy storage** aspect (bucket = battery)
- It captures the **refill/recharge** cycle
- It captures the **clean conversion** (no smoke, no noise)
- It abstracts away complex drivetrain details for a beginner

## 1.3 Why Study the History? — KTU Examiner's Perspective

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PEEET522 expects students to **trace the evolution of EV technology from the 19th century to the modern Tesla-era**. The history is **not** trivia — it is a **design philosophy** chapter. Every modern engineering choice (battery chemistry, motor type, charging standard) has a **historical root cause**.

The history matters for three engineering reasons:
1. **Technology Path Dependency** — why lead-acid dominated for 100 years.
2. **Market Disruption Cycles** — why EVs lost to ICE in 1920s and are winning now.
3. **Standards Evolution** — why today's charging protocols (CCS, CHAdeMO, Type-2) exist.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Eras of Electric Vehicle History

A systematic KTU-style breakdown of the historical timeline is given below. The history is divided into **five distinct engineering eras** based on the dominant technology and socio-economic conditions of the period.

### Era 1 — The Dawn (1832 – 1899)
- **1832**: Scottish inventor **Robert Anderson** builds the first crude electric carriage (non-rechargeable galvanic cells).
- **1834**: American blacksmith **Thomas Davenport** builds the first DC motor-driven electric vehicle.
- **1838**: Scottish inventor **Robert Davidson** demonstrates a battery-powered locomotive in Edinburgh.
- **1859**: French physicist **Gaston Planté** invents the **lead-acid battery** — the first rechargeable battery.
- **1881**: French engineer **Gustave Trouvé** presents the first **practical, tricycle-shaped electric vehicle** in Paris.
- **1899**: Belgian race-driver **Camille Jenatzy** drives the rocket-shaped **"La Jamais Contente"** to **105.88 km/h** — breaking the **100 km/h land speed barrier** for the first time in history (in an EV!).

### Era 2 — The Golden Age (1900 – 1920)
- EVs were **dominant** in early automobile markets.
- At the peak (around 1900), **28% of all automobiles in the United States were electric**.
- EVs were preferred by urban women drivers (no crank-starting, no gear shifting).
- Famous models: **Detroit Electric (1907–1939)**, **Fritchle Electric, Baker Electric**.

### Era 3 — The Decline (1920 – 1990)
- **Henry Ford's Model T (1908)** made ICE cars cheap via **mass production assembly line** (price dropped to ~$260).
- **Charles Kettering's electric starter (1912)** eliminated the dangerous hand crank.
- **Texas oil discovery (Spindletop, 1901)** → cheap petrol.
- **Goodall's road infrastructure** (interstate highways) made ICE long-distance travel possible.
- Result: EVs became a **niche, low-volume product** for decades.

### Era 4 — The Revival (1990 – 2010)
- **1990**: **California Air Resources Board (CARB)** issued the **Zero Emission Vehicle (ZEV) Mandate** — forcing automakers to build EVs.
- **1996**: **General Motors EV1** launched — the first modern mass-produced EV (lead-acid, then NiMH).
- **1997**: **Toyota Prius** launched in Japan — the world's first mass-produced **hybrid** (HEV).
- **2008**: **Tesla Roadster** launched — first highway-legal **lithium-ion** BEV.
- **2009**: **Nissan Leaf** concept unveiled; **Mahindra Reva** launched in India.

### Era 5 — The Mass-Market Revolution (2010 – Present)
- **2010**: **Nissan Leaf** and **Chevrolet Volt** enter mass production.
- **2012**: **Tesla Model S** launched —打破了 ICE range anxiety (480 km range).
- **2015**: **Paris Climate Agreement** signed → global EV push.
- **2018**: **India's NITI Aayog** releases EV policy.
- **2020+**: **Solid-state battery research**, **V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid)** technology, **800V architectures** (Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (History & Foundations)

Since this topic is qualitative, the "formulas" are **comparative performance metrics** and **physical constants** that frequently appear in KTU numerical/analytical questions.

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Value / Expression | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Energy stored in a battery | $E$ | $E = V \cdot Q = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | **Watt-hour (Wh)** |
| 2 | Battery Capacity | $C$ | $C = I \cdot t$ | **Ampere-hour (Ah)** |
| 3 | Energy Density (Volumetric) | $\rho_E$ | $\rho_E = E / V_{cell}$ | **Wh/L** |
| 4 | Energy Density (Gravimetric) | $\omega_E$ | $\omega_E = E / m_{cell}$ | **Wh/kg** |
| 5 | Vehicle range (ideal) | $R$ | $R = E_{bat} / E_{per\_km}$ | **km** |
| 6 | Energy per km | $E_{per\_km}$ | $E_{per\_km} = m \cdot g \cdot C_r + \frac{1}{2} \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | **Wh/km** |
| 7 | EV efficiency target | $\eta$ | $\eta \approx 0.85 - 0.95$ | **Dimensionless** (vs ICE $\eta \approx 0.25-0.40$) |
| 8 | First EV land speed record | $v_{1900}$ | $v = 105.88$ | **km/h** (La Jamais Contente, 1899) |
| 9 | Lead-acid energy density | $\omega_{Pb}$ | $\omega_{Pb} \approx 30 - 50$ | **Wh/kg** (Historical) |
| 10 | Li-ion energy density | $\omega_{Li}$ | $\omega_{Li} \approx 150 - 265$ | **Wh/kg** (Modern) |
| 11 | Cost decline of Li-ion | $C_{pack}$ | **−19% per year** (Learned curve) | **$/kWh** (BloombergNEF) |
| 12 | Faradays constant | $F$ | $F = 96485$ | **C/mol** (Electrochemistry) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick:** The number **96485 C/mol** (Faraday's constant) and the value **30–50 Wh/kg** for lead-acid frequently appear in derivation questions on battery basics. Memorize them.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Historical Shift

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Every historical transition in EV technology is driven by **one of three forces**:
> 1. **Energy density improvement** (lead-acid → NiMH → Li-ion)
> 2. **Cost reduction** (assembly line, economies of scale)
> 3. **Policy push** (CARB ZEV, Paris Agreement, FAME-II India)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to **link cause and effect** — for example, *"Why did the NiMH battery in the EV1 fail to scale?"* — Answer: Patent control by Ovonic (Chevron), cost, and limited energy density (~80 Wh/kg).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Faraday's Law to the "Range Equation" of an EV

This derivation connects the historical origin (Faraday's 1831 laws of electrolysis) to the modern "How far can an EV go on a single charge?" question. This is a **standard KTU 14-mark analytical question**.

**Step 1 — Start with Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis (1834):**

The mass of a substance deposited at an electrode is directly proportional to the charge passed.

$$m = Z \cdot Q$$

where:
- $m$ = mass deposited (kg)
- $Z$ = electrochemical equivalent (kg/C)
- $Q$ = total charge (C)

**Step 2 — Relate charge to current and time:**

$$Q = I \cdot t$$

where:
- $I$ = current in Amperes
- $t$ = time in seconds

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$m = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Express the energy stored in a battery using voltage:**

When a cell of voltage $V$ delivers charge $Q$, the electrical energy released is:

$$E = V \cdot Q = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — The "Capacity" formulation (KTU standard):**

Battery capacity $C$ (in **Ah**) is defined as:

$$C = I \cdot t$$

Therefore, total energy:

$$E = V \cdot C \quad \text{(units: V \cdot Ah = Wh)}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kiloWatt-hour (kWh) for automotive use:**

$$E_{kWh} = \frac{V \cdot C}{1000} \quad \text{[kWh]}$$

**Step 7 — The KTU "Vehicle Range Equation" (analytical matrix):**

The ideal range of an EV is given by:

$$R = \frac{E_{bat}}{E_{per\_km}}$$

where the energy consumed per kilometre is:

$$E_{per\_km} = F_{rolling} + F_{aerodynamic} + F_{acceleration}$$

In a steady-state cruise at constant speed $v$ (neglecting acceleration), this becomes:

$$E_{per\_km} = m \cdot g \cdot C_r + \frac{1}{2} \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$$

Substituting:

$$R = \frac{V \cdot C}{1000 \cdot \left( m \cdot g \cdot C_r + \frac{1}{2} \rho_{air} \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2 \right)}$$

**Step 8 — Numerical Application (KTU 14-mark sub-part):**

Assume a historical Nissan Leaf (2010): $V = 360$ V nominal, $C = 66$ Ah, $m = 1525$ kg, $C_r = 0.01$, $C_d = 0.29$, $A = 2.27$ m², $v = 80$ km/h = 22.22 m/s, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³.

$$E_{bat} = \frac{360 \cdot 66}{1000} = 23.76 \text{ kWh}$$

$$F_{roll} = 1525 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.01 = 149.6 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.29 \cdot 2.27 \cdot (22.22)^2 \approx 200.5 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{total} \approx 350.1 \text{ N}$$

$$E_{per\_km} = 350.1 \cdot \frac{1000}{1000} = 0.350 \text{ kWh/km}$$

$$R = \frac{23.76}{0.350} \approx 67.9 \text{ km}$$

(Note: Real-world Leaf range was ~117 km, the difference is regenerative braking and lower drag conditions.)

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Historical vs Modern EV Comparison (KTU PEAK Yield)

| Parameter | EV1 (1996) | Tesla Model S (2012) | Tata Nexon EV (2020) | Tesla Model 3 (2023) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Battery type** | Lead-acid → NiMH | Li-ion (NCA) | Li-ion (LFP) | Li-ion (NCA 4680) |
| **Energy (kWh)** | 16.5 | 85 | 30.2 | 60–82 |
| **Range (km)** | 120 | 480 | 312 | 510–700 |
| **Motor type** | Induction AC | AC induction | PMSM | PMSM |
| **Power (kW)** | 102 | 270 | 95 | 208–377 |
| **0–100 km/h (s)** | 9.0 | 4.2 | 9.4 | 3.1–6.1 |
| **Charging time** | 8 h (AC) | 1 h (Supercharger) | 8 h (AC) | 25 min (V3 Supercharger) |
| **Cost (USD)** | ~$33,000 (lease only) | $69,000 | $15,000 | $39,000 |
| **Energy density (Wh/kg)** | ~80 (NiMH) | ~200 | ~130 (LFP) | ~250 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** This matrix is **the single most important analytical tool** for any "Compare" or "Discuss evolution" question in Part B (14 marks). Students who draw this table in their answer script **always score 12+ marks**.

## 3.3 The "Lithium Revolution" — Why Li-ion Won (1991–2010)

Sony commercialized the **lithium-ion battery in 1991** (Nobel Prize 2019 to Goodenough, Whittingham, Yoshino). The "why" of its dominance over lead-acid can be derived from the **energy density equation**:

$$\omega_E = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{V \cdot C}{m}$$

- **Lead-acid** (1890s tech): $V = 2.0$ V, $\omega_E \approx 35$ Wh/kg
- **NiMH** (1990s): $V = 1.2$ V, $\omega_E \approx 80$ Wh/kg
- **Li-ion** (1991+): $V = 3.7$ V, $\omega_E \approx 200$ Wh/kg

Since **range is directly proportional to energy density** (from Section 3.1's range equation), a 5–6× improvement in $\omega_E$ translated directly into a **5–6× improvement in vehicle range** — the single most important historical transition in EV history.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Five-Era Timeline of Electric Vehicles

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Era1: 1832-1899\nDawn of EV\nRobert Anderson 1832\nLa Jamais Contente 1899\n105.88 kmph record"]
    B["Era2: 1900-1920\nGolden Age\nDetroit Electric\n28pct of US cars"]
    C["Era3: 1920-1990\nThe Decline\nModel T mass production\nKettering starter 1912"]
    D["Era4: 1990-2010\nThe Revival\nCARB ZEV Mandate\nGM EV1 1996\nToyota Prius 1997\nTesla Roadster 2008"]
    E["Era5: 2010-Present\nMass Market\nNissan Leaf 2010\nTesla Model S 2012\nParis Agreement 2015\n800V Architecture 2020"]

    A -- "1900: Lead-acid matured" --> B
    B -- "1920: ICE undercut EV cost" --> C
    C -- "1990: Pollution regulations" --> D
    D -- "2010: Li-ion cost crossover" --> E

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Block Architecture of a Modern BEV (vs Historical 1900s EV)

graph TB
    subgraph HISTORICAL["Historical EV: 1900s Detroit Electric"]
        H1["Lead-acid battery\n16 cells, 48V"]
