# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Pattern Note:** Part A questions are short, direct, and test definitional / conceptual recall. Each answer should be **3–4 sentences** with one supporting diagram or formula.

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]
**Define an EV motor drive. List the three main subsystems that constitute it.**

**Model Answer:**
An EV motor drive is the integrated system that converts electrical energy from the battery into controlled mechanical motion at the wheels of an electric vehicle. The three main subsystems are:
1. **The Electric Machine** — motor/generator (PMSM, IM, or BLDC in most modern EVs).
2. **The Power Electronic Converter** — typically a 3-phase Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) using IGBTs or SiC MOSFETs.
3. **The Digital Controller** — the VCU and MCU that run algorithms such as Field-Oriented Control (FOC) or Direct Torque Control (DTC).

Together, these three elements regulate the torque, speed, and power flow of the vehicle in all four operating quadrants.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]
**Why is regenerative braking a defining feature of EV motor drives, and how is it achieved?**

**Model Answer:**
Regenerative braking is the process of converting the vehicle's kinetic energy during deceleration into electrical energy that is fed back into the battery, thereby extending the driving range.

It is achieved because the EV motor drive operates in **four quadrants** — the same motor and inverter hardware can be commanded to act as a **generator**. When the driver lifts off the accelerator, the MCU commands a **negative torque** in the motor. The spinning wheels drive the motor, the motor produces a back-EMF higher than the battery voltage, and the inverter's diodes/IGBTs channel the resulting current back into the battery. This is the fundamental difference from a conventional ICE vehicle, which dissipates braking energy as heat.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has **two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each**. You are expected to write **approximately 7–8 pages** for a 14-mark answer. Diagrams are **mandatory** — they carry **2–3 marks** by themselves.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an EV motor drive system. Identify each block and state its function.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the four major types of electric motors used in electric vehicles (Brushed DC, Induction Motor, PMSM, BLDC) on the basis of efficiency, power density, cost, control complexity, and typical EV applications. State, with justification, which motor type is most preferred for a modern mid-size passenger EV.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

**Introduction:** The EV motor drive system is the integrated electromechanical assembly that delivers controlled torque to the wheels of an electric vehicle. It converts stored DC electrical energy from the battery into variable-frequency 3-phase AC (or chopped DC) that the motor uses to produce mechanical rotation.

**Block Diagram (Must Be Drawn):**

```
[Driver Pedal] → [VCU] → [MCU] → [Inverter] → [3-Phase Motor] → [Gearbox] → [Wheels]
                       ↑         ↑              ↑                                    ↑
                    [Battery]  [Position    [DC Link                              [Regen
                                Sensor]      Capacitor]                          Feedback]
```

**Function of Each Block:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **Driver Pedal** | Sends analog/digital torque request (0–5 V or CAN message) to VCU |
| **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** | Top-level controller. Arbitrates driver's request, battery SOC, drive mode, and safety limits |
| **MCU (Motor Control Unit)** | Runs FOC/DTC algorithm. Generates PWM gate signals for inverter switches |
| **Inverter** | Converts DC battery voltage into variable-voltage, variable-frequency 3-phase AC |
| **DC Link Capacitor** | Smooths battery voltage ripple, absorbs regenerative energy |
| **3-Phase Motor** | Converts electrical energy to mechanical torque (PMSM, IM, or BLDC) |
| **Position Sensor** | Provides rotor angle feedback to MCU (essential for FOC) |
| **Gearbox** | Single-speed reducer that amplifies torque to wheels |
| **Wheels** | Contact patch with road; vehicle propulsion |
| **Battery** | Energy source; supplies DC to inverter |
| **Regen Feedback Path** | During braking, energy flows from wheels back to battery |

**Conclusion:** The architecture enables precise control of torque and speed, four-quadrant operation, and regenerative braking, making it functionally superior to ICE powertrains for traction applications.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Comparison Table (Must Be Drawn):**

| Parameter | Brushed DC | Induction Motor (IM) | PMSM | BLDC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Efficiency** | 70–80% (lowest) | 85–92% | **95–97%** (highest) | 90–93% |
| **Power Density** | Low | Medium | **Very High** | High |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | High (rare-earth magnets) | Medium |
| **Control Complexity** | Very Simple (chopper) | High (vector control) | High (FOC) | Medium (6-step) |
| **Torque Ripple** | Moderate | Low | **Low** | High |
| **Maintenance** | High (brushes) | **Very Low** | Low | Low |
| **EV Applications** | Vintage EVs, e-rickshaws (old) | Tesla S/X/3, Tigor EV | **Nissan Leaf, Kona, MG ZS, Nexon** | Ather, Ola, TVS iQube |

**Most Preferred Motor for Modern Mid-Size Passenger EV:**

The **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** is the most preferred choice for the following reasons:

1. **Highest efficiency** (95–97%) over the entire operating map → directly translates to **longer driving range**.
2. **Highest power and torque density** → lighter and more compact motor, freeing up cabin and boot space.
3. **Silent operation** and low torque ripple → superior NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness).
4. **Excellent constant-power speed range** → enables single-speed gearbox, no need for transmission shifts.
5. **Field weakening capability** → allows operation at 2–3× base speed for high-speed cruising.

**Drawback Acknowledged:** PMSM uses rare-earth magnets (NdFeB) which are expensive and subject to supply chain concerns (China dominates supply). This is why some manufacturers like Tesla use **PMSM + IM dual-motor** configurations to balance cost, supply, and performance.

**Conclusion:** PMSM is the gold standard for modern mid-size passenger EVs, with a clear efficiency and performance advantage over the alternatives.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of four-quadrant operation of an EV motor drive. With suitable torque-speed diagrams, describe the four modes of operation and explain how regenerative braking is achieved.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1500 kg electric vehicle is to be driven at a constant speed of 90 km/h on a flat road. The coefficient of rolling resistance is 0.012, the aerodynamic drag coefficient is 0.30, the frontal area is 2.2 m², the wheel radius is 0.32 m, the total gear ratio is 8.5, and the transmission efficiency is 0.94. Calculate: (i) the tractive force required, (ii) the torque required at the motor shaft, and (iii) the motor power required.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

**Concept of Four-Quadrant Operation:**

EV motor drives must operate the motor in four distinct modes depending on the direction of vehicle motion and the direction of torque. This is called **four-quadrant (4Q) operation** and is essential because the vehicle must move forward, reverse, accelerate, and decelerate — all using the same motor.

**The Four Quadrants:**

| Quadrant | Speed Direction | Torque Direction | Mode | Vehicle Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Q1 — Forward Motoring** | Forward (+) | Forward (+) | **Power = +ve** (Motor draws energy) | Accelerating / Cruising forward |
| **Q2 — Forward Braking** | Forward (+) | Reverse (−) | **Power = −ve** (Motor generates energy) | Decelerating / Regen braking while moving forward |
| **Q3 — Reverse Motoring** | Reverse (−) | Reverse (−) | **Power = +ve** | Accelerating / Moving backward |
| **Q4 — Reverse Braking** | Reverse (−) | Forward (+) | **Power = −ve** | Decelerating while moving backward |

**Torque-Speed Diagram (Must Be Drawn):**

```
            Torque (T)
                 ↑
                 │   Q2 │   Q1
        (Regen)  │      │ (Motoring)
                 │      │
   ──────────────┼──────┼──────────────→ Speed (N)
                 │      │
        (Regen)  │      │ (Motoring)
                 │   Q3 │   Q4
                 ↓
```

**How Regenerative Braking is Achieved:**

When the driver lifts the accelerator pedal, the VCU commands the MCU to apply a **negative torque reference** (instead of the positive torque used for acceleration). The motor's rotor is still spinning (car is moving forward), so the motor now acts as a **generator** due to Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.

**Step-by-step regen process:**

1. Wheels are still rotating forward (kinetic energy of vehicle).
2. Motor acts as generator — back-EMF $E_b$ rises above battery voltage $V_{batt}$.
3. The inverter's diodes become forward-biased, and current flows from the motor back into the battery.
4. This current creates a **counter-torque** that decelerates the vehicle.
5. The kinetic energy is converted to electrical energy and stored in the battery.

**Limitation:** Regenerative braking alone cannot provide emergency-stop braking force. Hence EVs use a **blended braking system** — regen up to ~0.3 g deceleration, with **hydraulic friction brakes** taking over for harder stops.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Vehicle mass $m = 1500$ kg
- Constant speed $V = 90$ km/h $= \dfrac{90 \times 1000}{3600} = 25$ m/s
- Rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr} = 0.012$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$
- Frontal area $A = 2.2$ m²
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- Wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m
- Total gear ratio $G = 8.5$
- Transmission efficiency $\eta_t = 0.94$
- Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s²

---

**(i) Tractive Force Required**

**Step 1 — Rolling Resistance Force:**

$$F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$$
$$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81$$
$$F_{rr} = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic Drag Force:**

$$F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2$$
$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (25)^2$$
$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 625$$
$$F_{aero} = 252.66 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Total Tractive Force (no gradient, constant speed):**

$$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{aero}$$
$$F_t = 176.58 + 252.66$$
$$\boxed{F_t = 429.24 \text{ N}}$$

---

**(ii) Torque Required at the Motor Shaft**

Using the relationship between tractive force and motor torque:

$$T_{motor} = \frac{F_t \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_t}$$
$$T_{motor} = \frac{429.24 \times 0.32}{8.5 \times 0.94}$$
$$T_{motor} = \frac{137.36}{7.99}$$
$$\boxed{T_{motor} \approx 17.19 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Verification:** This is a very low torque — makes sense because the car is cruising at constant speed (no acceleration), and the gear ratio is high (8.5), so the motor torque is small. The same motor delivering this torque is enough to overcome drag and rolling resistance.

---

**(iii) Motor Power Required**

**Step 1 — Power at the Wheels:**

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot V$$
$$P_{wheel} = 429.24 \times 25$$
$$P_{wheel} = 10{,}731 \text{ W} = 10.73 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Motor Power (after transmission losses):**

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_t}$$
$$P_{motor} = \frac{10.73}{0.94}$$
$$\boxed{P_{motor} \approx 11.42 \text{ kW}}$$

**Alternative verification using torque-speed:**
- Wheel angular speed: $\omega_w = \dfrac{V}{r_w} = \dfrac{25}{0.32} = 78.13$ rad/s
- Motor angular speed: $\omega_m = G \cdot \omega_w = 8.5 \times 78.13 = 664.08$ rad/s
- Motor power: $P_{motor} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_m = 17.19 \times 664.08 = 11{,}415$ W ≈ **11.42 kW** ✓

**Conclusion:** A continuous motor power of about **11.4 kW** is sufficient to cruise this vehicle at 90 km/h on a flat road. The peak power for 0–100 km/h acceleration would be 5–10× this value (typically 80–150 kW in real EVs).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Mistake (–2 marks):** Converting 90 km/h to 25 m/s is **mandatory**. If you plug in 90 directly into $F_t \cdot V$, the answer will be 50× too large.
> 2. **Forgetting Transmission Efficiency (–1 mark):** The factor $\eta_t = 0.94$ must appear in the motor torque and power formulas. KTU examiners explicitly check for this.
> 3. **Missing the Block Diagram in 7-mark sub-questions (–2 to –3 marks):** A neat, labeled block diagram is **non-negotiable** for 7-mark theory questions. Without it, you cap at 5/7.
> 4. **Wrong Direction of Energy in 4Q Explanation (–1 mark):** Many students write "current flows from battery to motor in all quadrants." In Q2 and Q4, the current flows **back** to the battery. This is the essence of regen.
> 5. **Forgetting the Gearing Ratio in Tractive Force (–1 mark):** The tractive force at the wheel is **not** the same as the motor torque. The gear ratio amplifies torque. Use $T_{wheel} = G \cdot \eta_t \cdot T_{motor}$ to convert.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as your last-minute revision sheet the night before the exam.**

### ✅ Core Definitions
- **EV Motor Drive** = Motor + Power Electronic Converter + Digital Controller
- **Four-Quadrant Operation** = Forward/Reverse × Motoring/Braking
- **Regenerative Braking** = Converting kinetic energy to electrical energy during deceleration
- **Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** = Standard control technique for PMSM and IM in EVs
- **CPSR (Constant Power Speed Range)** = The speed range over which motor delivers constant power (typically 3:1 to 5:1)

### ✅ Key Numbers to Remember
- Standard EV battery voltage: **400 V** (modern 800 V architectures emerging)
- PMSM efficiency: **95–97%** (highest among EV motors)
- Induction motor efficiency: **85–92%**
- BLDC used in: **Two-wheelers, e-rickshaws, small EVs**
- PMSM used in: **Mid-size to luxury passenger EVs**
- Induction motor used in: **Tesla vehicles**

### ✅ Master Formulas (Top 5 Most Tested)
1. $P = T \cdot \omega$ (power from torque and speed)
2. $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ (synchronous motor speed)
3. $V = \omega \cdot r_w$ (linear velocity from wheel speed)
4. $F_t = \dfrac{T_m \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r_w}$ (tractive force from motor torque)
5. $P_{motor} = \dfrac{F_t \cdot V}{\eta_t}$ (motor power to overcome tractive force)

### ✅ Most Important Real-World Examples
- **Nissan Leaf** → PMSM
- **Tesla Model 3** → PMSM + IM (dual motor)
- **Ather 450X** → BLDC
- **Tata Nexon EV** → PMSM
- **Hyundai Kona EV** → PMSM

### ✅ Frequently Asked KTU Question Types
1. *Define EV motor drive and list its components* (3 marks – Part A)
2. *Compare PMSM and Induction Motor* (7 marks – Part B)
3. *Explain four-quadrant operation with diagram* (7 marks – Part B)
4. *Calculate motor power for a given vehicle* (7 marks – Part B – numerical)
5. *Why are brushed DC motors not used in modern EVs?* (3 marks – Part A)
6. *Explain regenerative braking with a block diagram* (7 marks – Part B)
7. *Draw and explain the EV motor drive block diagram* (7 marks – Part B)

### ✅ Common Confusion to Avoid
- **Tractive Force ≠ Motor Torque.** They are related via the gear ratio and wheel radius.
- **PMSM ≠ BLDC** in strict terminology, though both use permanent magnets. PMSM has sinusoidal back-EMF; BLDC has trapezoidal.
- **Inverter ≠ Converter.** Inverter converts DC→AC. Converter (DC-DC) converts one DC level to another.
- **DC Link Capacitor ≠ Battery.** The DC link capacitor is for high-frequency ripple filtering; the battery is the energy source.
- **Field Weakening is NOT "weakening" the motor** in a negative sense — it is a controlled technique to extend the constant-power speed range by reducing the d-axis flux component.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Motor Drive Technology**
> You are now prepared for all Module 1 questions on motor drives. Proceed to Module 2 (Power Electronics & Converters) for the deep-dive into inverter design and control.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Source Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Source Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Energy Source Technology" in EVs?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Energy Source Technology** in the context of Electric Vehicles (EVs) refers to the complete study of on-board energy storage and generation systems that supply electrical energy to the traction motor. It encompasses the **electrochemical**, **electrical**, and **thermal** characteristics of batteries, fuel cells, supercapacitors, and hybrid energy storage systems, along with their **charging methodologies**, **state estimation algorithms**, and **management electronics (BMS)**.

In simple words, the "Energy Source" is the **fuel tank of an electric car**. Just as an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle stores energy in petrol/diesel, an EV stores it in **batteries, supercapacitors, or hydrogen fuel cells**. The "Technology" part means understanding *how* these devices store energy, *how much* they can store, *how fast* they can give it to the motor, *how long* they last, and *how safely* they can be recharged.

---

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of EV Energy Source Technology

Think of EV energy storage like three friends working together on a road trip:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars:**
> 1. **🔋 Energy Density Devices (Batteries)** – The "long-haul trucker". Stores lots of energy for long driving range, but is slow to fill up (charge). *Example: Li-ion battery.*
> 2. **⚡ Power Density Devices (Supercapacitors / Ultracapacitors)** – The "sprint athlete". Releases energy extremely fast for acceleration/regenerative braking, but cannot store much. *Example: EDLC supercapacitor.*
> 3. **⛽ Fuel-Based Devices (Fuel Cells / ICE-hybrid gensets)** – The "endless marathon runner". As long as fuel is supplied, it keeps generating electricity. *Example: PEMFC (Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell).*

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket" Model

Imagine the EV's energy source as a **water bucket system**:

| EV Energy Parameter | Water Bucket Analogy | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Capacity** | Size of the bucket (how much water it holds) | **kWh** |
| **Power Rating** | Width of the pipe (how fast water flows out) | **kW** |
| **State of Charge (SOC)** | Current water level (%) | **%** |
| **Depth of Discharge (DOD)** | How much water you emptied | **%** |
| **C-Rate** | Time to empty/fill the bucket | **1/h** |
| **Cycle Life** | Number of times you can fill & empty | **cycles** |
| **Internal Resistance** | Friction inside the pipe | **Ω (Ohm)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A *large bucket* (high energy density) gives long range, but a *wide pipe* (high power density) gives fast acceleration. Modern EVs use a **hybrid source** (battery + supercapacitor) to get **both** benefits — just like carrying a big water tank *and* a high-pressure nozzle.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Weightage Area

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEEET522)**, Module 1 lays the foundation for understanding *why* EVs behave the way they do. Every later module (motor drives, chargers, vehicle dynamics) depends on knowing:

- The **voltage and current characteristics** of the source
- The **energy limits** that determine range
- The **thermal behavior** that determines safety

Examiners frequently (≈ 30–40% marks) test:
- Definitions of **SOC, DOD, C-rate, specific energy, energy density**
- Comparison between **battery chemistries**
- Working principle of **Li-ion / fuel cell**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Battery — The Heart of an EV

A **battery** is an electrochemical device that converts stored chemical energy into electrical energy via **redox (reduction–oxidation) reactions**. It consists of one or more **electrochemical cells**, each having:

- **Anode (−):** Oxidation occurs; releases electrons
- **Cathode (+):** Reduction occurs; accepts electrons
- **Electrolyte:** Medium for ion transport (liquid, gel, or solid)
- **Separator:** Prevents physical contact between anode & cathode

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Stock Notation:**
> A cell is written as: **Anode | Electrolyte | Cathode**
> Example: **Li | LiPF₆ in EC:DMC | LiCoO₂**

---

## 2.2 Critical Battery Parameters (KTU Frequently Asked)

### 2.2.1 Voltage Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Value (Li-ion) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Voltage** | $V_{nom}$ | Average working voltage per cell | **3.6 V – 3.7 V** |
| **Open Circuit Voltage** | $V_{oc}$ | Voltage when no load is connected | **3.0 V – 4.2 V** |
| **Cut-off Voltage** | $V_{cut}$ | Min voltage allowed during discharge | **≈ 2.8 V** |
| **Maximum Charging Voltage** | $V_{max}$ | Max voltage allowed during charge | **4.2 V** |

### 2.2.2 Capacity & Energy Parameters

- **Capacity (C):** Total charge a battery can deliver, measured in **Ampere-hour (Ah)**.
- **Specific Energy:** Energy per unit mass → **Wh/kg** (determines vehicle range).
- **Energy Density:** Energy per unit volume → **Wh/L** (determines packaging).
- **Specific Power:** Power per unit mass → **W/kg** (determines acceleration).

### 2.2.3 State & Aging Parameters

- **State of Charge (SOC):** The percentage of remaining capacity relative to full charge.

$$SOC = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$$

- **Depth of Discharge (DOD):** The percentage of capacity that has been discharged.

$$DOD = 100\% - SOC$$

- **State of Health (SOH):** Current maximum capacity relative to original rated capacity.

$$SOH = \frac{Q_{max,current}}{Q_{max,new}} \times 100\%$$

### 2.2.4 C-Rate — The Speed of Charge/Discharge

> [!NOTE]
> **C-Rate Definition:**
> A **1C rate** means the battery is fully discharged in **1 hour**.
> A **2C rate** means full discharge in **30 minutes**.
> A **0.5C rate** means full discharge in **2 hours**.

Mathematically:

$$I = C_{rated} \times C\text{-}rate$$

If a battery is rated **50 Ah** and discharged at **1C**:

$$I = 50 \times 1 = 50\ A$$

### 2.2.5 Internal Resistance & Efficiency

Every battery has a small **internal resistance $r$** that causes **I²r losses** (heat).

$$V_{terminal} = V_{oc} - I \cdot r$$

Energy efficiency of a charge–discharge cycle:

$$\eta = \frac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} \times 100\%$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = V \times Q$ | Stored electrical energy (Joules) | Energy content |
| 2 | $E_{Wh} = V_{nom} \times Q_{Ah}$ | Energy in Watt-hours | Pack sizing |
| 3 | $SOC = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$ | State of charge (%) | BMS logic |
| 4 | $DOD = 100 - SOC$ | Depth of discharge | Cycle life estimation |
| 5 | $I = C_{rated} \times C\text{-rate}$ | Current from C-rate | Charging design |
| 6 | $P_{peak} = V \times I_{max}$ | Peak power capability | Motor sizing |
| 7 | $SOH = \dfrac{C_{now}}{C_{new}} \times 100$ | Battery aging | Warranty tests |
| 8 | $V_t = V_{oc} - I r$ | Terminal voltage under load | Equivalent circuit |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ | Round-trip efficiency | Loss analysis |
| 10 | $R_{range} = \dfrac{E_{battery}}{E_{per\_km}}$ | Vehicle range (km) | Vehicle design |

---

## 2.4 Comparison of Battery Chemistries (KTU Favorite Table)

| Parameter | Lead-Acid | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) | LiFePO₄ (LFP) | Solid-State Li |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Voltage (V/cell)** | 2.0 | 1.2 | 3.6 – 3.7 | 3.2 | 3.6 – 3.8 |
| **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** | 30 – 50 | 60 – 120 | 150 – 250 | 90 – 160 | 250 – 400 |
| **Cycle Life** | 300 – 500 | 500 – 1000 | 1000 – 3000 | 2000 – 5000 | > 5000 (projected) |
| **Energy Efficiency (%)** | 70 – 80 | 65 – 75 | 85 – 95 | 90 – 95 | > 95 |
| **Cost (USD/kWh)** | 100 – 150 | 250 – 350 | 120 – 200 | 90 – 150 | High (R&D) |
| **Safety** | Moderate (acid) | Moderate | Low (thermal runaway) | High (stable) | High |
| **EV Use** | Older e-rickshaws | Toyota Prius (1st gen) | Tesla, modern EVs | BYD, e-buses | Future EVs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Tip:** "**LFP** = **L**ong life, **F**ire-safe, **P**oor energy density" (used in entry-level EVs). "**NMC** = **N**ewer, **M**ore energy, **C**ommonly used" (used in premium EVs).

---

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Vehicle Range Anxiety** is solved by increasing **specific energy** (Wh/kg) — that is why Tesla switched from NCA to NMC 811 chemistries.
- **Fast Charging** requires low internal resistance — that is why modern cells use **silicon-graphite anodes**.
- **Battery Fires** are a real safety concern — that is why **BMS (Battery Management System)** is mandatory and why **LFP** is gaining market share.
- **Second-Life Batteries** (e.g., used in Nissan Leaf packs for home solar storage) depend on **SOH** measurement.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Battery Pack

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> An EV uses a battery pack with the following specifications:
> - Nominal pack voltage: **360 V**
> - Rated capacity: **75 Ah**
> Calculate:
> (a) The total energy stored in **kWh**
> (b) If the vehicle consumes **150 Wh/km**, calculate the theoretical range
> (c) If the driver discharges at a constant **0.5C rate**, find the discharge current and total discharge time

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Write down the energy formula**

The electrical energy stored is given by:

$$E = V_{nom} \times Q_{rated}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values**

$$E = 360\ V \times 75\ Ah$$

$$E = 27{,}000\ Wh$$

**Step 3: Convert to kWh**

$$E = \frac{27{,}000}{1000} = 27\ kWh$$

✅ **Answer (a):** The battery stores **27 kWh** of energy.

---

**Step 4: Use the range formula**

The theoretical driving range is given by:

$$R_{range} = \frac{E_{battery}}{E_{per\_km}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute values**

$$R_{range} = \frac{27{,}000\ Wh}{150\ Wh/km}$$

$$R_{range} = 180\ km$$

✅ **Answer (b):** The theoretical range is **180 km**.

---

**Step 6: Find the discharge current at 0.5C**

Using the C-rate relation:

$$I = C_{rated} \times C\text{-}rate$$

**Step 7: Substitute values**

$$I = 75\ Ah \times 0.5$$

$$I = 37.5\ A$$

**Step 8: Find the discharge time**

$$t = \frac{Q_{rated}}{I} = \frac{75\ Ah}{37.5\ A} = 2\ h$$

✅ **Answer (c):** Discharge current = **37.5 A**, total time = **2 hours**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Battery Pack Configuration (Series-Parallel)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Design a battery pack with the following target:
> - Total voltage: **360 V**
> - Total capacity: **75 Ah**
> - Each individual cell: **3.6 V nominal, 2.5 Ah**
> Find the number of cells in **series (Ns)** and **parallel (Np)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Cells in series raise voltage**

$$N_s = \frac{V_{pack}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{360}{3.6} = 100\ cells$$

**Step 2: Cells in parallel raise capacity**

$$N_p = \frac{Q_{pack}}{Q_{cell}} = \frac{75}{2.5} = 30\ cells$$

**Step 3: Total number of cells**

$$N_{total} = N_s \times N_p = 100 \times 30 = 3000\ cells$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Exam:** A real Tesla Model S 85 kWh pack contains ~7104 individual **18650 cells** arranged as **74 parallel groups × 96 series cells** ≈ 7104 cells. This is why EV batteries are engineering marvels.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Charging Methods (CC, CV, CC-CV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Theory:**
> The three dominant charging protocols used in EV chargers and mobile phones alike are:

| Method | Full Name | How it Works | Where Used | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CC** | Constant Current | Charger delivers fixed current; voltage rises slowly | Initial bulk charging | Risk of over-voltage at end |
| **CV** | Constant Voltage | Charger holds fixed voltage; current tapers down | Final saturation phase | Very slow at start |
| **CC-CV** | Constant Current → Constant Voltage | First CC, then switches to CV at $V_{max}$ | **All Li-ion EVs** ✅ | Needs precise BMS |

**The CC-CV charging curve is the KTU most-tested charging profile:**

$$
I(t) = 
\begin{cases}
I_{max} & \text{for } V < V_{max} \quad (CC\ phase) \\
I_{max} \cdot e^{-t/\tau} & \text{for } V = V_{max} \quad (CV\ phase)
\end{cases}
$$

The transition happens when terminal voltage reaches **$V_{max} = 4.2$ V per cell** for conventional Li-ion.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Exercise: SOC Estimation via Coulomb Counting

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A 100 Ah battery is fully charged. The current profile is:
> - Discharge at 20 A for 2 hours
> - Rest for 1 hour (negligible current)
> - Charge at 10 A for 1.5 hours
> Calculate the final SOC using Coulomb counting.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Initialize SOC**

$$SOC_0 = 100\%$$

**Step 2: Apply Coulomb counting equation**

$$SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau)\ d\tau$$

where **discharge current is positive** and **charge current is negative** (sign convention varies — KTU accepts either, as long as you state it).

**Step 3: Compute charge transferred in each phase**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= I_1 \cdot t_1 = 20 \times 2 = +40\ Ah \quad (\text{discharged}) \\[4pt]
Q_2 &= 0\ Ah \quad (\text{rest}) \\[4pt]
Q_3 &= -10 \times 1.5 = -15\ Ah \quad (\text{charged back})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Net charge transferred**

$$Q_{net} = 40 - 0 - 15 = +25\ Ah \text{ (net discharged)}$$

**Step 5: Calculate final SOC**

$$SOC_{final} = SOC_0 - \frac{Q_{net}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$$

$$SOC_{final} = 100\% - \frac{25}{100} \times 100\% = 75\%$$

✅ **Final Answer:** **SOC = 75%**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Exercise: Power, Energy, and Range Triangle

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> An EV has the following data:
> - Battery: 60 kWh, 400 V nominal
> - Curb weight: 1500 kg
> - Average energy consumption: 0.15 kWh/km
> - Peak motor power: 150 kW
> Find: (a) Battery capacity in Ah (b) Specific energy assuming 350 kg pack mass (c) Maximum discharge current at 1C

### Solution

**Part (a): Capacity in Ah**

$$Q = \frac{E}{V} = \frac{60{,}000\ Wh}{400\ V} = 150\ Ah$$

**Part (b): Specific energy**

$$\text{Specific Energy} = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{60{,}000\ Wh}{350\ kg} \approx 171.4\ Wh/kg$$

**Part (c): Max discharge current at 1C**

$$I_{max} = 1C \times Q = 150\ A$$

For peak motor demand of 150 kW at 400 V:

$$I_{peak} = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{150{,}000}{400} = 375\ A$$

So peak power actually requires a **2.5C discharge**, which means cells must support high C-rates.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: EV Energy Source Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Grid or Solar Input] --> nodeB[On-board Charger]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Battery Management System BMS]
    nodeC --> nodeD[DC Bus]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Fuel Cell Stack]
    nodeE --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeF[Power Inverter]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Supercapacitor Bank]
    nodeG --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH[Traction Motor]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Wheels and Transmission]
    nodeJ[Regenerative Braking] --> nodeG
    nodeJ --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeK[Temperature Sensors]
    nodeC --> nodeL[Cell Voltage Monitors]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **Grid/Solar** charges the battery through the **On-board Charger (OBC)**.
> - The **BMS** sits like a "brain" between battery and DC bus, protecting it from over-voltage, over-current, over-temperature.
> - The **Supercapacitor Bank** captures energy from **Regenerative Braking** and supplies burst power to the motor.
> - The **Fuel Cell Stack** is an *optional* hydrogen-based energy source for FCEVs (Fuel Cell EVs).
> - The **DC Bus** is the common electrical highway that feeds the **Power Inverter**, which drives the **Traction Motor**.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Inside a Single Li-ion Cell

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Anode: Graphite with Lithium] --> nodeB[Separator: Porous Polymer Film]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Cathode: Lithium Metal Oxide e.g. NMC]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Electrolyte: LiPF6 in Carbonate Solvent]
    nodeD --> nodeA
    nodeE[Current Collector: Copper Foil] --- nodeA
    nodeF[Current Collector: Aluminum Foil] --- nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeG[Negative Terminal]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Positive Terminal]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight — How the cell works:**
> - During **discharge**, Li⁺ ions move from **anode → cathode** through the electrolyte; electrons flow through the external circuit (motor).
> - During **charge**, an external power source forces Li⁺ ions back from cathode → anode.
> - The **separator** prevents direct electron flow inside the cell (which would cause a short circuit and fire).

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: CC-CV Charging Profile

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Connect Charger] --> nodeB[Battery SOC less than 80 percent]
    nodeB --> nodeC[CC Phase: Constant Current I max]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Cell Voltage reaches 4.2 V?}
    nodeD -->|No| nodeC
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeE[CV Phase: Constant Voltage 4.2 V]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Current drops below 0.05 C?}
    nodeF -->|No| nodeE
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[Charging Complete SOC equals 100 percent]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Charger Disconnects]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CC-CV?**
> - The **CC phase** rapidly fills ~70–80% of the battery in the first 30–60 minutes.
> - The **CV phase** slowly saturates the remaining 20–30% over 30–60 more minutes to avoid **lithium plating** at the anode, which permanently damages the cell.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Comparison of Energy Sources

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[EV Energy Sources] --> nodeB[Electrochemical Batteries]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Supercapacitors]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Fuel Cells]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Lead Acid]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Nickel Metal Hydride NiMH]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Lithium Ion Li ion]
    nodeG --> nodeH[NMC]
    nodeG --> nodeI[LFP]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[NCA]
    nodeG --> nodeK[Solid State]
    nodeD --> nodeL[PEMFC Proton Exchange Membrane]
    nodeD --> nodeM[SOFC Solid Oxide]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip — Draw this in your exam:** A simple **tree diagram** showing the hierarchy of EV energy sources is worth **2–3 easy marks** and demonstrates structured thinking. Most students skip diagrams and lose easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** Define the terms *State of Charge (SOC)*, *Depth of Discharge (DOD)*, and *C-rate* as applied to EV batteries. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **State of Charge (SOC):** It is the ratio of the remaining capacity in a battery to its rated (full-charge) capacity, expressed as a percentage.
> $$SOC = \frac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$$

> **Depth of Discharge (DOD):** It is the percentage of the rated capacity that has been discharged from the battery. It is the complement of SOC: $DOD = 100\% - SOC$.

> **C-Rate:** It is a measure of the rate at which a battery is charged or discharged relative to its maximum capacity. A 1C rate means the battery will be fully discharged in one hour. If a 50 Ah battery is discharged at 1C, the current is 50 A.

**(Marks distribution: 1 mark each definition + formula)**

---

### **Question 2:** Compare Lead-acid, NiMH, and Li-ion batteries with respect to **specific energy**, **cycle life**, and **cost**. Mention one EV application of each. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Lead-Acid | NiMH | Li-ion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy | 30–50 Wh/kg | 60–120 Wh/kg | 150–250 Wh/kg |
| Cycle Life | 300–500 | 500–1000 | 1000–3000 |
| Cost | Low | Moderate | High (but dropping) |
| **EV Application** | Electric rickshaws, low-speed EVs | Toyota Prius (1st gen) | Tesla, modern BEVs |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Li-ion dominates modern EVs because of its **highest specific energy** and **longest cycle life** despite higher upfront cost.

---

## 📚 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **construction and working principle of a Lithium-ion battery cell**. Discuss the electrode and electrolyte materials used. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Li-ion battery pack has **96 cells in series**, each of nominal voltage **3.6 V** and capacity **3.5 Ah**. Calculate:
>   (i) Total pack voltage
>   (ii) Total pack energy in Wh and kWh
>   (iii) Total pack energy in MJ
>   (iv) Specific energy if the pack mass is **25 kg**
>   (v) If the EV consumes **120 Wh/km**, find the theoretical range. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction & Working of Li-ion Cell (7 Marks):**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Anode (Negative Electrode):** Typically **graphite** coated on a **copper current collector**. Lithium ions are intercalated (stored) in the graphite layers during charging.
- **Cathode (Positive Electrode):** A **lithium metal oxide** such as LiCoO₂ (LCO), LiNiₓMnᵧCozO₂ (NMC), or LiFePO₄ (LFP), coated on an **aluminum current collector**.
- **Separator:** A thin porous **polyethylene (PE) or polypropylene (PP)** film that allows Li⁺ ions to pass but blocks electrons to prevent internal short circuit.
- **Electrolyte:** A **lithium salt (e.g., LiPF₆)** dissolved in an **organic carbonate solvent** (e.g., ethylene carbonate + dimethyl carbonate). It acts as the ion-transport medium.
- **Cell Housing:** Metallic can (cylindrical 18650/21700) or laminated aluminum pouch (prismatic).

**Working Principle — Discharge (2 marks):**
During discharge, the **anode undergoes oxidation** and releases Li⁺ ions and electrons:
$$\text{Li}_x\text{C}_6 \rightarrow x\text{Li}^+ + x\text{e}^- + 6\text{C}$$

The Li⁺ ions travel through the electrolyte to the cathode, while electrons flow through the external circuit (the motor). At the cathode:
$$\text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 + x\text{Li}^+ + x\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{LiCoO}_2$$

**Working Principle — Charge (2 marks):**
During charging, an external power source (OBC) drives the reaction in reverse, forcing Li⁺ ions back to the anode where they are intercalated into graphite.

**Diagram (Refer to Section 4.2 above for the block diagram; a labeled sketch of cylindrical 18650 cell is expected.)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Total pack voltage**

$$V_{pack} = N_s \times V_{cell} = 96 \times 3.6\ V = 345.6\ V$$

✅ **$V_{pack} = 345.6\ V$** (1 mark)

**(ii) Total pack energy in Wh**

$$E_{Wh} = V_{pack} \times Q = 345.6\ V \times 3.5\ Ah = 1209.6\ Wh$$

Converting to kWh:

$$E_{kWh} = \frac{1209.6}{1000} = 1.2096\ kWh$$

✅ **E ≈ 1209.6 Wh ≈ 1.21 kWh** (1.5 marks)

**(iii) Total pack energy in MJ**

Using conversion $1\ Wh = 3600\ J$:

$$E_{MJ} = 1209.6 \times 3600 = 4{,}354{,}560\ J \approx 4.35\ MJ$$

✅ **E ≈ 4.35 MJ** (1.5 marks)

**(iv) Specific energy**

$$\text{Specific Energy} = \frac{E_{Wh}}{m_{pack}} = \frac{1209.6\ Wh}{25\ kg} = 48.4\ Wh/kg$$

✅ **Specific energy ≈ 48.4 Wh/kg** (1.5 marks)

> *Note: This is low because a small lab-scale pack; real Tesla/NMC packs achieve 150–250 Wh/kg.*

**(v) Theoretical range**

$$R = \frac{E_{battery}}{E_{per\_km}} = \frac{1209.6\ Wh}{120\ Wh/km} = 10.08\ km$$

✅ **Range ≈ 10.08 km** (1.5 marks)

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

> **(a)** Explain the **working principle of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)**. How is it different from a battery? State two advantages and two challenges of using fuel cells in EVs. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** An EV uses a **hybrid energy storage system** consisting of a Li-ion battery and a supercapacitor bank. The Li-ion pack provides **50 kWh** at a steady average load, while the supercapacitor handles **peak power demands of 200 kW for 15 seconds** during acceleration.
>   (i) What is the energy delivered by the supercapacitor during this 15-second burst? (in kJ and Wh)
>   (ii) If the supercapacitor bank has an operating voltage range of **200 V to 100 V**, calculate the required capacitance.
>   (iii) Justify why a hybrid system is preferred over a single Li-ion battery for high-performance EVs. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**Part (a) — PEMFC Working (7 Marks):**

- PEMFC = Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell
- **Anode reaction:** $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{e}^-$
- **Cathode reaction:** $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Net reaction:** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy (Electricity + Heat)}$
- The membrane (**Nafion**) allows only **H⁺ protons** to pass; electrons go through external circuit (motor).
- **Differences from battery:** Fuel cell is an *energy converter* (hydrogen in → electricity out continuously), while battery is an *energy storage* device.
- **Advantages:** Zero tailpipe emissions (only water), high energy density with H₂, fast refueling.
- **Challenges:** Hydrogen storage safety, expensive platinum catalyst, lack of refueling infrastructure.

**Part (b) — Hybrid System Calculation (7 Marks):**

**(i) Energy delivered by supercapacitor during 15-s burst**

$$E = P \times t = 200\ kW \times 15\ s = 3000\ kJ$$

$$E = \frac{3000}{3.6} = 833.33\ Wh \approx 0.833\ kWh$$

**(ii) Required capacitance**

The energy stored in a capacitor from $V_1$ to $V_2$ is:

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C (V_1^2 - V_2^2)$$

Solving for C:

$$C = \frac{2E}{V_1^2 - V_2^2}$$

Convert E to Joules: $E = 3000\ kJ = 3 \times 10^6\ J$

$$C = \frac{2 \times 3 \times 10^6}{200^2 - 100^2} = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{30{,}000} = 200\ F$$

✅ **C = 200 Farads** (large, hence "super"-capacitor)

**(iii) Justification (3 marks):**
- A Li-ion battery can deliver 200 kW only briefly; sustained high current degrades it and causes overheating.
- A supercapacitor can deliver **MW-level power bursts** for seconds without damage.
- The **hybrid system** uses battery for *range* (energy) and supercapacitor for *acceleration* (power) → best of both worlds, longer battery life, better performance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SOC with SOH:** SOC = current charge level; SOH = battery's age/health. Examiners *love* asking the difference.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numericals:** Writing $E = 1209.6$ without specifying Wh vs kWh loses 0.5 mark. **Always state units.**
> 3. **Sign error in C-rate current formula:** Use $I = C_{rated} \times C\text{-rate}$ (positive for discharge). Some students invert this.
> 4. **Saying "fuel cell stores energy":** It doesn't! It *converts* hydrogen's chemical energy. Battery *stores* it. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this error.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions:** A 14-mark question with sub-part (a) for 7 marks **must** have a labeled diagram. A text-only answer scores at most 5/7.
> 6. **Wrong conversion $1\ kWh = 3.6\ MJ$:** This is the most tested conversion. Memorize it.
> 7. **Mixing up energy and power:** $kWh$ = energy; $kW$ = power. Many students use them interchangeably and lose full marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Energy Source Technology:**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [ ] **EV energy sources** = Batteries (energy) + Supercapacitors (power) + Fuel cells (conversion)
- [ ] **Cell = Anode + Cathode + Electrolyte + Separator**
- [ ] **Li-ion = highest specific energy** in commercial EVs; **LFP = safest** chemistry
- [ ] **Fuel cells *generate* electricity; batteries *store* it**

### ✅ Key Formulas (Memorize These 5)
- [ ] $E = V \times Q$ (energy in Wh)
- [ ] $SOC = (Q_{rem} / Q_{rated}) \times 100$
- [ ] $DOD = 100 - SOC$
- [ ] $I = C_{rated} \times C\text{-rate}$
- [ ] $C = 2E / (V_1^2 - V_2^2)$ (supercapacitor sizing)

### ✅ Must-Know Numerical Conversions
- [ ] $1\ kWh = 3.6\ MJ = 1000\ Wh = 3.6 \times 10^6\ J$
- [ ] $1\ Ah = 3600\ C$ (Coulombs)
- [ ] Range $= E_{battery} / E_{per\_km}$

### ✅ Charging Protocol
- [ ] **CC-CV** is universal for Li-ion
- [ ] CC phase → reaches $V_{max} = 4.2\ V/cell$
- [ ] CV phase → current tapers, SOC → 100%

### ✅ Quick Comparison Mnemonics
- [ ] **LFP** = Long life, Fire-safe, Poor range
- [ ] **NMC** = Newer, More energy, Commonly used
- [ ] **PEMFC** = Proton Exchange Membrane FC, cold start, H₂ + O₂ → H₂O

### ✅ Exam Smart Moves
- [ ] Always draw a **labeled diagram** for 7-mark theory questions
- [ ] State **units** explicitly in every numerical
- [ ] Use **Coulomb counting** for SOC numericals
- [ ] Use $V_{terminal} = V_{oc} - Ir$ for terminal voltage problems
- [ ] Mention **BMS functions** (protection, balancing, SOC estimation) wherever relevant for bonus marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Charging Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Battery Charging Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Battery Charging Technology?

**Battery Charging Technology** in the context of Electric Vehicles (EVs) refers to the engineered set of electrical, electrochemical, thermal, and digital control methodologies employed to safely and efficiently replenish the stored electrochemical energy in a **traction battery pack** (most commonly Lithium-ion) from an external power source, while simultaneously preserving battery health, longevity, and user safety.

Formally, in KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, it is defined as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** Battery Charging Technology is the integrated science of *charging algorithms* (CC, CV, CC-CV, Pulse), *charging levels* (AC Level 1/2 and DC Level 3 Fast Charging), *charging standards/protocols* (SAE J1772, IEC 62196, CCS, CHAdeMO, GB/T), *thermal management during charging*, and *smart-grid communication* (ISO 15118, OCPP, V2G) used to transfer energy from the grid/renewable source to the EV traction battery in a controlled, safe, and optimized manner.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel Pump Meets Smart Thermostat"

Imagine your phone's battery. You plug it in at night, the current is high initially (the **fast-pouring tap**), then slows down as the battery fills (the **drip-feed tap**), and the phone gets warm. Multiply this by a factor of ~200–600 (a phone is ~15 Wh, an EV battery is ~50–100 kWh) and you have an EV.

The better analogy: **Battery Charging is like filling a balloon with water from a pressurized tap.**

- If you blast water in too hard (high current too early) → balloon bursts (**thermal runaway, fire**).
- If you drip too slowly (low current always) → it takes days (**Level 1 AC charging**).
- The **smart valve** is the **Battery Management System (BMS)**.
- The **tap pressure** is the **charging protocol** (CCS, CHAdeMO, etc.).
- The **water temperature** is the **battery cell temperature**, monitored and controlled by the **thermal management system (TMS)**.

## 1.3 Why is Charging Technology a "Technology" and not just "plugging in"?

> [!NOTE]
> EV battery packs store enough energy to be classified as **Class 9 hazardous goods** under UN transport regulations. A typical 60 kWh EV battery at 400 V contains ~150 kJ of energy — equivalent to ~36 g of TNT. Charging it is essentially *controlled defusing*. This is why the BMS, the connector pinout, the isolation monitoring, the thermal loop, and the charging algorithm are all part of a single, tightly-coupled **technology stack**.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **C-rate** | 0.5C – 3C (passenger EV) | Speed of charge/discharge relative to capacity |
| **Nominal cell voltage (Li-ion NMC)** | **3.6 V – 3.7 V** | Building block of pack voltage |
| **Pack voltage (modern EV)** | 400 V (nominal) / **800 V** (newer) | Determines charging architecture |
| **State of Charge (SoC)** | 0% – 100% | Energy content indicator |
| **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** | 80% – 100% (usable) | Cycles vs. longevity trade-off |
| **Energy throughput** | **kWh** | Utility billing unit |
| **Charging efficiency (AC)** | 85% – 92% | Grid-to-battery losses |
| **Charging efficiency (DC fast)** | 90% – 95% | Higher due to off-board rectification |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of EV Battery Charging Technology

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups the topic into five interacting pillars. Mastering these is essential for both theory and numerical problems.

### Pillar 1: Charging Algorithms (The "How" of filling)

These are the **electrochemical current/voltage profiles** applied to the battery.

**(a) Constant Current (CC) Charging**
- A fixed current $I_c$ is applied until the cell reaches its upper voltage limit.
- Simple, but stresses the cell near full charge → lithium plating risk.
- Used in: laboratory cycling, very small batteries.

**(b) Constant Voltage (CV) Charging**
- A fixed voltage $V_{max}$ is applied; current tapers as the cell approaches full charge.
- Gentle on the cell, but slow at the start.

**(c) Constant Current – Constant Voltage (CC-CV) — *The Industry Standard***
- **Stage 1 (CC):** Pump in a controlled current (e.g., 0.5C–1C) until the terminal voltage reaches $V_{max}$ (e.g., 4.2 V/cell for NMC).
- **Stage 2 (CV):** Hold voltage at $V_{max}$, current exponentially decays. Terminate when $I < 0.05C$ (C/20 cutoff).
- This is the **dominant algorithm** for every Li-ion EV in production.

**(d) Pulse Charging**
- Alternating charge and rest/discharge pulses.
- Reduces concentration polarization, mitigates lithium dendrite growth, and can rejuvenate SEI layer.
- Used in research-grade fast charging; Tesla's early patents referenced this.

**(e) Trickle / Float Charging**
- Very low current ($I \le 0.02C$) used to maintain a fully charged battery.
- Rarely used in EVs (only for 12 V auxiliaries).

**(f) Multi-Stage Constant Current (MSCC)**
- A series of decreasing CC steps without a true CV stage.
- Used in some Asian OEM protocols for ultra-fast charging.

### Pillar 2: Charging Levels (The "How Fast" spectrum)

Defined by SAE J1772 and adopted globally:

| Level | Supply | Power | Time (60 kWh) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 1 (AC)** | 120 V / 15 A (US) | **1.4 kW – 1.9 kW** | ~36 h | Home (emergency) |
| **Level 2 (AC)** | 240 V / 30–80 A | **3.3 kW – 19.2 kW** | 4 – 10 h | Home / Workplace |
| **DC Level 1 (Fast)** | 200 – 450 V DC | up to 50 kW | ~1.3 h | Public stations |
| **DC Level 2 (Fast)** | 200 – 950 V DC | **50 – 350 kW** | 15 – 30 min | Highway corridors |
| **DC Level 3 (Ultra)** | up to 1000 V | **>350 kW** (e.g., 500 kW) | < 15 min | Next-gen 800 V EVs |

### Pillar 3: Charging Connectors & Standards (The "Language" spoken)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connector ≠ Protocol ≠ Standard.** A CCS2 connector can carry both AC and DC; the protocol (ISO 15118, DIN 70121) defines the *handshake*, not the *plug shape*.

| Standard | Region | Power | Communication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SAE J1772 (Type 1)** | North America / Japan | AC Level 1 & 2 | PWM pilot |
| **IEC 62196 (Type 2 / Mennekes)** | Europe, India (adopted) | AC up to 43 kW | Type 2 signals |
| **CCS1 / CCS2 (Combo 1/2)** | NA / EU + IN | DC up to 350 kW | ISO 15118 / DIN 70121 |
| **CHAdeMO** | Japan, Korea | DC up to 400 kW (v2.0) | CAN bus, supports V2G |
| **GB/T** | China | DC up to 250 kW | GB/T 27930 |
| **NACS (Tesla)** | NA (adopted by Ford, GM, Rivian) | DC up to 250 kW | ISO 15118-ready |

### Pillar 4: The Battery Management System (BMS) — The "Referee"

During charging, the BMS performs:
- **Cell voltage monitoring** (each cell $V_{cell,i}$)
- **Cell temperature monitoring** (NTC sensors, 16–96 channels)
- **State of Charge (SoC) estimation** via Coulomb counting + Kalman filter
- **State of Health (SoH)** tracking for warranty
- **Cell balancing** (passive resistive vs. active capacitive/inductive)
- **Contactors control** (main +ve, -ve, pre-charge, HVIL loop)
- **Isolation monitoring** (resistance between HV bus and chassis, must be > 100 Ω/V)
- **Thermal runaway early warning** (gas sensor, voltage drop detection)

### Pillar 5: Smart Charging & Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)

- **ISO 15118:** "Plug & Charge" + bidirectional power flow protocol. The car authenticates itself to the charger via digital certificates.
- **V2G:** EV battery discharges back to grid/building during peak demand. CHAdeMO and CCS2 both support it.
- **OCPP 2.0.1:** Charger-to-backend-cloud communication standard.
- **V2H (Vehicle-to-Home) / V2L (Vehicle-to-Load):** Powering a house or running appliances.
- **Smart charging features:** Time-of-Use (ToU) optimization, demand response, frequency regulation.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C\text{-rate} = \dfrac{I}{Q_{rated}}$ | Charge/discharge current normalized to capacity | dimensionless |
| 2 | $I_{1C} = Q_{rated}\,(\text{Ah})$ | Current that fully charges in 1 hour | A |
| 3 | $t_{charge} = \dfrac{C_{missing}}{I_{avg}\,\eta}$ | Time to reach target SoC | hours |
| 4 | $P_{ch} = V_{pack}\cdot I_{pack}$ | Instantaneous charging power | W or kW |
| 5 | $E_{stored} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} P_{ch}(t)\,dt$ | Total energy delivered | Wh or kWh |
| 6 | $\eta_{ch} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{E_{drawn}} \times 100\%$ | Charger-to-battery efficiency | % |
| 7 | $\Delta \text{SoC} = \dfrac{\int I\,dt}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | Coulomb-counting SoC update | % |
| 8 | $Q_{heat} = I^2 R_{cell}$ | I²R heat generated per cell | W |
| 9 | $P_{cool} = \dot{m}\,c_p\,\Delta T$ | Thermal management load | W |
| 10 | $R_{iso} = \dfrac{V_{HV}}{I_{leak,max}}$ | Required isolation resistance | Ω |
| 11 | $E_{pack} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot V_{cell} \cdot C_{cell}$ | Pack energy | Wh |
| 12 | $V_{pack} = n_s \cdot V_{cell,nom}$ | Pack nominal voltage | V |
| 13 | $Q_{pack} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell}$ | Pack capacity | Ah |
| 14 | $\text{SoH} = \dfrac{C_{actual}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | Battery health indicator | % |
| 15 | $L_{cycles} = L_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{\text{DoD}_a}{\text{DoD}_b}\right)^{-k}$ | Peukert-like cycle life (approx.) | cycles |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL TYPING RULE for tables:** In all the above table cells, when you need to write absolute value or conditioning, use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|`, so the markdown table does not break. Example: write $V \vert_{max}$ not $V \mid_{max}$ inside a table.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Range Anxiety Reduction:** A 350 kW charger adds ~200 km in 10 minutes — directly competes with gasoline refuelling time.
- **Grid Stability:** Coordinated V2G from 10 million EVs = 500 GW of distributed storage — more than 2× India's peak demand.
- **Battery Longevity:** Smart charging (raising min SoC, capping max SoC at 80%, slowing above 80%) can extend cycle life from 1,500 to >3,000 cycles — saving tons of lithium per year.
- **Renewable Integration:** EVs as mobile storage can absorb midday solar and dispatch at night.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Charging Time from Constant Current

**Problem statement:** A 60 kWh EV battery pack is at 20% SoC. The BMS limits charging to **CC stage at 120 A** with a 400 V nominal pack voltage. Charging efficiency is 92%. Compute the time to reach 80% SoC under CC alone (assume voltage rises from 380 V to 420 V; use average).

**Step 1 — Identify the energy to be added.**

$$\Delta E = (SoC_{final} - SoC_{initial}) \cdot E_{pack} = (0.80 - 0.20) \cdot 60\,\text{kWh}$$

$$\Delta E = 0.60 \cdot 60 = 36\,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the average charging power.**

The pack voltage during CC stage ramps from 380 V to 420 V. Average voltage:

$$\bar{V}_{pack} = \frac{380 + 420}{2} = 400\,\text{V}$$

Average power delivered to the battery (DC side):

$$\bar{P}_{DC} = \bar{V}_{pack} \cdot I = 400 \cdot 120 = 48{,}000\,\text{W} = 48\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the efficiency factor.**

The grid side must supply more than what the battery receives:

$$\bar{P}_{grid} = \frac{\bar{P}_{DC}}{\eta_{ch}} = \frac{48}{0.92} = 52.17\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Compute time.**

The energy delivered to the battery per hour is 48 kWh. To transfer 36 kWh:

$$t_{ch} = \frac{\Delta E}{\bar{P}_{DC}} = \frac{36\,\text{kWh}}{48\,\text{kW}} = 0.75\,\text{h} = 45\,\text{minutes}$$

**Step 5 — Cross-check using grid-side.**

$$t_{ch}^{grid} = \frac{36}{0.92 \cdot 48} = \frac{36}{44.16} = 0.815\,\text{h} \approx 48.9\,\text{minutes}$$

(The extra 4 minutes accounts for grid-side losses during the actual 45 min of useful charging.)

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CC-CV Charging Time (Full Cycle)

**Problem statement:** Same 60 kWh pack, charging at 1C rate (60 A, wait — capacity in Ah needed). Let's redefine: 60 kWh at 400 V nominal means $Q_{pack} = 60{,}000/400 = 150$ Ah. So 1C = 150 A.

**Stage 1: CC at 1C = 150 A, from 20% to ~80% SoC.**

Capacity to add: $0.60 \cdot 150 = 90$ Ah.

Time at CC:

$$t_{CC} = \frac{\Delta Q}{I_{CC}} = \frac{90\,\text{Ah}}{150\,\text{A}} = 0.6\,\text{h} = 36\,\text{minutes}$$

**Stage 2: CV, voltage held at 420 V, current decays exponentially.**

A common empirical model for the current decay:

$$I(t) = I_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $\tau \approx 15$ min (typical Li-ion value at 80% SoC and 25°C) and $I_0 = 150$ A.

The CV stage ends when $I \le 0.05C = 0.05 \cdot 150 = 7.5$ A.

Solve for cutoff time $t_{cut}$:

$$7.5 = 150 \cdot e^{-t_{cut}/\tau}$$

$$e^{-t_{cut}/\tau} = \frac{7.5}{150} = 0.05$$

$$t_{cut} = -\tau \cdot \ln(0.05) = \tau \cdot \ln(20)$$

$$t_{cut} = 15 \cdot 2.996 \approx 44.9\,\text{minutes}$$

**Total charging time (20% → 100%):**

$$t_{total} = t_{CC} + t_{CV} = 36 + 44.9 = 80.9\,\text{minutes}$$

This is the classic **"barrel curve"** of Li-ion charging: fast in the middle, slow at the top.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Heat Generated During Fast Charging

**Problem statement:** 400 V pack, internal resistance per cell $R_{cell} = 25$ mΩ, 100 cells in series ($n_s = 100$, $n_p = 1$). Charge at 350 A (≈ 2.3C for 150 Ah). Compute total heat dissipation.

**Step 1 — Pack resistance.**

For pure series:

$$R_{pack} = n_s \cdot R_{cell} = 100 \cdot 0.025 = 2.5\,\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Ohmic heat.**

$$Q_{heat} = I^2 \cdot R_{pack} = (350)^2 \cdot 2.5 = 122{,}500 \cdot 2.5 = 306{,}250\,\text{W} \approx 306\,\text{kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **306 kW of heat** is the thermal load on the cooling system. This is why a Taycan (800 V architecture) splits the pack: at 800 V, current is halved → I²R losses drop to **¼** (≈ 76 kW). This is the primary engineering motivation for 800 V architectures.

**Step 3 — Coolant flow required.**

Coolant: 50/50 glycol-water, $c_p \approx 3.5$ kJ/(kg·K), allowed $\Delta T = 10$ K:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{Q_{heat}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{306{,}250}{3{,}500 \cdot 10} = 8.75\,\text{kg/s} \approx 31.5\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

This is the scale at which EV thermal loops operate — almost a swimming-pool pump.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: State of Charge via Coulomb Counting

**Problem statement:** A 150 Ah pack. For 30 minutes, charging current is 120 A, then 80 A for another 30 minutes. Initial SoC = 25%. Find final SoC.

**Step 1 — Charge added in first half-hour.**

$$Q_1 = I_1 \cdot t_1 = 120 \cdot 0.5 = 60\,\text{Ah}$$

**Step 2 — Charge added in second half-hour.**

$$Q_2 = I_2 \cdot t_2 = 80 \cdot 0.5 = 40\,\text{Ah}$$

**Step 3 — Total charge added.**

$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 = 100\,\text{Ah}$$

**Step 4 — SoC increment.**

$$\Delta SoC = \frac{Q_{total}}{Q_{rated}} \cdot 100\% = \frac{100}{150} \cdot 100\% = 66.67\%$$

**Step 5 — Final SoC.**

$$SoC_{final} = SoC_{initial} + \Delta SoC = 25 + 66.67 = 91.67\%$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: The 800 V Advantage — Quantitative

**Problem statement:** Compare the charging time to add 100 kWh at 400 V vs 800 V architecture. Pack internal resistance scales: $R_{800} = 4R_{400}$ (four times the cells in series, but same parallel path). Use the same 350 kW target power.

**At 400 V architecture:**

$$I_{400} = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{350{,}000}{400} = 875\,\text{A}$$

Assume $R_{pack,400} = 0.10\,\Omega$ (modern value for 400 V pack):

$$P_{loss,400} = I^2 R = (875)^2 \cdot 0.10 = 76{,}562.5\,\text{W} \approx 76.6\,\text{kW}$$

**At 800 V architecture:**

$$I_{800} = \frac{350{,}000}{800} = 437.5\,\text{A}$$

$$R_{pack,800} = 4 \cdot 0.10 = 0.40\,\Omega$$

$$P_{loss,800} = (437.5)^2 \cdot 0.40 = 76{,}562.5\,\text{W} = 76.6\,\text{kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Interesting — if we keep the same *cable cross-section per cell* and the same number of parallel strings, the *percentage* loss is identical. The real advantage of 800 V is **halving the cable current** for the same power → thinner, lighter HV cables, faster charging without overheating the wiring harness, and **lower I²R in the charging cable itself** (which is outside the pack).

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Feature | AC Charging (L1/L2) | DC Fast Charging (L3) | Battery Swap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectification | On-board (OBC) | Off-board | N/A |
| Power | 1.4 – 19.2 kW | 50 – 350+ kW | N/A (mechanical) |
| Time (60 kWh) | 4 – 36 h | 15 – 60 min | ~5 min |
| Battery degradation | Minimal | High (≈ 2× cycle loss) | Battery itself swapped |
| Grid impact | Spread out, friendly | High peak demand | Concentrated |
| Infrastructure cost | Low | Very high | Highest |
| Best for | Home, workplace | Highway corridors | Fleets (taxis, buses) |

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DC Fast Charging System

The following Mermaid block diagrams the *end-to-end* information and power flow during DC Level 3 charging. It is a **functional architecture**, not a physical sketch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    gridAC[Grid AC Supply 11kV/33kV] --> rectifier[High Power Rectifier AC to DC]
    pfc[PFC and Power Factor Correction Stage] --> rectifier
    rectifier --> dcdc[Isolated DC DC Converter]
    control[Charger Controller PLC and DSP] --> rectifier
    control --> dcdc
    iso[Isolation Transformer and Filter] --> dcdc
    dcdc --> cableOut[CCS2 or CHAdeMO Output Cable]
    cableOut --> evInlet[EV Inlet]
    evInlet --> bms[Battery Management System BMS]
    bms --> contactors[HV Contactors and Precharge]
    contactors --> pack[Battery Pack 400V or 800V]
    pack --> cells[Series Parallel Cell Modules]
    bms --> thermal[Thermal Management Loop]
    thermal --> pack
    bms --> can[CAN Bus to VCU]
    isoMonitor[Isolation Monitor IMD] --> pack
    comm[ISO 15118 PLC Communication] <--> evInlet
    comm <--> control
    can <--> vcu[Vehicle Control Unit VCU]
    vcu --> can
    cloud[OCPP Backend Cloud] <--> control
    cloud --> billing[Billing and Authentication Server]
    billing --> cloud
    can --> dash[Driver Dashboard HMI]
```

## 4.2 CC-CV Charging Profile (The "Barrel Curve")

This Mermaid block diagram traces the *logical state machine* of the BMS during a charge cycle.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitCheck
    InitCheck --> Handshake: Cable plugged in
    Handshake --> IsoTest: ISO 15118 auth OK
    IsoTest --> CCStage: IMD pass and contactors closed
    CCStage --> CVStage: Vcell reaches Vmax 4.2V
    CVStage --> CCStage: Vcell drops below Vmax 0.05V hysteresis
    CVStage --> Terminate: I less than C divided by 20
    Terminate --> Balancing: Cell delta V less than 30 mV
    Balancing --> FullCharge: All cells within tolerance
    FullCharge --> Sleep: Contactors open
    Sleep --> [*]
    CCStage --> Fault: Vcell greater than Vmax 0.05V
    CVStage --> Fault: Tcell greater than 45C
    Fault --> Emergency: Thermal runaway
    Emergency --> [*]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 Marks, Module 1]**
> **Q: Compare Level 1, Level 2, and DC Fast Charging in terms of power, time, and typical use case.**

**Model Answer:**
Level 1 AC charging uses 120 V/15 A (≈ 1.4 kW) and takes ~36 hours for a 60 kWh pack — used only for home emergency top-ups. Level 2 AC charging uses 240 V up to 80 A (3.3 – 19.2 kW) and takes 4–10 hours — the workhorse of home and workplace. DC Fast Charging (Level 3) off-board rectifies and delivers 50 – 350 kW DC directly to the pack, taking 15–60 minutes for the same pack — used in highway corridors and public fast-charging stations.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 Marks, Module 1]**
> **Q: What is CC-CV charging? Why is it the preferred algorithm for Li-ion EV batteries?**

**Model Answer:**
CC-CV is a two-stage algorithm. **Stage 1 (CC):** A controlled constant current (e.g., 0.5C–1C) is forced into the cell until its terminal voltage reaches the upper cut-off (4.2 V for NMC). **Stage 2 (CV):** The voltage is then held constant at this maximum while the current exponentially tapers; charging terminates when current falls below C/20. It is preferred because the CC stage allows fast bulk charging while limiting internal current/heat generation, and the CV stage prevents over-voltage, lithium plating, and electrolyte decomposition near full charge — extending cycle life.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks, Module 1]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] With neat block diagrams, explain the **functional architecture of a DC fast charging station** and describe the role of the **Battery Management System (BMS)** during charging. List any four BMS protection functions.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Derive the **charging time for a 60 kWh, 400 V, 150 Ah EV battery pack** being charged from 20% to 80% SoC using the CC-CV method at 1C rate. Assume CV cutoff at C/20 and the CV current decay follows $I(t) = I_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ with $\tau = 15$ min. Average pack voltage during CC = 400 V; charging efficiency = 90%.

### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) [7 Marks] DC Fast Charger + BMS Architecture:**

A DC fast charger has three main stages — (1) **AC-to-DC rectifier with PFC** that takes grid AC and produces a controlled DC bus; (2) **Isolated DC-DC converter** that steps the DC bus to the EV's pack voltage and provides galvanic isolation for safety; (3) **Controller & communication unit** (DSP + PLC) implementing ISO 15118 / DIN 70121 for the digital handshake with the EV.

The **BMS** is the gatekeeper of the pack. When the EV inlet detects the CCS2/CHAdeMO plug, the BMS performs: **handshake, isolation test, pre-charge, and contactor closure**. Once charging begins, the BMS:
- **Monitors** every cell's voltage and temperature (NTC sensors).
- **Estimates SoC** (Coulomb counting + Kalman filter) and broadcasts it to the charger.
- **Demands** the maximum allowable current from the charger (based on SoC, temperature, and SoH).
- **Balances** cells (passive bleed resistors or active DC-DC transfer).
- **Opens contactors** on any fault (over-voltage, over-temperature, over-current, communication loss).

**Four BMS protection functions:** (i) Over-voltage cutoff per cell; (ii) Over-temperature cutoff (typically > 45–55 °C); (iii) Under-voltage cutoff at deep discharge; (iv) Isolation fault detection (IMD).

**(b) [7 Marks] Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $E_{pack} = 60$ kWh, $V_{pack,nom} = 400$ V, $Q_{pack} = 150$ Ah, SoC from 20% → 80%, 1C charging → $I_{CC} = 150$ A, $\tau = 15$ min, $\eta = 0.90$, $V_{avg,CC} = 400$ V.

**Step 1 — Energy to add:**
$\Delta E = 0.60 \cdot 60 = 36$ kWh.

**Step 2 — Ah to add:**
$\Delta Q = 0.60 \cdot 150 = 90$ Ah.

**Step 3 — CC stage time:**
$t_{CC} = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{I_{CC}} = \dfrac{90}{150} = 0.6$ h $= 36$ min.

**Step 4 — CV stage time:**
At end of CC, current = 150 A. Cutoff = 0.05C = 7.5 A. Decay $I(t) = 150 e^{-t/15}$.

$$7.5 = 150 e^{-t_{CV}/15} \implies e^{-t_{CV}/15} = 0.05 \implies t_{CV} = 15 \cdot \ln(20) \approx 15 \cdot 2.996 \approx 44.9\,\text{min}$$

**Step 5 — Total time (no efficiency in time, only in delivered energy):**
$t_{total} = 36 + 44.9 = 80.9$ min $\approx 1$ h 21 min.

**Step 6 — With efficiency:**
If the 90% efficiency means the charger must supply $\Delta E / \eta = 40$ kWh, then at average DC power $P_{DC} = 400 \cdot 150 = 60$ kW:

$$t_{total}^{grid} = \frac{40}{60} = 0.667\,\text{h} = 40\,\text{min (CC only at the grid)}$$

**Conclusion:** The 20→80% region (the "sweet spot" of Li-ion fast charging) takes ~36 min at 1C; the slow CV tail adds another ~45 min, illustrating why EV manufacturers recommend daily charging only to 80%.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **concept of V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid) technology** with a block diagram. Discuss the relevant communication standards (ISO 15118, OCPP) and the role of the EV aggregator.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 50 kWh EV battery has an internal resistance of 0.1 Ω. It is charged at (i) 400 V architecture and (ii) 800 V architecture, both delivering 150 kW to the pack. Calculate the **cable current** and the **I²R power loss in the HV cable** (assume cable resistance = 0.02 Ω for 400 V, 0.04 Ω for 800 V — same cross-section, more length). Comment on the result.

### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) [7 Marks] V2G Technology:**

**Concept:** V2G enables a bidirectional energy flow between the EV battery and the grid. The EV is treated as a *distributed energy storage resource* (DESR). When grid frequency drops or prices peak, the EV discharges back; when renewable generation is high or prices low, the EV charges.

**Functional block diagram (from §4.3 above):** Grid Operator → Aggregator → OCPP Backend → Charger (SECC) → ISO 15118 PLC → EV BMS.

**Standards:**
- **ISO 15118:** The "language" between EV and charger. Defines *Plug & Charge* (digital certificate authentication), bidirectional power flow (ISO 15118-20), and AC/DC charging.
- **OCPP 2.0.1:** The "language" between charger and central management system. Handles smart charging profiles, transactions, firmware updates, and remote control.
- **IEEE 2030.5 / OpenADR:** Used for utility-to-aggregator dispatch signals.
- **CHAdeMO** was the first protocol with V2G; **CCS2 + ISO 15118-20** is the upcoming global standard.

**Role of the Aggregator:** No single EV is large enough to participate in wholesale electricity markets. The **aggregator** pools tens of thousands of EVs, treats them as a virtual power plant (~ 50–500 MW), bids into ancillary service markets (frequency regulation, spinning reserve), and dispatches individual cars via cloud software. The aggregator handles settlement, SoH warranties, and grid-code compliance.

**(b) [7 Marks] Numerical Solution:**

**Case (i) — 400 V architecture, P = 150 kW:**
$$I_{400} = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{150{,}000}{400} = 375\,\text{A}$$

$$P_{loss,cable} = I^2 R_{cable} = (375)^2 \cdot 0.02 = 140{,}625 \cdot 0.02 = 2{,}812.5\,\text{W} \approx 2.81\,\text{kW}$$

**Case (ii) — 800 V architecture, P = 150 kW:**
$$I_{800} = \frac{150{,}000}{800} = 187.5\,\text{A}$$

$$P_{loss,cable} = (187.5)^2 \cdot 0.04 = 35{,}156.25 \cdot 0.04 = 1{,}406.25\,\text{W} \approx 1.41\,\text{kW}$$

**Comment:** Even though the 800 V cable has **double** the resistance (twice the length, same cross-section), the loss is **half** of the 400 V case — because loss scales as $I^2$, and current is halved. The 800 V architecture reduces charging cable losses by 50% at the same power, allowing thinner conductors and less heat — a key reason Porsche, Hyundai (E-GMP), Lucid, and BYD are migrating to 800 V.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse connector with protocol.** CCS2 is a *plug shape*; ISO 15118 is the *handshake*. Students often write "CCS supports V2G" — wrong. It is *ISO 15118-20* that enables bidirectional power over CCS.
> 2. **Units trap:** C-rate is dimensionless; current must be in A and capacity in Ah. Writing "C = I/C" with wrong units costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing AC and DC Level 2:** "Level 2 DC" is a misnomer — Level 2 is AC by SAE definition. DC starts at "Level 3" (or DC Fast).
> 4. **CC-CV: forgetting CV stage.** Many answers compute only the CC time and forget the exponentially-decaying CV tail — losing 2–3 marks on the derivation.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions.** In numerical problems, always list: "Assume ideal rectifier", "negligible cable loss", "cells balanced" — these guard marks are awarded for clarity.
> 6. **Confusing SoC and SoH:** SoC is *instantaneous charge level*; SoH is *long-term capacity retention*. They are independent concepts.
> 7. **800 V derivation — common error:** Students often assume $R_{800} = 2R_{400}$. For *purely series* cells (same parallel strings), $R \propto n_s$, so $R_{800} = 4R_{400}$ if you double voltage by doubling series count. Always state your pack topology.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 30-Second Cheat Sheet — Battery Charging Technology**

- ☐ **Definition:** Controlled energy transfer from grid to EV traction battery, governed by *algorithm + level + standard + BMS + smart-grid comms*.
- ☐ **Five Pillars:** (1) Algorithm (CC-CV dominant), (2) Level (L1/L2 AC, L3 DC Fast), (3) Connector+Protocol (CCS2, CHAdeMO, GB/T, NACS), (4) BMS (safety referee), (5) Smart Charging (V2G, ISO 15118, OCPP).
- ☐ **CC-CV = two stages:** CC at fixed $I$ until $V = V_{max}$; CV at fixed $V$ until $I < C/20$.
- ☐ **The 80% Rule:** Li-ion charges fast to 80%, then slow above (CV tail). Daily charging to 80% is best practice.
- ☐ **C-rate** $C = I/Q_{rated}$. 1C of 150 Ah = 150 A.
- ☐ **400 V vs 800 V:** Same power → half current → ¼ cable loss (for same $R$) or ½ loss (for $2R$). Thinner cables, faster charging.
- ☐ **Standards to memorize:** SAE J1772 (Type 1), IEC 62196 (Type 2), CCS1/2, CHAdeMO, GB/T, NACS, ISO 15118, OCPP 2.0.1.
- ☐ **Heat = $I^2 R$.** Fast charging's enemy is heat — managed by liquid cooling of cells and cable.
- ☐ **V2G** requires ISO 15118-20 (over CCS2) or CHAdeMO. Aggregator is essential for market participation.
- ☐ **BMS** does: cell V/T monitoring, SoC/SoH estimation, balancing, contactor control, isolation monitoring.
- ☐ **Charging efficiency:** AC ~ 85–92%, DC fast ~ 90–95%. Loss is dominated by charger electronics, not cable.
- ☐ **Battery swap** is a non-charging alternative: 5 min swap vs 30 min DC fast — best for high-utilization fleets.
- ☐ **Examiner loves:** derivations, 800 V numericals, BMS function lists, standards comparison tables, and any *block diagram* of the charging system.
- ☐ **Watch out for:** confusing SoC/SoH, mixing connector and protocol, omitting units, skipping CV stage, and confusing Level 2 AC with "Level 2 DC".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle-to-Grid Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle-to-Grid Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** technology is an advanced smart-grid integration framework that enables bidirectional flow of electrical energy between the **onboard Energy Storage System (ESS)** of an electric vehicle (typically the high-voltage traction battery) and the **external utility grid** through a certified bidirectional power converter (charger/inverter). Unlike conventional unidirectional EV charging (G2V – Grid-to-Vehicle), V2G allows the EV battery to act as a Distributed Energy Resource (DER), supplying stored energy back to the grid during periods of peak demand or grid instability, and absorbing energy during periods of excess generation.

The entire operation is orchestrated by an **Energy Management System (EMS)** supported by **ISO 15118** communication protocols, ensuring synchronized power quality, frequency regulation (50 Hz in India), and grid-code compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** V2G is treated as a *system-level application* of EV powertrains, sitting at the intersection of power electronics, smart grid communication, and battery management. Questions typically test its working principle, architecture, benefits, and grid-impact calculations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a typical Indian household scenario:

> 🪔 *During Diwali evening, every house in your street turns on lights and ACs simultaneously. The local transformer "struggles" and voltage dips occur.* Now suppose **every parked EV in that street could, like a giant power bank, pump electricity back into the wires for 2-3 hours during this peak**. Once the peak passes, the EVs would quietly recharge overnight when electricity is cheap. That is exactly what V2G does — your parked car becomes a **mobile, dispatchable power plant**.

**In plain English:** Your EV is normally a *load* on the grid (it consumes electricity). With V2G hardware, it transforms into a *controllable source* that can either consume or supply power depending on the grid's instantaneous need — much like how a rechargeable torch can either receive charge from mains or illuminate your room during a power cut.

## 1.3 The V2X Family — Related Bidirectional Concepts

The KTU syllabus expects students to clearly distinguish between the variants of bidirectional EV-grid interaction:

| Acronym | Full Form | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **V1G** | Vehicle-to-Grid (Controlled/Unidirectional) | EV charges at variable rate in response to grid signal (no discharge) |
| **V2G** | Vehicle-to-Grid (Bidirectional) | EV both charges from AND discharges to the grid |
| **V2H** | Vehicle-to-Home | EV supplies power to a single residential load |
| **V2B** | Vehicle-to-Building | EV supplies power to a commercial/office building |
| **V2X** | Vehicle-to-Everything | Umbrella term for all bidirectional EV energy flows |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard:** **ISO 15118** is the international standard governing *Plug & Charge* and bidirectional power transfer communication. It defines how the EV, EV Supply Equipment (EVSE), and backend utility platforms negotiate charging/discharging schedules, authenticate users, and settle payments.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standardized parameters are essential for any KTU numerical on V2G:

- **Indian Grid Frequency:** **f = 50 Hz**
