- **Standard AC Charging Levels (India):** Level 1 (≤ 3.3 kW), Level 2 (3.3–22 kW), Level 3 (DC fast, up to 50–150 kW)
- **Typical EV Battery Capacity:** **15 kWh – 100 kWh**
- **Usable Depth of Discharge (DoD) for V2G:** **70 % – 80 %** (to protect battery life)
- **Round-trip Efficiency (Charger + Battery):** **85 % – 92 %**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The V2G Power Chain

A V2G-enabled system requires **five synchronized subsystems**. The KTU 2024 scheme expects the student to identify and explain each:

1. **EV Battery Pack (BESS):** Lithium-ion traction battery, typically 400 V or 800 V architecture. Must support high cycle life (>3000 cycles) to withstand V2G cycling.
2. **Bidirectional On-Board Charger (OBC) or Off-Board DC Charger:** Contains an AC-DC stage (rectifier) **and** a DC-AC stage (inverter), allowing power flow in either direction. Uses advanced topologies such as **Dual Active Bridge (DAB)** or **CLLC resonant converters**.
3. **Smart Meter & Energy Management Controller (EMC):** Measures real-time grid parameters (V, f, P, Q) and issues charge/discharge commands.
4. **Communication Backbone:** Wireless (4G/5G) or wired (PLC – Power Line Communication) link following **OCPP 2.0.1 / ISO 15118-20** standards.
5. **Utility Aggregator Platform:** Software layer that aggregates thousands of EVs to provide **Ancillary Services** (frequency regulation, spinning reserves, peak shaving).

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Connection & Handshaking:**
The EV is plugged into a bidirectional EVSE. The EVSE and EV exchange digital certificates via **ISO 15118 Plug & Charge**, automatically identifying the user, contract, and tariff.

**Step 2 — Parameter Sensing:**
The smart meter continuously samples grid frequency and voltage. A drop in frequency (e.g., f < 49.8 Hz in India) indicates **generation deficit** → V2G discharge is triggered.

**Step 3 — Power Conversion:**
The bidirectional converter inverts the DC battery voltage into **grid-synchronized AC** (230 V, 50 Hz single-phase or 415 V three-phase). A PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) ensures frequency and phase matching.

**Step 4 — Dispatch & Settle:**
Power flows onto the grid. The aggregator measures kWh delivered and credits the EV owner per the dynamic tariff.

**Step 5 — Reverse Operation (G2V):**
When grid frequency rises above nominal (over-generation, e.g., solar midday), the system switches to *charging* mode, absorbing surplus power.

## 2.3 Grid Services Provided by V2G

The "WHY" of V2G lies in the **value stack** of grid services:

| Service | Description | Response Time |
|---|---|---|
| **Peak Shaving** | Discharging during high-tariff hours | Minutes |
| **Frequency Regulation** | Maintaining 50 Hz by injecting/absorbing power | **Seconds** (PFR) |
| **Voltage Support** | Reactive power (VAR) injection | Seconds |
| **Spinning Reserve** | Stand-by dispatchable capacity | < 15 min |
| **Renewable Integration** | Buffering solar/wind intermittency | Continuous |
| **Load Balancing** | Three-phase balancing | Real-time |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **high-yield mathematical backbone** for any numerical problem on V2G in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

$$
E_{batt} \; = \; V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}
$$

$$
\text{State of Charge (SoC)} \; = \; \frac{E_{available}}{E_{rated}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Depth of Discharge (DoD)} \; = \; 100\% - \text{SoC}\%
$$

$$
P_{V2G} \; = \; V_{grid} \times I_{discharge} \times \cos\phi \times \eta_{conv}
$$

$$
t_{discharge} \; = \; \frac{C_{Ah} \times \text{DoD}}{I_{discharge}}
$$

$$
E_{delivered} \; = \; P_{V2G} \times t_{discharge} \times \eta_{roundtrip}
$$

$$
N_{EVs} \; = \; \frac{P_{grid\;deficit}}{P_{V2G\;per\;EV}}
$$

$$
\text{Revenue} \; = \; E_{delivered} \times \text{Tariff}_{peak}
$$

$$
\text{Battery Degradation Rate} \; = \; \frac{\Delta C_{battery}}{\text{Cycle count}}
$$

$$
\eta_{roundtrip} \; = \; \eta_{charger} \times \eta_{inverter} \times \eta_{battery}
$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $E_{batt}$ | Battery stored energy | **kWh** |
| $V_{nom}$ | Nominal DC battery voltage | **V** |
| $C_{Ah}$ | Battery ampere-hour rating | **Ah** |
| SoC | State of Charge | **%** |
| DoD | Depth of Discharge | **%** |
| $P_{V2G}$ | V2G discharge power | **kW** |
| $\eta_{conv}$ | Converter efficiency | **0.85 – 0.95** |
| $\eta_{roundtrip}$ | Round-trip efficiency | **0.85 – 0.92** |
| $N_{EVs}$ | Number of EVs required | **vehicles** |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Utility Sector (DISCOMs in India):** TATA Power, BSES Rajdhani, and Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB) are piloting V2G to manage evening peak demand in metro cities.
- **Renewable Integration:** Solar PV generation peaks at 1 PM; EV fleets can absorb this surplus, reducing curtailment.
- **Microgrids & Islanding:** During grid outages, a V2G-enabled EV can **island** a hospital or data center — a concept called **Vehicle-to-Building (V2B)**.
- **Fleet Operators:** Companies like Amazon (Rivian vans) and UPS are exploring V2G for depot-based load management.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum V2G Discharge Time from a Given Battery Pack

### Problem Context
A KTU-style numerical typically gives: Battery capacity (kWh), Discharge power (kW), Depth of Discharge (%), and asks for the **maximum time** the EV can support the grid.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the available usable energy.**
The traction battery cannot be fully depleted during V2G (to avoid cell damage). Only a fraction equal to the allowable DoD is usable:

$$
E_{usable} \; = \; E_{battery} \times \text{DoD}_{V2G}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the real discharge power at the grid terminals.**
Power delivered to the grid is always less than the battery's internal discharge power due to conversion losses:

$$
P_{grid} \; = \; P_{battery} \times \eta_{converter}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the fundamental energy-time relation.**
Since energy is the product of power and time:

$$
E_{usable} \; = \; P_{grid} \times t_{max}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for the maximum discharge duration.**

$$
t_{max} \; = \; \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{grid}} \; = \; \frac{E_{battery} \times \text{DoD}_{V2G}}{P_{battery} \times \eta_{converter}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (Sample KTU-style substitution).**
Given: $E_{battery} = 60 \text{ kWh}$, $\text{DoD}_{V2G} = 0.70$, $P_{battery} = 10 \text{ kW}$, $\eta_{converter} = 0.92$:

$$
t_{max} \; = \; \frac{60 \times 0.70}{10 \times 0.92}
$$

$$
t_{max} \; = \; \frac{42.0}{9.2}
$$

$$
t_{max} \; \approx \; 4.565 \text{ hours}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 60 kWh EV battery, discharging at 10 kW with 70 % usable DoD and 92 % efficient converter, can sustain V2G operation for approximately **4 hours 34 minutes**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of EVs Required to Compensate a Grid Power Deficit

### Problem Context
A feeder transformer in a locality is experiencing a peak evening load of 1 MW above its rated capacity. Each V2G-enabled EV can supply 7 kW. Calculate the **minimum fleet size** needed to shave this peak.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the grid power deficit.**

$$
P_{deficit} \; = \; P_{peak\;load} - P_{transformer\;rated}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the V2G contribution per EV.**
Each EV delivers real power $P_{EV}$ to the grid, subject to the bidirectional charger's continuous rating.

**Step 3 — The total V2G fleet contribution (assuming simultaneous dispatch) is:**

$$
P_{fleet} \; = \; N_{EVs} \times P_{EV} \times \eta_{conv}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate fleet contribution to deficit and solve:**

$$
N_{EVs} \; = \; \frac{P_{deficit}}{P_{EV} \times \eta_{conv}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example.**
Given: $P_{deficit} = 1000 \text{ kW}$, $P_{EV} = 7 \text{ kW}$, $\eta_{conv} = 0.90$:

$$
N_{EVs} \; = \; \frac{1000}{7 \times 0.90}
$$

$$
N_{EVs} \; = \; \frac{1000}{6.3}
$$

$$
N_{EVs} \; \approx \; 158.73
$$

**Step 6 — Apply engineering rounding rule (always round UP for "minimum" requirement):**

$$
N_{EVs,min} \; = \; \lceil 158.73 \rceil \; = \; 159 \text{ vehicles}
$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately **159 EVs**, each dispatching 7 kW through a 90 %-efficient bidirectional charger, are needed simultaneously to shave 1 MW of peak grid load.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Economic Revenue from V2G Arbitrage

### Problem Context
An EV owner participates in a Time-of-Use (ToU) tariff. They charge at ₹6/kWh (off-peak, 11 PM – 6 AM) and discharge back to the grid at ₹14/kWh (peak, 6 PM – 10 PM). Compute the **net daily revenue** per kWh cycled.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Cost of charging 1 kWh into the battery.**

$$
C_{charge} \; = \; \text{Tariff}_{offpeak} \times \frac{1}{\eta_{charge}}
$$

**Step 2 — Revenue earned by discharging 1 kWh onto the grid.**

$$
R_{discharge} \; = \; \text{Tariff}_{peak} \times \eta_{discharge}
$$

**Step 3 — Net arbitrage per kWh of battery throughput:**

$$
R_{net} \; = \; R_{discharge} - C_{charge}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute assumed efficiencies ($\eta_{charge} = \eta_{discharge} = 0.92$, $\eta_{battery} = 0.98$):**

$$
C_{charge} \; = \; 6 \times \frac{1}{0.92 \times 0.98} \; = \; 6 \times 1.109 \; = \; 6.655 \text{ ₹/kWh}
$$

$$
R_{discharge} \; = \; 14 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 \; = \; 14 \times 0.9016 \; = \; 12.622 \text{ ₹/kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Net profit calculation:**

$$
R_{net} \; = \; 12.622 - 6.655
$$

$$
R_{net} \; \approx \; 5.967 \text{ ₹ per kWh}
$$

**Step 6 — Scale to daily cycle (e.g., 30 kWh throughput):**

$$
\text{Daily Revenue} \; = \; 5.967 \times 30 \; = \; 179.01 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Interpretation:** A single EV cycling **30 kWh per day** under the assumed ToU differential earns nearly **₹179/day**, translating to over **₹65,000/year** — a powerful economic incentive for fleet operators.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — V2G vs Conventional EV Charging

| Parameter | Unidirectional (G2V only) | Bidirectional (V2G) |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Electronics Cost** | ₹40,000 – ₹80,000 | ₹1,20,000 – ₹2,00,000 |
| **Cycles per Day** | 1 (overnight) | 2–4 (charge + discharge) |
| **Battery Degradation** | Low | Moderate (needs NMC/LFP chemistry) |
| **Communication Standard** | OCPP 1.6 | ISO 15118-20, OCPP 2.0.1 |
| **Grid Service Revenue** | None | ₹5 – ₹15 per kWh cycled |
| **Cybersecurity Risk** | Low | High (needs PKI encryption) |
| **Payback Period** | N/A | 4–6 years |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — V2G System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Utility Grid\n50 Hz AC] -->|Smart Meter| B[EV Supply Equipment\nBidirectional Charger]
    B -->|ISO 15118\nCommunication| C[On-board Charger\nAC-DC + DC-AC]
    C -->|DC Link 400-800V| D[Traction Battery\nBESS Li-ion]
    D -->|BMS Telemetry| E[Energy Management\nController]
    E <-->|OCPP 2.0.1| F[Aggregator Platform\nCloud Server]
    F -->|Tariff and\nDispatch Signals| E
    A -.->|Frequency Drop\nf less than 49.8 Hz| E
    E -->|Discharge Command| C
    C -->|Inverted AC| B
    B -->|Power Flow| A
    G[Local Home Load] -.->|Optional V2H| B
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **Utility Grid** is the primary energy sink/source.
- The **EV Supply Equipment (EVSE)** acts as the physical and communication gateway.
- The **On-Board Charger (OBC)** performs AC→DC (charging) and DC→AC (V2G) conversion.
- The **Energy Management Controller (EMC)** acts as the "brain" — receiving signals from the **Aggregator Platform** (cloud) and grid frequency sensors.
- Dotted lines indicate optional/configurable paths (e.g., V2H to home loads).

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — V2G Decision Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([EV Plugged-in to\nBidirectional Charger]) --> Check1{Battery SoC\nabove 30 percent?}
    Check1 -- No --> ModeCharge[Force G2V\nCharging Mode]
    Check1 -- Yes --> Check2{Grid Frequency\nbelow 49.8 Hz?}
    Check2 -- Yes --> ModeV2G[Initiate V2G\nDischarge to Grid]
    Check2 -- No --> Check3{ToU Tariff\nPeak Hours?}
    Check3 -- Yes --> ModeV2G
    Check3 -- No --> ModeIdle[Standby or\nTrickle Charge]
    ModeV2G --> Monitor[Monitor SoC\nand Grid Stability]
    ModeCharge --> Monitor
    ModeIdle --> Monitor
    Monitor --> Check1
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The controller continuously loops through three questions: *Battery health → Grid frequency → Tariff signal*.
- The decision tree prioritizes **battery safety** first, **grid stability** second, and **economic arbitrage** third — matching KTU-expected reasoning for an "intelligent" V2G system.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) technology. List any four key components required for its implementation.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** is a bidirectional energy exchange system in which an Electric Vehicle (EV), when connected to the grid through a bidirectional charger, can either draw power from the grid (G2V mode) or feed stored DC battery energy back into the AC grid (V2G mode), thereby acting as a Distributed Energy Resource (DER).

**Four Key Components:**
1. **Bidirectional On-Board/D Off-Board Charger** — Performs AC-DC and DC-AC conversion.
2. **Smart Meter & Grid Sensor** — Monitors real-time voltage, frequency, and tariff.
3. **Communication Module (ISO 15118 / OCPP)** — Enables handshake and dispatch signaling.
4. **Aggregator / Energy Management Platform** — Coordinates fleet-level dispatch and billing.

*(Note: Battery Management System (BMS) may also be credited as a 5th component.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between V1G, V2G, and V2H technologies with one-line clarity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **V1G** | Unidirectional (Grid → EV, controlled) | EV charges at variable rate in response to grid signal; **no discharge**. |
| **V2G** | Bidirectional (Grid ↔ EV) | EV both absorbs and supplies power to the grid for ancillary services. |
| **V2H** | Bidirectional (EV → Home load) | EV powers a residential load during outage or peak tariff (off-grid use). |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Paper — Module 1]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a V2G system. Describe the role of ISO 15118 and the Energy Management Controller in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

A Vehicle-to-Grid system operates through **five coordinated stages**:

1. **Physical Connection:** The EV is plugged into a bidirectional EVSE. The CHAdeMO / CCS2 connector supports both power and data lines.

2. **Authentication (ISO 15118 Role):** Using the **Plug & Charge** feature of ISO 15118, the EV and EVSE perform a Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)-based handshake. The EV's digital certificate authenticates the user, contract, and tariff plan **automatically** — no RFID card or app required.

3. **Grid Parameter Sensing:** The EVSE's smart meter continuously samples grid frequency (f), voltage (V), and real-time tariff. If $f < 49.8$ Hz or peak tariff window is detected, the **Energy Management Controller (EMC)** issues a *discharge command*.

4. **Power Conversion:** The bidirectional OBC inverts the DC battery voltage (e.g., 400 V) into grid-synchronized AC (230 V, 50 Hz). A Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) ensures the injected AC matches the grid's phase angle to within ±0.5°.

5. **Dispatch, Monitor & Settle:** Power flows onto the feeder. The aggregator measures kWh delivered, the BMS reports SoC, and the platform credits the EV owner per the dynamic tariff. When SoC falls to the lower threshold (e.g., 30 %), the controller reverts to G2V mode.

**Role Summary:**
- **ISO 15118:** Communication and authentication backbone.
- **EMC:** Decision-making brain — implements grid-support logic and battery safety constraints.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A 50 kWh EV battery has an allowable Depth of Discharge (DoD) for V2G of 75 %. If the battery discharges at a constant 11 kW through a bidirectional charger with 92 % efficiency, calculate:**
1. **The maximum discharge duration.**
2. **The total energy delivered to the grid (in kWh).**
3. **The daily revenue earned if the peak ToU tariff is ₹15/kWh.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute usable energy:**
$$
E_{usable} = E_{battery} \times \text{DoD} = 50 \times 0.75 = 37.5 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute power delivered to grid (after conversion loss):**
$$
P_{grid} = P_{battery} \times \eta_{converter} = 11 \times 0.92 = 10.12 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — (1) Maximum discharge duration:**
$$
t_{max} = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{grid}} = \frac{37.5}{10.12} \approx 3.706 \text{ hours} \approx 3 \text{ h } 42 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — (2) Total energy delivered to grid:**
$$
E_{delivered} = P_{grid} \times t_{max} = 10.12 \times 3.706 \approx 37.5 \text{ kWh}
$$

*Note: This matches $E_{usable}$ since power was assumed constant; the conversion efficiency is already baked into $P_{grid}$.*

**Step 5 — (3) Daily revenue:**
$$
\text{Revenue} = E_{delivered} \times \text{Tariff}_{peak} = 37.5 \times 15 = \text{₹ } 562.5
$$

**Final Answer:**
1. **t_max ≈ 3.71 hours (3 h 42 min)**
2. **E_delivered = 37.5 kWh**
3. **Daily Revenue = ₹562.5**

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Model Paper — Module 1]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the various grid services that V2G-enabled EVs can provide. Discuss the economic and environmental benefits with at least three examples each.**

**Model Solution:**

**Grid Services Provided:**
1. **Peak Shaving (Demand Response):** Discharging during 6 PM – 10 PM reduces the load on the distribution transformer, deferring capital investment in new substations.
2. **Frequency Regulation:** EVs respond in **seconds** to frequency deviations by injecting or absorbing real power, helping the State Load Despatch Centre (SLDC) maintain 50 Hz.
3. **Spinning Reserve:** A parked EV fleet acts as dispatchable capacity, ready to inject power within 15 minutes — replacing costly diesel generator reserves.
4. **Voltage/Reactive Power Support:** Through VAR control, EVs can stabilize local bus voltages.
5. **Renewable Energy Storage:** EVs absorb midday solar surplus, mitigating curtailment.

**Economic Benefits (3 examples):**
1. **ToU Arbitrage:** EV owner earns ₹5 – ₹15 per kWh cycled (as shown in Section 3.3).
2. **Capacity Deferral:** DISCOMs save crores by postponing new transformer installations.
3. **Aggregator Revenue:** Fleet owners can sell aggregated capacity to the Ancillary Services market.

**Environmental Benefits (3 examples):**
1. **Higher Renewable Penetration** → reduced coal-based generation.
2. **Lower Diesel Genset Use** → reduced PM2.5 and NOx emissions.
3. **Improved Grid Efficiency** → reduced transmission/distribution losses.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A 1.2 MW industrial feeder is experiencing peak demand. The feeder operator plans to use V2G-enabled EVs to offset 400 kW. If each EV delivers 6.6 kW through a 90 %-efficient bidirectional charger:**
1. **Calculate the minimum number of EVs required.**
2. **If each EV battery is 40 kWh with 80 % usable DoD, how long can the fleet sustain the 400 kW offset?**
3. **List two major technical challenges in implementing V2G at scale.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — (1) Minimum number of EVs:**
$$
P_{EV,grid} = 6.6 \times 0.90 = 5.94 \text{ kW per EV}
$$

$$
N_{EVs} = \frac{400}{5.94} \approx 67.34
$$

$$
N_{EVs,min} = \lceil 67.34 \rceil = 68 \text{ EVs}
$$

**Step 2 — (2) Sustained discharge duration:**
$$
E_{usable,fleet} = 68 \times 40 \times 0.80 = 2176 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
t = \frac{E_{usable,fleet}}{P_{offset}} = \frac{2176}{400} = 5.44 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — (3) Two major technical challenges:**

1. **Battery Degradation:** Frequent deep cycling accelerates capacity fade, reducing battery life from ~8 years to ~5 years, increasing TCO.
2. **Communication & Cybersecurity:** Real-time coordination requires robust ISO 15118 / OCPP 2.0.1 infrastructure; any latency or cyberattack can destabilize the grid.

*(Other acceptable answers: Standardization across OEMs, Lack of uniform bidirectional EVSE hardware, Grid synchronization complexity, Regulatory & tariff ambiguity in India.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing V2G with V2H:** Many students write "V2G means EV powering a house" — wrong. V2H is specifically home backup; V2G is grid-scale bidirectional flow. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting efficiency in numericals:** Students often compute $t = E / P$ directly using battery rating without applying $\eta_{converter}$. This leads to an inflated time and costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Not rounding UP for fleet size:** When asked "minimum number of EVs," always round UP (use ceiling). 67.34 means **68 EVs**, not 67.
> 4. **Skipping units in final answer:** KTU deducts **0.5–1 mark** if numerical answers are written without units (kWh, kW, ₹, etc.).
> 5. **Ignoring DoD:** Using full battery capacity (100 %) instead of 70–80 % DoD in calculations is a **common 2-mark mistake**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **V2G = Bidirectional energy flow** between EV battery and the utility grid.
- ✅ **Five components:** EV Battery, Bidirectional Charger, Smart Meter, Communication Module, Aggregator Platform.
- ✅ **Communication Standard:** **ISO 15118-20** + **OCPP 2.0.1**.
- ✅ **Core Formulae to memorize:** $E = V \times C_{Ah}$, $t = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{grid}}$, $N_{EVs} = \frac{P_{deficit}}{P_{EV} \times \eta}$.
- ✅ **Typical DoD for V2G:** 70 % – 80 % (NOT 100 %).
- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency:** 85 % – 92 %.
- ✅ **V2G grid services:** Peak shaving, frequency regulation, spinning reserve, voltage support, renewable integration.
- ✅ **Indian grid nominal:** 230 V / 415 V, **50 Hz**.
- ✅ **Cybersecurity:** ISO 15118 mandates **PKI-based** authentication.
- ✅ **Major challenges:** Battery degradation, lack of OEM standardization, cybersecurity, regulatory/tariff clarity.
- ✅ **Always apply converter efficiency** before computing discharge time in numericals.
- ✅ **Always round UP** fleet size to the next integer.
- ✅ **Distinguish carefully:** V1G (no discharge), V2G (grid), V2H (home), V2B (building).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Dynamics and Load Forces: Mathematical Models to Describe Vehicle Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Dynamics and Load Forces: Mathematical Models to Describe Vehicle Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vehicle Dynamics** is the branch of engineering that studies the motion of ground vehicles and the forces acting upon them under various operating conditions. In the context of Electric Vehicles (EVs), it involves the mathematical modelling of the **tractive force**, **resistive forces** (rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, gradient/climbing force), and **inertial (acceleration) forces** that govern acceleration, top speed, gradeability, and energy consumption.

**Load Forces** are the external and inertial loads that oppose (or demand) vehicle motion along the longitudinal axis. The fundamental longitudinal dynamic equation of a road vehicle is given by Newton's Second Law applied along the direction of travel:

$$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$$

where every term is a force in Newtons (N), and $F_t$ is the total tractive effort available at the wheel-road contact patch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly demands the derivation and application of this **mathematical model** to predict vehicle performance indices — namely **top speed, acceleration, gradability, and range**. Mastering the force equation is the *gateway* to every other module (powertrain sizing, battery sizing, motor selection).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **pushing a loaded shopping cart** up a sloped ramp on a windy day at a supermarket:
- The **weight of groceries** is like the **vehicle mass** ($m$).
- The **roughness of the floor (wheels squeaking)** is like **rolling resistance** ($F_{rr}$).
- The **wind blowing in your face** is like **aerodynamic drag** ($F_{ad}$).
- The **ramp's incline** is like the **gradient/climbing force** ($F_{hc}$).
- The **urge to start moving / speed up** is like the **acceleration (inertial) force** ($F_{la}$).

Your legs (the **electric motor + drivetrain**) must supply enough push (the **tractive force** $F_t$) to overcome ALL of these simultaneously. The harder any single component gets, the more "push" you need. In an EV, this translates to **higher motor torque demand**, **higher battery current draw**, and ultimately, **lower range**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Constants (must memorize for KTU exam):**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Air density at sea level, 20°C: $\rho_a = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Standard passenger car mass range: $m = 800 - 2500 \text{ kg}$
> - Coefficient of rolling resistance (passenger car tire on asphalt): $\mu_{rr} = 0.008 - 0.015$
> - Typical aerodynamic drag coefficient (sedan): $C_d = 0.25 - 0.35$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Longitudinal Forces on an EV

A battery-electric vehicle moving at velocity $v$ (m/s) along a road of slope angle $\theta$ (radians) and accelerating at $a$ (m/s²) experiences **four primary longitudinal load forces**. Each is analysed below.

### 2.1.1 Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{rr}$)
- **Why it exists:** As the tire deforms (flat patches form where it contacts the road), internal hysteresis in the rubber dissipates energy as heat. This is what saps efficiency at low speeds.
- **Mathematical model:** Linear with vehicle weight on a hard surface.

$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

- For small angles ($\theta < 15°$): $\cos(\theta) \approx 1$, simplifying to $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$.
- **Engineering note:** Wider tires and softer compounds *increase* $\mu_{rr}$ but *improve* grip. EVs with heavy battery packs (~300–600 kg) suffer disproportionately more rolling resistance losses.

### 2.1.2 Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$)
- **Why it exists:** Air molecules must be pushed aside and accelerated around the moving vehicle. Energy is lost to turbulence and pressure differentials.
- **Mathematical model:** Proportional to the **square** of velocity (the dominant speed-dependent loss above ~60 km/h).

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

- $\rho_a$ = air density (kg/m³), $C_d$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless), $A_f$ = frontal area (m²), $v$ = vehicle speed (m/s).
- **Engineering utility:** This is why EVs aggressively pursue low-$C_d$ shapes (e.g., Lucid Air $C_d = 0.197$, Tesla Model S $C_d = 0.208$).

### 2.1.3 Hill-Climbing (Gradient) Force ($F_{hc}$)
- **Why it exists:** A component of the vehicle's weight vector acts along (and against) the direction of motion when the road is sloped.
- **Mathematical model:**

$$F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

- For small angles, $\sin(\theta) \approx \tan(\theta) = \text{grade} \, (\%)$.
- A 30% grade means 30 m rise per 100 m run, $\theta \approx 16.7°$.

### 2.1.4 Linear Acceleration (Inertial) Force ($F_{la}$)
- **Why it exists:** Newton's second law — accelerating a mass requires a net force. For EVs, the **effective mass** includes rotational inertia of rotating components (rotor, wheels, gears).
- **Mathematical model:**

$$F_{la} = m \cdot a \cdot (1 + \delta)$$

where $\delta$ is the **mass factor** (typically $0.05 - 0.15$ for EVs, accounting for rotational inertia).

## 2.2 The Master Longitudinal Dynamic Equation

Combining all four:

$$F_t = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta) + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) + m \cdot a \cdot (1+\delta)$$

## 2.3 Power Required at the Wheels

The instantaneous mechanical power needed at the wheel-road interface:

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v$$

Therefore:

$$P_{wheel} = \left[ \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta) + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) + m \cdot a \cdot (1+\delta) \right] \cdot v$$

## 2.4 Tractive Force Limit (Friction Circle)

The maximum tractive force the tires can transmit is governed by the **no-slip condition** on the contact patch:

$$F_{t,\max} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

where $\mu$ is the coefficient of road adhesion (≈0.7–1.0 on dry asphalt, 0.1–0.3 on ice).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** If $F_t > F_{t,\max}$, the wheels **spin** (acceleration) or **lock** (braking). This is why traction control in EVs is critical for safety and is implemented in the vehicle dynamics control unit (VDCU).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Dependency |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | Rolling resistance | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$ | N | Constant with speed |
| 2 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2$ | N | $\propto v^2$ |
| 3 | Hill-climbing | $F_{hc} = m g \sin\theta$ | N | Constant with speed |
| 4 | Acceleration | $F_{la} = m a (1 + \delta)$ | N | $\propto a$ |
| 5 | Master equation | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$ | N | — |
| 6 | Power at wheels | $P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v$ | W | — |
| 7 | Max tractive force | $F_{t,\max} = \mu m g \cos\theta$ | N | Tire-road friction |
| 8 | Top speed condition | $F_t(v_{max}) = F_{rr} + F_{ad}$ on flat road | — | When $a=0$, no grade |
| 9 | Max gradability | $\sin\theta_{max} = \dfrac{F_{t,\max} - F_{rr}}{m g}$ | rad/° | At low speed |
| 10 | 0–100 km/h time | Use $t = \int \dfrac{m(1+\delta) \, dv}{F_t(v) - F_{resistive}(v)}$ | s | Numerical integration |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Models

- **Motor Sizing:** Peak power requirement at top speed + 30% safety margin.
- **Battery Sizing:** Energy needed to overcome these forces over a drive cycle (NEDC, WLTP, FTP-75).
- **Range Estimation:** Integrates $P_{wheel}$ over time → kWh consumption.
- **Thermal Management:** Sustained hill climbing defines continuous power ratings.
- **Regenerative Braking Design:** When $F_t < 0$, the same model governs energy recovery.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Longitudinal Force Equation

### Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram of the Vehicle
Consider a rigid vehicle of mass $m$ on an inclined road (angle $\theta$), moving at velocity $v$, accelerating at $a$. The forces along the longitudinal axis are:
- $F_t$ — tractive force at wheels (driving)
- $F_{rr}$ — rolling resistance (opposing)
- $F_{ad}$ — aerodynamic drag (opposing)
- $F_{hc}$ — weight component along slope (opposing when climbing)
- $F_{la}$ — pseudo-force equivalent to $m \cdot a$ (opposing when accelerating)

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law
Newton's second law along the longitudinal axis states that the sum of forces equals the rate of change of momentum. For constant mass:

$$\sum F_{long} = m \cdot a$$

Substituting the components:

$$F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc} = m \cdot a \cdot (1 + \delta)$$

### Step 3 — Isolate the Tractive Force

$$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + m \cdot a \cdot (1+\delta)$$

Substituting the closed-form expressions for each resistive force:

$$\boxed{F_t = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + m \cdot a \cdot (1+\delta)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Top-Speed Equation

### Step 1 — Definition
Top speed $v_{max}$ is the **maximum steady-state velocity** the vehicle can sustain on a level road. "Steady state" implies no acceleration, and "level" implies $\theta = 0$.

### Step 2 — Apply Steady-State Conditions
With $a = 0$ and $\theta = 0$, the master equation collapses:

$$F_t(v_{max}) = F_{rr} + F_{ad}(v_{max})$$

$$F_t(v_{max}) = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v_{max}^2$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $v_{max}$

$$\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v_{max}^2 = F_t - \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$$

$$v_{max}^2 = \dfrac{2 (F_t - \mu_{rr} m g)}{\rho_a C_d A_f}$$

$$\boxed{v_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 (F_t - \mu_{rr} m g)}{\rho_a C_d A_f}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** $v_{max}$ is **independent** of motor power in this derivation — but the motor must produce $F_t$ at the corresponding speed. Power $P = F_t \cdot v_{max}$ then defines the **minimum motor power rating** for top speed.

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Gradability

### Step 1 — Setup
Maximum gradability $\theta_{max}$ is the steepest grade the vehicle can **just barely** climb at **very low speed** (so that $F_{ad} \to 0$). We assume the motor can deliver the maximum allowable tractive force $F_{t,\max} = \mu \cdot m g$ (limited by tire friction).

### Step 2 — Apply Master Equation at Low Speed (Neglect $F_{ad}$)

$$F_{t,\max} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta_{max} + m g \sin\theta_{max}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\theta_{max}$
Divide both sides by $m g$:

$$\mu = \mu_{rr} \cos\theta_{max} + \sin\theta_{max}$$

Recognizing that $\mu \gg \mu_{rr}$ (typically $\mu \approx 0.8$, $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.01$), and for moderate angles $\cos\theta \approx 1$:

$$\sin\theta_{max} \approx \mu - \mu_{rr} \approx \mu$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{max} \approx \arcsin(\mu)}$$

For $\mu = 0.8$: $\theta_{max} \approx 53°$, or a grade of approximately **133%**. In practice, this is far above the structural/regulatory limits (typical EVs: 30–45% grade).

## 3.4 Derivation of Acceleration Capability (0–100 km/h Time)

### Step 1 — Time-Integral Formulation
Time to accelerate from $v_1$ to $v_2$ is found by rearranging the master equation for $a$ and integrating:

$$a = \dfrac{F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc}}{m(1+\delta)}$$

$$a(v) = \dfrac{F_t(v) - \mu_{rr} m g - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2}{m(1+\delta)}$$

### Step 2 — Time Integral

$$t = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} \dfrac{m(1+\delta) \, dv}{F_t(v) - \mu_{rr} m g - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2}$$

For a **simplified** constant-force case (negligible drag, no grade), this reduces to:

$$t = \dfrac{m(1+\delta) (v_2 - v_1)}{F_t - \mu_{rr} m g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use simplified form for KTU exams:** The full integral has no closed-form solution for typical motor curves. The KTU examiner expects students to **derive the integral form** and then evaluate it under stated simplifying assumptions.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix Style)

**Given:**
- $m = 1500$ kg, $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2$ m²
- $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\delta = 0.10$
- 8% grade climb, $v = 60$ km/h = 16.67 m/s, $a = 1.5$ m/s²

**Find:** Tractive force $F_t$ required and wheel power $P_{wheel}$.

**Solution — Step 1: Convert the grade to radians**
$\theta = \arctan(0.08) = 0.0798$ rad; $\sin\theta \approx 0.0797$, $\cos\theta \approx 0.9968$

**Step 2: Compute each force**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{rr} &= 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.9968 \\
&= 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.9968 \\
&= 176.06 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.89 \\
&= 112.34 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{hc} &= 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.0797 \\
&= 1172.8 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{la} &= 1500 \times 1.5 \times 1.10 \\
&= 2475 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Sum the components**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_t &= 176.06 + 112.34 + 1172.8 + 2475 \\
&= 3936.2 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute wheel power**

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v = 3936.2 \times 16.67 = 65{,}617 \text{ W} \approx 65.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Conclusion:** The vehicle's powertrain must deliver at least **~66 kW** at the wheels to maintain 60 km/h up an 8% grade while accelerating at 1.5 m/s². Real-world design adds 15–25% drivetrain loss and a 30% safety margin, yielding a **motor rating of ~90 kW**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Longitudinal Force Resolution

The following diagram maps the **mathematical flow** of the vehicle dynamics model, showing how each input parameter contributes to the tractive force and ultimately to performance metrics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Mass m kg] --> B[Effective Mass m 1+delta]
    B --> C[Acceleration Force F la]
    A --> D[Gravity mg]
    D --> E[Sin theta term F hc]
    D --> F[Cos theta term F rr]
    A --> G[Veh Frontal Area Af]
    G --> H[Drag Coeff Cd]
    H --> I[Aerodynamic Drag F ad]
    J[Velocity v m s] --> I
    J --> K[Power at Wheel P = Ft times v]
    C --> L[Master Equation Summation]
    E --> L
    F --> L
    I --> L
    L --> M[Tractive Force F t at Wheels]
    M --> N[Performance Outputs]
    N --> O[Top Speed v max]
    N --> P[Gradeability theta max]
    N --> Q[0 to 100 km h Time]
    N --> R[Energy Consumption kWh per 100 km]
    S[Tire-Road Friction mu] --> T[Friction Limit F t max]
    M --> U{Is F t less than F t max}
    T --> U
    U -->|Yes| V[Stable Drive]
    U -->|No| W[Wheel Slip Traction Loss]
```

## 4.2 Force Resolution Diagram (Schematic)

The diagram below shows the **vector decomposition** of the vehicle weight along the slope — the geometric basis of the rolling resistance and hill-climbing terms.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Slope_Plane [Inclined Road Plane]
        R[Road Surface]
    end
    M[Vehicle of mass m] -->|Weight mg| V[Vertical Vector]
    V -->|Perpendicular Component mg cos theta| N[Normal Reaction N]
    V -->|Parallel Component mg sin theta| HC[Hill Climbing Force F hc]
    N --> RR[Rolling Resistance F rr = mu rr times N]
    VEL[Velocity v] --> AD[Aerodynamic Drag F ad]
    M --> ACC[Inertial Force F la = m a 1+delta]
    RR --> SUM[Master Equation F t equals F rr + F ad + F hc + F la]
    HC --> SUM
    AD --> SUM
    ACC --> SUM
    SUM --> FT[Tractive Force F t at Wheels]
    FT --> TIRE[Tire-Road Contact Patch]
    TIRE -->|F t less than mu mg| SAFE[No Slip Safe]
    TIRE -->|F t greater than mu mg| SLIP[Slip / Loss of Traction]
```

## 4.3 Performance Index Mapping (Inter-Relationship)

This diagram clarifies **how the same master equation is solved for different performance indices** by imposing different boundary conditions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Master[Master Longitudinal Force Equation F t equals F rr + F ad + F hc + F la] --> Case1[Case 1: Top Speed]
    Master --> Case2[Case 2: Max Grade]
    Master --> Case3[Case 3: Max Accel]
    Master --> Case4[Case 4: Energy Use]
    Case1 --> B1[Set a = 0, theta = 0, solve for v]
    Case2 --> B2[Set a = 0, v approx 0, solve for theta]
    Case3 --> B3[Set theta = 0, v = 0, solve for a]
    Case4 --> B4[Integrate P wheel over drive cycle]
    B1 --> Out1[v max = sqrt of 2 times Ft minus mu rr mg over rho Cd Af]
    B2 --> Out2[theta max = arcsin mu minus mu rr]
    B3 --> Out3[a max = Ft minus F rr over m 1+delta]
    B4 --> Out4[E total = integral of P dt in kWh]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Modeled)]

**Q:** List and briefly define the four major longitudinal forces acting on an electric vehicle in motion. State the dependency of each force on vehicle speed.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four longitudinal forces are:
1. **Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{rr}$):** Opposes motion due to tire deformation hysteresis. Approximately constant with speed: $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$.
2. **Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$):** Opposes motion due to air displacement. **Proportional to $v^2$:** $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2$.
3. **Hill-Climbing Force ($F_{hc}$):** Gravitational component along slope. **Independent of speed:** $F_{hc} = m g \sin\theta$.
4. **Linear Acceleration Force ($F_{la}$):** Inertial force. **Independent of speed, depends on acceleration:** $F_{la} = m a (1+\delta)$.

(1 mark for listing, 1.5 marks for defining with correct formula, 0.5 mark for speed dependency)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Modeled)]

**Q:** What is the significance of the mass factor $\delta$ in the longitudinal dynamics model of an EV? How does it differ from a conventional ICE vehicle?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The mass factor $\delta$ accounts for the **rotational inertia** of rotating components (electric motor rotor, transmission gears, wheels). When a vehicle accelerates, these components also accelerate angularly, consuming additional energy beyond the translational kinetic energy of the chassis.
- Mathematical inclusion: effective mass becomes $m(1+\delta)$.
- For EVs, $\delta \approx 0.05 - 0.15$ (lower than ICE because no heavy flywheel/clutch).
- Without $\delta$, the model **underestimates** tractive force and power required by 5–15%.
(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mathematical effect, 1 mark for EV-specific comparison)

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Modeled)] — Choice (i)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the master longitudinal force equation for an electric vehicle moving on an inclined road, clearly defining each term. State the simplifying assumption that allows the equation to be used for level-road analysis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An electric car has the following specifications:
- Mass $m = 1200$ kg, $\mu_{rr} = 0.010$, $C_d = 0.28$, $A_f = 2.0$ m²
- $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m², $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Maximum motor tractive force = 3500 N (assumed constant for low speeds)
- Find: (i) Top speed on a level road, (ii) Maximum gradability at 5 km/h (neglect $F_{ad}$ at this speed)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Derivation:**
- Free-body diagram with weight, normal, and four longitudinal forces. (1 mark)
- Apply Newton's second law along slope: $F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad} - F_{hc} = m a (1+\delta)$. (2 marks)
- Substitute closed-form expressions for each term. (2 marks)
- Arrive at the master equation. (1 mark)
- Simplifying assumption for level road: $\theta = 0$, so $\sin\theta = 0$ and $\cos\theta = 1$. (1 mark)

**(b) [7 Marks] — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Top speed (4 marks):**
At $v_{max}$: $a = 0$, $\theta = 0$:
$$F_t = \mu_{rr} m g + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v_{max}^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
3500 &= 0.010 \times 1200 \times 9.81 + 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.0 \times v_{max}^2 \\
3500 &= 117.72 + 0.343 \cdot v_{max}^2 \\
v_{max}^2 &= \dfrac{3500 - 117.72}{0.343} = 9856.8 \\
v_{max} &= 99.28 \text{ m/s} = 357.4 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Max gradability (3 marks):**
At 5 km/h = 1.389 m/s, $F_{ad} \approx 0.343 \times (1.389)^2 = 0.66$ N (negligible). (1 mark)
$$3500 = 0.010 \times 1200 \times 9.81 \cos\theta + 1200 \times 9.81 \sin\theta$$
$$3500 = 117.72 \cos\theta + 11772 \sin\theta$$
For small angle, $\cos\theta \approx 1$: (1 mark)
$$\sin\theta = \dfrac{3500 - 117.72}{11772} = 0.2875$$
$$\theta_{max} = \arcsin(0.2875) = 16.7° \approx 30\% \text{ grade}$$ (1 mark)

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Modeled)] — Choice (ii)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat sketches, explain the variation of rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and total tractive force with vehicle speed. Identify the dominant loss mechanism in three distinct speed regimes: city (≤50 km/h), highway (80–120 km/h), and high-speed (>150 km/h).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 4-wheel-drive electric SUV with the following data is to accelerate from rest to 100 km/h on a level road in 10 seconds:
- $m = 2200$ kg, $\mu_{rr} = 0.013$, $C_d = 0.35$, $A_f = 2.8$ m², $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m², $\delta = 0.12$
- Find: (i) The average tractive force required (using the mean speed method), (ii) The peak power needed at the wheels at 100 km/h.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks]:**

| Regime | Speed | Dominant Loss | Reason |
|--------|-------|---------------|--------|
| City | ≤50 km/h | Rolling resistance | $F_{rr}$ is constant; $F_{ad} \propto v^2$ is small at low $v$ |
| Highway | 80–120 km/h | Aerodynamic drag | $F_{ad} \approx 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (27.7)^2 \approx 314$ N; equals/exceeds $F_{rr}$ |
| High-speed | >150 km/h | Aerodynamic drag (cubic in power) | $P_{ad} = F_{ad} \cdot v \propto v^3$ — very steep rise |

(2 marks for each regime with correct identification + 1 mark for the speed-cubic power argument)

**(b) [7 Marks]:**

**(i) Average tractive force (4 marks):**
Mean speed: $\bar{v} = 50$ km/h = 13.89 m/s. (0.5 mark)
Mean acceleration: $\bar{a} = 27.78 / 10 = 2.778$ m/s². (0.5 mark)
Mean $F_{ad}$ at 50 km/h: $0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 2.8 \times (13.89)^2 = 115.7$ N. (1 mark)
Mean $F_{rr}$: $0.013 \times 2200 \times 9.81 = 280.5$ N. (0.5 mark)
Mean $F_{la}$: $2200 \times 2.778 \times 1.12 = 6845$ N. (1 mark)
$$F_{t,avg} = 280.5 + 115.7 + 6845 = 7241 \text{ N}$$ (0.5 mark)

**(ii) Peak power at 100 km/h = 27.78 m/s (3 marks):**
$F_{ad}$ at 100 km/h: $0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 2.8 \times (27.78)^2 = 462.7$ N. (1 mark)
$F_{rr}$: 280.5 N (same as before). (0.5 mark)
Steady-state $F_{t,100}$: $280.5 + 462.7 = 743.2$ N. (0.5 mark)
$$P_{peak} = 743.2 \times 27.78 = 20{,}646 \text{ W} \approx 20.6 \text{ kW}$$ (1 mark)

(Note: A more complete answer would also add the inertial term at peak, but the question specifies "peak at 100 km/h", i.e., end of acceleration. Full marks require both interpretations discussed.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mass factor $(1+\delta)$** → loses 1–2 marks. The examiner *always* looks for this.
> 2. **Using $\tan\theta$ instead of $\sin\theta$** for the hill-climbing force. Technically valid only for small angles, but conventionally $F_{hc} = m g \sin\theta$. Marking penalty: 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing units:** $\rho_a$ in kg/m³, $v$ in m/s, $A_f$ in m², $F$ in N. A single unit slip (e.g., using $v$ in km/h) gives a **wrong answer** with **no partial credit**.
> 4. **Ignoring the $\cos\theta$ term** in $F_{rr}$ on a slope — many students write $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} m g$ regardless. Marks deducted for slope problems.
> 5. **Top-speed confusion:** Students often confuse $v_{max}$ with motor no-load speed. $v_{max}$ is the *operating* speed where motor force balances drag.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions explicitly:** Every derivation must list its assumptions (constant $C_d$, no crosswind, rigid chassis, dry road, etc.).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ⚡ **The Master Equation** is the **single most important** formula in EV dynamics — memorize it cold: $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$.
- ⚡ **Speed dependency rule of thumb:** $F_{rr} \propto v^0$, $F_{ad} \propto v^2$, $P_{drag} \propto v^3$ — the cubic power dependence is why EVs struggle at extreme top speeds.
- ⚡ **City driving = rolling-resistance-dominated; Highway = drag-dominated.** This drives design choices (low-rolling-resistance tires vs. low-$C_d$ body).
- ⚡ **Effective mass $m(1+\delta)$** captures rotational inertia. EVs benefit from low $\delta$ (no flywheel), gaining 3–7% efficiency vs. ICE.
- ⚡ **Tire-road friction** is the **hard ceiling** on tractive force ($F_{t,\max} = \mu m g$). EVs with heavy battery packs *increase* this ceiling (more normal force) — an unexpected advantage.
- ⚡ **Top speed** is set by force balance, not power. **Power rating** must support $F_t \cdot v_{max}$.
- ⚡ **Gradability** at low speed ≈ $\mu - \mu_{rr}$ (in sine). In practice, it's limited by motor torque and gear ratio, not by friction.
- ⚡ **For KTU 14-mark derivations:** Always draw a free-body diagram, label forces, write the equilibrium, substitute closed-form expressions, and end with a boxed final answer.
- ⚡ **For numerical problems:** Carry at least 4 significant figures, convert all speeds to **m/s** (not km/h), and present answers with **correct units**.
- ⚡ **Energy analysis extension:** Integrating $P_{wheel}$ over a drive cycle gives the **battery energy throughput** (kWh) — a critical bridge to Module 2 (Energy Storage).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Load Forces: Aerodynamic Drag
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Load Forces: Aerodynamic Drag

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Aerodynamic Drag** is the resistive force component acting on a vehicle parallel to the direction of motion, generated by the vehicle's interaction with the surrounding air mass as it displaces air during forward motion. In the context of electric vehicle dynamics, it is one of the four primary **vehicle load forces** that the traction motor must overcome, alongside **rolling resistance**, **gradient (climbing) resistance**, and **inertial (acceleration) force**.

Mathematically, the instantaneous aerodynamic drag force experienced by a moving vehicle is defined as:

$$F_d \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,v_{rel}^{2}$$

where each term carries SI units as specified in the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Aerodynamic drag is classified under *speed-dependent vehicle resistive forces* in Module 1. Because the force scales with the **square of velocity**, and the **power** required to overcome it scales with the **cube of velocity** ($P_d \propto v^3$), it dominates the energy budget of an electric vehicle at highway cruising speeds (typically above 60 km/h).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are walking across a swimming pool with a **large umbrella held open in front of you**. The faster you walk, the harder the water pushes back against the umbrella. Now, two things matter:

1. **The size of the umbrella (Frontal Area, $A_f$)** — bigger umbrella, more push-back.
2. **The shape of the umbrella (Drag Coefficient, $C_d$)** — a flat wooden board catches far more water than a streamlined torpedo-shaped board, even if both have the same frontal area.
3. **How fast you move (Velocity, $v$)** — doubling your speed roughly **quadruples** the resistance.
4. **How thick the fluid is (Air Density, $\rho$)** — water is "thicker" than air, so resistance is higher; similarly, on a humid, sea-level day, air is denser and drag increases.

A vehicle behaves exactly like the swimmer with the umbrella. The "umbrella" is the **front face of the car** as seen by the oncoming air. A well-designed EV tries to make this umbrella as small as possible, and as **aerodynamically slippery** (streamlined) as possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world benchmark for intuition:**
> - **Tesla Model S**: $C_d \approx 0.208$ (one of the lowest production cars ever)
> - **Lucid Air**: $C_d \approx 0.21$
> - **Conventional pickup truck**: $C_d \approx 0.65 - 0.80$
> - **Ideal teardrop shape**: $C_d \approx 0.04$ (theoretical minimum)

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are universally used in KTU-level EV aerodynamic calculations:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value | Unit |
|:------:|----------|:--------------:|:----:|
| $\rho$ | Air density at sea level, 20 °C | **1.225** | kg/m³ |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81** | m/s² |
| $C_d$ | Drag coefficient (passenger EV) | 0.20 – 0.35 | dimensionless |
| $A_f$ | Frontal area (typical sedan) | 2.0 – 2.5 | m² |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Vehicle Load Forces — Where Drag Fits

When an EV is in motion at velocity $v$ on a road of slope angle $\alpha$, the **tractive force** $F_t$ supplied by the motor must overcome the sum of four resistive forces. From the KTU 2024 Module 1 framework:

$$F_t \;=\; F_{rr} \;+\; F_{ad} \;+\; F_{hc} \;+\; F_{ia}$$

where:
- $F_{rr}$ = **Rolling Resistance Force** (velocity-dependent, linear) → covered in a separate topic
- $F_{ad}$ = **Aerodynamic Drag Force** (velocity-squared dependent) ← **current topic**
- $F_{hc}$ = **Hill-Climbing Force** (independent of velocity, depends on slope)
- $F_{ia}$ = **Inertial/Acceleration Force** (depends on mass and linear acceleration)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is aerodynamic drag treated separately from rolling resistance?**
> Because rolling resistance grows **linearly** with speed ($F_{rr} \propto v$), while aerodynamic drag grows with the **square of speed** ($F_d \propto v^2$). At low city speeds (20–40 km/h), rolling resistance dominates. At highway speeds (>80 km/h), aerodynamic drag dominates and can consume **over 60%** of the total tractive power.

## 2.2 The Aerodynamic Drag Equation — Step-by-Step Logic

The drag equation is not arbitrary; it is derived from fundamental **fluid dynamics** (a combination of momentum transfer theory and dimensional analysis). Each parameter has a clear physical meaning:

| Step | Physical Reasoning |
|:----:|--------------------|
| 1 | Air has mass. A vehicle of frontal area $A_f$ moving at velocity $v$ must push aside a column of air of mass $\rho \cdot A_f \cdot v$ every second. |
| 2 | By Newton's 2nd law (rate of change of momentum), the force is proportional to $A_f$, $\rho$, and $v^2$. |
| 3 | Real vehicles are not ideal streamlined bodies; some vehicles disturb the air more than others. The **proportionality constant** is the drag coefficient $C_d$, capturing shape, surface roughness, and flow separation effects. |
| 4 | The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convention that arises from the kinetic energy per unit volume of a fluid in motion ($\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$). |
| 5 | If there is a headwind or tailwind, the velocity relative to the air mass is $v_{rel} = v_{vehicle} \pm v_{wind}$, not just the vehicle's ground speed. |

## 2.3 Components of Aerodynamic Drag

Aerodynamic drag is **not a single phenomenon**; it is the sum of four sub-components:

1. **Pressure Drag (Form Drag)** — Largest contributor (~80% of total). Caused by the pressure difference between the front (high-pressure stagnation region) and the rear (low-pressure wake) of the vehicle. Strongly dependent on vehicle shape and flow separation.

2. **Skin Friction Drag (Viscous Drag)** — Caused by the shearing of air molecules along the body surface. Depends on surface roughness, Reynolds number, and wetted area.

3. **Internal Flow Drag** — Air that enters the vehicle for engine cooling, ventilation, and brake cooling must exit; this internal circulation contributes ~10–15% of total drag.

4. **Induced Drag** — A byproduct of lift generation; less significant for ground vehicles than for aircraft, but still present in vehicles with wings, spoilers, or underbody airflow.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete, exam-ready formula inventory** for aerodynamic drag problems in the KTU 2024 ESE. **Memorize all rows.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | SI Units | When to Use |
|:-:|:-------------|:--------:|:--------:|:------------|
| 1 | Drag Force (basic) | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^{2}$ | N (Newton) | Vehicle in still air |
| 2 | Drag Force (with wind) | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f (v \pm v_w)^{2}$ | N | Headwind (+) or tailwind (−) |
| 3 | Drag Power (mechanical) | $P_d = F_d \cdot v = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^{3}$ | W (Watt) | Power to overcome drag only |
| 4 | Drag Energy (over time $t$) | $E_d = P_d \cdot t = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^{3} t$ | J (Joule) | Total energy spent against drag |
| 5 | Frontal Area (approximation) | $A_f \approx 0.8 \cdot W \cdot H$ | m² | For sedans, when exact value unknown |
| 6 | Drag coefficient typical EVs | $C_d \in [0.20,\; 0.35]$ | — | Recall from data tables |
| 7 | Air density (ISA, sea level) | $\rho = 1.225$ | kg/m³ | Standard value |
| 8 | Vehicle speed conversion | $1 \text{ km/h} = \tfrac{5}{18} \text{ m/s}$ | — | Always convert km/h to m/s before plugging in |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Minimizing aerodynamic drag is a **first-order design priority** for any electric vehicle. The reason is rooted in the cubic relationship $P_d \propto v^3$:

- A car cruising at **120 km/h** requires **27 times more** aerodynamic power than the same car at **40 km/h** — a direct consequence of the cube law.
- This is why **range anxiety** at high speed is so acute for EVs: doubling the speed more than doubles the energy consumed per kilometre.
- Industry design strategies include: **flush door handles**, **underbody panels**, **active grille shutters**, **rear diffusers**, **cambered wheels**, and **rear spoilers** tuned for low drag.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From First Principles to the Drag Equation

We begin with the **control volume** approach. Consider a vehicle of frontal area $A_f$ moving at velocity $v$ through stationary air of density $\rho$.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate intercepted by the vehicle:**

In one second, the vehicle sweeps through a cylindrical column of air of volume $A_f \cdot v$ and mass:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_f \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Momentum flux of incoming air (rate of momentum striking the front):**

$$\dot{p}_{in} = \dot{m} \cdot v = \rho \, A_f \, v^{2}$$

**Step 3 — In an ideal (perfectly streamlined) body**, the air would smoothly close behind the vehicle and recover all its kinetic energy. In that case, force would be zero. In **reality**, flow separates at the rear, leaving a low-pressure wake. The air loses a fraction of its momentum; this loss is captured by the dimensionless coefficient $C_d$ (drag coefficient):

$$F_d = C_d \cdot \rho \, A_f \, v^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Historically, the fluid dynamic community normalized this expression by writing the factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ in front, because the dynamic pressure of a free stream is defined as $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$:**

$$\boxed{\,F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,v^{2}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Units check (essential for KTU board exams):**

$$\bigl[\rho\bigr]\,\bigl[C_d\bigr]\,\bigl[A_f\bigr]\,\bigl[v^{2}\bigr] = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^{3}} \cdot 1 \cdot \text{m}^{2} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^{2}}{\text{s}^{2}} = \frac{\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}}{\text{s}^{2}} = \text{N} \;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$?** It is purely a notational convention in aerodynamics. Physically, the drag is the product of **dynamic pressure** $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ and an **effective drag area** $C_d \cdot A_f$. KTU examiners will not penalize students for writing either form, but $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2$ is the **standard form** expected in the answer key.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Aerodynamic Drag Power

Once the drag force is known, the **instantaneous mechanical power** required to overcome it is:

**Step 1 — Definition of mechanical power** (force times velocity along the line of action):

$$P_d = F_d \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the drag force:**

$$P_d = \Bigl(\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^{2}\Bigr) \cdot v$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponent of $v$:**

$$\boxed{\,P_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,v^{3}\,}$$

This is the **most important** result in the topic, because it reveals the **cubic speed dependence** of aerodynamic power loss.

**Step 4 — Numerical demonstration of the cube law:**

For a representative EV with $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.2$ m²:

- At $v = 40$ km/h $= 11.11$ m/s:

$$P_d = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 2.2 \cdot (11.11)^{3}$$

$$P_d = 0.4043 \cdot 1371.7 \approx 554.6 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 0.55 \text{ kW}$$

- At $v = 80$ km/h $= 22.22$ m/s (double the speed):

$$P_d = 0.4043 \cdot (22.22)^{3} = 0.4043 \cdot 10973.9 \approx 4437 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 4.44 \text{ kW}$$

- At $v = 120$ km/h $= 33.33$ m/s (triple the speed):

$$P_d = 0.4043 \cdot (33.33)^{3} = 0.4043 \cdot 37037 \approx 14978 \text{ W} \;\approx\; 15.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Ratio check:** $\dfrac{P_d(80)}{P_d(40)} = \left(\dfrac{80}{40}\right)^{3} = 8\times$ ; $\dfrac{P_d(120)}{P_d(40)} = 27\times$. This confirms the cube law.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Drag Energy over a Time Interval

For a vehicle cruising at constant speed $v$ for a duration $t$ seconds, the **total energy** dissipated against aerodynamic drag is:

$$E_d = P_d \cdot t = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^{3}\,t$$

If the trip distance $d = v \cdot t$, this can be rewritten in a more useful form for **range estimation**:

$$E_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^{2}\,d$$

Dividing by $d$ gives the **energy per unit distance** (in J/m or Wh/km):

$$\frac{E_d}{d} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for KTU exams:** Energy per kilometre scales with the **square** of speed, not the cube. This is why range estimates must always be qualified with a reference speed (e.g., "WLTP range at 120 km/h is X km").

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A Tesla Model 3 has $C_d = 0.23$, frontal area $A_f = 2.22$ m². It cruises at $100$ km/h on a calm day. Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Convert velocity:**

$$v = 100 \text{ km/h} = 100 \times \frac{5}{18} = 27.78 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Drag force:**

$$F_d = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.23 \times 2.22 \times (27.78)^{2}$$

$$F_d = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.23 \times 2.22 \times 771.6$$

$$F_d = 0.3127 \times 771.6 \approx 241.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Drag power:**

$$P_d = F_d \cdot v = 241.3 \times 27.78 \approx 6703 \text{ W} \approx 6.70 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Energy for a 1-hour trip:**

$$E_d = P_d \cdot t = 6.70 \text{ kW} \times 1 \text{ h} = 6.70 \text{ kWh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A real Tesla Model 3 battery is ~60 kWh. So at 100 km/h, **purely aerodynamic losses** would consume the entire pack in roughly 9 hours of continuous driving. Of course, real losses include rolling resistance, accessories, and inefficiencies, which is why the practical range is shorter than the idealized calculation suggests.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Aerodynamic Drag Functional Architecture

The following block-level diagram maps the **functional architecture** of aerodynamic drag, showing the inputs, governing equation, influencing parameters, output forces/powers, and the design strategies used in modern EVs to mitigate drag.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle In Motion] --> B[Air Mass Encountered]
    B --> C{Velocity Component vrel}
    C --> D[Dynamic Pressure 0.5 rho v2]
    D --> E[Frontal Area Af]
    D --> F[Drag Coefficient Cd]
    E --> G[Drag Force Fd]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Drag Power Pd]
    H --> I[Drag Energy Ed over Trip]
    G --> J[Net Tractive Force Ft]
    I --> J
    J --> K[EV Traction Motor Demand]
    L[Vehicle Shape Design] --> F
    M[Body Surface Finish] --> F
    N[Active Grille Shutters] --> F
    O[Underbody Panels] --> F
    P[Flush Door Handles] --> F
    Q[Rear Diffuser Spoiler] --> F
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#7b1fa2,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#7b1fa2,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#7b1fa2,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#7b1fa2,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#7b1fa2,color:#ffffff
    style Q fill:#7b1fa2,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Diagram Interpretation Guide (For Exam Use)

| Node Group | Meaning | Colour Code |
|------------|---------|:-----------:|
| Vehicle In Motion → Drag Energy | The **causal chain** of aerodynamic drag formation | Blue → Red |
| Vehicle Shape, Surface, Shutters, Panels, Handles, Diffuser | **Design mitigation strategies** used in modern EVs to reduce $C_d$ | Purple |
| Net Tractive Force → Traction Motor Demand | The **final consequence**: motor must supply this power continuously | Green |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for KTU board answers:** When the question asks to "explain how EVs minimize aerodynamic drag," trace the chain in the diagram in reverse: start from the design strategies (purple nodes) and show how each one *reduces* $C_d$, *reduces* $A_f$, or *manages flow separation* — and how the combined effect reduces $F_d$ and the cumulative $E_d$ over a trip.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define aerodynamic drag. State the equation for the aerodynamic drag force acting on a moving vehicle and explain the physical significance of each term. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
Aerodynamic drag is the resistive force experienced by a vehicle due to its motion through air. It arises from the pressure differential across the vehicle body and viscous shearing of air along the surface.

The drag force is given by:

$$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,v^{2}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of air (kg/m³)
- $C_d$ = coefficient of drag (dimensionless), dependent on vehicle shape
- $A_f$ = frontal area of the vehicle (m²)
- $v$ = velocity of the vehicle relative to the air (m/s)

---

### Question A2
**Q: Why is aerodynamic drag considered the dominant resistive force at high vehicle speeds in electric vehicles? Justify with the relevant equation. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
At high speeds, aerodynamic drag dominates because the power required to overcome it scales with the **cube of velocity**:

$$P_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,v^{3}$$

For example, tripling the speed from 40 km/h to 120 km/h increases the aerodynamic power loss by a factor of $3^3 = 27$. This rapid growth, combined with the absence of engine idling losses, makes aerodynamic drag the largest energy consumer in highway EV driving, directly impacting the cruising range.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for aerodynamic drag force acting on a moving vehicle from first principles. Discuss how the drag coefficient $C_d$ is influenced by vehicle shape. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An electric car has a frontal area of 2.3 m² and drag coefficient 0.27. The car travels at a constant speed of 90 km/h on a level road. Air density is 1.22 kg/m³. Calculate:
- (i) The aerodynamic drag force
- (ii) The power required to overcome the drag
- (iii) The total energy consumed in overcoming drag during a 2-hour trip

**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to Section 3.1 above. The full derivation must include:
- (1 mark) Defining the control volume and mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho A_f v$
- (1 mark) Computing the momentum flux $\dot{p} = \rho A_f v^2$
- (1 mark) Introducing the drag coefficient $C_d$ as a dimensionless shape factor
- (1 mark) Writing $F_d = C_d \rho A_f v^2$
- (1 mark) Recasting in standard form with the factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ and dynamic pressure
- (1 mark) Final boxed equation
- (1 mark) Discussion on $C_d$: smooth, rounded, teardrop shapes give $C_d \approx 0.2$–0.3; boxy shapes (SUV, trucks) give $C_d \approx 0.4$–0.8; flow separation at the rear increases $C_d$ dramatically.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Velocity conversion:**

$$v = 90 \text{ km/h} = 90 \times \frac{5}{18} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Drag force (i):**

$$F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,v^{2}$$

$$F_d = 0.5 \times 1.22 \times 0.27 \times 2.3 \times (25)^{2}$$

$$F_d = 0.3787 \times 625 \approx 236.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Drag power (ii):**

$$P_d = F_d \cdot v = 236.7 \times 25 \approx 5917.5 \text{ W} \approx 5.92 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Energy for 2-hour trip (iii):**

$$E_d = P_d \times t = 5.9175 \text{ kW} \times 2 \text{ h} = 11.835 \text{ kWh}$$

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: (a)** With the help of a sketch, explain the various components of aerodynamic drag experienced by a moving vehicle. Discuss at least four design strategies used in modern electric vehicles to minimize this drag. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the aerodynamic drag power requirement for an electric vehicle at three different speeds: 40 km/h, 80 km/h, and 120 km/h. Given: $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.0$ m², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Comment on the cubic relationship between power and velocity. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Components and design strategies (7 marks):**

Components of aerodynamic drag (each carries ~1.5 marks):
- (1) **Pressure drag (form drag):** Caused by flow separation at the rear creating a low-pressure wake.
- (2) **Skin friction drag:** Caused by viscous shearing along the body surface; depends on surface roughness.
- (3) **Internal flow drag:** Air that enters the engine bay, cabin HVAC, and brake ducts.
- (4) **Induced drag:** Drag due to lift generation; minor for ground vehicles.

Design strategies (each ~0.75 mark — minimum four needed):
- **Flush door handles** (e.g., Tesla, Lucid) — eliminate protrusions that disturb laminar flow.
- **Active grille shutters** — close at low speeds to reduce internal flow drag.
- **Underbody panels and diffusers** — smooth the airflow beneath the car.
- **Cambered/closed wheels** — modern EVs use aero wheel covers to reduce wheel-well turbulence.
- **Sloping roofline and fastback design** — manages the rear flow separation zone.
- **Camera-based side mirrors** — replace bulky traditional mirrors (improves $C_d$ by ~0.02–0.03).

**Part (b) — Numerical comparison (7 marks):**

Common factor: $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.0 = 0.3675$

**Speed 1: $v_1 = 40$ km/h $= 11.11$ m/s**

$$P_{d1} = 0.3675 \times (11.11)^{3} = 0.3675 \times 1371.7 \approx 504.1 \text{ W} \approx 0.50 \text{ kW}$$

**Speed 2: $v_2 = 80$ km/h $= 22.22$ m/s**

$$P_{d2} = 0.3675 \times (22.22)^{3} = 0.3675 \times 10973.9 \approx 4032.4 \text{ W} \approx 4.03 \text{ kW}$$

**Speed 3: $v_3 = 120$ km/h $= 33.33$ m/s**

$$P_{d3} = 0.3675 \times (33.33)^{3} = 0.3675 \times 37037.0 \approx 13611.1 \text{ W} \approx 13.61 \text{ kW}$$

**Comment on the cubic relationship:**

$$\frac{P_{d2}}{P_{d1}} = \left(\frac{80}{40}\right)^{3} = 8\times$$

$$\frac{P_{d3}}{P_{d1}} = \left(\frac{120}{40}\right)^{3} = 27\times$$

$$\frac{P_{d3}}{P_{d2}} = \left(\frac{120}{80}\right)^{3} = 3.375\times$$

The aerodynamic drag power increases as the **cube of the velocity**. Doubling the speed increases the power loss by a factor of 8, while tripling the speed increases it by a factor of 27. This is the fundamental reason EVs exhibit a sharp drop in range at high speeds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s.** A surprising number of students plug 90 directly into $v^2$, producing an answer off by a factor of $\approx 7.7$. Always show the conversion $1 \text{ km/h} = \tfrac{5}{18} \text{ m/s}$.
> 2. **Confusing $C_d$ and $A_f$ as having the same units.** They do not. $C_d$ is dimensionless; $A_f$ is in m². The product $C_d \cdot A_f$ has units of m².
> 3. **Mixing up force and power.** Force is in N; power is in W or kW. Power = Force × Velocity, **not** Force × Area.
> 4. **Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor.** A full mark is often reserved for the standard form $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2$. Do not omit the half.
> 5. **Writing $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³ without specifying the conditions.** Mention "at standard sea-level, 15 °C" or "ISA standard" to score the full unit-awareness mark.
> 6. **In the cube-law question, forgetting to comment** on the engineering implication. Just computing numbers is not enough — KTU awards 1–2 marks for the *interpretation*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Master equation:** $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2$ — memorize verbatim with units.
- [x] **Power equation:** $P_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^3$ — the **cube law** is the single most testable insight in this topic.
- [x] **Energy equation:** $E_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^3 t$ or $E_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2 d$ for distance-based estimation.
- [x] **Standard $\rho$:** 1.225 kg/m³ (sea level, 15 °C, ISA conditions).
- [x] **Speed conversion:** $1$ km/h $= 5/18$ m/s $\approx 0.2778$ m/s.
- [x] **Drag coefficient range:** Modern EV: 0.20–0.35; trucks: 0.6–0.8; ideal teardrop: 0.04.
- [x] **Frontal area range:** Sedan EV: 2.0–2.5 m²; SUV: 2.5–3.2 m².
- [x] **Four drag components:** Pressure drag, skin friction drag, internal flow drag, induced drag.
- [x] **Dominance rule:** At speeds above 60–70 km/h, aerodynamic drag is the **largest** energy consumer for an EV.
- [x] **EV design strategies to reduce $C_d$:** Flush handles, active grilles, underbody panels, aero wheels, rear diffusers, fastback roofline, camera mirrors.
- [x] **Range impact:** Energy per kilometre $\propto v^2$ — so doubling the speed **more than doubles** energy consumption per km.
- [x] **Typical exam trap:** Forgetting the $\tfrac{1}{2}$ factor, or plugging in km/h directly into m/s equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rolling Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Rolling Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rolling Resistance** (also termed *rolling friction* or *rolling drag*) is defined as the resistive force opposing the motion of a body (typically a wheel or tire) as it rolls over a surface. It arises primarily due to **non-conservative energy dissipation mechanisms** at the tire–road contact patch, where mechanical work is converted into heat through the hysteresis of the tire's viscoelastic materials and the inelastic deformation of the contact interface.

Mathematically, the rolling resistance force $F_{rr}$ acting opposite to the direction of motion at the tire–road interface is expressed as:

$$F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot N$$

where $C_{rr}$ is the dimensionless **coefficient of rolling resistance** and $N$ is the normal reaction force exerted by the surface on the wheel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Rolling resistance is one of the *three fundamental resistive forces* (alongside aerodynamic drag and grade/climbing resistance) that every electric vehicle powertrain must overcome during tractive effort calculations. It dominates at low-to-medium speeds (typically below 60 km/h) and is a critical parameter in **energy consumption modeling**, **range estimation**, and **battery sizing** for EVs.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pushing a **heavy suitcase on a soft carpet** versus rolling it on a **hard airport floor**. The carpet case feels "sticky" and heavy to push, while the airport floor version glides with minimal effort. Why?

- The **soft carpet** deforms under the suitcase wheels, forming a small rut. As the wheel rolls forward, it has to continuously **climb out** of this depression, losing energy in the process. The carpet fibers also bend and recover slowly, absorbing energy as heat.
- The **rigid airport floor** barely deforms, so the wheel rolls smoothly with negligible energy loss.

This "energy tax" charged by the surface for letting a wheel roll over it is exactly what we call **rolling resistance**. It is *not* friction in the classical sliding sense — the wheel is not slipping. It is the **price paid for deforming the contact patch**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:** Rolling resistance is fundamentally different from *sliding friction*. Sliding friction arises when two surfaces rub against each other without rolling (e.g., a locked brake disc). Rolling resistance arises from **distributed deformation** at the contact zone and is typically **100 to 1000 times smaller** than kinetic sliding friction for the same materials.

## 1.3 Physical Mechanisms Behind Rolling Resistance

Rolling resistance is not caused by a single phenomenon but by the **superposition of several energy-dissipating mechanisms**:

1. **Hysteresis Loss in Tire Materials** (Dominant mechanism — contributes ~80–90% of total rolling resistance for pneumatic tires): Real tire rubber is *viscoelastic*, meaning it exhibits both viscous (liquid-like, time-dependent) and elastic (spring-like, energy-conserving) behavior. When the tire enters the contact patch, it gets compressed; when it leaves, it tries to recover its shape. However, because of the viscous component, this recovery is **incomplete** — some energy is permanently lost as heat. This is called **hysteresis**.

2. **Elastic Deformation of the Surface**: The road or rail deforms under the wheel load, creating a small depression. The wheel must continuously push itself up the leading edge of this depression, doing work against gravity that is not fully recovered on the trailing edge.

3. **Micro-slip at the Contact Patch**: Even an "ideal" rolling wheel experiences small amounts of slip in the contact region due to the curvature difference between the tire and the road. This microslip dissipates energy as heat.

4. **Air Pumping and Sidewall Flexing**: As the tire rotates, the leading edge of the contact patch is constantly being pushed into the road, compressing air trapped in the tire treads. The trailing edge releases this air, creating a small pumping action that dissipates energy.

5. **Bearing Friction**: In the wheel hub, mechanical bearings cause small but non-zero resistive torque. This is usually included in the broader rolling resistance term for system-level analysis.

## 1.4 Significance in Electric Vehicle Design

In an EV context, rolling resistance directly impacts:

- **Energy consumption per kilometer** (Wh/km)
- **Driving range** on a single battery charge
- **Regenerative braking efficiency** at low speeds (rolling resistance is the main reason regen cannot recover 100% of kinetic energy)
- **Tire selection strategy** — low rolling resistance tires can extend EV range by **5–10%**
- **Vehicle mass optimization** — since $F_{rr}$ is proportional to weight, every kilogram of mass removed reduces rolling resistance linearly (unlike aerodynamic drag, which is mass-independent)

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Numerical Ranges (Memorize for KTU):**
> - Bicycle tire on smooth asphalt: $C_{rr} \approx 0.002 - 0.005$
