> - Car tire on smooth asphalt: $C_{rr} \approx 0.008 - 0.015$
> - Car tire on rough concrete: $C_{rr} \approx 0.015 - 0.020$
> - Truck tire on rough road: $C_{rr} \approx 0.020 - 0.030$
> - Steel wheel on steel rail: $C_{rr} \approx 0.001 - 0.002$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Mechanics of Rolling Resistance

To understand rolling resistance at a deep theoretical level, consider a perfectly rigid wheel rolling on a perfectly elastic but lossy surface. The contact between them is not a single point but a **distributed contact patch** with a leading entry point and a trailing exit point.

The total vertical load $W = m \cdot g$ acting downward on the wheel's axle is supported by the surface's distributed normal reaction force across the contact patch. The centroid (center of pressure) of this normal reaction distribution lies *slightly ahead* of the geometric center of the contact patch — closer to the leading edge. This is because the tire material at the leading edge is *being compressed* (stiffer reaction), while the trailing edge is *recovering* (softer reaction).

The horizontal offset between the wheel's geometric center and the centroid of the normal reaction force is called the **pressure offset**, denoted by $a$. This offset is the geometric origin of rolling resistance.

### 2.1.1 The Pressure Offset Model

For a wheel of radius $r_w$ carrying vertical load $W$ and experiencing horizontal tractive force $F_{rr}$ at the axle:

- The normal force distribution across the contact patch is **asymmetric**.
- The resultant normal force $N = W$ acts at a horizontal distance $a$ ahead of the wheel's vertical centerline.
- Taking moments about the contact patch centroid, equilibrium requires a resisting horizontal force at the axle:

$$F_{rr} \cdot r_w = N \cdot a$$

$$F_{rr} = \frac{N \cdot a}{r_w} = \frac{W \cdot a}{r_w}$$

This is the **fundamental physical origin** of rolling resistance. When $a = 0$ (perfectly symmetric contact pressure), rolling resistance vanishes — this is the ideal "no deformation" limit.

### 2.1.2 Definition of the Coefficient of Rolling Resistance

Comparing the result with $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot N$:

$$C_{rr} = \frac{a}{r_w}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation of $C_{rr}$:** The coefficient of rolling resistance is the **ratio of the pressure offset distance to the wheel radius**. A larger wheel or a more symmetric pressure distribution yields a smaller $C_{rr}$. This is why **larger-diameter wheels** are preferred for heavy EVs and trucks — they geometrically reduce rolling resistance.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting the Coefficient of Rolling Resistance

The value of $C_{rr}$ is **not a true material constant** like the coefficient of kinetic friction. It depends on a complex combination of:

| Factor | Effect on $C_{rr}$ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Tire material stiffness** | Softer rubber → higher $C_{rr}$ | Stiffer sidewalls reduce $C_{rr}$ |
| **Tire pressure (inflation)** | Under-inflated → higher $C_{rr}$ | Higher pressure reduces deformation hysteresis |
| **Vehicle speed** | Increases slightly with speed | Important for highway driving efficiency |
| **Road surface roughness** | Rougher surface → higher $C_{rr}$ | Smooth asphalt is optimal for EVs |
| **Temperature** | Warm tire → lower $C_{rr}$ (within limits) | Tire warm-up period affects initial range |
| **Wheel diameter** | Larger wheel → lower $C_{rr}$ | EV manufacturers favor 18"–20" wheels for range |
| **Load on the wheel** | Heavy load → lower $C_{rr}$ per unit | Diminishing returns in heavy vehicles |
| **Tread pattern** | Aggressive tread → higher $C_{rr}$ | Low-rolling-resistance treads save energy |

## 2.3 Rolling Resistance in Vehicle Dynamics

In a complete vehicle dynamics model, the rolling resistance force opposing motion at the tire–road interface is:

$$F_{rr,\text{total}} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

where:
- $m$ is the total vehicle mass (kg)
- $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity ($9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$)
- $\theta$ is the road gradient angle (in radians or degrees)
- $\cos(\theta)$ accounts for the fact that on an incline, only the normal component of weight contributes to the contact normal force

For small inclines (typical urban and highway driving), $\cos(\theta) \approx 1$, so the formula simplifies to:

$$F_{rr} \approx C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$$

### 2.3.1 Rolling Resistance as a Tractive Load Component

The total tractive force $F_{tr}$ required at the wheels of an EV to maintain constant speed $v$ on a flat road is:

$$F_{tr} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

where $F_{ad}$ is the aerodynamic drag force, $\rho$ is air density ($\approx 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at sea level), $C_d$ is the drag coefficient, and $A_f$ is the frontal area.

At **low speeds**, the rolling resistance term dominates. At **high speeds**, aerodynamic drag dominates. The crossover speed $v^*$ can be found by equating the two terms:

$$v^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f}}$$

## 2.4 Power and Energy Required to Overcome Rolling Resistance

The instantaneous mechanical power required at the wheels to overcome rolling resistance at speed $v$ is:

$$P_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot v = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v$$

The energy consumed per unit distance traveled is:

$$E_{rr,\text{per km}} = F_{rr} \cdot 1000 = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 1000 \quad \text{(in Joules per km)}$$

Converting to watt-hours (1 Wh = 3600 J):

$$E_{rr,\text{per km}} = \frac{C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 1000}{3600} \quad \text{(in Wh per km)}$$

This is a critical quantity for **EV range estimation** and appears in the **energy consumption label** of every production electric vehicle.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot N$ | Basic rolling resistance force | N (Newtons) | Fundamental definition |
| 2 | $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | Rolling resistance for a vehicle on a slope | N | Vehicle dynamics |
| 3 | $F_{rr} = \frac{W \cdot a}{r_w}$ | Physical origin via pressure offset | N | Deep understanding |
| 4 | $C_{rr} = \frac{a}{r_w}$ | Coefficient from geometry | dimensionless | Tire design |
| 5 | $P_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v$ | Power to overcome rolling resistance | W (Watts) | Powertrain sizing |
| 6 | $E_{rr,\text{per km}} = \frac{C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 1000}{3600}$ | Energy per km from rolling resistance | Wh/km | Range estimation |
| 7 | $v^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f}}$ | Crossover speed (rolling vs. aero) | m/s | Driving cycle analysis |
| 8 | $N = m \cdot g$ | Normal reaction on flat ground | N | Force diagram |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** In KTU 14-mark problems, students often confuse the formulas for $F_{rr}$ on a flat road vs. on a slope. Always remember: $N = m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ on a slope, not $m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$. The sine component relates to grade/climbing resistance, which is a *separate* force.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The rolling resistance formula is used in:

- **EV range modeling software** (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink vehicle blocksets)
- **Tire industry standardization** (ISO 28580, SAE J1269 — coast-down test methods)
- **Coast-down testing** of road vehicles: the vehicle is brought to a test speed and allowed to coast to rest on a flat road. The deceleration observed is dominated by rolling resistance and aerodynamic drag; by fitting a model, both $C_{rr}$ and $C_d$ can be estimated.
- **Energy labeling regulations** (e.g., EU Tyre Label, which grades tires on rolling resistance from A to G)
- **Trucking and rail freight economics** where even a $0.001$ reduction in $C_{rr}$ saves millions of liters of fuel annually

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rolling Resistance from the Pressure Offset Model

This derivation shows how the simple formula $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot N$ emerges from a free-body diagram of a rolling wheel.

**Step 1 — Setup the Free-Body Diagram**

Consider a wheel of radius $r_w$ loaded with vertical force $W$ at its axle. The wheel rolls to the right at constant speed (no acceleration). The contact patch is the small region where the tire touches the road.

Forces acting on the wheel:
- Vertical load $W$ downward at the axle center.
- Distributed normal reaction $N$ upward across the contact patch.
- A horizontal driving force $F_{d}$ applied at the axle (to keep the wheel rolling at constant speed against rolling resistance).
- A horizontal resistive force $F_{rr}$ at the contact patch (rolling resistance), acting opposite to the direction of motion.

**Step 2 — Vertical Force Equilibrium**

Since the wheel is not accelerating vertically, the sum of vertical forces is zero:

$$\sum F_y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N - W = 0$$

$$N = W = m \cdot g$$

This says: the normal reaction at the contact patch equals the weight of the vehicle (on a flat surface).

**Step 3 — Identify the Pressure Offset**

The normal reaction $N$ is not a point force at the geometric center of the contact patch. It is the **resultant** of a distributed pressure profile that is asymmetric — it peaks slightly **ahead** of the wheel's vertical centerline (i.e., on the side toward which the wheel is moving into the contact zone). Let the horizontal distance from the wheel's geometric center to the centroid of the normal reaction be $a$. This distance $a$ is the **pressure offset**.

**Step 4 — Horizontal Force Equilibrium**

The wheel rolls at constant speed, so horizontal acceleration is zero:

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_d - F_{rr} = 0$$

$$F_d = F_{rr}$$

This is just Newton's first law in the horizontal direction.

**Step 5 — Moment Equilibrium About the Contact Patch**

Take moments about the centroid of the contact patch to eliminate the unknown internal reaction forces. The wheel has no angular acceleration (constant speed, no net torque), so:

$$\sum M_{\text{contact}} = 0$$

The driving force $F_d$ at the axle acts at a perpendicular distance of $r_w$ (the wheel radius) from the contact patch, and creates a counter-clockwise moment (assuming rolling to the right):

$$M_{F_d} = F_d \cdot r_w$$

The vertical load $W$ acts at the axle, but its line of action is offset by the pressure offset $a$ from the contact patch centroid. This creates a clockwise moment:

$$M_W = W \cdot a$$

For rotational equilibrium:

$$F_d \cdot r_w = W \cdot a$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the Rolling Resistance Force**

Substituting $F_d = F_{rr}$ and $W = N$ from the equilibrium equations:

$$F_{rr} \cdot r_w = N \cdot a$$

$$F_{rr} = \frac{N \cdot a}{r_w}$$

**Step 7 — Define the Coefficient of Rolling Resistance**

By convention, we define:

$$C_{rr} = \frac{a}{r_w}$$

So the rolling resistance force becomes:

$$F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot N$$

$\blacksquare$

**Physical Insight from the Derivation:**
- A perfectly rigid wheel on a perfectly rigid surface would have $a = 0$ (no pressure offset) and hence $C_{rr} = 0$.
- A softer tire or a softer road surface increases $a$, raising $C_{rr}$.
- A larger wheel radius $r_w$ reduces $C_{rr}$ for the same $a$ — this is the geometric reason why large wheels roll more easily.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy per Kilometer from Rolling Resistance

This derivation quantifies the energy penalty imposed by rolling resistance per unit distance traveled.

**Step 1 — Work Done Against Rolling Resistance**

When a vehicle travels a small distance $ds$ against a constant resistive force $F_{rr}$, the work done is:

$$dW = F_{rr} \cdot ds$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Force Expression**

Using $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$ for a flat road:

$$dW = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot ds$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Over 1 km**

To find the energy consumed per kilometer, integrate $ds$ from $0$ to $1000$ m:

$$W_{1\,\text{km}} = \int_{0}^{1000} C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot ds$$

Since $C_{rr}$, $m$, and $g$ are constants:

$$W_{1\,\text{km}} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \int_{0}^{1000} ds$$

$$W_{1\,\text{km}} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 1000 \quad \text{(in Joules)}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Watt-Hours**

Since $1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$:

$$E_{rr,\text{1 km}} = \frac{C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 1000}{3600} \quad \text{(in Wh per km)}$$

$\blacksquare$

**Numerical Example (Worked Out for Clarity):**

Consider an EV with:
- Mass $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$
- $C_{rr} = 0.012$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

The rolling resistance force is:

$$F_{rr} = 0.012 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

The energy per km is:

$$E_{rr,\text{1 km}} = \frac{0.012 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1000}{3600} = 49.05 \text{ Wh/km}$$

If the EV has a **60 kWh battery**, the rolling resistance alone (ignoring aerodynamic drag, climbing, and accessory loads) would deplete the battery over:

$$\text{Range}_{\text{only-rolling}} = \frac{60000 \text{ Wh}}{49.05 \text{ Wh/km}} \approx 1223 \text{ km}$$

This unrealistically large number (real EVs get 300–500 km) shows that **rolling resistance is just one of several energy sinks**; aerodynamic drag and auxiliary loads dominate real-world consumption.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Crossover Speed Between Rolling and Aerodynamic Dominance

**Step 1 — Set Rolling Resistance Equal to Aerodynamic Drag**

At the crossover speed $v^*$, the two forces are equal in magnitude:

$$F_{rr} = F_{ad}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Two Expressions**

$$C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot (v^*)^2$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v^*$**

$$(v^*)^2 = \frac{2 \cdot C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f}$$

$$v^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g}{\rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f}}$$

$\blacksquare$

**Numerical Example:**

For a typical mid-size EV:
- $C_{rr} = 0.010$
- $m = 1600 \text{ kg}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $C_d = 0.28$
- $A_f = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$

$$v^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 0.010 \cdot 1600 \cdot 9.81}{1.225 \cdot 0.28 \cdot 2.2}}$$

$$v^* = \sqrt{\frac{313.92}{0.7546}}$$

$$v^* = \sqrt{416.0} \approx 20.4 \text{ m/s} \approx 73.4 \text{ km/h}$$

This means: below ~73 km/h, rolling resistance contributes more to the total resistive force than aerodynamic drag; above 73 km/h, aerodynamic drag dominates. This is why **low-rolling-resistance tires are particularly effective in city EVs** that operate predominantly below this crossover speed.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Rolling vs. Other Resistive Forces

| Property | Rolling Resistance | Aerodynamic Drag | Grade (Climbing) Resistance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | $F_{rr}$ | $F_{ad}$ | $F_{gr}$ |
| **Formula** | $C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | $\frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$ | $m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ |
| **Depends on mass?** | Yes (linearly) | No | Yes (linearly) |
| **Depends on speed?** | Weakly (linear) | Strongly (quadratic) | No (only on slope) |
| **Depends on slope?** | Weakly (via $\cos\theta$) | No | Strongly (via $\sin\theta$) |
| **Dominant at** | Low–medium speed, urban | High speed, highway | Steep inclines |
| **Recoverable via regen?** | Partially (at low speed) | Not directly | Fully (downhill) |
| **Tunable by** | Tire pressure, material, diameter | Vehicle shape, frontal area | Route choice (passive) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Pearl:** A common 7-mark question asks to **derive the total tractive force equation** and identify which term dominates in three driving scenarios: city stop-and-go, highway cruising, and hill climbing. Mastering this matrix is the key to a full-marks answer.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Rolling Resistance in an EV Powertrain

The following diagram shows how rolling resistance enters the EV powertrain energy flow and where it interacts with other vehicle subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Battery[Energy Source: High Voltage Battery Pack]
    Inverter[Power Electronics: Traction Inverter]
    Motor[Electric Traction Motor]
    Trans[Transmission and Final Drive]
    Wheels[Drive Wheels]
    Contact[Tire Road Contact Patch]
    Surfaces[Resistive Force Generation Layer]
    Sensors[Vehicle Dynamics Sensors: Speed IMU Slope]
    BMS[BMS and Range Estimator]

    Battery -->|DC electrical energy| Inverter
    Inverter -->|Three phase AC power| Motor
    Motor -->|Mechanical torque| Trans
    Trans -->|Tractive force at axle| Wheels
    Wheels -->|Rolling motion with pressure offset| Contact
    Contact -->|Energy dissipation as heat| Surfaces
    Sensors -->|Wheel speed and slope data| BMS
    BMS -->|Updates state of charge estimate| Battery

    classDef energy fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
    classDef info fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6,color:#000

    class Battery energy
    class Inverter,Motor power
    class Trans,Wheels,Contact mech
    class Sensors,BMS info
    class Surfaces loss
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **yellow energy block** is the battery (chemical → electrical).
- The **blue power blocks** are the inverter and motor (electrical → mechanical).
- The **green mechanical blocks** are the transmission, wheels, and contact patch (mechanical motion).
- The **purple loss block** represents the energy dissipated by rolling resistance at the contact patch.
- The **red information blocks** close the feedback loop: sensors measure actual vehicle motion, and the BMS uses this data to refine range estimates that account for the rolling resistance energy sink in real time.

## 4.2 Process Flow: Energy Conversion Path with Rolling Resistance Losses

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery Chemical Energy] --> B[Inverter and Motor: 90 percent efficient]
    B --> C[Transmission: 97 percent efficient]
    C --> D[Wheels at Contact Patch]
    D --> E{Energy Distribution at Contact}
    E -->|Useful KE| F[Vehicle Forward Motion]
    E -->|Lost to Rolling Resistance| G[Tire Hysteresis Heat]
    E -->|Lost to Aerodynamic Drag| H[Air Turbulence Heat]
    E -->|Lost to Climbing| I[Gravitational Potential]

    classDef src fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,color:#000
    classDef eff fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000
    classDef split fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000
    classDef use fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6,color:#000

    class A src
    class B,C eff
    class D,E split
    class F use
    class G,H,I loss
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **battery chemical energy** flows through a series of conversion stages, each with an efficiency loss.
- The **efficiency blocks** show the cumulative penalty of electrical-to-mechanical conversion (~87% combined for inverter + motor + transmission).
- The **split block** at the contact patch is the critical juncture: the energy delivered to the road is partitioned into the useful kinetic energy of forward motion and three loss channels — rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and gravitational climbing.
- The **three loss sinks** (purple) all eventually degrade to heat, which is why an EV's range is highly sensitive to driving style and conditions.

## 4.3 Causal Loop: How Tire Pressure Affects Rolling Resistance and EV Range

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TirePressure[Low Tire Pressure] --> TireDeform[Increased Tire Deformation per Revolution]
    TireDeform --> Hysteresis[Higher Hysteresis Loss in Sidewall]
    Hysteresis --> HigherCrr[Increased Coefficient Crr]
    HigherCrr --> HigherFrr[Higher Rolling Resistance Force Frr]
    HigherFrr --> HigherPower[Higher Power Draw from Motor]
    HigherPower --> LowerSOC[Faster Battery State of Charge Decline]
    LowerSOC --> ReducedRange[Reduced EV Driving Range]

    classDef cause fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000
    classDef effect fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000

    class TirePressure,TireDeform,Hysteresis,HigherCrr,HigherFrr,HigherPower cause
    class LowerSOC,ReducedRange effect
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- A **chain of causation** links under-inflated tires all the way to reduced driving range.
- This is why **Tire Pressure Monitoring Systems (TPMS)** are mandated in many regions: even a 0.2 bar pressure drop can reduce range by 3–5%.
- The diagram is a **linear causal chain** with no feedback loops in this simplified version; in a full vehicle model, regenerative braking would add a feedback path (recovering some of the rolling resistance loss when decelerating).

## 4.4 Schematic: Free-Body Diagram of a Rolling Wheel (Logical Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Wheel_System["Wheel Free-Body Model"]
        AxlePoint[Axle Center]
        LoadArrow[Vertical Load W = m times g downward]
        NormalArrow[Distributed Normal Force N upward]
        DriveArrow[Horizontal Driving Force Fd to the right]
        ResistArrow[Rolling Resistance Frr to the left at contact]
        OffsetPoint[Centroid of Normal Reaction]
        OffsetDist[Pressure Offset Distance a ahead of center]
        WheelRadius[Wheel Radius rw]
    end

    AxlePoint --> LoadArrow
    AxlePoint --> DriveArrow
    OffsetPoint --> NormalArrow
    ContactZone[Contact Patch Zone] --> ResistArrow
    AxlePoint --- OffsetDist
    OffsetDist --- OffsetPoint
    AxlePoint --- WheelRadius
    WheelRadius --- ContactZone

    classDef force fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00,color:#000
    classDef geom fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000
    classDef contact fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000

    class LoadArrow,NormalArrow,DriveArrow,ResistArrow force
    class AxlePoint,OffsetPoint,OffsetDist,WheelRadius geom
    class ContactZone contact
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- This is a **logical/functional representation** of the free-body diagram, not a literal geometric drawing (Mermaid does not easily render vector arrows with precise angles).
- The **orange force blocks** are the four forces acting on the wheel during steady-state rolling.
- The **blue geometry blocks** show the key distances: the wheel radius $r_w$ and the pressure offset $a$.
- The **green contact block** is the contact patch where the rolling resistance force originates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In an actual free-body diagram, the line of action of the normal force $N$ is shifted horizontally by the offset $a$ from the wheel's vertical centerline. This asymmetry is the geometric origin of the rolling resistance moment, and the resulting horizontal force at the contact patch is the rolling resistance force $F_{rr}$ that opposes motion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEEET522, Dec 2024 Scheme]**
**Define rolling resistance in the context of electric vehicles. State the formula relating the rolling resistance force to the vehicle's weight and identify the key physical mechanisms responsible for this force.**

**Model Answer:**

Rolling resistance is the resistive force that opposes the motion of a vehicle's tires as they roll over a road surface. It arises primarily from the **hysteresis loss** in the viscoelastic tire material and the inelastic deformation of the tire–road contact patch. The formula is:

$$F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$$

where $C_{rr}$ is the coefficient of rolling resistance (typically **0.008 to 0.015** for car tires on smooth asphalt), $m$ is the vehicle mass in kg, and $g$ is gravitational acceleration.

**Key mechanisms (any two of the following are required for full marks):**
1. **Hysteresis loss in tire rubber** — dominant mechanism; the tire is compressed at the leading edge of the contact patch and recovers imperfectly at the trailing edge, dissipating energy as heat.
2. **Elastic deformation of the road surface** — the road deforms under load, and the wheel continuously climbs a small ramp.
3. **Micro-slip at the contact patch** — small relative motions within the contact zone dissipate energy.
4. **Air pumping and sidewall flexing** — air trapped in tire treads is compressed and released, dissipating energy.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PEEET522, 2024 Scheme]**
**Explain why larger-diameter wheels tend to reduce the rolling resistance of an electric vehicle. Use the pressure-offset model in your justification.**

**Model Answer:**

According to the **pressure-offset model**, the rolling resistance force is given by:

$$F_{rr} = \frac{W \cdot a}{r_w}$$

where $W$ is the vertical load, $a$ is the horizontal offset of the resultant normal reaction ahead of the wheel's geometric center, and $r_w$ is the wheel radius.

Since $a$ typically scales with the size of the contact patch (and hence with the deformation depth) but does **not** scale linearly with the wheel radius, the ratio $a/r_w$ **decreases** as $r_w$ increases. Therefore, for the same vertical load $W$, a larger-diameter wheel produces a **smaller** rolling resistance force.

Equivalently, the coefficient of rolling resistance is:

$$C_{rr} = \frac{a}{r_w}$$

A larger $r_w$ reduces $C_{rr}$ for a given $a$. This is why EVs designed for **maximum range** (such as long-range variants of Tesla, Lucid, and Hyundai Ioniq) typically use larger-diameter wheels with low-profile, low-rolling-resistance tires.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **[KTU ESE Pattern 2024 Scheme – Module 1 Choice Question]**
> *Note: KTU Part B questions on Module 1 carry 14 marks. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – PEEET522, Model ESE Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a free-body diagram, derive the expression for the rolling resistance force acting on a wheel of radius $r_w$ carrying a vertical load $W$. Clearly explain the role of the **pressure offset** in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric vehicle has the following specifications:
- Vehicle mass (including battery and payload): $m = 1800 \text{ kg}$
- Coefficient of rolling resistance: $C_{rr} = 0.011$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Air density: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient: $C_d = 0.29$
- Frontal area: $A_f = 2.3 \text{ m}^2$

Calculate:
(i) The rolling resistance force at constant speed on a flat road.
(ii) The aerodynamic drag force at a cruising speed of 90 km/h.
(iii) The total tractive force required at 90 km/h on a flat road.
(iv) The energy consumed per kilometer due to rolling resistance alone, in Wh/km. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Free-Body Diagram Description (2 marks):*

Consider a wheel of radius $r_w$ rolling to the right at constant speed on a flat road. The forces acting on the wheel are:
- Vertical load $W$ downward at the axle center.
- Distributed normal reaction $N$ upward across the contact patch.
- Horizontal driving force $F_d$ to the right at the axle.
- Horizontal rolling resistance $F_{rr}$ to the left at the contact patch.

The normal force distribution is **asymmetric** — the resultant $N$ acts at a horizontal distance $a$ ahead of the wheel's vertical centerline. This offset is the **pressure offset** and is the geometric origin of rolling resistance.

*Derivation Steps (5 marks):*

**Step 1 — Vertical equilibrium:**

$$\sum F_y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = W = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal equilibrium (constant speed, no acceleration):**

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_d = F_{rr}$$

**Step 3 — Moment equilibrium about the centroid of the contact patch:**

Taking moments (sum equals zero since there is no angular acceleration):

$$F_d \cdot r_w = W \cdot a$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the rolling resistance force:**

$$F_{rr} \cdot r_w = N \cdot a$$

$$F_{rr} = \frac{N \cdot a}{r_w} = C_{rr} \cdot N$$

where $C_{rr} = a / r_w$ is the **coefficient of rolling resistance**.

**Physical Interpretation (1 mark):**

The pressure offset $a$ is non-zero only when there is **asymmetric deformation** at the contact patch — this asymmetry is caused by the viscoelastic (hysteretic) nature of the tire material and the inelastic deformation of the road. For a perfectly rigid wheel on a perfectly rigid surface, $a = 0$ and rolling resistance vanishes.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Rolling resistance force on a flat road (2 marks):**

$$F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$$

$$F_{rr} = 0.011 \cdot 1800 \cdot 9.81$$

$$F_{rr} = 194.24 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Aerodynamic drag force at 90 km/h (2 marks):**

First convert speed to m/s:

$$v = 90 \text{ km/h} = \frac{90}{3.6} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

Then compute:

$$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.29 \cdot 2.3 \cdot (25)^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 0.29 \cdot 2.3 \cdot 625$$

$$F_{ad} \approx 255.2 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Total tractive force at 90 km/h on a flat road (1 mark):**

$$F_{tr} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} = 194.24 + 255.2 \approx 449.4 \text{ N}$$

**(iv) Energy per km from rolling resistance (2 marks):**

$$E_{rr,\text{1 km}} = \frac{C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 1000}{3600}$$

$$E_{rr,\text{1 km}} = \frac{0.011 \cdot 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1000}{3600}$$

$$E_{rr,\text{1 km}} = \frac{194238}{3600} \approx 53.96 \text{ Wh/km}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – PEEET522, Model ESE Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the **various factors** that affect the coefficient of rolling resistance $C_{rr}$ of a pneumatic tire. Explain how each factor can be optimized in an electric vehicle to improve range. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A lightweight urban electric scooter has the following parameters:
- Total mass (rider + scooter): $m = 220 \text{ kg}$
- Tire radius: $r_w = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- Pressure offset (from contact patch analysis): $a = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 0.0015 \text{ m}$
- Cruising speed: $v = 40 \text{ km/h}$
- Road gradient: $\theta = 5°$

Calculate:
(i) The coefficient of rolling resistance.
(ii) The rolling resistance force on the flat road and on the inclined road.
(iii) The mechanical power required at the wheels to overcome rolling resistance at the cruising speed (on the flat road).
(iv) If the scooter's battery has 2.5 kWh of usable energy and rolling resistance accounts for 60% of total consumption, estimate the cruising range due to rolling resistance alone. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Discussion of Factors (7 marks):**

| Factor | Effect on $C_{rr}$ | EV Optimization Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Tire inflation pressure** (1.5 marks) | Higher pressure → less deformation → lower $C_{rr}$ | Maintain manufacturer-recommended pressure; use **TPMS** (Tire Pressure Monitoring System); EVs typically specify higher pressures (2.5–3.0 bar) than ICE vehicles to maximize range. |
| **Tire material composition** (1.5 marks) | Softer rubber compounds with more hysteresis → higher $C_{rr}$ | Use **silica-rich tread compounds** that maintain grip with lower hysteresis; some EV-specific tires use "magic triangle" optimization: low rolling resistance + high wet grip + long life. |
| **Tire width** (1 mark) | Wider tires → larger contact patch → higher $C_{rr}$ | Choose **narrow-section tires** for range-optimized EVs (e.g., Hyundai Ioniq 5 base model uses 235-section tires vs. 255 on performance variant). |
| **Wheel diameter** (1 mark) | Larger wheel → lower $C_{rr}$ (since $C_{rr} = a/r_w$) | For maximum range, select the **largest available wheel diameter** (e.g., 19" or 20" aero wheels on Tesla Model 3 Long Range). |
| **Tread pattern** (0.5 marks) | Aggressive/blocky treads → higher $C_{rr}$ | Use **low-rolling-resistance (LRR) tread patterns** with shallower grooves and continuous ribs. |
| **Speed and temperature** (0.5 marks) | $C_{rr}$ increases slightly with speed and decreases slightly with temperature (within operating range) | Pre-condition tires in cold weather; cruise at moderate highway speeds. |
| **Road surface** (0.5 marks) | Rougher surface → higher $C_{rr}$ | Cannot be controlled by the vehicle, but **route planning software** can prefer smoother roads. |
| **Vehicle load** (0.5 marks) | Heavier load → slightly lower $C_{rr}$ per unit (load-dependent coefficient) | **Mass reduction** through lightweight body materials (aluminum, carbon fiber) is the most effective EV-specific lever. |

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Coefficient of rolling resistance (1.5 marks):**

$$C_{rr} = \frac{a}{r_w} = \frac{0.0015}{0.25} = 0.006$$

**(ii) Rolling resistance force on flat and inclined road (2 marks):**

*Flat road ($\theta = 0°$, $\cos 0° = 1$):*

$$F_{rr,\text{flat}} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(0°) = 0.006 \cdot 220 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1$$

$$F_{rr,\text{flat}} = 12.95 \text{ N}$$

*Inclined road ($\theta = 5°$, $\cos 5° \approx 0.9962$):*

$$F_{rr,\text{slope}} = 0.006 \cdot 220 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \cos(5°)$$

$$F_{rr,\text{slope}} = 0.006 \cdot 220 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.9962$$

$$F_{rr,\text{slope}} = 12.90 \text{ N}$$

(The reduction is small at low gradients; for $\theta = 30°$, $\cos 30° = 0.866$, giving ~13.4% reduction.)

**(iii) Mechanical power at 40 km/h on flat road (1.5 marks):**

Convert speed:

$$v = 40 \text{ km/h} = \frac{40}{3.6} = 11.11 \text{ m/s}$$

Power:

$$P_{rr} = F_{rr,\text{flat}} \cdot v = 12.95 \cdot 11.11 = 143.9 \text{ W} \approx 144 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Range estimate from rolling resistance (2 marks):**

Energy available for rolling resistance (60% of total):

$$E_{\text{rolling}} = 0.60 \cdot 2.5 \text{ kWh} = 1.5 \text{ kWh} = 1500 \text{ Wh}$$

Energy per km from rolling resistance:

$$E_{rr,\text{1 km}} = \frac{C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 1000}{3600} = \frac{0.006 \cdot 220 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1000}{3600}$$

$$E_{rr,\text{1 km}} = \frac{12948}{3600} \approx 3.60 \text{ Wh/km}$$

Range due to rolling resistance alone:

$$\text{Range} = \frac{1500 \text{ Wh}}{3.60 \text{ Wh/km}} \approx 417 \text{ km}$$

(Note: the **total** scooter range will be lower than 417 km because the remaining 40% of battery energy — 1000 Wh — must also cover aerodynamic drag, accessory loads, hill climbing, and inefficiencies in the drivetrain.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** when calculating power or aerodynamic drag. Always check the unit of velocity before substituting into the formula. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per unit error.
> 2. **Confusing $\cos\theta$ with $\sin\theta$** in the rolling resistance formula on a slope. Use $N = m g \cos\theta$ for the normal force; the $\sin\theta$ term is for grade/climbing resistance, which is a *different* force component.
> 3. **Omitting the pressure offset explanation** in derivations. The phrase "pressure offset" or "asymmetric contact pressure distribution" is a high-value term in KTU marking schemes. Simply stating $F_{rr} = C_{rr} N$ without justification will earn only partial marks.
> 4. **Using $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$ in numerical problems** when the question specifies $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. Always use the value given in the problem statement.
> 5. **Forgetting to include units in the final answer**. KTU examiners explicitly look for units (N, W, Wh/km); missing units can cost 0.5–1 mark per sub-question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Rolling Resistance (Module 1, PEEET522)**

**Core Definition:**
- Rolling resistance is a **distributed contact force** opposing the motion of a rolling wheel, arising from viscoelastic hysteresis and contact patch deformation.
- Fundamental formula: $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot N = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$.
- Coefficient $C_{rr} = a / r_w$, where $a$ is the pressure offset and $r_w$ is the wheel radius.

**Key Physical Mechanisms (ranked by contribution for pneumatic tires):**
1. Hysteresis in tire rubber (~80–90% of total)
2. Elastic road deformation
3. Micro-slip in contact patch
4. Air pumping in treads
5. Bearing friction (usually grouped separately)

**Factors That DECREASE $C_{rr}$ (Range-Optimizing Strategies for EVs):**
- ✅ Higher tire pressure (within manufacturer spec)
- ✅ Larger wheel diameter
- ✅ Narrower tire section
- ✅ Stiffer, silica-rich tread compounds
- ✅ Smooth road surface
- ✅ Lower vehicle mass
- ✅ Smooth tread pattern (low-rolling-resistance LRR tires)

**Factors That INCREASE $C_{rr}$:**
- ❌ Under-inflated tires
- ❌ Smaller wheels
- ❌ Aggressive tread patterns
- ❌ Soft, high-grip rubber compounds
- ❌ Rough or sandy road surfaces
- ❌ Cold tire temperature
- ❌ High vehicle mass

**Essential Numerical Facts to Memorize:**
- Typical $C_{rr}$ for car tire on smooth asphalt: **0.008 – 0.015**
- Typical $C_{rr}$ for bicycle tire: **0.002 – 0.005**
- Typical $C_{rr}$ for truck tire: **0.015 – 0.030**
- Pressure offset $a$ for a car tire: **~1–3 mm**
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (use $10$ only if specified)
- Air density at sea level: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Power and Energy Formulas:**
- Power: $P_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot v = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v$
- Energy per km: $E_{rr,\text{1 km}} = C_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot 1000 / 3600$ (in Wh/km)
- Crossover speed with aerodynamic drag: $v^* = \sqrt{2 C_{rr} m g / (\rho C_d A_f)}$

**Comparison With Other Resistive Forces:**
- Rolling resistance: $\propto$ mass, $\propto$ speed (linear), dominant at **low speed**
- Aerodynamic drag: independent of mass, $\propto$ speed², dominant at **high speed**
- Grade resistance: $\propto$ mass, independent of speed, dominant on **steep inclines**

**EV-Specific Insights:**
- Low-rolling-resistance tires can improve EV range by **5–10%**.
- Rolling resistance is **partially recoverable** through regenerative braking at low speeds.
- The pressure offset concept is the **physical origin** of $C_{rr}$ — memorize this for derivations.
- On inclines, use $\cos\theta$ for $F_{rr}$ and $\sin\theta$ for grade resistance $F_{gr}$ — never mix them up.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grading Resistance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚙️ GRADING RESISTANCE — Fundamentals for Electric Vehicles

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Grading Resistance

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grading Resistance (F_g)** is defined as the **component of the effective gravitational force (vehicle weight) acting parallel to the road surface** that opposes (or assists) the forward motion of a vehicle while it traverses a road having a longitudinal slope (gradient/grade).

Mathematically, it is the projection of the vehicle's weight vector along the inclined travel direction. For a vehicle of mass *m* moving up an incline of angle *α* with respect to the horizontal:

$$F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha) \quad [\text{Newtons, N}]$$

where:
- *m* = mass of the vehicle in **kg**
- *g* = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- *α* = angle of inclination (road slope) in **degrees** or **radians**

### 🌄 Real-World Analogy — "The Bicycle on a Hill"

Imagine you are riding a bicycle on a perfectly flat road — you feel only air drag, rolling friction, and your own inertia. Now imagine you reach the bottom of a hill and start pedalling **uphill**. Almost immediately, you feel an extra invisible "push-back" pulling you backwards. **That extra force is Grading Resistance.**

It is literally the **shadow of your weight** falling along the slope. When you go **uphill**, this force is a *hindrance* (resistive); when you go **downhill**, the same force becomes a *helper* (it pulls the car forward — this is precisely why EVs recover energy through **regenerative braking** on descents!).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Grading resistance is a *gravitational body force component*, NOT a friction or aerodynamic force. It exists purely because of the road's geometry, and is independent of velocity, surface roughness, or air.

### 🔑 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

| Term | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|------|--------|---------------|
| Grade (angle) | α | degrees (°) or radians (rad) |
| Grade (percent) | G | % |
| Vehicle Mass | m | kg |
| Gravitational Acceleration | g | m/s² (≈ **9.81**) |
| Grading Resistance | F_g | N (Newtons) |
| Grade Angle Relationship | — | tan(α) = G/100 |

> [!TIP]
> **Road sign convention:** A road sign showing "**8% grade**" means the road rises (or falls) **8 metres vertically for every 100 metres of horizontal travel**. In EV brochures, you will see terms like "**30% maximum gradability**" — this is the steepest incline the vehicle can climb under specific conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧠 The "Why and How" Behind Grading Resistance

To master this topic for KTU examinations, you must understand the **physics of an inclined plane** applied to a moving vehicle. Let us break it down logically:

### 🔹 Step 1 — Decompose the Weight Vector

When a vehicle sits on a flat road, its entire weight (W = m·g) acts **straight down**, perpendicular to the road. There is no component along the road, hence zero grading resistance.

When the road tilts by an angle *α*, the weight vector W can be split into **two perpendicular components** using basic trigonometry:

- **Perpendicular (Normal) Component:** W·cos(α) — pushes the vehicle into the road. This contributes to the *normal reaction force* and influences rolling resistance, but **does NOT** oppose forward motion.
- **Parallel (Tangential) Component:** W·sin(α) — acts **along the road surface**. THIS is the grading resistance. It is parallel to the direction of travel.

### 🔹 Step 2 — Identify Direction of Action

- **Uphill motion (positive grade):** F_g acts **opposite** to vehicle's forward direction → it is a *resistive* force that the traction motor must overcome.
- **Downhill motion (negative grade):** F_g acts **along** the vehicle's forward direction → it is a *propulsive* force (helpful in regenerative braking).
- **Level road (α = 0°):** sin(0°) = 0 → F_g = 0. No grading resistance.

### 🔹 Step 3 — Why This Matters for EVs Specifically

For an **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicle**, a steep hill demands more fuel and more gear shifts. For an **Electric Vehicle**, a steep hill demands more **battery current** and may trigger **thermal limits** on the motor and inverter. Conversely, downhill driving becomes a **free energy source** through regenerative braking — a feature ICE vehicles fundamentally cannot replicate as efficiently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Battery Sizing:** Manufacturers must size batteries to handle worst-case hill climbs (e.g., the Ghat roads in Kerala/Western Ghats).
> - **Motor Torque Selection:** Peak torque of traction motors is often dictated by the requirement to climb a specified maximum gradient from standstill.
> - **Range Estimation:** Range calculators (like the one in Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV dashboards) factor in cumulative elevation gain/loss.
> - **Thermal Management:** Sustained hill climbs are a primary cause of motor overheating in EVs.

---

## 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the table below — at least 70% of KTU numerical questions on this topic use these formulas directly.**

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Special Case |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | Exact grading resistance (any angle) | Always valid |
| 2 | $F_g \approx m \cdot g \cdot \tan(\alpha) = W \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | Small-angle approximation | Valid only when α ≤ 10°–15° |
| 3 | $F_g \approx \dfrac{m \cdot g \cdot G}{100}$ | Grading resistance in terms of % grade | Valid only for small angles |
| 4 | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{G}{100}$ | Conversion from percent grade to angle | Exact |
| 5 | $G (\%) = 100 \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | Conversion from angle to percent grade | Exact |
| 6 | $P_{grade} = F_g \cdot v = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha) \cdot v$ | Power required to overcome grading resistance | v = velocity in m/s |
| 7 | $F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g + F_{acc}$ | Total tractive force (full vehicle dynamics) | F_rr = rolling, F_ad = aero drag, F_acc = inertial |
| 8 | $\alpha_{max} = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{F_{tractive,max} - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{m \cdot g}\right)$ | Maximum climbable grade | At constant speed (a = 0) |

> [!TIP]
> **Unit Conversion Alerts frequently tested in KTU:**
> - Grade % → angle: $\alpha = \arctan(G/100)$
> - km/h → m/s: divide by 3.6
> - Power in kW = (Force in N × Velocity in m/s) / 1000

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📐 Derivation 1 — Grading Resistance from First Principles

We derive $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ rigorously by analysing the free body diagram of a vehicle on an inclined plane.

### Setup

Consider a vehicle of mass *m* parked on (or moving up) an inclined road making an angle *α* with the horizontal. The weight of the vehicle is:

$$W = m \cdot g \quad \text{[acting vertically downward]}$$

### Step 1 — Choose a Convenient Reference Frame

We resolve forces along two axes that are **natural to the road surface**:
- **x-axis:** along the road surface, pointing in the direction of intended motion (up the slope).
- **y-axis:** perpendicular to the road surface, pointing away from the road (upward normal).

### Step 2 — Resolve the Weight Vector

The weight vector W points **vertically downward** (i.e., in the negative global Y direction). We need its components in our road-aligned (x, y) frame.

Using the geometry of the inclined plane:

\begin{aligned}
W_x &= W \cdot \sin(\alpha) \quad \text{(component parallel to road, pointing down the slope)} \\
W_y &= W \cdot \cos(\alpha) \quad \text{(component perpendicular to road, pointing into the surface)}
\end{aligned}

### Step 3 — Identify the Grading Resistance

The component of weight acting **parallel to the road surface in the direction opposing motion** is precisely the grading resistance:

\begin{aligned}
F_g &= W \cdot \sin(\alpha) \\
    &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)
\end{aligned}

**This is the exact, general formula for grading resistance.** ✅

### Step 4 — Derive the Small-Angle Approximation

For small angles (α < ~15°), we can use the Taylor series expansion:

$$\sin(\alpha) \approx \tan(\alpha) \approx \alpha \quad \text{(in radians, for small α)}$$

This gives the **engineering approximation** used in most EV design spreadsheets:

\begin{aligned}
F_g &\approx m \cdot g \cdot \tan(\alpha) \\
    &\approx m \cdot g \cdot \frac{G}{100} \quad \text{where } G \text{ is the percent grade}
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the approximation is acceptable:** Road gradients in practice rarely exceed 30% (≈ 16.7°). At α = 15°, sin(15°) = 0.2588 and tan(15°) = 0.2679 — an error of only **~3.5%**. For KTU problems, unless stated otherwise, you may use the exact sine formula.

---

## 📐 Derivation 2 — Maximum Gradability of an EV

The **maximum grade** an EV can climb is a frequently asked KTU question. We derive it from the tractive force equilibrium.

### Tractive Force Equation

For a vehicle moving at **constant speed** (zero acceleration) on a slope:

$$F_{trac} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g$$

For **maximum gradability**, the vehicle is typically considered to be at low speed (so aerodynamic drag $F_{ad} \approx 0$) and just starting to climb (so rolling resistance may be neglected for simplicity, or retained as a small term).

Setting the **maximum available tractive force** equal to the required force:

\begin{aligned}
F_{trac,max} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha_{max}) + F_{rr} \\
F_{trac,max} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha_{max}) + m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos(\alpha_{max})
\end{aligned}

Solving for the maximum grade angle:

$$\alpha_{max} = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{F_{trac,max} - m \cdot g \cdot C_{rr} \cdot \cos(\alpha_{max})}{m \cdot g}\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Simplified form (assuming F_rr is small and α is small):**
> $$\alpha_{max} \approx \arctan\!\left(\frac{F_{trac,max}}{m \cdot g}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{F_{trac,max}}{W}\right)$$

---

## 📐 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

### Problem

> An electric vehicle of mass **1500 kg** (including 200 kg battery) climbs a road with a **uniform gradient of 12%** at a constant speed of **40 km/h**. Calculate:
> (a) The grading resistance in Newtons.
> (b) The power required to overcome grading resistance alone.
> [Take g = 9.81 m/s²]

### Solution

**Step 1 — Convert percent grade to angle:**

$$\alpha = \arctan\!\left(\frac{G}{100}\right) = \arctan(0.12) = 6.843^\circ$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the exact grading resistance:**

\begin{aligned}
F_g &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha) \\
    &= 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \sin(6.843^\circ) \\
    &= 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.11914 \\
    &= 1753.07 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Verification using the small-angle approximation:**

\begin{aligned}
F_g &\approx m \cdot g \cdot \frac{G}{100} \\
    &= 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.12 \\
    &= 1765.8 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}

> ✅ The approximation overestimates by only **0.7%** — well within engineering tolerance.

**Step 4 — Calculate the power required:**

Convert velocity: $v = 40 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{40}{3.6} = 11.11 \text{ m/s}$

\begin{aligned}
P_{grade} &= F_g \cdot v \\
          &= 1753.07 \cdot 11.11 \\
          &= 19,477 \text{ W} \\
          &= 19.48 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}

### Final Answer

> - (a) **F_g ≈ 1753 N** (exact) or **1766 N** (approximate)
> - (b) **P_grade ≈ 19.48 kW**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🖼️ Diagram 1 — Free Body Diagram of a Vehicle on an Incline (Conceptual Block Representation)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **force resolution and calculation pipeline** for grading resistance, mimicking the standard inclined-plane free body diagram used in vehicle dynamics textbooks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["VEHICLE ON INCLINE\nMass m, Weight W = m x g"] --> B["RESOLVE WEIGHT VECTOR\nW acts vertically downward"]
    B --> C["W sin alpha\nComponent along slope"]
    B --> D["W cos alpha\nComponent perpendicular to slope"]
    C --> E["GRADING RESISTANCE Fg\nOpposes uphill motion"]
    C --> F["ASSISTS MOTION downhill\nEnables regen braking in EVs"]
    D --> G["NORMAL REACTION N\nNo effect on forward motion"]
    E --> H["ADD TO TOTAL TRACTIVE FORCE\nF_total = F_rr + F_ad + Fg + F_acc"]
    F --> H
    H --> I["MOTOR TORQUE REQUIRED\nT = F_total x r_wheel"]
    I --> J["BATTERY POWER DEMAND\nP_batt = F_total x v / eta_drivetrain"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the top with the vehicle's weight on the slope. The weight is split into two components — only the *sin(α)* component causes grading resistance. This force is then summed with rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and acceleration force to give the total tractive force the motor must deliver.

---

## 🖼️ Diagram 2 — Calculation Flowchart for KTU Problem Solving

This block flow shows the **exact decision sequence** to follow when solving any grading-resistance numerical problem in the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["START: Read given data\nm, alpha or G percent, v"] --> S2["Is grade given as percent?"]
    S2 -- YES --> S3["Convert to angle:\nalpha = arctan G divided by 100"]
    S2 -- NO --> S4["Use alpha directly"]
    S3 --> S5["Apply exact formula:\nFg = m x g x sin alpha"]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6{"Is angle small\nless than 15 deg?"}
    S6 -- YES --> S7["Optional check:\nFg approx m x g x G divided by 100"]
    S6 -- NO --> S8["Use exact sin value only"]
    S7 --> S9["Convert velocity to m/s if needed"]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10["Compute power if asked:\nP = Fg x v"]
    S10 --> S11["Convert W to kW if required"]
    S11 --> S12["END: Final Answer with units"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in exams:** Examiners award method marks for showing the *conversion* from percent grade to angle. Skipping this step is one of the most common reasons students lose 1–2 marks even when their final numerical answer is correct.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Module 1 carries 20–25% weightage in the End Semester Exam. Within Module 1, vehicle dynamics (including grading resistance) is a **high-yield area** — expect at least one 14-mark question or two 7-mark sub-parts in every exam cycle.

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define grading resistance. On what factors does it depend?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Grading resistance** is the component of a vehicle's weight that acts parallel to the road surface when the vehicle travels on a slope (incline). It opposes the motion when the vehicle moves uphill and assists motion when moving downhill.
>
> **It depends on:**
> 1. **Mass of the vehicle (m)** — heavier vehicles experience greater grading resistance.
> 2. **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** — a constant (9.81 m/s² on Earth).
> 3. **Angle of inclination (α)** of the road — greater the slope, greater the resistance (sin α relationship).
>
> The governing equation is **F_g = m · g · sin(α)**. Grading resistance is independent of vehicle speed, aerodynamic drag, or road surface texture.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is meant by "percent grade" in vehicle dynamics? How is it related to the angle of inclination?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Percent grade (G)** is a dimensionless measure of road steepness defined as the **rise (vertical climb) per 100 units of horizontal run**. It is mathematically expressed as:
> $$G (\%) = 100 \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$
> where α is the angle of inclination with the horizontal.
>
> **Example:** A road sign indicating "8% grade" means the road rises 8 m for every 100 m of horizontal distance. This corresponds to an angle α = arctan(0.08) = **4.57°**.
>
> Percent grade is the preferred metric in road engineering and automotive specifications (e.g., "**30% maximum gradability**") because it is intuitive and dimensionless.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** for velocity (km/h → m/s) costs **2 marks** almost every time. Always divide km/h by 3.6.
> 2. **Skipping the percent-grade → angle conversion** loses the "method" mark. Examiners want to see $ \alpha = \arctan(G/100) $ explicitly.
> 3. **Using sin(α) for small angles AND tan(α) inconsistently** in the same solution confuses the examiner. Pick one (preferably the exact sine formula) and stick to it.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify units** in the final answer (N, kW, m/s) can cost **0.5–1 mark**.
> 5. **Not mentioning direction of grading force** in conceptual questions — graders expect "uphill: resistive; downhill: assistive" as a complete answer.

---

### ✅ **Question A (Choice 1)** — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the grading resistance experienced by a vehicle moving up an incline of angle α. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electric car of total mass **1800 kg** is travelling at a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a road with a **uniform upward gradient of 8%**. Calculate: (i) the grading resistance, (ii) the power required to overcome this resistance. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. The road is a straight, uniform incline (constant angle α).
2. The vehicle moves at constant speed (no acceleration component along slope).
3. The vehicle's mass is assumed to be lumped at its centre of gravity for force analysis.
4. Air resistance and rolling resistance are neglected in this derivation (only the gravitational component is considered).
5. The road surface is rigid and the wheels are assumed to be rolling without slipping.

**Derivation:**

Consider a vehicle of mass *m* moving up a uniform incline of angle *α*. The weight of the vehicle acts vertically downward:

$$W = m \cdot g$$

This weight vector is resolved into two components along axes parallel and perpendicular to the road surface:

\begin{aligned}
\text{Component parallel to road (opposing motion)} &: W \sin(\alpha) = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha) \\
\text{Component perpendicular to road (into the surface)} &: W \cos(\alpha) = m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha)
\end{aligned}

The component parallel to the road surface, acting opposite to the direction of motion, is the **grading resistance**:

$$\boxed{F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha) \quad [\text{Newtons}]}$$

**Special cases:**
- For α = 0° (flat road): F_g = 0.
- For α = 30° (steep climb): F_g = 0.5 · m · g.
- For a road of percent grade G, using the small-angle approximation:
$$F_g \approx m \cdot g \cdot \frac{G}{100}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- m = 1800 kg
- v = 60 km/h = 60 / 3.6 = **16.67 m/s**
- G = 8% → α = arctan(0.08) = **4.572°**
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**(i) Grading Resistance:**

\begin{aligned}
F_g &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha) \\
    &= 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot \sin(4.572^\circ) \\
    &= 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.07973 \\
    &= 1407.6 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}

**Verification (small-angle approximation):**

$$F_g \approx 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.08 = 1412.6 \text{ N} \quad (\text{error } \approx 0.36\%, \text{ acceptable})$$

**(ii) Power Required:**

\begin{aligned}
P_{grade} &= F_g \cdot v \\
          &= 1407.6 \cdot 16.67 \\
          &= 23,465 \text{ W} \\
          &= \mathbf{23.47 \text{ kW}}
\end{aligned}

---

### ✅ **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch how grading resistance acts on a vehicle moving up and down a slope. Discuss how electric vehicles can exploit downhill gradients for energy recovery. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A delivery EV with a **curb weight of 1200 kg** is fully loaded with **400 kg of cargo**. The vehicle must climb a **3-km long road with a constant slope of 5°** at **50 km/h**. Compute: (i) total grading resistance, (ii) total energy expended against grading resistance, and (iii) the energy that could be recovered by regenerative braking if the vehicle descends the same road with the same load. Take g = 9.81 m/s² and assume 100% regen efficiency for this calculation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Explanation with Forces:**

When a vehicle moves **up** a slope of angle α, its weight *W = m·g* has a component *W·sin(α)* directed **down the slope** — this is the grading resistance, which the traction motor must overcome.

When the vehicle moves **down** the same slope, the same component *W·sin(α)* now acts **along the direction of motion** — i.e., it becomes a *propulsive* force.

**EV-Specific Insight — Regenerative Braking:**

Unlike ICE vehicles that must dissipate this downhill gravitational energy as heat via brakes, EVs use the **traction motor as a generator** during descent. The wheels drive the motor's rotor, which converts kinetic + potential energy into electrical energy, charging the battery. This is called **regenerative braking**.

The energy recovered descending a height *h* is:

$$E_{recovered} = m \cdot g \cdot h \quad [\text{Joules}]$$

Practical regen systems recover **60–80%** of this theoretical maximum due to motor/controller inefficiencies and battery charge acceptance limits. In hilly regions like Kerala's Western Ghats, this can extend EV range by **15–25%** in real-world driving.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:**
- Total mass m = 1200 + 400 = **1800 kg**
- Slope angle α = **5°**
- Distance d = 3 km = 3000 m
- v = 50 km/h = 13.89 m/s
- g = 9.81 m/s²

**(i) Total Grading Resistance:**

\begin{aligned}
F_g &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(5^\circ) \\
    &= 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.08716 \\
    &= \mathbf{1538.9 \text{ N}}
\end{aligned}

**(ii) Total Energy Expended Uphill:**

Since the vehicle moves at constant speed, all the work done against grading resistance is stored as gravitational potential energy:

\begin{aligned}
E_{up} &= F_g \cdot d \\
       &= 1538.9 \cdot 3000 \\
       &= 4{,}616{,}700 \text{ J} \\
       &= \mathbf{4.617 \text{ MJ}} \;(\approx 1.28 \text{ kWh})
\end{aligned}

**(iii) Energy Recovered on Descent (100% regen):**

On the descent, the same gravitational potential energy is available:

$$E_{recovered} = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

where h = d · sin(α) = 3000 · sin(5°) = 261.47 m

\begin{aligned}
E_{recovered} &= 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 261.47 \\
              &= 4{,}616{,}700 \text{ J} \\
              &= \mathbf{4.617 \text{ MJ}} \;(\approx 1.28 \text{ kWh})
\end{aligned}

> ✅ **Result:** In a perfectly efficient system, the descent fully refunds the energy spent on the climb — a beautiful illustration of energy conservation and one of the strongest advantages of EV powertrains over ICE vehicles.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist as your last-minute revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- [x] **Definition:** Grading resistance is the component of vehicle weight parallel to the road on a slope. Symbol: **F_g**.
- [x] **Master formula:** $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ — always preferred for accuracy.
- [x] **Engineering approximation:** $F_g \approx m \cdot g \cdot G / 100$ — valid only for small slopes (α < 15°).
- [x] **Percent grade to angle:** $\alpha = \arctan(G/100)$ — show this conversion explicitly in answers.
- [x] **Direction matters:** Uphill = resistive force; Downhill = assistive (enables regen braking in EVs).
- [x] **Independence:** Grading resistance does **NOT** depend on speed, surface roughness, or aerodynamic shape.
- [x] **Power relation:** $P_{grade} = F_g \cdot v$ — remember to convert v from km/h to m/s.
- [x] **EV advantage:** EVs can recover grading-related potential energy via regenerative braking, unlike ICE vehicles.
- [x] **Vehicle dynamics context:** F_g is one of four tractive force components: $F_{total} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g + F_{acc}$.
- [x] **Maximum gradability:** Determined by the maximum available tractive force vs. weight: $\alpha_{max} \approx \arctan(F_{trac,max} / W)$.
- [x] **Units trap:** Always include N, kW, m/s in final answers — losing units can cost marks.
- [x] **Approximation error:** At α = 15°, sin(α) and tan(α) differ by only ~3.5% — safe to use approximation.
- [x] **Common KTU question type:** "Calculate grading resistance AND power" — practice both parts together.
- [x] **Practical number to remember:** At a 10% grade, α ≈ 5.71°, sin(α) ≈ 0.0995 — handy for quick mental checks.

---

> 🏁 **End of Notes — Grading Resistance (PEEET522, Module 1)**

topics.title: Vehicle Acceleration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Acceleration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of vehicle dynamics applied to **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**, **Vehicle Acceleration** is defined as the time-rate of change of the longitudinal velocity of the vehicle along its direction of travel, expressed mathematically as the ratio of the **net available tractive force** (after subtracting all resistive forces) to the **equivalent effective mass** of the vehicle system.

$$\boxed{a \;=\; \dfrac{F_{\text{trac}} \;-\; F_{\text{roll}} \;-\; F_{\text{aero}} \;-\; F_{\text{grade}}}{m_{\text{eff}}}}$$

Where every term carries SI units (m/s², N, kg). Acceleration is fundamentally a **longitudinal dynamic performance metric** that quantifies how quickly a vehicle can increase its speed, reach a target velocity, or climb a specified gradient.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Vehicle acceleration is the FIRST performance parameter evaluated for any EV design. It links the **prime-mover (electric motor) characteristics** to the **road-load dynamics**, making it the single most important bridge between the electrical sub-system and the mechanical sub-system.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **pushing a loaded shopping cart** across a supermarket parking lot.

- The **push force** you apply with your hands = **Tractive Force ($F_{\text{trac}}$)**
- The **friction of the wheels** on the asphalt = **Rolling Resistance ($F_{\text{roll}}$)**
- The **wind pushing against the cart** as you speed up = **Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{\text{aero}}$)**
- If the parking lot slopes uphill = **Climbing Resistance ($F_{\text{grade}}$)**
- The **weight of the cart plus groceries** = **Effective Mass ($m_{\text{eff}}$)**

Now, if you push **harder than the resisting forces combined**, the cart speeds up — that extra force, divided by the total mass you are pushing, gives you the **acceleration**. This is precisely what an EV's motor does: it delivers torque to the wheels, and any surplus torque over what is needed to overcome road loads becomes *acceleration*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Electric Vehicles specifically:**
> Unlike an Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) that must rev up to build torque, an EV motor delivers **peak torque from zero RPM**. This is why EVs such as the Tesla Model S Plaid, Rimac Nevera, and Tata Tiago EV exhibit breathtaking off-the-line acceleration — the tractive force is **maximal at standstill**, giving the highest possible initial $a$.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Air density (sea level, 25°C) | $\rho$ | **1.225** | kg/m³ |
| Typical rolling resistance coefficient (car tire on asphalt) | $\mu_{rr}$ | **0.010 – 0.015** | dimensionless |
| Typical aerodynamic drag coefficient (sedan EV) | $C_d$ | **0.22 – 0.30** | dimensionless |
| Standard 0–100 km/h benchmark | $V_{100}$ | **27.78** | m/s |
| Equivalent mass factor (rotational inertia correction) | $\delta$ | **1.05 – 1.15** | dimensionless |

---

## 1.4 Definition Box — Key Terms

> [!NOTE]
> **Tractive Force ($F_{\text{trac}}$):** The driving force exerted by the propulsion system at the tire-pavement contact patch, transmitted from the motor torque through the transmission and wheels.
>
> **Road Load Forces ($F_{\text{roll}}$, $F_{\text{aero}}$, $F_{\text{grade}}$):** All non-accelerating resistive forces the vehicle must continuously overcome to maintain motion.
>
> **Effective Mass ($m_{\text{eff}}$):** The vehicle's translational mass *plus* an equivalent lumped mass representing the kinetic energy stored in rotating components (wheels, motor rotor, gearbox shafts).
>
> **Gradeability:** The maximum road gradient (in % or degrees) that a vehicle can climb at a given constant speed without the tractive force being exhausted.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Vehicle Force Balance (Newton's 2nd Law on Wheels)

The dynamic equilibrium of any road vehicle — whether an EV, ICE vehicle, or hybrid — in the longitudinal direction follows a single, elegant scalar equation:

$$F_{\text{trac}} \;=\; F_{\text{roll}} \;+\; F_{\text{aero}} \;+\; F_{\text{grade}} \;+\; F_{\text{inertia}}$$

**Reading this equation like an engineer:**

1. **LHS — Tractive Force ($F_{\text{trac}}$):** What the motor delivers to the road. For an EV, this is computed from motor torque, transmission ratio, differential ratio, wheel radius, and drivetrain efficiency.
2. **RHS — Road Load Forces:** The price the vehicle pays for moving through air, deforming tires, and fighting gravity on inclines. These *must* be overcome before any acceleration can occur.
3. **RHS — Inertia Force ($F_{\text{inertia}}$):** The force *stored* as kinetic energy of both the translating mass and the rotating components. This is the force that *produces* acceleration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A frequent valuation trap is writing $F_{\text{trac}} = m \cdot a$ alone. This is **incorrect** unless road loads are zero (which they never are on real roads). The complete form **must** be used for full marks.

---

## 2.2 Component-by-Component Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{\text{roll}}$)

Caused by the **hysteresis energy loss** in the tire rubber as it deforms under load. For low-speed to moderate-speed EVs:

$$F_{\text{roll}} \;=\; \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

- **Why $\cos(\theta)$?** On a slope of angle $\theta$, the normal force pressing the tire onto the road is reduced from $mg$ to $mg\cos(\theta)$.
- For small angles (urban driving, $\theta < 5°$), $\cos(\theta) \approx 1$, simplifying to $F_{\text{roll}} \approx \mu_{rr} m g$.

### 2.2.2 Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{\text{aero}}$)

Caused by **pressure differential and viscous shear** as the vehicle body pushes air aside. Highly speed-dependent:

$$F_{\text{aero}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot V^2$$

- Quadratic in velocity — this is why high-speed EV efficiency drops dramatically. Doubling the speed **quadruples** the drag force.
- $A_f$ is the **frontal area** (typically 2.0 – 2.5 m² for a sedan).

### 2.2.3 Climbing / Grade Resistance Force ($F_{\text{grade}}$)

Caused by **gravity acting on a mass on an inclined surface**:

$$F_{\text{grade}} \;=\; m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

- For small $\theta$, $\sin(\theta) \approx \tan(\theta) \approx \theta$ (in radians). Road gradient is typically given as **grade %** = $100 \cdot \tan(\theta)$.

### 2.2.4 Inertia Force — Acceleration Force ($F_{\text{inertia}}$)

By Newton's 2nd law, this is the net force producing acceleration, but it includes the equivalent mass of rotating parts:

$$F_{\text{inertia}} \;=\; m_{\text{eff}} \cdot a \;=\; \delta \cdot m \cdot a$$

Where the **equivalent mass factor** $\delta$ accounts for rotational inertia:

$$\delta \;=\; 1 \;+\; \dfrac{J_w}{m \cdot r_w^2} \;+\; \dfrac{J_m \cdot i_g^2 \cdot i_0^2}{m \cdot r_w^2 \cdot \eta_{\text{dt}}}$$

- $J_w$ = wheel moment of inertia, $J_m$ = motor rotor moment of inertia
- $r_w$ = wheel radius, $i_g$ = gearbox ratio, $i_0$ = final drive ratio
- $\eta_{\text{dt}}$ = drivetrain efficiency (~0.90–0.95 for single-speed EV)

### 2.2.5 Tractive Force from Motor (EV-specific)

For an electric drivetrain:

$$F_{\text{trac}} \;=\; \dfrac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_{\text{dt}}}{r_w}$$

Where $T_m$ is the **instantaneous motor torque**. In an EV, this is bounded by the **motor's torque-speed envelope** and the **battery's discharge C-rate limit**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU problem on acceleration uses these equations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Force balance (general) | $F_{\text{trac}} = F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{inertia}}$ | All forces | N |
| 2 | Acceleration | $a = (F_{\text{trac}} - F_{\text{roll}} - F_{\text{aero}} - F_{\text{grade}}) / m_{\text{eff}}$ | $a$ | m/s² |
| 3 | Rolling resistance | $F_{\text{roll}} = \mu_{rr} \, m \, g \, \cos\theta$ | $\mu_{rr}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, C_d \, A_f \, V^2$ | $\rho$, $C_d$, $A_f$ | N |
| 5 | Grade resistance | $F_{\text{grade}} = m \, g \, \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ | N |
| 6 | Inertia force | $F_{\text{inertia}} = \delta \, m \, a$ | $\delta$ | N |
| 7 | Effective mass factor | $\delta = 1 + J_w / (m r_w^2) + J_m i_g^2 i_0^2 / (m r_w^2 \eta_{\text{dt}})$ | $J$ | – |
| 8 | Tractive force (EV) | $F_{\text{trac}} = T_m \, i_g \, i_0 \, \eta_{\text{dt}} / r_w$ | $T_m$ | N |
| 9 | Time to accelerate | $t = m \int_{V_1}^{V_2} dV / (F_{\text{trac}}(V) - F_{\text{load}}(V))$ | – | s |
| 10 | Distance to accelerate | $s = m \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V \, dV / (F_{\text{trac}}(V) - F_{\text{load}}(V))$ | – | m |
| 11 | Gradeability (%) | $\tan\theta_{\max} \approx (\mu_{rr} + F_{\text{trac,max}}/(mg)) \cdot 100$ at low speed | – | % |
| 12 | Power required to accelerate | $P_{\text{accel}} = F_{\text{inertia}} \cdot V = \delta \, m \, a \, V$ | – | W |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV Manufacturer Performance Claims:** A "0–100 km/h in 4.5 s" claim is a *direct* statement of acceleration performance, used as a marketing benchmark against ICE rivals.
- **Battery Sizing:** The required discharge power during hard acceleration dictates the battery's peak C-rate and thermal management budget.
- **Motor Selection:** Peak torque and the shape of the torque-speed curve (constant-torque region vs constant-power region) directly determine achievable acceleration.
- **Regenerative Braking Symmetry:** Understanding the forward acceleration equation is the first step toward reversing it for energy recovery during deceleration.
- **Range Estimation:** Aggressive driving (high acceleration events) penalizes range disproportionately due to cubic velocity losses and high instantaneous power demand.
