# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Form Acceleration for a Constant-Flat Road

### Problem Setup
A vehicle of mass $m$ travels on a **flat road** ($\theta = 0$, so $\cos\theta = 1$ and $\sin\theta = 0$) at constant velocity $V$. We wish to find the instantaneous acceleration if the tractive force is known.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Starting from Newton's second law applied at the wheel:

$$F_{\text{trac}} \;=\; F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{inertia}}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute each road-load term:**

$$F_{\text{trac}} \;=\; \mu_{rr} \, m \, g \, \cos\theta \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2 \;+\; m g \sin\theta \;+\; \delta m a$$

**Step 2 — Apply the flat-road condition $\theta = 0$:**

For $\theta = 0$: $\cos\theta = 1$ and $\sin\theta = 0$.

$$F_{\text{trac}} \;=\; \mu_{rr} \, m \, g \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2 \;+\; 0 \;+\; \delta m a$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the inertia term:**

$$\delta m a \;=\; F_{\text{trac}} \;-\; \mu_{rr} m g \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for acceleration $a$:**

$$\boxed{a \;=\; \dfrac{F_{\text{trac}} \;-\; \mu_{rr} m g \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2}{\delta \, m}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- At **low speeds**, $V^2$ is small, so aerodynamic drag is negligible. Acceleration ≈ $(F_{\text{trac}} - \mu_{rr} m g)/(\delta m)$.
- At **high speeds**, the $V^2$ term dominates, and acceleration drops sharply.
- At **maximum speed** ($a = 0$), the tractive force exactly balances road loads.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time to Accelerate from $V_1$ to $V_2$ (Constant Tractive Force, Neglecting Drag)

### Problem Setup
Assume tractive force $F_{\text{trac}}$ is constant (typical of EV constant-torque region), rolling resistance is small enough to lump with $F_{\text{trac}}$ as a net constant force $F_{\text{net}}$, and aerodynamic drag is negligible. Find $t$ to go from $V_1$ to $V_2$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write Newton's 2nd law with $F_{\text{net}}$:**

$$F_{\text{net}} \;=\; \delta m a \;=\; \delta m \dfrac{dV}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $dt$:**

$$dt \;=\; \dfrac{\delta m}{F_{\text{net}}} \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $V_1$ to $V_2$:**

$$t \;=\; \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{\delta m}{F_{\text{net}}} \, dV \;=\; \dfrac{\delta m}{F_{\text{net}}} \Big[ V \Big]_{V_1}^{V_2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the definite integral:**

$$\boxed{t \;=\; \dfrac{\delta \, m \,(V_2 - V_1)}{F_{\text{net}}}}$$

**Cross-check (worked numerical example):**
- $m = 1500$ kg, $\delta = 1.08$, $V_1 = 0$, $V_2 = 27.78$ m/s (100 km/h), $F_{\text{net}} = 4500$ N.
- $t = (1.08 \times 1500 \times 27.78)/4500 = 9.998$ s ≈ **10 s** for 0–100 km/h. Reasonable for a typical family EV.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Distance to Accelerate from $V_1$ to $V_2$ (Constant Force, No Drag)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Chain rule for $a$:**

$$a \;=\; \dfrac{dV}{dt} \;=\; V \dfrac{dV}{ds}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the force equation:**

$$F_{\text{net}} \;=\; \delta m \, V \, \dfrac{dV}{ds}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange and integrate over distance:**

$$ds \;=\; \dfrac{\delta m}{F_{\text{net}}} \, V \, dV \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; s \;=\; \dfrac{\delta m}{F_{\text{net}}} \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V \, dV$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$s \;=\; \dfrac{\delta m}{F_{\text{net}}} \cdot \dfrac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{s \;=\; \dfrac{\delta \, m \, (V_2^2 - V_1^2)}{2 \, F_{\text{net}}}}$$

**Cross-check:** Using same numbers, $s = (1.08 \times 1500 \times (27.78)^2)/(2 \times 4500) = 138.85$ m. That is the distance covered during the 0–100 km/h sprint.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — General Closed-Form with All Road Loads (Quadratic in V)

When aerodynamic drag is **not negligible**, integration becomes tractable but produces a closed-form involving **inverse hyperbolic functions**. Define:

$$\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{\mu_{rr} m g + m g \sin\theta}{\delta m} \quad\text{(decelerating constant)}$$

$$\beta \;=\; \dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f}{\delta m} \quad\text{(drag coefficient on a)}$$

Then:

$$a(V) \;=\; \dfrac{F_{\text{trac}}}{\delta m} \;-\; \alpha \;-\; \beta V^2 \;=\; A \;-\; \beta V^2$$

where $A = F_{\text{trac}}/(\delta m) - \alpha$.

**Time integral:**

$$t(V_1 \to V_2) \;=\; \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{dV}{A - \beta V^2}$$

**Using the standard integral** $\int dV/(A - \beta V^2) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{A\beta}} \tanh^{-1}\!\left(V\sqrt{\beta/A}\right)$ when $A > 0$ and $V < \sqrt{A/\beta}$:

$$\boxed{t(V_1 \to V_2) \;=\; \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{A \beta}} \left[ \tanh^{-1}\!\left(V_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{\beta}{A}}\right) - \tanh^{-1}\!\left(V_1 \sqrt{\dfrac{\beta}{A}}\right) \right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** For exam problems, the standard approach is to either (i) neglect drag if $V < 30$ km/h, or (ii) explicitly state the assumption. Showing both the simplified form and the rigorous integral demonstrates deep mastery and earns full marks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — EV vs ICE Acceleration Behavior

| Parameter | Electric Vehicle (EV) | Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Peak torque availability | **Available from 0 RPM** | Available only in mid-RPM band (2000–5000 rpm) | EV motor: PMSM/Induction design; ICE: needs turbo spool-up |
| 0–100 km/h typical time | **3 – 8 s** | 6 – 12 s (family sedans) | Constant-torque region of EV is wider |
| Maximum speed | Often lower (160–250 km/h) | Higher (250–300+ km/h) | EV single-speed gearbox limits top speed; ICE has multi-gear |
| Transmission | Single-speed (most EVs) | 6–10 speed automatic/manual | EV motor has wide constant-power region |
| Noise during acceleration | Very low | High (engine + exhaust) | EV has minimal mechanical vibration |
| Thermal stress on power source | Battery heating during hard accel | Engine cooling demands | EV: thermal runaway risk; ICE: well-managed |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vehicle Acceleration Force Flow Architecture

The following block-level architecture illustrates the complete flow of forces from the **battery** through to the **road**, showing how every component contributes to (or resists) longitudinal acceleration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Battery Pack DC Source] --> B[Motor Controller Inverter]
    B --> C[Electric Motor Torque Generation]
    C --> D[T_m Motor Torque Nm]
    D --> E[Transmission Single Speed Gearbox]
    E --> F[Final Drive Differential Ratio i0]
    F --> G[Wheel Rotational Speed]
    G --> H[Contact Patch Tire Road Interface]
    H --> I[F_trac Tractive Force at Road]

    J[Road Surface Normal Reaction] --> K[F_roll Rolling Resistance]
    L[Vehicle Body Cross Section] --> M[F_aero Aerodynamic Drag]
    N[Road Gradient theta] --> O[F_grade Climbing Resistance]
    P[Translating Mass plus Rotating Inertia] --> Q[F_inertia Acceleration Force]

    K --> R[Newtonian Force Balance Node]
    M --> R
    O --> R
    Q --> R
    I --> R

    R --> S[Net Acceleration a m per s squared]
    S --> T[Vehicle Longitudinal Velocity V m per s]
    T --> U[Performance Output 0 to 100 km per h Time]
    T --> V[Performance Output Maximum Speed]
    T --> W[Performance Output Gradeability]
```

---

## 4.2 Acceleration Operating Regimes — EV Motor Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Motor Standstill Zero RPM] --> B[Region 1 Constant Torque Peak T max]
    B --> C[Base Speed omega base]
    C --> D[Region 2 Constant Power Peak P max]
    D --> E[Maximum Speed omega max]
    E --> F[No Acceleration Possible F trac equals F load]

    B --> G[Maximum Acceleration a max High]
    D --> H[Reducing Acceleration Falling]
    F --> I[Zero Acceleration Cruising]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Region 1 (Constant Torque):** From 0 to base speed. Acceleration is at its **highest value** because tractive force is maximum and drag is negligible. This is where the famous EV "instant push" feeling originates.
- **Region 2 (Constant Power):** From base speed to maximum speed. Torque drops as $\omega$ rises (since $P = T \cdot \omega$ is held constant), so acceleration **decays hyperbolically**.
- **At $\omega_{\max}$:** Tractive force equals total road load → no further acceleration possible.

---

## 4.3 Acceleration Time Computation Flowchart (Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Given Vehicle Parameters] --> B[Compute m effective Mass]
    B --> C[Compute delta Equivalent Mass Factor]
    C --> D[Compute F rolling at current V]
    D --> E[Compute F aero at current V]
    E --> F[Compute F grade from theta]
    F --> G[Lookup F trac from Motor Curve]
    G --> H{F net more than zero?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Compute a at current V]
    H -->|No| J[Vehicle Cannot Accelerate Further]
    I --> K[Update V V plus a times dt]
    K --> L{V reach V target?}
    L -->|No| D
    L -->|Yes| M[Record Total Time and Distance]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "vehicle acceleration" in the context of electric vehicles. List the four forces that constitute the longitudinal force balance equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Vehicle acceleration is the **time-rate of change of the longitudinal velocity** of a vehicle along its direction of travel, given by:

$$a = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{F_{\text{trac}} - F_{\text{roll}} - F_{\text{aero}} - F_{\text{grade}}}{m_{\text{eff}}}$$

The **four forces** in the longitudinal force balance are:
1. **Tractive Force ($F_{\text{trac}}$)** — driving force delivered by the motor through the drivetrain to the road contact patch.
2. **Rolling Resistance ($F_{\text{roll}}$)** — resistance due to tire deformation hysteresis.
3. **Aerodynamic Drag ($F_{\text{aero}}$)** — air resistance opposing motion, proportional to $V^2$.
4. **Grade / Climbing Resistance ($F_{\text{grade}}$)** — gravitational component acting along the inclined road surface.

*(1 mark for definition + formula, 2 marks for correctly listing and briefly explaining all four forces.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Why do electric vehicles typically achieve faster 0–100 km/h acceleration than comparable internal combustion engine vehicles of similar mass?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Electric vehicles achieve superior 0–100 km/h acceleration due to the **instantaneous availability of peak motor torque at zero RPM**.

1. **PMSM / Induction motors** used in EVs deliver their **maximum rated torque from standstill** because the electromagnetic field can be established instantly via stator current, without any mechanical build-up.
2. **ICE vehicles**, by contrast, must rev the engine past idle (~800 RPM) into the torque-producing band (~2000–5000 RPM) and often need turbocharger spool-up, introducing significant lag.
3. Additionally, EV **single-speed transmissions** keep the motor in its constant-torque region throughout the 0–100 km/h sprint, whereas ICE multi-gear transmissions have brief torque interruptions during shifts.

Net result: EV tractive force at low speeds is **substantially higher** than the equivalent ICE's, producing a higher $(F_{\text{trac}} - F_{\text{load}})/m_{\text{eff}}$ value and faster acceleration.

*(1 mark per point above. Avoid vague answers like "EVs are faster" without technical justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`**

A four-wheeled electric vehicle has the following specifications:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Total mass $m$ | **1800 kg** |
| Equivalent mass factor $\delta$ | **1.10** |
| Wheel radius $r_w$ | **0.35 m** |
| Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr}$ | **0.012** |
| Aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d$ | **0.28** |
| Frontal area $A_f$ | **2.20 m²** |
| Air density $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Maximum motor torque $T_m$ | **320 Nm** |
| Gearbox ratio $i_g$ | **9.0** |
| Final drive ratio $i_0$ | **1.0** |
| Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{\text{dt}}$ | **0.93** |
| Road gradient $\theta$ | **0° (flat road)** |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate (i) the maximum tractive force available, (ii) the rolling resistance force, and (iii) the aerodynamic drag force at 50 km/h.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Tractive Force:**

$$F_{\text{trac,max}} = \dfrac{T_m \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_{\text{dt}}}{r_w}$$

$$F_{\text{trac,max}} = \dfrac{320 \times 9.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.93}{0.35}$$

$$F_{\text{trac,max}} = \dfrac{2678.4}{0.35} = 7652.57 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{trac,max}} \approx 7653 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Rolling Resistance Force (flat road, $\cos 0° = 1$):**

$$F_{\text{roll}} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta = 0.012 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times 1$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{roll}} = 211.896 \text{ N} \approx 211.9 \text{ N}}$$

**(iii) Aerodynamic Drag at 50 km/h:**

Convert speed: $V = 50 \text{ km/h} = 50/3.6 = 13.889 \text{ m/s}$.

$$F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.20 \times (13.889)^2$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.20 \times 192.91$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.3773 \times 192.91 \approx 72.79 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{\text{aero}} \approx 72.8 \text{ N}}$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the maximum acceleration at 50 km/h on a flat road. Also compute the time required to accelerate from 0 to 50 km/h on a flat road, neglecting aerodynamic drag (assume constant tractive force).**

**Model Solution:**

**Maximum acceleration at 50 km/h:**

$$a = \dfrac{F_{\text{trac,max}} - F_{\text{roll}} - F_{\text{aero}}}{\delta \, m}$$

$$a = \dfrac{7652.57 - 211.896 - 72.79}{1.10 \times 1800} = \dfrac{7367.88}{1980}$$

$$\boxed{a \approx 3.72 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**Time to accelerate from 0 to 50 km/h (neglecting drag, constant $F_{\text{trac}}$):**

Net force (approx): $F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{trac,max}} - F_{\text{roll}} = 7652.57 - 211.896 = 7440.67$ N.

Using $t = \delta m (V_2 - V_1)/F_{\text{net}}$:

$$t = \dfrac{1.10 \times 1800 \times 13.889}{7440.67} = \dfrac{27500}{7440.67}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 3.70 \text{ seconds}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
A 0–50 km/h time of ~3.7 s combined with a peak acceleration of 3.72 m/s² (≈ 0.38 g) is characteristic of a **performance-oriented family EV**. Production vehicles like the Tata Nexon EV or Hyundai Kona EV exhibit similar numbers.

---

### CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]`**

An electric delivery van operates in a hilly city. Its parameters are:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Curb mass $m$ | **2200 kg** |
| Equivalent mass factor $\delta$ | **1.08** |
| $\mu_{rr}$ | **0.015** |
| $C_d$ | **0.35** |
| $A_f$ | **3.20 m²** |
| $\rho$ | **1.20 kg/m³** |
| Maximum motor torque | **350 Nm** |
| $i_g \times i_0$ | **8.5** |
| $\eta_{\text{dt}}$ | **0.91** |
| $r_w$ | **0.38 m** |
| Maximum speed (flat, no accel) | **120 km/h** |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compute the maximum grade (in percent and degrees) the van can climb at a steady speed of 30 km/h. Assume rolling resistance and aerodynamic drag are non-negligible.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute tractive force at 30 km/h (motor is in constant-power region; use rated torque to get peak force):**

$V_{30} = 30/3.6 = 8.333$ m/s.

$$F_{\text{trac,max}} = \dfrac{350 \times 8.5 \times 0.91}{0.38} = \dfrac{2706.725}{0.38} \approx 7123 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute road loads at 30 km/h on a slope $\theta$:**

$$F_{\text{roll}} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta = 0.015 \times 2200 \times 9.81 \cos\theta = 323.73 \cos\theta$$

$$F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \times 1.20 \times 0.35 \times 3.20 \times (8.333)^2 = 0.672 \times 69.43 \approx 46.66 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{\text{grade}} = m g \sin\theta = 2200 \times 9.81 \sin\theta = 21582 \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — At maximum grade with $a = 0$ and constant speed:**

$$F_{\text{trac,max}} = F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{grade}}$$

$$7123 = 323.73 \cos\theta + 46.66 + 21582 \sin\theta$$

$$7076.34 = 323.73 \cos\theta + 21582 \sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Iterative solution. Try $\theta = 18°$:**

- $\sin 18° = 0.3090$, $\cos 18° = 0.9511$.
- RHS $= 323.73 \times 0.9511 + 21582 \times 0.3090 = 307.91 + 6668.84 = 6976.75$ N.

Try $\theta = 18.2°$:

- $\sin 18.2° = 0.3123$, $\cos 18.2° = 0.9500$.
- RHS $= 307.54 + 6739.28 = 7046.82$ N.

Try $\theta = 18.4°$:

- $\sin 18.4° = 0.3156$, $\cos 18.4° = 0.9489$.
- RHS $= 307.18 + 6810.48 = 7117.66$ N (just above).

So $\theta_{\max} \approx 18.4°$ → **Grade ≈ 33.3 %**.

$$\boxed{\theta_{\max} \approx 18.4° \;\; (\text{grade} \approx 33.3\%)}$$

**Note:** A grade of 33% is very steep and is included here as a numerical demonstration; in practice, urban roads rarely exceed 15–20% grade.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**If this van accelerates from rest to 60 km/h on a flat road, neglecting drag and grade, compute (i) the time required and (ii) the distance covered. Assume the tractive force equals the value computed in Part (a).**

**Model Solution:**

$V_1 = 0$, $V_2 = 60 \text{ km/h} = 16.667 \text{ m/s}$.
$F_{\text{trac,max}} = 7123$ N, $F_{\text{roll}} = 323.73$ N (since $\cos 0° = 1$).
$F_{\text{net}} = 7123 - 323.73 = 6799.27$ N.

**(i) Time to accelerate:**

$$t = \dfrac{\delta \, m \, (V_2 - V_1)}{F_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{1.08 \times 2200 \times 16.667}{6799.27}$$

$$t = \dfrac{39600.5}{6799.27} \approx 5.82 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 5.82 \text{ seconds}}$$

**(ii) Distance to accelerate:**

$$s = \dfrac{\delta \, m \, (V_2^2 - V_1^2)}{2 \, F_{\text{net}}} = \dfrac{1.08 \times 2200 \times (16.667)^2}{2 \times 6799.27}$$

$$s = \dfrac{1.08 \times 2200 \times 277.78}{13598.54} = \dfrac{660018.6}{13598.54}$$

$$\boxed{s \approx 48.54 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Vehicle Acceleration Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the rolling resistance term.** Writing $F_{\text{trac}} = \delta m a$ alone (ignoring $F_{\text{roll}}$) loses 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Mixing up $\sin\theta$ vs $\cos\theta$.** Grade force uses $\sin\theta$; rolling force uses $\cos\theta$. This is the #1 conceptual error.
> 3. **Unit conversion mistakes.** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s before applying the drag formula. $V$ **must** be in m/s.
> 4. **Neglecting $\delta$ (rotational inertia).** Many students write $F = ma$ instead of $F = \delta m a$. Full marks require $\delta$.
> 5. **Reporting answers without units.** KTU examiners *will* deduct marks for "7653" without "N".
> 6. **Not stating assumptions.** Always declare: "Neglecting aerodynamic drag" or "On a flat road" before solving.
> 7. **Sign confusion in slope problems.** Uphill grade = positive force opposing motion. Always make sure your equation balances.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Vehicle Acceleration**
>
> - **Master Equation (must memorize):** $F_{\text{trac}} = F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{inertia}}$
> - **Acceleration Formula:** $a = (F_{\text{trac}} - F_{\text{roll}} - F_{\text{aero}} - F_{\text{grade}}) / (\delta m)$
> - **Rolling Resistance:** $F_{\text{roll}} = \mu_{rr} m g \cos\theta$ — use **$\cos\theta$**
> - **Aerodynamic Drag:** $F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V^2$ — use **m/s** for $V$
> - **Grade Resistance:** $F_{\text{grade}} = m g \sin\theta$ — use **$\sin\theta$**
> - **Inertia Force:** $F_{\text{inertia}} = \delta m a$ — always include $\delta$ for rotating parts
> - **Constant Force Time Integral:** $t = \delta m (V_2 - V_1)/F_{\text{net}}$
> - **Constant Force Distance Integral:** $s = \delta m (V_2^2 - V_1^2)/(2 F_{\text{net}})$
> - **EV Advantage:** Peak torque at zero RPM → superior 0–100 km/h performance vs ICE.
> - **EV Limitation:** Single-speed gearbox → typically lower top speed than ICE.
> - **Standard values to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.01$ for car tires.
> - **Always state assumptions** (flat road, no drag, constant force) before solving.
> - **Always include units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct marks otherwise.
> - **Performance Metrics derived from acceleration:** 0–100 km/h time, maximum speed, gradeability.
> - **Power required to accelerate:** $P = F_{\text{inertia}} \cdot V = \delta m a V$ — important for battery sizing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Motor Power from Traction Torque
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Motor Power from Traction Torque**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Traction Torque (T_t):** The torque delivered at the driving wheels of an electric vehicle, which, when reacted against the ground through the tire-road contact patch, produces the **Tractive Effort (TE)** required to overcome resistance forces and accelerate the vehicle.

**Motor Power (P_m)** is the mechanical power developed at the output shaft of the traction electric motor. It is fundamentally related to the **traction torque** through the vehicle's **transmission gear ratio (G)** and the **drivetrain efficiency (η_t)**.

The general energy-flow chain in an EV powertrain is:

$$
\text{Battery} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Inverter} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Motor (Torque, Speed)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Transmission} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Wheels (Traction Torque, Tractive Effort)}
$$

The traction torque at the wheel and the motor torque are linked by the gear reduction ratio. Since the gear reduces speed and multiplies torque, we have:

$$
T_{\text{wheel}} = T_m \cdot G \cdot \eta_t
$$

Where:
- $T_m$ = Motor torque (N·m)
- $G$ = Overall gear ratio (motor:wheel)
- $\eta_t$ = Transmission/drivetrain efficiency
- $T_{\text{wheel}}$ = Traction torque at the wheel (N·m)

The **Tractive Effort (TE)** is then obtained by dividing the wheel torque by the wheel rolling radius:

$$
TE = \frac{T_{\text{wheel}}}{r} = \frac{T_m \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r}
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Gearing" Intuition

Imagine you are cycling up a hill. When you shift to a **low gear (small chainring, large rear sprocket)**:
- Your **pedal torque (motor torque)** stays roughly the same
- But the **wheel torque (traction torque) increases dramatically** because of the gear reduction
- This lets you climb the hill (high force, low speed)

When you shift to a **high gear** on flat road:
- The **wheel torque decreases**
- But the **wheel speed (and hence vehicle speed) increases**
- This is the classic **torque-speed trade-off** governed by constant power

> [!NOTE]
> **The Law of Conservation of Power:** In an ideal (lossless) transmission, the power entering the gearbox equals the power leaving it. So if torque increases by a factor of G, the rotational speed decreases by the same factor of G. This is the **heart** of motor power calculation from traction torque.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Air density (sea level) | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Wheel rolling radius | $r$ | 0.25 – 0.40 m (typical) |
| Overall drivetrain efficiency | $\eta_t$ | **0.85 – 0.95** |
| Conversion: rpm to rad/s | — | $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ |
| Conversion: km/h to m/s | — | $V_{(m/s)} = \frac{V_{(km/h)}}{3.6}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Six Foundational Relationships

The complete chain of relationships for calculating motor power from traction torque follows this hierarchical logic:

**Step 1 — Force Balance at the Tire-Road Interface**

The Tractive Effort (TE) developed at the wheel contact patch must overcome four resistance forces:

$$
TE = F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{acc}}
$$

Where each term is:
- $F_{\text{roll}} = M \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos(\theta)$ → Rolling resistance force
- $F_{\text{grade}} = M \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ → Grade climbing force
- $F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A_f \cdot C_d \cdot V^2$ → Aerodynamic drag force
- $F_{\text{acc}} = M \cdot a$ → Linear acceleration force (plus rotational inertia term $I_w \cdot \alpha / r$)

**Step 2 — Conversion of Force to Wheel Torque**

The traction torque at the wheel is the force at the contact patch multiplied by the wheel's effective rolling radius:

$$
T_{\text{wheel}} = TE \cdot r
$$

**Step 3 — Conversion of Wheel Torque to Motor Torque**

Working backward through the gearbox, the motor must produce a higher speed but lower torque:

$$
T_m = \frac{T_{\text{wheel}}}{G \cdot \eta_t} = \frac{TE \cdot r}{G \cdot \eta_t}
$$

**Step 4 — Conversion of Vehicle Speed to Motor Speed**

If the wheel rotates at $N_w$ rpm and the motor at $N_m$ rpm, the gear ratio relates them:

$$
N_m = G \cdot N_w
$$

The wheel rotational speed is tied to vehicle linear speed through:

$$
V = \omega_w \cdot r = \frac{2\pi N_w}{60} \cdot r
$$

Combining the two:

$$
N_m = G \cdot \frac{60 \cdot V}{2\pi r} = \frac{30 \cdot G \cdot V}{\pi r}
$$

**Step 5 — Mechanical Power at the Motor Shaft**

Power is the product of torque and angular velocity. For the motor:

$$
P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = T_m \cdot \frac{2\pi N_m}{60}
$$

Substituting Step 3 and Step 4:

$$
P_m = \frac{TE \cdot r}{G \cdot \eta_t} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{60} \cdot \frac{30 \cdot G \cdot V}{\pi r} = \frac{TE \cdot V}{\eta_t}
$$

This elegant result shows that the **motor power is the tractive power divided by the drivetrain efficiency**.

**Step 6 — Power at the Wheels (Tractive Power)**

$$
P_{\text{wheel}} = TE \cdot V
$$

This is the actual power delivered to the road.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** The gear ratio $G$ **cancels out** in the power equation! This is because power is invariant under ideal gear reduction (a fundamental conservation principle). The gear ratio only affects the **partition** of power between high-torque/low-speed and low-torque/high-speed regions.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why This Calculation Matters |
|---|---|
| **Motor Sizing** | Determines the rated kW of the traction motor for a target vehicle performance (0-100 km/h time, gradability). |
| **Battery Pack Design** | Continuous power draw determines the battery's C-rate and thermal management. |
| **Thermal Management** | Peak motor power dictates the cooling system capacity of the inverter and motor. |
| **Range Estimation** | Integrating tractive power over a drive cycle gives energy consumption (kWh/km). |
| **Transmission Selection** | Single-speed vs. multi-speed gearbox decisions rely on torque-speed envelope analysis. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities in SI units** unless stated. Symbol $\vert$ denotes absolute magnitude where needed.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $TE = \dfrac{T_m \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r}$ | Tractive Effort from motor torque | N |
| 2 | $P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = \dfrac{2\pi N_m T_m}{60}$ | Motor shaft mechanical power | W |
| 3 | $P_{\text{wheel}} = TE \cdot V$ | Power at the wheels | W |
| 4 | $P_m = \dfrac{P_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_t} = \dfrac{TE \cdot V}{\eta_t}$ | Motor power (full chain) | W |
| 5 | $V = \dfrac{2\pi N_w r}{60}$ | Vehicle speed from wheel rpm | m/s |
| 6 | $N_m = G \cdot N_w = \dfrac{30 \, G \, V}{\pi r}$ | Motor rpm from vehicle speed | rpm |
| 7 | $F_{\text{roll}} = M g C_r \cos\theta$ | Rolling resistance force | N |
| 8 | $F_{\text{grade}} = M g \sin\theta$ | Grade climbing force | N |
| 9 | $F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A_f C_d V^2$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N |
| 10 | $F_{\text{acc}} = M \, a$ | Acceleration force (linear mass) | N |
| 11 | $P_{\text{total}} = V (F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{acc}})$ | Total tractive power | W |
| 12 | $\omega_m = G \cdot \dfrac{V}{r}$ | Motor angular speed (rad/s) | rad/s |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation: From Tractive Effort to Motor Power

We will derive the motor power required to propel a vehicle at velocity $V$ against a total resistance $F_{\text{total}} = F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{acc}}$.

**Starting Premise (Newton's Second Law on a Vehicle):**

$$
M \frac{dV}{dt} = TE - (F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{aero}})
$$

**Line 1 — Power Required to Overcome a Single Force $F_i$ at Speed $V$:**

$$
P_i = F_i \cdot V
$$

This follows directly from $P = F \cdot v$ (the rate of doing work against a force).

**Line 2 — Power at the Wheels (Summing All Resistances + Acceleration):**

$$
P_{\text{wheel}} = V \cdot (F_{\text{roll}} + F_{\text{grade}} + F_{\text{aero}} + F_{\text{acc}})
$$

$$
P_{\text{wheel}} = V \left[ M g C_r \cos\theta + M g \sin\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_f C_d V^2 + M a \right]
$$

**Line 3 — Accounting for Drivetrain Losses:**

The motor must produce *more* power than reaches the wheels, because the drivetrain absorbs some power as heat, friction, and windage. The transmission efficiency $\eta_t$ accounts for this:

$$
P_m = \frac{P_{\text{wheel}}}{\eta_t}
$$

**Line 4 — Expressed in Terms of Tractive Effort:**

Since $P_{\text{wheel}} = TE \cdot V$, we have the elegant result:

$$
P_m = \frac{TE \cdot V}{\eta_t}
$$

**Line 5 — Resolving TE into Motor Torque:**

From the drivetrain kinematics:

$$
T_m = \frac{TE \cdot r}{G \cdot \eta_t}
$$

**Line 6 — Angular Velocity at the Motor:**

$$
\omega_m = \frac{V \cdot G}{r}
$$

**Line 7 — Cross-Verification (Power = Torque × Angular Velocity):**

$$
P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = \left(\frac{TE \cdot r}{G \cdot \eta_t}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{V \cdot G}{r}\right) = \frac{TE \cdot V}{\eta_t} \quad \checkmark
$$

The gear ratio $G$ and wheel radius $r$ cancel out perfectly, confirming the **conservation of power principle**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Standard)

**Problem Statement:**

A passenger electric vehicle has the following specifications:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Vehicle mass $M$ | 1500 kg |
| Wheel rolling radius $r$ | 0.30 m |
| Frontal area $A_f$ | 2.2 m² |
| Drag coefficient $C_d$ | 0.30 |
| Rolling resistance coefficient $C_r$ | 0.012 |
| Overall gear ratio $G$ | 6.5 : 1 |
| Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t$ | 0.92 |
| Maximum vehicle speed $V_{\max}$ | 120 km/h |
| Maximum gradability (sustained) | 30 % grade |

**Required:**
1. The motor torque required at a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a **flat road** ($\theta = 0$).
2. The motor speed (rpm) at the same condition.
3. The motor power required.
4. The motor torque and power required to climb the 30 % grade at a steady 30 km/h.

---

### Solution — Part (a): Constant 60 km/h, Flat Road

**Step 1 — Convert speed to SI:**

$$
V = \frac{60}{3.6} = 16.667 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Rolling Resistance Force:**

$$
F_{\text{roll}} = M \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos(0) = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 \cdot 1
$$

$$
F_{\text{roll}} = 176.58 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Aerodynamic Drag Force:**

$$
F_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A_f \cdot C_d \cdot V^2
$$

$$
F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 0.30 \cdot (16.667)^2
$$

$$
F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 0.30 \cdot 277.78 = 112.31 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Grade and Acceleration Forces (Zero):**

$$
F_{\text{grade}} = 0, \quad F_{\text{acc}} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Total Tractive Effort:**

$$
TE = 176.58 + 112.31 + 0 + 0 = 288.89 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 6 — Motor Torque:**

$$
T_m = \frac{TE \cdot r}{G \cdot \eta_t} = \frac{288.89 \cdot 0.30}{6.5 \cdot 0.92}
$$

$$
T_m = \frac{86.667}{5.98} = 14.49 \;\text{N·m}
$$

**Step 7 — Motor Speed:**

$$
N_m = \frac{30 \cdot G \cdot V}{\pi r} = \frac{30 \cdot 6.5 \cdot 16.667}{\pi \cdot 0.30}
$$

$$
N_m = \frac{3250.005}{0.9425} = 3448.6 \;\text{rpm}
$$

**Step 8 — Motor Power:**

$$
P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m = T_m \cdot \frac{2\pi N_m}{60}
$$

$$
P_m = 14.49 \cdot \frac{2\pi \cdot 3448.6}{60} = 14.49 \cdot 361.05 = 5231.4 \;\text{W} \approx 5.23 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Cross-check using $P_m = TE \cdot V / \eta_t$:**

$$
P_m = \frac{288.89 \cdot 16.667}{0.92} = \frac{4814.6}{0.92} = 5233.3 \;\text{W} \approx 5.23 \;\text{kW} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### Solution — Part (b): 30 km/h on 30 % Grade

**Step 1 — Convert speed and grade angle:**

$$
V = \frac{30}{3.6} = 8.333 \;\text{m/s}
$$

For a 30 % grade, $\sin\theta = 0.30 / \sqrt{1 + 0.30^2} = 0.30 / 1.0440 = 0.2873$, and $\cos\theta = 1/1.0440 = 0.9578$.

**Step 2 — Force Components:**

$$
F_{\text{roll}} = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 \cdot 0.9578 = 169.16 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{grade}} = 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.2873 = 4228.3 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 2.2 \cdot 0.30 \cdot (8.333)^2 = 28.08 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Total TE (steady speed, no acceleration):**

$$
TE = 169.16 + 4228.3 + 28.08 + 0 = 4425.5 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Motor Torque:**

$$
T_m = \frac{4425.5 \cdot 0.30}{6.5 \cdot 0.92} = \frac{1327.65}{5.98} = 222.0 \;\text{N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Motor Speed:**

$$
N_m = \frac{30 \cdot 6.5 \cdot 8.333}{\pi \cdot 0.30} = \frac{1624.94}{0.9425} = 1724.3 \;\text{rpm}
$$

**Step 6 — Motor Power:**

$$
P_m = \frac{TE \cdot V}{\eta_t} = \frac{4425.5 \cdot 8.333}{0.92} = \frac{36872.1}{0.92} = 40,078 \;\text{W} \approx 40.1 \;\text{kW}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Driving Condition | Dominant Resistance | Typical Motor Power Range |
|---|---|---|
| Low speed, flat road | Rolling resistance | 5 – 15 kW |
| Cruise (60 – 80 km/h), flat | Aero + Rolling | 10 – 25 kW |
| Max speed (120 km/h) | Aerodynamic (V³) | 50 – 80 kW |
| Hill climb (6 – 10 % grade) | Grade + Aero | 40 – 70 kW |
| Hard acceleration (0-100) | Acceleration inertia | 80 – 150 kW (peak) |
| Extreme grade (30 %+) | Grade dominates | 60 – 100 kW |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define tractive effort in an electric vehicle. How is it related to the motor torque and gear ratio?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tractive effort (TE) is the net longitudinal force developed at the tire-road contact patch that propels the vehicle forward. It is generated by the motor torque transmitted through the gearbox and reduced at the wheel.

For a vehicle with motor torque $T_m$, overall gear ratio $G$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t$, and wheel rolling radius $r$:

$$
TE = \frac{T_m \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r}
$$

The gear ratio $G$ amplifies the motor torque (torque multiplication), while the drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t$ accounts for losses in the gearbox, differential, and bearings.

*(Keyword: "tractive effort" + formula + 1 line on gear ratio = full 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – December 2023]**
**Derive the relationship between motor power and tractive effort for a rear-wheel-drive electric vehicle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The motor power is the product of motor torque and motor angular velocity. Working through the drivetrain:

$$
P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m
$$

Substituting $T_m = \dfrac{TE \cdot r}{G \cdot \eta_t}$ and $\omega_m = \dfrac{G \cdot V}{r}$:

$$
P_m = \frac{TE \cdot r}{G \cdot \eta_t} \cdot \frac{G \cdot V}{r} = \frac{TE \cdot V}{\eta_t}
$$

Hence, the motor power equals the product of tractive effort and vehicle speed, divided by the drivetrain efficiency.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert km/h → m/s or rpm → rad/s. This is the #1 reason students lose 1-2 marks per question.
> 2. **Missing drivetrain efficiency:** Many students write $P_m = TE \cdot V$ without dividing by $\eta_t$. Always include the efficiency term.
> 3. **Confusing torque and force:** The wheel torque $T_w = TE \cdot r$ is NOT the motor torque. The motor torque is divided by $G \cdot \eta_t$.
> 4. **Neglecting aerodynamic drag** in low-speed calculations (it is small, but should be acknowledged in "complete" solutions).
> 5. **Forgetting gravitational components:** For non-zero grades, both $\cos\theta$ (in $F_{\text{roll}}$) and $\sin\theta$ (in $F_{\text{grade}}$) must be present.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** An electric vehicle of mass **1800 kg** is moving at a constant speed of **80 km/h** on a road with a **4% gradient**. The vehicle has the following parameters:

- Wheel radius $r = 0.32$ m
- Overall gear ratio $G = 7.0$
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t = 0.90$
- Coefficient of rolling resistance $C_r = 0.010$
- Frontal area $A_f = 2.5$ m²
- Drag coefficient $C_d = 0.28$
- Air density $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³

**Calculate:**
(i) The total tractive effort required.
(ii) The motor torque and motor speed (in rpm).
(iii) The motor power required.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the same vehicle, calculate the **additional motor power** required to accelerate from **40 km/h to 80 km/h in 6 seconds** on a flat road. Assume linear acceleration. **Comment on the dominant resistance at high speed.**

**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

### Part (a) (7 marks)

**Given:** $M = 1800$ kg, $V = 80$ km/h, grade = 4 %, $r = 0.32$ m, $G = 7.0$, $\eta_t = 0.90$, $C_r = 0.010$, $A_f = 2.5$ m², $C_d = 0.28$, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Speed and angle conversion:**

$$
V = \frac{80}{3.6} = 22.22 \;\text{m/s}
$$

For 4 % grade: $\sin\theta = 0.04$, $\cos\theta \approx 0.9992$.

**Step 2 — Rolling resistance:**

$$
F_{\text{roll}} = 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.010 \cdot 0.9992 = 176.4 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Grade force:**

$$
F_{\text{grade}} = 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.04 = 706.3 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Aerodynamic drag:**

$$
F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 0.28 \cdot (22.22)^2 = 207.5 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Total tractive effort:**

$$
TE = 176.4 + 706.3 + 207.5 = 1089.7 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 6 — Motor torque:**

$$
T_m = \frac{TE \cdot r}{G \cdot \eta_t} = \frac{1089.7 \cdot 0.32}{7.0 \cdot 0.90} = \frac{348.7}{6.3} = 55.35 \;\text{N·m}
$$

**Step 7 — Motor speed:**

$$
N_m = \frac{30 \cdot G \cdot V}{\pi \cdot r} = \frac{30 \cdot 7.0 \cdot 22.22}{\pi \cdot 0.32} = \frac{4666.2}{1.0053} = 4641.6 \;\text{rpm}
$$

**Step 8 — Motor power:**

$$
P_m = \frac{TE \cdot V}{\eta_t} = \frac{1089.7 \cdot 22.22}{0.90} = 26,901 \;\text{W} \approx 26.9 \;\text{kW}
$$

### Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute acceleration:**

$$
a = \frac{V_f - V_i}{t} = \frac{(80 - 40)/3.6}{6} = \frac{22.22 - 11.11}{6} = \frac{11.11}{6} = 1.852 \;\text{m/s}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Average speed for averaging:**

Use average speed $V_{\text{avg}} = 60$ km/h $= 16.67$ m/s.

**Step 3 — Resistances at average speed (flat road, $\theta = 0$):**

$$
F_{\text{roll}} = 1800 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.010 \cdot 1 = 176.6 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 0.28 \cdot (16.67)^2 = 116.7 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration force:**

$$
F_{\text{acc}} = M \cdot a = 1800 \cdot 1.852 = 3333.3 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 5 — Total TE at average condition:**

$$
TE = 176.6 + 0 + 116.7 + 3333.3 = 3626.6 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 6 — Motor power (additional):**

$$
P_{\text{acc}} = \frac{TE \cdot V_{\text{avg}}}{\eta_t} = \frac{3626.6 \cdot 16.67}{0.90} = 67,153 \;\text{W} \approx 67.2 \;\text{kW}
$$

Subtracting the steady-state cruise power at 60 km/h (≈ 5.2 kW), the **net additional power** is ≈ 62 kW.

**Comment:** At high speeds (above ~80 km/h), aerodynamic drag (proportional to $V^2$ and resulting in power $\propto V^3$) becomes the dominant resistance. At low speeds, rolling resistance dominates. For aggressive acceleration, the acceleration force $F_{\text{acc}} = M \cdot a$ is the largest contributor, and motor peak power must accommodate it.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A three-wheeled electric delivery vehicle has the following specifications:

- Total mass $M = 600$ kg (including payload)
- Wheel radius $r = 0.25$ m
- Single-speed gear ratio $G = 5.5$
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t = 0.88$
- $C_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.35$, $A_f = 1.8$ m²
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³

**Calculate the motor power required to:**
(i) Cruise at a constant **45 km/h** on a level road.
(ii) Climb a **15 % grade** at a constant **20 km/h**.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same vehicle, the motor is rated at **15 kW peak / 7.5 kW continuous**. Determine:
(i) The maximum gradient the vehicle can sustain at **30 km/h** on a level-to-grade transition (assume steady-state).
(ii) The maximum speed attainable on a **flat road** using the continuous power rating. Discuss the role of gear ratio in extending this maximum speed.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

### Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Speed conversions:**

$$
V_1 = \frac{45}{3.6} = 12.5 \;\text{m/s}, \quad V_2 = \frac{20}{3.6} = 5.556 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**(i) Cruise at 45 km/h, flat road:**

$$
F_{\text{roll}} = 600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.015 = 88.29 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 0.35 \cdot (12.5)^2 = 60.21 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
TE = 88.29 + 60.21 = 148.5 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
P_m = \frac{TE \cdot V}{\eta_t} = \frac{148.5 \cdot 12.5}{0.88} = 2109.4 \;\text{W} \approx 2.11 \;\text{kW}
$$

**(ii) 15 % grade at 20 km/h:**

For 15 % grade: $\sin\theta = 0.15 / \sqrt{1 + 0.15^2} = 0.1483$, $\cos\theta = 0.9889$.

$$
F_{\text{roll}} = 600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.015 \cdot 0.9889 = 87.32 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{grade}} = 600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.1483 = 873.0 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 0.35 \cdot (5.556)^2 = 11.89 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
TE = 87.32 + 873.0 + 11.89 = 972.2 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
P_m = \frac{972.2 \cdot 5.556}{0.88} = 6139.4 \;\text{W} \approx 6.14 \;\text{kW}
$$

### Part (b) (7 marks)

**(i) Maximum gradient at 30 km/h with 7.5 kW continuous:**

$$
V = \frac{30}{3.6} = 8.333 \;\text{m/s}
$$

Available tractive power: $P_{\text{wheel}} = 7.5 \cdot 0.88 = 6.6$ kW.

$$
TE_{\max} = \frac{6600}{8.333} = 792 \;\text{N}
$$

Aerodynamic drag and rolling resistance:

$$
F_{\text{roll}} = 600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.015 = 88.29 \;\text{N}
$$

$$
F_{\text{aero}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 0.35 \cdot (8.333)^2 = 26.83 \;\text{N}
$$

Force available for climbing:

$$
F_{\text{grade,max}} = 792 - 88.29 - 26.83 = 676.88 \;\text{N}
$$

Maximum grade:

$$
\sin\theta_{\max} = \frac{676.88}{600 \cdot 9.81} = \frac{676.88}{5886} = 0.1150
$$

$$
\theta_{\max} = \arcsin(0.1150) = 6.60° \approx 11.5\% \;\text{grade}
$$

**(ii) Maximum speed on flat road with 7.5 kW continuous:**

On flat road, $\theta = 0$, $F_{\text{roll}} = 88.29$ N, $F_{\text{aero}} = k \cdot V^2$, where $k = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 0.35 = 0.3859$ N·s²/m².

Power balance: $P_{\text{wheel}} = F_{\text{roll}} \cdot V + k \cdot V^3$

$$
6600 = 88.29 \cdot V + 0.3859 \cdot V^3
$$

Solving iteratively (cubic in $V$): try $V = 22$ m/s (79.2 km/h):

$$
88.29 \cdot 22 + 0.3859 \cdot (22)^3 = 1942.4 + 4108.5 = 6050.9 \;\text{W} \;(\text{too low})
$$

Try $V = 23$ m/s (82.8 km/h):

$$
88.29 \cdot 23 + 0.3859 \cdot (23)^3 = 2030.7 + 4693.6 = 6724.3 \;\text{W} \;(\text{slightly high})
$$

Interpolating, $V_{\max} \approx 22.5$ m/s $\approx$ **81 km/h**.

**Discussion on gear ratio:** A higher gear ratio provides more torque multiplication for hill climbing and acceleration (low-speed, high-torque region), but limits the maximum vehicle speed (since motor has a base/maximum rpm). A lower gear ratio allows higher top speed but compromises initial torque. A **multi-speed transmission** or a **single-speed with optimized ratio** balances both. In EVs, the wide constant-power region of the motor often eliminates the need for multi-speed gearboxes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Print This!):**

- ✅ **Tractive Effort (TE):** $TE = \dfrac{T_m \cdot G \cdot \eta_t}{r}$ — Motor torque × gear ratio × efficiency, all divided by wheel radius.
- ✅ **Power Invariance:** $P_m = \dfrac{TE \cdot V}{\eta_t}$ — The gear ratio and wheel radius **cancel out** in the power chain.
- ✅ **Speed Conversion:** $V_{(m/s)} = \dfrac{V_{(km/h)}}{3.6}$
- ✅ **Motor Speed:** $N_m = \dfrac{30 \cdot G \cdot V}{\pi r}$ (in rpm)
- ✅ **Mechanical Power:** $P = T \cdot \omega$, with $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$
- ✅ **Resistance Forces — Four Total:** Rolling ($MgC_r\cos\theta$), Grade ($Mg\sin\theta$), Aero ($\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_f C_d V^2$), Acceleration ($Ma$).
- ✅ **Dominance Hierarchy:** Low speed → Rolling; High speed → Aero ($V^3$ power); Hill climb → Grade; Launch → Acceleration.
- ✅ **Drivetrain Efficiency:** $\eta_t = 0.85$–$0.95$ — always **denominator** for motor-side calculations.
- ✅ **Units to Watch:** N·m for torque, W (or kW) for power, m/s for speed, m for wheel radius.
- ✅ **Numerical Approach:** Compute $TE$ first → derive $P_m = TE \cdot V / \eta_t$ → cross-verify with $P_m = T_m \cdot \omega_m$.
- ✅ **The "5-Step Method" for any problem:**
  1. Convert all units to SI.
  2. Compute each resistance force separately.
  3. Sum to get $TE$.
  4. Compute $P_m = TE \cdot V / \eta_t$.
  5. (If needed) Back-derive $T_m$ and $N_m$ using $G$ and $r$.
- ✅ **Conceptual Anchor:** Power is invariant across the drivetrain (minus efficiency losses); gear ratio only reshapes the torque-speed envelope.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Electric Vehicle Engineering**, **Numerical Problems** refer to the quantitative analytical exercises used to determine the **Tractive Effort, Resistance Forces, Power Requirements, Energy Consumption, Battery Sizing, and Performance Characteristics** of an electric vehicle under specified operating conditions. These problems form the foundational computational backbone of EV powertrain design and are directly assessable in the KTU University Examination under **Course Outcomes CO1 and CO2** of Module 1.

The core performance parameters that must be computed numerically are:
- **Total Resistance Force** ($F_t$): The sum of rolling, aerodynamic, gradient, and inertial forces opposing motion.
- **Tractive Effort** ($F_{te}$): The force the motor must deliver at the wheel-road interface.
- **Power at Wheels** ($P_w$): The mechanical power required to overcome all resistances.
- **Energy Consumption** ($E$): The electrical energy drawn from the battery for a defined drive cycle.
- **Vehicle Range** ($R$): The total distance achievable per charge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight (Module 1):**
> Numerical problems on **Vehicle Dynamics** (resistance forces, tractive effort) and **Energy Calculations** carry a combined weightage of approximately **40–50%** of the Module 1 marks. Every KTU ESE paper from the 2024 scheme has at least one full 14-mark numerical question from this unit.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pushing a loaded shopping cart up a slope on a windy day. You feel **four distinct resistances**:
1. The **weight of the cart pressing on the wheels** (Rolling Resistance) — like trying to roll a heavy tire on sand.
2. The **wind pushing against you** (Aerodynamic Drag) — like holding your hand out of a moving car window; faster you go, harder it pushes.
3. The **gravity pulling the cart back down the slope** (Grade Force) — the steeper the hill, the more you struggle.
4. The **heaviness you feel when you first start pushing** (Inertia / Acceleration Force) — once moving, it gets easier.

An electric vehicle motor must overcome **all four simultaneously** — the numerical problems in Module 1 train you to quantify exactly how big each force is and how much battery energy is consumed in the process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants (must be memorized for KTU exam):**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Air density at sea level: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$
> - Conversion: $\text{km/h} \rightarrow \text{m/s}$ via multiplying by $\frac{5}{18}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Resistance Forces — Operational Logic

When an EV moves, the motor must push the vehicle against four categories of opposing forces. Each is derived from fundamental Newtonian mechanics, and each has a different dependency on vehicle speed, mass, and road gradient.

**A. Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{rr}$)**
- **Why it exists:** Tires deform as they roll, dissipating energy as heat (hysteresis). On a slope, only the normal component of weight contributes.
- **How to compute:** $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\alpha)$
- **Behavior:** Approximately constant with speed; depends heavily on tire material and road surface.
- **Engineering reality:** For radial tires on asphalt, $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.008$ to $0.015$.

**B. Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$)**
- **Why it exists:** Air molecules must be pushed aside as the vehicle moves. Drag grows with the **square of velocity**, making it dominant at high speeds.
- **How to compute:** $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$
- **Engineering reality:** $C_d$ for modern EVs: Tesla Model 3 = 0.23, Tata Nexon EV = 0.34, Tata Tigor EV = 0.36.

**C. Grade / Climbing Force ($F_g$)**
- **Why it exists:** When road is inclined at angle $\alpha$, gravity pulls the vehicle backward along the slope.
- **How to compute:** $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\alpha)$
- **Alternative (grade %):** If grade is $G\%$, then $\tan(\alpha) = \frac{G}{100}$ and $\sin(\alpha) = \frac{G}{\sqrt{10000 + G^2}}$.
- **Engineering reality:** A 10% grade means $\alpha \approx 5.71°$, and $\sin(\alpha) \approx 0.0995$.

**D. Acceleration / Inertial Force ($F_a$)**
- **Why it exists:** Newton's second law — accelerating mass requires a net force. Also includes rotational inertia of rotating components.
- **How to compute:** $F_a = m \cdot (1 + \delta) \cdot a$
- **Where:** $\delta$ = mass factor (typically $0.05$ to $0.15$) accounting for rotating parts; $a$ = linear acceleration in $\text{m/s}^2$.

### 2.2 Total Tractive Effort and Power

The motor must deliver a total **Tractive Effort** ($F_{te}$) equal to the sum of all four forces. For cruising at constant speed on a level road, $F_a = 0$.

$$F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g + F_a$$

The mechanical power at the wheels is the product of tractive force and vehicle velocity:

$$P_w = F_{te} \cdot v$$

The battery must supply more power to account for drivetrain losses:

$$P_{battery} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{total}}$$

where $\eta_{total} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission} \cdot \eta_{inverter}$ (typically $0.85$ to $0.92$ for a modern EV).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Dependencies |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Rolling Resistance | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha$ | N | Mass, tire, road |
| 2 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$ | N | $v^2$, frontal area |
| 3 | Grade Force | $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$ | N | Slope angle |
| 4 | Acceleration Force | $F_a = m \cdot (1 + \delta) \cdot a$ | N | Acceleration |
| 5 | Total Tractive Effort | $F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g + F_a$ | N | Sum of all |
| 6 | Power at Wheels | $P_w = F_{te} \cdot v$ | W | Force and speed |
| 7 | Battery Power | $P_b = P_w \,/\, \eta_{total}$ | W | Includes losses |
| 8 | Energy (constant P) | $E = P_b \cdot t$ | Wh or kWh | Time-integrated |
| 9 | Energy (variable P) | $E = \int P_b(t)\,dt$ | Wh or kWh | Drive cycle |
| 10 | Vehicle Range | $R = E_{bat} \cdot \eta_{total} \,/\, E_{km}$ | km | Battery / consumption |
| 11 | Grade % to angle | $\sin\alpha = G \,/\, \sqrt{10000 + G^2}$ | dimensionless | Grade conversion |
| 12 | Speed conversion | $v \text{ (m/s)} = v \text{ (km/h)} \times 5/18$ | m/s | Unit conversion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> These equations are used in **MATLAB/Simulink ADVISOR modeling, ANSYS Fluent CFD validation, AVL Cruise vehicle simulation, and OEM design studios** (Tata Motors, Mahindra Electric, Ather Energy, Ola Electric) for predicting the WLTP-rated range of every Indian-market EV before prototyping. A KTU graduate entering the EV workforce will use these exact equations in their first industry project.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solved Numerical Problems — KTU Board Pattern (Full Working Shown)**
> All four problems below are calibrated to the **difficulty level of KTU 2024 Scheme University Examination questions** and follow the exact 14-mark valuation scheme (assumptions → formulae → substitution → numerical evaluation → result with units → interpretation).

---

### 📘 Problem 1 — Total Tractive Effort at Cruise (7-Mark Standard)

**Question:** An electric car has the following specifications:
- Total mass (including passengers): $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$
- Frontal area: $A_f = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$
- Drag coefficient: $C_d = 0.30$
- Rolling resistance coefficient: $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$
- Vehicle speed: $v = 90 \text{ km/h}$ on a **level road** (no grade, constant speed)

Calculate the **total tractive effort** required at the wheels.

**Step 1 — List assumptions and given data**

> Assume level road ($\alpha = 0$, so $\sin\alpha = 0$, $\cos\alpha = 1$)
> Constant speed cruise, so $a = 0$ (no acceleration force)
> Standard atmosphere: $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 2 — Convert speed to SI units**

$$v = 90 \text{ km/h} = 90 \times \frac{5}{18} = 25.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Rolling Resistance Force**

$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha$$

$$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times \cos(0°)$$

$$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.0$$

$$F_{rr} = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Aerodynamic Drag Force**

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (25.0)^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 625$$

$$F_{ad} = 252.66 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Grade and Acceleration Forces**

$$F_g = 0 \text{ N (level road)}$$

$$F_a = 0 \text{ N (constant speed)}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Total Tractive Effort**

$$F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g + F_a$$

$$F_{te} = 176.58 + 252.66 + 0 + 0$$

$$\boxed{F_{te} = 429.24 \text{ N}}$$

**Interpretation:** At 90 km/h cruise on a level road, the motor must push with **~429 N** — equivalent to lifting a 43 kg bag. Notice that **aerodynamic drag (252.66 N) > rolling resistance (176.58 N)** at this speed, confirming that for EVs above ~60 km/h, drag dominates.

---

### 📘 Problem 2 — Power Required and Battery Sizing for a Trip (Full 14-Mark Pattern)

**Question:** An EV with the specifications of Problem 1 must travel a distance of **120 km** at a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a **level road**. The overall drivetrain efficiency from battery to wheels is $\eta_{total} = 0.88$. Calculate:
**(a)** The power required at the wheels and the power drawn from the battery.
**(b)** The total energy consumed from the battery for the trip, in kWh.

**Step 1 — Convert speed to SI**

$$v = 60 \text{ km/h} = 60 \times \frac{5}{18} = 16.667 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Rolling Resistance**

$$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \times 1.0 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Aerodynamic Drag**

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (16.667)^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times 277.78$$

$$F_{ad} = 112.30 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Total Tractive Effort (level, constant speed)**

$$F_{te} = 176.58 + 112.30 + 0 + 0 = 288.88 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5(a) — Power at Wheels**

$$P_w = F_{te} \cdot v$$

$$P_w = 288.88 \times 16.667 = 4814.7 \text{ W} \approx 4.81 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6(a) — Power Drawn from Battery**

$$P_b = \frac{P_w}{\eta_{total}} = \frac{4.8147}{0.88}$$

$$\boxed{P_b = 5.471 \text{ kW}}$$

**Step 7 — Time for the Trip**

$$t = \frac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}} = \frac{120 \text{ km}}{60 \text{ km/h}} = 2.0 \text{ h}$$

**Step 8(b) — Total Energy from Battery**

$$E_{battery} = P_b \times t$$

$$E_{battery} = 5.471 \text{ kW} \times 2.0 \text{ h}$$

$$\boxed{E_{battery} = 10.94 \text{ kWh}}$$

**Interpretation:** A 120 km trip on a level road at 60 km/h requires only **~11 kWh** of battery energy — close to the **10.8 kWh** usable capacity of a **Tata Tiago EV**, demonstrating why city EVs are highly energy-efficient.

---

### 📘 Problem 3 — Grade Climbing Ability (7-Mark Standard)

**Question:** A delivery EV has a total mass of $m = 800 \text{ kg}$ (payload + vehicle). The motor can deliver a maximum tractive force of $F_{te,max} = 2400 \text{ N}$. The vehicle is climbing a road with grade $G = 20\%$ at a constant speed of $v = 30 \text{ km/h}$. Take $C_d = 0.35$, $A_f = 2.0 \text{ m}^2$, $\mu_{rr} = 0.015$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

Determine whether the motor can sustain this climb, and if not, find the **maximum grade** the vehicle can climb at this speed.

**Step 1 — Convert speed to SI**

$$v = 30 \text{ km/h} = 30 \times \frac{5}{18} = 8.333 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert 20% grade to angle and compute $\sin\alpha$, $\cos\alpha$**

For $G = 20\%$, slope angle $\alpha = \arctan(0.20) = 11.31°$

$$\sin\alpha = \frac{20}{\sqrt{10000 + 400}} = \frac{20}{101.98} = 0.1961$$

$$\cos\alpha = \frac{100}{101.98} = 0.9806$$

**Step 3 — Compute Rolling Resistance**

$$F_{rr} = 0.015 \times 800 \times 9.81 \times 0.9806$$

$$F_{rr} = 115.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Aerodynamic Drag**

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 2.0 \times (8.333)^2$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.35 \times 2.0 \times 69.44$$

$$F_{ad} = 29.77 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Grade Force at 20%**

$$F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha = 800 \times 9.81 \times 0.1961 = 1538.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6 — Total Force Required at 20% grade**

$$F_{te,required} = 115.4 + 29.77 + 1538.5 = 1683.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7 — Comparison with Motor Capacity**

Since $F_{te,required} = 1683.7 \text{ N} < F_{te,max} = 2400 \text{ N}$, the motor **CAN** sustain the 20% climb at 30 km/h. ✅

**Step 8 — Find Maximum Grade**

Maximum grade occurs when $F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g = F_{te,max}$. Since $F_g$ is the dominant grade-dependent term and depends on $\alpha$ (and so does $F_{rr}$ through $\cos\alpha$), we solve:

$$F_{te,max} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha_{max} + F_{ad} + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha_{max}$$

$$2400 = (0.015)(800)(9.81)\cos\alpha_{max} + 29.77 + (800)(9.81)\sin\alpha_{max}$$

$$2400 - 29.77 = 117.72 \cos\alpha_{max} + 7848 \sin\alpha_{max}$$

$$2370.23 = 117.72 \cos\alpha_{max} + 7848 \sin\alpha_{max}$$

**Approximation method (neglect $\cos\alpha$ variation, as it is nearly 1):**

$$\sin\alpha_{max} \approx \frac{2370.23}{7848} = 0.3020$$

$$\alpha_{max} = \arcsin(0.3020) = 17.58°$$

$$G_{max}\% = 100 \times \tan(17.58°) = 100 \times 0.317 = 31.7\%$$

$$\boxed{G_{max} \approx 31.7\% \text{ grade}}$$

**Interpretation:** This EV can climb grades up to ~32%, which is comparable to commercial three-wheeler EVs (Mahindra Treo, Euler HiLoad) used in hilly regions like Kerala's Western Ghats.

---

### 📘 Problem 4 — Acceleration Performance & Energy Use (7-Mark Standard)

**Question:** A sports EV has $m = 1800 \text{ kg}$, $C_d = 0.28$, $A_f = 2.1 \text{ m}^2$, $\mu_{rr} = 0.010$, mass factor $\delta = 0.10$. The motor delivers a constant tractive force of $F_{te} = 6000 \text{ N}$ on a level road. Starting from rest, calculate:
**(a)** The acceleration produced.
**(b)** The time to reach 60 km/h and distance covered.
**(c)** The total energy delivered at the wheels during this acceleration phase (assume $F_{ad}$ negligible at low speeds).

**Step 1 — At low speeds, neglect $F_{ad}$ and $F_g$ (level road)**

$$F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_a$$

But $F_{rr}$ depends on speed only weakly — at 30 km/h average: $F_{rr} \approx 0.010 \times 1800 \times 9.81 = 176.6 \text{ N}$

**Step 2(a) — Compute Acceleration**

Net accelerating force: $F_{net} = F_{te} - F_{rr} = 6000 - 176.6 = 5823.4 \text{ N}$

$$a = \frac{F_{net}}{m(1+\delta)} = \frac{5823.4}{1800 \times 1.10} = \frac{5823.4}{1980}$$

$$\boxed{a = 2.94 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**Step 3(b) — Time to Reach 60 km/h**

Final speed: $v_f = 60 \text{ km/h} = 16.667 \text{ m/s}$, initial: $v_0 = 0$

$$t = \frac{v_f - v_0}{a} = \frac{16.667}{2.94} = 5.67 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t = 5.67 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 4(b) — Distance Covered**

$$s = \frac{v_f^2 - v_0^2}{2a} = \frac{(16.667)^2}{2 \times 2.94} = \frac{277.78}{5.88}$$

$$\boxed{s = 47.24 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5(c) — Energy at the Wheels**

Using work-energy theorem: $E = F_{te} \cdot s = 6000 \times 47.24$

$$E_{wheels} = 283{,}440 \text{ J} = 78.73 \text{ Wh}$$

Accounting for drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{total} = 0.88$:

$$E_{battery} = \frac{78.73}{0.88} = 89.47 \text{ Wh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{battery} \approx 89.5 \text{ Wh}}$$

**Interpretation:** This 0–60 km/h acceleration burst consumes less than 0.1 kWh — small compared to cruising energy (~5.5 kWh per hour), confirming that **constant-speed cruising dominates EV energy consumption**, not aggressive acceleration.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Problem-Type → Formula Mapping

| KTU Problem Type | Primary Formulae | Marks Pattern | Common Mistake |
|------------------|------------------|---------------|----------------|
| Tractive Effort Calculation | $F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_g + F_a$ | 7 marks | Forgetting $\cos\alpha$ in $F_{rr}$ |
| Power at Wheels | $P_w = F_{te} \cdot v$ | 3–4 marks | Unit mismatch (W vs kW) |
| Battery Power | $P_b = P_w / \eta_{total}$ | 3–4 marks | Dividing instead of multiplying by $\eta$ |
| Trip Energy | $E = P_b \times t$ | 4–5 marks | Forgetting $t = d/v$ |
| Range Estimation | $R = E_{bat} / E_{km}$ | 7 marks | Using wheel energy, not battery |
| Grade Climbing | $F_g = mg\sin\alpha$ | 7 marks | Using $\tan$ instead of $\sin$ |
| Acceleration Time | $t = (v_f - v_i)/a$ | 4 marks | Ignoring $\delta$ (mass factor) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Force Interactions on an EV

The following Mermaid block-level schematic depicts the **interaction of all four resistance forces** on an electric vehicle, mapped to the analytical equations used in KTU numerical problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Motor Torque T] --> B[Transmission Gear]
    B --> C[Traction Force at Wheels]
    C --> D[Vehicle Mass m]
    D --> E1[Rolling Resistance F_rr]
    D --> E2[Aerodynamic Drag F_ad]
    D --> E3[Grade Force F_g]
    D --> E4[Acceleration Force F_a]
    E1 --> F[Total Resistance F_t]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Net Force = F_te - F_t]
    G --> H[Linear Acceleration a]
    H --> I[Velocity v]
    I --> J[Power at Wheels P_w]
    J --> K[Battery Pack P_b]
    K --> L[Range R and Energy E]
```

### 4.2 Problem-Solving Flowchart for KTU Numerical Problems

This flowchart guides a KTU student through the **logical order** of solving any numerical problem on vehicle dynamics in the 2024 scheme exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Read problem carefully] --> S2[Extract all given data and units]
    S2 --> S3[Convert km/h to m/s and percent grade to angle]
    S3 --> S4{Identify which forces are non-zero}
    S4 --> S5a[Level road, constant v: only F_rr and F_ad]
    S4 --> S5b[Slope, constant v: add F_g using sin alpha]
    S4 --> S5c[Accelerating: add F_a = m(1+delta)a]
    S5a --> S6[Compute each force using standard formula]
    S5b --> S6
    S5c --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Sum to get F_te]
    S7 --> S8[Power: P_w = F_te times v]
    S8 --> S9[Battery: P_b = P_w divided by eta_total]
    S9 --> S10[Energy: E = P_b times t]
    S10 --> S11[Express final answer with correct units]
    S11 --> S12[End: Verify dimensional consistency]
```

### 4.3 Energy Flow Architecture in an EV (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[Battery Pack E_bat] --> INV[Motor Controller and Inverter]
    INV --> MOT[Electric Motor eta_m]
    MOT --> TRN[Transmission eta_t]
    TRN --> WHL[Wheels]
    WHL --> R1[Overcome F_rr]
    WHL --> R2[Overcome F_ad]
    WHL --> R3[Overcome F_g]
    WHL --> R4[Provide F_a for acceleration]
    R1 --> OUT[Kinetic Energy to Vehicle]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    R4 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams for your KTU answer sheet:**
> The block diagram in 4.1 is the **most commonly asked 3-mark diagram question** in Part A. Memorize the four force names and their formulas. The flowchart in 4.2 helps you avoid missing steps during valuation — examiners award 1 mark for every correct formula, 1 mark for substitution, and 1 mark for the final numerical value with units.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📋 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
*State the four main forces opposing the motion of an electric vehicle. Write the formula for any two of them.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four forces opposing the motion of an electric vehicle are:
1. **Rolling Resistance Force** ($F_{rr}$) — due to tire deformation
2. **Aerodynamic Drag Force** ($F_{ad}$) — due to air resistance
3. **Grade/Climbing Force** ($F_g$) — due to road gradient
4. **Inertial/Acceleration Force** ($F_a$) — due to mass acceleration

Formulas:

$$F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2 \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha, \quad F_a = m(1+\delta)a \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*An EV travels at 72 km/h on a level road. Compute its speed in m/s. If the tractive force is 500 N, what is the power required at the wheels?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Conversion (1 mark):
$$v = 72 \text{ km/h} = 72 \times \tfrac{5}{18} = 20 \text{ m/s}$$

Power formula (1 mark):
$$P_w = F_{te} \times v$$

Final calculation (1 mark):
$$P_w = 500 \times 20 = 10{,}000 \text{ W} = 10 \text{ kW}$$

---

### 📋 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Part B questions on Module 1 typically offer a choice between two numerical problems, each carrying 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Below are two complete parallel choices with full sub-parts and step-by-step model solutions.

---

#### ✏️ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** An electric two-wheeler has the following parameters: total mass $m = 220 \text{ kg}$ (rider + vehicle), $C_d = 0.65$, $A_f = 0.5 \text{ m}^2$, $\mu_{rr} = 0.018$. The vehicle travels at a constant $50 \text{ km/h}$ on a level road. Compute the tractive force at the wheels. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same vehicle climbs a 6% grade at $30 \text{ km/h}$ and the drivetrain efficiency is 0.85, calculate the battery power required and the energy consumed in 45 minutes. **(7 marks)**

**Full Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Tractive Force at 50 km/h, level road**

Convert speed:
$$v = 50 \times \tfrac{5}{18} = 13.889 \text{ m/s} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Rolling resistance:
$$F_{rr} = 0.018 \times 220 \times 9.81 \times 1.0 = 38.85 \text{ N} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

Aerodynamic drag:
$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.65 \times 0.5 \times (13.889)^2 = 38.43 \text{ N} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

Total tractive force (level, constant speed):
$$F_{te} = F_{rr} + F_{ad} = 38.85 + 38.43 = 77.28 \text{ N} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

$$\boxed{F_{te} = 77.28 \text{ N}}$$

**Part (b) — Battery power at 6% grade, 30 km/h**

Convert speed:
$$v = 30 \times \tfrac{5}{18} = 8.333 \text{ m/s} \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

Grade conversion for $G = 6\%$:
$$\sin\alpha = \frac{6}{\sqrt{10000 + 36}} = \frac{6}{100.18} = 0.0599 \quad (0.5 \text{ mark})$$

Force components:
$$F_{rr} = 0.018 \times 220 \times 9.81 \times 0.9982 = 38.79 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.65 \times 0.5 \times (8.333)^2 = 13.86 \text{ N}$$

$$F_g = 220 \times 9.81 \times 0.0599 = 129.27 \text{ N} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

Total tractive force:
$$F_{te} = 38.79 + 13.86 + 129.27 = 181.92 \text{ N}$$

Power at wheels:
$$P_w = 181.92 \times 8.333 = 1515.9 \text{ W} = 1.516 \text{ kW} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Battery power:
$$P_b = \frac{1.516}{0.85} = 1.784 \text{ kW} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Energy for 45 minutes ($t = 0.75 \text{ h}$):
$$E = 1.784 \times 0.75 = 1.338 \text{ kWh} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

$$\boxed{P_b = 1.784 \text{ kW}, \quad E = 1.338 \text{ kWh}}$$

---

#### ✏️ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** A 1200 kg EV has a maximum motor torque of 220 Nm at the motor shaft. The transmission ratio is 9.5:1 and wheel radius is 0.32 m. Calculate the maximum tractive force at the wheels and the grade it can climb at constant 40 km/h, neglecting aerodynamic drag. Take $\mu_{rr} = 0.013$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The same EV uses a 30 kWh lithium-ion battery with usable depth-of-discharge of 80% and an average energy consumption of 150 Wh/km. Calculate the maximum range and the time required to fully charge from a 7 kW AC home charger. **(7 marks)**

**Full Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Tractive force from motor torque**

Maximum tractive force at wheels (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution):
$$F_{te,max} = \frac{T_{motor} \times G_r}{r_{wheel}} = \frac{220 \times 9.5}{0.32}$$

$$F_{te,max} = \frac{2090}{0.32} = 6531.25 \text{ N} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

Convert speed:
$$v = 40 \times \tfrac{5}{18} = 11.111 \text{ m/s}$$

Rolling resistance (level approximation):
$$F_{rr} = 0.013 \times 1200 \times 9.81 = 153.04 \text{ N} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Available force for grade:
$$F_g = F_{te,max} - F_{rr} = 6531.25 - 153.04 = 6378.21 \text{ N}$$

Grade angle:
$$\sin\alpha = \frac{F_g}{m \cdot g} = \frac{6378.21}{1200 \times 9.81} = \frac{6378.21}{11772} = 0.5418 \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

Grade percentage:
$$\alpha = \arcsin(0.5418) = 32.83°, \quad G\% = 100 \times \tan(32.83°) = 64.4\% \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$\boxed{F_{te,max} = 6531.25 \text{ N}, \quad G_{max} \approx 64.4\%}$$

(Note: This is a theoretical limit; in practice, tire-road friction limits gradeability to ~30–40%.)

**Part (b) — Range and Charging Time**

Usable battery energy:
$$E_{usable} = 30 \text{ kWh} \times 0.80 = 24 \text{ kWh} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

Range:
$$R = \frac{E_{usable}}{E_{per\,km}} = \frac{24{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{150 \text{ Wh/km}} = 160 \text{ km} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

$$\boxed{R = 160 \text{ km}}$$

Charging time (assuming 100% efficient charging for simplicity, or include charger efficiency):
With 7 kW charger, time to deliver 24 kWh (1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer):
$$t = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{charger}} = \frac{24 \text{ kWh}}{7 \text{ kW}} = 3.43 \text{ hours}$$

$$\boxed{t_{charge} = 3 \text{ h } 26 \text{ min} \approx 3.43 \text{ hours}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read This Carefully:**
> 
> **Pitfall 1: Grade Formula Confusion (Loses 2 marks)**
> Students often write $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \tan\alpha$ or $F_g = mg \cdot \text{grade}\%$. **Correct:** Always $F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$. For grade percent, use $\sin\alpha = G / \sqrt{10000 + G^2}$.
> 
> **Pitfall 2: Missing the $\cos\alpha$ in $F_{rr}$ (Loses 1–2 marks)**
> On a slope, $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha$, **not** $\mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$. Many students forget this, causing significant error on steep grades.
> 
> **Pitfall 3: Dividing instead of multiplying by efficiency (Loses 2 marks)**
> Battery delivers MORE power than wheels receive. So $P_b = P_w / \eta$, not $P_b = P_w \times \eta$. Get the direction right!
> 
> **Pitfall 4: Speed Unit Mismatch (Loses 1 mark per instance)**
> The formula $P = F \times v$ requires $v$ in **m/s**. If you use 60 km/h directly, your answer is off by a factor of 3.6. Always convert.
> 
> **Pitfall 5: Missing Units in Final Answer (Loses 1 mark)**
> "F = 429" is incomplete. Always write "F = 429.24 N" or "P = 4.81 kW". KTU examiners are instructed to deduct 1 mark for missing units in the final answer of a numerical problem.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

✅ **The Four Forces** — $F_{rr}$, $F_{ad}$, $F_g$, $F_a$ — must be drawn and named in every numerical problem (1 mark reserved for diagram in 14-mark questions).

✅ **Speed conversion** is mandatory: $v \text{ (m/s)} = v \text{ (km/h)} \times 5/18$.

✅ **Grade conversion:** $\sin\alpha = G / \sqrt{10000 + G^2}$, where $G$ is the grade percentage.

✅ **Rolling resistance** uses $\cos\alpha$, **grade force** uses $\sin\alpha$ — don't swap them.

✅ **Aerodynamic drag** is proportional to $v^2$ — at high speeds it dominates, at low speeds it is negligible.

✅ **Power** = $F_{te} \times v$ at the wheels; **Battery power** = $P_w / \eta_{total}$.

✅ **Energy** = Power × Time; for a trip, $t = \text{distance} / \text{speed}$.

✅ **Range** = $\text{Usable Battery Energy} / \text{Energy per km}$; remember to multiply battery capacity by **DoD** (typically 0.8 for Li-ion to extend cycle life).

✅ **Acceleration force** includes the mass factor $(1 + \delta)$ to account for rotating inertia of wheels, motor, and drivetrain.

✅ **Constants to memorize:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$.

✅ **Always include units** in the final boxed answer; always state assumptions explicitly before starting the calculation.

✅ **Typical KTU 2024 mark split for 14-mark numerical:** Formula (1) + Substitution (1) + Calculation (2) + Units & result (1) + Interpretation/comment (1–2) = 7 marks per sub-part.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Drive-Trains: Basic Concept of Electric Traction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electric Drive-Trains: Basic Concept of Electric Traction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electric Traction** is the branch of electrical engineering that deals with the **utilization of electrical energy for the propulsion (motion) of vehicles** such as electric cars, electric locomotives, trams, trolleybuses, and electric buses. The electrical energy is first converted into mechanical energy by means of one or more **traction motors**, and this mechanical energy is then transmitted to the driving wheels through a **mechanical transmission system** to produce the motion of the vehicle.

In an **Electric Drive-Train**, the complete electromechanical system responsible for propelling an Electric Vehicle (EV) — comprising the **energy source (battery/ultracapacitor)**, the **power electronic converter (inverter)**, the **electric traction motor**, the **mechanical transmission (gearbox, differential)**, and the **driving wheels** — is collectively called the electric drive-train. It is the heart of every modern electric vehicle.

**Key Vocabulary (KTU 2024 standard terms):**
- **Traction Motor:** The electric motor that produces the mechanical driving force.
- **Tractive Effort (TE):** The net force available at the wheel–road contact patch for vehicle propulsion.
- **Adhesion Coefficient (μ):** The maximum usable friction between tire and road before wheel slip occurs.
- **Power Train / Drive-Train:** The entire system that transmits power from the source to the wheels.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Rowing Boat vs The Motorboat:**
> Imagine pushing a small boat across a lake. If you use **oars**, you are the "engine" — you provide the mechanical effort directly. If you use a **motorboat with a battery**, the battery provides stored energy, an electric motor converts it to spinning rotation, and a propeller (analogous to a wheel) pushes water backwards to move the boat forwards. The **electric traction system is exactly this motorboat arrangement** for road vehicles: a **battery** stores the energy, an **electric motor** spins, and **wheels** push against the ground to move the vehicle.

A simpler way to remember: **"Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels = Motion"** — every component in this chain is a part of the electric drive-train.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics in Electric Traction

The following constants and metrics are the foundation of every calculation in this topic:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Magnitude (Passenger EV) |
|---|---|---|
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Air density (sea level, 25°C) | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** |
| Coefficient of rolling resistance | $\mu_{r}$ | **0.010 – 0.015** |
| Aerodynamic drag coefficient | $C_{d}$ | **0.25 – 0.35** |
| Frontal area of vehicle | $A$ | **2.0 – 2.5 m²** |
| Coefficient of adhesion (dry asphalt) | $\mu$ | **0.7 – 0.9** |
| Typical vehicle efficiency (tank-to-wheel) | $\eta_{veh}$ | **60 – 75 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> These values are **not chosen arbitrarily** — they are the standard KTU/IE/SAE benchmarks used in university numerical problems. Memorize the bold numbers; the examiner will not provide them in a 14-mark numerical.

---

### 1.4 Historical & Modern Context

| Era | Drive-Train Type | Energy Source |
|---|---|---|
| 1830s – 1900s | Non-electric (steam, then IC engine) | Coal, petrol |
| 1900 – 1935 | Early DC electric traction | Lead-acid battery / overhead line |
| 1960s – 1990s | Hybrid concepts (limited) | Petrol + small electric assist |
| 2008 – Present | Modern EV drive-trains (BLDC, PMSM, Induction) | Li-ion battery, Fuel cell |

The **2024 KTU syllabus emphasis** is on **modern EV drive-trains**, particularly those using **PMSM, BLDC, SRM and Induction motors** fed by **Li-ion battery packs via Voltage Source Inverters (VSI)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Need for Electric Traction — The "Why"

Conventional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicles suffer from:
- **Low energy conversion efficiency** (≈ 25–35 % tank-to-wheel).
