- **Tailpipe emissions** (CO₂, NOₓ, PM).
- **Complex multi-speed gearbox** required to match ICE torque–speed curve to wheel demand.
- **Frequent maintenance** (oil, filters, spark plugs, clutch).

Electric traction eliminates all of these by:
- **High efficiency** (≈ 80–95 % motor + inverter).
- **Zero tailpipe emissions** at point of use.
- **Flat / constant-power torque–speed curve** that closely matches the ideal wheel demand.
- **Single-speed reduction gearbox** is often sufficient.
- **Regenerative braking** recovers kinetic energy back to the battery.

---

### 2.2 The Five Core "Building Blocks" of an Electric Drive-Train

1. **Energy Storage Subsystem** — Battery pack (Li-ion), ultracapacitor, or fuel cell + H₂ tank.
2. **Power Electronic Converter** — DC-DC converter (for auxiliary voltages) and DC-AC inverter (to feed motor with variable frequency/voltage).
3. **Traction Motor** — Converts electrical → mechanical energy. Types: DC, Induction (IM), Permanent Magnet Synchronous (PMSM), Brushless DC (BLDC), Switched Reluctance (SRM).
4. **Mechanical Transmission** — Single-speed or multi-speed gearbox + differential + half-shafts.
5. **Wheels & Tires** — Where the **Tractive Effort (TE)** is applied to the road.

The **arrow of energy flow** is:
$$\text{Battery} \rightarrow \text{Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Motor} \rightarrow \text{Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Wheels} \rightarrow \text{Road}$$

---

### 2.3 Classification of Electric Traction (KTU High-Yield)

**(a) By Energy Source — 3 Types:**

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)** | Purely battery-powered. No IC engine. |
| **Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)** | Combines IC engine + electric motor. |
| **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV)** | HEV with externally chargeable battery. |
| **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)** | H₂ + O₂ → electricity via fuel cell → motor. |

**(b) By Transmission Configuration:**

| Configuration | Description |
|---|---|
| **Single-motor, single-speed** | One motor + fixed-ratio gearbox. Used in most modern BEVs (Nissan Leaf, Tesla). |
| **Single-motor, multi-speed** | One motor + 2-speed or multi-speed gearbox. (Porsche Taycan, BMW i3). |
| **Dual-motor AWD** | Two motors (one per axle). (Tesla Model S, Rivian). |
| **In-wheel / Hub motor** | Motor directly inside the wheel. (Protean Electric, Elaphe). |

---

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Component — Engineering Rationale

- **Why an inverter?** — DC batteries cannot directly produce a rotating magnetic field; the inverter **chops the DC into variable-frequency AC** so the motor's speed can be controlled smoothly from 0 rpm.
- **Why a single-speed gearbox?** — Electric motors deliver **peak torque at 0 rpm** and have a wide constant-power region, so unlike ICEs they do not need many gears.
- **Why PMSM dominant in 2024 EVs?** — Highest **power density (kW/kg)** and efficiency (>95 %) among all motor types.
- **Why a differential?** — Allows the inner and outer wheels to rotate at different speeds while cornering, preventing tire scrub.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulae below are examinable.** Memorize the table — every KTU Numerical question in Module 1 is built from these equations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tractive Effort at wheel | $TE \;=\; \dfrac{T_{motor} \cdot G \cdot \eta_{tr}}{r_{w}}$ | N | $G$ = gear ratio, $r_w$ = wheel radius |
| 2 | Total tractive resistance | $R_{total} \;=\; R_{roll} + R_{grad} + R_{air}$ | N | Sum of three components |
| 3 | Rolling resistance | $R_{roll} \;=\; \mu_{r} \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta$ | N | $W$ = weight, $\theta$ = slope |
| 4 | Gradient resistance | $R_{grad} \;=\; W \cdot \sin\theta$ | N | Acts against motion on slope |
| 5 | Aerodynamic drag | $R_{air} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot V^{2}$ | N | $V$ = vehicle speed in m/s |
| 6 | Dynamic force balance (acceleration) | $TE - R_{total} \;=\; M \cdot a$ | N | $M$ = mass, $a$ = acceleration |
| 7 | Power at wheels | $P_{wheel} \;=\; TE \cdot V$ | W | Mechanical power delivered to road |
| 8 | Power at motor shaft | $P_{motor} \;=\; T_{motor} \cdot \omega_{m}$ | W | Electrical-to-mechanical conversion |
| 9 | Vehicle linear speed | $V \;=\; \omega_{w} \cdot r_{w}$ | m/s | Wheel rotation to linear motion |
| 10 | Gear ratio definition | $G \;=\; \dfrac{\omega_{m}}{\omega_{w}} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{w}}{N_{m}}$ | dimensionless | Motor-to-wheel reduction |
| 11 | Adhesion limit | $TE_{max} \;=\; \mu \cdot W$ | N | Cannot exceed tire-road friction |
| 12 | Specific energy of battery | $E_{specific} \;=\; \dfrac{E_{capacity}}{m_{batt}}$ | Wh/kg | Determines range |

> **Notation warning:** $\mu_r$ (rolling resistance coefficient) and $\mu$ (adhesion coefficient) are **completely different physical quantities** — students often confuse them in exams.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle Range Estimation:** Tractive effort × total distance = mechanical energy required. From this, battery kWh is calculated.
- **Motor Sizing:** Peak torque from $TE_{max} \times r_w / (G \cdot \eta_{tr})$.
- **Battery Sizing:** Energy = $\int P_{wheel}\, dt$ / $\eta_{battery-inverter-motor}$.
- **Brake System Design:** Friction brakes must handle kinetic energy = $\frac{1}{2} M V^2$ that regenerative braking cannot absorb (at very high SOC).
- **Gradeability Test:** A standard KTU numerical asks: *"What is the maximum gradient the vehicle can climb at 30 km/h?"* — solved using $TE = R_{total} + M \cdot a$ with $a = 0$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Tractive Effort from Motor Torque

**Starting point:** The motor produces torque $T_{motor}$ at angular speed $\omega_m$. This torque passes through the transmission (gear ratio $G$, efficiency $\eta_{tr}$) to the wheel (radius $r_w$).

The torque available at the wheel is:
$$T_{wheel} = T_{motor} \cdot G \cdot \eta_{tr}$$

The tractive effort (linear force at contact patch) is force produced at the tire–road interface:
$$TE = \frac{T_{wheel}}{r_{w}} = \frac{T_{motor} \cdot G \cdot \eta_{tr}}{r_{w}}$$

**Assumption (clearly state in KTU answer):** The transmission is assumed lossless for ideal case ($\eta_{tr} = 1$); otherwise efficiency is given in the problem.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Total Resistance Force

**Step 1:** Identify the three resistive forces opposing motion.

**Step 2 — Rolling resistance:** Arises from tire hysteresis. The normal reaction on a slope of angle $\theta$ is $W \cos\theta$. Hence:
$$R_{roll} = \mu_{r} \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta$$

For small grades ($\theta < 10°$), $\cos\theta \approx 1$:
$$R_{roll} \approx \mu_{r} \cdot W$$

**Step 3 — Gradient resistance:** Component of weight along slope:
$$R_{grad} = W \cdot \sin\theta$$

For small grades, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx \text{grade (decimal)}$:
$$R_{grad} \approx W \cdot \text{grade}$$

**Step 4 — Aerodynamic drag:** From fluid dynamics, drag force is:
$$R_{air} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot V^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Total resistance:**
$$R_{total} = \mu_{r} \cdot W + W \cdot \sin\theta + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A \cdot V^{2}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Dynamic Tractive Effort Equation (Newton's Second Law for the Vehicle)

The vehicle is a rigid body of mass $M$ (including rotational inertia) moving with linear acceleration $a$. Summing forces along the direction of motion:

$$\sum F_{x} = M \cdot a$$

$$TE - R_{total} = M \cdot a$$

Solving for TE:
$$TE = R_{total} + M \cdot a = \mu_{r} W + W \sin\theta + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_{d} A V^{2} + M a$$

**Limiting cases (commonly asked in KTU):**

- **Cruise (constant speed, no slope, a = 0):** $TE = R_{air}$ at high speed.
- **Hill-climb (constant speed, slope, a = 0):** $TE = R_{roll} + R_{grad} + R_{air}$.
- **Maximum acceleration (level road, V small so $R_{air} \approx 0$):** $TE = \mu_{r} W + Ma$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Power Equations

**Power at wheels (mechanical power actually pushing the vehicle):**
$$P_{wheel} = TE \cdot V \;\; [\text{W}]$$

where $V$ is in m/s. If $V$ is in km/h, then:
$$P_{wheel} \;=\; \frac{TE \cdot V_{km/h}}{3.6} \;\; [\text{W}]$$

**Power at motor shaft (input to transmission):**
$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{tr}} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_{m}$$

**Power drawn from battery (input to inverter):**
$$P_{batt} = \frac{P_{motor}}{\eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$$

where $\eta_{inv} \approx 0.95\text{–}0.98$ and $\eta_{motor} \approx 0.85\text{–}0.97$.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of Vehicle Speed from Motor Speed

The motor spins at $\omega_m$ (rad/s). Through gear ratio $G = \omega_m / \omega_w$, the wheel spins at:
$$\omega_{w} = \frac{\omega_{m}}{G}$$

The linear speed of the vehicle equals the tangential speed of the wheel:
$$V = \omega_{w} \cdot r_{w} = \frac{\omega_{m} \cdot r_{w}}{G}$$

Converting to km/h ($V_{km/h} = 3.6 \cdot V_{m/s}$):
$$V_{km/h} = \frac{3.6 \cdot \omega_{m} \cdot r_{w}}{G}$$

**Worked KTU-Type Numerical Example:**

> **Problem (Module 1 typical):** A 1500 kg electric car climbs a 10 % grade at constant 40 km/h. Coefficient of rolling resistance = 0.012, $C_d A = 0.7$ m², $\eta_{tr} = 0.92$, $r_w = 0.3$ m, gear ratio = 6.0. Calculate (a) total resistance force, (b) tractive effort, (c) power at wheels, (d) motor torque.

**Solution:**

Given: $M = 1500$ kg, $W = M \cdot g = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14715$ N, grade = 10 % = 0.10, $V = 40/3.6 = 11.11$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³.

**Step (a) — Total resistance:**

$R_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot W = 0.012 \times 14715 = 176.58$ N

$R_{grad} = W \cdot \sin\theta \approx W \cdot \text{grade} = 0.10 \times 14715 = 1471.5$ N

$R_{air} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d A \cdot V^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times (11.11)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.7 \times 123.46 = 52.94$ N

$$R_{total} = 176.58 + 1471.5 + 52.94 = 1701.02 \text{ N}$$

**Step (b) — Tractive effort (constant speed, a = 0):**
$$TE = R_{total} = 1701.02 \text{ N}$$

**Step (c) — Power at wheels:**
$$P_{wheel} = TE \cdot V = 1701.02 \times 11.11 = 18898 \text{ W} \approx 18.9 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (d) — Motor torque:**
$$T_{motor} = \frac{TE \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{tr}} = \frac{1701.02 \times 0.3}{6.0 \times 0.92} = \frac{510.3}{5.52} = 92.45 \text{ N·m}$$

> **Answer:** $R_{total} = 1701$ N, $TE = 1701$ N, $P_{wheel} = 18.9$ kW, $T_{motor} = 92.45$ N·m.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Motor Type vs Drive-Train Suitability

| Motor Type | Power Density | Efficiency | Cost | Maintenance | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PMSM** | **High** | **> 95 %** | High (rare-earth magnets) | Low | **Premium EVs (Tesla, BMW i)** |
| **BLDC** | High | 90–93 % | Moderate | Low | Two-wheelers, small EVs |
| **Induction (IM)** | Moderate | 90–93 % | **Low** (no magnets) | Low | Mid-segment EVs (Tata Nexon EV earlier) |
| **SRM** | Moderate | 85–90 % | **Lowest** | Moderate | Commercial / heavy EVs |
| **DC (Separately Excited)** | Low | 75–85 % | Low | **High (brushes)** | Legacy / training kits only |

> **KTU examiner tip:** When asked *"Which motor is most used in modern EV drive-trains?"* — answer **PMSM**, with justification (highest efficiency + power density).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Complete Electric Drive-Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[Battery Pack - DC Source] --> DC1[DC DC Converter - 12V Aux]
    BAT --> INV[Power Inverter - VSI - DC to Variable AC]
    DC1 --> AUX[Auxiliary Loads - Lights ECU]
    INV --> MTR[Traction Motor - BLDC PMSM IM SRM]
    MTR --> GRX[Single Speed Gearbox and Differential]
    GRX --> WH1[Front Wheels]
    GRX --> WH2[Rear Wheels]
    BRK[Brake Pedal] --> CTRL[Vehicle Control Unit - VCU]
    ACC[Accelerator Pedal] --> CTRL
    CTRL --> INV
    WH1 -.regenerative energy.-> INV
    WH2 -.regenerative energy.-> INV
```

**Interpretation:** The **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)** is the "brain." It reads accelerator/brake signals, decides motor speed/torque command, and the inverter executes it. During braking, the motor becomes a **generator** and energy flows back to the battery (regenerative braking).

---

### 4.2 Power Flow Diagram — Direction of Energy Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Energy Source - Li ion Battery - Fuel Cell] --> CONV1[Power Converter - Inverter]
    CONV1 --> MOTOR[Electric Traction Motor]
    MOTOR --> MECH[Mechanical Transmission - Gearbox Differential]
    MECH --> LOAD[Wheels - Tractive Effort at Road]
    LOAD -.-> MECH
    MECH -.-> MOTOR
    MOTOR -.-> CONV1
    CONV1 -.-> SRC
```

**Forward arrow = motoring mode (acceleration/cruise).**  
**Dashed return arrow = regenerative braking mode.**

---

### 4.3 Classification Tree of Electric Drive-Trains

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EVT[Electric Drive Train] --> SES[By Energy Source]
    EVT --> TCS[By Transmission Configuration]
    EVT --> MOT[By Motor Type]
    SES --> BEV[BEV - Battery Only]
    SES --> HEV[HEV - Hybrid IC plus Electric]
    SES --> PHEV[PHEV - Plug in Hybrid]
    SES --> FCEV[FCEV - Fuel Cell]
    TCS --> SMS[Single Motor Single Speed]
    TCS --> SMM[Single Motor Multi Speed]
    TCS --> DUA[Dual Motor All Wheel Drive]
    TCS --> IWM[In Wheel Hub Motor]
    MOT --> PMSM[PMSM]
    MOT --> BLDC[BLDC]
    MOT --> IM[Induction Motor]
    MOT --> SRM[Switched Reluctance Motor]
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — VCU and Sub-Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS
        P1[Accelerator Pedal - Torque Demand]
        P2[Brake Pedal - Regen Level]
        P3[Steering Angle Sensor]
        P4[Vehicle Speed Sensor]
    end
    VCU[Vehicle Control Unit - Master ECU]
    INPUTS --> VCU
    VCU --> INV[Inverter Driver - IGBT SiC]
    VCU --> BMS[Battery Management System]
    VCU --> TMS[Thermal Management System]
    INV --> EM[Electric Motor]
    EM --> TR[Transmission and Wheels]
    TR --> SS[Sensors - Speed Torque Temp]
    SS --> VCU
    BMS --> BAT[Battery Pack]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define electric traction. List any four advantages of electric traction over conventional IC engine traction.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Electric Traction** is the system that uses electrical energy as the primary input for propelling vehicles; the energy is converted to mechanical work by one or more electric traction motors that drive the wheels.

**Four advantages (any four):**

1. **Higher energy efficiency** — Electric drive-trains operate at 80–95 % efficiency vs. 25–35 % for ICE vehicles.
2. **No exhaust emissions** at point of use — environmentally friendly.
3. **Regenerative braking** — kinetic energy is recovered to the battery during deceleration.
4. **Low maintenance** — fewer moving parts, no oil changes, no spark plugs, no clutch wear.
5. **Smooth and quiet operation** — low noise and vibration.
6. **Wide speed range with single gear** — flat torque–speed curve eliminates the need for multi-speed gearbox.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Compare the speed–torque characteristics of an electric traction motor and an IC engine. Why does an electric vehicle typically need only a single-speed gearbox?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | IC Engine | Electric Traction Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Starting torque | Low (≈ 0) | **Very high (peak at 0 rpm)** |
| Constant torque region | Narrow | **Wide (up to base speed)** |
| Constant power region | Narrow | **Wide (up to max speed)** |
| Power density | Low | High |
| Efficiency | 25–35 % | 85–95 % |
| Reverse gear needed | Yes (or reverse motor) | **No (reverse by phase sequence change)** |

**Reason for single-speed gearbox:** An electric motor delivers **peak torque at standstill** and maintains nearly constant power over a wide speed range (≈ 3–4× base speed). Hence one fixed gear ratio is sufficient to match the wheel-speed demand across the full vehicle operating range, unlike an ICE which needs 5–6 gears to keep the engine in its narrow power band.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the major components of an electric vehicle drive-train. State the function of each component. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An electric vehicle of mass 1200 kg (including battery) is moving at a constant speed of 60 km/h on a level road. The coefficient of rolling resistance is 0.015, the aerodynamic drag coefficient $C_d = 0.3$, the frontal area $A = 2.2$ m², the wheel radius $r_w = 0.32$ m, the gear ratio $G = 7.5$, and the transmission efficiency $\eta_{tr} = 0.9$. Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Calculate:
   (i) Total tractive resistance.
   (ii) Tractive effort required.
   (iii) Power at the wheels.
   (iv) Motor torque. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

> **Block diagram of electric drive-train:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT1[Battery Pack] --> INV1[Inverter - DC to AC]
    INV1 --> MTR1[Traction Motor]
    MTR1 --> TRX1[Gearbox and Differential]
    TRX1 --> WHL1[Wheels and Tires]
    WHL1 -.regen.-> INV1
```

**Functions of each component:**

1. **Battery Pack** — Stores electrical energy (DC) chemically; primary energy source. For Li-ion: ~150–250 Wh/kg.
2. **Inverter (VSI)** — Converts DC from battery into variable-frequency, variable-voltage three-phase AC to control motor speed/torque.
3. **Traction Motor** — Converts electrical energy to mechanical rotation (torque × speed). PMSM, BLDC, IM, or SRM.
4. **Gearbox & Differential** — Reduces motor speed, multiplies torque, and distributes power to wheels; differential allows different wheel speeds when cornering.
5. **Wheels & Tires** — Final mechanical interface with the road; transmits tractive effort via tire–road friction.
6. **VCU (not shown but essential)** — Brain of the system; reads driver inputs, controls inverter, manages BMS, enables regenerative braking.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $M = 1200$ kg, $V = 60$ km/h $= 60/3.6 = 16.67$ m/s, $\mu_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.3$, $A = 2.2$ m², $r_w = 0.32$ m, $G = 7.5$, $\eta_{tr} = 0.9$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, level road ($\theta = 0$).

**Weight of vehicle:**
$$W = M \cdot g = 1200 \times 9.81 = 11772 \text{ N}$$

**(i) Total tractive resistance:**

Rolling resistance:
$$R_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot W = 0.015 \times 11772 = 176.58 \text{ N}$$

Gradient resistance (level road):
$$R_{grad} = 0 \text{ N}$$

Aerodynamic drag:
$$R_{air} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.3 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^{2}$$

$$R_{air} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.3 \times 2.2 \times 277.78 = 112.3 \text{ N}$$

Total resistance:
$$\boxed{R_{total} = 176.58 + 0 + 112.3 = 288.88 \text{ N}}$$

**(ii) Tractive effort (constant speed, $a = 0$):**
$$\boxed{TE = R_{total} = 288.88 \text{ N}}$$

**(iii) Power at the wheels:**
$$P_{wheel} = TE \cdot V = 288.88 \times 16.67$$

$$\boxed{P_{wheel} = 4814.4 \text{ W} \approx 4.81 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iv) Motor torque:**
$$T_{motor} = \frac{TE \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{tr}} = \frac{288.88 \times 0.32}{7.5 \times 0.9} = \frac{92.44}{6.75}$$

$$\boxed{T_{motor} = 13.69 \text{ N·m}}$$

---

### ⭐ **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the different configurations of electric drive-trains based on the placement and number of traction motors. Mention one commercial EV example for each. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 4-wheel electric car of total mass 1800 kg climbs a uniform slope of 8 % at a steady speed of 36 km/h. Each wheel has radius 0.31 m, gear ratio 8.0, transmission efficiency 0.93, $C_d A = 0.65$ m², and rolling resistance coefficient 0.012. Take $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³. Compute the tractive effort, total resistance, and motor torque. If the road adhesion coefficient is 0.75, verify whether the vehicle can climb the slope without wheel slip. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Configurations of electric drive-trains:**

1. **Single-motor, single-speed (rear-wheel drive)**
   - One motor connected to a fixed-ratio gearbox and differential, driving the rear wheels.
   - *Example:* Nissan Leaf (early models).
   - Simple, lightweight, low cost.

2. **Single-motor, multi-speed**
   - One motor with a 2-speed gearbox for better efficiency at both low and high speeds.
   - *Example:* Porsche Taycan (2-speed rear gearbox).
   - Improved acceleration and high-speed efficiency.

3. **Dual-motor all-wheel drive (AWD)**
   - Two motors (front + rear); no mechanical connection between axles → torque vectoring possible.
   - *Example:* Tesla Model S, Rivian R1T.
   - Better traction, performance, and redundancy.

4. **In-wheel (hub) motor drive**
   - Motor integrated inside each wheel; no gearbox or differential.
   - *Example:* Lordstown Endurance, Protean Electric prototypes.
   - Maximum packaging flexibility; unsprung mass is a challenge.

> **Note:** All four sketches may be drawn as labelled rectangular blocks connected by shaft/arrow lines indicating power flow to the wheels.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $M = 1800$ kg, grade = 8 % = 0.08, $V = 36$ km/h $= 10$ m/s, $r_w = 0.31$ m, $G = 8.0$, $\eta_{tr} = 0.93$, $C_d A = 0.65$ m², $\mu_r = 0.012$, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\mu = 0.75$, $a = 0$ (constant speed).

**Weight:**
$$W = M \cdot g = 1800 \times 9.81 = 17658 \text{ N}$$

**Total resistance:**

Rolling resistance (small slope, $\cos\theta \approx 1$):
$$R_{roll} = 0.012 \times 17658 = 211.9 \text{ N}$$

Gradient resistance (small slope, $\sin\theta \approx 0.08$):
$$R_{grad} = 0.08 \times 17658 = 1412.6 \text{ N}$$

Aerodynamic drag:
$$R_{air} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.65 \times (10)^{2} = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.65 \times 100 = 39.0 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{R_{total} = 211.9 + 1412.6 + 39.0 = 1663.5 \text{ N}}$$

**Tractive effort (steady speed, $a = 0$):**
$$\boxed{TE = R_{total} = 1663.5 \text{ N}}$$

**Motor torque:**
$$T_{motor} = \frac{TE \cdot r_w}{G \cdot \eta_{tr}} = \frac{1663.5 \times 0.31}{8.0 \times 0.93} = \frac{515.7}{7.44}$$

$$\boxed{T_{motor} = 69.31 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Adhesion check:**

Maximum tractive effort that can be transmitted without slip:
$$TE_{max} = \mu \cdot W = 0.75 \times 17658 = 13243.5 \text{ N}$$

Since $TE_{required} = 1663.5$ N $\ll TE_{max} = 13243.5$ N:

$$\boxed{\text{The vehicle can climb the 8 \% slope safely without wheel slip.}}$$

**Safety margin:**
$$\text{Margin} = \frac{13243.5 - 1663.5}{13243.5} \times 100\% = 87.4\%$$

The vehicle is far below the adhesion limit — it can climb much steeper grades (theoretically up to ~75 % grade on this surface) before slipping.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (where students lose marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing $W$ and $M$:** Students write $W = M$ in Newtons (wrong) or $M = W$ in kg (wrong). Always remember $W = M \cdot g$ in Newtons.
> 2. **Using $V$ in km/h directly in $TE \times V$ formula:** This gives the wrong answer by a factor of 3.6. Always convert to m/s.
> 3. **Forgetting $R_{grad} = 0$ on level road:** Many students add a gradient term even when slope is zero.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption $a = 0$** when the problem says "constant speed" — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 5. **Mixing up $\mu_r$ (rolling) with $\mu$ (adhesion):** These are different physical quantities. $\mu_r \approx 0.01\text{–}0.02$; $\mu \approx 0.5\text{–}0.9$.
> 6. **Forgetting to include transmission efficiency** when computing motor torque from wheel TE.
> 7. **Incorrect sign for gradient:** Use $+W \sin\theta$ if climbing upward, $-W \sin\theta$ if descending (gravity helps).
> 8. **Block diagram without arrows or labels:** KTU requires a *labelled* block diagram — unmarked boxes fetch only partial credit.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this list for last-minute revision before the KTU exam.**

✅ **Definition:** Electric traction = use of electrical energy for vehicle propulsion via traction motor(s).

✅ **Five Building Blocks:** Battery → Inverter → Motor → Transmission → Wheels.

✅ **Three Resistive Forces:** $R_{roll} = \mu_r W$; $R_{grad} = W \sin\theta$; $R_{air} = \frac{1}{2}\rho C_d A V^2$.

✅ **Newton's Law for Vehicle:** $TE - R_{total} = M a$.

✅ **Tractive Effort from Motor:** $TE = \dfrac{T_{motor} \cdot G \cdot \eta_{tr}}{r_w}$.

✅ **Power Equations:** $P_{wheel} = TE \cdot V$; $P_{motor} = T_{motor} \cdot \omega_m$.

✅ **Adhesion Limit:** $TE \le \mu W$ (else wheels slip).

✅ **Speed Conversion:** $V_{m/s} = V_{km/h} / 3.6$.

✅ **Most-used EV Motor (2024):** PMSM (highest power density + efficiency).

✅ **Single-speed gearbox** is enough for EVs because of flat constant-power torque curve.

✅ **Regenerative Braking:** Motor acts as generator; kinetic energy → battery.

✅ **EV Classification:** BEV, HEV, PHEV, FCEV.

✅ **Drive-Train Configurations:** Single-motor RWD, Multi-speed, Dual-motor AWD, In-wheel hub.

✅ **Constants to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $\mu_r \approx 0.01\text{–}0.02$, $\mu \approx 0.5\text{–}0.9$.

✅ **Energy Flow Direction:** Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels (motoring); reverse during regen.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Various Electric Drive-Train Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Various Electric Drive-Train Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Electric Drive-Train Topology?

In the context of electric and hybrid vehicles, a **drive-train topology** refers to the **systematic arrangement and interconnection** of the prime mover (Internal Combustion Engine / Fuel Cell), energy storage system (Battery / Ultra-capacitor), power electronics converters, and electric traction motors that deliver tractive power to the driving wheels of a vehicle. The topology defines **how mechanical and electrical energy flows** between these subsystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> *An electric drive-train topology is the architectural configuration that dictates the power-flow path between the energy source, energy converter, transmission, and the final drive (wheels) of an electric or hybrid vehicle. The choice of topology fundamentally governs the vehicle's efficiency, performance, packaging, cost, and emission profile.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine the human circulatory system. The **heart** is the energy source (battery/IC engine), the **arteries** are the power transmission paths (shafts, cables, converters), and the **muscles/limbs** are the wheels doing the actual work. Just as a cardiologist decides whether blood should flow through a *single* path, *parallel* paths, or a *combination* of both, an EV engineer chooses a **drive-train topology** to decide how power flows from source to wheels.

A **series topology** is like a single highway where all traffic funnels through one express lane. A **parallel topology** is like a city with two parallel main roads — traffic can be split. A **series-parallel** is a smart interchange that uses both depending on traffic (load) conditions.

## 1.3 Classification of Electric Drive-Train Topologies

KTU 2024 classifies drive-train topologies into **five major families**:

1. **Conventional (Mechanical) Drive-Train** — Pure ICE vehicle (baseline reference).
2. **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)** — Mechanical decoupling; only motor drives wheels.
3. **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)** — Both ICE and motor can drive wheels mechanically.
4. **Series-Parallel Hybrid (PSHEV / Power-Split)** — Combined architecture using planetary gear sets.
5. **Fully Electric Vehicle (BEV) & Fuel Cell EV (FCEV)** — Zero on-board combustion; pure electric drive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Benchmarking Standards (used globally and adopted by KTU curriculum):**
> - **Specific Energy:** **Wh/kg** of energy source.
> - **Specific Power:** **W/kg** of energy source.
> - **Energy Efficiency:** Typically **60–95 %** for EVs vs. **20–35 %** for ICE vehicles.
> - **Gravimetric Density** and **Volumetric Density** of batteries/ultra-capacitors.

## 1.4 Why Drive-Train Topology Matters

| Selection Criterion | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|
| **Efficiency** | Determines energy conversion losses at each stage. |
| **Packaging & Weight** | Affects vehicle mass distribution and cabin space. |
| **Cost** | More power electronics → higher BOM cost. |
| **Control Complexity** | Series < Parallel < Power-Split. |
| **Emission Profile** | Determines tailpipe CO₂, NOₓ, PM reduction. |

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU exam framing: *"Justify why a series hybrid is preferred for city buses while a parallel hybrid is preferred for passenger cars."* — The answer hinges on the **duty cycle** (stop-go vs. cruising) and the **size of the traction motor** required.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Topology Analysis

### 2.1.1 Conventional (Mechanical) Drive-Train
- **Architecture:** Engine → Clutch → Transmission (gearbox) → Differential → Wheels.
- **Single energy source:** Petroleum fuel tank + ICE only.
- **No electrical component** other than 12 V auxiliaries.
- **Efficiency chain:** Tank-to-Wheel ≈ **20–35 %**.
- **Drawbacks for KTU context:** No regenerative braking, no idle stop, no torque-fill, fails every modern emission norm.

### 2.1.2 Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)
- **Architecture:** ICE → Generator → Rectifier → DC Bus → Inverter → Motor → Wheels.
- **Key feature:** **No mechanical link** between ICE and wheels. The ICE acts purely as a **range extender** (often called an *Auxiliary Power Unit, APU*).
- **Operating modes:**
  1. *Pure EV mode* — Battery alone powers the motor.
  2. *Pure series mode* — ICE charges the battery AND powers the motor.
  3. *Hybrid mode* — Both battery and ICE-generator contribute.
- **Why series?** ICE always operates at its **Maximum Efficiency Operating Point (MEOP)** → very low fuel consumption and emissions.
- **Drawback:** **Triple energy conversion** (mech → elec → mech) → losses at every stage → requires a **large, expensive traction motor** rated for peak vehicle power.

### 2.1.3 Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)
- **Architecture:** ICE ‖ Motor → Torque Coupler (clutch/gear) → Transmission → Wheels.
- **Key feature:** Both ICE and motor are **mechanically coupled** to the driveshaft and can **simultaneously** propel the vehicle.
- **Operating modes:**
  1. *Motor-alone mode* — Low-speed urban driving.
  2. *Engine-alone mode* — Highway cruising.
  3. *Combined mode* — Full acceleration / hill climbing.
  4. *Regenerative braking* — Motor acts as generator.
- **Advantage:** Smaller motor + smaller battery suffices (motor only needs to *assist*, not replace, the engine).
- **Drawback:** Mechanical coupling must be managed; ICE cannot always run at MEOP; complex clutch/gear control.

### 2.1.4 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid — PSHEV
- **Architecture:** Combines a series path (engine-generator) and a parallel path (engine-direct) using a **planetary gear set (PGS)** acting as a **power-split device (PSD)**.
- **Famous commercial example:** Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS) used in Prius.
- **Operating modes:** Continuously variable between series, parallel, and combined → optimal ICE operating point is maintained across a wide speed range.
- **Advantage:** Best of both worlds — high efficiency in city *and* highway.
- **Drawback:** Most complex and expensive; requires planetary gear with sun, ring, and carrier gears.

### 2.1.5 Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) and Fuel Cell EV (FCEV)
- **BEV:** Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels. No ICE. Zero tailpipe emission. **Tank-to-Wheel efficiency ≈ 80 %**.
- **FCEV:** Hydrogen tank → Fuel Cell → DC-DC converter → DC Bus → Inverter → Motor → Wheels. Water is the only emission.
- **Note for KTU:** BEV and FCEV are often grouped as **pure electric drive-trains** since mechanical-to-electrical energy conversion occurs *off-board* (power plant for BEV; hydrogen reformer for FCEV).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Applicable Topology |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{req} = \frac{v}{\eta_t}\left(m g f_r + \frac{1}{2}\rho A C_d v^2 + m g \sin\alpha + m \delta \frac{dv}{dt}\right)$ | $P_{req}$: required tractive power; $v$: velocity; $m$: mass; $f_r$: rolling resistance; $\rho$: air density; $C_d$: drag coefficient; $\alpha$: slope angle; $\delta$: mass factor | W (SI) | All |
| 2 | $F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$ | $F_t$: tractive force; $F_r$: rolling resistance; $F_{ad}$: aerodynamic drag; $F_{hc}$: hill climbing; $F_{la}$: linear acceleration | N | All |
| 3 | $F_r = m g f_r \cos\alpha$ | Rolling resistance force | N | All |
| 4 | $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A C_d v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag | N | All |
| 5 | $F_{hc} = m g \sin\alpha$ | Hill climbing force | N | All |
| 6 | $F_{la} = m \delta \frac{dv}{dt}$ | Linear acceleration force | N | All |
| 7 | $\eta_{TTW} = \frac{E_{wheel}}{E_{source}}$ | Tank-to-Wheel efficiency | dimensionless | All |
| 8 | $P_{ICE} + P_{bat} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_m \eta_{inv} \eta_{trans}}$ | Power balance in hybrid | W | SHEV, PHEV, PSHEV |
| 9 | $\omega_s N_s + \omega_r N_r + \omega_c N_c = 0$ | Willis equation for planetary gear set | rad/s | PSHEV |
| 10 | $E_{regen} = \int \eta_{regen} \cdot F_{brake} \cdot v \, dt$ | Energy recovered via regenerative braking | J | BEV, PHEV, PSHEV, SHEV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions & assumptions used in KTU textbook problems:**
> - Air density: $\rho \approx 1.25 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Standard gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Rolling resistance coefficient: $f_r \approx 0.015$ for cars, $0.006$ for truck tires
> - Drivetrain efficiency: $\eta_t \approx 0.85 - 0.95$
> - Rotational inertia factor: $\delta \approx 1.05 - 1.4$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding drive-train topology is the **foundation for**:
- **Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) design** — Energy management strategy (EMS) selection (rule-based vs. optimization-based).
- **Component sizing** — Motor power, battery kWh, engine displacement must be matched to topology.
- **Regulatory compliance** — EURO 6, BS-VI, CAFE norms reward certain topologies.
- **Cost engineering** — BOM cost vs. lifecycle cost trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tractive Force Equation

The total **tractive force** required at the wheels of any road vehicle is the sum of four opposing forces. We derive it from Newton's second law applied at the wheel-road contact patch.

**Step 1:** Identify the four resistive forces acting on a vehicle moving on a gradient.

$$
F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}
$$

**Step 2:** Express the rolling resistance force. A tire deforms as it rolls, and the normal reaction is reduced by the slope component. The component along the slope is:

$$
F_r = m \, g \, f_r \cos\alpha
$$

**Step 3:** Aerodynamic drag from Bernoulli-type fluid drag on the vehicle frontal area. Empirical drag equation:

$$
F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, A\, C_d\, v^2
$$

**Step 4:** Hill-climbing (gravitational) force due to elevation change:

$$
F_{hc} = m \, g \, \sin\alpha
$$

**Step 5:** Linear acceleration force, including rotational inertia of rotating parts (wheels, gears, motor rotor) via the mass factor $\delta$:

$$
F_{la} = m \, \delta \, \frac{dv}{dt}
$$

**Step 6:** Assemble the full expression:

$$
F_t = m g f_r \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A C_d v^2 + m g \sin\alpha + m \delta \frac{dv}{dt}
$$

**Step 7:** Tractive power required at the wheels is force times velocity:

$$
P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v
$$

**Step 8:** Accounting for drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t$, the power demanded from the energy source is:

$$
P_{req} = \frac{v}{\eta_t}\left(m g f_r \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A C_d v^2 + m g \sin\alpha + m \delta \frac{dv}{dt}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important equation** in EV design. It is asked in nearly every KTU Part B question on Module 1.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Planetary Gear Set (Willis Equation) for Power-Split Hybrid

A planetary gear set has three members: **sun gear (S)**, **ring gear (R)**, and **carrier (C)**, each rotating about a common axis. We derive the kinematic relation.

**Step 1:** Geometric constraint — the ring gear teeth equal the sun gear teeth plus twice the planet gear teeth:

$$
N_r = N_s + 2 N_p
$$

**Step 2:** Speed of the ring gear equals the speed of the sun gear plus the speed of the carrier, scaled by tooth counts. Using the no-slip condition at the planet-sun and planet-ring mesh:

$$
N_s\, \omega_s - N_p\, \omega_p = N_p\, \omega_p - N_r\, \omega_r
$$

**Step 3:** The planet gear rotates with the carrier (planets are mounted on carrier pins), so $\omega_p = \omega_c$.

$$
N_s\, \omega_s - N_p\, \omega_c = N_p\, \omega_c - N_r\, \omega_r
$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging:

$$
N_s\, \omega_s + N_r\, \omega_r = 2 N_p\, \omega_c
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $N_r = N_s + 2 N_p$ into the previous expression and simplify:

$$
N_s\, \omega_s + (N_s + 2 N_p)\, \omega_r = 2 N_p\, \omega_c
$$

$$
N_s(\omega_s + \omega_r) + 2 N_p (\omega_r - \omega_c) = 0
$$

**Step 6:** Divide throughout by $N_s$ and use $k = N_r / N_s$ to obtain the standard **Willis equation**:

$$
\omega_s\, N_s + \omega_r\, N_r + \omega_c\, N_c = 0
$$

where, by sign convention, $N_c = -(N_s + N_r)$.

**Step 7:** Solve for ICE speed (carrier in THS) in terms of motor (sun) and wheels (ring) speeds:

$$
\omega_c = -\frac{N_s\, \omega_s + N_r\, \omega_r}{N_c}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering insight:** By continuously varying the speed of the *sun gear* (MG1 in Toyota THS), the *carrier* (driven by the ICE) can be held at its MEOP irrespective of vehicle speed. This is the genius of the power-split topology.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Stored in Battery and Range Estimation

**Step 1:** Usable energy of a battery pack:

$$
E_{bat} = V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah} \cdot DoD
$$

where $V_{nom}$ is nominal voltage, $C_{Ah}$ is ampere-hour capacity, and $DoD$ is depth-of-discharge (typically 0.8).

**Step 2:** Convert to Wh:

$$
E_{bat}\text{[Wh]} = V_{nom}\text{[V]} \times C_{Ah}\text{[Ah]} \times DoD
$$

**Step 3:** Average power demand for a driving cycle (e.g., Indian Drive Cycle):

$$
\bar{P}_{cycle} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T F_t(t)\, v(t)\, dt
$$

**Step 4:** Theoretical driving range:

$$
R = \frac{E_{bat}\cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}{\bar{P}_{cycle}}
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the value of $E_{bat}$:

$$
R = \frac{V_{nom}\, C_{Ah}\, DoD \cdot \eta_t}{\bar{P}_{cycle}}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU-Favourite Table)

| Parameter | Conventional | Series Hybrid | Parallel Hybrid | Series-Parallel | BEV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Petrol/Diesel | Petrol + Battery | Petrol + Battery | Petrol + Battery | Battery only |
| **Mechanical link to wheels** | Yes (direct) | No (only via motor) | Yes (engine + motor) | Yes (engine + motor) | No (motor only) |
| **Motor Size** | None | Large (full propulsion) | Small (assist) | Medium | Large |
| **Battery Size** | 12 V starter only | Medium-Large | Small-Medium | Small-Medium | Large |
| **Regenerative Braking** | No | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | High (gearbox) | Low | Medium | Very High (planetary) | Very Low |
| **Control Complexity** | Low | Medium | Medium | Very High | Low |
| **Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency** | **20–35 %** | **35–45 %** | **40–50 %** | **45–55 %** | **75–90 %** |
| **Tailpipe Emission** | High | Low | Low-Medium | Low | Zero |
| **Typical Application** | Legacy IC cars | City buses, garbage trucks | Mid-size sedans | Premium hybrids (Prius) | Modern EVs (Tata Nexon EV) |
| **Example Vehicle** | Maruti Alto | Chevy Volt (range-extender mode) | Honda Civic Hybrid | Toyota Prius | Tata Tiago EV |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conventional Drive-Train (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ICE]
    B --> C[CLUTCH]
    C --> D[TRANSMISSION GEARBOX]
    D --> E[DIFFERENTIAL]
    E --> F[DRIVE WHEELS]
    G[12V BATTERY] -.-> B
```

## 4.2 Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ICE]
    B --> C[GENERATOR]
    C --> D[RECTIFIER]
    D --> E[DC BUS]
    F[TRACTION BATTERY] <--> E
    E --> G[INVERTER VSI]
    G --> H[TRACTION MOTOR]
    H --> I[FIXED RATIO GEAR]
    I --> J[DIFFERENTIAL]
    J --> K[DRIVE WHEELS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Note the **absence of a mechanical shaft** between the engine and the wheels. The ICE drives *only* the generator.

## 4.3 Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ICE]
    F[TRACTION BATTERY] --> G[INVERTER]
    G --> H[TRACTION MOTOR]
    B --> I[TORQUE COUPLER CLUTCH]
    H --> I
    I --> J[TRANSMISSION]
    J --> K[DIFFERENTIAL]
    K --> L[DRIVE WHEELS]
```

## 4.4 Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid — PSHEV

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL TANK] --> B[ICE]
    F[TRACTION BATTERY] --> G1[INVERTER MG1]
    F --> G2[INVERTER MG2]
    G1 --> M1[MG1 MOTOR-GENERATOR]
    G2 --> M2[MG2 MOTOR]
    B --> P[PLANETARY GEAR SET PSD]
    M1 <--> P
    P --> R[RING GEAR TO WHEELS]
    M2 --> R
    R --> S[DIFFERENTIAL]
    S --> T[DRIVE WHEELS]
```

## 4.5 Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY] --> B[DC-DC CONVERTER]
    B --> C[12V AUXILIARY BATTERY]
    A --> D[INVERTER VSI]
    D --> E[TRACTION MOTOR]
    E --> F[GEARBOX SINGLE SPEED]
    F --> G[DIFFERENTIAL]
    G --> H[DRIVE WHEELS]
    H -. REGEN .-> E
    E -. REGEN .-> D
    D -. REGEN .-> A
```

## 4.6 Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[H2 TANK] --> B[FUEL CELL STACK]
    B --> C[DC-DC BOOST CONVERTER]
    C --> D[DC BUS]
    E[TRACTION BATTERY BUFFER] <--> D
    D --> F[INVERTER]
    F --> G[TRACTION MOTOR]
    G --> H[TRANSMISSION]
    H --> I[DRIVE WHEELS]
```

## 4.7 Comparative Topology Selection Flowchart (Engineering Decision)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Vehicle Duty Cycle] --> B{Need Zero Tailpipe Emission?}
    B -- YES --> C[Long Range Required?]
    B -- NO --> D[Existing ICE Platform Available?]
    C -- YES --> E[FCEV]
    C -- NO --> F[BEV]
    D -- YES --> G[Budget for Electrification?]
    D -- NO --> H[BEV or FCEV]
    G -- LOW --> I[Parallel Hybrid]
    G -- MEDIUM --> J[Series Hybrid]
    G -- HIGH --> K[Series-Parallel Power-Split]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above decision tree is the **exact logic** the KTU examiner expects when asking *"Justify the choice of drive-train topology for a given vehicle application."*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define the term *electric drive-train topology*. List any four major electric drive-train topologies used in modern electric vehicles. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **electric drive-train topology** is the systematic arrangement of the energy source, energy converters, transmission, and traction drive in an electric or hybrid vehicle, defining how power flows from source to wheels. The four major topologies are:

1. **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV)**
2. **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (PHEV)**
3. **Series-Parallel (Power-Split) Hybrid (PSHEV)**
4. **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)**

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for the four topologies = 3 marks.)*

---

### **Q2.** State the *Willis equation* for a planetary gear set and explain the role of the planetary gear in a series-parallel hybrid electric vehicle. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Willis equation** governing the speeds of the three members (sun S, ring R, carrier C) of a planetary gear set is:

$$
N_s\, \omega_s + N_r\, \omega_r + N_c\, \omega_c = 0
$$

where $N_c = -(N_s + N_r)$. The **planetary gear set** acts as a **power-split device (PSD)** in a series-parallel hybrid. It continuously divides the ICE's mechanical power between a *mechanical path* to the wheels and an *electrical path* through MG1 (acting as generator), allowing the ICE to operate at its **Maximum Efficiency Operating Point (MEOP)** irrespective of vehicle speed. *(1 mark for equation + 2 marks for explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Skipping the energy-conversion chain:** In SHEV questions, students forget to mention that the ICE → Generator → Inverter → Motor path involves **three energy conversions**, and only state the components.
> - **Confusing PSHEV with SHEV:** Power-split topology is *not* a "series topology with two motors" — it must be drawn with the **planetary gear set explicitly** as the power-split device.
> - **Units slip-ups:** Tractive force should be in **N**; power in **W** (or kW); energy in **J** (or Wh). Marks are deducted for missing or wrong units.
> - **Willis equation sign convention:** Many students forget the negative sign on $N_c$. Always state the convention used.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Regular]`

**With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture, modes of operation, advantages, and limitations of a Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (SHEV). Derive the tractive force equation governing the vehicle motion.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture (7 marks):**

A SHEV consists of the following components connected in series: **Fuel tank → ICE (Internal Combustion Engine) → Generator → Power Electronics Rectifier → DC Bus → Traction Battery (in parallel) → Inverter (VSI) → Traction Motor → Transmission → Differential → Wheels.**

The defining feature is the **absence of any mechanical coupling** between the ICE and the driving wheels. The ICE is mechanically decoupled from the wheels and serves only as a prime mover for the electrical generator. The DC bus is a common electrical node shared by the rectifier output, the battery, and the inverter input. The battery is connected in parallel with the generator-rectifier output through a DC-DC converter (in some architectures) to allow bidirectional power flow.

**Modes of operation:**
1. **Pure EV mode:** ICE OFF. Battery alone supplies DC bus; inverter drives motor. (Used at low speed, low load, or zero-emission zones.)
2. **Pure series mode:** ICE ON at MEOP. Generator output equals motor demand; battery is neither charged nor discharged. (Used during steady cruising.)
3. **Charging mode:** ICE generates more than motor demand; excess charges battery. (Used during idle or light load.)
4. **Boost mode:** Both battery and ICE-generator supply the motor; used for peak acceleration. (Sum of powers = motor power.)

**Advantages:**
- ICE always runs at its **Maximum Efficiency Operating Point (MEOP)** → high fuel economy, low emissions.
- Simple mechanical layout → no clutch, no conventional multi-speed gearbox.
- Ideal for **stop-go city duty cycles** (city buses, refuse trucks, delivery vans).
- Smooth and silent operation.

**Limitations:**
- **Triple energy conversion** (mech → elec → mech) at full load → cumulative losses.
- Traction motor must be sized for **peak vehicle power** → large, heavy, expensive.
- Long highway drives are inefficient because the motor-generator chain incurs losses even when battery is full.

**(b) Tractive force equation derivation (7 marks):**

The total tractive force required at the wheel-road contact patch equals the sum of **four opposing forces**:

$$
F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}
$$

Substituting the expressions derived in Section 3.1:

$$
F_t = m g f_r \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A C_d v^2 + m g \sin\alpha + m \delta \frac{dv}{dt}
$$

The required power at the source is:

$$
P_{req} = \frac{v}{\eta_t}\left(m g f_r \cos\alpha + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A C_d v^2 + m g \sin\alpha + m \delta \frac{dv}{dt}\right)
$$

where $m$ = vehicle mass (kg), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $f_r$ = rolling resistance coefficient, $\alpha$ = road gradient angle, $\rho$ = air density (≈1.25 kg/m³), $A$ = frontal area (m²), $C_d$ = drag coefficient, $v$ = velocity (m/s), $\delta$ = rotational inertia factor (1.05–1.4), $\eta_t$ = drivetrain efficiency.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**Compare the Series, Parallel, and Series-Parallel (Power-Split) hybrid drive-train topologies. With a block diagram, explain the operation of a Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS) and derive the Willis equation for the planetary gear set used.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative analysis of the three hybrid topologies (7 marks):**

A comparative table is presented (see Section 3.4 of these notes). Key differentiating points:

- **SHEV:** Mechanical decoupling, ICE always at MEOP, large motor, ideal for city buses, lower highway efficiency.
- **PHEV:** Mechanical coupling of ICE and motor, both can propel wheels directly, smaller motor suffices, ideal for mid-size passenger cars with mixed urban-highway use.
- **PSHEV:** Combines series and parallel paths via a planetary gear set acting as a **Power-Split Device (PSD)**. The ICE, MG1, MG2, and wheels are interconnected through the PSD. MG1 (a small motor-generator) controls the ICE speed by adjusting the sun gear speed; MG2 (a larger motor) directly drives the wheels or recovers regenerative energy. The result is **continuous, seamless variation** of the operating point — the best of both series and parallel — at the cost of higher mechanical and control complexity.

**Toyota Hybrid Synergy Drive (THS) — block diagram and operation:**

The THS uses a **planetary gear set (PGS)** as the **power-split device**. The three members are:
- **Sun gear** → connected to **MG1 (motor-generator 1)**.
- **Carrier** → connected to the **ICE**.
- **Ring gear** → connected to the **driveshaft** (wheels) and **MG2 (motor-generator 2)**.

**Operating logic:**
- During **start-up and low-speed driving**: ICE is OFF. MG2 drives the wheels. MG1 may spin freely to balance the planetary gear.
- During **steady cruising**: ICE drives the carrier. MG1 acts as a generator (controlled speed) to feed MG2 and the battery; MG2 adds torque to the ring gear.
- During **full acceleration**: ICE + MG2 both deliver torque to the wheels; MG1 is controlled to keep the ICE at MEOP.
- During **regenerative braking**: MG2 acts as a generator, charging the battery; ICE is OFF.

**(b) Derivation of the Willis equation (7 marks):**

Detailed derivation provided in Section 3.2 of these notes. Final result:

$$
N_s\, \omega_s + N_r\, \omega_r + N_c\, \omega_c = 0
$$

where $N_c = -(N_s + N_r)$, and $\omega_s, \omega_r, \omega_c$ are the angular speeds of the sun, ring, and carrier gears respectively. This equation governs the kinematic constraint of the planetary gear set and is the **mathematical foundation** of the THS power-split strategy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must remember for KTU 2024 exams:**

- [ ] **Five topologies:** Conventional, SHEV, PHEV, PSHEV, BEV/FCEV.
- [ ] **SHEV = no mechanical link** between ICE and wheels. ICE drives a generator only.
- [ ] **PHEV = mechanical coupling** of ICE and motor via a clutch/coupler; both can drive wheels.
- [ ] **PSHEV uses a planetary gear set (PGS)** as the **Power-Split Device (PSD)**.
- [ ] **Willis equation:** $N_s \omega_s + N_r \omega_r + N_c \omega_c = 0$ — derive it cold, including the $N_c = -(N_s + N_r)$ convention.
- [ ] **Tractive force equation** has **four terms** — rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, hill climbing, linear acceleration. Memorize all four forms and units.
- [ ] **MEOP** = Maximum Efficiency Operating Point of ICE. Series and power-split topologies allow ICE to track MEOP.
- [ ] **Tank-to-Wheel efficiency** ordering: BEV (75–90 %) > PSHEV (45–55 %) > PHEV (40–50 %) > SHEV (35–45 %) > ICE (20–35 %).
- [ ] **Regenerative braking** is possible in SHEV, PHEV, PSHEV, and BEV — **not** in conventional ICE.
- [ ] **Motor sizing rule:** SHEV → motor rated for **peak** vehicle power. PHEV → motor rated for **~30–50 %** of peak power. PSHEV → split between MG1 and MG2.
- [ ] **FCEV** uses a **DC-DC boost converter** because fuel cell output voltage varies widely with load.
- [ ] **BEV** is sometimes called a **"pure electric"** drive-train; FCEV is also pure-electric in the sense that *no on-board combustion* occurs.
- [ ] **Toyota Prius / Camry Hybrid** = real-world example of PSHEV using THS.
- [ ] **Honda Insight (1st gen)** = example of PHEV with integrated motor assist (IMA).
- [ ] **Chevrolet Volt (1st gen)** = example of SHEV with a large battery (sometimes called "Extended-Range Electric Vehicle" or EREV).
- [ ] **Units to memorize:** N (force), W (power), J (energy), Wh (battery energy), rad/s (angular speed), kg/m³ (air density), m² (frontal area).
- [ ] **Decision criterion for topology selection:** Duty cycle, range requirement, cost budget, emission target, packaging constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**
> Always draw the **block diagram** before answering *any* topology question. Even if the question does not explicitly ask for one, including a clearly labeled block diagram is worth **1–2 easy marks** and demonstrates command over the topic. Always state the **units** in derivations. Always end a comparison question with a **summary table** — examiners love tables because they save valuation time and reward structured answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Flow Control in Electric Drive-Train Topologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Flow Control in Electric Drive-Train Topologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Power Flow Control in Electric Drive-Train Topologies** refers to the orchestrated management and regulation of electrical and mechanical energy transfer between the energy source (typically a high-voltage traction battery or fuel cell), power electronic converters (DC-DC converters and DC-AC inverters), the electric traction motor(s), the mechanical transmission system, and the vehicle's wheels, with the objective of meeting the driver's torque and speed demand while maximizing efficiency, range, and component longevity.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the **electric drive-train topology** defines the *physical architecture* connecting the energy storage, power conversion, and propulsion sub-systems, while **power flow control** defines the *real-time software and hardware strategy* that decides *which path* the power takes, *in what proportion*, and *at what efficiency* under varying road-load conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The module explicitly covers *configuration of electric drive-trains* (single-motor, dual-motor, in-wheel), *power flow control between battery–inverter–motor–wheels*, and the *role of DC-DC converters and regenerative braking* in establishing bidirectional power flow.

## 1.2 The Three Canonical Drive-Train Topologies

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Exam Critical Concept — Memorize the classification below verbatim.**

| Topology | Power Paths | Number of Energy Converters | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV) — Single Motor** | Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels (one path) | 1 inverter + 1 motor | Compact cars, two-wheelers |
| **Series Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV-S)** | Engine → Generator → Battery ↔ Inverter → Motor → Wheels | 2 electrical + 1 mechanical | Buses, long-haul trucks |
| **Parallel Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV-P)** | Engine ↔ Transmission ↔ Wheels **AND** Battery → Inverter → Motor → Transmission → Wheels | 1 inverter + 1 motor (mechanical torque coupling) | Passenger sedans (Toyota Prius-style) |
| **Series-Parallel / Power-Split HEV** | Combines series + parallel paths via planetary gear (Toyota THS) | 2 motors + 1 engine + planetary set | Mid-size premium hybrids |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Water-Pipeline Network" Analogy:**
> Imagine an EV drive-train as a **municipal water supply network**:
> - The **battery** is the *main water reservoir*.
> - The **DC-DC converter** is a *pressure booster pump* (steps voltage up or down).
> - The **inverter** is the *faucet controller* (converts steady DC "flow" into 3-phase AC "pulses").
> - The **motor** is the *turbine* that converts water energy into rotational mechanical work.
> - The **transmission and wheels** are the *pipes leading to the city*.
> - **Power Flow Control** is the *city's central SCADA system* — it monitors demand from every neighborhood (driver pedal input, road gradient, vehicle speed) and decides: "Should I open the reservoir? Should I send reverse flow back from the turbine during downhill braking (regeneration)? Should I split the supply between two parallel pipelines?"
>
> Without this central controller, water would flood some areas and leave others dry — just as uncontrolled power flow would either over-torque the wheels (danger) or waste battery energy as heat.

## 1.4 The Three Universal Control Objectives

1. **Torque Tracking:** The instantaneous motor electromagnetic torque $T_m$ must match the driver's demanded torque $T_{req}$ derived from the accelerator pedal position, with a dynamic response time typically **< 50 ms**.
2. **Speed Tracking:** The motor angular speed $\omega_m$ must be regulated to maintain the commanded vehicle speed, especially under cruise conditions.
3. **Energy Optimization:** Minimize $P_{loss}$ across all conversion stages so that battery State-of-Charge (SoC) depletion is minimized per kilometre — quantified as **kWh / 100 km**.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations

- **Vehicle mass** $M_v$ — typically **1500–2500 kg** for a passenger BEV.
- **Wheel radius** $r_w$ — typically **0.30–0.36 m**.
- **Gravitational acceleration** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.
- **Rolling resistance coefficient** $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.012$ (radial tyres on asphalt).
- **Aerodynamic drag coefficient** $C_d \approx 0.25$–$0.30$ (modern EVs).
- **Frontal area** $A_f \approx 2.2$–$2.5 \text{ m}^2$.
- **Air density** $\rho_a = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **wheel torque** $T_w$ with **motor torque** $T_m$. The transmission ratio $N$ (or gear ratio $G$) relates them: $T_w = N \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot T_m$. Forgetting the efficiency $\eta_{trans}$ costs a full mark.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Road-Load Force Equation — The Starting Point of All Power-Flow Analysis

The tractive force $F_t$ required at the wheels to propel the vehicle is governed by Newton's second law projected onto the road:

$$F_t = M_v \cdot a + M_v \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + \mu_{rr} \cdot M_v \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

where each term has a clear physical meaning (see Step 2 below).

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Physical Decomposition of the Road-Load Equation

- **Step 1 — Identify the four force components acting on the vehicle body parallel to the road surface:**
  - Inertial force $\to$ required to *accelerate* the mass.
  - Grade-climbing force $\to$ required to *lift* the mass against gravity on a slope of angle $\theta$.
  - Rolling resistance force $\to$ friction loss in the tyres.
  - Aerodynamic drag force $\to$ viscous loss in the surrounding air.
- **Step 2 — Why each coefficient?**
  - $M_v \cdot a$ → $a = \frac{dv}{dt}$ is the longitudinal acceleration in $\text{m/s}^2$.
  - $M_v \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ → the component of weight parallel to the slope.
  - $\mu_{rr} \cdot M_v \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta$ → tyre deformation losses (proportional to the *normal* reaction on flat ground).
  - $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2$ → from the standard drag equation in fluid mechanics.
- **Step 3 — Convert force at wheels to motor torque:** multiply by wheel radius $r_w$ to get wheel torque, then divide by transmission ratio $N$ and transmission efficiency $\eta_{trans}$ to get motor shaft torque.
- **Step 4 — Convert motor torque to electrical power:** multiply by motor angular speed $\omega_m$ and divide by motor + inverter efficiency $(\eta_m \cdot \eta_{inv})$.

## 2.3 Power Flow — Battery to Wheels (Propulsion Mode)

The mechanical power demand at the wheels $P_{wheel}$ cascades backward through the drive-train to determine the electrical power $P_{bat}$ that must be drawn from the battery:

$$P_{bat} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{DC-DC}}$$

This single equation is the **backbone of every KTU numerical problem** on drive-train power flow.

## 2.4 Power Flow — Wheels to Battery (Regenerative Braking Mode)

When the driver releases the accelerator or presses the brake, the motor becomes a generator. The recovered power flowing *back into* the battery is:

$$P_{bat,charge} = P_{wheel,brake} \cdot \eta_{trans} \cdot \eta_{m,gen} \cdot \eta_{inv,rect} \cdot \eta_{DC-DC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In propulsion mode, the inverter acts as a DC→AC converter. In regen mode, the *same* inverter operates in reverse as an AC→DC *rectifier*. This is why the inverter is called a **bidirectional** power electronic converter.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU board-exam problems on this topic reduce to the formulas below. Memorize the table.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tractive force | $F_t = M_v a + M_v g \sin\theta + \mu_{rr} M_v g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2$ | N | — |
| 2 | Wheel torque | $T_w = F_t \cdot r_w$ | N·m | — |
| 3 | Motor torque | $T_m = \dfrac{T_w}{N \cdot \eta_{trans}}$ | N·m | — |
| 4 | Motor angular speed | $\omega_m = \dfrac{v \cdot N}{r_w}$ | rad/s | — |
| 5 | Mechanical power at wheels | $P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v = T_w \cdot \omega_w$ | W | — |
| 6 | Electrical power from battery | $P_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{trans}\eta_m\eta_{inv}\eta_{DC-DC}}$ | W | Total $\eta$ ≈ 0.78–0.88 |
| 7 | Regenerative charging power | $P_{chg} = P_{wheel,brake} \cdot \eta_{trans}\eta_{m,gen}\eta_{inv,rect}\eta_{DC-DC}$ | W | Limited by $P_{bat,max}$ and $SoC < 100\%$ |
| 8 | Vehicle kinetic energy | $E_{kin} = \tfrac{1}{2} M_v v^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} I_w \omega_w^2$ | J | $I_w$ = wheel inertia |
| 9 | DC-DC converter ratio (boost) | $V_{link} = \dfrac{V_{bat}}{1 - D}$ | V | Duty $D \in [0,1)$ |
| 10 | Induction motor torque (steady state) | $T_m = \dfrac{3 P_p}{2} \cdot \dfrac{L_m^2}{L_r} \cdot \dfrac{\omega_{sl} \cdot I_d^2}{1 + (\omega_{sl}\tau_r)^2}$ | N·m | Slip $\omega_{sl}$ controlled |
| 11 | PMSM torque (dq-frame) | $T_m = \tfrac{3}{2} p \left[ \lambda_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q \right]$ | N·m | For non-salient: $L_d = L_q$ |
| 12 | SoC dynamics | $SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) - \dfrac{1}{E_{bat}} \int P_{bat}(t) \, dt$ | — | $E_{bat}$ in Wh |
| 13 | Energy consumed per km | $E_{km} = \dfrac{\int P_{bat} \, dt}{d} = \dfrac{1}{d}\int F_t v \, dt \cdot \dfrac{1}{\eta_{tot}}$ | Wh/km | Typical BEV: 120–180 Wh/km |
| 14 | Equivalent electric vehicle C-rate | $C = \dfrac{\vert I_{bat} \vert}{Q_{bat}}$ | — | Peak ≈ 3C to 5C for acceleration |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Safety:** All vertical bar symbols $\vert$ above are rendered using the LaTeX `\vert` command, not the keyboard pipe character. This preserves the table syntax strictly.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Range Estimation Software:** Every modern EV's trip planner (e.g., Tesla Trip Planner, Google Maps EV routing) integrates $P_{bat}$ over the predicted drive cycle (WLTP / EPA / Indian IDC) to predict remaining State-of-Charge at the destination.
- **Thermal Management Coupling:** Higher $P_{bat}$ demands active battery cooling — power flow control algorithms must co-ordinate with the thermal management ECU.
- **Drive-by-Wire Stability:** During regenerative braking on ice, the controller must blend friction brakes with regen to prevent wheel lock — this is the **Anti-lock Braking Regeneration (ABR)** strategy.
- **Grid Services (V2G):** Bidirectional power flow capability is the foundation of *Vehicle-to-Grid* — the parked EV becomes a distributed energy storage asset selling power back to the grid during peak hours.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 1 — Total Drive-Train Efficiency & Required Battery Power**

**Problem Context:** A BEV of mass $M_v = 1800 \text{ kg}$ is cruising at $v = 90 \text{ km/h}$ on a flat road ($\theta = 0$). Compute the electrical power the battery must deliver, the current drawn, and the energy consumed in 1 hour of steady driving.

**Given Data (KTU standard values):**
- $C_d = 0.28$, $A_f = 2.3 \text{ m}^2$, $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$, $r_w = 0.33 \text{ m}$, $N = 9$, $V_{bat} = 400 \text{ V}$
- $\eta_{trans} = 0.95$, $\eta_m = 0.93$, $\eta_{inv} = 0.97$, $\eta_{DC-DC} = 0.96$
- $\rho_a = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $a = 0$ (cruise)

### Step 1 — Convert speed to SI units
$$v = 90 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{90 \times 1000}{3600} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the four road-load force components

**Inertial force** (zero, since $a = 0$):
$$F_{inertia} = M_v \cdot a = 1800 \times 0 = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Grade force** (zero, since $\theta = 0$, so $\sin 0 = 0$):
$$F_{grade} = M_v \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta = 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0 = 0 \text{ N}$$

**Rolling resistance force:**
$$F_{roll} = \mu_{rr} \cdot M_v \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta = 0.012 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times \cos 0$$

Let us explicitly evaluate:
$$0.012 \times 1800 = 21.6$$
$$21.6 \times 9.81 = 211.896 \text{ N}$$

So,
$$F_{roll} = 211.9 \text{ N}$$

**Aerodynamic drag force:**
$$F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_a \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

Substitute the numbers:
$$\tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.3 \times 25^2$$

Evaluate piece by piece:
$$\tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 = 0.6125$$
$$0.6125 \times 0.28 = 0.1715$$
$$0.1715 \times 2.3 = 0.39445$$
$$25^2 = 625$$
$$0.39445 \times 625 = 246.53 \text{ N}$$

So,
$$F_{aero} \approx 246.5 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3 — Total tractive force
$$F_t = 0 + 0 + 211.9 + 246.5 = 458.4 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4 — Power at the wheels
$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v = 458.4 \times 25 = 11460 \text{ W} \approx 11.46 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 5 — Total drive-train efficiency (cascade)
$$\eta_{tot} = \eta_{DC-DC} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_{trans}$$

Substitute:
$$\eta_{tot} = 0.96 \times 0.97 \times 0.93 \times 0.95$$

Evaluate step by step:
$$0.96 \times 0.97 = 0.9312$$
$$0.9312 \times 0.93 = 0.8660$$
$$0.8660 \times 0.95 = 0.8227$$

So,
$$\eta_{tot} \approx 0.823 \; (\text{or } 82.3\%)$$

### Step 6 — Battery electrical power required
$$P_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{tot}} = \dfrac{11.46}{0.823} \approx 13.92 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 7 — Battery current
$$I_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{bat}}{V_{bat}} = \dfrac{13920}{400} = 34.8 \text{ A}$$

### Step 8 — Energy consumed in 1 hour
$$E_{1h} = P_{bat} \times 1 \text{ h} = 13.92 \text{ kWh}$$

### Step 9 — Distance covered in 1 hour
$$d = v \cdot t = 25 \text{ m/s} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 90000 \text{ m} = 90 \text{ km}$$

### Step 10 — Energy consumption per km
$$E_{km} = \dfrac{E_{1h}}{d} = \dfrac{13.92}{90} = 0.1547 \text{ kWh/km} = 154.7 \text{ Wh/km}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result Interpretation:** This number (154.7 Wh/km) is in the **typical real-world band for a mid-size BEV** (120–180 Wh/km). The 82.3% drive-train efficiency means roughly **17.7% of the battery energy** is lost as heat in the converter, motor, and transmission — the primary engineering target for range improvement.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 2 — Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery on a Downhill Gradient**

**Problem Context:** The same BEV descends a 7% grade ($\theta \approx 4^\circ$) at steady speed $v = 60 \text{ km/h}$ for $t = 30 \text{ s}$ with regenerative braking engaged. Compute the energy recovered in the battery.

### Step 1 — Convert speed
$$v = 60 \text{ km/h} = 16.667 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Road-load forces (note: grade is negative for descent in propulsion sign convention)

For a *descending* vehicle, the grade component becomes negative (it assists motion). The total tractive force required at the wheels to *hold* steady speed (resisting acceleration) is:

$$F_t = M_v a + M_v g \sin\theta - M_v g \mu_{rr} \cos\theta - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2$$

Wait — careful with sign convention: at *steady* descent with regenerative braking, the controller applies a *negative* tractive force (retarding torque). Let us re-state the energy balance correctly.

The *available* mechanical power from gravity + reduced rolling + drag is:
$$P_{mech,avail} = \left( - M_v g \sin\theta + \mu_{rr} M_v g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 \right) \cdot v$$

Note $\sin\theta$ is *negative* for a downward slope if we keep $\theta$ signed, so let us take $\theta = -4^\circ$:

$$\sin(-4^\circ) = -0.0698$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate each force at $v = 16.667 \text{ m/s}$

**Grade force (now assisting descent):**
$$F_{grade} = M_v g \sin\theta = 1800 \times 9.81 \times (-0.0698) = -1232 \text{ N}$$

**Rolling resistance:**
$$F_{roll} = 0.012 \times 1800 \times 9.81 \times \cos(4^\circ) = 211.5 \text{ N}$$

**Aerodynamic drag:**
$$F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.28 \times 2.3 \times 16.667^2 = 0.39445 \times 277.8 = 109.6 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4 — Net retarding force (gravity wins, so net is *propulsive*; we must apply regen to hold speed)
$$F_{net} = -1232 + 211.5 + 109.6 = -911 \text{ N}$$

The negative sign confirms the *gravitational* force exceeds drag + rolling — the vehicle would accelerate without intervention. The regen system must therefore *absorb* a mechanical power of:
$$P_{wheel,regen} = \vert F_{net} \vert \cdot v = 911 \times 16.667 = 15184 \text{ W} \approx 15.18 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 5 — Electrical power flowing back into the battery
$$P_{bat,chg} = P_{wheel,regen} \cdot \eta_{tot} = 15184 \times 0.823 = 12496 \text{ W} \approx 12.5 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 6 — Energy recovered in 30 seconds
$$E_{rec} = P_{bat,chg} \cdot t = 12.5 \times \dfrac{30}{3600} = 0.1042 \text{ kWh} = 104.2 \text{ Wh}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Energy Audit Insight:** In 30 s of downhill regen, we recovered ~104 Wh — enough to propel the same vehicle an extra **0.67 km** on flat road. This is why *eco-driving* apps display a "regen percent" gauge.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation 3 — Closed-Form Motor Torque Demand from Pedal Position**

**Problem Context:** A driver depresses the accelerator pedal to $\alpha = 0.6$ (60% of full travel). The accelerator maps linearly to a demanded *wheel* torque $T_{w,req} = \alpha \cdot T_{w,max}$ where $T_{w,max} = 2400 \text{ N·m}$. The transmission ratio is $N = 9$ with $\eta_{trans} = 0.95$. Compute the motor torque and the motor's q-axis current (PMSM with $\lambda_m = 0.1 \text{ V·s}$, $p = 4$ pole pairs, $L_d = L_q$).

### Step 1 — Demanded wheel torque
$$T_{w,req} = 0.6 \times 2400 = 1440 \text{ N·m}$$

### Step 2 — Motor shaft torque required (account for transmission efficiency)
$$T_{m,req} = \dfrac{T_{w,req}}{N \cdot \eta_{trans}} = \dfrac{1440}{9 \times 0.95} = \dfrac{1440}{8.55} = 168.4 \text{ N·m}$$

### Step 3 — For a non-salient PMSM, torque equation simplifies to
$$T_m = \tfrac{3}{2} p \lambda_m I_q$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $I_q$
$$I_q = \dfrac{2 T_m}{3 p \lambda_m} = \dfrac{2 \times 168.4}{3 \times 4 \times 0.1} = \dfrac{336.8}{1.2} = 280.7 \text{ A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write $T_m = \tfrac{3}{2} p (\lambda_m I_q + (L_d - L_q) I_d I_q)$. For *non-salient* surface-mounted PMSMs, $L_d = L_q$, so the reluctance term vanishes. Carrying the extra term is not wrong but it confuses the calculation. Always state the assumption: *"For a non-salient PMSM, $L_d = L_q$."*

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Compliance:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA1`, `flowB2`).
> - No reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone IDs.
> - All node labels contain raw uppercase alphanumeric text only — no bold, italic, or HTML tags.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — BEV (Single-Motor) Drive-Train Power Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1[BATTERY PACK 400V DC] -- DC CURRENT --> nodeA2
    nodeA2[DC DC CONVERTER BIDIRECTIONAL] -- STABILISED DC LINK --> nodeA3
    nodeA3[INVERTER VSI 3 PHASE IGBT] -- PWM GATING --> nodeA4
    nodeA4[TRACTION MOTOR PMSM OR IM] -- SHAFT TORQUE --> nodeA5
    nodeA5[FIXED RATIO TRANSMISSION] -- WHEEL TORQUE --> nodeA6
    nodeA6[TYRE ROAD CONTACT]
    nodeA6 -- REGEN TORQUE --> nodeA5
    nodeA5 -- BACK EMF --> nodeA4
    nodeA4 -- RECTIFIED DC --> nodeA2
    nodeA2 -- CHARGING CURRENT --> nodeA1
    nodeC1[VCU VEHICLE CONTROL UNIT] -. TORQUE COMMAND .-> nodeA3
    nodeC1 -. PEDAL MAP .-> nodeA4
    nodeC1 -. SOC GUARD .-> nodeA1
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram:** Solid arrows are *power flow*; dotted arrows are *control signals*. Notice the **symmetry** between the upper (propulsion) and lower (regen) paths — this is the visual signature of a *bidirectional* drive-train.

## 4.2 Series Hybrid Drive-Train — Engine Never Drives Wheels Directly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS1[FUEL TANK] --> nodeS2
    nodeS2[ICE ENGINE] --> nodeS3
    nodeS3[GENERATOR] --> nodeS4
    nodeS4[RECTIFIER] --> nodeS5
    nodeS5[HIGH VOLTAGE BATTERY] <--> nodeS6
    nodeS6[DC DC CONVERTER] --> nodeS7
    nodeS7[TRACTION INVERTER] --> nodeS8
    nodeS8[TRACTION MOTOR] --> nodeS9
    nodeS9[TRANSMISSION] --> nodeS10
    nodeS10[DRIVE WHEELS]
    nodeEC1[HCU HYBRID CONTROL UNIT] -. ENGINE ON OFF .-> nodeS2
    nodeEC1 -. GEN TORQUE .-> nodeS3
    nodeEC1 -. MOTOR TORQUE .-> nodeS8
    nodeEC1 -. SOC BALANCING .-> nodeS5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Series vs. Parallel:** In the **series** diagram, there is **no mechanical link** between the engine and the wheels. The engine's only output to the wheels is via *electricity*. In contrast, the parallel diagram (below) has a *direct* mechanical link.

## 4.3 Parallel Hybrid Drive-Train — Dual Mechanical + Electrical Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP1[ICE ENGINE] --> nodeP2
    nodeP2[CLUTCH OR TORQUE COUPLER] --> nodeP3
    nodeP3[TRANSMISSION] --> nodeP4
    nodeP4[FINAL DRIVE] --> nodeP5
    nodeP5[WHEELS]
    nodeB1[BATTERY] <--> nodeB2
    nodeB2[INVERTER] --> nodeB3
    nodeB3[ELECTRIC MOTOR] --> nodeP2
    nodeH1[HCU] -. ENGINE TORQUE .-> nodeP1
    nodeH1 -. MOTOR TORQUE .-> nodeB3
    nodeH1 -. CLUTCH STATE .-> nodeP2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Coupling Point Analysis:** In the parallel topology, the electric motor injects torque *into the same shaft* as the engine, **before** the transmission. This is a *torque-coupling* scheme, distinct from the *speed-coupling* (planetary) used in Toyota THS.

## 4.4 Power-Flow Mode State Machine (KTU Favourite Question)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    s1[MODE 0 IDLE] --> s2[MODE 1 PURE EV ACCEL]
    s2 --> s3[MODE 2 PURE EV CRUISE]
    s3 --> s4[MODE 3 REGEN BRAKING]
    s4 --> s1
    s2 --> s5[MODE 4 ENGINE BOOST PARALLEL]
    s3 --> s5
    s5 --> s2
    s3 --> s6[MODE 5 ENGINE ALONE]
    s6 --> s3
    s5 --> s7[MODE 6 BATTERY CHARGE FROM ENGINE]
    s7 --> s3
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mode Decoding for Exam Answers:**
> - **Mode 0** — Vehicle stationary, contactor open, no power flow.
> - **Mode 1** — High torque demand, low SoC threshold OK → only battery + motor.
> - **Mode 2** — Steady-state low torque, only motor sustains cruise.
> - **Mode 3** — Accelerator released or brake pressed → motor acts as generator, battery charges.
> - **Mode 4** — Peak acceleration (e.g., overtaking) → engine + motor both deliver torque.
> - **Mode 5** — SoC low, steady cruise, engine efficient point → engine alone drives wheels.
> - **Mode 6** — SoC critically low, vehicle cruising → engine drives *both* wheels and generator (series-like behaviour inside a parallel architecture).
