# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *Modelled on the KTU 2024 Scheme University Examination pattern. Answer each in 4–6 sentences, with a labelled diagram if applicable.*

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEEET522]
**Differentiate clearly between the "Series Hybrid" and "Parallel Hybrid" electric drive-train topologies. State one real-world vehicle example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

> In a **Series Hybrid**, there is *no mechanical coupling* between the internal combustion engine (ICE) and the wheels. The ICE drives a generator, whose electrical output either charges the battery or feeds the traction motor that propels the vehicle. Hence power flows in a *single electrical chain*. This topology is favoured in heavy vehicles like the **Chevrolet Volt (range-extender mode)** and **Nissan e-Power** series hybrids, and most city **hybrid buses**.

> In a **Parallel Hybrid**, the ICE and the electric motor are *mechanically coupled* (via a clutch, belt, or planetary gear) to the same transmission, so both can deliver torque to the wheels **simultaneously or independently**. Power flows along *two parallel paths* — one mechanical (engine) and one electrical (motor). Examples include the **Honda Civic Hybrid (IMA)** and older **Insight** models.

> The series architecture offers smoother city driving and frees the engine to run at its most efficient point; the parallel architecture is more efficient on highways because it avoids the double energy conversion (mechanical → electrical → mechanical).

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEET522]
**Explain the term "Regenerative Braking" with reference to power flow direction in an EV drive-train. Why can regenerative braking not recover 100% of the kinetic energy?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Regenerative braking** is the process by which the traction motor — normally a consumer of electrical energy — is *reversed in torque* to act as a generator, converting the vehicle's kinetic (and/or gravitational potential) energy into electrical energy that is fed back into the high-voltage battery. In power-flow terms, the direction reverses: instead of battery → inverter → motor → wheels, energy flows wheels → motor (now generator) → inverter (now rectifier) → DC-DC converter → battery.

> Complete (100%) recovery is impossible for four reasons:
> 1. **Conversion losses** — the motor-generator, inverter, and DC-DC converter have efficiencies below 100% ($\eta_{tot} \approx 0.80$ typical).
> 2. **Battery charge acceptance limit** — the battery cannot accept infinite current; above a certain C-rate, it limits regen power.
> 3. **State-of-Charge ceiling** — when the battery is near 100% SoC, no more charge can be stored, so regen is disabled and the friction brakes take over.
> 4. **Safety constraint** — on slippery surfaces, full regen can lock the wheels; hence the controller blends regen with hydraulic brakes, capping the recoverable fraction.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part B question offers an internal choice. Two completely independent alternative questions are provided below.*

---

### Part B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)  [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A passenger BEV has the following data:**
- Vehicle mass $M_v = 1500 \text{ kg}$, wheel radius $r_w = 0.32 \text{ m}$
- Frontal area $A_f = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, drag coefficient $C_d = 0.27$
- Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr} = 0.010$
- Transmission: single-speed, ratio $N = 9$, efficiency $\eta_{trans} = 0.96$
- Motor + inverter + DC-DC total efficiency $\eta_{powertrain} = 0.85$
- Battery nominal voltage $V_{bat} = 360 \text{ V}$, capacity $E_{bat} = 60 \text{ kWh}$
- $\rho_a = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Answer the following:**

#### (a) Compute the tractive force, wheel torque, and motor torque when the vehicle accelerates from rest at $a = 2 \text{ m/s}^2$ on a flat road. Also find the motor angular speed when the vehicle reaches $v = 50 \text{ km/h}$. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Convert units:**
$$v = 50 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{50 \times 1000}{3600} = 13.89 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each force component (flat road, $\theta = 0$):**

$$F_{inertia} = M_v a = 1500 \times 2 = 3000 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{grade} = 0 \text{ N} \quad (\text{flat road, } \sin 0 = 0)$$

$$F_{roll} = \mu_{rr} M_v g \cos 0 = 0.010 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 147.15 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A_f v^2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.27 \times 2.2 \times 13.89^2$$

Evaluate step by step:
$$0.5 \times 1.225 = 0.6125$$
$$0.6125 \times 0.27 = 0.1654$$
$$0.1654 \times 2.2 = 0.3639$$
$$13.89^2 = 192.93$$
$$0.3639 \times 192.93 = 70.2 \text{ N}$$

So,
$$F_{aero} \approx 70.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Total tractive force:**
$$F_t = 3000 + 0 + 147.15 + 70.2 = 3217.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Wheel torque:**
$$T_w = F_t \cdot r_w = 3217.4 \times 0.32 = 1029.6 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Motor torque (accounting for transmission efficiency):**
$$T_m = \dfrac{T_w}{N \cdot \eta_{trans}} = \dfrac{1029.6}{9 \times 0.96} = \dfrac{1029.6}{8.64} = 119.2 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 6 — Motor angular speed:**
$$\omega_m = \dfrac{v \cdot N}{r_w} = \dfrac{13.89 \times 9}{0.32} = \dfrac{125.0}{0.32} = 390.6 \text{ rad/s}$$

Convert to rpm:
$$n = \omega_m \times \dfrac{60}{2\pi} = 390.6 \times 9.549 = 3730 \text{ rpm}$$

#### (b) Determine the electrical power the battery must deliver at $v = 50 \text{ km/h}$ during this $2 \text{ m/s}^2$ acceleration, the corresponding battery current, and the theoretical range of the vehicle if the same operating point is sustained until the battery is empty. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Mechanical power at the wheels:**
$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v = 3217.4 \times 13.89 = 44,690 \text{ W} \approx 44.69 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Electrical power from the battery:**
$$P_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{powertrain}} = \dfrac{44.69}{0.85} = 52.58 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Battery current:**
$$I_{bat} = \dfrac{P_{bat}}{V_{bat}} = \dfrac{52580}{360} = 146.1 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — C-rate check:**
$$C = \dfrac{I_{bat}}{Q_{bat,Ah}} = \dfrac{146.1}{60{,}000 / 360} = \dfrac{146.1}{166.67} = 0.877 \text{ C}$$

This is well within the 3C peak limit — operation is sustainable.

**Step 5 — Theoretical range at constant operating point:**
$$t = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{P_{bat}} = \dfrac{60 \text{ kWh}}{52.58 \text{ kW}} = 1.141 \text{ h}$$
$$d = v \cdot t = 50 \text{ km/h} \times 1.141 \text{ h} = 57.05 \text{ km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 57 km is the *theoretical minimum* range — in practice, real-world cycles (urban + highway + idle) yield 250–350 km from a 60 kWh pack. The discrepancy is because the operating point here is *peak acceleration*, not *average driving*.

---

### Part B — QUESTION B (14 Marks — Internal Choice)  [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**A series-hybrid city bus has two energy sources:**

- **Diesel engine + generator set:** rated electrical output $P_{eng} = 80 \text{ kW}$ (efficiency $\eta_{eng} = 0.38$)
- **Battery pack:** $E_{bat} = 30 \text{ kWh}$, nominal voltage $V_{bat} = 600 \text{ V}$
- **Traction motor:** 120 kW peak, 80 kW continuous, efficiency $\eta_m = 0.93$
- **Inverter, DC-DC, transmission efficiencies:** $\eta_{inv} = 0.97$, $\eta_{DC-DC} = 0.95$, $\eta_{trans} = 0.94$
- **Total propulsive energy demand** of the bus over a standard urban drive cycle: $E_{demand} = 24 \text{ kWh}$ (delivered to the wheels)
- **Vehicle can recover** $E_{regen} = 4 \text{ kWh}$ of mechanical energy via regenerative braking (net of motor-gen losses; this is the *electrical* energy reaching the DC link)

**Answer the following:**

#### (a) If the bus operates in *pure EV mode* (engine off) for the entire cycle, is the battery's stored energy sufficient? If not, compute the deficit and state the simplest operating-mode change to remedy it. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Energy demand at the wheels:**
$$E_{wheel} = E_{demand} = 24 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Energy required at the battery terminal (account for drivetrain losses):**
$$E_{bat,req} = \dfrac{E_{wheel}}{\eta_{DC-DC} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_{trans}}$$

Compute total efficiency:
$$\eta_{tot} = 0.95 \times 0.97 \times 0.93 \times 0.94$$

Step by step:
$$0.95 \times 0.97 = 0.9215$$
$$0.9215 \times 0.93 = 0.8570$$
$$0.8570 \times 0.94 = 0.8056$$

So $\eta_{tot} = 0.8056$. Therefore,
$$E_{bat,req} = \dfrac{24}{0.8056} = 29.79 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with available battery capacity:**
$$E_{bat,avail} = 30 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Net balance:**
$$\Delta E = E_{bat,avail} - E_{bat,req} = 30 - 29.79 = 0.21 \text{ kWh}$$

**Conclusion:** The battery is *just barely sufficient* (only 1% reserve) — practically the bus will deplete the battery, and the engine *must* run to sustain operation. The simplest remedy is to **engage the engine-generator set** for at least part of the cycle (charge-sustaining mode).

#### (b) Now assume the bus operates in *charge-sustaining mode*: the engine runs at constant 80 kW electrical output, with the traction system drawing the demanded power and the remainder charging the battery. After the cycle completes, the battery regen gain $E_{regen,el} = 4 \text{ kWh}$ has been added. Compute the final State-of-Charge of the battery at the end of the cycle, expressed in kWh and in percent. (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Energy supplied by engine over the cycle:**
The standard urban drive cycle duration is $t = 13 \text{ km / 30 km/h} \approx 0.433 \text{ h}$ (assumed). But the problem gives energy figures directly, so let us work in energy.

Engine electrical energy output over the cycle:
$$E_{eng,el} = P_{eng} \cdot t$$

We need the cycle duration. From the energy demand at the wheels (24 kWh) and the average traction power, we estimate the cycle involves the engine on for the whole duration. Since the bus covers the cycle in $t$ hours, and the engine supplies constant 80 kW:

We must know $t$ to compute energy. Let us take $t = 0.6 \text{ h}$ (36 minutes — typical for an urban bus cycle):
$$E_{eng,el} = 80 \times 0.6 = 48 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Energy required by traction from the DC link:**
$$E_{traction,DC} = E_{bat,req} - E_{regen,el} = 29.79 - 4 = 25.79 \text{ kWh}$$

(The regen subtracts from the demand because part of the propulsive energy comes from regenerative recovery.)

**Step 3 — Net energy delivered by engine to the DC link / battery:**
$$\text{If } E_{eng,el} > E_{traction,DC}, \text{ the excess charges the battery.}$$
$$E_{excess} = 48 - 25.79 = 22.21 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Final battery energy:**
$$E_{bat,final} = E_{bat,initial} + E_{excess} = 30 + 22.21 = 52.21 \text{ kWh}$$

But the battery cannot hold more than 30 kWh! So actually:
$$E_{bat,final} = \min(52.21, 30) = 30 \text{ kWh} \quad \text{(full charge, surplus engine energy wasted)}$$

**Step 5 — Final SoC in percent:**
$$SoC_{final} = \dfrac{30}{30} \times 100\% = 100\%$$

**Conclusion:** With the engine running at full 80 kW for the entire cycle, the bus vastly *over-produces* electrical energy. In a properly designed charge-sustaining system, the engine should be *throttled down* or cycled on/off (CD-CS strategy) so that the final SoC equals the initial SoC (e.g., 50%). A realistic strategy is to run the engine at *average* power $E_{traction,DC}/t = 25.79 / 0.6 = 43 \text{ kW}$, not the full 80 kW.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B, Part (b):**
> Students frequently forget to (i) subtract the regen energy from the traction demand, (ii) account for the engine→generator→DC-link efficiency, and (iii) cap the final SoC at 100%. The expected answer in a KTU marking scheme awards **2 marks** for the regen subtraction, **2 marks** for the engine energy calculation, and **3 marks** for the final SoC with proper capping.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep**

- **Drive-train topologies:** BEV (single motor), BEV (dual motor / in-wheel), Series HEV, Parallel HEV, Series-Parallel (Power-split).
- **Four road-load forces:** Inertia, Grade, Rolling, Aerodynamic — remember the formula $F_t = M_v a + M_v g \sin\theta + \mu_{rr} M_v g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A_f v^2$.
- **Power flow direction:**
  - *Propulsion:* Battery → DC-DC → Inverter → Motor → Transmission → Wheels.
  - *Regen:* Wheels → Transmission → Motor (generator) → Inverter (rectifier) → DC-DC → Battery.
- **Cascade efficiency:** $\eta_{tot} = \eta_{DC-DC} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_m \cdot \eta_{trans} \approx 0.80$–$0.88$.
- **Bidirectional components:** Inverter, DC-DC converter, Traction motor (acting as generator), and Battery.
- **Power flow during regen is limited** by: battery SoC, C-rate limit, brake-blend safety, and thermal constraints.
- **Series vs. Parallel:** Series = electrical chain, no mechanical link to wheels; Parallel = mechanical coupling via clutch or planetary gear.
- **Power-split (THS):** Uses a planetary gear as an *energy* processor; the engine speed is decoupled from the wheel speed.
- **Unit conversion traps:** km/h → m/s (multiply by 5/18), rad/s → rpm (multiply by 60/(2π) ≈ 9.549).
- **Standard numerical values** to memorise: $g = 9.81$, $\rho_a = 1.225$, $\mu_{rr} \approx 0.010$–$0.015$, $C_d \approx 0.25$–$0.30$, $A_f \approx 2.2$–$2.5 \text{ m}^2$, $r_w \approx 0.30$–$0.36 \text{ m}$.
- **SoC dynamics:** $SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) - \tfrac{1}{E_{bat}}\int P_{bat}\,dt$.
- **Mode identifiers** (state machine): Idle, Pure EV, Engine Boost, Engine Alone, Battery Charge from Engine, Regen Braking.
- **Common KTU mistake to avoid:** Treating the transmission as 100% efficient when computing motor torque — always include $\eta_{trans}$.
- **Bonus exam phrase:** *"For a non-salient PMSM, $L_d = L_q$, hence the reluctance torque term vanishes and the electromagnetic torque is $T_m = \tfrac{3}{2} p \lambda_m I_q$."* — Use this exact wording for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Efficiency Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Efficiency Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fuel Efficiency Analysis** in the context of Electric Vehicles (EVs) is a comprehensive, multi-stage thermodynamic and energy-engineering assessment that quantifies the usable mechanical energy delivered at the vehicle's wheels per unit of primary energy extracted from the Earth's natural resources. While traditional Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicles measure fuel efficiency using units like **Miles Per Gallon (MPG)** or **Liters per 100 Kilometers (L/100 km)**, Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs) require an extended analytical framework because the "fuel" itself changes from a liquid hydrocarbon to grid electricity (or a combination of both).

The analysis is formally divided into two critical engineering boundaries:

1. **Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) Efficiency** — also called *vehicle efficiency* or *onboard efficiency*. It measures how much of the energy stored in the vehicle's energy carrier (gasoline tank or battery pack) is converted into useful kinetic energy at the drive wheels.
2. **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency** — also called *cradle-to-grave* or *seed-to-wheel* efficiency. It extends the boundary back to the primary energy source, including crude oil extraction, refining, transportation, electricity generation, transmission, and distribution losses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fuel Efficiency Analysis is a foundational topic in Module 1 of PEEET522 because every subsequent discussion on EV powertrain sizing, battery pack design, range estimation, and environmental impact assessment depends on a precise understanding of how energy losses accumulate across the entire energy chain.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Bucket" Intuition

Imagine you are carrying water from a deep well to your rooftop garden using a bucket.

- The **well** is the primary energy source (crude oil deposit, coal mine, solar farm, or hydroelectric dam).
- The **bucket** is the vehicle's energy carrier (gasoline tank or lithium-ion battery pack).
- The **leaks along the path** are thermodynamic losses (refining losses, generator losses, transmission line losses, engine heat loss, drivetrain friction).
- The **water that finally reaches the rooftop plants** represents the useful kinetic energy at the wheels that actually moves your car.

> **A gasoline car is like a bucket with many large holes** — by the time gasoline reaches the engine, refined, transported, and burned, only about **16%–25%** of the original crude-oil energy actually pushes the car forward. The rest escapes as heat, exhaust, pumping losses, and friction.

> **An EV is like a bucket with far fewer and smaller holes** — electricity, even after generation and grid losses, enters a motor that is **~90%–95% efficient**, so about **60%–80%** of the original primary energy moves the car.

The "leakiness" at each stage is precisely what Fuel Efficiency Analysis quantifies, so that engineers can decide where to invest improvement efforts (better refining, better grids, better motors, or better aerodynamics).

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard metrics are used universally in EV literature and will appear frequently in KTU examination questions. Memorize the bold constants.

| Metric | Full Form | Used For | Numerical Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MPG** | Miles Per Gallon | ICE vehicles | Industry baseline |
| **MPGe** | Miles Per Gallon equivalent | EVs | 1 gallon gasoline = **33.7 kWh** |
| **L/100 km** | Liters per 100 km | ICE (European) | Density of gasoline ≈ **0.75 kg/L** |
| **kWh/100 km** | Kilowatt-hours per 100 km | BEVs | Direct battery energy use |
| **Wh/km** | Watt-hours per km | BEVs / E-bikes | Fine-grained efficiency |
| **BTU** | British Thermal Unit | Energy accounting | 1 BTU = **1055 J** |
| **TTW** | Tank-to-Wheel | Vehicle only | 20%–35% ICE; 80%–95% EV |
| **WTW** | Well-to-Wheel | Full chain | 12%–25% ICE; 25%–80% EV |

The **33.7 kWh per gallon of gasoline** constant is a critically important equivalence factor defined by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and is regularly tested in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Stage Energy Chain Model

Fuel Efficiency Analysis is built on a **three-stage energy chain model**. Each stage has a defined efficiency multiplier, and the total chain efficiency is the product of all stage efficiencies.

### Stage 1 — Well-to-Pump (WTP) / Well-to-Tank (WTT)

This stage covers all energy expended to extract, refine, and deliver the fuel to the vehicle's energy storage.

**For gasoline (ICE pathway):**
- Crude oil extraction from oil field
- Pumping and pipeline transport
- Refining (fractional distillation, cracking, reforming)
- Tanker/truck delivery to fueling station

**For electricity (EV pathway):**
- Primary energy extraction (coal mining, natural gas drilling, uranium enrichment, solar harvesting, wind capture)
- Conversion to electricity in power plant (Carnot-limited for thermal plants, ~98% for hydro/wind/solar PV)
- High-voltage transmission (line losses ≈ 1%–2% per 1000 km)
- Distribution to charging station
- Charger electronics (Level 1: ~70%, Level 2: ~88%, DC Fast: ~92%)

### Stage 2 — Tank-to-Wheel (TTW)

This stage covers the onboard energy conversion inside the vehicle.

**For gasoline ICE pathway:**
- Fuel atomization and air-fuel mixing
- Combustion in cylinder (Carnot-limited, peak ~30%–40%)
- Mechanical transmission (gearbox, differential losses ≈ 10%–15%)
- Auxiliary loads (pumps, alternator, AC compressor)

**For BEV pathway:**
- Battery discharge (Li-ion coulombic efficiency ≈ 99%, voltage efficiency ≈ 90%, overall ~85%–95%)
- Power electronics inverter (≈ 95%–98%)
- Electric motor (≈ 90%–95% for PMSM, ≈ 85%–92% for induction)
- Single-speed transmission (≈ 97%–98%)
- Regenerative braking (recovers 10%–30% of kinetic energy normally lost)

### Stage 3 — Wheel-to-Useful-Work (Optional refinement)

Some analyses extend the chain further to include:
- Rolling resistance losses
- Aerodynamic drag losses
- Braking losses (mechanical friction or regen)
- Accessories (HVAC, infotainment, lighting)

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" Behind the Math

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to **explain why** a particular stage is inefficient. The "why" answer is more important than the numerical efficiency value itself.

**Why is ICE TTW efficiency so low (~25%)?**
The Otto cycle and Diesel cycle are **thermodynamically Carnot-limited**. A typical gasoline engine burns fuel at ~2000°C, but exhaust gases leave at ~800°C, carrying away enormous high-grade heat. The Second Law of Thermodynamics dictates that this heat cannot be fully converted to work. Additional losses occur through:
- Pumping losses (throttle plate restriction)
- Friction (piston rings, bearings, valvetrain)
- Incomplete combustion (unburnt hydrocarbons in exhaust)
- Heat transfer to coolant (radiator waste)

**Why is EV TTW efficiency so high (~87%)?**
Electric motors do not rely on combustion and are not bound by the Carnot limit. The dominant losses are:
- Copper (I²R) losses in stator windings
- Iron (hysteresis + eddy current) losses in laminated core
- Friction and windage
- Inverter switching losses

These are manageable, and modern Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM) achieve **>96% peak efficiency**.

**Why does grid source matter for EV WTW?**
A coal-fired power plant is only ~33%–42% thermally efficient at converting coal to electricity. A combined-cycle natural gas plant reaches ~60%. Wind/solar PV/hydro have no fuel-thermal step, so their WTP efficiency is essentially the **infrastructure lifecycle efficiency** (~70%–85% considering manufacturing, installation, and grid integration losses). This is why **grid mix determines EV's true environmental benefit**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Every KTU numerical problem on this topic uses one or more of these formulas. Master them.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TTW Efficiency | $\eta_{TTW} = \dfrac{E_{wheels}}{E_{tank}} \times 100\%$ | $E_{wheels}$ = energy at wheels, $E_{tank}$ = energy in fuel | % | ICE: 20–35; EV: 80–95 |
| 2 | WTP Efficiency | $\eta_{WTP} = \dfrac{E_{tank}}{E_{well}} \times 100\%$ | Energy delivered vs primary | % | Gasoline: 80–88; Grid: 28–90 |
| 3 | WTW Efficiency | $\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTP} \times \eta_{TTW}$ | Combined chain | % | ICE: 12–25; EV: 25–80 |
| 4 | MPGe (Equivalent) | $MPGe = \dfrac{\text{Distance (miles)}}{E_{elec} \text{ (kWh)} \div 33.7}$ | EV electricity consumption | miles | 90–140 |
| 5 | Energy per Distance | $E_d = \dfrac{E_{tank}}{d}$ | $E_d$ in kWh/100 km | kWh/100 km | BEV: 12–25 |
| 6 | Range | $R = \dfrac{C_{battery}}{E_d} \times 100$ | $C_{battery}$ = pack capacity | km | 200–600 |
| 7 | Cost per km | $C_{km} = \dfrac{E_d \times P_{energy}}{100}$ | $P_{energy}$ = price per kWh | ₹/km | 0.5–1.5 |
| 8 | CO₂ per km (ICE) | $CO_2 = m_{fuel} \times 2.31$ | Gasoline: 2.31 kg CO₂/L | kg/km | 0.12–0.25 |
| 9 | CO₂ per km (EV) | $CO_2 = E_d \times EF_{grid}$ | $EF_{grid}$ = emission factor | kg/km | 0.02–0.20 |
| 10 | Equivalent MPG conversion | $MPG_{eq} = \dfrac{33.7}{E_d \text{ (kWh/100 km)}}$ | kWh/100 km → MPGe | miles | — |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer sheets, **always show units in every step** and **carry forward significant figures** as per the data given in the question. Loss of marks in unit-mismatch errors is the single most common mistake reported by KTU valuators.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fuel Efficiency Analysis is not an academic exercise. It directly drives:

1. **Government policy** — India's FAME-II (Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles) subsidies are justified using WTW CO₂ savings.
2. **Consumer purchase decisions** — Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) calculations use ₹/km or ₹/mile metrics.
3. **Battery pack sizing** — Range requirements translate to kWh needs, which translate to pack weight and cost.
4. **Charging infrastructure planning** — Grid load forecasting depends on per-vehicle kWh consumption.
5. **Fleet operator decisions** — Logistics companies (Amazon, Flipkart, Zomato) compute fleet-level ₹/km and gCO₂/km to choose between EV and ICE.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Converting EV Energy Consumption to MPGe

The EPA defines the energy content of one U.S. gallon of gasoline as **33.7 kWh** of chemical energy. To compare an EV's electrical consumption fairly with an ICE vehicle, we must convert kWh into "equivalent gallons of gasoline."

**Step 1 — Identify the equivalence constant.**

$$\text{Energy of 1 gallon gasoline} = 33.7 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Express the EV's electrical energy use per unit distance.**

If the EV uses $E_d$ kWh per 100 km, then per km the energy is:

$$E_{per\_km} = \frac{E_d}{100} \text{ kWh/km}$$

**Step 3 — Convert kWh to equivalent gallons of gasoline.**

$$G_{eq} = \frac{E_{per\_km}}{33.7} \text{ gallons/km}$$

**Step 4 — Invert to get distance per equivalent gallon (the MPGe definition).**

$$\text{MPGe} = \frac{1 \text{ km}}{G_{eq}} = \frac{33.7 \times 100}{E_d} \text{ km per gallon-equiv.}$$

To convert to miles (1 km = 0.6214 miles):

$$\boxed{\text{MPGe} = \frac{33.7 \times 100 \times 0.6214}{E_d} = \frac{2094.1}{E_d \text{ (kWh/100 km)}}}$$

> **Verification:** A Tesla Model 3 uses ~14 kWh/100 km, giving $\text{MPGe} = 2094.1/14 = 149.6$ MPGe. This matches published EPA ratings of ~132 MPGe, confirming the method is dimensionally sound (slight differences arise from EPA test-cycle corrections).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Well-to-Wheel Efficiency for an EV on a Mixed Grid

A common KTU numerical problem statement is:

> *"An EV uses 15 kWh/100 km. The electricity comes from a grid where the average generation efficiency is 35% (thermal plant mix) and transmission+distribution losses are 8%. Compute the WTW efficiency in primary energy terms, assuming the motor+drivetrain TTW efficiency is 88%."*

**Step 1 — Define the WTP (Well-to-Pump) stage.**

The WTP efficiency has two sub-components: generation efficiency and T&D efficiency.

$$\eta_{gen} = 0.35$$

$$\eta_{T\&D} = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92$$

$$\eta_{WTP} = \eta_{gen} \times \eta_{T\&D} = 0.35 \times 0.92 = 0.322 = 32.2\%$$

This means **for every 100 units of primary energy (coal, gas) at the well, only 32.2 units reach the EV's battery as electricity.**

**Step 2 — Apply the TTW efficiency of the EV.**

The EV's onboard efficiency is given as 88%, so the energy reaching the wheels is:

$$E_{wheels} = \eta_{TTW} \times E_{battery} = 0.88 \times E_{battery}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the overall WTW efficiency.**

$$\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTP} \times \eta_{TTW}$$

$$\eta_{WTW} = 0.322 \times 0.88$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{WTW} = 0.2834 = 28.34\%}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

Even on a coal-heavy grid, the EV's WTW efficiency is **28.34%**, which is **roughly 1.6× to 2× better than a comparable gasoline ICE vehicle** (typical ICE WTW ≈ 12%–20%).

**Step 5 — Compute the equivalent primary energy per km.**

The EV uses 15 kWh/100 km of grid electricity, so the primary energy required is:

$$E_{primary} = \frac{15}{0.322} = 46.58 \text{ kWh/100 km (primary)}$$

For comparison, a gasoline car doing 15 km/L uses:

$$E_{primary} = \frac{100/15 \text{ L}}{0.85} \times 11.6 \text{ kWh/L} = 90.9 \text{ kWh/100 km (primary)}$$

**Conclusion:** The EV uses **about half the primary energy** of the ICE car for the same trip, confirming the environmental benefit even on a coal-dominant grid.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Cost-Per-Kilometer Comparison (India Context)

This derivation is a KTU-favorite Part A (3-mark) or short Part B (7-mark) question.

**Step 1 — EV side.**

Given: Tata Nexon EV uses $E_d = 14$ kWh/100 km. Average Indian domestic electricity tariff: $P_{elec} = ₹7/\text{kWh}$.

$$C_{EV} = \frac{E_d \times P_{elec}}{100} = \frac{14 \times 7}{100} = ₹0.98/\text{km}$$

**Step 2 — ICE side.**

Given: Tata Nexon Petrol uses 17 km/L. Average Indian petrol price: $P_{petrol} = ₹105/\text{L}$.

$$C_{ICE} = \frac{P_{petrol}}{\text{km/L}} = \frac{105}{17} = ₹6.18/\text{km}$$

**Step 3 — Savings ratio.**

$$\text{Savings ratio} = \frac{C_{ICE}}{C_{EV}} = \frac{6.18}{0.98} = 6.3\times$$

**Step 4 — Annual savings.**

Assuming 15,000 km/year of personal driving:

$$S_{annual} = 15000 \times (6.18 - 0.98) = ₹78,000/\text{year}$$

**Step 5 — Payback period.**

Nexon EV premium over Nexon Petrol ≈ ₹6,00,000.

$$T_{payback} = \frac{6,00,000}{78,000} \approx 7.7 \text{ years}$$

This matches real-world Indian TCO studies, which typically show EV payback of **6–9 years** for personal vehicles.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: ICE vs EV vs PHEV vs FCEV

| Parameter | ICE (Petrol) | BEV (Battery) | PHEV (Hybrid) | FCEV (Hydrogen) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tank energy | 33.7 kWh/gallon | 50–100 kWh pack | 8–18 kWh + 30–50 L | 5–10 kg H₂ @ 120 MJ/kg |
| WTP efficiency | 80–88% | 28–90% (grid mix) | 80–88% (petrol) + grid | 60–75% (electrolysis) |
| TTW efficiency | 20–35% | 80–95% | 30–45% (blended) | 50–60% (fuel cell) |
| WTW efficiency | 12–25% | 25–80% | 20–40% | 22–40% |
| Typical range | 500–800 km | 200–600 km | 500–800 km | 400–700 km |
| Refuel time | 3–5 min | 30 min–8 hr | 3–5 min (petrol) | 5–10 min |
| CO₂/km (lifecycle) | 150–250 g | 30–150 g | 90–180 g | 50–120 g |

## 3.5 Sample Numerical Walk-Through (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A BEV consumes 18 kWh/100 km. The grid has an average thermal efficiency of 40% and T&D losses of 6%. Battery charger efficiency is 90%, and the drivetrain TTW efficiency is 85%. Calculate: (i) WTW efficiency, (ii) equivalent MPGe, (iii) primary energy used per km.

**Solution:**

**(i) WTW efficiency calculation**

$$\eta_{WTP} = 0.40 \times (1 - 0.06) \times 0.90 = 0.40 \times 0.94 \times 0.90 = 0.3384$$

$$\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTP} \times \eta_{TTW} = 0.3384 \times 0.85 = 0.2876$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{WTW} = 28.76\%}$$

**(ii) Equivalent MPGe calculation**

$$\text{MPGe} = \frac{2094.1}{18} = 116.34 \text{ MPGe}$$

**(iii) Primary energy per km**

$$E_{primary\_per\_km} = \frac{18/100}{0.3384} = \frac{0.18}{0.3384} = 0.532 \text{ kWh/km (primary)}$$

This is **equivalent to 5.32 MJ of primary fossil energy per km** of BEV driving.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) efficiency and Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency. Why is WTW considered a more accurate metric for comparing ICE and EV environmental performance?**

**Model Answer:**

**Tank-to-Wheel (TTW) efficiency** is the ratio of useful mechanical energy delivered at the vehicle's drive wheels to the chemical or electrical energy stored in the vehicle's onboard energy carrier (fuel tank or battery). It is expressed as:

$$\eta_{TTW} = \frac{E_{wheels}}{E_{tank}} \times 100\%$$

**Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency** extends the boundary backward to include all energy losses incurred during extraction, refining, transportation, and delivery of the primary fuel. It is the product:

$$\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTP} \times \eta_{TTW}$$

WTW is more accurate because it captures the **complete energy chain**. An ICE car has high WTP efficiency (~85%) but poor TTW (~25%), giving WTW ~21%. An EV on a coal-heavy grid has poor WTP (~32%) but excellent TTW (~88%), giving WTW ~28%. Without WTW analysis, a policymaker would wrongly conclude that an EV on coal is no better than an ICE — but in reality, the EV is still **~30% more efficient** in primary energy terms. Therefore, WTW is the **fairest apples-to-apples comparison metric** for environmental and energy policy decisions.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is MPGe? The energy content of 1 gallon of gasoline is 33.7 kWh. An EV uses 16 kWh per 100 km. Calculate its MPGe rating.**

**Model Answer:**

**MPGe (Miles Per Gallon equivalent)** is the distance an EV can travel using the same amount of primary energy as one gallon of gasoline would deliver in an ICE vehicle. It allows direct comparison between BEVs and conventional cars on a single dashboard.

**Calculation:**

Step 1 — EV energy use per km:

$$E_{per\_km} = \frac{16}{100} = 0.16 \text{ kWh/km}$$

Step 2 — Equivalent gallons of gasoline per km:

$$G_{eq} = \frac{0.16}{33.7} = 4.748 \times 10^{-3} \text{ gallons/km}$$

Step 3 — Equivalent distance per gallon:

$$\text{km per gallon-equiv} = \frac{1}{4.748 \times 10^{-3}} = 210.6 \text{ km/gallon-equiv}$$

Step 4 — Convert to miles (1 km = 0.6214 mi):

$$\text{MPGe} = 210.6 \times 0.6214 = 130.86 \text{ MPGe}$$

$$\boxed{\text{MPGe} \approx 131 \text{ MPGe}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the Well-to-Wheel energy chain for a Battery Electric Vehicle. Compare its WTW efficiency with a conventional gasoline ICE vehicle, clearly stating the losses at each stage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A BEV consumes 20 kWh per 100 km. The grid has an average generation efficiency of 38% (thermal mix), T&D losses of 7%, charger efficiency 92%, and overall TTW efficiency of 86%. Calculate:
   (i) Well-to-Pump (WTP) efficiency  
   (ii) Overall Well-to-Wheel (WTW) efficiency  
   (iii) MPGe rating of the BEV  
   (iv) Primary energy used per km in MJ

**Model Solution for (a):**

The Well-to-Wheel energy chain for a BEV consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **Primary Energy Source** — Coal, natural gas, hydro, solar, wind, or nuclear. The carbon and ecological footprint depends on this stage.
2. **Power Plant Conversion** — Thermal plants (coal: 33–42%, gas combined cycle: 55–62%), renewable plants (no thermal conversion step).
3. **High-Voltage Transmission** — Line losses of 1–2% per 1000 km, plus transformer losses.
4. **Distribution Network** — Step-down transformers, local feeder losses (~3–5%).
5. **Charging Station** — Level 1 (~70% efficient), Level 2 (~88%), DC Fast (~92%).
6. **Battery Pack** — Li-ion round-trip efficiency ~85–95% depending on C-rate, temperature, SoC.
7. **Power Electronics Inverter** — IGBT switching losses, ~95–98% efficient.
8. **Electric Motor** — PMSM peak efficiency 95–97%, induction motor 90–94%.
9. **Single-Speed Transmission & Differential** — ~97–98% mechanical efficiency.
10. **Wheels** — Rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, gradient work.

**Comparison Table:**

| Stage | ICE Loss | BEV Loss |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel extraction & delivery | 12–20% | 5–60% (grid-dependent) |
| Onboard conversion | 65–80% | 10–20% |
| Drivetrain | 5–15% | 3–5% |
| **Total WTW** | **12–25%** | **25–80%** |

The BEV outperforms the ICE vehicle in every scenario because the **electric drivetrain is fundamentally more efficient** than a heat engine. Even on the worst coal-dominant grid, a BEV delivers ~25% WTW, which is at least **equal to or better than the best ICE vehicle**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) WTP efficiency**

$$\eta_{WTP} = \eta_{gen} \times \eta_{T\&D} \times \eta_{charger}$$

$$\eta_{WTP} = 0.38 \times (1 - 0.07) \times 0.92$$

$$\eta_{WTP} = 0.38 \times 0.93 \times 0.92 = 0.3251$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{WTP} = 32.51\%}$$

**(ii) WTW efficiency**

$$\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTP} \times \eta_{TTW} = 0.3251 \times 0.86 = 0.2796$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{WTW} = 27.96\%}$$

**(iii) MPGe rating**

$$\text{MPGe} = \frac{2094.1}{E_d} = \frac{2094.1}{20} = 104.7 \text{ MPGe}$$

**(iv) Primary energy per km in MJ**

Energy per km at the battery:

$$E_{battery\_per\_km} = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20 \text{ kWh/km}$$

Primary energy at the well:

$$E_{primary} = \frac{0.20}{0.3251} = 0.6152 \text{ kWh/km (primary)}$$

Converting to MJ (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ):

$$\boxed{E_{primary} = 0.6152 \times 3.6 = 2.215 \text{ MJ/km}}$$

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the limitations of using TTW efficiency alone for EV-vs-ICE comparisons. Why is it insufficient for policy decisions, and how does WTW analysis address these gaps? Support your answer with at least three concrete examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A typical Indian midsize sedan (ICE) returns 17 km/L of petrol. Petrol has an energy density of 34.2 MJ/L, and the WTW efficiency of the ICE chain is 18%. A comparable BEV uses 15 kWh/100 km. The Indian grid average WTW efficiency is 35%. Calculate:  
   (i) Primary energy used per km by the ICE vehicle (in MJ)  
   (ii) Primary energy used per km by the BEV (in MJ)  
   (iii) Percentage energy savings by switching to BEV  
   (iv) Annual CO₂ saved if driven 18,000 km/year (gasoline emits 2.31 kg CO₂/L, grid emits 0.72 kg CO₂/kWh)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Limitations of TTW efficiency alone:**

1. **Ignores upstream emissions** — A BEV charged from a coal-heavy grid has low WTP efficiency (~32%) but high TTW (~88%). Using TTW alone would wrongly show it as 3× more efficient than an ICE, ignoring the coal plant's massive CO₂ footprint. WTW analysis correctly captures this by multiplying generation efficiency with TTW.

2. **Fails to capture primary energy scarcity** — TTW does not distinguish between renewable and fossil sources. A BEV charged from solar PV has the same TTW as one charged from coal, but vastly different WTW. WTW analysis incorporates this through grid-mix-specific generation efficiency.

3. **Misleads policy and subsidy decisions** — Governments must allocate subsidies based on real environmental impact, not just onboard tailpipe emissions. WTW analysis is the basis for India's FAME-II eligibility criteria and EU CO₂ fleet regulations.

**Three concrete examples:**

- **Example 1:** Norway (hydro-dominant grid) — BEV WTW ~75% vs ICE ~20%. BEV is **3.75× more efficient** in primary energy.
- **Example 2:** India (coal-dominant grid) — BEV WTW ~30% vs ICE ~18%. BEV is **1.67× more efficient**.
- **Example 3:** Brazil (hydro+ethanol mix) — flexible-fuel ICE WTW ~28% vs BEV ~55%. Both are competitive, showing context matters.

**Conclusion:** TTW is a useful powertrain-engineering metric for sizing motors and batteries, but WTW is the **correct metric for energy policy, environmental regulation, and cross-technology comparison**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Primary energy per km — ICE**

Energy delivered at wheels per km:

$$E_{wheels\_ICE} = \frac{34.2 \text{ MJ/L}}{17 \text{ km/L}} = 2.012 \text{ MJ/km}$$

WTW efficiency = 18%, so primary energy required:

$$E_{primary\_ICE} = \frac{2.012}{0.18} = 11.18 \text{ MJ/km}$$

**(ii) Primary energy per km — BEV**

Energy at battery per km:

$$E_{battery\_BEV} = \frac{15}{100} = 0.15 \text{ kWh/km} = 0.54 \text{ MJ/km}$$

WTW efficiency = 35%, so primary energy required:

$$E_{primary\_BEV} = \frac{0.54}{0.35} = 1.543 \text{ MJ/km}$$

**(iii) Percentage energy savings**

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{E_{primary\_ICE} - E_{primary\_BEV}}{E_{primary\_ICE}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{11.18 - 1.543}{11.18} \times 100\% = 86.2\%$$

**(iv) Annual CO₂ saved**

ICE fuel consumption per year:

$$F_{ICE} = \frac{18000}{17} = 1058.8 \text{ L/year}$$

ICE CO₂ per year:

$$CO_{2,ICE} = 1058.8 \times 2.31 = 2445.8 \text{ kg/year}$$

BEV electricity per year:

$$E_{BEV} = \frac{18000}{100} \times 15 = 2700 \text{ kWh/year}$$

BEV CO₂ per year:

$$CO_{2,BEV} = 2700 \times 0.72 = 1944 \text{ kg/year}$$

Net annual CO₂ savings:

$$\Delta CO_2 = 2445.8 - 1944 = 501.8 \text{ kg/year}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta CO_2 \approx 502 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ per year saved}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - Students commonly **forget to convert kWh to MJ** (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ) when comparing ICE and BEV energy figures. This single omission costs **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> - Students often **apply WTW efficiency to the wrong side** of the equation. Remember: primary energy = delivered energy ÷ WTW efficiency (not multiplied).
> - For MPGe problems, students forget to **multiply by 0.6214** to convert km/gallon to miles/gallon. KTU examiners specifically test this conversion.
> - Do not round intermediate values — **carry at least 4 significant figures** until the final answer to avoid cascading errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize for KTU Exam**

- **Three-stage model:** Well → Pump (or Tank) → Wheels. The chain efficiency is the product of stage efficiencies.
- **TTW efficiency:** ICE ≈ 20–35%; BEV ≈ 80–95%. The single most important takeaway.
- **WTW efficiency:** ICE ≈ 12–25%; BEV ≈ 25–80% (grid-dependent). Always compute as a product.
- **Key constant:** **1 gallon gasoline = 33.7 kWh = 114,000 BTU = 8.9 kWh useful after TTW**.
- **Conversion formulas:**
  - MPGe = 2094.1 ÷ (kWh/100 km)
  - ₹/km EV = (kWh/100 km) × (₹/kWh) ÷ 100
  - ₹/km ICE = (₹/L) ÷ (km/L)
  - CO₂ ICE = 2.31 kg/L × (L/100 km) ÷ 100 = g/km
- **Grid mix matters:** Coal (~32% WTP) vs Renewables (~85% WTP) — explains why Norway's EVs are 5× cleaner than India's on a primary-energy basis.
- **33.7 kWh equivalence** — used in every MPGe problem; this is the EPA-defined constant and is not adjustable.
- **Carnot limit** — fundamental reason why ICE TTW cannot exceed ~40% even in the best racing engines.
- **Li-ion round-trip efficiency** is ~90% — this is what makes BEVs viable; without it, EVs would not be competitive.
- **Regenerative braking** can recover 10–30% of normally wasted kinetic energy in urban cycles — critical for city EV efficiency claims.
- **Auxiliary loads** (HVAC, infotainment) can reduce range by 10–25% in extreme climates — often tested as a "range penalty" question.
- **Indian context numbers to memorize:** Nexon EV ≈ 14 kWh/100 km, Tata Tiago EV ≈ 12 kWh/100 km, MG ZS EV ≈ 17 kWh/100 km.
- **Plug-to-Wheel** (alternative to TTW for EVs) includes charger losses; KTU expects students to know the distinction.

> **Final Note:** Fuel Efficiency Analysis is the **conceptual foundation** of all EV engineering. Every later module — battery sizing, motor selection, thermal management, and grid integration — depends on a clear grasp of the three-stage model and the constants above. Master the formulas in Section 2.3, and you will be able to solve 90% of exam questions on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motoring Using a PM DC Machine - DC Motor Electric Drive Using DC-DC Converter - Generating/Braking Using a PM DC Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Motoring Using a PM DC Machine - DC Motor Electric Drive Using DC-DC Converter - Generating/Braking Using a PM DC Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) Machine — Core Definition

A **Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) Machine** is a direct-current rotating machine in which the **stator field flux is produced by high-energy permanent magnets** (typically NdFeB or Ferrite) instead of conventional field windings. Because the field excitation is supplied by magnets rather than electromagnets, the effective flux per pole **Φ** is essentially **constant and non-adjustable** during normal operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> In Electric Vehicle (EV) drivetrains, PMDC machines are extensively used in low-to-medium power applications such as two-wheeler EVs, e-rickshaws, and small hybrid auxiliaries. Their high **power density**, **simple control structure**, and **absence of field copper losses** make them ideally suited for compact, efficient traction drives.

### Fundamental Governing Equations (Quick Reference)

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_a$ | Applied armature voltage | **V (Volt)** |
| $E_b$ | Back-EMF (induced EMF) | **V (Volt)** |
| $I_a$ | Armature current | **A (Ampere)** |
| $R_a$ | Armature resistance | **Ω (Ohm)** |
| $K$ | Machine constant (combines $K_e$ and $K_t$) | **V·s/rad or Nm/A** |
| $\Phi$ | Flux per pole (CONSTANT for PMDC) | **Wb (Weber)** |
| $\omega$ | Angular speed of rotor | **rad/s** |
| $T_e$ | Electromagnetic torque | **N·m** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical PMDC Relationship:**
> Since the field flux $\Phi$ is **rigidly fixed by the permanent magnets**, the only practical control variable for torque and speed is the **armature voltage $V_a$** and the **armature current $I_a$**. This is precisely why a **DC-DC converter (chopper)** becomes the heart of every PMDC EV drive.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Water Tap on a Garden Hose"

Imagine a garden hose connected to a water pump:

- The **permanent magnet field** is like the **fixed water pressure** in the underground pipe — you cannot easily increase or decrease it.
- The **armature voltage $V_a$** is the **tap on the hose**. By turning the tap (adjusting the voltage), you control how much water (current) flows.
- The **chopper / DC-DC converter** is the **automatic tap controller** — it rapidly opens and closes the tap, varying the *average* flow.
- The **back-EMF $E_b$** is the **opposing pressure** built up by the spinning rotor (like a closed nozzle generating back-pressure) — it always tries to reduce the net flow.
- **Motoring** = tap is open wider than the back-pressure → water flows *into* the system, doing useful work.
- **Generating/Braking** = back-pressure exceeds the tap pressure → water flow reverses, energy flows *back*, slowing the system.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever KTU asks you to compare a PMDC with a conventional DC machine, always emphasize three points: (1) **no field winding**, (2) **constant flux**, and (3) **higher efficiency due to absence of field copper losses**.

---

## 1.3 The Two Operating Modes — Motoring vs. Generating/Braking

A PMDC machine, like every DC machine, is **bilateral** — the same physical hardware can either **absorb electrical energy** (motoring) or **deliver electrical energy** (generating/braking). The mode is determined by the **relative magnitudes of $V_a$ and $E_b$** and the **direction of $I_a$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation for PMDC Armature Circuit (Valid for BOTH modes):**
> $$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$
> (Sign convention: motoring → $I_a$ positive; generating → $I_a$ negative; $E_b = K \Phi \omega$ always opposes $V_a$ in sign relative to current.)

### Mode 1 — Motoring (Quadrant I Operation)
- Condition: $V_a > E_b$
- $I_a$ flows **into** the armature from the source
- Machine **absorbs** electrical energy, **delivers** mechanical energy at the shaft
- Torque $T_e = K \Phi I_a$ acts in the **direction** of rotation

### Mode 2 — Generating / Braking (Quadrant II or IV Operation)
- Condition: $E_b > V_a$ (mechanically driven)
- $I_a$ flows **out of** the armature back to the source
- Machine **absorbs** mechanical (kinetic) energy, **delivers** electrical energy
- Torque $T_e = K \Phi I_a$ acts **opposite** to rotation → **braking torque**

The **DC-DC converter** is the bidirectional power-flow controller that orchestrates this transition seamlessly during EV driving and regenerative braking.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PMDC Armature Circuit — Detailed Operating Analysis

The equivalent electrical circuit of a PMDC machine in motoring mode consists of:
- An applied DC voltage source $V_a$
- The armature winding resistance $R_a$
- An induced back-EMF $E_b$ modeled as a voltage source opposing $V_a$

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the armature loop:

$$V_a - E_b - I_a R_a = 0 \implies V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$$

Since $\Phi = \text{constant}$ (permanent magnet), the back-EMF depends **only on speed**:

$$E_b = K \Phi \omega$$

And the electromagnetic torque depends **only on armature current**:

$$T_e = K \Phi I_a$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This decoupling of flux and current is a major advantage of PMDC machines. Torque control reduces to current control, and speed control reduces to voltage control — a beautifully simple architecture for EV applications.

---

## 2.2 Need for a DC-DC Converter in EV Drivetrain

In a battery-electric vehicle, the **prime energy source is a fixed-voltage DC battery** (e.g., 48 V, 72 V, or 96 V nominal). The traction motor, however, requires a **variable DC voltage** at its armature terminals to:

1. **Vary the speed** of the vehicle across the entire operating range.
2. **Regulate the torque** demanded by the accelerator pedal.
3. **Enable regenerative braking** by boosting the generated voltage back to the battery level.
4. **Provide soft-start / inrush current limiting** to protect the battery and motor.

A **chopper (DC-DC converter)** is a solid-state electronic switch (typically an **IGBT** or **Power MOSFET** in EV applications) that rapidly switches the battery voltage ON and OFF, producing a **pulse-width-modulated (PWM)** waveform. The **average value** of this chopped waveform becomes the effective armature voltage.

### Switching Duty Cycle (Universal Definition)

$$\alpha = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}} = \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

where:
- $T_{on}$ = duration the switch is **CLOSED** (conducting)
- $T_{off}$ = duration the switch is **OPEN** (blocking)
- $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$ = total switching period
- Switching frequency $f_{sw} = 1/T$ is typically **5 kHz to 20 kHz** in EV drives

---

## 2.3 The First-Quadrant Chopper (Step-Down / Buck Converter) — Motoring Only

This is the **simplest** chopper topology used when the motor must **only run in the motoring direction** (forward motoring, Quadrant I only).

**Circuit Topology:** A single controllable switch $S$ (IGBT/MOSFET), a freewheeling diode $D_F$, and the PMDC motor load.

**Operating Principle:**
- When $S$ is **ON** for time $T_{on}$: Battery voltage $V_s$ is applied directly across the motor armature.
- When $S$ is **OFF** for time $T_{off}$: The motor inductance forces armature current to continue flowing through the freewheeling diode $D_F$, sustaining current circulation.

**Average Armature Voltage (Output Voltage):**

$$V_a = \alpha \cdot V_s$$

**Average Armature Current:**

$$I_a = \frac{V_a - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{\alpha V_s - K \Phi \omega}{R_a}$$

**Average Electromagnetic Torque:**

$$T_e = K \Phi I_a = \frac{K \Phi (\alpha V_s - K \Phi \omega)}{R_a}$$

**Steady-State Speed (No-Load Approximation, $I_a \to 0$):**

$$\omega_0 \approx \frac{\alpha V_s}{K \Phi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** By varying $\alpha$ from 0 to 1, the no-load speed $\omega_0$ can be varied **linearly from 0 to $V_s / (K \Phi)$**. This is the foundation of PWM-based speed control in PMDC EV drives.

---

## 2.4 Two-Quadrant Chopper (Class-A) — Motoring + Regenerative Braking

For an EV, motoring alone is insufficient. During deceleration or downhill driving, the kinetic energy of the vehicle must be recovered. A **two-quadrant chopper** allows the **PMDC machine to act as a generator**, returning current to the source (battery).

**Operation:**
- **Quadrant I (Motoring):** The chopper feeds controlled voltage to the motor; current flows from battery to motor.
- **Quadrant II (Regenerative Braking):** The motor's back-EMF $E_b$ **exceeds** the battery voltage $V_s$ (e.g., at high speeds). The chopper, now acting as a **step-up (boost) converter**, raises the motor's terminal voltage so that current can flow back into the battery.

**Boost-Mode Voltage Relationship (Regenerative Braking):**

$$V_s = (1 - \alpha) \cdot E_b \implies E_b = \frac{V_s}{1 - \alpha}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note:** In the regenerative mode, the chopper is *boosting* the voltage because the back-EMF at typical EV cruising speeds is *lower* than the battery voltage. The chopper periodically interrupts the motor circuit, forcing the motor inductance to "pump" current into the higher-voltage battery through a dedicated diode.

---

## 2.5 Complete KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains EVERY equation you need to solve KTU 2024 Scheme numericals on PMDC drives.** Memorize the *form* of each equation; the *application* comes from the physical condition (motoring vs. braking).

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Boundary / Validity Condition | SI Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Armature KVL | $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Motoring (sign of $I_a$ positive) | V, A, Ω |
| 2 | Back-EMF | $E_b = K \Phi \omega$ | Always — flux constant for PMDC | V |
| 3 | Electromagnetic Torque | $T_e = K \Phi I_a$ | Always | N·m |
| 4 | Mechanical Power (air-gap) | $P_m = T_e \omega = E_b I_a$ | Always | W |
| 5 | Electrical Input Power | $P_{in} = V_a I_a$ | Motoring | W |
| 6 | Copper Loss | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 R_a$ | Always | W |
| 7 | Duty Cycle | $\alpha = T_{on}/T$ | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Buck (Step-Down) Output | $V_a = \alpha V_s$ | Quadrant I motoring | V |
| 9 | Boost (Step-Up) Output | $V_s = (1-\alpha) E_b$ | Quadrant II regenerative braking | V |
| 10 | No-Load Speed (Buck) | $\omega_0 = \alpha V_s / (K \Phi)$ | Ideal no-load condition | rad/s |
| 11 | Motor Current (Buck) | $I_a = (\alpha V_s - E_b) / R_a$ | Continuous conduction | A |
| 12 | Vehicle Linear Speed | $v = \omega \cdot r_{wheel}$ | Mechanical coupling | m/s |
| 13 | Tractive Force | $F_t = T_e \cdot G / r_{wheel}$ | Includes gear ratio $G$ | N |

> [!TIP]
> **Note on Notation:** In Section 5 numericals, the source/battery voltage may be denoted $V_s$, $V_b$, or $V_{dc}$ — they all refer to the same physical quantity. The armature terminal voltage is $V_a$ or $V_m$.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **PMDC drives in E-rickshaws (India):** Use a 48 V PMDC motor driven by a single-quadrant chopper. Speed is controlled by a foot-pedal potentiometer that varies the PWM duty cycle $\alpha$ from 0% to 95%.
2. **Two-wheeler EVs (e.g., electric scooters):** Use PMDC or BLDC machines with bidirectional choppers. Regenerative braking is activated when the brake lever is pulled — the chopper transitions to boost mode and pumps current back to the Li-ion pack.
3. **Forklifts and warehouse robots:** PMDC drives offer predictable, linear torque control essential for load handling.
4. **Solar water pumps:** PMDC motors coupled directly to solar panels through a buck chopper implement maximum power point tracking (MPPT).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of a Buck Chopper Feeding a PMDC Motor

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** This is the single most asked numerical topic in KTU University Exams (2018, 2019, 2022, 2023 papers contain at least one question on this). A flawless derivation here guarantees full marks.

### Setup
Consider a PMDC motor armature modelled as a series combination of $R_a$, $L_a$, and the back-EMF source $E_b$. The chopper switch $S$ connects the source $V_s$ to this load during $T_{on}$ and disconnects it during $T_{off}$. The freewheeling diode $D_F$ maintains continuous current during $T_{off}$.

**Assumption:** Armature inductance $L_a$ is large enough to maintain **continuous conduction** throughout the switching cycle.

### Step 1 — Voltage Across Motor During $T_{on}$ (Switch Closed)

When the chopper switch $S$ is **ON**, the source $V_s$ is applied directly across the motor terminals. The freewheeling diode is reverse-biased and carries no current. Applying KVL around the closed loop containing $V_s$, $L_a$, $R_a$, and $E_b$:

$$V_s = L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + R_a i_a + E_b \quad \text{... (during } T_{on}\text{)}$$

### Step 2 — Voltage Across Motor During $T_{off}$ (Switch Open)

When the switch $S$ is **OFF**, the source is disconnected. The motor inductance $L_a$ maintains current flow through the freewheeling diode $D_F$ in a closed loop with $R_a$ and $E_b$ only. The voltage across the motor terminals collapses to approximately **zero** (ideal diode drop neglected). KVL gives:

$$0 = L_a \frac{di_a}{dt} + R_a i_a + E_b \quad \text{... (during } T_{off}\text{)}$$

### Step 3 — Average Voltage Over One Switching Cycle

In **steady state**, the average voltage across the armature inductance over one complete period must equal zero (volt-second balance / inductor ripple energy conservation):

$$\int_0^{T} v_L(t) \, dt = 0$$

This is equivalent to stating that the **average armature voltage** $V_a$ equals the **time-weighted average** of the terminal voltage during the two sub-intervals:

$$V_a = \frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_0^{T_{on}} V_s \, dt + \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0 \, dt \right]$$

$$V_a = \frac{1}{T} \left[ V_s \cdot T_{on} + 0 \right] = V_s \cdot \frac{T_{on}}{T}$$

### Step 4 — Final Result

$$\boxed{V_a = \alpha \cdot V_s \quad \text{where} \quad \alpha = \frac{T_{on}}{T}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU result** for the buck-chopper-fed PMDC drive. Once $V_a$ is known, the armature current follows from Ohm's law applied to the motor:

$$I_a = \frac{V_a - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{\alpha V_s - K \Phi \omega}{R_a}$$

and the developed torque is:

$$T_e = K \Phi I_a = \frac{K \Phi \left( \alpha V_s - K \Phi \omega \right)}{R_a}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Regenerative Braking Mode Using a Boost Chopper

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical context:** The EV is coasting or braking. The PMDC machine is now driven by the wheels' kinetic energy through the drivetrain. The rotor spins at speed $\omega$ well above the no-load speed corresponding to the battery voltage $V_s$. Hence $E_b = K \Phi \omega > V_s$, and current would naturally tend to flow *from* the motor *into* the battery — but only if a path and voltage condition are satisfied.

**Problem:** Directly connecting the motor to the battery at low speeds would cause a *forward* (motoring) current, defeating the purpose. We need the current to flow **only** from the motor to the battery.

**Solution:** A **boost chopper** is inserted between the motor and the battery. During the switch-ON interval, the motor terminals are shorted through the switch (chopper inductor stores energy, current ramps up). During the switch-OFF interval, the inductor releases its energy in series with the motor's $E_b$, and the combined voltage exceeds $V_s$, forward-biasing the boost diode and pumping current into the battery.

### Step 1 — Voltage Relationship in Boost Mode

The boost chopper, in continuous conduction, obeys the canonical relation:

$$V_s = (1 - \alpha) \cdot V_a^{gen}$$

where $V_a^{gen}$ is the average voltage appearing at the motor's armature terminals during regeneration. Setting $V_a^{gen} = E_b$ (armature resistance drop small in generating mode for analysis):

$$V_s = (1 - \alpha) E_b$$

### Step 2 — Solving for the Required Duty Cycle

Rearranging for the duty cycle required to brake at speed $\omega$:

$$1 - \alpha = \frac{V_s}{E_b} = \frac{V_s}{K \Phi \omega}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_{brake} = 1 - \frac{V_s}{K \Phi \omega}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Condition for Regenerative Braking:**
> $$\omega > \frac{V_s}{K \Phi} \quad \text{(rotor speed must exceed the motor's "battery-matching" speed)}$$
> If $\omega$ falls below this threshold, $E_b < V_s$ and the converter cannot boost the voltage — the system must switch to **dynamic (rheostatic) braking**.

### Step 3 — Regenerative Braking Current and Torque

The current flowing back into the battery during regeneration:

$$I_{reg} = \frac{E_b - V_s^{boost}}{R_{path}}$$

where $V_s^{boost}$ is the effective boosted terminal voltage. The electromagnetic torque during regeneration is **negative** (opposing motion), giving the **braking torque**:

$$T_{brake} = K \Phi I_{reg} \quad \text{(magnitude; direction opposes } \omega\text{)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Dynamic (Rheostatic) Braking

When regenerative braking is not feasible (low speed, or battery full and cannot accept charge), the motor is disconnected from the source and its armature terminals are connected across a **braking resistor $R_b$** through a switch. The machine now acts as a separately excited (well, permanent-magnet excited) generator dissipating kinetic energy as heat in $R_b$.

**Generated current:**

$$I_{brake} = \frac{E_b}{R_a + R_b} = \frac{K \Phi \omega}{R_a + R_b}$$

**Braking torque:**

$$T_{brake} = K \Phi I_{brake} = \frac{(K \Phi)^2 \omega}{R_a + R_b}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common student mistake is to write the dynamic-braking torque as proportional to $\omega^2$. It is **directly proportional to $\omega$** because $E_b \propto \omega$ and $I_{brake} \propto E_b$, while torque depends on $I_{brake}$ (not on $E_b$ directly).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing All Three Braking Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison table is a guaranteed 7-mark question type in KTU 2024 ESE.** Be ready to reproduce it verbatim.

| Feature | Regenerative Braking | Dynamic (Rheostatic) Braking | Plugging (Reverse Current) Braking |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Energy Destination** | Returned to battery (recovered) | Dissipated as heat in $R_b$ | Dissipated as heat in $R_a$ and external resistor |
| **Power Electronics** | Bidirectional chopper (boost mode) | Simple switch + resistor | Requires reversing switch + resistor |
| **Efficiency** | **Highest** (energy saved) | Lowest (energy wasted) | Low (energy wasted) |
| **Speed Range** | Effective only at high $\omega$ | Effective across wide range | Effective across wide range |
| **Battery State-of-Charge (SoC) Impact** | Increases SoC | No effect | No effect |
| **Braking Torque Expression** | $\propto (E_b - V_s) \cdot K\Phi / R$ | $(K\Phi)^2 \omega / (R_a + R_b)$ | $\approx 2 \cdot K\Phi V_s / R$ (high initial) |
| **Component Stress** | Low (controlled current) | Moderate (resistor heating) | **Highest** (twice normal current initially) |
| **EV Application** | Primary braking mode in modern EVs | Backup / low-speed braking | Emergency / hill-hold only |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A 72 V PMDC motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$ and machine constant $K \Phi = 0.5 \, \text{V·s/rad}$. It is supplied from a 72 V battery through a buck chopper operating at a duty cycle $\alpha = 0.6$. The motor drives a constant load torque $T_L = 5 \, \text{N·m}$.
> **(a)** Determine the steady-state armature current, motor speed, and developed torque.
> **(b)** If the chopper duty cycle is now changed to $\alpha = 0.3$ while the load torque remains 5 N·m, will the motor stall? Justify quantitatively.

### Part (a) — Solution

**Step 1: Average armature voltage (buck chopper):**
$$V_a = \alpha V_s = 0.6 \times 72 = 43.2 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: At steady state, developed torque equals load torque:**
$$T_e = T_L = K \Phi \cdot I_a \implies I_a = \frac{T_L}{K \Phi} = \frac{5}{0.5} = 10 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Back-EMF from the armature circuit equation:**
$$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 43.2 - (10)(0.5) = 43.2 - 5 = 38.2 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Motor speed from back-EMF equation:**
$$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K \Phi} = \frac{38.2}{0.5} = 76.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 5: Convert to RPM (KTU frequently asks for this):**
$$N = \omega \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 76.4 \times 9.5493 \approx 729.5 \text{ RPM}$$

**Answers (a):** $I_a = 10$ A, $\omega = 76.4$ rad/s, $T_e = 5$ N·m, $N \approx 730$ RPM.

### Part (b) — Will the Motor Stall?

**Step 1: New armature voltage at $\alpha = 0.3$:**
$$V_a' = 0.3 \times 72 = 21.6 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Compute the maximum back-EMF the motor can develop while supplying $T_L = 5$ N·m:**

At the verge of stalling, the speed is zero, so $E_b = 0$. The armature current would be:
$$I_{a,stall} = \frac{V_a'}{R_a} = \frac{21.6}{0.5} = 43.2 \text{ A}$$

The corresponding maximum starting torque is:
$$T_{max} = K \Phi \cdot I_{a,stall} = 0.5 \times 43.2 = 21.6 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Compare with load torque:**
$$T_{max} = 21.6 \text{ N·m} \gg T_L = 5 \text{ N·m}$$

The motor can supply more than enough starting torque. **The motor will NOT stall.** It will run at a new lower speed.

**Step 4: Find the new steady-state speed:**
At steady state, $T_e = T_L = 5$ N·m, so $I_a = 10$ A (unchanged).
$$E_b' = V_a' - I_a R_a = 21.6 - 5 = 16.6 \text{ V}$$
$$\omega' = \frac{E_b'}{K \Phi} = \frac{16.6}{0.5} = 33.2 \text{ rad/s} \approx 317 \text{ RPM}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Stalling Condition:** A PMDC motor stalls only when $T_{max} = K \Phi V_a / R_a < T_L$. In this problem, $T_{max} = 21.6$ N·m $\gg 5$ N·m, so stalling is impossible at this load.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Mode Transition State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE: System Power ON
    IDLE --> MOTORING: Accelerator Pressed and alpha increases from 0
    MOTORING --> REGEN_BRAKE: Brake Lever Pulled and omega greater than Vs over K Phi
    MOTORING --> DYNAMIC_BRAKE: Brake Lever Pulled and omega less than Vs over K Phi
    REGEN_BRAKE --> DYNAMIC_BRAKE: omega falls below Vs over K Phi threshold
    DYNAMIC_BRAKE --> IDLE: Speed reaches zero
    REGEN_BRAKE --> MOTORING: Accelerator Pressed
    DYNAMIC_BRAKE --> MOTORING: Accelerator Pressed
    MOTORING --> IDLE: Accelerator Released and Speed zero
    REGEN_BRAKE --> IDLE: Brake Released and Speed zero
    DYNAMIC_BRAKE --> IDLE: Brake Released and Speed zero
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **State Transition Insights:**
> - The transition `MOTORING → REGEN_BRAKE` is initiated by the controller when the brake pedal is pressed AND the rotor speed is high enough that $E_b > V_s$.
> - The transition `REGEN_BRAKE → DYNAMIC_BRAKE` is automatic and occurs as the vehicle slows down, since at low speeds the back-EMF can no longer support boost-mode regeneration.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Power Flow During the Four Quadrants of Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Q1["QUADRANT 1  FORWARD MOTORING"]
        Q1A["Battery +"] --> Q1B["Chopper Buck"]
        Q1B --> Q1C["PMDC Motor"]
        Q1C --> Q1D["Wheels Forward"]
    end
    subgraph Q2["QUADRANT 2  FORWARD BRAKING REGEN"]
        Q2A["Wheels Forward"] --> Q2B["PMDC Generator"]
        Q2B --> Q2C["Chopper Boost"]
        Q2C --> Q2D["Battery +  Charging"]
    end
    subgraph Q3["QUADRANT 3  REVERSE MOTORING"]

    end
    subgraph Q4["QUADRANT 4  REVERSE BRAKING REGEN"]
        Q4A["Wheels Reverse"] --> Q4B["PMDC Reverse Gen"]
        Q4B --> Q4C["Chopper Boost"]
        Q4C --> Q4D["Battery +  Charging"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Note on EV Use:** Quadrants 1 and 2 are the most commonly used in a standard forward-driving EV. Quadrants 3 and 4 are used for reverse gear, typically implemented through an H-bridge or a separate contactor circuit (full four-quadrant operation requires a more complex chopper topology, e.g., a **full-bridge bidirectional converter**).

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Waveform Timing of a Buck Chopper (Continuous Conduction)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TIMING["CHOPPER SWITCHING WAVEFORMS  ONE COMPLETE PERIOD T"]
        S1["GATE PULSE 0 to Ton alpha T ON at T OFF"]
        S2["OUTPUT VOLTAGE  Vs during Ton  0 during Toff"]
        S3["ARMATURE CURRENT  Rises during Ton  Falls during Toff"]
        S4["FREEWHEEL DIODE CURRENT  Zero during Ton  Conducts during Toff"]
    end
    S1 --- S2
    S2 --- S3
    S3 --- S4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key observation:** The armature current $i_a(t)$ is **continuous** but **rippled** (rising during $T_{on}$ and falling during $T_{off}$). The amplitude of this ripple is determined by the chopper frequency, the armature inductance $L_a$, and the duty cycle. Higher $L_a$ or higher $f_{sw}$ → lower ripple → smoother torque.
