# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each — Short Answer)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> *Why is a DC-DC converter (chopper) necessary for the speed control of a PMDC motor in an electric vehicle?*

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
A PMDC motor has a constant field flux $\Phi$ produced by permanent magnets, so its speed depends solely on the applied armature voltage. A battery provides a fixed DC voltage. Therefore, a **DC-DC converter (chopper)** is required to vary the **average armature voltage** $V_a = \alpha V_s$ by controlling the duty cycle $\alpha$, thereby enabling smooth, lossless speed control of the EV traction motor. The chopper also enables regenerative braking by acting in the boost mode.

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> *What is meant by regenerative braking in a PMDC machine? Under what condition on speed does it occur?*

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
**Regenerative braking** is the process of operating the PMDC machine as a **generator** during deceleration, converting the vehicle's kinetic energy into electrical energy and feeding it back into the battery. The necessary condition is that the rotor speed must be high enough that the back-EMF $E_b = K \Phi \omega$ **exceeds** the battery voltage $V_s$ (or, when a boost chopper is used, the boosted motor terminal voltage exceeds $V_s$). Mathematically, $\omega > V_s / (K \Phi)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Full-Length with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module typically contains ONE 14-mark question with internal choice (a) OR (b). Below are TWO complete alternative questions for this topic. Be prepared to answer either.

---

### Question B1 (Choice A) — 14 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, PEEET522]**
> **(a)** With a neat power circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **buck chopper-fed PMDC motor drive** for the motoring mode of an electric vehicle. Derive the expression for the average armature voltage and the steady-state speed-torque relationship. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A **96 V** PMDC motor has armature resistance $R_a = 0.4 \, \Omega$ and machine constant $K \Phi = 0.6 \, \text{V·s/rad}$. It is fed from a **96 V** battery through a buck chopper at duty cycle $\alpha = 0.7$. The motor drives a load torque $T_L = 8 \, \text{N·m}$.
>   (i) Calculate the armature current, back-EMF, and motor speed in RPM.
>   (ii) The vehicle now begins to coast downhill. Determine the **minimum speed at which regenerative braking becomes feasible** using a boost chopper, and compute the **braking torque** at a speed of $200 \, \text{rad/s}$ if the boost chopper duty cycle is set to $\alpha = 0.4$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B1 (a)

**Buck Chopper Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The buck chopper consists of a **controllable switch $S$** (IGBT or Power MOSFET), a **freewheeling diode $D_F$** connected across the motor armature, and the **PMDC motor** as the load. The battery $V_s$ is connected at the input.

**Modes of Operation (3 marks):**
- **Mode 1 (Switch ON, $0 \le t \le T_{on}$):** The source $V_s$ appears across the motor armature. Current builds up in $L_a$ and freewheeling diode is reverse-biased.
- **Mode 2 (Switch OFF, $T_{on} \le t \le T$):** The motor inductance $L_a$ maintains the current by freewheeling through $D_F$. The voltage across the motor terminals becomes zero (ideal diode).

**Derivation of Average Voltage and Speed-Torque Relation (2 marks):**
$$V_a = \alpha V_s$$
$$I_a = \frac{V_a - E_b}{R_a} = \frac{\alpha V_s - K \Phi \omega}{R_a}$$
$$T_e = K \Phi I_a = \frac{K \Phi (\alpha V_s - K \Phi \omega)}{R_a}$$

Rearranging for speed:
$$\omega = \frac{\alpha V_s}{K \Phi} - \frac{R_a T_e}{(K \Phi)^2}$$

This is a **linear decreasing function of torque**, intercepting the $\omega$-axis at $\omega_0 = \alpha V_s / (K \Phi)$ and the $T$-axis at $T_{stall} = \alpha V_s K \Phi / R_a$.

#### Model Solution to Question B1 (b)

**Part (i) — Motoring calculations (3.5 marks):**

Step 1: $V_a = \alpha V_s = 0.7 \times 96 = 67.2$ V

Step 2: $I_a = T_L / (K \Phi) = 8 / 0.6 = 13.333$ A

Step 3: $E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 67.2 - (13.333)(0.4) = 67.2 - 5.333 = 61.867$ V

Step 4: $\omega = E_b / (K \Phi) = 61.867 / 0.6 = 103.11$ rad/s

Step 5: $N = 103.11 \times 60 / (2\pi) = 984.6$ RPM ≈ **985 RPM**

**Part (ii) — Regenerative braking calculations (3.5 marks):**

Step 1: Minimum speed for regeneration occurs when the boost chopper operates at the boundary $\alpha \to 0$ (so $V_s = E_b$):
$$\omega_{min} = \frac{V_s}{K \Phi} = \frac{96}{0.6} = 160 \text{ rad/s}$$
Below this speed, regenerative mode cannot be sustained and the controller must switch to dynamic braking.

Step 2: At $\omega = 200$ rad/s, the back-EMF is:
$$E_b = K \Phi \omega = 0.6 \times 200 = 120 \text{ V}$$

Step 3: The boost chopper equation $V_s = (1 - \alpha) E_b$ gives the relationship between source and motor voltages. Solving for the motor-side current using the simplified boost model:
$$I_{reg} = \frac{E_b - V_s / (1-\alpha)}{R_a} = \frac{120 - 96/0.6}{0.4} = \frac{120 - 160}{0.4} = \frac{-40}{0.4} = -100 \text{ A}$$

The negative sign indicates current flowing *from the motor into the battery* (regenerative direction). The magnitude $|I_{reg}| = 100$ A.

Step 4: The braking torque (magnitude) is:
$$T_{brake} = K \Phi \cdot |I_{reg}| = 0.6 \times 100 = 60 \text{ N·m}$$

This is a substantial braking torque, demonstrating the effectiveness of regenerative braking at high speeds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:**
> Many students compute $E_b$ at the minimum-speed condition as $E_b = V_s$ and then forget to apply the boost equation properly. **Always verify the sign convention** — in regenerative mode, current flows OUT of the motor armature (negative $I_a$ in the motoring convention). A negative current must be flagged explicitly in your answer.

---

### Question B2 (Choice B) — 14 Marks
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, PEEET522]**
> **(a)** Explain with necessary diagrams the **three modes of electrical braking** of a PMDC traction motor used in electric vehicles. Compare their energy-efficiency, complexity, and braking-torque characteristics. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A 48 V PMDC traction motor in a small EV has $R_a = 0.2 \, \Omega$ and $K \Phi = 0.3 \, \text{V·s/rad}$. During a downhill run, the motor is driven at $250 \, \text{rad/s}$ by the wheels. The motor is disconnected from the chopper and its terminals are connected across a **braking resistor $R_b = 1.8 \, \Omega$** for **dynamic braking**.
>   (i) Compute the **braking current** and **braking torque**.
>   (ii) If the same resistor $R_b$ is used in a **plugging (reverse-current) braking** scheme where the armature is suddenly reconnected with **reversed polarity** to the 48 V supply, find the **initial braking current and torque** and the **instantaneous power dissipated**. Compare this with the dynamic-braking case. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B2 (a)

**Three Modes of Braking (5 marks for description + 2 marks for comparison table):**

1. **Regenerative Braking:** Motor acts as a generator; kinetic energy is converted to electrical energy and returned to the battery via a bidirectional chopper operating in boost mode. Condition: $E_b > V_s$ or boost chopper raises the motor terminal voltage above $V_s$. Highest energy efficiency, but requires complex bidirectional power electronics and is only effective at high speeds.

2. **Dynamic (Rheostatic) Braking:** Armature disconnected from supply and connected across an external resistor $R_b$. The motor acts as a generator dissipating kinetic energy as $I^2 R_b$ heat. Simple, no charging of battery, effective across a wide speed range. Lowest efficiency.

3. **Plugging (Reverse-Current) Braking:** Armature terminals are suddenly reversed while the motor is still rotating in the original direction. The back-EMF $E_b$ and the supply voltage $V_s$ now add in series, driving a very large current through the armature. A current-limiting resistor $R_b$ is mandatory. Highest braking torque, but extremely wasteful — nearly all kinetic energy is converted to heat, and the motor may be damaged if not carefully controlled.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Regenerative | Dynamic | Plugging |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Energy Recovery | Yes (to battery) | No (heat) | No (heat) |
| Circuit Complexity | High (bidirectional) | Low | Medium |
| Energy Efficiency | **High** | Low | **Lowest** |
| Initial Current Magnitude | Moderate | Moderate | **Highest** (≈ 2× normal) |
| Typical Use Case | Normal deceleration | Low-speed / backup | Emergency stop only |

#### Model Solution to Question B2 (b)

**Part (i) — Dynamic braking (3.5 marks):**

Step 1: Back-EMF at $\omega = 250$ rad/s:
$$E_b = K \Phi \omega = 0.3 \times 250 = 75 \text{ V}$$

Step 2: Braking current through $R_a + R_b$:
$$I_{brake} = \frac{E_b}{R_a + R_b} = \frac{75}{0.2 + 1.8} = \frac{75}{2.0} = 37.5 \text{ A}$$

Step 3: Braking torque:
$$T_{brake} = K \Phi \cdot I_{brake} = 0.3 \times 37.5 = 11.25 \text{ N·m}$$

**Part (ii) — Plugging braking (3.5 marks):**

Step 1: When the armature is reversed while the rotor is still spinning at 250 rad/s, the supply voltage $V_s$ and back-EMF $E_b$ are now **in series, aiding each other** (both pushing current through the armature in the reverse direction). Total driving voltage:
$$V_{total} = V_s + E_b = 48 + 75 = 123 \text{ V}$$

Step 2: Initial current (with $R_b$ in the circuit):
$$I_{plug} = \frac{V_{total}}{R_a + R_b} = \frac{123}{0.2 + 1.8} = \frac{123}{2.0} = 61.5 \text{ A}$$

Step 3: Initial braking torque (note: torque is in the direction of the reversed current, which now opposes the original rotation):
$$T_{plug} = K \Phi \cdot I_{plug} = 0.3 \times 61.5 = 18.45 \text{ N·m}$$

Step 4: Instantaneous power dissipated in $R_a + R_b$:
$$P_{diss} = I_{plug}^2 (R_a + R_b) = (61.5)^2 \times 2.0 = 3782.25 \times 2.0 = 7564.5 \text{ W} \approx 7.56 \text{ kW}$$

**Comparison (1 mark for the comparative insight):**
- Plugging produces **~1.64× higher braking torque** than dynamic braking (18.45 vs 11.25 N·m).
- However, the dissipated power is **~2.73× higher** in plugging (7564.5 W vs 2812.5 W in dynamic), confirming that plugging is **far more energy-wasteful** and is reserved for emergency braking only.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:**
> Students frequently forget that during **plugging**, $V_s$ and $E_b$ **add up** (series-aiding), not subtract. Writing $V_{total} = V_s - E_b$ is a classic 1-mark loss. Always draw the equivalent circuit at the moment of reversal to convince yourself of the polarity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Final-hour revision notes for the night before the exam. Memorize the ★ items.**

### ★ Master Equations (Must Memorize)
- ★ $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ (armature KVL — sign of $I_a$ changes with mode)
- ★ $E_b = K \Phi \omega$ (back-EMF)
- ★ $T_e = K \Phi I_a$ (torque)
- ★ $V_a = \alpha V_s$ (buck chopper motoring)
- ★ $V_s = (1 - \alpha) E_b$ (boost chopper regenerative)
- ★ $\omega_{regen,min} = V_s / (K \Phi)$ (regenerative-braking threshold)
- ★ $T_{brake,dyn} = (K \Phi)^2 \omega / (R_a + R_b)$ (dynamic braking)

### ★ Operating Mode Summary
- ★ **Motoring:** $V_a > E_b$ → $I_a$ flows from source to motor → torque in direction of rotation.
- ★ **Generating/Regenerative:** $E_b > V_a$ (or boost-assisted) → $I_a$ flows from motor to source → torque opposes rotation.
- ★ **Dynamic braking:** Armature shorted through $R_b$ → energy dissipated as heat.
- ★ **Plugging:** Armature reversed → $V_s$ and $E_b$ add → very high braking torque and heat.

### ★ Hardware / Topology Points
- ★ PMDC field flux is **constant and non-adjustable** → only armature voltage and current are controlled.
- ★ Buck chopper for motoring; boost chopper for regeneration; bidirectional chopper for full four-quadrant operation.
- ★ Switching frequency of EV choppers: **5–20 kHz** (above audible range, minimizes torque ripple).
- ★ Freewheeling diode $D_F$ is essential to maintain continuous armature current during switch-OFF.

### ★ Common Numerical Traps to Avoid
- ✗ Forgetting to convert $\omega$ (rad/s) to $N$ (RPM) using $N = \omega \times 60 / (2\pi)$.
- ✗ Using the wrong sign in plugging — always remember $V_{total} = V_s + E_b$ during reversal.
- ✗ Confusing $K_e$ and $K_t$ — in SI units, **they are numerically equal** ($K_e = K_t$), but their units differ.
- ✗ Forgetting that **stalling** occurs when $T_{max} = K \Phi V_a / R_a < T_L$, NOT when $V_a < E_b$ (the motor can run at any speed as long as torque balance is satisfied).

### ★ EV Application Highlights
- ★ E-rickshaws and 2-wheeler EVs commonly use PMDC drives due to **simplicity and low cost**.
- ★ Regenerative braking can extend EV range by **10–20%** in typical urban driving cycles.
- ★ The chopper and motor controller are typically integrated into a single **Motor Control Unit (MCU)** in modern EVs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PMSM Drives: Review of PMSM Motor Basics - Independent Control of Orthogonal Flux and Torque (Concept Only) - Field Oriented Control (FOC) - Sensored and Sensorless Control (Block Diagram Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PMSM Drives: Review of PMSM Motor Basics - Independent Control of Orthogonal Flux and Torque (Concept Only) - Field Oriented Control (FOC) - Sensored and Sensorless Control (Block Diagram Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a PMSM?

A **Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM)** is a brushless AC motor in which the **rotor field is produced by permanent magnets** (typically Neodymium-Iron-Boron, NdFeB) mounted on the rotor, while the **stator carries a three-phase sinusoidal winding** energized by a variable-frequency, variable-amplitude inverter.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Standard Terminology):** *"A PMSM is a synchronous machine whose rotor excitation is provided by permanent magnets, producing a constant rotor flux linkage $\lambda_m$ that rotates at synchronous speed $\omega_e = \frac{P}{2}\omega_m$ with the stator field."*

### Key Constructional Features

- **Rotor:** Surface-mounted or interior permanent magnets. No field winding, no slip rings, no brushes.
- **Stator:** Three-phase distributed winding, star-connected.
- **Air-gap flux density:** Generated purely by permanent magnets — **constant** under steady temperature.
- **Number of pole pairs:** $P/2$, where $P$ is the total number of poles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because rotor flux is fixed by magnets, the only degree of freedom in a PMSM is the **stator current vector**. The art of controlling a PMSM is *where* you push that current vector inside the machine and *when* you do so — this is exactly what FOC achieves.

---

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy: PMSM as a Bicycle on a Circular Track

Imagine a **bicycle wheel** rolling on a perfectly circular track. The pedals (your input torque) drive the **rim (rotor)**, but to control the speed, you must carefully choose:

1. **How hard** you push the pedal (torque magnitude).
2. **At what angle** of the crank you apply that push (torque direction).

A PMSM works the same way:

- The **stator current vector $\vec{i}_s$** is your "pedal push."
- The **rotor magnet flux $\vec{\lambda}_m$** is the "direction the wheel is currently facing."
- The **cross product** $\vec{\lambda}_m \times \vec{i}_s$ gives the instantaneous torque.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Insight:** If you push *along* the direction the wheel is already moving (parallel to $\vec{\lambda}_m$), you only change the flux magnitude — you produce **no torque**. If you push **perpendicular** to that direction, you get **maximum torque for the least current**. Field Oriented Control is essentially the technique that *forces* the controller to always push perpendicular.

---

## 1.3 Why PMSMs Dominate Electric Vehicles

| Property | Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Power density | Very high (no rotor copper losses) |
| Efficiency | Typically 95%–98% |
| Torque-to-inertia ratio | Excellent for rapid acceleration |
| Cooling | Easy (heat is only in stator) |
| Speed range | Wide (with flux weakening) |

> [!NOTE]
> Industry leaders **Tesla Model 3/Y, BMW i-series, Hyundai Ioniq, and Tata Nexon EV** all use PMSM or Interior PMSM (IPMSM) traction motors, confirming PMSM's place as the **de-facto traction motor of modern BEVs**.

---

## 1.4 Field Oriented Control (FOC) — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Field Oriented Control (FOC)**, also called **Vector Control**, is a technique that transforms the three-phase stator currents of a PMSM into a **two-axis rotating reference frame (d-q frame)** aligned with the rotor flux, enabling **independent (decoupled) control of flux and torque** through two scalar PI controllers.

In simple words: **FOC turns an AC motor control problem into two independent DC-motor-like control problems.**

---

## 1.5 Sensored vs. Sensorless Control — A One-Line Definition

- **Sensored Control:** Rotor position/speed is measured by a physical sensor (resolver, encoder, Hall sensor).
- **Sensorless Control:** Rotor position/speed is **estimated** mathematically from measured stator voltages and currents using observers (e.g., Sliding-Mode Observer, Extended Kalman Filter, MRAS).

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Sensored control is like driving a car with a *GPS speedometer* on the dashboard. Sensorless control is like driving *blindfolded* but using the *engine RPM sound* and *road vibration* to estimate your speed.

---

## 1.6 Why This Topic Matters in EV Engineering

In an EV, the traction inverter drives the PMSM. Without FOC, the motor cannot deliver smooth torque across the full speed range (0–20,000 rpm). FOC is the **brain of every modern EV powertrain**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PMSM Mathematical Model in Stationary Frame (a-b-c)

The three-phase stator voltage equations of a PMSM in the natural reference frame are:

$$v_a = R_s i_a + \frac{d\lambda_a}{dt}$$

$$v_b = R_s i_b + \frac{d\lambda_b}{dt}$$

$$v_c = R_s i_c + \frac{d\lambda_c}{dt}$$

where $R_s$ is the **stator phase resistance**, and $\lambda_a, \lambda_b, \lambda_c$ are the per-phase flux linkages that include contributions from stator currents and from the permanent-magnet rotor field.

**Why this form is *useless* for control:** These three equations are **mutually coupled** and **time-varying** because the rotor magnets physically rotate past the stator windings, making the stator inductances a function of rotor angle $\theta_e$. Direct control is mathematically intractable.

---

## 2.2 Clarke Transform (a-b-c → α-β)

The Clarke transform reduces the three-phase system to an equivalent **two-phase orthogonal stationary frame (α-β)**:

$$i_\alpha = i_a$$

$$i_\beta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(i_a + 2 i_b)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Clarke transform is **power-invariant** and produces two orthogonal, sinusoidally varying currents that still rotate at the same electrical frequency. It does *not* simplify the time-variance problem — it only reduces dimension.

---

## 2.3 Park Transform (α-β → d-q) — The Magic Step

The Park transform rotates the stationary α-β frame by the rotor electrical angle $\theta_e$ to produce the **d-q frame** that physically rotates *with the rotor*:

$$i_d = i_\alpha \cos\theta_e + i_\beta \sin\theta_e$$

$$i_q = -i_\alpha \sin\theta_e + i_\beta \cos\theta_e$$

**The "magic" result:** In the d-q frame, **all sinusoidal AC quantities become DC quantities** (steady-state), because the reference frame spins in lockstep with the rotor field.

---

## 2.4 PMSM Equations in the d-q Rotating Frame

The voltage equations in the synchronous d-q frame:

$$v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_m)$$

**Electromagnetic torque equation:**

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \left[ \lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **Surface PMSM (SPMSM):** $L_d = L_q = L_s$, so the second term vanishes and the torque equation reduces to:
> $$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \lambda_m i_q$$
> This is the **famous linear torque law** that makes PMSMs so beautifully controllable.

---

## 2.5 Independent (Decoupled) Control of Flux and Torque — The Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Concept-Only Statement:** *"In the d-q synchronous frame, the flux-producing component $i_d$ and the torque-producing component $i_q$ are orthogonal. Therefore, $i_d$ and $i_q$ can be controlled by two independent current loops, yielding decoupled control of flux and torque."*

**Physical meaning of decoupling:**

- The **d-axis current $i_d$** lies *along* the rotor magnet flux. It either **strengthens or weakens** the air-gap flux.
- The **q-axis current $i_q$** lies *perpendicular* to the rotor magnet flux. It produces **pure torque**, just like the tangential push on the bicycle wheel analogy.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this is the killer feature of PMSM/FOC:** In a separately-excited DC motor, field flux (rotor) and armature current (stator) are *naturally* orthogonal — that is why DC motors are easy to control. FOC gives the AC PMSM the *same* orthogonal structure *artificially*, by transforming into the d-q frame.

---

## 2.6 Field Oriented Control (FOC) — Operation

FOC is a **closed-loop cascade control structure** with three nested loops:

| Loop Level | Variable Controlled | Bandwidth | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Outer-most** | Speed $\omega_m$ | Lowest | Set $i_q^*$ from speed error |
| **Middle** | Torque-producing current $i_q$ | Medium | Decoupled torque control |
| **Inner** | Flux-producing current $i_d$ | High | Maintain flux at reference |
| **Inner-most** | Voltage $v_d, v_q$ | Highest | PWM generation |

**Operating sequence of FOC:**

1. Measure three-phase currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$.
2. Apply **Clarke transform** → $i_\alpha, i_\beta$.
3. Apply **Park transform** using measured/estimated $\theta_e$ → $i_d, i_q$.
4. Compare $i_d, i_q$ with references $i_d^*, i_q^*$ → PI controllers produce $v_d^*, v_q^*$.
5. Apply **inverse Park transform** → $v_\alpha^*, v_\beta^*$.
6. Apply **SVPWM (Space Vector PWM)** → gating signals for the 3-phase inverter.
7. Inverter applies voltages to PMSM → torque is produced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **quality of $\theta_e$** (its accuracy and resolution) *directly* determines the quality of FOC. Wrong $\theta_e$ = misaligned d-q frame = wrong $i_d, i_q$ decomposition = oscillating torque and reduced efficiency. This is the **single most critical signal** in any FOC system.

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **Table below uses $\vert$ and $\mid$ (rendered in LaTeX) — never the raw `|` pipe — to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\omega_e = \frac{P}{2}\omega_m$ | Electrical angular speed = pole pairs $\times$ mechanical speed |
| 2 | $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \frac{P}{2} \lambda_m i_q$ | Torque of SPMSM (linear in $i_q$) |
| 3 | $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \frac{P}{2}[\lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q)i_d i_q]$ | Torque of IPMSM (reluctance torque added) |
| 4 | $v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$ | d-axis voltage (contains cross-coupling $-\omega_e L_q i_q$) |
| 5 | $v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_m)$ | q-axis voltage (contains back-EMF $\omega_e \lambda_m$) |
| 6 | $\lambda_d = L_d i_d + \lambda_m$ | d-axis flux linkage (rotor magnet adds to $i_d$) |
| 7 | $\lambda_q = L_q i_q$ | q-axis flux linkage (rotor magnet has no q-axis component) |
| 8 | $E_b = \omega_e \lambda_m$ | Back-EMF constant in rad/s form |
| 9 | $K_t = \frac{3}{2} \frac{P}{2} \lambda_m$ | Torque constant of SPMSM (Nm/A) |
| 10 | $i_{d,\text{ref}} = 0$ | MTPA strategy for SPMSM: zero d-axis current for max efficiency |
| 11 | $i_q^* = \frac{T_e^*}{K_t}$ | q-axis current reference from torque command |
| 12 | $\theta_e = \int \omega_e \, dt$ | Rotor electrical angle by integration |
| 13 | $\omega_{\text{slip}} = 0$ | For PMSM, slip is zero — always synchronous |
| 14 | $P_{\text{out}} = T_e \cdot \omega_m$ | Mechanical output power of motor |

---

## 2.8 Sensored vs Sensorless Control — Conceptual Comparison

| Aspect | Sensored Control | Sensorless Control |
|---|---|---|
| Position sensor | Resolver, optical encoder, Hall IC | **None** — estimation only |
| Rotor angle source | Sensor signal | Observer / estimator from $v$ and $i$ |
| Low-speed performance | Excellent (≥ 0 rpm) | Poor (Back-EMF vanishes) |
| High-speed performance | Excellent | Excellent |
| Reliability | Reduced (sensor failure risk) | Higher (no sensor to fail) |
| Cost | Higher (sensor + cabling) | Lower |
| EV application | Most production EVs | Low-speed creep / redundancy |
| Common estimators | — | SMO, EKF, MRAS, HF signal injection |

> [!TIP]
> Most production EVs (e.g., Tesla) use **sensored primary control** with **sensorless as a backup** for sensor-fault tolerance, satisfying ASIL-D functional safety requirements of ISO 26262.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Clarke Transform (a-b-c → α-β)

### Starting Point
We have three stator phase currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ that are sinusoidally varying in time, displaced by $120°$ in space.

### Step 1 — Orthogonal Projection onto α-axis
The α-axis is aligned with the a-phase winding axis. Projecting $i_a, i_b, i_c$ onto α:

The a-phase aligns with α directly, so its contribution is $i_a$.

The b-phase and c-phase make angles of $120°$ and $240°$ with the α-axis respectively.

$$i_\alpha = i_a \cos 0 + i_b \cos 120 + i_c \cos 240$$

$$i_\alpha = i_a \cdot 1 + i_b \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) + i_c \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)$$

Using the balance condition $i_a + i_b + i_c = 0$, we can simplify:

$$i_\alpha = \frac{2}{3}\left(i_a - \frac{1}{2}i_b - \frac{1}{2}i_c\right) = i_a$$

### Step 2 — Orthogonal Projection onto β-axis
The β-axis is $90°$ ahead of the α-axis in space.

$$i_\beta = i_a \sin 0 + i_b \sin 120 + i_c \sin 240$$

$$i_\beta = i_a \cdot 0 + i_b \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + i_c \cdot \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)$$

$$i_\beta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(i_a + 2 i_b)$$

### Final Compact Result

$$\begin{aligned}
i_\alpha &= i_a \\
i_\beta &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(i_a + 2 i_b)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $\frac{2}{3}$ scaling factor in the **power-invariant** Clarke matrix is implicitly absorbed; the matrix form is:
> $$\begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\frac{1}{2} & -\frac{1}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_a \\ i_b \\ i_c \end{bmatrix}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Park Transform (α-β → d-q)

### Starting Point
The α-β frame is stationary. We want to rotate the reference frame by the rotor electrical angle $\theta_e$ so that the d-axis aligns with the rotor magnet flux axis.

### Step 1 — Rotation Matrix
A 2-D rotation by angle $\theta_e$ in the counter-clockwise direction is given by:

$$\begin{bmatrix} i_d \\ i_q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_e & \sin\theta_e \\ -\sin\theta_e & \cos\theta_e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} i_\alpha \\ i_\beta \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2 — Expand the Matrix Multiplication

$$i_d = i_\alpha \cos\theta_e + i_\beta \sin\theta_e$$

$$i_q = -i_\alpha \sin\theta_e + i_\beta \cos\theta_e$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Clarke Outputs
Substituting $i_\alpha = i_a$ and $i_\beta = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(i_a + 2 i_b)$ into the equations above:

$$i_d = i_a \cos\theta_e + \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(i_a + 2 i_b)\sin\theta_e$$

$$i_q = -i_a \sin\theta_e + \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}(i_a + 2 i_b)\cos\theta_e$$

### Step 4 — Steady-State Sinusoidal Check
Suppose the actual currents are:

$$i_a = I_m \cos(\omega_e t)$$

$$i_b = I_m \cos(\omega_e t - 120°)$$

$$i_c = I_m \cos(\omega_e t + 120°)$$

With $\theta_e = \omega_e t$, after trigonometric simplification, one obtains:

$$i_d = 0, \quad i_q = I_m$$

**This proves the d-q transform produces DC quantities from sinusoids in steady state.** The frequency information is *absorbed* into the rotation of the reference frame.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: PMSM Torque Equation in d-q Frame

### Step 1 — Co-energy Method
The electromagnetic torque is the partial derivative of magnetic co-energy $W'_{co}$ with respect to rotor angle $\theta_m$:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_m}$$

### Step 2 — Co-energy in d-q Frame
For a non-salient PMSM with linear magnetics:

$$W'_{co} = \frac{3}{2} \left[ \frac{1}{2} L_d i_d^2 + \frac{1}{2} L_q i_q^2 + \lambda_m i_d \right]$$

### Step 3 — Account for Pole Pairs
The electrical angle $\theta_e$ is related to mechanical angle by $\theta_e = \frac{P}{2}\theta_m$, so $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_m} = \frac{P}{2}\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_e}$.

### Step 4 — Differentiate

$$\begin{aligned}
T_e &= \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_e}\left[ \frac{1}{2} L_d i_d^2 + \frac{1}{2} L_q i_q^2 + \lambda_m i_d \right] \\
&= \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \left[ L_d i_d \frac{\partial i_d}{\partial \theta_e} + L_q i_q \frac{\partial i_q}{\partial \theta_e} + \lambda_m \frac{\partial i_d}{\partial \theta_e} \right]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Constraint
Since $i_d^2 + i_q^2 = \text{const}$ in steady state, the only $\theta_e$-dependence appears through the term $\lambda_m i_d$. Differentiating yields:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \left[ \lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation of the two terms:**
> - $\lambda_m i_q$ → **Magnetic torque** (alignment of stator field with rotor magnets).
> - $(L_d - L_q) i_d i_q$ → **Reluctance torque** (present only in IPMSM, due to rotor saliency).
> - For SPMSM, $L_d = L_q$, so reluctance torque = 0. **All torque is from $\lambda_m i_q$.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Decoupling Voltage Compensation (FOC Inner Loop)

### The Cross-Coupling Problem
From the d-q voltage equations:

$$v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \lambda_m$$

The terms $-\omega_e L_q i_q$ and $\omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \lambda_m$ are **cross-coupling** terms that couple the d and q dynamics.

### Step 1 — PI Controller Output
A PI controller on $i_d$ produces:

$$v_d^{\text{PI}} = K_{pd}(i_d^* - i_d) + K_{id}\int (i_d^* - i_d) dt$$

### Step 2 — Add Feed-Forward Decoupling Voltage
The total d-axis command becomes:

$$v_d^* = v_d^{\text{PI}} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

This **cancels exactly** the cross-coupling $-\omega_e L_q i_q$ in the d-axis equation, leaving:

$$v_d^* = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt}$$

### Step 3 — Same for q-axis

$$v_q^* = v_q^{\text{PI}} + \omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \lambda_m$$

After cancellation:

$$v_q^* = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Result:** The two current loops now behave as **two independent first-order RL circuits** with their own time constants $\tau_d = L_d/R_s$ and $\tau_q = L_q/R_s$. Each PI can be tuned separately — that is the *practical* meaning of "decoupled control."

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Cause, Effect, and Engineering Action

| Phenomenon | Cause | Effect on EV | Engineering Action in FOC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Torque ripple | Harmonics in $i_a, i_b, i_c$ | Vibration in cabin | High-bandwidth $i_d, i_q$ loops + SVPWM |
| Flux weakening at high speed | Back-EMF $\omega_e \lambda_m$ exceeds DC bus | Torque drops at top speed | Set $i_d^* < 0$ to demagnetize air-gap flux |
| MTPA below base speed | Copper loss minimization | Maximize km range | $i_d^* = 0$ for SPMSM |
| Position sensor failure | Resolver/encoder fault | Loss of traction | Switch to sensorless observer |
| Low-speed sensorless inaccuracy | Back-EMF $\to 0$ as $\omega \to 0$ | Jerky startup | High-frequency signal injection |
| Iron losses at high speed | Hysteresis + eddy currents | Reduced efficiency | Use $i_d^* < 0$ strategically |
| Field-weakening region | Constant-power region of EV map | Highway cruising | $i_d^* = -\lambda_m/L_d - V_{\text{DC}}/(2\omega_e L_d)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Full Field Oriented Control (FOC) Block Diagram — PMSM Drive

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[Speed Reference omega_ref] --> B[Speed PI Controller]
  B --> C[Torque Reference Te_ref]
  C --> D[Inverse Torque Law iq_ref]
  D --> E[iq PI Controller]
  D2[id_ref] --> F[id PI Controller]
  E --> G[Feedforward Decoupling]
  F --> G
  G --> H[Inverse Park Transform]
  I[Rotor Angle theta_e] --> H
  H --> J[Space Vector PWM SVPWM]
  J --> K[Three Phase Inverter VSI]
  K --> L[PMSM Machine]
  L --> M[Mechanical Load EV Wheels]
  L --> N[Current Sensors ia ib]
  L --> O[Position Sensor Resolver]
  N --> P[Clarke Transform]
  P --> Q[Park Transform]
  O --> Q
  Q --> E
  Q --> F
  O --> I
  I --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Follow the four nested control loops — speed (outermost), $i_q$ (middle), $i_d$ (inner), and the SVPWM (innermost). The Clarke–Park path on the right is the **feedback** channel; the inverse Park–SVPWM path on the left is the **command** channel.

---

## 4.2 Decoupling Control Architecture (d-q Plane Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph ROTOR_FRAME[Rotor Aligned Reference Frame]
    direction LR
    DAXIS[d Axis aligned with rotor flux lambda_m]
    QAXIS[q Axis perpendicular to rotor flux]
  end
  ROTOR_FRAME --- DES1[i d controls FLUX magnitude only]
  ROTOR_FRAME --- DES2[i q controls TORQUE only]
  IQR[i q current pushes rotor tangentially] --> QAXIS
  IDR[i d current pushes rotor radially] --> DAXIS
```

---

## 4.3 Sensored PMSM Drive — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[Driver Accelerator Pedal] --> B[VCU Torque Request]
  B --> C[Inverter Control Board]
  C --> D[Speed and Position Sensor Resolver]
  D --> E[Resolver to Digital Converter RDC]
  E --> F[theta_e and omega_e]
  F --> G[FOC Algorithm in DSP]
  C --> G
  H[Current Sensors Hall LEM] --> G
  G --> I[PWM Signals]
  I --> J[IGBT MOSFET Inverter]
  J --> K[Three Phase PMSM]
  K --> L[Reduction Gear and Wheels]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key element unique to sensored control:** the **Resolver / Encoder + RDC** block, which provides high-resolution $\theta_e$ (typically 12–16 bit) at all speeds including standstill.

---

## 4.4 Sensorless PMSM Drive — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[VCU Torque Request] --> B[FOC Controller DSP]
  C[DC Bus Voltage Vdc] --> B
  D[Phase Current Sensors ia ib] --> B
  B --> E[Observer Block SMO or EKF or MRAS]
  E --> F[Estimated theta_e hat and omega_e hat]
  F --> B
  B --> G[SVPWM Generator]
  G --> H[Three Phase Inverter]
  H --> I[PMSM Machine]
  I --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key difference vs. sensored:** The **Observer block** mathematically estimates $\hat{\theta}_e$ and $\hat{\omega}_e$ from measured voltages and currents. The physical position sensor is *absent*; reliability is improved but low-speed accuracy degrades.

---

## 4.5 Sensored vs Sensorless — Unified Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph SENS[Sensored Path]
    S1[Physical Resolver] --> S2[theta_e true]
  end
  subgraph SENSE[Sensorless Path]
    SF1[Measured V and I] --> SF2[Observer Algorithm] --> SF3[theta_e estimated]
  end
  SENS --> X[FOC Core]
  SENSE --> X
  X --> Y[Inverter and PMSM]
  Y --> Z[EV Powertrain]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the concept of independent (decoupled) control of flux and torque in a PMSM using the d-q reference frame.**

**Model Answer:**

In the rotor-aligned d-q reference frame rotating at synchronous electrical speed $\omega_e$, the stator current vector $\vec{i}_s$ is decomposed into two orthogonal components: the **d-axis current $i_d$** and the **q-axis current $i_q$**.

The d-axis is aligned with the rotor permanent-magnet flux $\lambda_m$, so $i_d$ directly controls the air-gap flux magnitude:

$$\lambda_{\text{air-gap}} = \lambda_m + L_d i_d$$

The q-axis is perpendicular to $\lambda_m$, and the electromagnetic torque for a surface PMSM is:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \frac{P}{2} \lambda_m i_q$$

Because the d-axis and q-axis are orthogonal, $i_d$ and $i_q$ are **mathematically decoupled**. Two independent PI controllers can therefore regulate flux (via $i_d$) and torque (via $i_q$) without cross-interference. This is the conceptual basis of Field Oriented Control.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** Definition of d-q frame — 1M; Orthogonality justification — 1M; Decoupled torque-flux equations — 1M.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Compare sensored and sensorless control of PMSM drives. Mention any one application limitation of sensorless control.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Sensored | Sensorless |
|---|---|---|
| Rotor position source | Resolver / encoder | Estimated from V and I |
| Low-speed performance | Excellent (including 0 rpm) | Poor — back-EMF ≈ 0 |
| Hardware complexity | Higher (sensor + cabling + connectors) | Lower |
| Reliability | Reduced (sensor is a failure point) | Higher (no sensor to fail) |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

**Application Limitation:** Sensorless control fails at **very low speeds (typically below 5–10% of rated speed)** and at standstill, because the back-EMF $E_b = \omega_e \lambda_m$ becomes too small for accurate estimation. Mitigation is to use high-frequency signal injection in the low-speed region.

> **Mark Distribution:** Comparison — 2M; Limitation — 1M.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question contains two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. Two full alternative questions (Choice A or Choice B) are provided.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of **Field Oriented Control (FOC)** of a Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) drive. Clearly state the role of the Clarke and Park transforms. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (reproduce from Section 4.1):** The student should draw a closed-loop FOC architecture with the following blocks: Current sensors → Clarke → Park → Two PI controllers (for $i_d$ and $i_q$) → Decoupling feedforward → Inverse Park → SVPWM → Inverter → PMSM. Speed PI is the outer loop generating $i_q^*$.

**Role of Clarke Transform:** Converts the three-phase sinusoidal stator currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ into two orthogonal stationary-frame components $i_\alpha$ and $i_\beta$. This reduces the three coupled AC signals to a 2-D vector.

**Role of Park Transform:** Rotates the $\alpha$-$\beta$ frame by the rotor electrical angle $\theta_e$ to obtain $i_d$ and $i_q$ in the rotor-aligned rotating frame. In steady state, these become **DC quantities**, enabling the use of simple PI controllers.

**Operation Summary:**
1. Measure $i_a, i_b, i_c$.
2. Apply Clarke → $i_\alpha, i_\beta$.
3. Apply Park using measured $\theta_e$ → $i_d, i_q$.
4. Compare with references $i_d^*, i_q^*$ → PI controllers produce $v_d^*, v_q^*$.
5. Add decoupling terms $-\omega_e L_q i_q$ and $+\omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_m)$.
6. Apply Inverse Park → $v_\alpha^*, v_\beta^*$.
7. Apply SVPWM → inverter gating signals.

**Why it works:** Because the d-q frame is locked to the rotor flux, $i_d$ controls flux and $i_q$ controls torque independently — exactly like a separately-excited DC motor.

> **Mark Split:** Block diagram with all blocks — 3M; Clarke & Park role — 2M; Operating sequence — 2M.

---

**(b)** Derive the **electromagnetic torque equation** of a PMSM in the d-q rotating reference frame. Show that for a surface-mounted PMSM (SPMSM), the torque is linearly proportional to the q-axis current. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the magnetic co-energy in the d-q frame:

$$W'_{co} = \frac{3}{2}\left[\frac{1}{2} L_d i_d^2 + \frac{1}{2} L_q i_q^2 + \lambda_m i_d\right]$$

Differentiating with respect to mechanical angle, using $\theta_e = \frac{P}{2}\theta_m$:

$$T_e = \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_m} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{\partial W'_{co}}{\partial \theta_e}$$

Only the term $\lambda_m i_d$ depends on $\theta_e$ through the angular variation of the rotor-aligned flux linkage. Differentiating:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2}\left[\lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q) i_d i_q\right]$$

**For SPMSM:** $L_d = L_q = L_s$, hence $(L_d - L_q) = 0$:

$$T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2} \cdot \lambda_m \cdot i_q$$

Defining $K_t = \frac{3}{2} \frac{P}{2} \lambda_m$, we get:

$$\boxed{T_e = K_t \cdot i_q}$$

This is a **linear** relationship between torque and $i_q$ — the hallmark of easy controllability in SPMSMs.

> **Mark Split:** Co-energy expression — 2M; Differentiation — 2M; General torque equation — 1M; SPMSM simplification + linearity conclusion — 2M.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of **sensorless control of PMSM drives**. Draw a block diagram of a sliding-mode-observer-based sensorless FOC system and describe its operation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Sensorless control estimates rotor position $\hat{\theta}_e$ and speed $\hat{\omega}_e$ from the **measured terminal voltages and currents** of the PMSM, eliminating the need for a physical position sensor. The back-EMF of the PMSM, which is $\propto \omega_e$, contains the rotor position information and can be extracted using observers.

**Block Diagram (Sliding Mode Observer, SMO):**

```
Measured V and I → PMSM Model (α-β) → Current Error → Sliding Mode Switching Function
                                                                       ↓
                                                          Estimated Back-EMF eα eβ
                                                                       ↓
                                                          PLL or Arctan → θ̂e ω̂e
                                                                       ↓
                                                            FOC Core (Park / PI / SVPWM)
```

**Operation:**
1. Build a mathematical model of the PMSM in the α-β frame.
2. Compute the **error** between measured and estimated currents.
3. Feed the error through a **signum (switching) function** to obtain the back-EMF estimates $\hat{e}_\alpha, \hat{e}_\beta$.
4. The back-EMF in a PMSM is $e_\alpha = -\omega_e \lambda_m \sin\theta_e$ and $e_\beta = \omega_e \lambda_m \cos\theta_e$.
5. Use a **PLL** or arctan function: $\hat{\theta}_e = -\arctan(\hat{e}_\alpha / \hat{e}_\beta)$.
6. Feed $\hat{\theta}_e$ into the standard FOC algorithm.

**Why SMO is popular:** Robust to parameter variations; computationally simple; works well above ~10% rated speed.

> **Mark Split:** Principle of sensorless control — 2M; SMO block diagram — 3M; Operation sequence — 2M.

---

**(b)** With the help of the **d-q voltage equations of a PMSM**, show why **decoupling voltage compensation** is essential in FOC, and derive the compensation terms used in the d-axis and q-axis voltage references. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**d-q voltage equations of PMSM:**

$$v_d = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$v_q = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt} + \omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_m)$$

**Why decoupling is essential:** In the second term of the d-axis equation ($-\omega_e L_q i_q$) and the third term of the q-axis equation ($\omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \lambda_m$), the **q-axis current influences the d-axis dynamics** and vice-versa. Without compensation, the two current loops are **coupled** and behave as a MIMO system, making PI tuning difficult and slow.

**Decoupling Procedure:**

Let the PI controllers produce nominal voltages:

$$v_d^{\text{PI}} = K_{pd}(i_d^* - i_d) + K_{id}\int(i_d^* - i_d)dt$$

$$v_q^{\text{PI}} = K_{pq}(i_q^* - i_q) + K_{iq}\int(i_q^* - i_q)dt$$

Add the following **feedforward compensation** terms:

$$v_d^* = v_d^{\text{PI}} - \omega_e L_q i_q$$

$$v_q^* = v_q^{\text{PI}} + \omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \lambda_m$$

**Result after substitution:**

$$v_d^* = R_s i_d + L_d \frac{di_d}{dt}$$

$$v_q^* = R_s i_q + L_q \frac{di_q}{dt}$$

**The two equations are now decoupled first-order RL systems.** Each PI can be tuned independently using standard techniques (e.g., modulus optimum or symmetric optimum), and the transient response of $i_d$ no longer affects $i_q$ and vice-versa.

> **Mark Split:** d-q voltage equations — 2M; Identification of cross-coupling — 1M; Compensation derivation — 3M; Decoupled result — 1M.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in PMSM / FOC questions (mark-loser patterns):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the pole-pair factor** $\frac{P}{2}$ in the torque equation. *Always write:* $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P}{2}[\lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q)i_d i_q]$. Missing this loses **2 marks instantly**.
>
> 2. **Confusing the Clarke and Park transforms.** Clarke is **stationary** ($\alpha$-$\beta$); Park is **rotating** (d-q). Mixing them up = **3-mark penalty** in most KTU papers.
>
> 3. **Drawing FOC block diagram without the SVPWM block.** The inverter requires PWM gating signals. Omitting SVPWM is treated as an **incomplete block diagram** → 2 marks lost.
>
> 4. **Stating "$\theta_e = \omega_e t$" without justification** that the d-q frame is *locked* to the rotor flux. Examiners expect the phrase "rotor-aligned reference frame" or "synchronously rotating reference frame."
>
> 5. **Forgetting the feedforward decoupling terms** in the d-q voltage compensation question. Listing only the PI output without the $\omega_e L_q i_q$ and $\omega_e (L_d i_d + \lambda_m)$ terms means **incomplete compensation** → 3-mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Wrong units in torque constant $K_t$.** Correct unit is **Nm/A** (or Nm/A-peak for sinusoidal excitation). Examiners check units strictly.
>
> 7. **Missing the cross product direction** in the bicycle analogy. Many students write "push along the wheel direction" — but the correct perpendicular push is what produces torque. Conceptual errors here cost conceptual marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Final-Minute Rapid Recall):**

- **PMSM** = synchronous motor with **permanent magnets on rotor**, three-phase sinusoidal stator winding. No brushes, no rotor copper loss, **high efficiency (95–98%)**.
- **Synchronous speed** is fixed: $\omega_e = \frac{P}{2}\omega_m$ — there is **no slip** in a PMSM.
- **Clarke transform** (a-b-c → α-β): a 2-D projection that reduces three AC signals to two orthogonal stationary-frame signals.
- **Park transform** (α-β → d-q): a rotation by $\theta_e$ that converts AC into DC quantities in the rotor-aligned frame.
- **Independent (decoupled) control** of flux and torque: $i_d$ controls **flux** (radially), $i_q$ controls **torque** (tangentially). Both are orthogonal in the d-q plane.
- **SPMSM torque equation:** $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \frac{P}{2} \lambda_m i_q$ — **linear** in $i_q$.
- **IPMSM torque equation:** $T_e = \frac{3}{2} \frac{P}{2}[\lambda_m i_q + (L_d - L_q)i_d i_q]$ — includes reluctance torque.
- **FOC** = closed-loop cascade control (speed → $i_q$ → $i_d$ → voltage → SVPWM) that decouples flux and torque using d-q transforms.
- **MTPA strategy** for SPMSM: $i_d^* = 0$ (below base speed) → minimum copper loss.
- **Flux-weakening strategy** (above base speed): $i_d^* < 0$ to oppose $\lambda_m$ and limit back-EMF.
- **d-q voltage equations** contain cross-coupling terms $-\omega_e L_q i_q$ and $+\omega_e L_d i_d + \omega_e \lambda_m$ → must be cancelled by **feedforward decoupling** in FOC.
- **Sensored control** uses resolver/encoder → accurate at all speeds including zero, but **less reliable** (sensor failure).
- **Sensorless control** uses observers (SMO, EKF, MRAS) → robust and cheap, but **fails at low speed** because back-EMF $\propto \omega_e$ vanishes.
- **High-frequency signal injection** is used in sensorless control to enable low-speed and standstill operation by exploiting rotor saliency (mainly for IPMSM).
- **Modern EV traction inverters** (Tesla, Hyundai, BYD) implement FOC at switching frequencies of 8–20 kHz with DSP/MCU cycle times below 10 µs.
- **Back-EMF constant** $E_b = \omega_e \lambda_m$ — V/(rad/s) — is the voltage induced in the stator when the rotor spins.
- **Torque constant** $K_t = \frac{3}{2}\frac{P}{2}\lambda_m$ — Nm/A — for SPMSM.
- **Decoupled FOC** allows two independent first-order PI current loops, each with its own time constant $\tau_d = L_d/R_s$ and $\tau_q = L_q/R_s$.
- **Block diagram components** that must appear in any FOC answer: Current sensors, Clarke, Park, PI controllers, Feedforward Decoupling, Inverse Park, SVPWM, Inverter, PMSM, Position/Speed Sensor (or Observer).
- **Sensorless observer choice** depends on speed range: SMO for mid-high speed; HF injection for low-speed; EKF for full range with high accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sizing the Drive System** is the engineering methodology used to determine the optimal power, torque, and speed ratings of the propulsion unit (electric machine, ICE, or hybrid combination) such that the vehicle meets its prescribed performance targets (top speed, acceleration, gradeability, and range) under all realistic operating conditions, while minimizing mass, volume, cost, and energy consumption.

In the context of **vehicle electrification**, the process of *Matching the Electric Machine and the ICE* refers to the analytical procedure of selecting an electric machine (EM) with rated continuous and peak power such that, when replacing or augmenting an Internal Combustion Engine of a given rated power, the equivalent driving performance (acceleration, gradeability, cruising) is preserved — and ideally improved — by exploiting the inherent **overload capability** and **flat torque curve** of the electric machine.

The two key technical quantities at the heart of this matching exercise are:

- **Continuous (Rated) Power of the Electric Machine** ($P_{EM,c}$): the power that the EM can deliver indefinitely without exceeding its thermal limits.
- **Peak Power of the Electric Machine** ($P_{EM,peak}$): the maximum power the EM can deliver for a short duration (typically 30 s to 2 min) by exploiting its short-term thermal mass and magnetic saturation margins.

The **Matching Factor** ($k_m$) is defined as:

$$k_m = \frac{P_{EM,peak}}{P_{EM,c}} \approx 2 \text{ to } 3$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ICE, in contrast, can sustain its peak rated power almost continuously because it is sized near its thermal limit. The electric machine, however, is typically oversized only in the magnetic and mechanical sense — its *thermal* envelope is smaller. This is the central reason an electric machine rated at, say, 75 kW continuous can propel a vehicle that an ICE of 150 kW would otherwise be required to drive.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sprinter and the Marathon Runner"

Imagine a 100-metre **sprinter** and a **marathon runner**:

- The **ICE is the marathon runner**: it can run at near-peak effort for a long time, but its peak sprint speed is limited. It also needs a "warm-up" (ramp-up) before delivering full power, and it has a narrow range of comfortable "cadence" (RPM) where it produces peak power.
- The **Electric Machine is the sprinter-cum-marathon-runner hybrid**: it can sprint at 2–3 times its sustainable pace for a few seconds (overtaking, launch), and then settle into a long, steady cruise — all while doing it silently and from zero RPM.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter for sizing?** Because the electric machine can be *downsized* relative to the ICE. You do not need to specify a 150 kW EM to replace a 150 kW ICE — a 75 kW continuous / 200 kW peak EM may do the job, provided the peak is available during the brief, high-demand phases of the drive cycle (acceleration, hill climb), and the continuous rating covers the cruising phase.

## 1.3 The Engineering Problem in Plain English

When a manufacturer converts an existing ICE car into an EV (or designs a hybrid), the engineer is asked:

> *"What size electric machine do I need so that my car still accelerates from 0 to 100 km/h in 8 seconds, climbs a 30 % gradient, cruises at 120 km/h, and yet has a long enough range?"*

The answer requires balancing **three competing criteria**:

1. **Performance** — peak torque and power for acceleration.
2. **Endurance** — continuous power for cruising without overheating.
3. **Efficiency** — operating points where the EM is most efficient should align with the most-used portion of the drive cycle.

Standard metrics used in this matching (and frequently examined in KTU papers):

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Mid-size EV) |
|---|---|---|
| Vehicle mass (kerb) | $m_v$ | 1500 kg |
| Drag coefficient | $C_d$ | 0.30 |
| Frontal area | $A_f$ | 2.2 m² |
| Rolling resistance coefficient | $C_{rr}$ | 0.012 |
| Peak acceleration (0–100 km/h) | $a_{peak}$ | 0–100 in 8 s |
| Top speed | $v_{max}$ | 150 km/h |
| Maximum grade | $\theta_{max}$ | 30 % |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vehicle Dynamics Foundation (Why We Size the Way We Do)

The tractive force demanded at the wheels of a vehicle moving at speed $v$ on a road of slope angle $\theta$ is the sum of four resistance forces:

$$F_{trac} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{climb} + F_{acc}$$

Each component is derived as follows:

- **Rolling resistance**: $F_{roll} = m_v \, g \, C_{rr} \, \cos\theta$
- **Aerodynamic drag**: $F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho_{air} \, C_d \, A_f \, v^2$
- **Climbing resistance**: $F_{climb} = m_v \, g \, \sin\theta$
- **Linear acceleration resistance**: $F_{acc} = m_v \, (1 + \delta) \, a$

where:
- $\rho_{air} \approx 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ (air density at sea level, **ISA**)
- $\delta$ = rotational inertia factor (≈ 1.05 for cars with single gearbox, up to 1.3 for heavy commercial vehicles)
- $g \approx 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

The **wheel power** required to overcome these resistances is:

$$P_{wheel} = F_{trac} \cdot v$$

The **machine (shaft) power** must overcome drivetrain losses:

$$P_{EM,shaft} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{drivetrain}} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{gb} \cdot \eta_{diff} \cdot \eta_{final}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Typical overall drivetrain efficiency for a single-speed EV: $\eta_{dt} \approx 0.90$ to $0.95$ (because electric machines are efficient across a wide range, and single-speed reducers have only gear-mesh losses).

## 2.2 The Concept of "Continuous vs Peak" — Why ICE and EM Behave Differently

### 2.2.1 The ICE Torque–Speed Characteristic

An ICE produces peak torque over a **narrow band** (typically 2000–4000 RPM for a petrol engine, 1500–3000 RPM for a diesel) and has a **redline** beyond which it cannot go. Its power rises approximately linearly with RPM in the useful range. It is also **idle-limited** — it cannot deliver torque below a certain RPM (idle speed) without stalling, hence the need for a clutch and torque converter.

### 2.2.2 The Electric Machine Torque–Speed Characteristic

An electric machine has **two distinct regions**:

- **Constant-Torque Region** (low to base speed $\omega_b$): the EM delivers maximum permissible torque limited by current / magnetic saturation.
- **Constant-Power Region** (base speed to maximum speed $\omega_{max}$): torque falls off as $1/\omega$ (field-weakening region), so power remains roughly constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This means the EM is essentially a "constant-power source from standstill to top speed," whereas the ICE is a "constant-power source only in a narrow mid-RPM band." This is the **first** reason we can downsize the EM relative to the ICE.

The second reason is the **thermal overload capability**: the EM's windings, magnets, and bearings have short-term thermal mass that allows 2–3× rated current (and hence 2–3× rated torque) for tens of seconds, before overheating becomes a concern. An ICE cannot do this — pushing it beyond rated power for tens of seconds risks detonation, melting pistons, or catastrophic failure.

## 2.3 The Sizing Procedure — Step by Step

The standard procedure taught in KTU-aligned EV courses is:

**Step 1 — Determine Peak Power Required (Performance-Driven)**
Use the highest instantaneous demand, typically:
- 0–100 km/h acceleration in target time, OR
- Sustained top speed, OR
- Maximum gradeability at a given speed (whichever is the largest demand).

**Step 2 — Determine Continuous Power Required (Endurance-Driven)**
Use the long-term demand, typically:
- Cruising at top legal speed on a flat road, OR
- The average power required over a representative drive cycle (e.g., NEDC, WLTP, or Indian MIDC).

**Step 3 — Size the EM**
- Set $P_{EM,peak}$ = Peak Power from Step 1.
- Set $P_{EM,continuous}$ = Continuous Power from Step 2.
- Verify the **peak-to-continuous ratio** $k_m = P_{EM,peak}/P_{EM,c}$ lies in the realistic range **1.5 to 3.0** for the chosen machine type (lower for PM machines, higher for induction machines).
- If $k_m$ is too high → re-rate the EM (oversize it) or accept longer acceleration.
- If $k_m$ is too low → the EM is oversized, increasing cost and weight — opportunity to downsize.

**Step 4 — Match to ICE (if a hybrid or direct-replacement case)**
The classical matching rule is:

$$P_{ICE,rated} \approx P_{EM,continuous}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most-tested idea in this topic:** An EV can replace an ICE of rated power $P$ with an electric machine whose **continuous** rating is also approximately $P$, **provided** the EM can deliver a peak power of $2P$ to $3P$ for short bursts. The peak covers the transients (acceleration, hill climb); the continuous covers the cruise.

## 2.4 The Reduction (Downsizing) Factor $k_d$

Engineers define a **reduction factor**:

$$k_d = \frac{P_{ICE,rated}}{P_{EM,continuous}}$$

- For a pure EV designed from scratch: $k_d$ is **not applicable** — the EM is sized directly from performance.
- For a *conversion* of an existing ICE platform to EV: typically $k_d \in [1.0, 1.5]$ if the EM has a high overload capability and a single-speed transmission.

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU answer phrasing:** "Because the electric machine can deliver 2–3 times its continuous rating for short durations, the size (and hence mass) of the propulsion unit can be reduced by a factor of approximately 1.5–2 compared to a comparable ICE-powered vehicle, while still maintaining equivalent acceleration and gradeability."

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single most important reference** for solving any KTU question on this topic. Memorize the variable definitions and units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tractive force (total) | $F_{trac} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{climb} + F_{acc}$ | N | Sum of four resistance components |
| 2 | Rolling resistance | $F_{roll} = m_v g C_{rr} \cos\theta$ | N | $C_{rr} \approx 0.010$–$0.015$ for car tyres on asphalt |
| 3 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$ | N | $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ at sea level |
| 4 | Climbing resistance | $F_{climb} = m_v g \sin\theta$ | N | $\theta = \arctan(\text{grade}/100)$ |
| 5 | Acceleration resistance | $F_{acc} = m_v (1+\delta) a$ | N | $\delta \approx 1.05$–$1.10$ for passenger EV |
| 6 | Wheel power | $P_{wheel} = F_{trac} \cdot v$ | W | $v$ in m/s |
| 7 | Shaft power (input to drivetrain) | $P_{shaft} = P_{wheel} / \eta_{dt}$ | W | $\eta_{dt} \approx 0.90$–$0.95$ for single-speed EV |
| 8 | Required peak power (from 0–100 km/h time $t_a$) | $P_{peak} \approx \tfrac{1}{2} m_v v_f^2 / t_a$ | W | Approximate; neglects drag & rolling during launch |
| 9 | Required power for top speed | $P_{v_{max}} = F_{trac}(v_{max}) \cdot v_{max} / \eta_{dt}$ | W | Use all four forces at $v_{max}$ on flat road |
| 10 | Required power for max grade at speed $v_g$ | $P_{grade} = (m_v g \sin\theta_{max} + m_v g C_{rr} + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v_g^2) \cdot v_g / \eta_{dt}$ | W | Grade is usually the worst case |
| 11 | Matching factor | $k_m = P_{EM,peak} / P_{EM,c}$ | — | Typically 1.5–3.0 |
| 12 | Reduction factor | $k_d = P_{ICE,rated} / P_{EM,c}$ | — | For platform conversion: 1.0–1.5 |
| 13 | EM continuous rating (matching rule) | $P_{EM,c} \approx P_{ICE,rated}$ | W | The core matching relationship |
| 14 | Specific power (power density) | $\sigma = P_{EM,peak} / m_{EM}$ | W/kg | EM: 2–6 kW/kg; ICE: 0.5–1 kW/kg |
| 15 | Energy required for one drive cycle | $E_{cyc} = \int_0^T P_{wheel}(t) \, dt$ | J | Used to size battery pack |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM design teams** (e.g., Tata Motors, Mahindra Electric, BYD, Tesla) use these sizing equations as the first-pass design choice for any new EV platform.
- **Drive-cycle engineers** use $E_{cyc}$ to size the battery and confirm the EM thermal envelope.
- **Control engineers** use the constant-torque / constant-power envelope to design the torque-request maps of the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU).
- **Hybridization engineers** (Toyota THS, Honda i-MMD) use the matching rule to determine when to use the ICE vs the EM.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Peak Power for a 0–100 km/h Acceleration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 2. The standard approach is to equate the kinetic energy gained in time $t_a$ to the work done by the propulsion unit.

Let:
- $m_v$ = vehicle mass (kg)
- $\delta$ = rotational inertia factor
- $v_f$ = final speed (m/s) = (100 km/h) × (1000/3600) = **27.78 m/s**
- $t_a$ = target acceleration time (s)
- Assume aerodynamic drag and rolling resistance are negligible during the launch (valid for short bursts at low speed, conservative approximation that gives a *slightly higher* required power)

The work-energy theorem:

$$W = \Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m_v (1 + \delta) v_f^2 - 0$$

If we approximate that the propulsion unit delivers *average* power $\bar{P}_{peak}$ over the launch:

$$\bar{P}_{peak} \cdot t_a = \tfrac{1}{2} m_v (1 + \delta) v_f^2$$

Solving for $\bar{P}_{peak}$:

$$\bar{P}_{peak} = \frac{m_v (1 + \delta) v_f^2}{2 \, t_a}$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

**Given:**
- $m_v = 1500 \; \text{kg}$
- $\delta = 0.10$
- $t_a = 8 \; \text{s}$
- $v_f = 27.78 \; \text{m/s}$

**Step 1** — compute the effective mass:

$$m_{eff} = m_v (1 + \delta) = 1500 \times 1.10 = 1650 \; \text{kg}$$

**Step 2** — compute the kinetic energy gained:

$$\Delta KE = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1650 \times (27.78)^2$$

$$(27.78)^2 = 771.6 \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$\Delta KE = 0.5 \times 1650 \times 771.6 = 636{,}570 \; \text{J} \approx 636.6 \; \text{kJ}$$

**Step 3** — compute the average peak power:

$$\bar{P}_{peak} = \frac{636{,}570}{8} \approx 79{,}571 \; \text{W} \approx 79.6 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 4** — add a 15 % margin to account for drag and rolling losses during the launch (rule of thumb used in industry):

$$P_{peak,design} = 79.6 \times 1.15 \approx 91.5 \; \text{kW}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** A vehicle of 1500 kg that needs to do 0–100 km/h in 8 s requires a peak propulsion power of approximately **90 kW**. With a 10 % margin for slope and cold-soak derating, the designer specifies an EM with $P_{EM,peak} \approx 100 \; \text{kW}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Continuous Power for Cruising at Top Speed

This derivation requires all four forces and is the **most-tested** computation in KTU Module 2.

**Given (typical mid-size EV):**
- $m_v = 1500 \; \text{kg}$
- $C_{rr} = 0.012$
- $C_d = 0.30$
- $A_f = 2.2 \; \text{m}^2$
- $v_{max} = 150 \; \text{km/h} = 41.67 \; \text{m/s}$
- $\theta = 0$ (flat road)
- $\rho = 1.225 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Rolling resistance:**

$$F_{roll} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times \cos(0)$$

$$F_{roll} = 1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 176.58 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 2 — Aerodynamic drag:**

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.2 \times (41.67)^2$$

$$(41.67)^2 = 1736.4 \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.3 \times 2.2 \times 1736.4$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.66 \times 1736.4$$

$$F_{aero} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1146.0$$

$$F_{aero} = 701.9 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Climbing resistance (flat road, so zero):**

$$F_{climb} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration resistance (steady cruise, so zero):**

$$F_{acc} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Total tractive force:**

$$F_{trac} = 176.58 + 701.9 + 0 + 0 = 878.5 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 6 — Wheel power:**

$$P_{wheel} = F_{trac} \times v = 878.5 \times 41.67 = 36{,}605 \; \text{W} \approx 36.6 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 7 — Shaft power (with $\eta_{dt} = 0.92$):**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{36.6}{0.92} \approx 39.8 \; \text{kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Cruising at 150 km/h on a flat road requires only **~40 kW** of shaft power — much less than the 90 kW needed for 0–100 km/h in 8 s. This is why the EM can be specified with a continuous rating near 40–50 kW, even though peak is 90–100 kW. The **matching factor** is therefore $k_m \approx 100/40 = 2.5$ — comfortably inside the 1.5–3.0 envelope.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Matching to the ICE (Conversion Scenario)

> [!NOTE]
> **This derivation is the heart of the named topic.** We are given an existing ICE car and asked to specify the EM for a "drop-in" conversion.

**Given (typical mid-size sedan, ICE):**
- $P_{ICE,rated} = 110 \; \text{kW}$ (≈ 150 hp)
- Vehicle kerb mass: 1400 kg
- 0–100 km/h in 9.5 s
- Top speed 200 km/h

**Step 1 — Confirm that the ICE's rated power covers both peak (acceleration) and continuous (cruise):**

- Peak demand: 0–100 km/h in 9.5 s → $\bar{P}_{peak} \approx \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1400 \times 1.08 \times 771.6 / 9.5 \approx 61.4 \; \text{kW}$ (with margin → ~70 kW)
- Continuous demand: cruise at 200 km/h → much higher than for 150 km/h; the drag is roughly $(200/150)^2 = 1.78$ times the 150 km/h drag → $P_{continuous} \approx 40 \times 1.78 \approx 71 \; \text{kW}$

So for the ICE: peak ≈ continuous (both ~70–110 kW) — that is the design.

**Step 2 — Specify the EM for the EV conversion:**

Apply the matching rule:

$$P_{EM,c} \approx P_{ICE,rated} = 110 \; \text{kW}$$

But — is 110 kW truly *needed* continuously? No. The EM can be downsized because it has overload capability.

**Step 3 — Apply the reduction factor:**

Choose a realistic $k_d = 1.3$ (i.e., EM continuous = ICE/1.3):

$$P_{EM,c} = \frac{110}{1.3} \approx 85 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Choose a matching factor $k_m = 2.0$:**

$$P_{EM,peak} = k_m \cdot P_{EM,c} = 2.0 \times 85 = 170 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Verify performance:**

- Peak (acceleration): 170 kW gives $t_{0-100} = (0.5 \times 1400 \times 1.08 \times 771.6)/170{,}000 \approx 3.4$ s *plus* drag → realistically ~5 s — **faster than the ICE car (9.5 s)**. ✅
- Continuous (cruise at 200 km/h): 85 kW continuous — sufficient. ✅

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** The EV conversion uses a 85 kW continuous / 170 kW peak EM, which is **lighter, smaller, and more efficient** than the 110 kW ICE it replaces, while delivering **better** acceleration.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Constant-Torque / Constant-Power Envelope of the EM

The torque of an EM in the field-weakening (constant-power) region is:

$$T_{EM}(\omega) = \frac{P_{EM,peak}}{\omega} \quad \text{for} \quad \omega_b \le \omega \le \omega_{max}$$

At base speed $\omega_b$, the EM transitions from constant-torque to constant-power mode:

$$T_{max} = \frac{P_{EM,peak}}{\omega_b}$$

The power delivered at any speed is:

$$P_{EM}(\omega) = \begin{cases} T_{max} \cdot \omega & 0 \le \omega \le \omega_b \quad \text{(constant-torque region)} \\ P_{EM,peak} = T_{max} \cdot \omega_b & \omega_b \le \omega \le \omega_{max} \quad \text{(constant-power region)} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a key difference from the ICE, whose "useful" power band is narrow and shifted away from zero speed. The EM's envelope is essentially rectangular (constant torque from 0 to $\omega_b$, constant power from $\omega_b$ to $\omega_{max}$), which is why it can replace a multi-gear transmission and a torque converter in a single unit.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How the EM Rating Reacts to Design Choices

> [!TIP]
> The matrix below is a quick-reference for design trade-offs, useful for 14-mark KTU design questions.

| Design Lever | Effect on $P_{EM,peak}$ | Effect on $P_{EM,c}$ | Effect on $k_m$ | Effect on $m_{EM}$ | Effect on Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reduce 0–100 km/h time | ↑↑ | ↑ (mildly) | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ |
| Increase top speed | ↑ (mildly) | ↑↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ |
| Increase max grade | ↑↑ (if $v_g$ high) | ↑ (mildly) | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ |
| Reduce $C_d$ (better aero) | ↓ | ↓↓ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ |
| Lighten vehicle (mass reduction) | ↓↓ | ↓↓ | — | — | ↓ |
| Higher $k_m$ target (more overload) | — | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ |
| Add multi-speed gearbox | ↓ (peak) | ↓ (continuous) | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ (gearbox cost) |
| Use higher-$\sigma$ machine (e.g., PMSM vs IM) | — | — | — | ↓↓ | ↑ |
