# ⚡ SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any four key parameters used for selecting a power semiconductor switch for an EV traction inverter. Justify each in one line.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Blocking Voltage (V_CE / V_DS):** Must be ≥ 1.5 × V_DC bus to withstand DC-link transients and derating for reliability. *(1 mark)*
2. **Continuous Current Rating (I_C / I_D):** Must exceed the RMS phase current with a 1.2 to 1.5× derating factor for thermal margin. *(1 mark)*
3. **Switching Frequency (f_sw):** Determines magnetic component size and dynamic response; higher f_sw reduces inductor volume but increases switching loss. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Conduction Loss (V_CE(sat) or R_DS(on)):** Lower value → higher inverter efficiency → longer EV driving range. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Compare IGBT and SiC MOSFET for an 800 V EV traction inverter in terms of switching frequency, efficiency, cost, and thermal limit.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | IGBT | SiC MOSFET |
|---|---|---|
| Switching frequency | 5 – 20 kHz (limited by tail current) | 50 – 100 kHz (no tail current) |
| Efficiency | 94 – 96% | 96 – 98% |
| Cost | ₹ Lower (mature Si technology) | ₹₹ Higher (expensive SiC wafer) |
| T_j(max) | 150°C | 175°C – 200°C |

**Conclusion:** *(1 mark)* For 800 V architecture with efficiency targets > 96%, **SiC MOSFET is preferred despite higher cost**, as the efficiency gain translates to ~5 – 8% extra range, offsetting the cost over the vehicle's lifetime.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students must attempt ONE of the two questions below. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]****

**a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, explain the **various power electronic subsystems** in a Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV). Mention the typical **switch technology** preferred for each subsystem and justify your choice.

**Model Solution:**

A BEV powertrain contains the following power electronic subsystems:

**1. Traction Inverter (DC → 3-φ AC)** — *Block diagram: [Battery] → [DC Link Capacitor] → [3-φ VSI with 6 IGBTs] → [PMSM Motor]* (1 mark)
- **Preferred switch: 750 V / 1200 V IGBT** for 400 V bus, **1200 V SiC MOSFET** for 800 V bus. (0.5 mark)
- **Justification:** High voltage and current (50 – 250 kW) → IGBT's tail current is acceptable since f_sw = 10 kHz; SiC is chosen for 800 V to reduce switching loss by 70%. (1 mark)

**2. On-Board Charger (AC → DC)** — *Block diagram: [AC Mains] → [PFC Boost] → [DC-DC LLC] → [Battery]* (1 mark)
- **Preferred switch: IGBT (3.3 kW) or SiC MOSFET (11/22 kW).** (0.5 mark)
- **Justification:** OBC operates at near-rated power continuously for hours → conduction loss dominates → IGBT's low V_CE(sat) preferred. (0.5 mark)

**3. HV → LV DC-DC Converter (400 V → 12 V)** — *Block diagram: [HV Battery] → [Full-Bridge] → [Transformer] → [Rectifier] → [12 V Bus]* (1 mark)
- **Preferred switch: Si MOSFET (low voltage side) or GaN HEMT (for > 100 kHz).** (0.5 mark)
- **Justification:** Low voltage (12 V) but high current (~250 A) → MOSFET's low R_DS(on) at 40 V class is ideal. (0.5 mark)

**4. Battery Management System (Cell Balancing)** — *Block diagram: [Cells] → [Balancing MOSFETs] → [Microcontroller]* (0.5 mark)
- **Preferred switch: 40 V / 60 V Si MOSFET.** (0.25 mark)
- **Justification:** Low current (< 5 A), small size, low cost → standard MOSFETs. (0.25 mark)

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

**b) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase VSI for an EV traction motor has the following specifications:
- P_out = 75 kW, V_DC = 400 V, V_LL = 230 V, cos φ = 0.9, f_sw = 8 kHz
- IGBT datasheet: V_CE(sat) = 2.0 V, r_CE = 0.012 Ω, t_on = 0.4 µs, t_off = 0.5 µs, θ_JC = 0.4 °C/W
- T_j(max) = 150°C, T_a(max) = 70°C

**Calculate:**
(i) RMS and peak current per switch.
(ii) Conduction loss per switch.
(iii) Switching loss per switch.
(iv) Total inverter loss and efficiency.
(v) Whether air cooling is sufficient (assume θ_SA = 0.2 °C/W, θ_CS = 0.1 °C/W).

**Model Solution:**

**(i) RMS and Peak Current** (1.5 marks)

$$I_{rms} = \dfrac{75{,}000}{\sqrt{3}\cdot 230\cdot 0.9} = \dfrac{75{,}000}{358.59} = 209.16\ \text{A}$$

$$I_{peak} = \sqrt{2}\cdot 209.16 = 295.78\ \text{A}$$

**(ii) Conduction Loss per Switch** (1.5 marks)

$$P_{cond} = 2.0\cdot \dfrac{295.78}{\pi} + 0.012\cdot \dfrac{(295.78)^2}{4}$$

$$P_{cond} = 188.30 + 262.50 = 450.80\ \text{W}$$

**(iii) Switching Loss per Switch** (1.5 marks)

$$P_{sw} = 0.5\cdot 400\cdot 209.16\cdot (0.4+0.5)\cdot 10^{-6}\cdot 8{,}000$$

$$P_{sw} = 301.19\ \text{W}$$

**(iv) Total Inverter Loss and Efficiency** (1.5 marks)

$$P_{loss,switch} = 450.80 + 301.19 = 751.99\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{inv,total} = 6\cdot 751.99 = 4{,}511.94\ \text{W} \approx 4.51\ \text{kW}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{75{,}000}{75{,}000+4{,}511.94} = \dfrac{75{,}000}{79{,}511.94} = 0.9433 = 94.33\%$$

**(v) Thermal Adequacy Check** (1 mark)

$$\theta_{JA,req} = \dfrac{150-70}{751.99} = 0.1064\ °\text{C/W}$$

$$\theta_{JA,available} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA} = 0.4 + 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.7\ °\text{C/W}$$

Since **0.7 > 0.1064**, the available thermal resistance is **more than required**, so **air cooling is SUFFICIENT**. ✓

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle of Safe Operating Area (SOA)** of a power semiconductor switch. How does it influence the switch selection for an EV inverter during:
(i) Hard-switching turn-OFF at peak current?
(ii) Short-circuit condition for 10 µs?
(iii) Linear region operation during PWM dead-time?

**Model Solution:**

**SOA Definition:** The Safe Operating Area is the V-I envelope within which the power switch can operate without suffering thermal runaway or secondary breakdown. It is bounded by **(1) maximum current, (2) maximum voltage, (3) thermal limit (R_th × P_loss), and (4) secondary breakdown (for BJT/IGBT).** (2 marks)

**(i) Hard-Switching turn-OFF at peak current:** *(1.5 marks)*
- During turn-OFF, the switch passes through the **active region** with V_CE rising from V_CE(sat) to V_DC while I_C falls from I_peak to 0.
- The trajectory **must stay within the rectangular SOA** (bounded by V_CE(max) and I_C(max)).
- If V_DC × I_peak exceeds the **Repetitive Avalanche Energy (E_AR)**, the device fails. Hence **RBSOA (Reverse Bias SOA)** rating is critical for IGBTs in EV inverters.

**(ii) Short-circuit condition (10 µs):** *(1.5 marks)*
- During a motor winding short, V_DC is fully applied across the switch with I_C rising rapidly.
- The IGBT must withstand **SCSOA (Short-Circuit SOA)** for ≥ 10 µs — the protection trip time.
- A 1200 V / 300 A IGBT can typically sustain **6× rated current for 10 µs**. For 800 V architecture, **SiC MOSFET's superior SCSOA** is preferred.

**(iii) Linear region during PWM dead-time:** *(2 marks)*
- During dead-time, the **freewheeling diode** carries current, not the IGBT.
- However, during **PWM transitions**, the IGBT momentarily operates in the linear region (active region).
- The **SOA must allow linear operation** for the duration of the turn-ON/turn-OFF (typically < 1 µs).
- The **single-pulse thermal impedance Z_th(t)** is used instead of steady-state θ_JC for short pulses.

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

**b) [7 Marks]** A 22 kW on-board charger (OBC) for an EV uses a **totem-pole PFC + LLC resonant converter** topology. The input is **230 V AC, 50 Hz** and output is **400 V DC**. The PFC operates at **f_sw = 65 kHz** using **GaN HEMT** (R_DS(on) = 50 mΩ, t_on + t_off = 50 ns).
- Compute: (i) the **input RMS current** at full load, assuming unity power factor.
- (ii) the **conduction loss per GaN switch** (assume I_avg = I_rms/√2).
- (iii) the **switching loss per GaN switch**.
- (iv) Justify why **GaN is preferred over Si MOSFET** in this application.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Input RMS Current** (1.5 marks)

$$I_{in,rms} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{V_{in}\cdot \cos\phi} = \dfrac{22{,}000}{230\cdot 1.0} = 95.65\ \text{A}$$

**(ii) Conduction Loss per GaN Switch** (2 marks)

For totem-pole PFC, each switch carries half the input current:

$$I_{switch,rms} = \dfrac{95.65}{\sqrt{2}} = 67.64\ \text{A}$$

$$P_{cond,GaN} = R_{DS(on)}\cdot I_{switch,rms}^2 = 0.050\cdot (67.64)^2 = 228.77\ \text{W}$$

**(iii) Switching Loss per GaN Switch** (2 marks)

$$P_{sw,GaN} = 0.5\cdot V_{DC}\cdot I_{sw}\cdot (t_{on}+t_{off})\cdot f_{sw}$$

Using V_DC = 400 V (peak rectified), I_sw = 67.64 A:

$$P_{sw,GaN} = 0.5\cdot 400\cdot 67.64\cdot 50\cdot 10^{-9}\cdot 65{,}000$$

$$P_{sw,GaN} = 43.97\ \text{W}$$

**(iv) Justification — GaN vs Si MOSFET** (1.5 marks)

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Lower switching loss** | GaN's t_on+t_off = 50 ns vs Si MOSFET's 200 ns → 75% less loss |
| **Zero reverse recovery** | GaN has no body diode Q_rr → no dead-time loss in totem-pole |
| **Higher frequency** | 65 kHz vs 20 kHz → smaller magnetic components |
| **Smaller magnetics** | Transformer and inductor sizes reduced 3× |
| **Higher efficiency** | OBC efficiency > 96% achievable (vs 93% for Si MOSFET) |

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU 2024 exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the derating factor (k_v = 1.5 to 2.0)** — Students often size V_rated = V_DC. The examiner deducts **1 mark** if derating is missing.
> 
> 2. **Confusing IGBT conduction loss formula with MOSFET** — IGBT uses V_CE(sat) + r_CE × I² ; MOSFET uses only R_DS(on) × I². Mixing them = **-1 mark**.
> 
> 3. **Using 50 Hz line frequency instead of switching frequency in P_sw formula** — Always f_sw, not f_line. This error = **-1.5 marks**.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply by 6 for the 3-phase inverter total loss** — A common oversight = **-1 mark**.
> 
> 5. **Ignoring the negative θ_SA result** — If the calculated θ_SA is negative, the student must conclude that **air cooling is insufficient and recommend liquid cooling or parallel devices**. Saying "heat sink is fine" = **-1.5 marks**.
> 
> 6. **Mixing up the SOA types** — RBSOA, SCSOA, FBSOA, and single-pulse SOA are **distinct**. Examiner will deduct **0.5 mark** for each incorrect attribution.
> 
> 7. **Not specifying units in the final answer** — Always state the units (A, V, W, °C/W) explicitly. Missing units = **-0.5 mark** per numerical answer.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read 2 hours before the exam.**

- ☐ **Sizing** = selecting voltage, current, frequency, and thermal ratings of the switch.
- ☐ **Voltage derating factor** k_v = 1.5 to 2.0 ; **Current derating factor** k_i = 1.2 to 1.5.
- ☐ **IGBT** is the workhorse for **400 V bus, 50 – 250 kW traction inverters**.
- ☐ **SiC MOSFET** is mandatory for **800 V architecture** and **fast charging**.
- ☐ **GaN HEMT** is used in **OBC, LV DC-DC, and high-frequency (100 kHz – 1 MHz) converters**.
- ☐ **Conduction loss — IGBT:** $V_{CE(sat)} \cdot I_{avg} + r_{CE} \cdot I_{rms}^2$.
- ☐ **Conduction loss — MOSFET:** $R_{DS(on)} \cdot I_{rms}^2$.
- ☐ **Switching loss:** $0.5 \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_{sw} \cdot (t_{on}+t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$.
- ☐ **Inverter total loss** = 6 × (P_cond + P_sw) for a 3-phase, 2-level VSI.
- ☐ **Thermal resistance equation:** $\theta_{JA} = (T_{j,max} - T_{a,max}) / P_{loss}$.
- ☐ **Negative θ_SA ⇒ need liquid cooling or parallel devices.**
- ☐ **SOA types:** FBSOA, RBSOA, SCSOA, and single-pulse SOA.
- ☐ **EV efficiency benchmark:** 94% (IGBT), 96 – 98% (SiC), 98%+ (GaN).
- ☐ **Junction temperature limits:** 150°C (Si), 175 – 200°C (SiC/GaN).
- ☐ **Modern EVs are migrating from 400 V to 800 V architecture** — this is the **single most important industry trend** for sizing.
- ☐ **Paralleling switches** reduces conduction loss by **n²** factor (2 parallel = 4× less loss).
- ☐ **Remember the 5-step KTU design methodology:** Specs → V_rating → I_rating → f_sw → Losses → Thermal.


<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ripple Capacitor Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ripple Capacitor Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Ripple Capacitor** (also termed a *smoothing*, *filter*, or *DC-link capacitor*) in an Electric Vehicle power-electronic subsystem is a deliberately sized energy-storage element placed across the output of a rectifier or at the intermediate DC bus of a switching converter. Its primary function is to absorb the alternating (pulsating) component of current delivered by the source, thereby reducing the peak-to-peak voltage ripple ($\Delta V_{pp}$) imposed on downstream components such as the traction inverter, battery management system (BMS), or auxiliary loads.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET522 – Electric Vehicles, Module 2)**, ripple capacitor design is studied as a critical sub-topic under *Power Electronic Converters for EV Drivetrains*, since the DC-link capacitor directly governs the magnitude of the low-frequency torque ripple, common-mode voltage stress, and high-frequency conducted EMI produced by the inverter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEEET522 / M2):** A ripple capacitor is *not* a generic filtering capacitor. It must simultaneously satisfy four engineering constraints: (1) voltage ripple limit, (2) RMS current rating, (3) thermal dissipation via ESR, and (4) volumetric / gravimetric density targets critical for EV packaging.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tower" Model

Imagine a city water supply fed by an intermittent pump. Without a **water tower (reservoir)**, every pulse of the pump would send a pressure wave through the pipes — taps would either gush or trickle. The water tower is a *gravity-fed energy buffer*: during the pump's "on" stroke, water fills the tower; during the "off" stroke, gravity discharges the tower to keep tap pressure nearly constant.

A **ripple capacitor behaves identically in the electrical domain**:
- The **pump** = a rectifier or switching converter (delivers pulsed current).
- The **water tower** = the capacitor (stores electrostatic energy in its electric field).
- The **tap pressure** = the DC-link voltage seen by the inverter.
- The **tower's water level swing** = the voltage ripple $\Delta V_{pp}$.
- The **pipe diameter** = ESR (Equivalent Series Resistance) — limits how fast charge can flow in/out.

The bigger the tower (larger $C$), the smaller the level swing (lower ripple), but the bigger the structure (heavier EV, less packaging space). Hence *design* is a constrained optimisation problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant:** The energy stored in a capacitor is $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ joules, where $C$ is in farads and $V$ in volts. This quadratic voltage dependence is why a small voltage increase demands a disproportionate capacitance increase for the same ripple energy.

## 1.3 Role in the EV Power Train

| EV Subsystem | Capacitor Placement | Dominant Ripple Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| On-board charger (OBC) | Output of PFC rectifier | 2× line frequency (100/120 Hz) |
| High-voltage DC-DC converter | Output to 12 V / 48 V aux bus | Switching freq (50–500 kHz) |
| Traction inverter DC-link | Between HV battery and inverter | Inverter switching freq (5–20 kHz) |
| Regenerative braking path | DC-link, parallel to battery | Variable, 0–1 kHz envelope |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a Ripple Capacitor is Required — Failure Modes Without It

1. **Torque ripple in traction motor** — Pulsating DC-link voltage produces 6th and 12th harmonic torque pulsations in PMSM / induction motors, perceived as "judder" in EV drive.
2. **Insulation degradation** — High $dv/dt$ stresses on motor winding insulation; reduces winding life by up to **40 %** (industry empirical data).
3. **Battery stress** — Pulsating currents coupled back into the HV battery accelerate lithium plating and capacity fade.
4. **EMI compliance failure** — Exceeding CISPR 25 Class 5 conducted emissions limits for automotive.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Principle

**Step 1 — Charge phase:** When the switching node (or rectifier diode) is in conduction, the source delivers current $I_s(t)$ that exceeds the load current $I_L$. The surplus $I_s - I_L$ flows *into* the capacitor, raising its terminal voltage by an amount governed by $i = C \, dV/dt$.

**Step 2 — Discharge phase:** When the source current falls below $I_L$ (e.g., during the diode's off interval in a full-wave rectifier), the capacitor becomes the *sole* energy source for the load, discharging at a rate $dV/dt = -I_L / C$.

**Step 3 — Steady-state ripple:** In periodic steady state, the average charge delivered during charge phase equals the average charge removed during discharge phase. The peak-to-peak ripple amplitude becomes a function of $I_L$, switching/line frequency $f$, and $C$.

**Step 4 — ESR-induced spike:** Even if $C$ is infinite (ideal), the Equivalent Series Resistance $R_{ESR}$ causes an instantaneous voltage step $\Delta V_{ESR} = I_{C,peak} \times R_{ESR}$ on every switching edge. This *square-wave* component sits on top of the *triangular* capacitive component — the actual ripple is their superposition.

**Step 5 — Thermal equilibrium:** The RMS current $I_{C,rms}$ flowing through $R_{ESR}$ dissipates $P = I_{C,rms}^2 R_{ESR}$ watts. The capacitor's case temperature stabilises when this dissipation equals the heat rejected through conduction to the PCB / cold-plate. Exceeding rated $I_{C,rms}$ causes thermal runaway and catastrophic failure.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulae below are **exam-tested, syllabus-aligned** and use LaTeX-safe delimiters (`\vert`, `\mid`) inside any tabular text.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacitive stored energy | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Valid for any ideal capacitor |
| 2 | Instantaneous $i$-$v$ relation | $i_C(t) = C \, dV/dt$ | A | $C$ assumed constant |
| 3 | Peak-to-peak ripple (triangular, full-wave) | $\Delta V_{pp} = \dfrac{I_L}{2 f C}$ | V | For rectifier with cap filter, $f$ = line freq for half-wave, $2 \times$ for full-wave |
| 4 | Peak-to-peak ripple (buck CCM) | $\Delta V_{pp} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{8 f_{sw} C}$ | V | Inductor current is triangular |
| 5 | Peak-to-peak ripple (buck DCM) | $\Delta V_{pp} = \dfrac{V_{out}^2 (1-D)}{2 L f_{sw} I_{o,peak}}$ | V | $I_{o,peak}$ is load current at start of conduction |
| 6 | ESR-induced voltage step | $\Delta V_{ESR} = I_{C,peak} \cdot R_{ESR}$ | V | Dominant in ceramic/film caps at high $f$ |
| 7 | Total peak-to-peak ripple | $\Delta V_{pp,total} \approx \Delta V_{pp,cap} + \Delta V_{ESR}$ | V | Superposition; valid when ESL negligible |
| 8 | RMS current in cap (triangular ripple) | $I_{C,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | A | For symmetric triangular waveform |
| 9 | RMS current in cap (rectangular pulse) | $I_{C,rms} = I_{peak} \sqrt{D}$ | A | For duty cycle $D$ rectangular pulse |
| 10 | Power dissipation in ESR | $P_{loss} = I_{C,rms}^2 \cdot R_{ESR}$ | W | Determines thermal rating |
| 11 | Hold-up time | $t_{hold} = \dfrac{C (V_1^2 - V_2^2)}{2 P_{out}}$ | s | Time for $V_{DC}$ to drop from $V_1$ to $V_2$ at load $P_{out}$ |
| 12 | Required capacitance (ripple criterion) | $C \geq \dfrac{I_L}{2 f \, \Delta V_{pp,max}}$ | F | From Eq. (3) solved for $C$ |
| 13 | Required capacitance (hold-up criterion) | $C \geq \dfrac{2 P_{out} \, t_{hold}}{V_1^2 - V_2^2}$ | F | From Eq. (11) solved for $C$ |
| 14 | Voltage de-rating factor | $V_{op} \leq 0.8 \, V_{rated}$ | — | Industry practice for film/electrolytic reliability |
| 15 | Temperature de-rating (Arrhenius) | $L_{op} = L_{rated} \cdot 2^{\tfrac{T_{rated}-T_{op}}{10}}$ | h | "10 °C rule" — every 10 °C halves life |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive traction inverter (e.g., Tesla Model 3, BYD Blade):** A 600 V / 1 mF film DC-link capacitor absorbs the inverter's switching ripple, sized to keep $\Delta V_{pp} \leq 3$ % of nominal bus voltage.
- **12 V auxiliary DC-DC converter in EVs:** Output LC filter capacitor sized for $\Delta V_{pp} \leq 50$ mV at $I_{out} = 100$ A to comply with ISO 7637-2 transient immunity.
- **Solar-integrated EV chargers (V2G):** Hold-up capacitance sized to ride through 1 cycle (16.67 ms / 20 ms) of grid dropout, critical for grid-code compliance (IEEE 1547).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb for EV DC-link:** For a 400 V traction bus switching at 10 kHz with $\Delta I_L = 100$ A, the **minimum** DC-link capacitance to limit ripple to 2 % is $C \geq \dfrac{100}{2 \times 10\,000 \times 8} = 625 \,\mu F$. Designers typically choose 800–1000 µF with $\geq 500$ V rating, and 80 % de-rating in practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak-to-Peak Ripple Voltage for a Full-Wave Rectifier with Capacitor Filter

**Starting Premise:** A single-phase full-wave rectifier delivers a pulsating current to a resistive load $R_L$ through a smoothing capacitor $C$. During each half-cycle, the diode conducts only near the peak of the source voltage. We assume constant load current $I_L$ during the discharge interval (good approximation when ripple is small).

**Step 1 — Define the discharge interval.**  
The capacitor discharges from $V_{max}$ to $V_{min}$ over the time $\Delta t$ during which the diode is reverse-biased. For a full-wave rectifier at line frequency $f_{line}$, the period between successive peaks is $T = 1/(2 f_{line})$.

**Step 2 — Apply the capacitor current-voltage relation.**  
During discharge, the capacitor supplies the entire load current:

$$
i_C(t) = -I_L
$$

Using $i_C = C \, dV/dt$:

$$
C \, \dfrac{dV}{dt} = -I_L
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the discharge interval.**

$$
\int_{V_{max}}^{V_{min}} dV = -\dfrac{I_L}{C} \int_0^{\Delta t} dt
$$

$$
V_{min} - V_{max} = -\dfrac{I_L \, \Delta t}{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for peak-to-peak ripple.**

$$
\Delta V_{pp} = V_{max} - V_{min} = \dfrac{I_L \, \Delta t}{C}
$$

**Step 5 — Approximate $\Delta t \approx T = 1/(2 f_{line})$ (worst case, full discharge).**

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta V_{pp} \approx \dfrac{I_L}{2 f_{line} \, C}\,}
$$

This is Equation (3) of the formula sheet and is the **most-tested ripple relation** in KTU Module 2.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hold-Up Time Capacitance Requirement

**Starting Premise:** Following a power interruption, the DC bus must remain above a minimum threshold $V_2$ for at least $t_{hold}$ seconds to allow orderly shutdown or transfer.

**Step 1 — Energy balance.**  
The energy extracted from the capacitor during hold-up equals the load energy:

$$
E_{extract} = E_{initial} - E_{final} = P_{out} \cdot t_{hold}
$$

**Step 2 — Express energies in terms of $C$ and $V$.**

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} C V_1^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} C V_2^2 = P_{out} \cdot t_{hold}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor $C$ and solve.**

$$
\tfrac{1}{2} C (V_1^2 - V_2^2) = P_{out} \cdot t_{hold}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C \geq \dfrac{2 P_{out} \, t_{hold}}{V_1^2 - V_2^2}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Application to EV 12 V bus.**  
If $P_{out} = 600$ W, $t_{hold} = 10$ ms, $V_1 = 13.5$ V, $V_2 = 9$ V (cranking immunity):

$$
C \geq \dfrac{2 \times 600 \times 0.010}{13.5^2 - 9^2} = \dfrac{12}{182.25 - 81} = \dfrac{12}{101.25} \approx 0.1186 \text{ F}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C \approx 119\,000 \,\mu F\,}
$$

The required capacitance is the *larger* of the ripple-limited and hold-up-limited values — both must be checked.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RMS Ripple Current for a Triangular Waveform

**Starting Premise:** A buck converter in CCM delivers a triangular inductor current with peak deviation $\Delta I_L$ about DC value $I_L$. The capacitor supplies the *AC component* only.

**Step 1 — Express instantaneous capacitor current as the AC portion of inductor current.**

$$
i_C(t) = i_L(t) - I_L = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} \cdot \text{triangular}(t)
$$

where the triangular wave is symmetric about zero with peak $\pm \Delta I_L/2$.

**Step 2 — Define RMS for a periodic waveform.**

$$
I_{C,rms}^2 = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T i_C^2(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — For a symmetric triangular wave with peak $I_p = \Delta I_L/2$, the mean-square value is known:**

$$
\langle i_C^2 \rangle = \dfrac{I_p^2}{3} = \dfrac{(\Delta I_L / 2)^2}{3} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L^2}{12}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root.**

$$
\boxed{\,I_{C,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2\sqrt{3}}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check.**  
If $\Delta I_L = 10$ A, then $I_{C,rms} = 10 / (2 \times 1.732) \approx 2.89$ A. The capacitor must be rated for at least this continuous RMS current, or its internal heating will exceed the manufacturer's limit.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Total Ripple Considering ESR (Superposition)

**Starting Premise:** Real capacitors exhibit parasitic $R_{ESR}$. The total ripple is the sum of the (slow, triangular) capacitive component and the (fast, square) resistive component.

**Step 1 — Capacitive component** (from Derivation 1, or buck variant):

$$
\Delta V_{C} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{8 f_{sw} C}
$$

**Step 2 — Resistive component.** The capacitor current jumps from $-\Delta I_L/2$ to $+\Delta I_L/2$ on each switching edge — a step of magnitude $\Delta I_L$. The corresponding voltage step across $R_{ESR}$ is:

$$
\Delta V_{ESR} = \Delta I_L \cdot R_{ESR}
$$

**Step 3 — Total ripple (worst-case addition).**

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta V_{pp,total} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{8 f_{sw} C} + \Delta I_L \cdot R_{ESR}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In **ceramic** MLCC capacitors, $C$ is large and $R_{ESR}$ is small — the first term dominates. In **aluminium electrolytic** capacitors, the situation is reversed — $R_{ESR}$ dominates. This is why EV traction inverters use **film capacitors** (PP, PET) which balance both.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Tree

| Design Constraint | Dominant Parameter | Trade-off Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| Low $\Delta V_{pp}$ | Increase $C$ | Larger size, weight, cost |
| Low $\Delta V_{ESR}$ | Decrease $R_{ESR}$ | Use film/ceramic, not electrolytic |
| High $I_{C,rms}$ rating | Lower $R_{ESR}$ + parallel banks | Heat spreading, PCB layout |
| Wide temperature range | X7R/X8R ceramic or film | Cost vs. polymer Al |
| Long lifetime | De-rate voltage, control $T$ | Bigger or more caps |
| EV mass / volume | High energy density (J/cm³) | Film > ceramic > Al electrolytic |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Ripple Capacitor in EV Powertrain

This block-level functional architecture maps the **signal and energy flow** through a typical battery-electric vehicle traction inverter, highlighting all ripple capacitor locations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[HV Battery Pack 400 V] --> DC1[DC Link Capacitor C_dc]
    DC1 --> INV[Three Phase Inverter 10 kHz PWM]
    INV --> M[BLDC or PMSM Traction Motor]
    M --> WHEEL[Driven Wheels via Transmission]
    INV -.-> REGEN[Regenerative Braking Current Path]
    REGEN -.-> DC1
    DC1 -.-> BMS[BMS Voltage Sampling]
    subgraph AuxPath [Auxiliary Power Path]
        DC1 --> DCDC[HV LV DC DC Converter]
        DCDC --> C1[Output Filter Capacitor C_out]
        C1 --> AUX12V[12 V Auxiliary Bus Loads]
    end
    classDef cap fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#1f4e79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class DC1,C1 cap
    class BAT,INV,DCDC power
    class M,WHEEL,AUX12V,BMS,REGEN load
```

## 4.2 Ripple Waveform Anatomy (Schematic)

The following Mermaid timing diagram illustrates the **superposition** of capacitive (triangular) and ESR (square) ripple components to form the total observed ripple on a DC link.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TriCap [Capacitive Component - Slow Triangular]
        A1[Rising Slope = I_C_pos / C] --> A2[Peak V_max]
        A2 --> A3[Falling Slope = I_L / C]
        A3 --> A4[Trough V_min]
        A4 --> A1
    end
    subgraph ESRStep [ESR Component - Instantaneous Step]
        B1[Switch ON: V jumps by Delta_I_L times R_ESR] --> B2[Switch OFF: V drops by Delta_I_L times R_ESR]
        B2 --> B1
    end
    subgraph TotalWave [Total Ripple = Triangular + Step]
        C1[Peak = V_cap_max + Delta_V_ESR] --> C2[Trough = V_cap_min - Delta_V_ESR]
        C2 --> C1
    end
    TriCap --> TotalWave
    ESRStep --> TotalWave
    classDef tri fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef esr fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c65911,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tot fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4 tri
    class B1,B2 esr
    class C1,C2 tot
```

## 4.3 Capacitor Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose EV DC Link Capacitor] --> B{Voltage Level?}
    B -- "Less than 50 V" --> C[Ceramic MLCC or Polymer Al]
    B -- "50 to 500 V" --> D[Multilayer Film or Tantalum]
    B -- "Greater than 500 V" --> E[DC Link Film Capacitor PP or PET]
    C --> F{Capacitance Target uF to mF?}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F -- "uF range" --> G[MLCC X7R or X8R]
    F -- "hundreds of uF" --> H[Film or Polymer]
    F -- "mF range" --> I[Aluminium Electrolytic or Super-cap bank]
    G --> J[Verify Ripple Criterion and ESR]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Thermal Validation P_loss vs T_case]
    K --> L{Meets Lifetime Target?}
    L -- "Yes" --> M[Approved Design]
    L -- "No" --> N[Increase C, Parallel Bank, or De-rate V]
    N --> J
    classDef ok fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef warn fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class B,F,J,L decision
    class N warn
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define a ripple capacitor and state any two design parameters that govern its selection in an EV power-electronic converter.

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **ripple capacitor** is a filter capacitor connected across the DC output of a rectifier or across the DC link of a switching converter, intended to limit the peak-to-peak voltage ripple ($\Delta V_{pp}$) superimposed on the DC voltage supplied to downstream loads such as the traction inverter.

Two governing design parameters:

1. **Capacitance value ($C$)** — Determines the magnitude of the capacitive ripple component via $\Delta V_{pp} = I_L / (2 f C)$. Sized to keep ripple below a stipulated percentage (typically 2–5 %) of nominal DC voltage.
2. **Equivalent Series Resistance ($R_{ESR}$)** — Governs the instantaneous voltage step $\Delta V_{ESR} = \Delta I_L \cdot R_{ESR}$ at each switching edge and the internal power dissipation $I_{C,rms}^2 R_{ESR}$. Critical for thermal reliability.

*(Mention of a third parameter — RMS current rating — earns a bonus mark; not strictly required.)*

---

### **Q2.** Why is film capacitor preferred over aluminium electrolytic capacitor for the DC link of an EV traction inverter?

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Film capacitors (typically polypropylene) are preferred for EV traction DC-link applications for the following reasons:

1. **Low $R_{ESR}$ and low ESL** — results in very small ESR-induced ripple and ability to handle the fast $di/dt$ pulses of an inverter switching at 10–20 kHz without overheating.
2. **Long operational lifetime** — film capacitors do not have a dry-out failure mode; lifetime is limited only by thermal stress, with typical operational life exceeding 100 000 hours at rated conditions, matching or exceeding EV warranty periods.
3. **Higher ripple current rating per unit volume** — vital for the space- and weight-constrained EV packaging.

Aluminium electrolytic capacitors suffer from electrolyte evaporation, higher $R_{ESR}$, and shorter lifetime, making them unsuitable for the high-stress DC-link role in a traction inverter (although they may still appear in lower-power auxiliary supplies).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Full derivations, labelled diagrams, and boxed final answers are expected.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): DC Link Capacitor Sizing for EV Traction Inverter**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the peak-to-peak ripple voltage across a DC-link capacitor in a three-phase inverter supplying a PMSM traction motor, expressing it in terms of load current, switching frequency, and capacitance. Clearly state assumptions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the operating mode.**  
The DC-link capacitor of a three-phase voltage-source inverter (VSI) supplying a PMSM absorbs the *instantaneous* difference between the DC source current $I_s$ and the inverter input current $i_{inv}(t)$.

**Step 2 — Assume sinusoidal balanced motor currents.**

$$
i_a = I_m \sin(\omega t), \quad i_b = I_m \sin(\omega t - 2\pi/3), \quad i_c = I_m \sin(\omega t - 4\pi/3)
$$

**Step 3 — Inverter input current under SVPWM.**  
For space-vector PWM, the instantaneous DC-link current drawn by the inverter is:

$$
i_{inv}(t) = S_a i_a + S_b i_b + S_c i_c
$$

where $S_a, S_b, S_c \in \{0, 1\}$ are the upper-switch gating functions.

**Step 4 — Capacitor current.**

$$
i_C(t) = I_s - i_{inv}(t)
$$

In steady state, $I_s = \langle i_{inv} \rangle = (3/2) \, I_m \cos\phi$ where $\phi$ is the load power-factor angle.

**Step 5 — Peak-to-peak ripple approximation.**  
Over a switching period $T_{sw} = 1/f_{sw}$, the worst-case swing occurs at the switching boundary. The charge imbalance is $\Delta Q = \Delta I_L \cdot T_{sw}/2$ (for triangular approximation), leading to:

$$
\Delta V_{pp} = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2 f_{sw} C}
$$

where $\Delta I_L$ is the peak-peak variation of $i_{inv}$ in a switching period.

**Step 6 — Express $\Delta I_L$ in terms of motor current.**  
For a PMSM with peak phase current $I_{ph,pk}$:

$$
\Delta I_L \approx 0.5 \, I_{ph,pk}
$$

(Detailed derivation depends on modulation index $m_a$ and $\cos\phi$; this factor is conventionally used in industry datasheets.)

**Step 7 — Final boxed expression.**

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta V_{pp,DC-link} = \dfrac{I_{ph,pk}}{4 f_{sw} C_{DC}}\,}
$$

**Assumptions stated:**
- Sinusoidal, balanced, three-phase motor currents.
- Constant DC source (battery internal impedance neglected at switching frequency).
- SVPWM modulation; other schemes give similar order of magnitude.
- Capacitor ESR contribution neglected (added separately).

---

**(b) A 400 V / 250 kW EV traction inverter switches at 10 kHz. Peak phase current is 600 A. The DC-link capacitor must limit the peak-to-peak voltage ripple to 4 V. Calculate the required minimum DC-link capacitance, assuming ESR contribution is negligible. Also compute the hold-up time if the load is 250 kW, the bus drops from 400 V to 350 V during the hold-up interval. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Required capacitance from ripple criterion.**

Using the boxed equation from (a):

$$
C_{DC} \geq \dfrac{I_{ph,pk}}{4 f_{sw} \Delta V_{pp}} = \dfrac{600}{4 \times 10\,000 \times 4}
$$

$$
C_{DC} \geq \dfrac{600}{160\,000} = 3.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ F}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_{DC} \geq 3.75 \text{ mF}\,}
$$

**Step 2 — Hold-up time calculation.**  
Use the hold-up equation (formula sheet Eq. 11):

$$
t_{hold} = \dfrac{C (V_1^2 - V_2^2)}{2 P_{out}} = \dfrac{3.75 \times 10^{-3} \times (400^2 - 350^2)}{2 \times 250\,000}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$
400^2 = 160\,000, \quad 350^2 = 122\,500
$$

$$
160\,000 - 122\,500 = 37\,500 \text{ V}^2
$$

$$
3.75 \times 10^{-3} \times 37\,500 = 140.625 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 — Denominator evaluation.**

$$
2 \times 250\,000 = 500\,000
$$

**Step 5 — Final division.**

$$
t_{hold} = \dfrac{140.625}{500\,000} = 2.8125 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{hold} \approx 281.25 \,\mu s = 0.281 \text{ ms}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering commentary.**  
The hold-up time is short (~0.28 ms, or about 3 switching cycles at 10 kHz). This indicates that for a 250 kW traction drive, the *ripple* criterion (3.75 mF) is the binding constraint, while hold-up is trivially satisfied. Real EV designs use 4–5 mF film capacitor banks to provide additional safety margin and to handle the surge currents during regenerative braking.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Output Filter Capacitor Design for EV Auxiliary DC–DC Converter**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) With the aid of a labelled circuit diagram, derive the expression for output voltage ripple of a buck DC–DC converter operating in continuous conduction mode (CCM), considering both capacitive and ESR components. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit diagram (described textually for marking).**  
The buck converter consists of: input $V_{in}$, MOSFET switch $S$, diode $D$ (freewheeling), inductor $L$, output capacitor $C_{out}$ with parasitic $R_{ESR}$ and $R_L$ as the load. The output voltage $V_{out}$ is taken across $C_{out}$ and $R_L$.

**Step 2 — Inductor current waveform (CCM).**  
The inductor current is triangular with slope:

- During ON-time $DT_s$: slope $= (V_{in} - V_{out})/L$
- During OFF-time $(1-D)T_s$: slope $= -V_{out}/L$

The peak-to-peak swing is:

$$
\Delta I_L = \dfrac{(V_{in} - V_{out}) D}{L f_{sw}} = \dfrac{V_{out} (1-D)}{L f_{sw}}
$$

**Step 3 — Capacitor current.**  
The capacitor current is the AC component of inductor current (after subtracting the DC load current $I_L$):

$$
i_C(t) = i_L(t) - I_L
$$

This is triangular, with peak $\pm \Delta I_L / 2$ symmetric about zero.

**Step 4 — Capacitive ripple component.**  
Charge balance over half a switching period gives:

$$
\Delta Q = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} \cdot \dfrac{T_{sw}}{2} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L \, T_{sw}}{8}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\Delta V_{cap} = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{8 f_{sw} C}
$$

**Step 5 — ESR ripple component.**  
On each switching edge, the capacitor current steps by $\Delta I_L$, producing an instantaneous voltage step:

$$
\Delta V_{ESR} = \Delta I_L \cdot R_{ESR}
$$

**Step 6 — Total ripple (superposition).**

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta V_{out,pp} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{8 f_{sw} C} + \Delta I_L \cdot R_{ESR}\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Special-case limits.**  
- If $R_{ESR} \ll 1/(8 f_{sw} C)$ (ceramic or film cap): ripple dominated by capacitive term.
- If $R_{ESR} \gg 1/(8 f_{sw} C)$ (electrolytic cap): ripple dominated by ESR term; *increasing $C$ further yields no improvement.*

---

**(b) A 48 V → 12 V buck converter is used as an auxiliary DC-DC stage in an EV. The converter operates at $f_{sw} = 200$ kHz, $L = 22 \,\mu H$, $D = 0.25$, $I_{out} = 20$ A. The output capacitor is rated $C = 100 \,\mu F$ with $R_{ESR} = 5$ m$\Omega$. Compute (i) the peak-to-peak inductor ripple current, (ii) the capacitive ripple, (iii) the ESR ripple, and (iv) the total peak-to-peak output voltage ripple. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Output voltage.**  
For an ideal buck: $V_{out} = D V_{in} = 0.25 \times 48 = 12$ V ✓ (consistent with rating).

**Step 2 — Peak-to-peak inductor ripple (Part i).**  
Using formula sheet equation variant:

$$
\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_{out} (1-D)}{L f_{sw}} = \dfrac{12 \times (1 - 0.25)}{22 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^3}
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
22 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^3 = 22 \times 0.2 = 4.4
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
12 \times 0.75 = 9
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta I_L = \dfrac{9}{4.4} \approx 2.045 \text{ A}\,}
$$

**Step 3 — Capacitive ripple (Part ii).**  
Using $\Delta V_{cap} = \Delta I_L / (8 f_{sw} C)$:

$$
\Delta V_{cap} = \dfrac{2.045}{8 \times 200 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

Denominator:

$$
8 \times 200 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 20 = 160
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta V_{cap} = \dfrac{2.045}{160} \approx 12.78 \text{ mV}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — ESR ripple (Part iii).**  
Using $\Delta V_{ESR} = \Delta I_L \cdot R_{ESR}$:

$$
\Delta V_{ESR} = 2.045 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 0.01023 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta V_{ESR} \approx 10.23 \text{ mV}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Total ripple (Part iv).**

$$
\Delta V_{out,pp} = \Delta V_{cap} + \Delta V_{ESR} = 12.78 + 10.23
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta V_{out,pp} \approx 23.0 \text{ mV}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Design check.**  
The 12 V automotive bus must comply with ISO 16750-2 ripple limits (typically ±0.5 V, or 1 V peak-to-peak). The calculated 23 mV is well within tolerance, indicating that the chosen $C$ and $R_{ESR}$ are over-designed. A designer might consider reducing $C$ to 47 µF and accepting a slightly higher ripple (~50 mV) to save cost and PCB area — provided the ESR component remains bounded.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Ripple Capacitor Problems (Module 2):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for full-wave rectifiers.** Half-wave gives $1/(fC)$; full-wave gives $1/(2fC)$. Mixing these up loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using $V_{DC}$ as $V_{in}$ in hold-up equations.** The *initial* and *final* voltages appear squared — squaring is not linear, and students who linearise $\tfrac{1}{2}C(V_1^2 - V_2^2) \approx C V_1 (V_1 - V_2)$ lose accuracy and marks.
> 3. **Neglecting ESR contribution in ceramic/film caps at high $f$.** Even low-ESR parts have ESL that becomes significant above 1 MHz; examiners often include a sub-part on ESL to test this.
> 4. **Failing to state assumptions.** KTU examiners award marks for *explicitly* listing assumptions (sinusoidal currents, ideal source, CCM operation, etc.). A correct answer without assumptions loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency.** Mixing µF and F without conversion is a frequent error in numerical problems — leading to answers off by a factor of $10^6$.
> 6. **Ignoring the de-rating rule (80 %).** Specifying a capacitor rated exactly at the operating voltage is non-compliant; examiners deduct marks for not applying the safety de-rating.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Recap:** A ripple capacitor is a filter capacitor placed across the DC link / output to suppress voltage pulsations, sized to satisfy both ripple and hold-up criteria.
- **Core Formulae to Memorise (KGU / KTU Exam Essentials):**
  - $\Delta V_{pp} = I_L / (2 f C)$ — full-wave rectifier
  - $\Delta V_{pp} = \Delta I_L / (8 f_{sw} C)$ — buck CCM
  - $\Delta V_{out,pp} = \Delta I_L / (8 f_{sw} C) + \Delta I_L R_{ESR}$ — total ripple (superposition)
  - $t_{hold} = C (V_1^2 - V_2^2) / (2 P_{out})$ — hold-up time
  - $I_{C,rms} = \Delta I_L / (2\sqrt{3})$ — triangular RMS
  - $P_{loss} = I_{C,rms}^2 R_{ESR}$ — capacitor self-heating
- **Key Design Constraints:** Voltage ripple limit, RMS current rating, thermal dissipation, lifetime, mass/volume.
- **Capacitor Hierarchy in EVs:** MLCC < Tantalum < Film (PP/PET) < Aluminium Electrolytic < Supercapacitor — choose by trade-off between $C$, $R_{ESR}$, $I_{C,rms}$, lifetime, and energy density.
- **EV-Specific Insight:** Traction inverter DC-link is the most demanding ripple-capacitor application in any EV; **film** is the industry default.
- **Safety / Reliability Rule:** Always de-rate operating voltage to ≤ 80 % of rated; apply the 10 °C rule for lifetime estimation.
- **Cross-Connection:** This topic links Module 2 (Power Converters) to Module 3 (Motor Drives) because DC-link ripple directly affects motor torque ripple.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switching Frequency and PWM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switching Frequency and PWM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Switching Frequency?

**Switching Frequency (f<sub>sw</sub>)** is the rate at which the power semiconductor devices (IGBTs, MOSFETs, SiC/GaN transistors) inside the traction inverter of an Electric Vehicle are turned ON and OFF per second. It is measured in **Hertz (Hz)** and typically expressed in **kilohertz (kHz)** for EV applications, ranging anywhere from **5 kHz to 50 kHz** depending on the power device technology, motor type, and EMC/thermal constraints.

In an EV traction inverter, the DC link voltage from the high-voltage battery (typically **400 V** or **800 V** architectures) must be converted into a variable-frequency, variable-magnitude three-phase AC supply for the Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor (PMSM) or Induction Motor (IM). This conversion is achieved NOT through a linear amplifier (which would be grossly inefficient) but through rapid high-speed switching of power devices, modulated using **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** techniques.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2):** Switching frequency is the fundamental operating parameter of any Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) used in EV powertrains. The choice of f<sub>sw</sub> directly determines inverter efficiency, motor torque ripple, acoustic noise, and electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) performance.

## 1.2 What is Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)?

**Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** is the intelligent control technique that determines HOW the power switches are turned ON and OFF to synthesize a desired reference AC waveform from a fixed DC bus. By varying the **width of the ON pulses** within each switching period, the average voltage applied to the motor phase is precisely controlled.

In EVs, PWM is the "language" used by the Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) and Motor Control Unit (MCU) to command the inverter switches. The most common PWM techniques used in modern EV traction inverters are:

1. **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)** — The classical approach using a sine reference and triangular carrier.
2. **Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)** — Industry standard for EVs, offering 15.47% higher DC bus utilization.
3. **Hysteresis (Bang-Bang) Current Control PWM** — Used for direct torque/flux control.
4. **Discontinuous PWM (DPWM)** — Reduces switching losses by clamping one phase per sector.
5. **Selective Harmonic Elimination PWM (SHE-PWM)** — Used at very low switching frequencies for high-power EVs.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Water Tap

Imagine you are filling a bucket with water at a rate that varies — sometimes you want a gentle flow, sometimes a strong flow. You have only an ON/OFF tap (no variable valve). 

- **Switching Frequency** = How fast you flick your hand on the tap per second.
- **Pulse Width** = How long you keep the tap open in each flick.
- **PWM** = The strategy of flicking the tap — short flicks for low average flow, long flicks for high flow.

If you flick the tap **very fast** (high f<sub>sw</sub>), the water level in the bucket rises smoothly, and the bucket (motor) hardly notices the discrete pulses. However, your hand gets tired faster (switching losses in the IGBTs). If you flick slowly, your hand rests more, but the water rises in lumps (torque ripple, current ripple).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Trade-Off in EV Design:** Increasing switching frequency improves motor-side smoothness (lower current ripple, lower torque ripple, quieter operation) but increases inverter losses and reduces driving range. EV engineers must balance this trade-off intelligently.

## 1.4 Why PWM and Switching Frequency Matter in EVs

In an EV powertrain, the traction inverter switches at high frequencies to:

- **Synthesize sinusoidal currents** in the stator windings for smooth torque production.
- **Regulate motor speed and torque** precisely under all driving conditions (regen braking, launch, highway cruising).
- **Minimize torque ripple** which otherwise causes uncomfortable "cogging" vibrations felt by passengers.
- **Avoid acoustic noise** in the **5 kHz – 20 kHz** human-audible range.
- **Limit DC link current ripple** to protect the high-voltage battery and bus capacitors.
- **Achieve high efficiency (>95%)** in the inverter to maximize EV driving range.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Module 2 Focus:** This topic is foundational to understanding the **DC-AC Inverter block** of the EV powertrain. Students must understand the mathematical relationship between modulation index, switching frequency, fundamental output voltage, and harmonic distortion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle of PWM

The PWM process for a single phase of an EV inverter operates as follows:

1. A **reference waveform** (sinusoidal in SPWM, a rotating space vector in SVPWM) is generated by the Field Oriented Control (FOC) algorithm in the MCU.
2. A **high-frequency triangular carrier wave** (or sawtooth) is generated at the switching frequency f<sub>sw</sub>.
3. The two signals are compared in real time by a comparator (hardware) or computed digitally (DSP/MCU).
4. When reference > carrier → **Upper switch ON, Lower switch OFF** (phase connected to +V<sub>dc</sub>).
5. When reference < carrier → **Upper switch OFF, Lower switch ON** (phase connected to ground).
6. This generates a train of pulses with varying widths that, when **low-pass filtered** by the motor winding inductance, produces the desired average sinusoidal current.

## 2.2 The Three Critical Parameters of PWM

### A. Modulation Index (m or m<sub>a</sub>)

The **Modulation Index (m)** is the ratio of the peak value of the reference (control) signal to the peak value of the carrier signal. It directly controls the **fundamental output voltage amplitude** of the inverter.

$$m_a = \frac{V_{ref,peak}}{V_{carrier,peak}}$$

For SPWM: m<sub>a</sub> is limited to **≤ 1.0** in the linear region.
For SVPWM: m<sub>a</sub> can reach **1.1547** (i.e., 2/√3) before entering overmodulation, giving **15.47% more DC bus utilization**.

### B. Frequency Modulation Ratio (m<sub>f</sub>)

The ratio of carrier frequency to fundamental reference frequency. Must be an **integer multiple** in synchronous PWM to avoid subharmonics.

$$m_f = \frac{f_{sw}}{f_{fundamental}}$$

Typical m<sub>f</sub> in EV drives: **odd multiples of 3** (e.g., 21, 39, 63, 99, 201) to eliminate certain harmonic families.

### C. Switching Frequency (f<sub>sw</sub>)

Independent parameter. For Si-IGBT EV inverters: **5–20 kHz**; for SiC-MOSFET EV inverters: **20–100 kHz**; for GaN: up to **500 kHz** in low-voltage auxiliary drives.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The Following Table Is the Most Tested Set of Equations in KTU University Exams for Inverter/PWM Topics. Memorize These.**

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Modulation Index (SPWM) | $m_a = \dfrac{V_{ref,peak}}{V_{carrier,peak}}$ | Dimensionless; 0 ≤ m<sub>a</sub> ≤ 1.0 (linear region) |
| 2 | Modulation Index (SVPWM) | $m = \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ref,peak}}{V_{dc}}$ | Range: 0 to $\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1547$ |
| 3 | Fundamental RMS Line Voltage (SPWM, linear) | $V_{L,rms} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot m_a \cdot \dfrac{V_{dc}}{2}$ | Volts; valid for 0 ≤ m<sub>a</sub> ≤ 1.0 |
| 4 | Fundamental RMS Line Voltage (SVPWM, linear) | $V_{L,rms} = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{3}} = m \cdot \dfrac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Volts; max = $\dfrac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{2}}$ at m = 1.1547 |
| 5 | Max Fundamental Phase Voltage (SPWM) | $V_{ph,max} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{2}$ | Limited by DC bus |
| 6 | Max Fundamental Phase Voltage (SVPWM) | $V_{ph,max} = \dfrac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $\approx 0.577 \cdot V_{dc}$, **15.47%** higher than SPWM |
| 7 | Frequency Modulation Ratio | $m_f = \dfrac{f_{sw}}{f_1}$ | Integer (synchronous); e.g., 21, 63, 99 |
| 8 | Total Harmonic Distortion (Voltage) | $THD_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} V_h^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$ | Percentage; lower is better |
| 9 | Switching Loss per Device (Linear Approx.) | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{dc} \cdot I_{avg} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$ | Watts; **directly proportional to f<sub>sw</sub>** |
| 10 | Conduction Loss per Device | $P_{cond} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R_{ds,on}$ (MOSFET) or $V_{CE,sat} \cdot I_{avg}$ (IGBT) | Watts; depends on duty cycle |
| 11 | RMS Current Ripple (Approx.) | $\Delta I_{rms} \approx \dfrac{V_{dc}}{8 \cdot L_s \cdot f_{sw}}$ | Amperes; inversely proportional to L<sub>s</sub> and f<sub>sw</sub> |
| 12 | Peak-to-Peak Current Ripple | $\Delta I_{pp} \approx \dfrac{V_{dc}}{4 \cdot L_s \cdot f_{sw}}$ | Amperes |
| 13 | Torque Ripple (Approximate) | $T_{ripple} \approx \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot p \cdot \psi_m \cdot \Delta I_q$ | Nm; dominated by current ripple at low speeds |
| 14 | A-Weighted Acoustic Noise Limit | Driver perception peaks at **2–5 kHz** | Choose f<sub>sw</sub> > **20 kHz** for silent operation |
| 15 | Inverter Efficiency (Typical EV) | $\eta_{inv} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{sw} + P_{cond} + P_{gate}}$ | **95–99%** for SiC inverters |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Substitution:** Wherever absolute value or magnitude is required, use `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $V_{ref,peak} = \vert V_{ref} \vert_{max}$) — NEVER use the vertical bar `|` character alone, as it breaks the markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in EVs

- **Tesla Model 3 / Model Y** uses a SiC-MOSFET inverter switching at ~20–40 kHz for the rear PMSM.
- **Tata Nexon EV** uses a Si-IGBT inverter at 8–12 kHz for its PMSM.
- **Lucid Air** uses SiC inverters at very high f<sub>sw</sub> to reduce motor size and weight.
- **Formula E Racing EVs** use SVPWM at switching frequencies up to 50 kHz to maximize efficiency and minimize motor heating.

## 2.5 Why These Equations Matter — The "How" Behind the "What"

- The **Modulation Index** equation tells the EV engineer: "To get 85% of max motor torque, I must command m = 0.85 in the inverter."
- The **Switching Loss** equation reveals: "Doubling f<sub>sw</sub> doubles my inverter losses — I will lose ~1% efficiency and reduce range."
- The **Current Ripple** equation shows: "To cut ripple in half, I need to double either the motor inductance OR the switching frequency — but doubling f<sub>sw</sub> doubles losses."

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fundamental Output Voltage of a Sinusoidal PWM Inverter

We will derive the **RMS line-to-line fundamental voltage** of a 3-phase VSI using bipolar SPWM, starting from first principles.

### Step 1: Define the Input Quantities

Let the **DC bus voltage** be $V_{dc}$ (the EV high-voltage battery, e.g., 400 V). The reference phase voltage is sinusoidal:

$$v_{ref}(t) = m_a \cdot V_{tri,peak} \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

The **triangular carrier** varies between 0 and $V_{tri,peak}$ at frequency $f_{sw}$. For unipolar SPWM, the comparator output for phase A is:

$$
v_{AN}(t) = 
\begin{cases}
+V_{dc}/2, & \text{if } v_{ref}(t) > v_{tri}(t) \\
-V_{dc}/2, & \text{if } v_{ref}(t) < v_{tri}(t)
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Fourier Analysis to the Switching Function

The output $v_{AN}(t)$ is a periodic, piecewise constant waveform. Its **Fourier series** representation gives:

$$
v_{AN}(t) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left[ a_n \cos(n\omega t) + b_n \sin(n\omega t) \right]
$$

The **fundamental component** (n = 1) amplitude, derived by double-Fourier integration, yields the well-known result for bipolar SPWM:

$$
V_{AN,1,peak} = m_a \cdot \frac{V_{dc}}{2}
$$

**Textual Logic of Step 2:** The reference sinusoid is being "averaged" by the triangular carrier. Since the reference and carrier are both zero-mean symmetric, only the fundamental of the reference survives, scaled by the ratio of the reference amplitude to the carrier amplitude (which is m<sub>a</sub>) and by the available DC swing of $\pm V_{dc}/2$.

### Step 3: Convert Peak Phase Voltage to RMS Phase Voltage

$$
V_{AN,1,rms} = \frac{V_{AN,1,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

**Textual Logic of Step 3:** Standard sinusoidal RMS conversion: divide peak by $\sqrt{2}$.

### Step 4: Convert Phase Voltage to Line Voltage

For a balanced 3-phase system, the line-to-line voltage is $\sqrt{3}$ times the phase voltage (this is a standard three-phase relationship derived from vector subtraction of phase phasors separated by 120°):

$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{AN,1,rms} = \sqrt{3} \cdot \frac{m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

### Step 5: State the Final Result

$$
\boxed{V_{LL,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc} \approx 0.612 \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}}
$$

**Textual Logic of Step 5:** This is the **single most important equation for KTU problems on SPWM inverters**. It shows that the maximum RMS line voltage (at m<sub>a</sub> = 1.0) is only **0.612 × V<sub>dc</sub>**, meaning SPWM can only use ~61.2% of the DC bus voltage — the rest is "wasted" harmonic energy.

## 3.2 Derivation: SVPWM Maximum Voltage Boost (15.47% Advantage)

### Step 1: Reference Voltage Magnitude in αβ Plane

In SVPWM, the reference is a rotating space vector $\vec{V}_{ref}$ in the stationary αβ reference frame. The magnitude of the largest inscribed circle within the hexagon formed by the six inverter switching states is:

$$
\vert \vec{V}_{ref} \vert_{max} = \frac{2}{3} \cdot V_{dc} \cdot \cos(30°) = \frac{2}{3} \cdot V_{dc} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Textual Logic of Step 1:** A 2-level VSI has 8 switching states forming a hexagon in the αβ plane. The fundamental of the active state vectors has magnitude $\frac{2}{3} V_{dc}$, but the largest circle that fits inside the hexagon (touches the hexagon's vertices' midpoints) has radius $\frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$.

### Step 2: SPWM Equivalent Phase Voltage

For SPWM, the max phase voltage amplitude is $V_{dc}/2$. Compare:

$$
\frac{V_{SVPWM,max}}{V_{SPWM,max}} = \frac{V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}}{V_{dc}/2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.1547
$$

### Step 3: Express as Percentage Boost

$$
\text{Boost} = \left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} - 1\right) \times 100\% = 15.47\%
$$

**Textual Logic of Step 3:** SVPWM can deliver 15.47% more fundamental voltage from the same DC bus. For a 400 V EV battery, this means the motor can deliver 15.47% more torque at high speed without overmodulation. **This is why every modern EV uses SVPWM or its variants.**

## 3.3 Derivation: Current Ripple in an Inductive Motor Phase

### Step 1: Apply KVL to One Phase

For phase A, during the interval when the upper switch is ON:

$$
V_{dc}/2 - L_s \frac{di_a}{dt} - R_s i_a - e_a = 0
$$

Where $e_a$ is the back-EMF. Ignoring $R_s$ for ripple analysis (since L dominates at high frequency):

$$
L_s \frac{di_a}{dt} \approx \frac{V_{dc}}{2} - e_a
$$

### Step 2: Approximate the Switching Period Behavior

Over one switching period $T_{sw} = 1/f_{sw}$, the current rises for time $t_1 = D \cdot T_{sw}$ (duty cycle D) and falls for $t_2 = (1-D) T_{sw}$. The peak-to-peak ripple is:

$$
\Delta I_{pp} = \frac{1}{L_s} \int_0^{t_1} \left( \frac{V_{dc}}{2} - e_a \right) dt
$$

### Step 3: Worst-Case Ripple (Steady-State, Mid-Power)

At the worst operating point where $e_a = 0$ (motor at standstill or low speed):

$$
\Delta I_{pp} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2 L_s} \cdot D \cdot T_{sw}
$$

For D = 0.5 (worst case):

$$
\Delta I_{pp} = \frac{V_{dc}}{2 L_s} \cdot 0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{f_{sw}} = \frac{V_{dc}}{4 L_s f_{sw}}
$$

**Textual Logic of Step 3:** This proves that the current ripple is **inversely proportional to f<sub>sw</sub>**. Doubling f<sub>sw</sub> halves the ripple. This is the fundamental justification for using higher switching frequencies in EVs.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Trade-Off Comparison

| Parameter | Increase f<sub>sw</sub> | Decrease f<sub>sw</sub> | EV Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Current Ripple | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | High f<sub>sw</sub> → smoother torque, less motor heating |
| Torque Ripple | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | High f<sub>sw</sub> → better NVH (comfort) |
| Acoustic Noise | ↑ Shifts out of audible band | ↓ Falls into 2–5 kHz range | Choose f<sub>sw</sub> > 20 kHz for silent EV |
| Switching Losses | ↑ Linearly increases | ↓ Decreases | High f<sub>sw</sub> → reduced range, more cooling needed |
| Conduction Losses | Roughly constant | Roughly constant | Independent of f<sub>sw</sub> |
| Inverter Efficiency | ↓ Decreases (1–3% drop) | ↑ Increases | EV design optimization critical |
| DC Link Capacitor Size | ↓ Smaller needed | ↑ Larger needed | High f<sub>sw</sub> → smaller, lighter, cheaper capacitors |
| Motor Lamination Losses | ↑ Slightly increases | ↓ Decreases | High-frequency eddy currents in iron |
| Bearing Currents | Can ↑ if too high | ↓ Reduces | Modern EVs use insulated bearings |
| EMI Filter Size | ↓ Smaller | ↑ Larger | High f<sub>sw</sub> → easier EMC compliance |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PWM Modulation Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete PWM signal generation chain inside an EV Motor Control Unit (MCU), from reference generation to gate driver outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VCU Torque Command Nm] --> B[FOC Algorithm]
    B --> C[Id Ref and Iq Ref Generation]
    C --> D[Inverse Park Transform]
    D --> E[Alpha Beta Reference Voltage]
    E --> F[SVPWM Modulator]
    F --> G[Switching Frequency fsw 20 kHz]
    G --> H[Compare Reference with Triangular Carrier]
    H --> I[Generate 6 PWM Gate Signals]
    I --> J[Gate Driver Circuit]
    J --> K[SiC MOSFET Inverter 800V DC]
    K --> L[3 Phase PMSM Motor]
    L --> M[Resolver Encoder Feedback]
    M --> B
    K --> N[Current Sensors LEM]
    N --> B
```

**Textual Explanation of the Diagram:** The Vehicle Control Unit (VCU) sends a torque command. The Field Oriented Control (FOC) algorithm computes the required d-axis and q-axis currents. The Inverse Park transform converts these DC quantities into a rotating αβ reference. The SVPWM modulator compares this reference with a triangular carrier at switching frequency f<sub>sw</sub>. Six gate signals are produced for the three inverter legs. The gate drivers amplify these logic-level signals to drive the SiC MOSFETs. The inverter converts 800 V DC into three-phase AC for the PMSM. Resolver and current sensor feedback close the control loop.

## 4.2 Space Vector Hexagon and Modulation Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC Bus + Vdc] --> B[Inverter Switches S1 to S6]
    B --> C[8 Switching States]
    C --> D[6 Active Vectors V1 to V6]
    C --> E[2 Zero Vectors V0 V7]
    D --> F[Hexagon in Alpha Beta Plane]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Reference Vector Vref Rotates Inside Hexagon]
    G --> H[Sector Detection 1 to 6]
    H --> I[Time Calculation T1 T2 T0]
    I --> J[Duty Cycle Generation for Each Leg]
```

**Textual Explanation:** The eight switching states of a 2-level inverter form six active vectors (V1–V6) and two zero vectors (V0, V7) in the αβ plane. These six active vectors form a regular hexagon. The reference vector V<sub>ref</sub> rotates inside this hexagon at the fundamental electrical frequency. The SVPWM algorithm detects which of the six sectors (60° each) contains V<sub>ref</sub>, calculates the time spent on the two adjacent active vectors (T1, T2) and the zero vector (T0), then generates the appropriate duty cycles for the three inverter legs.

## 4.3 Switching Frequency Effect on Current and Torque Ripple

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Switching Frequency fsw] --> B{Region of Operation}
    B -->|Low fsw 5 kHz| C[High Current Ripple]
    B -->|Mid fsw 10 to 20 kHz| D[Moderate Current Ripple]
    B -->|High fsw 40 to 100 kHz| E[Low Current Ripple]
    C --> F[High Torque Ripple]
    C --> G[Audible Buzz 2 to 5 kHz]
    D --> H[Acceptable NVH]
    E --> I[Smooth Torque]
    E --> J[Silent Operation]
    E --> K[Higher Switching Losses]
    K --> L[Reduced EV Range]
    F --> M[Passenger Discomfort]
    H --> N[Standard EV Tuning]
    I --> O[Premium EV Tuning]
```

**Textual Explanation:** This decision tree illustrates the trade-off. Low f<sub>sw</sub> (typical of cheap Si-IGBT inverters) gives high current ripple, audible buzz, and torque pulsations felt by passengers. Mid f<sub>sw</sub> (10–20 kHz) is the standard EV tuning sweet spot. High f<sub>sw</sub> (40–100 kHz, typical of SiC) gives smooth, silent operation but consumes more energy as switching losses, slightly reducing the driving range.

## 4.4 Harmonic Spectrum Comparison (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Sine 50 Hz] --> B[SPWM Modulator]
    A --> C[SVPWM Modulator]
    B --> D[Harmonic Spectrum SPWM]
    C --> E[Harmonic Spectrum SVPWM]
    D --> F[Carrier Cluster at mfx f1]
    E --> G[Carrier Cluster at mfx f1]
    F --> H[Triple Harmonics Significant]
    G --> I[Triple Harmonics Suppressed]
    H --> J[Higher THD approx 80 percent]
    I --> K[Lower THD approx 65 percent]
```

**Textual Explanation:** Both SPWM and SVPWM produce carrier-cluster harmonics around multiples of the switching frequency. SPWM has substantial triple harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th) that must be filtered or absorbed by the motor. SVPWM inherently suppresses these triple harmonics in the line-to-line voltage, giving a lower total harmonic distortion. This is another reason SVPWM dominates EV traction inverters.
