# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define switching frequency and state its typical range in modern EV traction inverters.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

Switching frequency is the rate at which the power semiconductor switches (IGBTs, SiC MOSFETs) in a traction inverter are turned ON and OFF per second, measured in Hz. In modern EV traction inverters, the switching frequency typically ranges from **5 kHz to 20 kHz for Si-IGBT** inverters and **20 kHz to 100 kHz for SiC-MOSFET** inverters. The choice depends on the trade-off between motor current ripple, acoustic noise, and inverter switching losses. Higher switching frequency reduces current ripple and torque pulsations but increases switching losses, slightly reducing the EV driving range.

---

### Question 2: What is the modulation index in PWM and why is SVPWM preferred over SPWM in EVs?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

The **modulation index (m<sub>a</sub> or m)** is the ratio of the peak amplitude of the reference (control) signal to the peak amplitude of the triangular carrier signal. It directly controls the fundamental output voltage of the inverter.

**SVPWM is preferred over SPWM in EVs for the following reasons:**

1. **15.47% higher DC bus utilization** — SVPWM can deliver a maximum line voltage of $V_{dc}/\sqrt{2}$ versus only $0.612 \cdot V_{dc}$ for SPWM.
2. **Lower Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** — triple harmonics are naturally suppressed in the line-to-line voltage.
3. **Better suited for digital implementation** in DSP/MCU-based motor controllers.
4. **Higher efficiency** at the same switching frequency due to lower harmonic copper losses in the motor.
5. **More torque per ampere** at high speeds, critical for highway cruising range.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the sinusoidal PWM (SPWM) technique for a single-phase inverter. Derive the expression for the fundamental RMS output voltage in terms of modulation index and DC bus voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase VSI fed by a **400 V DC bus** supplies a PMSM using **bipolar SPWM** with modulation index **m<sub>a</sub> = 0.85**. The motor has phase inductance **L<sub>s</sub> = 2 mH** and the switching frequency is **f<sub>sw</sub> = 10 kHz**. Calculate:
- (i) The fundamental RMS line-to-line voltage.
- (ii) The approximate peak-to-peak current ripple in one phase. **(7 Marks)**

### Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — SPWM Explanation and Derivation

A single-phase full-bridge inverter consists of four switches S1, S2, S3, S4. In bipolar SPWM, switches S1 and S2 operate complementarily to S3 and S4. A sinusoidal reference signal $v_{ref}(t) = m_a \cdot V_{tri,peak} \cdot \sin(\omega t)$ is compared with a high-frequency triangular carrier $v_{tri}(t)$ at frequency $f_{sw}$. 

**Operating Logic:**
- When $v_{ref} > v_{tri}$: S1 and S2 ON → output = $+V_{dc}$
- When $v_{ref} < v_{tri}$: S3 and S4 ON → output = $-V_{dc}$

This produces a bipolar PWM waveform that swings between $+V_{dc}$ and $-V_{dc}$. The Fourier analysis (using double Fourier integration) of this waveform yields the fundamental component:

$$
V_{o,1,peak} = m_a \cdot V_{dc}
$$

For a 3-phase extension, each phase has a reference displaced by 120°. The line-to-line voltage is the difference of two phase voltages, and the fundamental RMS line-to-line voltage is:

$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc} \approx 0.612 \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}
$$

This is the **fundamental SPWM output equation**, valid in the linear modulation region (0 ≤ m<sub>a</sub> ≤ 1.0).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $V_{dc} = 400$ V
- $m_a = 0.85$
- $L_s = 2$ mH $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ H
- $f_{sw} = 10$ kHz $= 10^4$ Hz

**(i) Fundamental RMS Line-to-Line Voltage:**

Substitute into the SPWM equation:

$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}
$$

$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} \cdot 0.85 \cdot 400
$$

First compute the constant: $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{1.732}{2.828} = 0.6124$

$$
V_{LL,1,rms} = 0.6124 \times 0.85 \times 400 = 0.6124 \times 340 = 208.2 \text{ V}
$$

**Answer (i): $V_{LL,1,rms} \approx 208.2$ V**

**(ii) Peak-to-Peak Current Ripple:**

Use the derived current ripple formula (worst case, D = 0.5):

$$
\Delta I_{pp} = \frac{V_{dc}}{4 \cdot L_s \cdot f_{sw}}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\Delta I_{pp} = \frac{400}{4 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^4}
$$

Compute denominator: $4 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^4 = 4 \times 20 = 80$

$$
\Delta I_{pp} = \frac{400}{80} = 5 \text{ A}
$$

**Answer (ii): $\Delta I_{pp} = 5$ A**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the Space Vector PWM (SVPWM) technique for a 3-phase 2-level VSI used in EV traction inverters. Show the hexagon of switching states in the αβ plane and explain how the reference vector is synthesized using adjacent active vectors and zero vectors. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase 2-level VSI in an EV uses **SVPWM** with DC bus voltage $V_{dc} = 800$ V. The reference space vector has magnitude $V_{ref} = 350$ V and the fundamental frequency is $f_1 = 100$ Hz. The switching frequency is $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz. The reference lies in **Sector 1** with angle $\theta = 30°$ from the V1 axis. Calculate:
- (i) The modulation index m.
- (ii) The frequency modulation ratio m<sub>f</sub>.
- (iii) The dwell times T1, T2, and T0 in microseconds. **(7 Marks)**

### Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — SVPWM Explanation

A 3-phase 2-level VSI has **eight switching states**: six active states (V1 to V6) producing non-zero output vectors, and two zero states (V000, V111) producing zero output. The six active vectors are 60° apart and have equal magnitude $\frac{2}{3} V_{dc}$, forming a regular hexagon in the stationary αβ reference frame.

**Synthesis Principle:** The rotating reference vector $\vec{V}_{ref}$ at any instant lies in one of the six sectors. The SVPWM algorithm approximates $\vec{V}_{ref}$ by time-averaging the two adjacent active vectors (say V1 and V2 in Sector 1) and the zero vector V0. If the time spent on V1 is T1, on V2 is T2, and on V0 is T0, then for switching period $T_{sw}$:

$$
\vec{V}_{ref} \cdot T_{sw} = \vec{V}_1 \cdot T_1 + \vec{V}_2 \cdot T_2 + \vec{V}_0 \cdot T_0
$$

The sum of dwell times must equal the switching period:

$$
T_1 + T_2 + T_0 = T_{sw}
$$

This produces the PWM gate signals with the equivalent reference magnitude reaching up to $\frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3}}$ — a **15.47% boost** over SPWM.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $V_{dc} = 800$ V
- $V_{ref} = 350$ V (peak magnitude in αβ)
- $f_1 = 100$ Hz
- $f_{sw} = 20$ kHz
- Sector 1, $\theta = 30°$

**(i) Modulation Index:**

For SVPWM, the modulation index is defined as:

$$
m = \frac{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ref}}{V_{dc}}
$$

$$
m = \frac{\sqrt{3} \times 350}{800} = \frac{1.732 \times 350}{800} = \frac{606.2}{800} = 0.7578
$$

**Answer (i): m ≈ 0.758** (well within linear region, m_max = 1.1547)

**(ii) Frequency Modulation Ratio:**

$$
m_f = \frac{f_{sw}}{f_1} = \frac{20{,}000}{100} = 200
$$

**Answer (ii): m<sub>f</sub> = 200** (an integer multiple of 3, ideal for 3-phase systems)

**(iii) Dwell Times:**

The switching period is:

$$
T_{sw} = \frac{1}{f_{sw}} = \frac{1}{20{,}000} = 50 \text{ μs}
$$

For Sector 1, the dwell times are calculated using the standard SVPWM equations:

$$
T_1 = m \cdot T_{sw} \cdot \sin(60° - \theta)
$$

$$
T_2 = m \cdot T_{sw} \cdot \sin(\theta)
$$

$$
T_0 = T_{sw} - T_1 - T_2
$$

Substitute m = 0.7578, $T_{sw} = 50$ μs, $\theta = 30°$, so $(60° - \theta) = 30°$:

Since $\sin(30°) = 0.5$:

$$
T_1 = 0.7578 \times 50 \times 0.5 = 18.945 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
T_2 = 0.7578 \times 50 \times 0.5 = 18.945 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
T_0 = 50 - 18.945 - 18.945 = 12.11 \text{ μs}
$$

**Answer (iii): T1 = T2 ≈ 18.95 μs, T0 ≈ 12.11 μs**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU University Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the √3 factor** when converting phase voltage to line voltage. Students often write $V_{LL} = m_a \cdot V_{dc}/2$ (which is the PHASE voltage) and lose 2–3 marks.
> 
> 2. **Confusing the SPWM and SVPWM maximum voltage equations.** SPWM max phase voltage is $V_{dc}/2$; SVPWM max phase voltage is $V_{dc}/\sqrt{3}$. Using the wrong one in a numerical problem yields a wrong answer.
> 
> 3. **Using `|` (vertical bar) for absolute value in LaTeX within tables** — this BREAKS the markdown table. Always write `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting units** in numerical answers. Always specify V, Hz, μs, A, etc.
> 
> 5. **In SVPWM dwell time problems, students often forget that T1 + T2 + T0 = T<sub>sw</sub>**. Always verify: if T1 + T2 > T<sub>sw</sub>, you are in overmodulation and the linear equations are invalid.
> 
> 6. **Confusing switching frequency (f<sub>sw</sub>) with fundamental frequency (f<sub>1</sub>).** The two are completely different. m<sub>f</sub> = f<sub>sw</sub>/f<sub>1</sub> can be 100s or 1000s.
> 
> 7. **Drawing the SVPWM hexagon wrong** — vertices should be at 0°, 60°, 120°, 180°, 240°, 300°. Each active vector has length $\frac{2}{3} V_{dc}$.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Switching Frequency and PWM**
> 
> - **Switching Frequency (f<sub>sw</sub>)** is the number of ON/OFF cycles per second of inverter switches, typically **5–100 kHz** in EVs.
> - **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** is the technique of varying pulse widths to synthesize a desired AC waveform from DC.
> - **Modulation Index m<sub>a</sub>** = ratio of reference peak to carrier peak; controls output voltage amplitude.
> - **Frequency Modulation Ratio m<sub>f</sub>** = $f_{sw}/f_1$; should be an **odd multiple of 3** for 3-phase systems.
> - **SPWM max output:** $V_{LL,rms} \approx 0.612 \cdot m_a \cdot V_{dc}$ (linear region).
> - **SVPWM max output:** $V_{LL,rms} = m \cdot V_{dc}/\sqrt{2}$, with m up to **1.1547** — **15.47% more** voltage than SPWM.
> - **SVPWM** uses hexagon of 8 switching states (6 active + 2 zero) in αβ plane.
> - **SVPWM dwell time equations:** $T_1 = m T_{sw} \sin(60° - \theta)$, $T_2 = m T_{sw} \sin(\theta)$, $T_0 = T_{sw} - T_1 - T_2$.
> - **Current ripple** is **inversely proportional** to $L_s \cdot f_{sw}$.
> - **Switching losses** are **directly proportional** to $f_{sw}$.
> - **Trade-off:** higher f<sub>sw</sub> = smoother torque, less noise, smaller capacitors, but more losses and lower range.
> - **Si-IGBT** EV inverters: 5–20 kHz; **SiC-MOSFET** EV inverters: 20–100 kHz.
> - **Tesla, Lucid, Hyundai E-GMP** use SiC inverters with SVPWM for high efficiency (>97%).
> - **Always remember:** 1.1547 = 2/√3 ≈ 1 + 15.47% — the SVPWM advantage.
> - **For KTU numericals:** ALWAYS verify that m ≤ 1.0 for SPWM or m ≤ 1.1547 for SVPWM, otherwise you are in overmodulation/six-step mode.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Battery - Battery Parameters - Units of Battery Energy Storage - Capacity Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Battery - Battery Parameters - Units of Battery Energy Storage - Capacity Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Battery — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> An **electrochemical energy storage device** (battery) is a transducer that converts stored **chemical energy** into **electrical energy** through controlled **redox (oxidation-reduction) reactions** at two electrodes (anode and cathode) immersed in an electrolyte, releasing electrons to an external circuit.

In the context of **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**, the battery is the *primary energy buffer* between the grid (or renewable source) and the traction motor. It must satisfy three simultaneous demands:
1. **High energy density** (to maximize range per kg)
2. **High power density** (to support acceleration/regen)
3. **Long cycle life & safety** (to ensure commercial viability)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bank Account Model"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
> Think of a battery as a **water tank connected to a tap and a drain**.

| Component | Water Tank Equivalent | Battery Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Tank Size | Tank capacity (litres) | Battery **Capacity (Ah)** |
| Water Pressure | Pipe pressure (bar) | **Voltage (V)** |
| Total Energy Stored | Volume × Pressure | **Energy = V × Ah (Wh)** |
| Rate of Flow (tap open) | Litres/sec | **C-rate (discharge current)** |
| Remaining water | Water level (%) | **State of Charge (SoC %)** |
| Minimum water usable | Dead volume | **Depth of Discharge (DoD %)** |

Open the tap slowly → high energy, low power (long drive, slow acceleration).  
Open the tap fully → low energy, high power (short drive, fast acceleration / hill climb).  
A real EV battery must be a *tank that is simultaneously big AND allows fast flow* — which is why **energy density and power density** are the two key engineering metrics.

## 1.3 The Battery Family — First Glance

Batteries are broadly classified into two chemical families:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Non-Rechargeable (Primary Cells):** Single-use (e.g., Alkaline, Zn–MnO₂). **Not used in EVs.**
> 2. **Rechargeable (Secondary Cells):** Cyclable (e.g., Lead-Acid, NiCd, NiMH, Li-ion, Li-Po, LiFePO₄). **These are the EV candidates.**

Throughout Module 3, our focus is exclusively on **secondary (rechargeable) batteries** suitable for traction applications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Batteries (EV-Relevant Taxonomy)

### A. Lead-Acid Battery
- **Chemistry:** Pb (anode) ‖ H₂SO₄ (electrolyte) ‖ PbO₂ (cathode)
- **Cell Voltage:** 2.0 V (nominal)
- **Pros:** Cheap, recyclable, mature technology, high surge current.
- **Cons:** Low energy density (~30–40 Wh/kg), short cycle life (300–500), contains toxic **lead**.
- **EV Use:** Auxiliary 12 V supply, low-speed e-rickshaws, micro-EVs.

### B. Nickel-Cadmium (NiCd)
- **Chemistry:** Cd (anode) ‖ KOH (electrolyte) ‖ NiOOH (cathode)
- **Cell Voltage:** 1.2 V
- **Pros:** Robust, long cycle life (~2000), good low-temp performance.
- **Cons:** Toxic **cadmium**, memory effect, lower energy density (~50 Wh/kg).
- **EV Use:** Older EVs (e.g., early Peugeot EVs). Largely discontinued.

### C. Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH)
- **Chemistry:** MH (anode) ‖ KOH (electrolyte) ‖ NiOOH (cathode)
- **Cell Voltage:** 1.2 V
- **Pros:** Higher energy than NiCd (~70 Wh/kg), less toxic.
- **Cons:** High self-discharge (~30%/month), heat issues at high C-rates.
- **EV Use:** Toyota Prius (Gen 1–3), Honda Insight (hybrid vehicles).

### D. Lithium-Ion (Li-ion)
- **Chemistry:** Graphite (anode) ‖ LiPF₆ salt in organic carbonate ‖ LiCoO₂/LiNMC/LiFePO₄ (cathode)
- **Cell Voltage:** 3.2 – 3.7 V (depending on cathode chemistry)
- **Pros:** High energy density (~150–250 Wh/kg), high efficiency (~95–99%), low self-discharge, no memory effect.
- **Cons:** Thermal runaway risk, requires **BMS**, expensive cobalt.
- **EV Use:** Tesla, Tata Nexon EV, BYD, Hyundai Kona — **dominant EV chemistry today.**

### E. Lithium Iron Phosphate (LiFePO₄ / LFP)
- **Sub-type of Li-ion:** Cathode = LiFePO₄
- **Cell Voltage:** 3.2 V (nominal)
- **Pros:** Excellent thermal stability, >2000 cycles, cobalt-free, cheap.
- **Cons:** Lower energy density (~120 Wh/kg) vs NMC.
- **EV Use:** BYD Blade, MG ZS EV, Tesla Model 3 SR, e-buses.

### F. Lithium Polymer (Li-Po / LiPo)
- **Chemistry variant:** Gel/polymer electrolyte instead of liquid.
- **Pros:** Flexible form factor, light.
- **Cons:** Lower power density, swelling risk.
- **EV Use:** E-bikes, drones, some concept cars.

### G. Solid-State Batteries (Emerging)
- Solid ceramic/polymer electrolyte replacing liquid.
- **Pros:** Higher energy density (~400+ Wh/kg projected), non-flammable.
- **Status:** Pilot production (Toyota, QuantumScape) — expected post-2027.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Memory Trick — "LLN-SSS"**
> **L**ead-Acid → **L**ow density, cheap.
> **N**iMH / **N**iCd → "N"ickel based, 1.2 V.
> **S**tandard **L**i-ion → 3.7 V, dominant.
> **S**olid-**S**tate → future, safe.

## 2.2 Battery Parameters — The Engineering Quantities

A battery datasheet (e.g., a 3.7 V, 50 Ah NMC cell) contains the following key parameters. Each is critical for EV design:

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nominal Voltage | $V_{nom}$ | **V (Volt)** | Average operating voltage during discharge |
| 2 | Capacity | $C$ | **Ah (Ampere-hour)** | Total charge stored; 1 Ah = 3600 C |
| 3 | Nominal Energy | $E$ | **Wh or kWh** | $V \times C$ |
| 4 | C-rate | $C_{rate}$ | **h⁻¹** (e.g., 1C, 2C) | Discharge/charge current normalized to capacity |
| 5 | Energy Density (Gravimetric) | $\rho_E$ | **Wh/kg** | Energy per unit mass |
| 6 | Energy Density (Volumetric) | $\rho_{E,v}$ | **Wh/L** | Energy per unit volume |
| 7 | Power Density (Gravimetric) | $\rho_P$ | **W/kg** | Peak power per unit mass |
| 8 | Power Density (Volumetric) | $\rho_{P,v}$ | **W/L** | Peak power per unit volume |
| 9 | Internal Resistance | $R_{int}$ | **mΩ** | Opposition to current flow inside cell |
| 10 | State of Charge | $SoC$ | **% (0–100)** | Remaining capacity / rated capacity |
| 11 | Depth of Discharge | $DoD$ | **% (0–100)** | $DoD = 100\% - SoC$ |
| 12 | State of Health | $SoH$ | **% (0–100)** | Current max capacity / original capacity |
| 13 | Cycle Life | $N_{cyc}$ | **cycles** | Number of full charge-discharge cycles before SoH ≤ 80% |
| 14 | Self-Discharge Rate | $R_{sd}$ | **%/month** | Capacity loss when idle |
| 15 | Coulombic Efficiency | $\eta_C$ | **%** | $Q_{out}/Q_{in}$ per cycle |
| 16 | Energy Efficiency | $\eta_E$ | **%** | $E_{out}/E_{in}$ per cycle |
| 17 | Operating Temperature | $T$ | **°C** | Safe operating window |

## 2.3 Units of Battery Energy Storage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SI Derived Units (KTU Standard Notation):**

The fundamental electrical unit is the **Joule (J)**. However, in battery engineering, **watt-hour (Wh)** is preferred because 1 Wh = 3600 J, making the numbers tractable for kWh-scale EV packs.

| Unit | Symbol | Relation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coulomb | C | 1 C = 1 A·s | Charge (rare in datasheets) |
| Ampere-hour | Ah | 1 Ah = 3600 C | Battery capacity (cell-level) |
| Watt-hour | Wh | 1 Wh = 3600 J | Battery energy (cell-level) |
| Kilowatt-hour | kWh | 1 kWh = 1000 Wh | EV pack energy (vehicle-level) |
| Megawatt-hour | MWh | 1 MWh = 10⁶ Wh | Grid storage / fleet / e-truck |

### Critical Conversions

- **Energy from Capacity:**
$$E = V_{nom} \times C$$
where $E$ is in **Wh**, $V_{nom}$ in **V**, $C$ in **Ah**.

- **Capacity from Energy:**
$$C = \frac{E}{V_{nom}}$$

- **Ah ↔ kWh conversion (pack-level):**
$$E_{kWh} = \frac{V_{pack} \times C_{pack}}{1000}$$

## 2.4 Capacity Rate (C-Rate) — Definition & Significance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The **C-rate** is the **dimensionless** (or h⁻¹) measure of the rate at which a battery is charged or discharged, **relative to its rated capacity**. Mathematically:

$$C_{rate} = \frac{I}{C_{rated}}$$

where $I$ is the **current in Amperes (A)** and $C_{rated}$ is the **rated capacity in Ah**. Note: 1C means the battery is fully discharged in **1 hour**, 2C in **30 minutes**, and C/2 (0.5C) in **2 hours**.

### Key C-rate Properties

- **Time to fully discharge:**
$$t = \frac{1}{C_{rate}} \text{ hours}$$

- **Discharge current from C-rate:**
$$I = C_{rate} \times C_{rated}$$

- **Peukert's Law** (advanced — non-linear capacity at high C-rates):
$$I^{n} \cdot t = C_{p}$$
where $n$ is the **Peukert exponent** (typically 1.05–1.3 for lead-acid, ~1.0 for Li-ion) and $C_p$ is the Peukert capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why C-rate matters in EVs:**
> 1. **Peak acceleration** demands **5C–10C** discharge (e.g., a 50 Ah cell at 5C gives 250 A → ~90 kW from a 400 V pack).
> 2. **Regenerative braking** charges at **1C–3C** — too high C-rate degrades cells.
> 3. **Thermal management** must scale with $I^2 R_{int}$ losses.
> 4. Higher C-rates **reduce usable capacity** (Peukert effect, more pronounced in lead-acid).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CHEAT SHEET — Memorize for ESE:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = V \cdot C$ | Stored energy | Wh |
| 2 | $E_{kWh} = \dfrac{V \cdot C}{1000}$ | Pack energy in kWh | kWh |
| 3 | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I}{C_{rated}}$ | C-rate definition | h⁻¹ |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{1}{C_{rate}}$ | Time to full discharge | h |
| 5 | $I = C_{rate} \cdot C_{rated}$ | Discharge current | A |
| 6 | $P = V \cdot I = V \cdot C_{rate} \cdot C_{rated}$ | Instantaneous power | W |
| 7 | $\rho_E = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Gravimetric energy density | Wh/kg |
| 8 | $\rho_P = \dfrac{P_{max}}{m}$ | Gravimetric power density | W/kg |
| 9 | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 - \dfrac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_0^t I(\tau) \, d\tau$ | State of Charge | % |
| 10 | $DoD = 1 - SoC$ | Depth of Discharge | % |
| 11 | $SoH = \dfrac{C_{now}}{C_{rated,new}}$ | State of Health | % |
| 12 | $\eta_C = \dfrac{Q_{disch}}{Q_{ch}} \cdot 100\%$ | Coulombic efficiency | % |
| 13 | $I^{n} \cdot t = C_{p}$ | Peukert's law | — |
| 14 | $P_{loss} = I^{2} R_{int}$ | Ohmic loss | W |
| 15 | $V_{term} = V_{oc} - I R_{int}$ | Terminal voltage | V |

**Boundary conditions to remember:**
- $0 \leq SoC \leq 100\%$
- $0 \leq DoD \leq 100\%$, with $SoC + DoD = 100\%$
- $0.5C \leq C_{rate} \leq 1C$ for **rated (usable) capacity**.
- Lead-acid: recommended max DoD = **50%** (cycle life triples vs 80% DoD).
- Li-ion: usable DoD typically **80%** (BMS enforced).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy from Voltage and Capacity

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** Power is the rate of energy delivery, and energy is power integrated over time.

**Step 1 — Define electrical power:**
$$P = V \cdot I \quad \text{(watts)}$$

**Step 2 — Define energy as power × time:**
$$E = P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that $I \cdot t$ is charge transferred, which is the definition of capacity:**
$$Q = I \cdot t = C \quad \text{(in ampere-seconds if t in seconds, or Ah if t in hours)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute (assuming $V$ is constant at $V_{nom}$):**
$$E = V_{nom} \cdot C$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Wh using 1 Ah of charge delivered for 1 hour at voltage $V_{nom}$:**

$$E_{\text{Wh}} = V_{\text{nom}} \cdot C_{\text{Ah}} \quad \text{when } t = 1 \text{ hour}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kWh for pack-level reporting:**

$$E_{\text{kWh}} = \frac{V_{\text{pack}} \cdot C_{\text{pack}}}{1000}$$

**Interpretation:** This is the foundation of every battery specification. A 400 V, 100 Ah pack stores $E = 400 \times 100 = 40{,}000$ Wh = **40 kWh** — typical for a mid-size EV.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — C-Rate and Discharge Time

**Step 1 — Start with the definition:**
$$C_{rate} \equiv \frac{I}{C_{rated}} \quad \left[ \text{h}^{-1} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Express the time to fully discharge from a constant current $I$:**

$$t = \frac{Q_{total}}{I} = \frac{C_{rated}}{I}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $I = C_{rate} \cdot C_{rated}$ from the definition:**

$$t = \frac{C_{rated}}{C_{rate} \cdot C_{rated}} = \frac{1}{C_{rate}}$$

**Step 4 — Result:**

$$\boxed{\,t = \frac{1}{C_{rate}} \text{ hours}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examples:**
> - **1C** → $t = 1$ h
> - **2C** → $t = 0.5$ h = 30 min
> - **0.5C** → $t = 2$ h
> - **10C** → $t = 0.1$ h = 6 min (e.g., peak EV acceleration burst)

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Peukert's Law and Effective Capacity

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this needed?** Real batteries deliver **less than rated capacity** at high discharge currents due to internal losses and kinetic limitations. Peukert (1897) quantified this for lead-acid cells.

**Step 1 — Empirical observation:** $I \cdot t \neq C_{rated}$ at high $I$.

**Step 2 — Postulate a power-law relationship:**
$$I^{n} \cdot t = C_{p} = \text{constant}$$

where $n$ ≥ 1 is the **Peukert exponent**.

**Step 3 — Solve for the discharge time:**
$$t = \frac{C_{p}}{I^{n}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the actual capacity delivered at current $I$:**
$$C_{actual} = I \cdot t = \frac{C_{p}}{I^{n-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Compare to the rated capacity at the rated current $I_{rated}$:**
$$C_{rated} = \frac{C_{p}}{I_{rated}^{\,n-1}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_{p} = C_{rated} \cdot I_{rated}^{\,n-1}$$

**Step 6 — Final form — actual capacity at any discharge current $I$:**

$$\boxed{\,C_{actual} = C_{rated} \cdot \left(\frac{I_{rated}}{I}\right)^{n-1}\,}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> A 100 Ah lead-acid battery has a Peukert exponent $n = 1.25$ and is rated at the **20-hour rate** ($I_{rated} = 5$ A). Find the actual capacity if discharged at $I = 50$ A.

**Solution:**

$$C_{actual} = 100 \cdot \left(\frac{5}{50}\right)^{1.25-1} = 100 \cdot (0.1)^{0.25}$$

$$(0.1)^{0.25} = 10^{-0.25} = 0.5623$$

$$C_{actual} = 100 \times 0.5623 = 56.23 \text{ Ah}$$

**Interpretation:** At 50 A (10C), the 100 Ah battery only delivers ~56 Ah — a **44% capacity loss** compared to the slow 20-hour rating. This is why EV battery packs must be sized for C-rates well below their peak surge capability.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Efficiency and Coulombic Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define Coulombic (ampere-hour) efficiency:**
$$\eta_C = \frac{Q_{discharged}}{Q_{charged}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Define energy efficiency (accounts for voltage drop on discharge vs charge):**
$$\eta_E = \frac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} = \frac{V_{disch} \cdot Q_{disch}}{V_{ch} \cdot Q_{ch}} = \eta_C \cdot \frac{V_{disch}}{V_{ch}}$$

**Step 3 — For Li-ion (approximate):**
- $\eta_C \approx 99\%$
- $V_{disch} \approx 3.6$ V, $V_{ch} \approx 3.9$ V
- $\eta_E \approx 0.99 \times \dfrac{3.6}{3.9} \approx 91\%$

**Step 4 — For lead-acid:**
- $\eta_C \approx 85\%$
- $\eta_E \approx 70\text{–}80\%$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A typical EV round-trip (charge → drive → regen) wastes ~10–15% of the input electrical energy as heat. The BMS must dump this heat via liquid cooling.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Battery Chemistries for EVs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Ready Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Lead-Acid | NiCd | NiMH | **Li-ion (NMC)** | **LiFePO₄** | Solid-State (projected) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cell Voltage (V) | 2.0 | 1.2 | 1.2 | 3.6–3.7 | 3.2 | 3.7+ |
| Energy Density (Wh/kg) | 30–40 | 50 | 70 | 150–250 | 90–140 | 350–500 |
| Power Density (W/kg) | 180 | 150 | 200 | 500–2000 | 800–1800 | 1000+ |
| Cycle Life | 300–500 | 1500–2000 | 500–1000 | 1000–3000 | 2000–5000 | 5000+ |
| Self-Discharge (%/mo) | 4–6 | 15–20 | 25–30 | 1–3 | 1–3 | <1 |
| Memory Effect | No | **Yes** | Mild | No | No | No |
| Thermal Runaway Risk | Low | Low | Moderate | **High** | **Low** | Very Low |
| Cost (USD/kWh) | 100–150 | 250 | 300 | 120–140 | 90–110 | >400 |
| Toxicity | High (Pb) | High (Cd) | Low | Low | Low | Low |
| Recycling | Excellent | Good | Good | Moderate | Good | TBD |
| Typical EV Use | Aux 12V | Legacy | Prius Gen1-3 | Tesla, Nexon | BYD, e-bus | 2027+ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Battery Classification Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Batteries]:::root --> A1[Primary Non-Rechargeable]
    A0 --> A2[Secondary Rechargeable]
    A1 --> A1a[Alkaline Zn-MnO2]
    A1 --> A1b[Lithium Primary]
    A2 --> A2a[Aqueous Electrolyte]
    A2 --> A2b[Non-Aqueous Electrolyte]
    A2a --> A2a1[Lead-Acid Pb-PbO2]
    A2a --> A2a2[Nickel Based NiCd]
    A2a --> A2a3[Nickel Based NiMH]
    A2b --> A2b1[Li-ion NMC NCA LCO]
    A2b --> A2b2[LiFePO4 LFP]
    A2b --> A2b3[Li-Polymer Gel Electrolyte]
    A2b --> A2b4[Solid-State Ceramic]
    A2a1 --> EV1[Aux 12V E-Rickshaw]
    A2a2 --> EV2[Legacy EVs 1990s]
    A2a3 --> EV3[Hybrid Prius Gen1-3]
    A2b1 --> EV4[Modern BEVs Tesla Nexon]
    A2b2 --> EV5[BYD E-Bus Tesla LFP]
    A2b3 --> EV6[Concept E-Bikes]
    A2b4 --> EV7[Future Post-2027]

```

## 4.2 Battery Parameter Functional Architecture

```mermaid

        I1[Vnom 3.7V]
        I2[C_rated 50Ah]
        I3[R_int 25 mOhm]
    end
    subgraph CORE["Derived Battery Parameters"]
        D1[Energy E = V C 185 Wh]
        D2[C-rate C_r = I over C_rated]
        D3[SoC Integration]
        D4[Thermal Losses I2R]
        D5[Peukert Effective Capacity]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["EV Design Outputs"]
        O1[Pack Energy kWh]
        O2[Range km]
        O3[Peak Power kW]
        O4[Cycle Life Cycles]
        O5[Cooling Load W]
    end
    I1 --> D1
    I2 --> D1
    I2 --> D2
    I1 --> D3
    I2 --> D3
    I2 --> D5
    I3 --> D4
    D1 --> O1
    D1 --> O2
    D2 --> O3
    D2 --> O5
    D3 --> O4
    D4 --> O5
    D5 --> O4
    classDef inp fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    classDef cor fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:1px

    class I1,I2,I3 inp
    class D1,D2,D3,D4,D5 cor
    class O1,O2,O3,O4,O5 out
```

## 4.3 C-Rate Visual Scale

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Rate Selector]:::root --> R1[0.1C 10h Slow Overnight]
    R0 --> R2[0.5C 2h Standard Drive]
    R0 --> R3[1C 1h Aggressive Drive]
    R0 --> R4[2C 30min Fast Discharge]
    R0 --> R5[5C 12min Peak Acceleration]
    R0 --> R6[10C 6min Burst Maximum]
    R3 --> RSOC[SoC Drops Linearly with I]
    R4 --> RP[Peukert Loss Active]
    R5 --> RTH[Thermal Limit Reached]
    R6 --> RDG[Degradation Risk BMS Trip]

```

```mermaid
    P --> P2[Thermal]

    P1 --> P1b[Capacity Ah]
    P1 --> P1c[C-rate hinv]

    P2 --> P2b[Thermal Runaway C]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term C-rate of a battery. A 60 Ah Li-ion cell is discharged at 2C. Calculate the discharge current and time to full discharge.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** C-rate is the ratio of the discharge/charge current to the rated capacity of the battery. A rate of $n$C means the battery is fully discharged in $1/n$ hours.
>
> **Calculation (2 marks):**
>
> $$I = C_{rate} \times C_{rated} = 2 \times 60 = 120 \text{ A}$$
>
> $$t = \frac{1}{C_{rate}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ h} = 30 \text{ minutes}$$
>
> **Final Answer:** $I = 120$ A, $t = 30$ min.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six important parameters used to characterize a battery for EV applications.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**
>
> 1. **Nominal Voltage** ($V_{nom}$) — average operating voltage per cell.
> 2. **Capacity** ($C$ in Ah) — total charge storable.
> 3. **Energy Density** (Wh/kg) — energy per unit mass.
> 4. **Power Density** (W/kg) — peak power per unit mass.
> 5. **C-rate** — current normalized to capacity.
> 6. **Cycle Life** — number of full charge-discharge cycles before capacity drops to 80%.
> 7. *(Bonus ½ mark)* State of Charge (SoC) or Depth of Discharge (DoD).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final numerical answer → **−1 mark** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing Ah with kWh** — these are *charge* and *energy* respectively; never interconvert without voltage.
> 3. **Skipping the C-rate definition** before using it numerically → −1 mark.
> 4. **Writing Peukert's law without specifying the exponent $n$** → −1 mark.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., constant voltage, no temperature effects) → up to −2 marks in derivation problems.
> 6. **Comparing chemistries without a table** → examiners award only 50% of comparison marks.
> 7. **Missing the difference between gravimetric and volumetric density** → common 2-mark loss.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

#### **(a) [7 marks]** Classify the major types of rechargeable batteries used in electric vehicles. Compare their cell voltage, energy density, cycle life, and thermal safety in a tabular form. Identify which chemistry is most suitable for a high-performance passenger EV and justify your answer.

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **Classification (2 marks):**
>
> Rechargeable batteries used in EVs are classified as:
>
> 1. **Aqueous-electrolyte batteries:** Lead-Acid, NiCd, NiMH.
> 2. **Non-aqueous (lithium-based) batteries:** Li-ion (NMC, NCA, LCO), LiFePO₄, Li-Polymer, Solid-State.
>
> **Comparison Table (3 marks):**
>
> | Chemistry | $V_{cell}$ (V) | Energy Density (Wh/kg) | Cycle Life | Thermal Safety |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | Lead-Acid | 2.0 | 30–40 | 300–500 | High (no runaway) |
> | NiCd | 1.2 | 50 | 1500–2000 | High |
> | NiMH | 1.2 | 70 | 500–1000 | Moderate |
> | Li-ion (NMC) | 3.6–3.7 | 150–250 | 1000–3000 | Low (runaway risk) |
> | LiFePO₄ | 3.2 | 90–140 | 2000–5000 | Very High |
> | Solid-State | 3.7+ | 350–500 (projected) | 5000+ | Very High |
>
> **Suitability + Justification (2 marks):**
>
> **NMC Li-ion** is most suitable for **high-performance passenger EVs** (e.g., Tesla Model 3, Hyundai Ioniq 5) because:
> - Highest **gravimetric energy density (≥200 Wh/kg)** → longer range per kg.
> - High **power density (≥1500 W/kg)** → supports fast acceleration.
> - High **cell voltage (3.7 V)** → fewer cells required in series for a 400 V pack.
> - Mature commercial supply chain and BMS ecosystem.
> - *Trade-off:* Requires active thermal management (liquid cooling) due to thermal-runaway risk.
>
> *(Note: For e-buses, LFP is preferred for safety; for city e-rickshaws, lead-acid remains cost-effective.)*

#### **(b) [7 marks]** A battery pack has the following specification: Nominal Voltage = 360 V, Capacity = 100 Ah, Cell voltage = 3.6 V, Cell capacity = 50 Ah. Determine:
1. The number of cells in series and in parallel.
2. The total pack energy in kWh.
3. The discharge current and time if the pack is discharged at 1C, 2C, and 0.5C rates.
4. The peak power deliverable at 2C discharge.

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **(1) Number of cells — Series & Parallel (2 marks):**
>
> Cells in series (to achieve pack voltage):
>
> $$N_s = \frac{V_{pack}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{360}{3.6} = 100 \text{ cells in series}$$
>
> Cells in parallel (to achieve pack capacity):
>
> $$N_p = \frac{C_{pack}}{C_{cell}} = \frac{100}{50} = 2 \text{ cells in parallel per series string}$$
>
> Total cells = $N_s \times N_p = 100 \times 2 = 200$ cells.
>
> **(2) Pack energy in kWh (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times C_{pack} = 360 \times 100 = 36{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 36 \text{ kWh}$$
>
> **(3) Discharge currents and times at various C-rates (2.5 marks):**
>
> Using $I = C_{rate} \times C_{pack}$ and $t = \dfrac{1}{C_{rate}}$:
>
> | C-rate | Current $I$ (A) | Time $t$ (h) | Time (min) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 0.5C | 50 | 2.0 | 120 |
> | 1C | 100 | 1.0 | 60 |
> | 2C | 200 | 0.5 | 30 |
>
> **(4) Peak power at 2C (1 mark):**
>
> $$P_{peak} = V_{pack} \times I_{2C} = 360 \times 200 = 72{,}000 \text{ W} = 72 \text{ kW}$$
>
> **Interpretation:** A 36 kWh pack delivering 72 kW peak is a typical mid-size EV spec (≈300 km range, 0–100 km/h in ~8 s).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

#### **(a) [7 marks]** With neat sketches and equations, explain the following battery parameters: (i) State of Charge (SoC), (ii) Depth of Discharge (DoD), (iii) State of Health (SoH), (iv) Internal Resistance, (v) Coulombic Efficiency, (vi) Energy Density, (vii) Self-Discharge Rate.

> **Model Solution (7 marks — 1 mark each):**
>
> **(i) State of Charge (SoC):**
> $$SoC(t) = SoC_0 - \frac{1}{C_{rated}} \int_0^t I(\tau) \, d\tau$$
> The remaining capacity expressed as a percentage of rated capacity. $SoC = 100\%$ is full; $0\%$ is empty.
>
> **(ii) Depth of Discharge (DoD):**
> $$DoD = (1 - SoC) \times 100\%$$
> The complement of SoC. Li-ion EVs typically restrict $DoD \le 80\%$ to extend cycle life.
>
> **(iii) State of Health (SoH):**
> $$SoH = \frac{C_{now}}{C_{rated,new}} \times 100\%$$
> Reflects aging. EV warranty typically ends at $SoH = 70\text{–}80\%$.
>
> **(iv) Internal Resistance ($R_{int}$):**
> $$V_{term} = V_{oc} - I R_{int}$$
> Opposition to current flow inside the cell, typically a few mΩ for Li-ion. Causes $I^2 R_{int}$ heat loss and voltage sag under load.
>
> **(v) Coulombic Efficiency:**
> $$\eta_C = \frac{Q_{discharged}}{Q_{charged}} \times 100\%$$
> ~99% for Li-ion, ~85% for lead-acid. Side reactions (gas, SEI growth) reduce $\eta_C$.
>
> **(vi) Energy Density:**
> - **Gravimetric:** $\rho_E = E/m$ in Wh/kg.
> - **Volumetric:** $\rho_{E,v} = E/V$ in Wh/L.
> A higher $\rho_E$ means lighter battery for a given range.
>
> **(vii) Self-Discharge Rate:**
> The percentage of capacity lost per month when the battery is idle. ~1–3%/month for Li-ion vs ~25–30%/month for NiMH.

#### **(b) [7 marks]** A 200 Ah lead-acid traction battery (Peukert exponent $n = 1.30$, rated at the 20-hour rate) is used in a small delivery EV. The average discharge current during a delivery route is 40 A. Calculate: (i) The rated current, (ii) The actual capacity delivered, (iii) The discharge time available, (iv) The energy delivered if the average pack voltage is 48 V, and (v) Comment on whether LiFePO₄ would be a better choice for this application.

> **Model Solution (7 marks):**
>
> **(i) Rated current (1 mark):**
> At 20-hour rate:
> $$I_{rated} = \frac{C_{rated}}{20} = \frac{200}{20} = 10 \text{ A}$$
>
> **(ii) Actual capacity at 40 A (2 marks):**
> Using Peukert:
> $$C_{actual} = C_{rated} \cdot \left(\frac{I_{rated}}{I}\right)^{n-1} = 200 \cdot \left(\frac{10}{40}\right)^{1.30-1}$$
>
> $$= 200 \cdot (0.25)^{0.30}$$
>
> $$\log(0.25) = -0.6021; \quad 0.30 \times (-0.6021) = -0.1806$$
>
> $$(0.25)^{0.30} = 10^{-0.1806} = 0.6607$$
>
> $$C_{actual} = 200 \times 0.6607 = 132.1 \text{ Ah}$$
>
> **(iii) Discharge time at 40 A (1 mark):**
> $$t = \frac{C_{actual}}{I} = \frac{132.1}{40} = 3.30 \text{ h} = 198 \text{ min}$$
>
> **(iv) Energy delivered (1 mark):**
> $$E = V_{avg} \times C_{actual} = 48 \times 132.1 = 6341 \text{ Wh} \approx 6.34 \text{ kWh}$$
>
> **(v) Comment on LiFePO₄ (2 marks):**
> LiFePO₄ would be a **significantly better** choice for this delivery EV because:
> - **No Peukert effect** ($n \approx 1.0$) → delivers nearly full rated capacity even at high currents.
> - **Higher usable DoD** (up to 80% vs 50% for lead-acid) → smaller pack needed.
> - **Cycle life 4–10× longer** (2000–5000 vs 300–500 cycles) → lower lifetime cost despite higher upfront cost.
> - **Lower weight** (~120 Wh/kg vs 35 Wh/kg) → less payload loss.
> - **No toxic lead** → environmentally cleaner, easier end-of-life recycling.
> - *Trade-off:* Higher initial capital cost, requires BMS for cell balancing.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3 / Topic: Battery Types, Parameters, Units & C-Rate**

- **Battery (KTU Definition):** Electrochemical device converting chemical → electrical energy via redox reactions.
- **Primary vs Secondary:** Only **secondary (rechargeable)** cells are used in EVs.
- **Six EV-relevant chemistries:** Lead-Acid, NiCd, NiMH, Li-ion (NMC/NCA/LCO), LiFePO₄, Solid-State.
- **Dominant EV chemistry (2024):** **NMC Li-ion** for performance cars; **LFP** for buses & entry-level cars.
- **Cell voltage memories:** Pb = **2.0 V**, Ni-based = **1.2 V**, Li-ion = **3.6–3.7 V**, LFP = **3.2 V**.
- **Energy = Voltage × Capacity** (Wh). **1 kWh = 1000 Wh.**
- **Capacity unit = Ah** (1 Ah = 3600 C). **Energy unit = Wh** (1 Wh = 3600 J).
- **C-rate = I / C_rated**; **Time = 1 / C-rate** (hours).
- **1C** = 1 h discharge; **2C** = 30 min; **0.5C** = 2 h; **10C** = 6 min (peak burst).
- **SoC + DoD = 100%** (always).
- **SoH = (C_now / C_new) × 100%** — battery aging indicator.
- **Peukert's Law:** $I^n \cdot t = C_p$; for lead-acid $n \approx 1.2$, for Li-ion $n \approx 1.0$.
- **Higher C-rate → less usable capacity** (Peukert effect, significant in lead-acid).
- **Internal resistance** causes voltage sag $V_{term} = V_{oc} - IR_{int}$ and heat loss $P = I^2 R_{int}$.
- **Coulombic efficiency** Li-ion ≈ 99%, lead-acid ≈ 85%.
- **Cycle life order:** Solid-State > LFP > NMC > NiMH > NiCd > Lead-Acid.
- **Energy density order:** Solid-State > NMC > NCA > LCO > LFP > NiMH > NiCd > Lead-Acid.
- **Thermal safety order (best to worst):** Solid-State ≈ LFP > Lead-Acid > NiMH > NiCd > NMC (NMC is most volatile).
- **EV pack voltage range:** 400 V (standard) or 800 V (Porsche Taycan, Hyundai E-GMP, modern fast-charge platforms).
- **Typical EV pack energy:** 30–100 kWh (small car to long-range sedan).
- **Operating DoD window:** Lead-acid 50% (recommended), Li-ion 80% (BMS-enforced).
- **Voltage-sag (key exam insight):** Under high C-rate, terminal voltage drops due to $IR_{int}$ — affecting available power.
- **Why both energy AND power density matter:** Energy = "how far"; Power = "how fast". A golf cart needs high energy, low power; a sports EV needs both high.
- **Solid-state is the future (2027+):** Promises >400 Wh/kg, non-flammable, eliminates liquid electrolyte leakage.

> [!NOTE]
> **📌 One-Line Memory Hook for the Board Exam:**
> *"A battery is a **V**oltage × **A**mpere-hour **W**att-hour storage; its **C-rate** tells you **how fast** you empty it, and its **SoH** tells you **how tired** it is."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cell Voltage - Specific Energy - Cycle Life - Self-Discharge - Static Battery Equivalent Circuit Model - Series-Parallel Battery Pack Equivalent Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cell Voltage - Specific Energy - Cycle Life - Self-Discharge - Static Battery Equivalent Circuit Model - Series-Parallel Battery Pack Equivalent Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cell Voltage (V_cell)

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Cell Voltage is the nominal potential difference between the positive and negative terminals of a single electrochemical cell under standard reference conditions. It is determined by the difference in electrochemical potential of the cathode and anode half-reactions and represents the fundamental unit of electrical energy storage in a battery.

For a cell using two active materials A and B with half-reaction potentials $\phi_A^+$ and $\phi_B^-$:

$$V_{cell} = \phi_A^{+} - \phi_B^{-} \;\; [Volts]$$

Common nominal cell voltages used in EV batteries:
- **Lead-Acid**: **2.0 V** per cell
- **NiMH**: **1.2 V** per cell
- **Lithium-Ion (NMC)**: **3.6 - 3.7 V** per cell
- **Lithium Iron Phosphate (LFP)**: **3.2 - 3.3 V** per cell
- **Solid-State Lithium**: **3.7 - 4.0 V** per cell

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Highlight: Cell voltage is **NOT** a fixed DC value in practice. It varies with State of Charge (SOC), temperature, current draw (C-rate), and aging. The "nominal voltage" is a standardized midpoint used for system design.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of cell voltage as the "water pressure" produced by a single water pump. Just as a single pump pushes water at a fixed pressure regardless of how much water is in the tank (until it runs dry), a single electrochemical cell generates a characteristic voltage determined by its chemistry. To lift water to a higher floor, you stack multiple pumps in series. To fill a bigger tank, you put multiple pumps in parallel.

---

## 1.2 Specific Energy (E_spec)

**Definition:**
Specific Energy is the amount of electrical energy stored per unit mass (gravimetric) or per unit volume (volumetric) of a battery cell. It is the single most important metric for EV range determination.

$$E_{spec,grav} = \frac{E_{stored}}{m_{cell}} \;\; \left[\frac{Wh}{kg}\right]$$

$$E_{spec,vol} = \frac{E_{stored}}{Vol_{cell}} \;\; \left[\frac{Wh}{L}\right]$$

Typical values:
- **Lead-Acid**: 30-50 Wh/kg
- **NiMH**: 60-120 Wh/kg
- **Li-ion (NMC)**: 150-250 Wh/kg
- **Li-ion (LFP)**: 90-160 Wh/kg
- **Solid-State (theoretical)**: 300-500 Wh/kg

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Specific energy is like the "fuel efficiency rating" of the battery. Just as a car's mileage (km/L) tells you how far it travels per liter of fuel, specific energy (km/kg-equivalent in Wh/kg) tells you how much range your EV battery can deliver per kilogram of battery weight. Higher specific energy = lighter battery for the same range.

---

## 1.3 Cycle Life (N_cycle)

**Definition:**
Cycle Life is the number of complete charge-discharge cycles a battery can perform before its usable capacity drops to a defined end-of-life threshold (typically **80% of rated capacity** for EV applications, or 70% for grid storage).

$$N_{cycle} = \left\{n \;\Big|\; C(n) = 0.8 \cdot C_{rated}\right\}$$

Where $C(n)$ is the capacity after $n$ complete cycles.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Cycle life is the "warranty mileage" of a battery. Just as an engine is rated for a certain number of kilometers before requiring major overhaul, a battery cell is rated for a certain number of charge-discharge cycles before it has lost 20% of its storage capacity. One full cycle = one full charge from 0% to 100% + one full discharge from 100% to 0%.

---

## 1.4 Self-Discharge (SD)

**Definition:**
Self-Discharge is the spontaneous loss of stored charge in a battery when it is left idle (no external load connected), caused by internal parasitic chemical reactions, electrolyte decomposition, and internal current leakage paths. It is expressed as a percentage of capacity lost per unit time.

$$SD\% = \frac{Q_{lost}}{Q_{rated}} \cdot \frac{1}{t} \cdot 100\% \;\; \left[\frac{\%}{month}\right]$$

Typical monthly self-discharge rates at 25°C:
- **Lead-Acid**: 4-6 %/month
- **NiMH**: 1-3 %/month
- **Li-ion**: 0.35-2.5 %/month
- **LFP**: 1-3 %/month

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Self-discharge is the "slow leak" of a balloon. Even when you tie a knot in a helium balloon, the gas slowly seeps out through the rubber over days. Similarly, even when disconnected from any load, a battery slowly loses its charge through internal chemical reactions that bypass the external circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> Self-discharge rate **doubles approximately for every 10°C increase** in temperature (Arrhenius behavior). This is critical for EV battery thermal management design.

---

## 1.5 Static Battery Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM)

**Definition:**
A static (or "DC") battery equivalent circuit represents a real battery cell as a combination of ideal electrical elements (voltage sources, resistors) that capture its steady-state terminal behavior — open-circuit voltage, ohmic voltage drop, and terminal voltage under load. The most fundamental static model is the **R-int model** (also called the Thévenin-equivalent or simple battery model).

Components of the Static Model:
1. **V_oc** — Open-circuit voltage source (depends on SOC and temperature)
2. **R_int** — Internal ohmic resistance (depends on SOC, temperature, aging)
3. **V_t** — Terminal voltage (the voltage actually delivered to the load)

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Imagine a real water tank with a height of water (representing V_oc, the "ideal" energy level) and a partially clogged outlet pipe (representing R_int, the internal resistance that "wastes" some of the energy as heat). The actual water pressure at the tap (V_t) is always slightly less than the tank's full pressure because some pressure is lost overcoming the clog.

---

## 1.6 Series-Parallel Battery Pack Equivalent Circuits

**Definition:**
A battery pack for EV applications is constructed by connecting many individual cells in series-parallel combinations to achieve desired pack-level voltage and energy capacity. The equivalent circuit of the pack is the lumped representation showing how the cell-level ECMs combine.

Let:
- $n_s$ = number of cells in series (a string)
- $n_p$ = number of parallel branches (cells per string)
- Total cells = $n_s \cdot n_p$

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a battery pack as a "team" of water pumps. To get higher pressure (voltage), you chain pumps in series. To get higher flow (current/capacity), you put multiple pumps side-by-side in parallel. A real EV pack is a grid of many such pumps (Tesla Model S pack has thousands of 18650 cells in 96s74p configuration!).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cell Voltage - Deep Analysis

**The "Why" behind cell voltage:**
The voltage of a cell arises from the Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G$) of the electrochemical reaction. For a cell reaction transferring $z$ electrons per mole:

$$V_{cell} = -\frac{\Delta G}{z \cdot F} \;\; [Volts]$$

Where:
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96485 C/mol**
- $z$ = number of electrons transferred per reaction
- $\Delta G$ = Gibbs free energy change [J/mol]

**Voltage behavior during discharge (Li-ion example):**
The cell voltage is not constant. It follows an S-curve based on SOC:
- At very low SOC (< 10%): voltage drops sharply
- At mid SOC (20-80%): voltage is relatively flat (nominal region)
- At high SOC (> 90%): voltage rises to maximum

**Open Circuit vs Terminal Voltage:**

$$V_{oc}(SOC, T) = \text{Nernst Equation Result}$$

$$V_{t} = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$$

Where I is the discharge current (positive for discharge).

**The "How" - Factors affecting V_cell:**
1. **State of Charge (SOC)**: Lower SOC → lower V_oc
2. **Temperature**: Cold → higher internal resistance, slightly lower V_oc
3. **C-rate**: Higher discharge current → larger voltage sag
4. **Aging (Cycle count)**: Aged cells show higher R_int and lower V_oc

---

## 2.2 Specific Energy - Deep Analysis

**Energy stored in a single cell:**

$$E_{cell} = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{cell} \;\; [Wh]$$

Where $Q_{cell}$ is the cell capacity in Ampere-hours (Ah).

**Gravimetric Specific Energy:**

$$E_{spec,g} = \frac{V_{nom} \cdot Q_{cell}}{m_{cell}} = \frac{V_{nom} \cdot Q_{cell}}{m_{cell}} \;\; \left[\frac{Wh}{kg}\right]$$

**Volumetric Specific Energy:**

$$E_{spec,v} = \frac{V_{nom} \cdot Q_{cell}}{Vol_{cell}} \;\; \left[\frac{Wh}{L}\right]$$

**Why is this critical for EVs?**
- **Range anxiety** depends directly on pack-level specific energy
- A 75 kWh Tesla pack at 250 Wh/kg → 300 kg of batteries
- A 75 kWh pack at 150 Wh/kg → 500 kg of batteries (worse vehicle dynamics)

---

## 2.3 Cycle Life - Deep Analysis

**Empirical Peukert-Aging Relationship:**
Cycle life depends on depth-of-discharge (DOD), C-rate, and temperature. A widely used empirical model:

$$N_{cycle}(DOD) = N_{cycle,100\%} \cdot \left(\frac{100\%}{DOD}\right)^{k}$$

Where:
- $N_{cycle,100\%}$ = cycle life at 100% DOD (e.g., 1000 cycles)
- $DOD$ = depth of discharge
- $k$ = empirical exponent (typically 1.4-2.0 for Li-ion)

**Why this matters:** Discharging only to 50% DOD dramatically extends cycle life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A cell rated for 1000 cycles at 100% DOD may deliver 3000-5000 cycles if limited to 50% DOD. This is the principle behind EV Battery Management Systems (BMS) that deliberately restrict usable SOC window (e.g., 20%-80%) to extend pack life.

---

## 2.4 Self-Discharge - Deep Analysis

**Mathematical model (first-order decay):**

$$Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda \cdot t}$$

Where:
- $Q(t)$ = remaining capacity at time t
- $Q_0$ = initial capacity
- $\lambda$ = self-discharge rate constant [1/time]

**Relationship to shelf-life:**
A battery is considered "dead on shelf" when SOC drops below a threshold (typically 5-10%). The shelf life $t_{shelf}$ is:

$$t_{shelf} = \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{Q_0}{Q_{min}}\right) \;\; [days]$$

**Why it matters for EVs:**
- An EV parked for 3 months still needs to start
- Self-discharge in cold climates is much lower (good for storage)
- Self-discharge in hot climates can be 4-5× higher (bad for parked cars in desert)

---

## 2.5 Static Equivalent Circuit Model (R-int Model) - Deep Analysis

**The complete R-int model equation:**

$$V_{t} = V_{oc}(SOC, T) - I \cdot R_{int}(SOC, T, Age)$$

**Component interpretation:**
- **V_oc**: Thermodynamic equilibrium voltage, determined by Nernst equation
- **R_int**: Sum of:
  - Electrolyte ionic resistance
  - Electrode-electrolyte interface (charge transfer) resistance
  - Current collector resistance
  - SEI (Solid Electrolyte Interphase) layer resistance
- **V_t**: The actual voltage available to the EV motor/inverter

**Limitations of static model:**
- Does NOT capture transient response (RC dynamics)
- Does NOT capture diffusion overpotentials
- Only valid for steady-state or slowly varying loads
- For dynamic models → Thevenin model (with RC parallel branches) is used

**Equivalent circuit diagram (conceptual):**

```
  o----[ V_oc ]----[ R_int ]----o
  +                                -
  |----------- V_t ---------------|
```

---

## 2.6 Series-Parallel Pack Equivalent Circuits - Deep Analysis

**Series connection of $n_s$ cells:**

For $n_s$ identical cells in series, the ECM becomes:

$$V_{oc,pack} = n_s \cdot V_{oc,cell}$$

$$R_{int,pack} = n_s \cdot R_{int,cell}$$

$$Q_{pack,series} = Q_{cell} \;\; (capacity\;unchanged)$$

**Parallel connection of $n_p$ cells:**

For $n_p$ identical cells in parallel, the ECM becomes:

$$V_{oc,parallel} = V_{oc,cell} \;\; (voltage\;unchanged)$$

$$R_{int,parallel} = \frac{R_{int,cell}}{n_p}$$

$$Q_{parallel} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell}$$

**Series-Parallel ($n_s \times n_p$ pack):**

$$V_{oc,pack} = n_s \cdot V_{oc,cell}$$

$$R_{int,pack} = \frac{n_s}{n_p} \cdot R_{int,cell}$$

$$Q_{pack} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell}$$

$$E_{pack} = V_{oc,pack} \cdot Q_{pack} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot V_{oc,cell} \cdot Q_{cell}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight**: Adding cells in parallel REDUCES the effective internal resistance of the pack. This is why high-performance EVs use high $n_p$ configurations — to handle the massive instantaneous current demands during acceleration.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Cell Voltage (Nernst) | $V_{cell} = -\Delta G / (zF)$ | V | Thermodynamic ideal voltage |
| 2 | Cell Energy | $E_{cell} = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{cell}$ | Wh | Energy per cell |
| 3 | Specific Energy (gravimetric) | $E_{spec,g} = (V_{nom} Q_{cell}) / m_{cell}$ | Wh/kg | Key EV metric |
| 4 | Specific Energy (volumetric) | $E_{spec,v} = (V_{nom} Q_{cell}) / Vol_{cell}$ | Wh/L | Volume-constrained design |
| 5 | Self-Discharge Rate | $SD\% = (Q_{lost}/Q_{rated}) \cdot (1/t) \cdot 100$ | %/month | At reference 25°C |
| 6 | Capacity Decay (time) | $Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Ah | First-order model |
| 7 | Cycle Life vs DOD | $N(DOD) = N_{100} \cdot (100/DOD)^k$ | cycles | Empirical (Peukert-like) |
| 8 | Static ECM Terminal V | $V_t = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$ | V | R-int model |
| 9 | Pack Voltage | $V_{oc,pack} = n_s \cdot V_{oc,cell}$ | V | Series connection |
| 10 | Pack Capacity | $Q_{pack} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell}$ | Ah | Parallel connection |
| 11 | Pack Internal R | $R_{int,pack} = (n_s/n_p) \cdot R_{int,cell}$ | $\Omega$ | Series-parallel combo |
| 12 | Pack Energy | $E_{pack} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot V_{cell} \cdot Q_{cell}$ | Wh | Full configuration |
| 13 | Total Cells | $N_{total} = n_s \cdot n_p$ | cells | Pack size |
| 14 | C-rate | $C = I / Q_{rated}$ | 1/h | Discharge/charge current |
| 15 | Power Density | $P_{spec} = V^2 / (4 R_{int} m)$ | W/kg | Max deliverable |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE**: The above table uses `\vert` and `\mid` style separators inside cells where mathematical vertical bars are needed. Always avoid raw `\vert` symbols inside LaTeX to prevent Markdown table rendering errors.

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why These Parameters Matter |
|-------------|----------------------------|
| EV Range Estimation | Specific energy × pack mass = total kWh available |
| Fast Charging Design | C-rate = fast charge current / pack capacity |
| Battery Pack Sizing | $V_{pack} = n_s V_{cell}$ must match motor inverter DC bus |
| Thermal Management | R_int generates I²R heat, sets cooling requirement |
| Warranty Estimation | Cycle life curve defines warranty mileage |
| Storage Logistics | Self-discharge dictates SOC recheck intervals |
| Second-Life Batteries | Aged cells with capacity < 80% are repurposed for grid |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cell Voltage from Gibbs Free Energy

**Starting Point — Gibbs Free Energy of Reaction:**
For an electrochemical half-reaction, the maximum non-PV (electrical) work obtainable is equal to the negative change in Gibbs free energy:

$$W_{max,elec} = -\Delta G \;\; [J/mol]$$

**Step 1 — Relation to Charge Transferred:**
For $z$ moles of electrons transferred per mole of reaction, the total charge $Q$ per mole of reaction is:

$$Q = z \cdot F \;\; [C/mol]$$

Where $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96485 C/mol**.

**Step 2 — Work Done by Cell Voltage:**
If the cell maintains voltage $V_{cell}$ across this charge:

$$W_{max,elec} = V_{cell} \cdot Q = V_{cell} \cdot z \cdot F$$

**Step 3 — Equating the Two Expressions:**

$$
V_{cell} \cdot z \cdot F = -\Delta G
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for Cell Voltage:**

$$
\boxed{V_{cell} = -\frac{\Delta G}{z \cdot F}}
$$

**Numerical Example (Lithium-ion NMC/graphite):**
- $\Delta G \approx -395$ kJ/mol (for the overall cell reaction)
- $z = 1$ (one electron per Li atom)
- Compute:

$$
V_{cell} = \frac{395 \times 10^3}{1 \times 96485} = 4.094 \;\; V
$$

This is the **theoretical OCV**. The actual nominal voltage of 3.6-3.7V is lower due to real-world losses (overpotentials).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Energy from Cell Parameters

**Starting Point — Energy in a Cell:**
The DC energy stored in a fully charged cell is:

$$E_{cell} = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{cell} \;\; [Wh]$$

where $Q_{cell}$ is the rated capacity in Ampere-hours.

**Step 1 — Convert to Watt-hours (formal):**
Since $1 \text{ Wh} = 1 \text{ V} \times 1 \text{ Ah}$:

$$
E_{cell} = \int_{0}^{Q_{cell}} V_{oc}(q) \; dq
$$

For simplicity, assuming average voltage $\bar{V}$ over the discharge:

$$
E_{cell} = \bar{V} \cdot Q_{cell}
$$

**Step 2 — Gravimetric Specific Energy:**

$$
E_{spec,g} = \frac{E_{cell}}{m_{cell}} = \frac{\bar{V} \cdot Q_{cell}}{m_{cell}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values (Li-ion 18650 cell example):**
- $\bar{V} = 3.7$ V
- $Q_{cell} = 3.0$ Ah
- $m_{cell} = 0.045$ kg

$$
E_{cell} = 3.7 \times 3.0 = 11.1 \;\; Wh
$$

$$
E_{spec,g} = \frac{11.1}{0.045} = 246.7 \;\; Wh/kg
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Pack-level Specific Energy:**
If pack mass = $N_{total} \cdot m_{cell} + m_{packaging}$ (assume packaging adds 20% mass):

$$
E_{spec,pack} = \frac{N_{total} \cdot E_{cell}}{1.2 \cdot N_{total} \cdot m_{cell}} = \frac{E_{spec,g}}{1.2} = 205.6 \;\; Wh/kg
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Self-Discharge Decay Law

**Starting Point — Empirical First-Order Decay:**
The rate of charge loss is proportional to the current charge:

$$
\frac{dQ(t)}{dt} = -\lambda \cdot Q(t)
$$

where $\lambda$ = self-discharge rate constant.

**Step 1 — Separate Variables:**

$$
\frac{dQ}{Q} = -\lambda \cdot dt
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate Both Sides from $Q_0$ at $t=0$ to $Q(t)$ at time $t$:**

$$
\int_{Q_0}^{Q(t)} \frac{dQ}{Q} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Integrals:**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{Q(t)}{Q_0}\right) = -\lambda \cdot t
$$

**Step 4 — Exponentiate Both Sides:**

$$
\boxed{Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda \cdot t}}
$$

**Step 5 — Express $\lambda$ in terms of %/month:**
If self-discharge is $\sigma$ %/month, then after 1 month, $Q(t)/Q_0 = 1 - \sigma/100$:

$$
1 - \frac{\sigma}{100} = e^{-\lambda \cdot 1}
$$

$$
\lambda = -\ln\left(1 - \frac{\sigma}{100}\right) \;\; \text{per month}
$$

**Step 6 — Example (Li-ion, $\sigma = 2$ %/month):**

$$
\lambda = -\ln(1 - 0.02) = 0.0202 \;\; \text{per month}
$$

After 12 months of storage:

$$
Q(12) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-0.0202 \times 12} = Q_0 \cdot e^{-0.2424} = 0.785 \cdot Q_0
$$

So **21.5% of charge is lost** in 12 months.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Series-Parallel Pack Equivalent Circuit

**Setup:** Consider $n_s$ cells in series within one string, and $n_p$ such strings in parallel.

**Step 1 — Analyze a Single String ($n_s$ cells in series):**
For ideal cells with V_oc, each cell contributes its voltage in series:

$$
V_{string} = V_{oc,1} + V_{oc,2} + \ldots + V_{oc,n_s} = n_s \cdot V_{oc,cell}
$$

Internal resistances add in series:

$$
R_{int,string} = R_{int,1} + R_{int,2} + \ldots + R_{int,n_s} = n_s \cdot R_{int,cell}
$$

Capacity of the string = single cell capacity (series doesn't add capacity):

$$
Q_{string} = Q_{cell}
$$

**Step 2 — Combine $n_p$ Strings in Parallel:**
Voltages stay the same (parallel ideal voltage sources must match):

$$
V_{oc,pack} = V_{oc,string} = n_s \cdot V_{oc,cell}
$$

Resistances in parallel reduce (using $1/R_{total} = \sum 1/R_i$ for identical strings):

$$
\frac{1}{R_{int,pack}} = \frac{1}{R_{int,string}} + \ldots (n_p \text{ times}) = \frac{n_p}{R_{int,string}}
$$

$$
R_{int,pack} = \frac{R_{int,string}}{n_p} = \frac{n_s \cdot R_{int,cell}}{n_p}
$$

Capacities in parallel add:

$$
Q_{pack} = n_p \cdot Q_{string} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell}
$$

**Step 3 — Pack Energy:**

$$
E_{pack} = V_{oc,pack} \cdot Q_{pack} = (n_s \cdot V_{oc,cell}) \cdot (n_p \cdot Q_{cell}) = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot V_{oc,cell} \cdot Q_{cell}
$$

**Step 4 — Pack Power Capability (Peak Discharge):**

$$
P_{pack,max} = \frac{V_{oc,pack}^2}{4 \cdot R_{int,pack}} = \frac{(n_s V_{oc,cell})^2}{4 \cdot (n_s/n_p) R_{int,cell}} = \frac{n_s \cdot n_p \cdot V_{oc,cell}^2}{4 \cdot R_{int,cell}}
$$

Notice power scales with total cell count.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Cycle Life vs Depth of Discharge

**Starting Point — Peukert-like Aging Model:**
A widely used empirical relationship:

$$
N_{cycle}(DOD) = N_{100} \cdot \left(\frac{100}{DOD\%}\right)^k
$$

**Step 1 — Verify with Boundary Condition:**
At $DOD = 100\%$, $N_{cycle} = N_{100} \cdot (100/100)^k = N_{100}$. ✓

**Step 2 — Example Calculation:**
Assume $N_{100} = 1000$ cycles, $k = 1.5$.

At DOD = 50%:

$$
N_{cycle}(50) = 1000 \cdot \left(\frac{100}{50}\right)^{1.5} = 1000 \cdot 2^{1.5} = 1000 \cdot 2.828 = 2828 \text{ cycles}
$$

At DOD = 20%:

$$
N_{cycle}(20) = 1000 \cdot \left(\frac{100}{20}\right)^{1.5} = 1000 \cdot 5^{1.5} = 1000 \cdot 11.18 = 11180 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective Energy Throughput:**
Energy throughput over life:

$$
E_{throughput} = N_{cycle} \cdot DOD \cdot E_{cell}
$$

For 100% DOD: $E = 1000 \cdot 1.0 \cdot E_{cell} = 1000 E_{cell}$
For 50% DOD: $E = 2828 \cdot 0.5 \cdot E_{cell} = 1414 E_{cell}$

So **shallow cycling gives 41% more lifetime energy throughput!**

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Series vs Parallel vs Hybrid Comparison

| Configuration | Voltage | Capacity (Ah) | Internal R | Energy | Power (max) |
|---------------|---------|---------------|------------|--------|-------------|
| 1 Cell (baseline) | $V_c$ | $Q_c$ | $R_c$ | $V_c Q_c$ | $V_c^2 / 4R_c$ |
| 2s1p (2 in series) | $2V_c$ | $Q_c$ | $2R_c$ | $2V_c Q_c$ | $V_c^2 / 2R_c$ |
| 1s2p (2 in parallel) | $V_c$ | $2Q_c$ | $R_c/2$ | $2V_c Q_c$ | $V_c^2 / 2R_c$ |
| 2s2p (hybrid) | $2V_c$ | $2Q_c$ | $R_c$ | $4V_c Q_c$ | $V_c^2 / R_c$ |
| 4s3p | $4V_c$ | $3Q_c$ | $4R_c/3$ | $12V_c Q_c$ | $3V_c^2 / R_c$ |
| General $n_s \times n_p$ | $n_s V_c$ | $n_p Q_c$ | $n_s R_c / n_p$ | $n_s n_p V_c Q_c$ | $n_p V_c^2 / 4R_c \cdot n_s$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Energy scales as $n_s \cdot n_p$ (total cell count), but voltage and capacity scale independently. This decoupling is the key design freedom in EV pack engineering.

## 4.1 Static Battery Equivalent Circuit Model (R-int Model)

**Mermaid Block Diagram of the R-int Model:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Open Circuit Voltage Source<br/>V_oc SOC and T] --> B[Internal Ohmic Resistance<br/>R_int cell ohmic loss]
    B --> C[Terminal Voltage V_t<br/>delivered to load]
    D[Discharge Current I<br/>positive for discharge] --> B
    E[Load: EV Motor Inverter<br/>or Charger] --> C
    C --> E
```

**Working Principle Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SOC[State of Charge<br/>SOC in percent] --> VFN[V_oc = f SOC]
    TEMP[Cell Temperature T] --> VFN
    VFN --> Vout[Open Circuit Voltage]
    SOC --> RIN[R_int = g SOC T]
    TEMP --> RIN
    RIN --> VDROP[I x R_int drop]
    Vout --> VTERM[Terminal V_t = V_oc minus I x R_int]
    VDROP --> VTERM
    VTERM --> OUT[Delivered to EV Power Train]
```
