# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define cell voltage and specific energy. Why is specific energy a critical parameter in EV battery selection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Cell Voltage (1 mark):** Cell voltage is the potential difference between the positive and negative terminals of a single electrochemical cell, determined by the difference in electrochemical potentials of the cathode and anode half-reactions. It is given by the Nernst equation and is the fundamental unit of EMF in a battery.

**Specific Energy (1 mark):** Specific energy is the amount of electrical energy stored per unit mass (Wh/kg) or volume (Wh/L) of a battery cell. Mathematically, $E_{spec} = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{cell} / m_{cell}$.

**Criticality for EV (1 mark):** Specific energy directly determines the range of an EV per kg of battery. A higher specific energy means a lighter battery for the same range, improving vehicle dynamics, energy efficiency, and payload capacity. This is why Li-ion (150-250 Wh/kg) has largely replaced NiMH (60-120 Wh/kg) in modern EVs.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain self-discharge in a battery cell. What is the effect of temperature on self-discharge rate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Self-Discharge Definition (1.5 marks):** Self-discharge is the spontaneous loss of stored charge in a battery when it is left idle (no external load), caused by internal parasitic chemical reactions such as electrolyte decomposition, side reactions at electrodes, and internal leakage current paths. It is expressed as percentage of rated capacity lost per unit time (%/month).

**Effect of Temperature (1.5 marks):** The self-discharge rate follows the Arrhenius equation, and approximately **doubles for every 10°C rise in temperature**. This is because internal parasitic reactions have activation energies that increase exponentially with temperature. For example, a Li-ion cell with 1%/month self-discharge at 25°C may have 4%/month at 45°C. This makes thermal management crucial for parked EVs in hot climates.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: In the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination, Part B questions on this topic commonly test the **derivation of pack-level parameters** ($V_{pack}$, $R_{int,pack}$, $E_{pack}$) for a given $n_s \times n_p$ configuration. Students frequently lose 4-5 marks by:
> 1. Forgetting the $n_s/n_p$ relationship for $R_{int,pack}$
> 2. Not specifying units in final answers
> 3. Confusing series capacity vs parallel capacity
> 4. Missing the "self-discharge is temperature-dependent" nuance in cycle life discussions
> **Always draw the equivalent circuit diagram and label all parameters.**

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expressions for the equivalent open-circuit voltage, internal resistance, and total energy of an $n_s \times n_p$ series-parallel battery pack, where each cell has $V_{oc,cell}$, $R_{int,cell}$, and $Q_{cell}$. Assume identical cells. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Li-ion EV battery pack is designed using 96s74p configuration of 18650 cells. Each cell has nominal voltage 3.7 V, capacity 3.0 Ah, and internal resistance 35 m$\Omega$. Calculate:
- (i) Pack open-circuit voltage
- (ii) Pack internal resistance
- (iii) Pack total energy in kWh
- (iv) Gravimetric specific energy if each cell weighs 45 g and packaging mass is 15% of total cell mass. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Derivation of Pack Parameters (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Series Combination Within a String (2 marks):**
For $n_s$ identical cells in series:
- Voltages add: $V_{oc,string} = n_s \cdot V_{oc,cell}$
- Resistances add: $R_{int,string} = n_s \cdot R_{int,cell}$
- Capacity unchanged: $Q_{string} = Q_{cell}$

**Step 2 — Parallel Combination of $n_p$ Strings (2 marks):**
- Voltage unchanged: $V_{oc,pack} = V_{oc,string} = n_s \cdot V_{oc,cell}$
- Resistance reduces by $n_p$: $R_{int,pack} = R_{int,string} / n_p = (n_s/n_p) \cdot R_{int,cell}$
- Capacity adds: $Q_{pack} = n_p \cdot Q_{string} = n_p \cdot Q_{cell}$

**Step 3 — Total Energy (1.5 marks):**

$$
E_{pack} = V_{oc,pack} \cdot Q_{pack} = n_s \cdot V_{oc,cell} \cdot n_p \cdot Q_{cell} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot V_{oc,cell} \cdot Q_{cell}
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Circuit (1.5 marks):**
The pack can be represented as a single static ECM:

```
  o----[V_oc_pack]----[R_int_pack]----o
  +                                    -
  |------- V_t_pack = V_oc_pack - I R_int_pack -------|
```

##### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Configuration: 96s × 74p
- $V_{oc,cell} = 3.7$ V
- $Q_{cell} = 3.0$ Ah
- $R_{int,cell} = 35$ m$\Omega$ = $0.035$ $\Omega$
- $m_{cell} = 45$ g = 0.045 kg
- $N_{total} = 96 \times 74 = 7104$ cells

**Step (i) — Pack Open-Circuit Voltage (1.5 marks):**

$$
V_{oc,pack} = 96 \times 3.7 = 355.2 \;\; V
$$

This matches the typical Tesla Model S DC bus voltage (~400 V nominal for the inverter).

**Step (ii) — Pack Internal Resistance (1.5 marks):**

$$
R_{int,pack} = \frac{n_s}{n_p} \cdot R_{int,cell} = \frac{96}{74} \times 0.035
$$

$$
R_{int,pack} = 1.297 \times 0.035 = 0.0454 \;\; \Omega
$$

**Step (iii) — Pack Total Energy (2 marks):**

$$
E_{cell} = 3.7 \times 3.0 = 11.1 \;\; Wh
$$

$$
E_{pack} = N_{total} \cdot E_{cell} = 7104 \times 11.1 = 78,854.4 \;\; Wh \approx 78.85 \;\; kWh
$$

(This matches the actual ~85 kWh useable capacity of the Tesla Model S 85 kWh pack.)

**Step (iv) — Gravimetric Specific Energy (2 marks):**

Total cell mass: $7104 \times 0.045 = 319.68$ kg
Packaging mass: $0.15 \times 319.68 = 47.95$ kg
Total pack mass: $319.68 + 47.95 = 367.63$ kg

$$
E_{spec,pack} = \frac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack,total}} = \frac{78.85 \text{ kWh}}{367.63 \text{ kg}} = 0.2145 \;\; \frac{kWh}{kg} = 214.5 \;\; \frac{Wh}{kg}
$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a static equivalent circuit, derive the equation for the terminal voltage of a battery cell under discharge. Explain the significance of the R-int model in EV Battery Management Systems (BMS). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Li-ion cell has the following specifications: $V_{oc} = 3.6$ V (at 50% SOC), $R_{int} = 25$ m$\Omega$, $Q_{cell} = 2.5$ Ah, mass = 42 g. The cell undergoes a 1C discharge. Calculate:
- (i) Discharge current
- (ii) Terminal voltage during 1C discharge
- (iii) Power delivered to load
- (iv) Specific energy and energy density (Wh/kg and Wh/L, assuming cell volume 18 cm³)
- (v) If 5000 such cells are used in a 100s50p configuration, find the pack open-circuit voltage, total energy, and pack internal resistance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Static ECM Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define the R-int Model (2 marks):**
A real battery cell is modeled as an ideal voltage source $V_{oc}$ (function of SOC and temperature) in series with an internal resistance $R_{int}$. The terminal voltage is what appears across the load terminals.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL Around the Loop (2 marks):**

$$
V_{oc} = V_t + I \cdot R_{int}
$$

Solving for terminal voltage:

$$
V_t = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}
$$

Where I is the discharge current (positive for discharge, negative for charge).

**Step 3 — Significance in BMS (3 marks):**
The R-int model is foundational for BMS because it allows:
1. **SOC Estimation**: Measuring $V_t$ and I allows calculation of $V_{oc}$, which uniquely maps to SOC via the OCV-SOC curve.
2. **Power Capability Prediction**: $P_{max} = V_{oc}^2 / (4 R_{int})$ defines available discharge power.
3. **Overcurrent Protection**: $V_t$ must stay above cutoff under maximum current draw to prevent cell damage.
4. **State of Health (SOH) Tracking**: As cell ages, $R_{int}$ increases; the BMS tracks this to predict end-of-life.
5. **Thermal Load Estimation**: Heat generated = $I^2 \cdot R_{int}$, used to control thermal management.

**Equivalent Circuit:**

```
  o-----[ V_oc ]----[ R_int ]-----o
  +                                 -
  |-------- V_t (across load) ------|
```

##### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V_{oc} = 3.6$ V at 50% SOC
- $R_{int} = 0.025$ $\Omega$
- $Q_{cell} = 2.5$ Ah
- $m_{cell} = 0.042$ kg
- Volume = 18 cm³ = $18 \times 10^{-6}$ m³
- Discharge rate = 1C
- Pack: 100s × 50p = 5000 cells

**Step (i) — Discharge Current at 1C (1 mark):**
By definition, 1C means full capacity in 1 hour:

$$
I = 1C \cdot Q_{cell} = 1 \times 2.5 = 2.5 \;\; A
$$

**Step (ii) — Terminal Voltage (1.5 marks):**

$$
V_t = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int} = 3.6 - 2.5 \times 0.025 = 3.6 - 0.0625 = 3.5375 \;\; V
$$

**Step (iii) — Power Delivered to Load (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_{load} = V_t \cdot I = 3.5375 \times 2.5 = 8.844 \;\; W
$$

(For verification, also useful to compute total power generated: $V_{oc} \cdot I = 3.6 \times 2.5 = 9.0$ W. Loss in R_int = $I^2 R_{int} = 6.25 \times 0.025 = 0.156$ W. So $9.0 - 0.156 = 8.844$ W ✓)

**Step (iv) — Specific Energy & Energy Density (1.5 marks):**
Cell energy:

$$
E_{cell} = V_{oc} \cdot Q_{cell} = 3.6 \times 2.5 = 9.0 \;\; Wh
$$

Gravimetric:

$$
E_{spec,g} = \frac{9.0}{0.042} = 214.3 \;\; \frac{Wh}{kg}
$$

Volumetric:

$$
E_{spec,v} = \frac{9.0}{18 \times 10^{-6}} = 500{,}000 \;\; \frac{Wh}{m^3} = 500 \;\; \frac{Wh}{L}
$$

**Step (v) — Pack Parameters (1.5 marks):**

- $n_s = 100$, $n_p = 50$

Pack voltage:

$$
V_{oc,pack} = 100 \times 3.6 = 360 \;\; V
$$

Total energy:

$$
E_{pack} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot V_{oc} \cdot Q_{cell} = 5000 \times 3.6 \times 2.5 = 45{,}000 \;\; Wh = 45 \;\; kWh
$$

Pack internal resistance:

$$
R_{int,pack} = \frac{n_s}{n_p} \cdot R_{int,cell} = \frac{100}{50} \times 0.025 = 2 \times 0.025 = 0.050 \;\; \Omega = 50 \;\; m\Omega
$$

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> This recap serves as your rapid-revision checklist for the KTU End Semester Examination. Memorize these bullet points verbatim.

- [x] **Cell Voltage (V_cell)** is fixed by chemistry, given by $V_{cell} = -\Delta G / (zF)$, where $F = 96485$ C/mol.
- [x] **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** = $V_{nom} \cdot Q_{cell} / m_{cell}$ — most critical EV battery metric.
- [x] **Energy Density (Wh/L)** = $V_{nom} \cdot Q_{cell} / Vol_{cell}$ — matters for space-constrained designs.
- [x] **Cycle Life** is rated at 80% capacity retention; restricted DOD extends cycle life (Peukert-like).
- [x] **Self-Discharge** doubles every 10°C rise; modeled as $Q(t) = Q_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.
- [x] **Static R-int Model**: $V_t = V_{oc} - I \cdot R_{int}$ — fundamental battery equation.
- [x] **Series Connection** → voltage adds ($n_s \cdot V_c$), capacity unchanged, resistance adds.
- [x] **Parallel Connection** → capacity adds ($n_p \cdot Q_c$), voltage unchanged, resistance divided by $n_p$.
- [x] **Series-Parallel Pack**: $V_{oc,pack} = n_s V_{oc,cell}$, $Q_{pack} = n_p Q_{cell}$, $R_{int,pack} = (n_s/n_p) R_{int,cell}$.
- [x] **Pack Energy**: $E_{pack} = n_s \cdot n_p \cdot V_{oc,cell} \cdot Q_{cell}$ [Wh].
- [x] **Total Cells** $N_{total} = n_s \cdot n_p$.
- [x] **C-rate** definition: $C = I_{discharge} / Q_{rated}$ — 1C = full discharge in 1 hour.
- [x] **Peak Power**: $P_{max} = V_{oc}^2 / (4 R_{int})$ — determined by cell internal resistance.
- [x] **Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc)** depends on SOC and Temperature; never equal to nominal voltage at all conditions.
- [x] **Internal Resistance (R_int)** depends on SOC, Temperature, and Age; increases as cell ages.
- [x] **Thermal Runaway Risk** scales with $I^2 R_{int}$ heat generation — drives BMS cooling design.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Trick**: "**S**eries adds **V**oltage, **P**arallel adds **C**apacity (and reduces resistance)." Use "**S-V, P-C**" to remember the key difference. Cells in **P**arallel **P**revent Power loss by halving resistance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Storage Topologies: Fuel Cell Based Energy Storage Systems - Supercapacitors - Flywheel - Hybridization of Different Energy Storage Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Other Storage Topologies: Fuel Cell Based Energy Storage Systems - Supercapacitors - Flywheel - Hybridization of Different Energy Storage Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Alternative Energy Storage Topologies

In the previous modules, we established the dominance of **Lithium-ion (Li-ion)** and **Lead-acid** batteries as the primary electrochemical reservoirs in modern Electric Vehicles (EVs). However, the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET522)** explicitly mandates the study of *alternative* storage topologies because a single energy source cannot optimally satisfy the dual, often contradictory, demands of an EV powertrain: **High Energy Density** (for long range) and **High Power Density** (for acceleration and regenerative braking).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Energy Storage Topologies** in the context of electric vehicles refer to the diverse set of electrical, electrochemical, electrochemical-mechanical, and electromechanical systems used to store, convert, and dispatch energy on-board the vehicle. These include **Fuel Cells (FC)**, **Supercapacitors (SC)**, and **Flywheels (FES)**, each of which addresses a specific weakness of conventional batteries.

---

## 1.2 Fuel Cell Based Energy Storage Systems (FCESS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Fuel Cell** is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that continuously converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically **Hydrogen, H₂**) and an oxidant (typically **Oxygen, O₂**) directly into **electrical energy**, with **water (H₂O)** and **heat** as the only by-products. It is *not* a battery — it does not store charge; instead, it generates electricity *as long as fuel is supplied*.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Leaf That Never Sleeps":**
Think of a fuel cell as an *artificial, mechanical leaf*. Just as a plant leaf takes in sunlight, water, and CO₂ to produce sugar (and oxygen), a fuel cell takes in **hydrogen and oxygen** to produce **electricity (and water)**. The "fuel tank" is a hydrogen cylinder — as long as you keep feeding the leaf (hydrogen), it keeps producing electricity without ever needing to be "recharged" in the traditional sense. You simply *refuel* it in 3–5 minutes, just like a petrol car.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant:** The lower heating value (LHV) of hydrogen is **120 MJ/kg**, and its electrochemical energy conversion efficiency in a PEMFC is roughly **50–60%** — nearly **2× more efficient** than an internal combustion engine.

**Types of Fuel Cells (for EV application):**

| Type | Electrolyte | Operating Temp | EV Suitability |
|------|------------|----------------|----------------|
| **PEMFC** (Proton Exchange Membrane) | Polymer Membrane | 60–100 °C | ★★★★★ (Best for EVs) |
| **SOFC** (Solid Oxide) | Ceramic (YSZ) | 600–1000 °C | Heavy vehicles only |
| **AFC** (Alkaline) | KOH solution | 60–120 °C | Space applications |
| **DMFC** (Direct Methanol) | Polymer Membrane | 60–100 °C | Auxiliary units |

---

## 1.3 Supercapacitors (Electrochemical Capacitors / Ultracapacitors)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Supercapacitor (SC)**, also called an **Electric Double-Layer Capacitor (EDLC)** or **Ultracapacitor**, is an electrochemical energy storage device that stores energy **electrostatically** by forming an **electric double layer** at the interface between a high-surface-area electrode (typically **activated carbon**) and an electrolyte. No chemical reaction occurs during charge/discharge.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Power Athlete vs. The Marathon Runner":**
Imagine a battery is a **marathon runner** — it carries a heavy water bottle (lots of energy) and runs steadily for hours but cannot sprint. A supercapacitor is a **sprinter** — it cannot run a long distance, but in just 5 seconds, it can discharge an enormous burst of energy. In an EV, the battery is the marathon runner (powers the car for 300 km), while the supercapacitor is the sprinter (provides the instant kick during overtaking or absorbs the instant energy from regenerative braking).

**Key Performance Metrics of a Supercapacitor:**

- **Capacitance Range:** **1 F to 5000 F** (compare to a normal capacitor: microfarads)
- **Specific Power:** Up to **10 kW/kg** (Batteries: ~1 kW/kg)
- **Specific Energy:** **5–10 Wh/kg** (Batteries: 100–250 Wh/kg)
- **Cycle Life:** **> 1,000,000 cycles** (Batteries: ~2000 cycles)

---

## 1.4 Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS)** is a **mechanical-electrical** energy storage device that stores energy in the form of **rotational kinetic energy** in a high-speed rotating mass (the *rotor*). Energy is transferred to/from the flywheel through an integrated **motor-generator** (M/G) unit.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Potter's Wheel":**
Remember a potter's wheel? The faster the potter spins it, the more energy is stored. If you stop pushing, the wheel keeps spinning for a long time. Now imagine a *super-strong, vacuum-sealed, magnetically suspended* potter's wheel spinning at **60,000 rpm** — that is a flywheel for an EV. When you brake, the car's kinetic energy is transferred to the wheel (it spins faster). When you accelerate, the wheel's spinning energy is transferred back to the wheels.

**Ragone Chart Context (Visualizing the trade-off):**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Ragone Plot** plots **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** on the X-axis vs. **Specific Power (W/kg)** on the Y-axis. On this plot:
> - **Batteries** sit in the *top-right* (high energy, low power).
> - **Supercapacitors** sit in the *bottom-right* (low energy, very high power).
> - **Flywheels** sit in the *middle-right* (moderate energy, high power).
> - **Fuel Cells** sit in the *top-left* (very high energy, low power).
> - **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** sits in the *top-right* with fuel tank.

---

## 1.5 Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices (HESS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS)** is a multi-source energy storage topology in which **two or more heterogeneous storage devices** (e.g., Battery + Supercapacitor, or Fuel Cell + Battery + Supercapacitor) are electrically or mechanically coupled through **power electronic converters** and a dedicated **Energy Management System (EMS)** to synergistically meet the *combined* energy and power demands of the EV.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Backpack with Two Pockets":**
Imagine going on a long hike. You carry a **large water bottle** in one pocket (the battery — gives you steady hydration for 10 hours) and a **small, high-energy energy gel packet** in the other (the supercapacitor — gives you an instant boost when climbing a hill). You don't use the gel when walking on flat ground (you'd waste it); you only use it during bursts. The **Energy Management System (EMS)** is your brain that decides *when* to use which pocket.

This single concept — *decoupling energy and power duties* — is the philosophical backbone of modern EV powertrains.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fuel Cell (FC) — Operational Theory

A **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** is the dominant FC topology for automotive applications. Its operation is governed by **three simultaneous half-reactions**:

**Step 1 — At the Anode (Hydrogen Oxidation):**
$$\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$

**Step 2 — Proton Migration:**
The protons ($H^+$) travel through the polymer membrane (e.g., **Nafion™**) from anode to cathode. Electrons are forced through the **external circuit**, generating useful DC current.

**Step 3 — At the Cathode (Oxygen Reduction):**
$$\frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Overall Reaction:**
$$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?**
> The voltage produced by a single cell is only **~1.23 V at standard conditions** (open-circuit). Because a practical EV drivetrain needs **300–400 V**, hundreds of cells are *stacked* in series to form a **Fuel Cell Stack**. This is why the Toyota Mirai has a **370-cell stack** producing ~114 kW.

### 2.1.1 Nernst Equation (Theoretical Cell Voltage)

$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

Where:
- $E^0_{cell} = 1.229$ V (standard cell potential at 25 °C, 1 atm)
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) (Universal gas constant)
- $T$ = Temperature in **K**
- $n = 2$ (number of electrons transferred per H₂ molecule)
- $F = 96485$ C/mol (Faraday's constant)

### 2.1.2 Fuel Cell Efficiency

$$\eta_{FC} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = 1 - \frac{T \cdot \Delta S}{\Delta H}$$

- **ΔG** = Gibbs free energy change = **237.2 kJ/mol**
- **ΔH** = Higher heating value of H₂ = **285.8 kJ/mol**
- **Theoretical LHV efficiency = 83%**
- **Practical efficiency = 50–60%** (due to activation, ohmic, and concentration losses)

---

## 2.2 Supercapacitor (EDLC) — Operational Theory

An EDLC is a **non-Faradaic** device — meaning *no* charge-transfer reaction occurs at the electrode. Energy is stored purely in the **electric double layer** formed at the electrode-electrolyte interface, first described by **Helmholtz (1853)**.

**The Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Equivalent:**
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_r$ = Relative permittivity of the electrolyte
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- $A$ = Effective surface area of the electrode (using activated carbon, $A$ can be **1500–3000 m²/g**)
- $d$ = Effective separation distance (~0.3–0.8 nm = the size of an ion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is C so enormous?**
> Because $A$ is *thousands* of square meters per gram and $d$ is *nanometers*, the resulting $C$ is in the **kilo-Farad** range — hence the name "Super"capacitor.

### 2.2.1 Energy and Power of a Supercapacitor

$$E_{SC} = \frac{1}{2} C \cdot V^2 \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

$$P_{SC,max} = \frac{V^2}{4 \cdot R_{ESR}} \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

Where $R_{ESR}$ is the **Equivalent Series Resistance** (typically 0.1–10 mΩ for an SC).

### 2.2.2 Time Constant

The fundamental time constant of a supercapacitor is:

$$\tau = R_{ESR} \cdot C$$

A typical EDLC has $\tau \approx 1$ second, which means it can deliver **~63% of its stored energy in just 1 second** — making it ideal for capturing braking energy.

---

## 2.3 Flywheel Energy Storage System (FESS) — Operational Theory

The flywheel is a **purely mechanical kinetic energy reservoir**. The energy stored in a rotating rigid body is given by:

$$E_{FES} = \frac{1}{2} J \omega^2 \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

Where:
- $J$ = Moment of inertia of the rotor (kg·m²)
- $\omega$ = Angular velocity in **rad/s**

The moment of inertia for a solid cylindrical rotor is:
$$J = \frac{1}{2} m r^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy scales with $\omega^2$ — Doubling speed quadruples stored energy!**
> This is why modern FESS use **carbon-fiber rotors at 60,000+ rpm** rather than steel discs at 6,000 rpm. The tensile strength limit of the rotor material dictates the maximum safe $\omega$ — this is called the **specific energy limit**.

### 2.3.1 Flywheel Specific Energy Limit (Tensile Stress Constraint)

For a thin-walled cylinder of density $\rho$ and tensile strength $\sigma_{uts}$:

$$E_{specific} = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho} \quad \text{[J/kg]}$$

- **Steel rotor:** ~ 200 kJ/kg (theoretical)
- **Carbon-fiber composite rotor:** ~ 500–1000 kJ/kg (theoretical)

### 2.3.2 Charge/Discharge Power

The instantaneous charge/discharge power is governed by the motor-generator's torque capability:

$$P = T \cdot \omega$$

Where $T$ is the motor torque (N·m) and $\omega$ is the angular speed (rad/s).

### 2.3.3 Friction and Self-Discharge Losses

A flywheel in vacuum with **magnetic bearings** has a self-discharge time of several hours to days. The energy decay follows:

$$E(t) = E_0 \cdot e^{-2 \cdot t \cdot f_{drag}}$$

Where $f_{drag}$ is the rotational friction coefficient.

---

## 2.4 Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS) — Operational Theory

The HESS architecture is characterized by the **topology of the power electronic interface**. There are **three canonical topologies**:

### 2.4.1 Passive Parallel Topology
- Both sources connected directly across the DC bus.
- No converter flexibility — load current splits *naturally* based on internal resistance.
- Simple, cheap, but **inefficient and rigid**.

### 2.4.2 Semi-Active (Battery/UC) Topology
- The supercapacitor is connected through a **DC-DC converter** to the DC bus.
- Battery connects directly. SC handles *transients*.
- Most common in production EVs (e.g., Mazda i-ELOOP).

### 2.4.3 Fully-Active Topology
- **Both** sources are connected through dedicated **bidirectional DC-DC converters**.
- The **Energy Management System (EMS)** actively controls power flow.
- Best performance, but most expensive and complex.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:**
> The Toyota Mirai (Fuel Cell EV) uses a **fully-active HESS** with a PEMFC stack + a small **Li-ion battery + supercapacitor buffer**, because the PEMFC's response time (~5 s) is too slow to handle sudden acceleration demands.

---

## 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (CHEAT SHEET)

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Value (EV) |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | $E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2}P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | Nernst cell voltage | V | 1.0–1.23 V |
| 2 | $\eta_{FC} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} = \frac{237.2}{285.8} = 83\%$ | FC theoretical LHV eff. | % | 50–60% practical |
| 3 | $E_{SC} = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ | SC stored energy | J | 5–10 Wh/kg |
| 4 | $P_{SC,max} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_{ESR}}$ | SC peak power | W | 5–10 kW/kg |
| 5 | $\tau = R_{ESR} \cdot C$ | SC time constant | s | ~1 s |
| 6 | $E_{FES} = \frac{1}{2}J\omega^2$ | Flywheel KE | J | 100–500 Wh |
| 7 | $J = \frac{1}{2}mr^2$ | Solid cylinder inertia | kg·m² | — |
| 8 | $E_{sp,lim} = \sigma_{uts}/\rho$ | Flywheel specific energy limit | J/kg | 5e5 (carbon) |
| 9 | $P = T\omega$ | Rotational power | W | — |
| 10 | $SOH = \frac{C_{actual}}{C_{rated}} \times 100\%$ | State of Health | % | 80% = EOL |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:**
> - FC: Buses, long-range trucks (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo)
> - SC: Crane peak shaving, EV regenerative braking (Mazda, Lamborghini Sian)
> - FES: Formula 1 KERS, grid frequency regulation, light-rail regenerative braking (e.g., NY Subway, London Underground)
> - HESS: Almost all modern performance EVs
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Nernst Voltage of a Hydrogen Fuel Cell at Non-Standard Conditions

**Problem Setup (from a typical KTU problem):**
A PEM fuel cell operates at **T = 353 K (80 °C)** with partial pressures: $P_{H_2} = 3$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 4$ atm, and the product water is in liquid form (so $P_{H_2O} = 1$ atm, activity = 1). Find the open-circuit (Nernst) voltage. Take $E^0_{cell} = 1.229$ V at 25 °C, but use the **corrected standard potential** at 353 K from thermodynamic data: $E^0_{cell}(T) = 1.18$ V.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Recall the Nernst equation for the FC:**
$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the known values into the numerator of the logarithm:**
- $P_{H_2} = 3$ atm
- $P_{O_2} = 4$ atm, so $P_{O_2}^{1/2} = \sqrt{4} = 2$ atm$^{1/2}$
- $P_{H_2O} = 1$ atm (since product water is liquid, activity = 1)

**Step 3 — Calculate the argument of the logarithm:**
$$Q = \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} = \frac{3 \times 2}{1} = 6$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Nernst equation:**
$$E_{cell} = 1.18 + \frac{(8.314)(353)}{(2)(96485)} \ln(6)$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the thermal voltage term $\frac{RT}{nF}$:**
$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 353}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{2934.84}{192970} = 0.01521 \text{ V}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the natural logarithm of 6:**
$$\ln(6) = 1.7918$$

**Step 7 — Compute the final Nernst voltage:**
$$E_{cell} = 1.18 + (0.01521)(1.7918)$$
$$E_{cell} = 1.18 + 0.02725$$
$$\boxed{E_{cell} \approx 1.207 \text{ V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice the open-circuit voltage of a single PEM cell is *always* between **1.0 V and 1.23 V** under normal operating conditions. Under load, the actual operating voltage drops to **0.6–0.7 V** per cell due to irreversible losses.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Energy Stored in a Composite-Material Flywheel

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
A flywheel rotor is made of carbon-fiber composite with mass $m = 50$ kg, outer radius $r = 0.15$ m, and inner radius negligible. The maximum safe angular speed is $\omega_{max} = 10{,}000$ rpm. Calculate (a) the moment of inertia, (b) the stored energy in kWh, and (c) the specific energy in Wh/kg.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Convert angular velocity to rad/s:**
$$\omega = 10{,}000 \text{ rpm} \times \frac{2\pi \text{ rad}}{1 \text{ rev}} \times \frac{1 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ s}} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 2\pi}{60} = 1047.2 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the moment of inertia (solid disc approximation):**
$$J = \frac{1}{2} m r^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times (0.15)^2$$
$$J = 25 \times 0.0225 = 0.5625 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the stored kinetic energy:**
$$E_{FES} = \frac{1}{2} J \omega^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.5625 \times (1047.2)^2$$
$$E_{FES} = 0.28125 \times 1{,}096{,}624 = 308{,}426 \text{ J}$$

**Step 4 — Convert Joules to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J):**
$$E_{FES} = \frac{308{,}426}{3{,}600{,}000} = 0.0857 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate specific energy:**
$$E_{sp} = \frac{E_{FES}}{m} = \frac{308{,}426}{50} = 6168.5 \text{ J/kg} = 1.714 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

$$\boxed{E_{FES} \approx 0.0857 \text{ kWh}, \quad E_{sp} \approx 1.71 \text{ Wh/kg}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This is *much* less than a battery (~150 Wh/kg). The flywheel's value is in *power density*, not energy. Note that 1.71 Wh/kg is well below the theoretical limit of carbon fiber (~138 Wh/kg); the gap exists because we are not spinning the rotor at the material's stress limit.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Ragone Plot Point Calculation for a Hybrid Source

**Problem Setup:**
A HESS consists of a **Li-ion battery** (Energy = 30 kWh, Mass = 200 kg, Max Power = 100 kW) and a **supercapacitor** (Energy = 0.5 kWh, Mass = 25 kg, Max Power = 100 kW). Calculate the **specific energy** and **specific power** of (a) the battery alone, (b) the SC alone, and (c) the HESS as a whole, assuming the EMS is ideal.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Battery-specific metrics:**
$$E_{sp,batt} = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{200 \text{ kg}} = 150 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

$$P_{sp,batt} = \frac{100{,}000 \text{ W}}{200 \text{ kg}} = 500 \text{ W/kg} = 0.5 \text{ kW/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Supercapacitor-specific metrics:**
$$E_{sp,SC} = \frac{500 \text{ Wh}}{25 \text{ kg}} = 20 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

$$P_{sp,SC} = \frac{100{,}000 \text{ W}}{25 \text{ kg}} = 4000 \text{ W/kg} = 4 \text{ kW/kg}$$

**Step 3 — HESS combined metrics (energy-mode, peak power demand):**
Total Mass = 200 + 25 = 225 kg
Total Energy = 30 + 0.5 = 30.5 kWh
Total Power = 100 + 100 = 200 kW

$$E_{sp,HESS} = \frac{30{,}500 \text{ Wh}}{225 \text{ kg}} \approx 135.6 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

$$P_{sp,HESS} = \frac{200{,}000 \text{ W}}{225 \text{ kg}} \approx 888.9 \text{ W/kg} = 0.89 \text{ kW/kg}$$

$$\boxed{\text{HESS: } 135.6 \text{ Wh/kg, } 0.89 \text{ kW/kg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The HESS gives ~78% more peak power than the battery alone, while losing only ~9% specific energy.** This is the engineering trade-off the KTU paper loves to test.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Storage Topologies

| Parameter | Li-ion Battery | Fuel Cell (PEMFC) | Supercapacitor | Flywheel |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|----------------|----------|
| **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** | 100–250 | 400–800 (system) | 5–10 | 10–50 |
| **Specific Power (W/kg)** | 250–1000 | 200–500 | 5000–10000 | 1000–5000 |
| **Cycle Life (cycles)** | 1000–3000 | > 5000 (stack) | > 1,000,000 | > 100,000 |
| **Charge Time** | 1–4 h | 3–5 min (refuel) | Seconds | Seconds |
| **Energy Efficiency (%)** | 85–95 | 50–60 | 90–95 | 80–90 |
| **Operating Temp (°C)** | -20 to 60 | 60–100 (PEM) | -40 to 65 | 20–200 |
| **Response Time** | 100 ms–1 s | 1–5 s | < 1 ms | 10–100 ms |
| **Self-Discharge** | 1–5%/month | Negligible | ~10% in 24 h | 1–5%/hour |
| **EV Suitability** | Primary | Range extender | Peak power | Research |

---

## 3.5 DERIVATION 4: HESS Optimal Power-Split Ratio

**Concept:**
For a HESS comprising a Battery (B) and a Supercapacitor (SC), the optimal design satisfies both the *energy demand* ($E_{req}$) and *peak power demand* ($P_{req}$) of a standard drive cycle.

Let the battery handle *average power* $P_{avg}$ and the SC handle *transient power* $P_{trans}$ such that:
$$P_{trans} = P_{req} - P_{avg}$$

**Energy required from the SC for the transient:**
$$E_{SC,req} = P_{trans} \times \Delta t_{trans}$$

**For an aggressive drive cycle (e.g., UDDS, WLTP Class 3):**
- $P_{avg} = 15$ kW
- $P_{req,peak} = 75$ kW
- $P_{trans} = 60$ kW
- $\Delta t_{trans} = 10$ s

**Required SC energy:**
$$E_{SC,req} = 60{,}000 \text{ W} \times 10 \text{ s} = 600{,}000 \text{ J} = 0.167 \text{ kWh}$$

**Required battery energy for the 1-hour drive cycle:**
$$E_{B,req} = 15 \text{ kW} \times 1 \text{ h} = 15 \text{ kWh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A 100:1 ratio of Battery to SC energy capacity is typical for urban HESS designs. Without the SC, the battery would need to handle 75 kW peaks, drastically reducing its cycle life.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Operational Principle of a PEMFC

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    J --> K[AC Traction Motor]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** H₂ enters the anode, splits into H⁺ + e⁻. H⁺ crosses the membrane, e⁻ flows through the external circuit (doing work), and they recombine at the cathode with O₂ to form H₂O. A boost converter raises the ~300 V stack voltage to the 400 V DC bus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** This is the architecture of the **Toyota Mirai (2nd Gen)**. The PEMFC stack is the *primary* energy source, the Li-ion battery buffers *transients*, and a supercapacitor bank (in some research variants) handles *microsecond-level* pulses. The **ECU** is the brain that decides how much current each source provides at any instant.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define a fuel cell. Why is a hydrogen PEMFC the preferred fuel cell type for light-duty electric vehicles?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A fuel cell is an electrochemical energy conversion device that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (H₂) and an oxidant (O₂) directly into DC electrical energy, with water and heat as by-products, *as long as fuel is supplied*.

**Reasons PEMFC is preferred for light-duty EVs (2 marks):**
1. **Low operating temperature (60–100 °C)** — fast start-up (cold-start in < 30 s), unlike SOFC which needs 800 °C.
2. **High power density** — fits compact vehicle packaging.
3. **Solid polymer electrolyte** — no leakage, robust against vehicle vibrations.
4. **Quick refueling (3–5 min)** — comparable to gasoline, vs. hours for a battery charge.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Compare supercapacitors and batteries in terms of energy density, power density, and cycle life. State one EV application where supercapacitors are preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Supercapacitor | Li-ion Battery |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| Energy Density (Wh/kg) | 5–10 | 100–250 |
| Power Density (W/kg) | 5000–10000 | 250–1000 |
| Cycle Life (cycles) | > 1,000,000 | 1000–3000 |

**EV Application (1 mark):** Regenerative braking energy recovery in city buses and trams, where high instantaneous power (up to 200 kW for a few seconds) would otherwise damage the battery's cycle life.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)**. State the half-cell reactions and overall cell reaction.

**(b) [7 marks]** A PEM fuel cell operates at **70 °C** with the following parameters: $P_{H_2} = 1.5$ atm, $P_{O_2} = 1.0$ atm, and product water is gaseous at $P_{H_2O} = 0.5$ atm. The standard cell potential at 70 °C is $E^0 = 1.19$ V. Calculate the **Nernst open-circuit voltage**. Also calculate the **theoretical LHV efficiency** of the fuel cell.

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — PEMFC Working Principle (7 marks)**

**Diagram (2 marks):** A neat labelled diagram showing H₂ inlet, O₂ inlet, anode, cathode, proton exchange membrane, electron flow through external circuit, and water outlet. *(Refer to SECTION 4 Diagram 1)*.

**Half-cell reactions (3 marks):**

**At the Anode (Hydrogen Oxidation Reaction — HOR):**
$$\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \quad (E^0 = 0.00 \text{ V vs. SHE})$$

**At the Cathode (Oxygen Reduction Reaction — ORR):**
$$\frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad (E^0 = +1.23 \text{ V vs. SHE})$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**
$$\text{H}_2 + \frac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
Hydrogen gas is fed to the anode, where a platinum catalyst splits H₂ into protons (H⁺) and electrons (e⁻). The protons migrate through the **Nafion™ membrane** to the cathode. The electrons, blocked by the membrane, are forced through an **external circuit** — this is the **useful DC electrical current**. At the cathode, the protons, electrons, and oxygen combine to form water and release heat. Multiple cells are stacked in series to achieve the high voltage (~300–400 V) required for vehicle traction.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Convert temperature to Kelvin:**
$$T = 70 + 273 = 343 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute the argument of the Nernst logarithm:**
$$Q = \frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}} = \frac{1.5 \times \sqrt{1.0}}{0.5} = \frac{1.5 \times 1}{0.5} = 3.0$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Calculate the Nernst voltage:**
$$E_{cell} = E^0_{cell} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln(Q)$$
$$E_{cell} = 1.19 + \frac{(8.314)(343)}{(2)(96485)} \ln(3.0)$$

Compute the thermal voltage:
$$\frac{RT}{nF} = \frac{8.314 \times 343}{2 \times 96485} = \frac{2851.7}{192970} = 0.01478 \text{ V}$$

Compute the logarithm:
$$\ln(3.0) = 1.0986$$

Therefore:
$$E_{cell} = 1.19 + (0.01478)(1.0986) = 1.19 + 0.01624$$
$$\boxed{E_{cell} \approx 1.206 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Calculate the LHV efficiency:**

Using Gibbs free energy and LHV of hydrogen:
$$\eta_{LHV} = \frac{\Delta G^0}{\Delta H_{LHV}} = \frac{237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}}{241.8 \text{ kJ/mol}} = 0.981$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{LHV} \approx 98.1\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** If the *Higher Heating Value* (HHV = 285.8 kJ/mol) is used instead of LHV, the efficiency becomes $\frac{237.2}{285.8} = 83\%$. KTU papers often specify which to use — read the question carefully.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **construction and working of a supercapacitor (EDLC)**. How is it different from a conventional capacitor and a battery? Mention two key EV applications.

**(b) [7 marks]** A supercapacitor module has the following specifications: **C = 100 F**, **Rated Voltage = 48 V**, **ESR = 10 mΩ**. Calculate:
1. The maximum energy stored (in Wh).
2. The peak discharge power.
3. The time constant of the module.
4. The time required to deliver 90% of the stored energy at constant power.

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Supercapacitor Construction and Working (7 marks)**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A supercapacitor consists of:
- **Two carbon electrodes** (typically activated carbon) with extremely high specific surface area (1500–3000 m²/g).
- **Two current collectors** (aluminum foils) coated with the carbon.
- A **separator** (porous polymer membrane) preventing physical contact between electrodes.
- An **electrolyte** (organic or aqueous) — provides ionic conductivity.
- The entire assembly is sealed in a cylindrical or prismatic metal can.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
When a voltage is applied, **positive ions** in the electrolyte accumulate at the negative electrode, and **negative ions** accumulate at the positive electrode. This forms two thin "double layers" of charge at each electrode-electrolyte interface, each acting as a nanoscale capacitor. Because the separation distance $d$ is only ~0.3 nm (ionic radius), and the surface area $A$ is enormous, the resulting capacitance is in the **kilo-Farad** range.

**Differences Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | Conventional Capacitor | Supercapacitor (EDLC) | Battery (Li-ion) |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Storage Mechanism | Electrostatic | Electrostatic (double-layer) | Chemical (Faradaic) |
| Energy Density | Very low | 5–10 Wh/kg | 100–250 Wh/kg |
| Cycle Life | Infinite | > 1,000,000 | 1000–3000 |

**Two EV Applications (1 mark):**
1. Regenerative braking energy capture in city buses.
2. Peak power assist during acceleration in hybrid EVs (e.g., Lamborghini Sian).

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:** $C = 100$ F, $V = 48$ V, $R_{ESR} = 10 \text{ m}\Omega = 0.01$ Ω

**1. Maximum Energy Stored (2 marks):**
$$E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times (48)^2$$
$$E_{max} = 50 \times 2304 = 115{,}200 \text{ J}$$

Converting to Wh:
$$E_{max} = \frac{115{,}200}{3600} = 32 \text{ Wh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{max} = 32 \text{ Wh}}$$

**2. Peak Discharge Power (2 marks):**
$$P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_{ESR}} = \frac{(48)^2}{4 \times 0.01} = \frac{2304}{0.04}$$
$$\boxed{P_{max} = 57{,}600 \text{ W} = 57.6 \text{ kW}}$$

**3. Time Constant (1.5 marks):**
$$\tau = R_{ESR} \times C = 0.01 \times 100 = 1 \text{ second}$$
$$\boxed{\tau = 1 \text{ s}}$$

**4. Time to deliver 90% Energy at Constant Power (1.5 marks):**
The energy delivered at constant power $P$ in time $t$ is $E = P \times t$.
Setting $E = 0.9 \times E_{max}$ and using $P = P_{max}/2$ (typical average):

$$t = \frac{0.9 \times E_{max}}{0.5 \times P_{max}} = \frac{0.9 \times 115{,}200}{0.5 \times 57{,}600} = \frac{103{,}680}{28{,}800} = 3.6 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 3.6 \text{ seconds}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the exponent 1/2 on $P_{O_2}$ in the Nernst equation.** The reaction is $\frac{1}{2}O_2$, not $O_2$. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction if missed.
>
> 2. **Confusing HHV (285.8 kJ/mol) with LHV (241.8 kJ/mol) of hydrogen** when computing efficiency. The question *always* specifies — read it carefully.
>
> 3. **Using $J = mr^2$ instead of $J = \frac{1}{2}mr^2$ for a solid disc flywheel.** If the question says "solid cylinder", the formula is $J = \frac{1}{2}mr^2$. If "hollow cylinder/hoop", then $J = mr^2$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s in flywheel energy calculation.** $1 \text{ rpm} = \frac{2\pi}{60}$ rad/s. Always include this step explicitly.
>
> 5. **Confusing "active" and "passive" HESS topologies.** In a *passive* HESS, the supercapacitor and battery are connected in **direct parallel** with **no DC-DC converter**. In a *fully-active* HESS, **both** are connected via **bidirectional DC-DC converters**. Marks are lost for mislabelling the diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Page Rapid-Revision Summary**

- [x] **Fuel Cell** is a *converter*, not a storage device. Single cell voltage ≈ **1.0–1.23 V**. PEMFC is the EV-preferred type.
- [x] **Nernst Equation** for H₂/O₂ FC: $E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{2F}\ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2}\sqrt{P_{O_2}}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$
- [x] **FC LHV Efficiency** = $\frac{237.2}{241.8} = 98\%$ (theoretical); practical ≈ **50–60%**.
- [x] **Supercapacitor** is an **electrostatic** device (EDLC). Stores charge at the electrode-electrolyte interface, **not** chemically.
- [x] **Capacitance** $C = \frac{\varepsilon A}{d}$; huge $A$ and tiny $d$ → thousands of Farads.
- [x] **SC Energy** $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$. **SC Peak Power** $P = \frac{V^2}{4R_{ESR}}$.
- [x] **Flywheel** stores **kinetic energy** $E = \frac{1}{2}J\omega^2$; $E \propto \omega^2$.
- [x] **Flywheel Specific Energy Limit** = $\frac{\sigma_{uts}}{\rho}$ (set by rotor material's tensile strength).
- [x] **HESS** combines high-energy + high-power sources via power-electronic converters and an **EMS**.
- [x] **Three HESS topologies**: Passive, Semi-Active, Fully-Active.
- [x] **Ragone Plot** is a log-log plot of Specific Energy vs Specific Power.
- [x] **Ragone Positions** (rough): FC top-left, Battery top-right, Flywheel mid-right, SC bottom-right, ICE far top-right.
- [x] **KERS** (Kinetic Energy Recovery System) in Formula 1 uses a **flywheel**, not a battery.
- [x] **Toyota Mirai** is the most-produced fuel cell EV; uses a **PEMFC stack + Li-ion battery buffer**.
- [x] **Mazda i-ELOOP** uses a **supercapacitor** (branded as *i-ELOOP*) for regenerative braking.
- [x] **Constant to memorize**: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- [x] **EV Energy Management System (EMS)** implements control strategies: **Rule-based**, **Fuzzy Logic**, **Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy (ECMS)**, and **Dynamic Programming**.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Battery Sizing — The Formal Definition

**Battery Sizing** is the engineering process of selecting the appropriate **Energy capacity (kWh)**, **Power capability (kW)**, **Voltage (V)**, and **Chemistry** of a battery pack to meet the specified performance requirements of an Electric Vehicle (EV) over its operational life. It is a constrained multi-variable optimization problem that simultaneously satisfies:

- **Range requirement** (driving distance per charge)
- **Performance requirement** (acceleration, top speed, gradeability)
- **Cycle life requirement** (number of charge/discharge cycles)
- **Weight and packaging constraints**
- **Cost and thermal constraints**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Battery sizing determines the rated capacity ($Q$ in Ah), energy ($E$ in kWh), and pack voltage ($V_{pack}$) by balancing the energy demand of the traction motor, the auxiliary loads, the cycle life expectations, and the Depth-of-Discharge (DOD) limits imposed by the chemistry.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel Tank" and the "Water Bucket" Analogy

Imagine your battery is a **large water bucket with a small tap at the bottom**.

- **Full bucket** = $100\%$ State of Charge (SOC) → Beginning of Life (BOL)
- **Empty bucket** = $0\%$ SOC → End of useful charge for that cycle
- **How much water you actually take out per cycle** = Depth of Discharge (DOD)
- **Number of times you can refill and empty** before the bucket develops cracks/leaks = **Cycle Life**
- **When the bucket can no longer hold more than 80% of the original water** = End of Life (EOL)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy for Energy vs Power:** A **large water tank** (high energy) supplies a long drive range, but a **wide pipe** (high power) is what allows a waterfall flow (acceleration). Battery sizing must independently address **Energy ($kWh$)** and **Power ($kW$)**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology — Standardized Definitions

| Term | Standard Symbol | Formal Definition | Typical Unit |
|------|----------------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Depth of Discharge** | DOD | Fraction of battery capacity removed in a cycle | **%** or **fraction** |
| **State of Charge** | SOC | Remaining usable capacity as a fraction of rated | **%** or **fraction** |
| **Beginning of Life** | BOL | Battery state when first commissioned | — |
| **End of Life** | EOL | Point at which capacity drops to **80% of rated** | — |
| **Cycle Life** | $N_{cycle}$ | Number of charge/discharge cycles before EOL | **cycles** |
| **Calendar Life** | $t_{cal}$ | Time duration (irrespective of use) before EOL | **years** |
| **C-rate** | $C$ | Rate of charge/discharge normalized to capacity | **$h^{-1}$** |
| **Pack Efficiency** | $\eta_{pack}$ | Ratio of output energy to input energy over a cycle | **%** |
| **Nominal Pack Voltage** | $V_{pack}$ | Total series string voltage of cells | **V (Volts)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Standard:** When solving numerical problems, the universally accepted EOL threshold is when the battery can deliver only **80%** of its original rated capacity, regardless of chemistry. This corresponds to a **20%** permanent capacity loss.

## 1.4 The Relationship: SOC, DOD, and the Forbidden Zone

$$
\text{SOC}(\%) + \text{DOD}(\%) = 100\%
$$

This is the **most-tested relationship** in the KTU board paper. However, in real EVs we never allow DOD to reach $100\%$ because deep discharge permanently damages the cell. Hence:

$$
\text{Usable DOD} = (SOC_{max} - SOC_{min}) \times 100\%
$$

Typical design limits for Lithium-ion chemistries:

| Chemistry | Usable DOD Window | Reserve SOC Buffer |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **LFP (LiFePO₄)** | $10\% - 90\%$ | $20\%$ |
| **NMC (Li-Ni-Mn-Co)** | $20\% - 80\%$ | $40\%$ |
| **LTO (Li-Titanate)** | $5\% - 95\%$ | $10\%$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students equate the **rated pack capacity** with the **usable capacity**. The rated capacity is the *total* stored energy, but the *usable* capacity is always smaller by a factor equal to the DOD window. Failure to apply this correction is the single largest source of mark loss in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of Battery Sizing

The sizing of a battery pack for an EV is governed by **seven interdependent pillars**. Each pillar produces a sizing equation, and the *most conservative* (largest) value is selected for the final design.

### Pillar 1 — Energy Sizing (Range-based)

The battery must store enough energy to propel the vehicle over the target range $R$ (km).

$$
E_{battery} = \frac{E_{consumed\ per\ km} \times R}{\eta_{powertrain} \times \text{DOD}_{usable}}
$$

### Pillar 2 — Power Sizing (Performance-based)

The battery must deliver peak power for acceleration and grade climbing.

$$
P_{peak} = \frac{P_{motor,peak}}{\eta_{motor} \times \eta_{inverter}}
$$

### Pillar 3 — Voltage Sizing

The pack voltage must match the motor inverter's DC bus voltage window.

$$
V_{pack} = N_{series} \times V_{cell,nominal}
$$

### Pillar 4 — Capacity Sizing (Ah-based)

The ampere-hour rating determines the discharge duration at a given load.

$$
Q_{pack} = N_{parallel} \times Q_{cell}
$$

### Pillar 5 — Cycle Life Sizing

The pack must survive the required number of cycles.

$$
N_{cycles} \ge N_{required} = \frac{\text{Total lifetime km}}{\text{Range per cycle}}
$$

### Pillar 6 — Thermal Sizing

The pack must dissipate heat at a rate consistent with the cell chemistry's safe temperature window.

$$
Q_{heat} = I^2 R_{cell} = \text{cooling\ load}
$$

### Pillar 7 — Cost & Weight Sizing

Specific energy ($Wh/kg$) and energy density ($Wh/L$) constraints.

$$
m_{pack} = \frac{E_{battery}}{\text{Specific Energy of Chemistry}}
$$

## 2.2 Time, Charge/Discharge Cycles, and C-rate

A **cycle** is defined as one full charge followed by one full discharge. The **C-rate** describes the speed at which a battery is charged or discharged relative to its maximum capacity.

$$
\text{C-rate} = \frac{I\ (\text{Amps})}{Q\ (\text{Ah})}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** A **1C** rate on a 100 Ah battery means a current of 100 A. A **0.5C** rate means 50 A (full charge/discharge in 2 hours). A **2C** rate means 200 A (full charge/discharge in 30 minutes).

**Time to fully discharge (or charge) at constant C-rate:**

$$
t_{discharge} = \frac{Q_{usable}}{I} = \frac{Q_{rated} \times \text{DOD}}{C \times Q_{rated}} = \frac{\text{DOD}}{C}
$$

## 2.3 Battery Lifetime — Cycle Life and Calendar Life

Battery lifetime has **two simultaneous degradation mechanisms** that operate in parallel:

1. **Cycle Aging (Cyclable degradation):** Degradation proportional to the energy cycled (Ah-throughput).
2. **Calendar Aging (Time-based degradation):** Degradation proportional to the time elapsed, even when idle.

$$
\text{Total Fade} = f_1(\text{Ah-throughput}) + f_2(\text{time}, T)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Law (Arrhenius-type):** Calendar aging roughly doubles for every **$10^\circ C$** rise in storage temperature. This is a *high-yield* KTU short-answer fact.

The **End of Life (EOL)** criterion in EV applications is universally:

$$
\text{Capacity at EOL} = 0.80 \times \text{Capacity at BOL}
$$

$$
\text{Resistance at EOL} \approx 2 \times \text{Resistance at BOL}
$$

## 2.4 Efficiency of the Battery Pack

The battery pack efficiency is the product of two distinct efficiency factors:

### 2.4.1 Coulombic Efficiency ($\eta_C$)

The ratio of charge extracted during discharge to the charge supplied during charge.

$$
\eta_C = \frac{Q_{discharged}}{Q_{charged}}
$$

### 2.4.2 Energy (Voltage) Efficiency ($\eta_V$)

The ratio of average discharge voltage to average charge voltage.

$$
\eta_V = \frac{V_{avg,discharge}}{V_{avg,charge}}
$$

### 2.4.3 Round-Trip Energy Efficiency ($\eta_{pack}$)

$$
\eta_{pack} = \eta_C \times \eta_V
$$

Typical values:

| Chemistry | $\eta_C$ | $\eta_V$ | $\eta_{pack}$ |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| **LFP** | 99.9% | 92% | ~92% |
| **NMC** | 99.5% | 96% | ~95% |
| **LTO** | 99.9% | 95% | ~95% |
| **Lead-Acid** | 90% | 80% | ~72% |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Pack Voltage | $V_{pack} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$ | V | $350 - 800$ V |
| 2 | Pack Capacity (Ah) | $Q_{pack} = N_p \cdot Q_{cell}$ | Ah | $50 - 400$ Ah |
| 3 | Pack Energy | $E_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot Q_{pack}$ | Wh | $30 - 100$ kWh |
| 4 | C-rate | $C = I / Q$ | $h^{-1}$ | $0.2 - 3$ |
| 5 | Discharge Time | $t = DOD / C$ | hours | varies |
| 6 | Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{pack} = \eta_C \cdot \eta_V$ | dimensionless | $0.85 - 0.95$ |
| 7 | Energy Required | $E_b = E_{km} \cdot R / (\eta_{pt} \cdot DOD)$ | kWh | vehicle-specific |
| 8 | EOL Threshold | $Q_{EOL} = 0.80 \cdot Q_{BOL}$ | Ah | fixed |
| 9 | Cycle Life vs DOD | $N \approx N_{100} / (DOD)^{k}$ | cycles | $k \approx 1.5$ |
| 10 | Arrhenius Aging | $\text{Fade} \propto e^{-E_a / (R T)}$ | — | doubles per 10°C |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** When asked "Determine the pack voltage," always begin by stating the **inverter DC bus voltage requirement** and then reverse-engineer the series cell count. Marks are awarded for the *methodology*, not just the final number.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Determination of Pack Voltage ($V_{pack}$)

The pack voltage is *not* an arbitrary design choice; it is **constrained by the inverter DC bus voltage** and the **cell's open-circuit voltage curve**.

**Step 1 — Identify the Inverter DC Bus Voltage**

Modern EV inverters operate on a high-voltage DC bus to reduce cable current (and thus $I^2R$ losses). Typical inverter DC bus voltages:

| Vehicle Class | DC Bus Voltage |
|---------------|----------------|
| Small EV (e-scooter) | $48 - 96$ V |
| Compact EV (Tata Nexon EV) | $350 - 400$ V |
| Premium EV (Tesla Model 3) | $400$ V |
| High-Performance EV (Porsche Taycan) | $800$ V |

**Step 2 — Select the Cell Chemistry and Nominal Voltage**

Common nominal cell voltages:

| Chemistry | $V_{cell,nom}$ | $V_{cell,min}$ | $V_{cell,max}$ |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| LFP | 3.2 V | 2.5 V | 3.65 V |
| NMC | 3.7 V | 3.0 V | 4.2 V |
| NCA | 3.6 V | 3.0 V | 4.2 V |
| LTO | 2.3 V | 1.5 V | 2.8 V |

**Step 3 — Compute the Number of Cells in Series**

$$
N_s = \frac{V_{DC\ bus,min}}{V_{cell,min}}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Maximum Pack Voltage**

$$
V_{pack,max} = N_s \cdot V_{cell,max}
$$

This value must be within the inverter's **maximum allowable DC bus voltage** (with a safety margin of $5 - 10\%$).

**Step 5 — Compute the Nominal Pack Voltage**

$$
V_{pack,nom} = N_s \cdot V_{cell,nom}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — Worked Numerical Example (Pack Sizing)

**Problem:** Design the battery pack for a compact EV with the following requirements:
- Daily commute range: $R = 250$ km
- Energy consumption: $E_{km} = 150$ Wh/km
- Powertrain efficiency: $\eta_{pt} = 0.85$
- Chemistry: NMC cells with nominal voltage $3.7$ V, capacity $50$ Ah
- Inverter DC bus: $350$ V (nominal), $400$ V (max)
- Usable DOD: $80\%$

**Step 1 — Compute Required Pack Energy**

$$
E_{req} = \frac{E_{km} \cdot R}{\eta_{pt} \cdot DOD} = \frac{150 \times 250}{0.85 \times 0.80}
$$

$$
E_{req} = \frac{37{,}500}{0.68} = 55{,}147\ \text{Wh} \approx 55.15\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine Series Cell Count**

$$
N_s = \frac{V_{DC\ bus}}{V_{cell,nom}} = \frac{350}{3.7} = 94.6 \approx 95\ \text{cells}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Actual Pack Nominal Voltage**

$$
V_{pack,nom} = 95 \times 3.7 = 351.5\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Maximum Pack Voltage**

$$
V_{pack,max} = 95 \times 4.2 = 399\ \text{V} \le 400\ \text{V}\ \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Pack Nominal Energy (with 95 cells in series, 1 parallel string)**

$$
E_{pack,nom} = 351.5 \times 50 = 17{,}575\ \text{Wh} = 17.58\ \text{kWh}
$$

This is too small. We need **more parallel strings**.

**Step 6 — Compute Required Parallel Strings**

$$
N_p = \frac{E_{req}}{E_{pack,nom}} = \frac{55{,}147}{17{,}575} = 3.14 \approx 4\ \text{strings}
$$

**Step 7 — Final Pack Configuration**

$$
\text{Configuration: 95S4P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{pack} = 351.5\ \text{V}, \quad Q_{pack} = 200\ \text{Ah}
$$

$$
E_{pack,final} = 351.5 \times 200 = 70{,}300\ \text{Wh} = 70.3\ \text{kWh}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The 95S4P configuration gives $70.3$ kWh, which exceeds the $55.15$ kWh minimum by a factor of $1.27$. This is the **design margin** to compensate for aging, auxiliary loads, and reserve capacity.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Cycle Life Estimation Using the Peukert-Like Law

Battery cycle life depends strongly on the **average DOD per cycle**. A widely used empirical relationship is:

$$
N_{cycle}(DOD) = N_{100\%} \cdot \left(\frac{100\%}{DOD}\right)^{k}
$$

where $N_{100\%}$ is the cycle life at $100\%$ DOD and $k \approx 1.4 - 1.7$ for NMC cells.

**Worked Example:**
A cell is rated for $1500$ cycles at $100\%$ DOD. Estimate the cycle life if the average DOD is restricted to $60\%$.

$$
N_{60\%} = 1500 \times \left(\frac{100}{60}\right)^{1.5} = 1500 \times (1.667)^{1.5}
$$

$$
N_{60\%} = 1500 \times 2.151 = 3227\ \text{cycles}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Limiting DOD to $60\%$ more than **doubles** the cycle life. This is why EVs use a *buffer zone* (e.g., using only $20 - 80\%$ of the actual capacity) — the user pays a small penalty in range for a large gain in longevity.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Pack Efficiency Calculation

**Problem:** A $400$ V, $100$ Ah Li-ion pack is charged at a constant current of $50$ A at an average voltage of $410$ V. It is then discharged at $50$ A at an average voltage of $380$ V until $80\%$ DOD is reached.

**Step 1 — Charge Supplied**

$$
Q_{charged} = 50\ \text{A} \times t_{charge} = Q_{rated} \times DOD / 0.5C = 100 \times 0.8 / 0.5 = 160\ \text{Ah}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy Input**

$$
E_{in} = V_{avg,charge} \cdot Q_{charged} = 410 \times 160 = 65{,}600\ \text{Wh} = 65.6\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Output**

$$
E_{out} = V_{avg,discharge} \cdot Q_{discharged} = 380 \times 160 = 60{,}800\ \text{Wh} = 60.8\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Round-Trip Efficiency**

$$
\eta_{pack} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} = \frac{60{,}800}{65{,}600} = 0.9269 \approx 92.7\%
$$

**Step 5 — Voltage Efficiency**

$$
\eta_V = \frac{380}{410} = 0.9268 \approx 92.7\%
$$

**Step 6 — Coulombic Efficiency**

$$
\eta_C = \frac{Q_{discharged}}{Q_{charged}} = 1.0\ (\text{for this idealized example})
$$

**Step 7 — Verification**

$$
\eta_{pack} = \eta_C \times \eta_V = 1.0 \times 0.9268 = 0.9268 \approx 92.7\%\ \checkmark
$$

## 3.5 Derivation V — Sizing from BOL to EOL (Capacity Fade)

**Problem:** A pack is rated at $60$ kWh at BOL. If the vehicle is driven $30{,}000$ km/year and consumes $150$ Wh/km, estimate the years to reach EOL.

**Step 1 — Annual Energy Throughput**

$$
E_{annual} = 30{,}000 \times 150 = 4{,}500{,}000\ \text{Wh} = 4500\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual DOD Cycles (at $80\%$ usable DOD)**

$$
\text{Energy per full cycle} = 60 \times 0.80 = 48\ \text{kWh}
$$

$$
N_{cycles\ per\ year} = \frac{4500}{48} = 93.75\ \text{equivalent full cycles}
$$

**Step 3 — EOL Criterion**

$$
\text{Capacity at EOL} = 0.80 \times 60 = 48\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Time to EOL (assuming linear fade for simplicity)**

$$
\text{Total fade allowed} = 20\% = 12\ \text{kWh}
$$

$$
\text{Fade per cycle} \approx \frac{12\ \text{kWh}}{N_{total\ cycles\ at\ DOD\ of\ 80\%}}
$$

For a typical NMC cell at $80\%$ DOD: $N_{total} \approx 3000$ cycles.

$$
\text{Fade per cycle} = \frac{12}{3000} = 0.004\ \text{kWh/cycle} = 4\ \text{Wh/cycle}
$$

$$
\text{Years to EOL} = \frac{12{,}000\ \text{Wh}}{4\ \text{Wh/cycle} \times 93.75\ \text{cycles/year}} = \frac{12{,}000}{375} = 32\ \text{years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Correction:** In practice, calendar aging reduces this to $10 - 15$ years. Calendar aging is the **dominant degradation mode** in low-utilization EVs.
