# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define the terms **Beginning of Life (BOL)** and **End of Life (EOL)** as applied to EV battery packs. State the standard EOL capacity threshold used by the EV industry.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Beginning of Life (BOL):** The state of a battery pack at the time of its first commissioning in the vehicle, when the pack delivers $100\%$ of its rated capacity, has the lowest internal resistance, and has undergone zero aging. (1.5 marks)

- **End of Life (EOL):** The state of a battery pack when its maximum deliverable capacity has degraded to a defined fraction of the BOL capacity. The universally accepted EV-industry threshold is when the capacity falls to **$80\%$ of the rated BOL value** (i.e., a $20\%$ permanent capacity loss). At this point, the pack is typically retired from traction use and may be repurposed for stationary storage. (1.5 marks)

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** What is **Depth of Discharge (DOD)**? Why is a usable DOD of only $20 - 80\%$ (rather than $0 - 100\%$) typically employed in EV battery management systems?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Depth of Discharge (DOD)** is the percentage of the rated battery capacity that is removed from a fully charged cell during a discharge cycle. Mathematically, $DOD = 1 - SOC$. (1 mark)

- The usable DOD window is restricted to $20 - 80\%$ (or similar) for three reasons: (2 marks)
  1. **Cycle life extension:** Cycling within a shallow DOD window dramatically increases the number of achievable cycles (per the $N \propto DOD^{-k}$ empirical law).
  2. **Safety:** Operating above $4.2$ V/cell (NMC) or below $2.5$ V/cell risks thermal runaway, lithium plating, and cell rupture.
  3. **Reserve capacity:** The $20\%$ buffer at the top and bottom of the SOC range absorbs capacity fade due to aging, ensuring the vehicle retains usable range even at the end of life.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 - Model Paper Style]`**

**(a)** An electric two-wheeler is to be designed with the following specifications:
- Required range: $R = 100$ km
- Energy consumption: $E_{km} = 60$ Wh/km
- Powertrain efficiency: $\eta_{pt} = 0.80$
- Usable DOD: $DOD = 0.80$
- Cell type: NMC cylindrical $3.7$ V nominal, $20$ Ah capacity
- Inverter DC bus voltage: $72$ V (nominal)

Determine:
1. The required pack energy in kWh.
2. The series cell count $N_s$.
3. The parallel string count $N_p$.
4. The final pack configuration in **NsNp** notation and the resulting nominal pack voltage.
5. The actual achievable range if the DOD is restricted to $60\%$ for extended life.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Required Pack Energy:**

$$
E_{req} = \frac{E_{km} \cdot R}{\eta_{pt} \cdot DOD} = \frac{60 \times 100}{0.80 \times 0.80} = \frac{6000}{0.64} = 9375\ \text{Wh} = 9.375\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Series Cell Count:**

$$
N_s = \frac{V_{DC\ bus}}{V_{cell,nom}} = \frac{72}{3.7} = 19.46 \approx 20\ \text{cells in series}
$$

**Step 3 — Pack Nominal Voltage:**

$$
V_{pack,nom} = 20 \times 3.7 = 74\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Pack Nominal Energy (1P configuration):**

$$
E_{pack,1P} = 74 \times 20 = 1480\ \text{Wh} = 1.48\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Parallel Strings Required:**

$$
N_p = \frac{E_{req}}{E_{pack,1P}} = \frac{9375}{1480} = 6.33 \approx 7\ \text{parallel strings}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Configuration: 20S7P**

$$
E_{pack,final} = 74 \times 140 = 10{,}360\ \text{Wh} = 10.36\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 7 — Achievable Range at $DOD = 0.60$:**

$$
R_{new} = \frac{E_{pack,final} \cdot \eta_{pt} \cdot DOD}{E_{km}} = \frac{10360 \times 0.80 \times 0.60}{60} = \frac{4972.8}{60} = 82.88\ \text{km}
$$

**Final Answer Box:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Required Energy | $9.375$ kWh |
| Series Cells $N_s$ | $20$ |
| Parallel Strings $N_p$ | $7$ |
| Configuration | 20S7P |
| Pack Voltage | $74$ V |
| Pack Energy | $10.36$ kWh |
| Range at 60% DOD | $82.88$ km |

---

**(b)** With reference to EV battery packs, explain the following with their governing equations:
1. Coulombic efficiency and voltage (energy) efficiency
2. Round-trip efficiency of the pack
3. C-rate and its effect on usable capacity (Peukert effect)

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**1. Coulombic Efficiency and Voltage Efficiency:**

- **Coulombic efficiency ($\eta_C$):** The ratio of charge extracted during discharge to the charge supplied during charging. It accounts for the parasitic side reactions (e.g., SEI growth, lithium plating) that consume charge without delivering usable output.
$$
\eta_C = \frac{Q_{discharged}}{Q_{charged}}
$$

- **Voltage (Energy) efficiency ($\eta_V$):** The ratio of average voltage during discharge to average voltage during charge. It accounts for polarization losses (ohmic, kinetic, and concentration).
$$
\eta_V = \frac{\bar{V}_{discharge}}{\bar{V}_{charge}}
$$

**2. Round-Trip Efficiency:**

The overall pack efficiency is the product of the two efficiencies:
$$
\eta_{pack} = \eta_C \times \eta_V
$$

For a typical Li-ion pack, $\eta_{pack} \approx 0.90 - 0.95$.

**3. C-rate and the Peukert Effect:**

The **C-rate** is the discharge or charge current normalized to the cell's rated capacity:
$$
C = \frac{I}{Q_{rated}}
$$

According to the **Peukert effect** (originally for lead-acid, but qualitatively applicable to all chemistries), the usable capacity of a battery decreases as the discharge current increases:
$$
Q_{usable} = Q_{rated} \cdot C^{-k}
$$

where $k$ is the Peukert exponent ($k \approx 1.05 - 1.20$ for Li-ion, $\approx 1.20 - 1.40$ for lead-acid).

**Numerical illustration:**

For a $100$ Ah Li-ion cell with $k = 1.10$:
- At $C = 0.5$ ($50$ A): $Q_{usable} = 100 \times 0.5^{-1.10} = 100 \times 2.143 = 214.3$ Ah-equivalent
- At $C = 2$ ($200$ A): $Q_{usable} = 100 \times 2^{-1.10} = 100 \times 0.466 = 46.6$ Ah-equivalent

This shows that at high C-rates, the effective deliverable capacity falls sharply — a key reason why EV packs are sized with C-rate margins.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper Style]`**

**(a)** Define **calendar life** and **cycle life** of a battery. An NMC battery cell is rated at $2000$ cycles at $100\%$ DOD. Estimate its cycle life if it is cycled at $50\%$ DOD using the empirical law $N_{cycle}(DOD) = N_{100\%} \cdot (100/DOD)^{1.5}$. State the design implications of your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definitions:**

- **Cycle life** is the number of full charge-discharge cycles a battery can undergo before its capacity falls to the EOL threshold ($80\%$ of BOL).
- **Calendar life** is the total elapsed time (in years) a battery can remain in service, regardless of cycling, before reaching EOL. Calendar life is primarily governed by storage temperature and SOC.

**Cycle Life Calculation:**

Given: $N_{100\%} = 2000$ cycles, $DOD = 50\%$, $k = 1.5$.

$$
N_{50\%} = 2000 \times \left(\frac{100}{50}\right)^{1.5} = 2000 \times 2^{1.5} = 2000 \times 2.828 = 5657\ \text{cycles}
$$

**Design Implications:**

1. The cycle life increases by a factor of $\approx 2.83$ when DOD is reduced from $100\%$ to $50\%$.
2. The energy throughput per cycle is halved, so the *total energy delivered* over life is $5657 \times 0.5 \times Q_{rated} \cdot V_{nom} = 2.83$ times the $100\%$-DOD throughput — a substantial improvement.
3. The trade-off is reduced range per cycle; the user must accept a smaller buffer for the same pack size, OR carry a larger pack to meet the range target.
4. Most production EVs use $20 - 80\%$ SOC window (equivalent to $60\%$ DOD) to balance range, life, and cost.

---

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **functional architecture of a battery pack** and the role of the Battery Management System (BMS) in ensuring safe and efficient operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Block Diagram (in text form):**

The battery pack architecture consists of three hierarchical levels:

1. **Cell Level:** Individual electrochemical cells (cylindrical, prismatic, or pouch), each with nominal voltage $3.2 - 3.7$ V and capacity $2 - 100$ Ah.

2. **Module Level:** $10 - 20$ cells connected in series, monitored by a **Slave BMS** that reports cell voltages and temperatures to the master controller.

3. **Pack Level:** $8 - 12$ modules in series, monitored by a **Master BMS** that computes SOC, State of Health (SOH), State of Power (SOP), and performs cell balancing. The pack interfaces with the vehicle through a **Battery Control Unit (BCU)** via CAN bus.

**BMS Functions:**

| Function | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| **Cell Voltage Monitoring** | Detect over-voltage (>4.2 V) and under-voltage (<2.5 V) to prevent damage. |
| **Temperature Monitoring** | Trigger cooling or heating; cut off at $> 60^\circ C$. |
| **SOC Estimation** | Coulomb counting + Kalman filter for accurate range display. |
| **SOH Estimation** | Track capacity fade and resistance growth to predict EOL. |
| **Cell Balancing** | Equalize cell voltages (passive: resistors; active: DC-DC converters). |
| **Isolation Monitoring** | Detect high-voltage leaks to chassis for safety. |
| **Communication** | CAN / LIN bus to VCU, charger, and dashboard. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When asked about BMS, many students write only "BMS monitors the battery." This earns only **1 out of 7 marks**. The examiner expects you to enumerate **at least 5 specific BMS functions** with their voltage or temperature thresholds. Always write in **bullet points** with **specific numerical limits**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **1. Confusing kWh and kW:** Energy is in **kWh**, power is in **kW**. A pack with $400$ V and $100$ Ah stores $40$ **kWh** of *energy*, not *power*. Mixing these units costs **2 - 3 marks** instantly.

> [!WARNING]
> **2. Forgetting the DOD Buffer:** Using $100\%$ of rated capacity in range calculations inflates the range unrealistically. Always state the *usable* DOD (e.g., $80\%$) and apply it as a divisor.

> [!WARNING]
> **3. EOL Threshold Slip:** Some students use $70\%$ or $90\%$ as the EOL cutoff. The **standard, KTU-accepted** value is **$80\%$**. Any other value must be explicitly justified.

> [!WARNING]
> **4. Series vs Parallel Confusion:** Series connections **increase voltage**; parallel connections **increase capacity (Ah)**. Energy = $V \times Q$. Many students mistakenly compute $E = N_s \times N_p \times V \times Q$ (which is correct) but get the wrong units by mixing series strings with parallel cells inconsistently.

> [!WARNING]
> **5. Ignoring $\eta_{pt}$:** Always include powertrain efficiency in the energy demand calculation. A problem stating $\eta_{pt} = 0.85$ expects you to divide by $0.85$, not to use it as a multiplier of range.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

- **Sizing is multi-objective:** Energy, power, voltage, life, weight, and cost must all be satisfied simultaneously — the largest required value dictates the final pack size.

- **DOD and SOC are complements:** $DOD = 1 - SOC$ (in fraction). For range calculations, use the **usable DOD**, not the rated capacity.

- **C-rate governs speed, not energy:** A higher C-rate delivers more power but reduces usable energy (Peukert effect). $t_{discharge} = DOD / C$.

- **BOL to EOL threshold is $80\%$:**
  - At BOL: $100\%$ capacity, minimum resistance.
  - At EOL: $80\%$ capacity, $\approx 2\times$ resistance.

- **Cycle life and DOD are inversely related (nonlinearly):**
  $N_{cycle} = N_{100\%} \cdot (100/DOD)^k$ with $k \approx 1.4 - 1.7$ for Li-ion.

- **Calendar aging is temperature-driven:** Doubles for every $10^\circ C$ rise. Keep the pack cool!

- **Pack efficiency = Coulombic × Voltage efficiency:**
  $\eta_{pack} = \eta_C \times \eta_V \approx 0.90 - 0.95$ for Li-ion.

- **Pack voltage determination steps:**
  1. Identify the inverter DC bus voltage requirement.
  2. Select cell chemistry and nominal voltage.
  3. Compute $N_s = V_{DC\ bus} / V_{cell,nom}$ (round up).
  4. Verify $V_{pack,max} = N_s \cdot V_{cell,max} \le V_{DC\ bus,max}$.
  5. The nominal pack voltage is $V_{pack,nom} = N_s \cdot V_{cell,nom}$.

- **Configuration notation:** **NsNp** (e.g., 95S4P = 95 cells in series, 4 in parallel).

- **Energy of a pack:** $E_{pack} = V_{pack,nom} \times Q_{pack} = N_s V_{cell} \times N_p Q_{cell}$ (in Wh, divide by $1000$ for kWh).

- **Power of a pack:** $P_{pack} = V_{pack,nom} \times I_{max,pack} = V_{pack,nom} \times N_p \times C_{max} \times Q_{cell}$.

- **Standard cell voltages to memorize:** LFP = 3.2 V; NMC = 3.7 V; LTO = 2.3 V; Lead-Acid = 2.0 V.

- **Standard EOL threshold:** $80\%$ of BOL capacity (universal across EV industry).

- **Arrhenius aging rule:** Calendar fade doubles per $10^\circ C$ rise in storage temperature.

- **Three numbers always end your answer:** Pack voltage in V, pack energy in kWh, and configuration in NsNp notation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Range for EV - Determination of Cell/Pack Voltage for a Given Output/Input Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Range for EV - Determination of Cell/Pack Voltage for a Given Output/Input Power # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Driving Range of an Electric Vehicle** is defined as the maximum distance (typically expressed in **kilometers (km)** or **miles (mi)**) that a fully charged EV can travel on a single charge under a standard driving cycle (e.g., WLTP, FTP-75, or Indian Drive Cycle IDC) before the battery pack reaches its minimum allowable State of Charge (SOC), generally taken as **20%** for lithium-ion chemistries to prevent deep discharge damage. The **determination of cell/pack voltage for a given output/input power** is the engineering design procedure where the required **battery pack nominal voltage (V_pack)** is calculated based on the peak/average tractive power demanded by the vehicle's propulsion system, the chosen cell's nominal voltage (V_cell), the pack's energy capacity, and the permissible current limits of the Battery Management System (BMS). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a core design foundation. Students must master the link between tractive force, power flow, and the resulting battery sizing (both voltage and energy). Questions typically appear in **Part B (14-mark)** format involving numerical design calculations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of an EV battery like a **water tank connected to a tap (motor)**: - **Voltage (V)** is like the **water pressure** (how strongly water pushes out). - **Current (I)** is the **flow rate of water** (how much water comes out per second). - **Power (P = V × I)** is the **total energy delivered per second** — equivalent to the force of the water jet times the flow rate. - **Energy (kWh)** is the **total water in the tank** — equivalent to how long you can keep the tap open. - **Range** is the **distance the water jet can push a small cart** before the tank empties. If your cart (vehicle) needs a big push (high power), you either increase the **pressure (higher pack voltage)** or the **flow rate (higher current)**. But high current generates heat and damages cells, so engineers prefer to **raise the voltage** by stacking many cells in series. The range, on the other hand, depends on the **total tank size (energy, kWh)** and how fast the cart consumes water (Wh/km). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit | |-----------|--------|----------------------| | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | | Air density (at sea level, 25 °C) | $\rho$ | **1.225 kg/m³** | | Coefficient of rolling resistance (car tires on asphalt) | $\mu_{rr}$ | **0.008 – 0.015** | | Aerodynamic drag coefficient (typical sedan) | $C_d$ | **0.25 – 0.35** | | Frontal area of a compact car | $A_f$ | **2.0 – 2.5 m²** | | Drivetrain efficiency (single-speed EV) | $\eta_{dt}$ | **0.85 – 0.92** | | Cell nominal voltage (Li-ion) | $V_{cell}$ | **3.6 – 3.7 V** | > [!NOTE] > **Definition – State of Charge (SOC):** SOC is the equivalent of a "fuel gauge" expressed as a percentage. **SOC = 100%** means fully charged; **SOC = 0%** means fully discharged. The usable SOC window in EVs is usually **20% to 95%** to maximize cycle life. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Vehicle Range The driving range of an EV is fundamentally governed by three pillars: 1. **Onboard Energy Storage:** Total usable energy in the battery pack, $E_{pack}$ (in kWh). 2. **Energy Consumption Rate:** How much energy the vehicle consumes per kilometer, $\varepsilon$ (in Wh/km or kWh/100 km). 3. **Operating Conditions:** Speed, terrain slope, payload, ambient temperature, and driving style. Mathematically: $$ R = \frac{E_{pack}}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{[km]} $$ Where the usable pack energy is: $$ E_{pack} = \frac{V_{pack} \times Q_{pack} \times (SOC_{max} - SOC_{min})}{1000} \quad \text{[kWh]} $$ with $Q_{pack}$ in Ah and $V_{pack}$ in V. ## 2.2 The Tractive Force Equation — The "Four Horsemen" of Vehicle Dynamics To compute the power the traction motor must deliver, we must first compute the **total tractive force** at the wheels. Four fundamental resistive forces oppose motion: ### (i) Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{rr}$) Caused by tire hysteresis deformation as the tire rolls on the road. $$ F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta) $$ For small road gradients, $\cos(\theta) \approx 1$. - **Why?** It is a velocity-independent force (mostly), so it dominates at low speeds. - **How to reduce?** Low-rolling-resistance tires, higher tire pressure, lightweight wheels. ### (ii) Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$) Caused by air displacement at high speeds. $$ F_{ad} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A_f \cdot C_d \cdot v^2 $$ - **Why?** It scales with the **square of velocity**, so it dominates at high speeds (> 60 km/h). - **How to reduce?** Streamlined body, low $C_d$, smooth underbody. ### (iii) Climbing (Grade) Force ($F_{cl}$) The component of vehicle weight acting along the slope during an ascent. $$ F_{cl} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) $$ - **Why?** Even a 5% gradient adds significant load. A 30% grade doubles the climb force. - **How to reduce?** Lower vehicle mass, regenerative braking on descents. ### (iv) Acceleration (Inertial) Force ($F_{acc}$) Newton's second law for linear acceleration, including rotational inertia of rotating parts (effective mass factor $\gamma$). $$ F_{acc} = m \cdot (1 + \gamma) \cdot a = \gamma \cdot m \cdot a $$ Typical $\gamma \approx 1.05$ to $1.10$ for passenger EVs. ### Total Tractive Force $$ \boxed{F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{cl} + F_{acc}} $$ ## 2.3 From Force to Power: The Tractive Power Once the tractive force is known, the **tractive power at the wheels** is: $$ P_{tractive} = F_t \cdot v \quad \text{[W]} $$ where $v$ is the linear velocity in m/s. This is the **mechanical output power** that the traction motor must produce at the wheel-road interface. ## 2.4 From Output to Input Power (Battery Side) Because every component in the drivetrain (motor controller/inverter, motor, gearbox, differential) consumes power, the **input power drawn from the battery** is always greater: $$ \boxed{P_{input} = \frac{P_{tractive}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{inverter} \cdot \eta_{transmission}}} $$ The combined product is often simplified as a **total drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dt}$**: $$ P_{input} = \frac{P_{tractive}}{\eta_{dt}} $$ where $\eta_{dt}$ typically lies between **0.80 and 0.92**. ## 2.5 Determining the Battery Pack Voltage Given a required input power $P_{input}$, the pack voltage is chosen based on: **(a) Maximum allowable discharge current:** $$ I_{max} = \frac{P_{input}}{V_{pack}} \leq I_{cell,max} \times N_{parallel} $$ **(b) Number of cells in series to achieve pack voltage:** $$ \boxed{N_{series} = \frac{V_{pack}}{V_{cell,nom}}} $$ **(c) Number of cells in parallel to achieve desired current capacity:** $$ \boxed{N_{parallel} = \frac{Q_{pack}}{Q_{cell}} = \frac{I_{max}}{I_{cell,max}}} $$ **(d) Total pack energy:** $$ E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack} \quad \text{[Wh]} $$ ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$ | Rolling resistance force | N | | 2 | $F_{ad} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot A_f \cdot C_d \cdot v^2$ | Aerodynamic drag force | N | | 3 | $F_{cl} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | Climbing (grade) force | N | | 4 | $F_{acc} = \gamma \cdot m \cdot a$ | Linear acceleration force | N | | 5 | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{cl} + F_{acc}$ | Total tractive force | N | | 6 | $P_{out} = F_t \cdot v$ | Tractive power at wheels | W | | 7 | $P_{in} = P_{out} \mid \eta_{dt}$ | Input power from battery | W | | 8 | $N_s = V_{pack} \mid V_{cell}$ | Series cells required | integer | | 9 | $N_p = Q_{pack} \mid Q_{cell}$ | Parallel branches required | integer | | 10 | $E_{pack} = V_{pack} \cdot Q_{pack} \mid 1000$ | Pack energy | kWh | | 11 | $R = E_{pack} \mid \varepsilon$ | Driving range | km | | 12 | $\varepsilon = P_{in} \mid v$ | Energy consumption rate | W·s/m = J/m ≈ Wh/km | > [!NOTE] > **Energy Conversion Note:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$. For range calculations, it is convenient to keep energy in **Wh** and power in **W**, because $1 \text{ W} = 1 \text{ J/s}$ and the unit $\text{Wh/km}$ is exactly what consumption meters display. ## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields - **Automotive OEM Design:** Tesla, Tata Motors, Mahindra Electric, and BYD use these exact equations during early-stage vehicle concept design to size the pack and motor. - **Fleet Operations:** Logistics companies (e.g., Amazon EV delivery vans, BlueDart) use range prediction models to plan routes and charging stops. - **Battery Second-Life Applications:** Used EV packs are repurposed for stationary storage; pack voltage must be matched to inverter DC bus specifications. - **Charging Infrastructure Design:** Range anxiety drives fast-charger placement; understanding range helps civil engineers locate charging stations along highways. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples ## 3.1 Derivation: From Required Range to Required Pack Energy **Given:** Target range $R$ (km), specific energy consumption $\varepsilon$ (Wh/km). **Step 1 — Recognize the physical definition of range.** Range is the integral of velocity over time until energy is depleted. For constant consumption and velocity: $$ R = \int_{0}^{t_{end}} v \, dt = v \cdot t_{end} $$ **Step 2 — Express total energy consumed.** The battery delivers a constant power $P_{in}$ over the trip duration $t_{end}$: $$ E_{consumed} = P_{in} \cdot t_{end} $$ **Step 3 — Combine Step 1 and Step 2.** Solving $t_{end} = R / v$ from Step 1: $$ E_{consumed} = P_{in} \cdot \frac{R}{v} $$ **Step 4 — Define specific energy consumption $\varepsilon$.** By definition, $\varepsilon = P_{in} / v$, so: $$ E_{consumed} = \varepsilon \cdot R $$ **Step 5 — Equate to usable pack energy.** $$ \boxed{E_{pack,usable} = \varepsilon \cdot R} $$ **Step 6 — Include usable SOC window.** If the total installed pack energy is $E_{pack,total}$ and only $\Delta SOC$ is usable: $$ E_{pack,usable} = E_{pack,total} \cdot \Delta SOC $$ Therefore: $$ \boxed{E_{pack,total} = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot R}{\Delta SOC}} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation: From Required Input Power to Pack Voltage **Step 1 — Start from input power.** $$ P_{in} = \frac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_{dt}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the tractive force components.** $$ P_{in} = \frac{\left( \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g + 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot A_f \cdot C_d \cdot v^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta + \gamma \cdot m \cdot a \right) \cdot v}{\eta_{dt}} $$ **Step 3 — Relate input power to pack voltage and current.** $$ P_{in} = V_{pack} \cdot I_{pack} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for pack voltage, given a current limit $I_{max}$.** $$ \boxed{V_{pack} = \frac{P_{in}}{I_{max}}} $$ **Step 5 — Determine number of series cells.** $$ N_{series} = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{pack}}{V_{cell,nom}}\right) $$ **Step 6 — Update pack voltage to actual (integer cell) value.** $$ V_{pack,actual} = N_{series} \cdot V_{cell,nom} $$ **Step 7 — Recompute current for actual pack voltage.** $$ I_{pack} = \frac{P_{in}}{V_{pack,actual}} $$ **Step 8 — Determine parallel branches for current capacity.** $$ N_{parallel} = \text{ceil}\left(\frac{I_{pack}}{I_{cell,max}}\right) $$ ## 3.3 KTU-Style Numerical Example (Full Solution) > **[KTU-Style Problem — Module 3, 14 Marks]** > A small electric car has the following specifications: > - Kerb mass $m = 1200$ kg (including 200 kg battery) > - Frontal area $A_f = 2.2$ m², drag coefficient $C_d = 0.30$ > - Rolling resistance coefficient $\mu_{rr} = 0.012$ > - Effective mass factor $\gamma = 1.08$ > - Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dt} = 0.88$ > - Maximum cruise speed $v_{max} = 100$ km/h on flat road > - Cell: Li-ion, $V_{cell} = 3.7$ V, $Q_{cell} = 50$ Ah, $I_{cell,max} = 150$ A > - Target range $R = 250$ km, usable SOC window = 80% (from 95% → 15%) **Required:** **(a)** Compute the tractive force and tractive power required at 100 km/h on a level road. **(b)** Determine the required input power and the battery pack voltage (number of cells in series). **(c)** Calculate the required pack energy, pack capacity, and verify that the range is met. ### Solution **(a) Tractive Force and Power** **Step 1 — Convert velocity.** $$ v = 100 \text{ km/h} = \frac{100 \times 1000}{3600} = 27.78 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Rolling resistance (flat road, $\theta = 0$).** $$ F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g = 0.012 \times 1200 \times 9.81 = 141.26 \text{ N} $$ **Step 3 — Aerodynamic drag.** $$ F_{ad} = 0.5 \times \rho \times A_f \times C_d \times v^2 $$ Substituting: $$ F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2.2 \times 0.30 \times (27.78)^2 $$ $$ F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2.2 \times 0.30 \times 771.6 $$ $$ F_{ad} = 311.9 \text{ N} $$ **Step 4 — Climbing force.** Flat road, so: $$ F_{cl} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(0) = 0 \text{ N} $$ **Step 5 — Acceleration force.** Cruising, not accelerating: $$ F_{acc} = 0 \text{ N} $$ **Step 6 — Total tractive force.** $$ F_t = 141.26 + 311.9 + 0 + 0 = 453.16 \text{ N} $$ **Step 7 — Tractive power at wheels (output power).** $$ P_{out} = F_t \cdot v = 453.16 \times 27.78 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{out} \approx 12{,}590 \text{ W} \approx 12.59 \text{ kW}} $$ **(b) Input Power and Pack Voltage** **Step 8 — Input power.** $$ P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_{dt}} = \frac{12{,}590}{0.88} = 14{,}307 \text{ W} \approx 14.31 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 9 — Peak power consideration.** Typical design uses a **peak power** $P_{peak} \approx 3 \times P_{cruise}$ to handle accelerations. Let us take: $$ P_{peak} \approx 3 \times 14.31 = 42.93 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 10 — Choose pack voltage from current limit.** Limiting pack current to a safe value, say $I_{max} = 200$ A: $$ V_{pack} = \frac{P_{peak}}{I_{max}} = \frac{42{,}930}{200} = 214.65 \text{ V} $$ **Step 11 — Choose number of series cells.** $$ N_{series} = \frac{214.65}{3.7} = 58.01 $$ Round up to nearest integer: $$ \boxed{N_{series} = 60 \text{ cells}} $$ **Step 12 — Actual pack voltage.** $$ V_{pack,actual} = 60 \times 3.7 = 222 \text{ V} $$ **Step 13 — Required parallel branches.** $$ I_{pack,peak} = \frac{42{,}930}{222} = 193.4 \text{ A} $$ $$ N_{parallel} = \text{ceil}\left(\frac{193.4}{150}\right) = \text{ceil}(1.29) = 2 $$ **Step 14 — Total pack capacity in Ah.** $$ Q_{pack} = 2 \times 50 = 100 \text{ Ah} $$ **(c) Range Verification** **Step 15 — Total pack energy.** $$ E_{pack,total} = \frac{V_{pack,actual} \times Q_{pack}}{1000} = \frac{222 \times 100}{1000} = 22.2 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step 16 — Usable energy (80% SOC window).** $$ E_{usable} = 22.2 \times 0.80 = 17.76 \text{ kWh} $$ **Step 17 — Specific energy consumption at cruise.** $$ \varepsilon = \frac{P_{in}}{v} = \frac{14{,}307}{27.78} = 515.0 \text{ W·s/m} = 515 \text{ Wh/km} $$ **Step 18 — Achievable range.** $$ R = \frac{E_{usable}}{\varepsilon} = \frac{17{,}760 \text{ Wh}}{515 \text{ Wh/km}} $$ $$ \boxed{R \approx 34.5 \text{ km}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **This range is far short of the 250 km target!** This tells us that at 100 km/h cruising with only 14 kW input, our consumption rate is **515 Wh/km**. To achieve 250 km, we would need either a much larger battery or the cruising speed must be lower (where rolling resistance dominates and energy consumption drops). For a **250 km target**, a more realistic pack would be 50–60 kWh (as in modern EVs like Tata Nexon EV / Tata Tiago EV). **Step 19 — Re-design for 250 km range.** $$ E_{pack,total,required} = \frac{\varepsilon \cdot R}{\Delta SOC} = \frac{515 \times 250}{0.80} $$ $$ E_{pack,total,required} = \frac{128{,}750}{0.80} = 160{,}938 \text{ Wh} \approx 161 \text{ kWh} $$ This is impractical; instead, we would **reduce cruise speed** to ~50 km/h where consumption drops, OR use a **larger 50–60 kWh pack** with a higher cruising consumption budget of ~150 Wh/km (achievable in real-world driving with regenerative braking and average speeds ~50 km/h). **Step 20 — Revised pack for 250 km with $\varepsilon = 160$ Wh/km.** $$ E_{pack,total} = \frac{160 \times 250}{0.80} = 50{,}000 \text{ Wh} = 50 \text{ kWh} $$ $$ Q_{pack} = \frac{50{,}000}{222} = 225.2 \text{ Ah} $$ $$ N_{parallel} = \text{ceil}\left(\frac{225.2}{50}\right) = 5 $$ Final pack: **60S5P**, 222 V nominal, 250 Ah, 55.5 kWh total. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Power Flow Architecture in an EV (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR BATT[Battery Pack
Vpack x Qpack] INV[Motor Controller
Inverter] MOT[Traction Motor
AC PMSM] GB[Transmission and
Differential] WH[Wheels and Tires] RD[Road Load Forces
Rolling Drag Grade Acceleration] BATT -->|DC Power| INV INV -->|3 phase AC| MOT MOT -->|Mechanical Torque| GB GB -->|Drive Torque| WH WH -->|Tractive Force| RD RD -->|Opposing Force| WH RD -->|Velocity v| BATT ``` ## 4.2 Cell-to-Pack Sizing Logic Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Required Output Power Pout] B[Compute Tractive Force Ft] C[Add Four Force Components] D[Multiply by velocity v] E[Pout = Ft x v] F[Pin = Pout / eta_dt] G[Choose Imax from BMS limits] H[Vpack_desired = Pin / Imax] I[Ns = ceil Vpack / Vcell] J[Vpack_actual = Ns x Vcell] K[Np = ceil Ipack / Icell_max] L[Qpack = Np x Qcell] M[Epack = Vpack x Qpack / 1000] N[Range R = Eusable / epsilon] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I I --> J J --> K K --> L L --> M M --> N ``` ## 4.3 Force Decomposition — Free Body Diagram (Conceptual Block) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Forces_Acting_On_EV FRR[F_rr Rolling Resistance
Acts opposite to motion] FAD[F_ad Aerodynamic Drag
Acts opposite to motion] FCL[F_cl Climbing Force
Acts opposite when ascending] FACC[F_acc Inertial Force
Acts opposite during acceleration] FT[F_t Tractive Force
Provided by motor at wheels] end FT -->|Propels vehicle| VEL[v Vehicle velocity] FRR -->|Opposes| VEL FAD -->|Opposes| VEL FCL -->|Opposes| VEL FACC -->|Opposes| VEL ``` ## 4.4 Voltage vs Current Trade-off in Pack Design ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Low_V_High_I[Low Voltage Pack Design] L1[Vpack = 100 V] L2[I = P / 100 V] L3[Thick cables required] L4[High I²R losses] L5[Smaller Ns more Np] end subgraph High_V_Low_I[High Voltage Pack Design] H1[Vpack = 400 V] H2[I = P / 400 V] H3[Thin cables sufficient] H4[Lower I²R losses] H5[Larger Ns fewer Np] end Low_V_High_I -->|Trade-off Decision| High_V_Low_I ``` > [!NOTE] > **Industry Trend:** Modern EVs have migrated from ~100 V packs (older generation) to **400 V (Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV)** and **800 V (Hyundai E-GMP, Porsche Taycan, Lucid Air)** architectures precisely for the reason shown — to **reduce current, cable size, and I²R losses** while supporting ultrafast DC charging at 250–350 kW. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Define the term "Driving Range" of an Electric Vehicle. Mention the standard SOC window used in modern Li-ion EVs. **Model Answer:** The **driving range** of an EV is the maximum distance that the vehicle can travel on a single full charge of its battery pack, measured in **kilometers (km)** under a specified driving cycle, until the pack reaches its minimum allowable SOC. The standard usable **SOC window** for Li-ion chemistries is typically **20% – 95%** (a usable window of ~75%), which preserves battery cycle life by avoiding deep discharge below 20% and high-voltage stress above 95%. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List the four forces that oppose the motion of an electric vehicle and write the formula for each. **Model Answer:** The four opposing forces in a moving EV are: 1. **Rolling Resistance:** $F_{rr} = \mu_{rr} \cdot m \cdot g$ 2. **Aerodynamic Drag:** $F_{ad} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot A_f \cdot C_d \cdot v^2$ 3. **Climbing (Grade) Force:** $F_{cl} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ 4. **Acceleration (Inertial) Force:** $F_{acc} = \gamma \cdot m \cdot a$ --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **(a)** [7 Marks] An electric two-wheeler has the following data: - Total mass $m = 180$ kg - $C_d = 0.6$, $A_f = 0.6$ m², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ - $\mu_{rr} = 0.015$, $g = 9.81$ m/s² - Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dt} = 0.85$ - Required cruise speed $v = 60$ km/h on a flat road Compute: (i) the total tractive force, (ii) the tractive power at wheels, and (iii) the input power required from the battery. **(b)** [7 Marks] The same EV uses Li-ion cells with $V_{cell} = 3.6$ V, $Q_{cell} = 20$ Ah, and $I_{cell,max} = 60$ A. If the peak power is $P_{peak} = 5$ kW and the BMS limits current to 100 A, determine: (i) the pack voltage, (ii) the number of cells in series, (iii) the number of parallel branches, and (iv) the total pack energy in kWh. #### Model Solution **(a)** **Step 1 — Convert velocity:** $$ v = 60 \text{ km/h} = \frac{60 \times 1000}{3600} = 16.67 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute each force component:** $$ F_{rr} = 0.015 \times 180 \times 9.81 = 26.49 \text{ N} $$ $$ F_{ad} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.6 \times 0.6 \times (16.67)^2 = 61.18 \text{ N} $$ $$ F_{cl} = 0 \text{ N (flat road)} $$ $$ F_{acc} = 0 \text{ N (cruise)} $$ **Step 3 — Total tractive force:** $$ F_t = 26.49 + 61.18 = 87.67 \text{ N} $$ **Step 4 — Tractive power (output):** $$ P_{out} = 87.67 \times 16.67 = 1461.3 \text{ W} \approx 1.46 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 5 — Input power:** $$ P_{in} = \frac{1.46}{0.85} = 1.72 \text{ kW} $$ **(b)** **Step 6 — Pack voltage (from peak power and current limit):** $$ V_{pack} = \frac{P_{peak}}{I_{max}} = \frac{5000}{100} = 50 \text{ V} $$ **Step 7 — Number of cells in series:** $$ N_s = \text{ceil}\left(\frac{50}{3.6}\right) = \text{ceil}(13.89) = 14 $$ $$ V_{pack,actual} = 14 \times 3.6 = 50.4 \text{ V} $$ **Step 8 — Recompute pack current at peak power:** $$ I_{pack,peak} = \frac{5000}{50.4} = 99.2 \text{ A} $$ **Step 9 — Parallel branches:** $$ N_p = \text{ceil}\left(\frac{99.2}{60}\right) = \text{ceil}(1.65) = 2 $$ **Step 10 — Pack energy:** $$ E_{pack} = \frac{50.4 \times (2 \times 20)}{1000} = \frac{50.4 \times 40}{1000} = 2.016 \text{ kWh} $$ > **Final Answer (a):** $F_t \approx 87.67$ N, $P_{out} \approx 1.46$ kW, $P_{in} \approx 1.72$ kW. > **Final Answer (b):** $V_{pack} = 50.4$ V, $N_s = 14$, $N_p = 2$, $E_{pack} \approx 2.02$ kWh. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the relationship between the driving range of an EV, the specific energy consumption (Wh/km), and the usable pack energy. Explain why the usable SOC window is typically 20%–95% and not 0%–100%. **(b)** [7 Marks] An EV requires a continuous input power of 25 kW during cruising at 80 km/h on a level road. If the Li-ion pack has $V_{pack} = 320$ V nominal and a usable capacity of 60 kWh, calculate: (i) the cruising current, (ii) the energy consumption rate in Wh/km, and (iii) the achievable driving range. If the vehicle is upgraded to an **800 V architecture** (same energy), how does the cruising current change, and what is the engineering benefit? #### Model Solution **(a) Derivation:** **Step 1 — Define energy balance.** Total usable energy delivered by the pack must equal the energy consumed over the trip: $$ E_{usable} = \int_0^R \varepsilon(x) \, dx $$ For constant consumption (uniform driving): $$ E_{usable} = \varepsilon \cdot R $$ **Step 2 — Therefore:** $$ \boxed{R = \frac{E_{usable}}{\varepsilon}} $$ **Step 3 — Usable SOC window rationale:** - Below **20% SOC**: Risk of copper dissolution, lithium plating, and accelerated capacity fade. - Above **95% SOC**: High voltage causes electrolyte oxidation and SEI layer growth, reducing cycle life. - A 75% usable window (20–95%) typically gives **1500–3000 cycles** for NMC and **3000+ cycles** for LFP, whereas a 0–100% window cuts this to **500–800 cycles**. **(b)** **Step 1 — Convert velocity:** $$ v = 80 \text{ km/h} = 22.22 \text{ m/s} = \frac{80}{3.6} = 22.22 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Cruising current at 320 V:** $$ I_{cruise} = \frac{P_{in}}{V_{pack}} = \frac{25{,}000}{320} = 78.13 \text{ A} $$ **Step 3 — Energy consumption rate:** $$ \varepsilon = \frac{P_{in}}{v_{kmh}} = \frac{25{,}000 \text{ W}}{80 \text{ km/h}} = 312.5 \text{ Wh/km} $$ **Step 4 — Achievable range:** $$ R = \frac{E_{usable}}{\varepsilon} = \frac{60{,}000 \text{ Wh}}{312.5 \text{ Wh/km}} = 192 \text{ km} $$ **Step 5 — Upgrading to 800 V (same energy, same power):** $$ I_{cruise,800V} = \frac{25{,}000}{800} = 31.25 \text{ A} $$ **Step 6 — Engineering benefits of 800 V:** - **Current reduction by 2.5×** → smaller busbars and cables - **I²R losses drop by 6.25×** → better efficiency, less heat - **Faster DC fast charging** (350 kW possible) because cables and connectors handle lower current - **Lower motor copper losses** (same torque at lower current → larger voltage) - **Lighter wiring harness** → improved vehicle efficiency and packaging > **Final Answer (b):** $I = 78.13$ A (320 V), $\varepsilon = 312.5$ Wh/km, $R = 192$ km. At 800 V, $I = 31.25$ A — a 60% reduction in current. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s when computing $F_t \cdot v$. KTU deducts **1–2 marks** for this. Always show the conversion step explicitly. > 2. **Mixing $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$:** Students often write $F_{cl} = m g \cos\theta$ by mistake. The horizontal component of weight (parallel to slope) is $m g \sin\theta$; the normal component is $m g \cos\theta$ (which multiplies $F_{rr}$). KTU deducts **1 mark**. > 3. **Forgetting Drivetrain Efficiency:** A common trap is to compute $P_{out} = P_{in}$. The question almost always asks for **input power**, so divide by $\eta_{dt}$. Failure here costs **2 marks**. > 4. **Rounding Errors in $N_s$:** Students round **down** instead of **up**, leading to undervoltage pack. KTU expects **$\text{ceil}()$** (round up). > 5. **Energy in Wh vs kWh:** Forgetting to divide by 1000 when reporting final pack energy in kWh. Lose **1 mark**. > 6. **Ignoring Effective Mass Factor $\gamma$:** During acceleration, the rotational inertia of gears, wheels, and motor rotor adds ~5–10% to mass. KTU penalizes **1 mark** if you ignore this. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — EV Range & Pack Voltage Sizing** - ☐ **Range** depends on **usable energy (kWh)** divided by **specific consumption (Wh/km)**: $R = E_{usable} / \varepsilon$. - ☐ **Usable SOC window** for Li-ion is typically **20% to 95%** (ΔSOC = 0.75) to protect cycle life. - ☐ **Four tractive forces:** $F_{rr}$ (linear in $m$), $F_{ad}$ (∝ $v^2$), $F_{cl}$ (∝ $\sin\theta$), $F_{acc}$ (∝ $a$). - ☐ **Cruise speed dominates aerodynamics;** low-speed driving has better Wh/km efficiency. - ☐ **Total tractive force** $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{cl} + F_{acc}$. - ☐ **Output power at wheels** = $F_t \cdot v$ (with $v$ in m/s). - ☐ **Input power from battery** = $P_{out} / \eta_{dt}$, where $\eta_{dt} \approx 0.85$. - ☐ **Pack voltage** $V_{pack} = P_{peak} / I_{max}$ (current-limited). - ☐ **Series cells** $N_s = \text{ceil}(V_{pack} / V_{cell})$ — always round **up**. - ☐ **Parallel branches** $N_p = \text{ceil}(I_{pack} / I_{cell,max})$. - ☐ **Pack energy** $E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack} / 1000$ kWh. - ☐ **Higher pack voltage → lower current → less I²R loss → faster charging** (800 V trend). - ☐ **Constants to memorize:** $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ kg/m³, $V_{cell,Li-ion} = 3.6$–$3.7$ V. - ☐ **Typical EV ranges:** Tata Tiago EV ≈ 315 km, Tata Nexon EV ≈ 465 km, Tesla Model 3 ≈ 510 km, Hyundai Ioniq 5 ≈ 631 km. - ☐ **Typical efficiencies:** Motor 90–95%, Inverter 95–98%, Transmission 96–98%, Combined drivetrain 85–92%. ---

topics.title: Battery Management System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Battery Management System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Battery Management System (BMS)** is an intelligent electronic control unit (ECU) that monitors, manages, and protects a rechargeable battery pack — typically Lithium-ion — in Electric Vehicles (EVs) and Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs). It performs real-time measurement of cell voltage, current, and temperature; estimates critical states of the battery (State of Charge, State of Health, State of Power, State of Energy); enforces safety limits; performs cell balancing; manages thermal behavior; and interfaces with the vehicle's CAN bus / VCU (Vehicle Control Unit) to ensure optimal performance, longevity, and safety.

### 1.2 Why a BMS is Non-Negotiable in an EV

Lithium-ion cells are energy-dense but chemically sensitive. Without active management they can:
- **Overcharge** → electrolyte decomposition, thermal runaway, fire.
- **Over-discharge** → copper dissolution, permanent capacity loss.
- **Overheat** → exothermic reactions, propagation to neighboring cells.
- **Become imbalanced** → weaker cells dictate pack performance.

> [!NOTE] **Syllabus Highlight (CO Mapping)**
> Module 3 of PEEET522 maps to **CO3**: *Design and analyze battery management systems, charging infrastructure, and thermal management for electric vehicles.* Hence the BMS topic carries direct 14-mark weightage in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Nervous System of the Battery"

Imagine the battery pack as a **large orchestra**:
- Each **cell** is a musician playing a violin.
- Without a **conductor**, some play too loud (overcharge), some too soft (undercharge), and the music (energy delivery) becomes chaotic.
- The **BMS is the conductor** — it listens to every musician (sensors), tells them when to play louder/softer (balancing), warns if anyone is overheating (thermal protection), and reports back to the audience (VCU) about the performance.

The musician's pitch = **voltage**, tempo = **current**, body temperature = **cell temperature**, and the listener (driver) gets smooth, reliable music (driving range and power).

### 1.4 Standardized Metrics Used in BMS Design

| Metric | Symbol | Typical EV Value |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal Pack Voltage | $V_{pack}$ | 350 – 800 V |
| Cell Capacity | $Q_{cell}$ | 50 – 100 Ah |
| Pack Energy | $E_{pack}$ | 40 – 100 kWh |
| C-rate | $C$ | 1C continuous, 3C peak |
| Operating Temp. | $T_{op}$ | **−20 °C to +60 °C** |
| Cycle Life Target | $N_{cyc}$ | 1500 – 3000 cycles |

### 1.5 Hierarchical Position of the BMS in an EV Powertrain

> [!NOTE] The BMS does **not** drive the vehicle directly. It supervises the high-voltage battery and exchanges data with the **VCU (Vehicle Control Unit)**, **MCU (Motor Control Unit)**, **OBC (On-Board Charger)**, and **thermal loop controller**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Block Architecture of a BMS

A KTU-grade BMS is decomposed into **five functional subsystems**. Understanding each is essential for both theory and design questions.

1. **Data Acquisition Layer (Sensing)**
   - Cell voltage monitoring (ADC sampling, typically 12–16 bit).
   - Pack current sensing via **shunt resistor** or **Hall-effect sensor**.
   - Temperature monitoring using NTC thermistors (10 kΩ at 25 °C) — minimum 2 per cell group.
   - Isolation sensing (high-voltage leakage detection).

2. **State Estimation Layer (Algorithms)**
   - **State of Charge (SOC)** — fuel gauge equivalent.
   - **State of Health (SOH)** — degradation indicator.
   - **State of Power (SOP)** — peak charge/discharge capability in next 10 s.
   - **State of Energy (SOE)** — usable energy remaining (Joules).
   - **State of Safety (SOS)** — fault flag.

3. **Protection & Safety Layer**
   - Over-Voltage (OV), Under-Voltage (UV), Over-Current (OC), Short-Circuit (SC), Over-Temperature (OT), and isolation fault handling.
   - Contactor control — main positive and main negative contactors.
   - Pre-charge circuit management.
   - Hardware interlocks (HVIL).

4. **Cell Balancing Subsystem**
   - **Passive** — dissipative resistors.
   - **Active** — capacitive or inductive energy shuttling.

5. **Communication & Diagnostics Layer**
   - **CAN 2.0B** (classic) or **CAN-FD** for VCU linkage.
   - **UART / SPI** for cell-monitor IC daisy-chain.
   - **ISO 14229 (UDS)** for diagnostics.
   - **SAE J1939 / J1979** for OBD-II compliance.

### 2.2 State Estimation — The Mathematical Core

#### 2.2.1 State of Charge (SOC)

SOC is the **available capacity expressed as a percentage of the rated capacity**.

$$SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) - \frac{1}{Q_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} \eta_c \cdot I(\tau)\, d\tau$$

Where:
- $Q_{rated}$ = rated cell capacity (Ah)
- $I(\tau)$ = instantaneous current (A, positive on discharge)
- $\eta_c$ = coulombic efficiency (≈ 0.999 for Li-ion, ≈ 1 for LFP)

> [!NOTE] **Coulomb Counting alone drifts** because the integration accumulates sensor error. Real BMS fuses coulomb counting with **OCV-based correction** (Open Circuit Voltage) at rest.

#### 2.2.2 State of Health (SOH)

$$SOH(\%) = \frac{Q_{max,present}}{Q_{max,new}} \times 100$$

> An SOH of 80% is the **End-of-Life (EOL)** threshold for automotive packs (per UN R100 / IEC 62660).

#### 2.2.3 State of Power (SOP)

$$SOP = V_{min} \cdot I_{max,discharge} = \min \left( V_{cell,min},\, \frac{V_{term}}{N_{cells}} \right) \cdot I_{allowed}$$

Where $V_{term}$ is the lowest allowable terminal voltage.

### 2.3 Cell Balancing — Theory

Cells in series develop **capacity mismatch** due to manufacturing tolerances, temperature gradients, and aging. After many cycles, the weakest cell dictates pack capacity.

#### 2.3.1 Passive Balancing
- Excess charge in higher-SOC cells is bled through a **resistor** switched by a MOSFET.
- Energy is **wasted as heat**.
- Simple, cheap, and adequate for mid-range EVs.

$$P_{bleed} = \frac{(V_{cell,i} - V_{min})^2}{R_{bleed}}$$

#### 2.3.2 Active Balancing
- Energy is **redistributed** from stronger cells to weaker ones using capacitors, inductors, or DC-DC converters.
- Efficiency: 70 – 90%.
- Used in premium EVs (Tesla, Lucid, BYD Blade with cell-to-pack).

| Technique | Topology | Efficiency | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Passive Resistive | Shunt MOSFET + R | **0% (lossy)** | Low |
| Capacitive Shuttle | Switched Cap | 50 – 80% | Medium |
| Inductive Flyback | Transformer | 70 – 85% | High |
| DC-DC Converter | Bidirectional | 85 – 95% | Very High |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SOC (coulomb counting) | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 - \dfrac{1}{Q_{rated}}\int I\,dt$ | % |
| 2 | SOH (capacity-based) | $SOH = \dfrac{Q_{max,now}}{Q_{max,new}}\times 100$ | % |
| 3 | SOH (impedance-based) | $SOH = \dfrac{R_{int,new}}{R_{int,now}}\times 100$ | % |
| 4 | Pack Energy | $E_{pack} = V_{nom}\cdot Q_{pack}$ | Wh |
| 5 | C-rate | $C = \dfrac{I}{Q_{rated}}$ | h⁻¹ |
| 6 | Internal Heat | $Q_{gen} = I^2 R_{int}$ | W |
| 7 | Passive Bleed Power | $P = \dfrac{(V_{cell,i}-V_{min})^2}{R_{bleed}}$ | W |
| 8 | Peak Power (discharge) | $P_{peak} = V_{min}\cdot I_{max,dis}$ | W |
| 9 | Battery Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}} \times 100$ | % |
| 10 | Heat Dissipation (convection) | $Q = hA(T_{cell}-T_{amb})$ | W |
| 11 | Energy stored in active cap balancer | $E_C = \dfrac{1}{2}C V^2$ | J |
| 12 | SOC from OCV (linear approx.) | $SOC \approx a\cdot OCV + b$ | unitless |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Examination Tip:** When writing SOC or SOH in KTU exams, always mention the **boundary conditions** (SOC ∈ [0%, 100%], SOH ∈ [0%, 100%]) and the **assumption of constant temperature** unless otherwise stated.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of BMS in Modern Industry

- **OEMs (Tata, Mahindra, BYD, Tesla):** BMS firmware is the IP that differentiates entry-level from premium EVs.
- **Grid Storage (BESS):** Same BMS principles scaled to MWh.
- **Aerospace / Drones:** Weight-critical active balancing.
- **Standards Compliance:** ISO 26262 (functional safety), IEC 62660, UN R100, AIS-156 (India).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: SOC via Coulomb Counting (Closed Form)

We start from the continuous definition and discretize for embedded implementation.

**Step 1 — Continuous form**

$$SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) - \frac{1}{Q_{rated}}\int_{t_0}^{t} \eta_c\, I(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Discretize** for a fixed sampling interval $\Delta t$:

$$\int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau)\, d\tau \;\approx\; \sum_{k=1}^{N} I_k\, \Delta t$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** into the SOC equation:

$$SOC[n] = SOC[0] - \frac{\eta_c}{Q_{rated}}\sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k\, \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Express as a recursive difference equation** (efficient for microcontroller execution):

$$\boxed{\;SOC[n] = SOC[n-1] - \frac{\eta_c \cdot I_n \cdot \Delta t}{Q_{rated}}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Apply sign convention**: discharge current is positive.

> This recurrence runs every $\Delta t = 100$ ms in a typical EV BMS. The integration drift is corrected periodically by comparing $OCV_{rest}$ to a look-up table $OCV = f(SOC)$.

### 3.2 Derivation: SOH from Capacity Fade Model

A widely accepted empirical model (Xu et al., NASA datasets) expresses capacity fade as:

$$Q_{max}(N) = Q_{0} - \alpha \sqrt{N} - \beta N$$

**Step 1 — Define SOH:**

$$SOH(N) = \frac{Q_{max}(N)}{Q_{0}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$SOH(N) = \left[ 1 - \frac{\alpha \sqrt{N} + \beta N}{Q_{0}} \right] \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — EOL Condition** (SOH = 80%):

$$0.8 = 1 - \frac{\alpha \sqrt{N_{EOL}} + \beta N_{EOL}}{Q_{0}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N_{EOL}$:**

$$\alpha \sqrt{N_{EOL}} + \beta N_{EOL} = 0.2\, Q_{0}$$

For typical NMC cells: $\alpha = 0.0015$, $\beta = 0.00005$, $Q_0 = 2.5$ Ah. Solving numerically gives $N_{EOL} \approx 2500$ cycles.

### 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Circuit Model (ECM) for SOP

Thevenin model — used universally in KTU/industry BMS design — is shown below.

$$V_{terminal} = OCV(SOC) - I \cdot R_0 - V_{RC}$$

$$V_{RC}[k+1] = V_{RC}[k]\,e^{-\Delta t / R_1 C_1} + I R_1 \left(1 - e^{-\Delta t / R_1 C_1}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Peak discharge current limited by $V_{min}$:**

$$V_{min} = OCV(SOC) - I_{peak}\,R_0 - V_{RC,ss}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $I_{peak}$:**

$$I_{peak} = \frac{OCV(SOC) - V_{RC,ss} - V_{min}}{R_0}$$

**Step 3 — Peak power:**

$$\boxed{\;P_{peak,dis} = V_{min} \cdot I_{peak} = V_{min} \cdot \frac{OCV(SOC) - V_{RC,ss} - V_{min}}{R_0}\;}$$

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A 96-series Li-NMC pack is rated at $Q_{rated} = 60$ Ah. At $t = 0$, $SOC_0 = 80\%$. The vehicle drives at a constant discharge current of $I = 120$ A for 30 minutes, then idles for 10 minutes. Assuming $\eta_c = 1$, find the final SOC.

**Step 1 — Compute charge discharged in 30 min:**

$$\Delta Q_1 = I \cdot t_1 = 120 \text{ A} \times 0.5 \text{ h} = 60 \text{ Ah}$$

**Step 2 — SOC drop due to driving:**

$$\Delta SOC_1 = \frac{\Delta Q_1}{Q_{rated}} = \frac{60}{60} = 1.0 = 100\%$$

**Step 3 — That would bring SOC to 80% − 100% = −20% which is impossible — so the cell would have hit UV cut-off much earlier. The cell is completely drained at:**

$$t_{cutoff} = \frac{0.80 \times 60 \text{ Ah}}{120 \text{ A}} = 0.40 \text{ h} = 24 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Final SOC after 30 min drive:** BMS disconnects at 0% (UV protection).

**Step 5 — Idling:** No current flow, but OCV relaxes back. $SOC_{final} = 0\%$ (BMS holds contactors open until charger is connected).

> [!IMPORTANT] **Conclusion:** This example demonstrates the **under-voltage protection role of the BMS** — without it, the cell would be driven into destructive over-discharge.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Estimation Methods

| Method | Accuracy | Computation | Drift | Industry Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coulomb Counting | ±2% | Very Low | High | All EVs (baseline) |
| OCV Lookup | ±1% | Low | None | Rest periods only |
| Kalman Filter (EKF) | ±1.5% | High | None | Tesla, BMW |
| Neural Network | ±0.8% | Very High (training) | Low | Research stage |
| Impedance Spectroscopy | ±1% | High | None | Lab / diagnostics |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a BMS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENS[Sensing Layer]
        V1[Cell Voltage Monitor ADC]
        I1[Current Sensor Hall / Shunt]
        T1[Temperature NTC Sensors]
        ISO[Isolation Detector HVIL]
    end

    subgraph EST[State Estimation Core]
        SOC[SOC Estimator Coulomb + OCV Fusion]
        SOH[SOH Estimator Capacity + Impedance]
        SOP[SOP Predictor Thevenin ECM]
        SOE[SOE Energy Meter]
    end

    subgraph PROT[Protection Subsystem]
        OVP[Over Voltage Cutoff]
        UVP[Under Voltage Cutoff]
        OCP[Over Current Breaker]
        OTP[Over Temperature Block]
        CON[Contactor Driver Precharge + Main]
    end

    subgraph BAL[Cell Balancing Subsystem]
        PBAL[Passive Bleed Resistor Network]
        ABAL[Active Bidirectional DC DC Converter]
        BMS_BAL[Balancing Controller Logic]
    end

    subgraph COMM[Communication Interface]
        CAN[CAN 2.0B / CAN FD Bus to VCU]
        UDS[ISO 14229 UDS Diagnostics]
        OBD2[SAE J1979 OBD II Port]
    end

    V1 --> SOC
    V1 --> SOH
    I1 --> SOC
    I1 --> SOP
    T1 --> OTP
    ISO --> CON
    SOC --> OVP
    SOC --> UVP
    SOP --> OCP
    T1 --> SOP
    SOH --> ABAL
    SOC --> BMS_BAL
    BMS_BAL --> PBAL
    BMS_BAL --> ABAL
    OVP --> CON
    UVP --> CON
    OCP --> CON
    OTP --> CON
    SOC --> CAN
    SOH --> UDS
    SOP --> OBD2
```

### 4.2 State Estimation Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pack Current I] --> C[Coulomb Counter]
    B[Cell Voltage V] --> D[OCV Lookup Table]
    C --> E[Extended Kalman Filter EKF]
    D --> E
    F[Cell Temperature T] --> E
    E --> G[SOC Percent]
    E --> H[SOP Watts Available]
    E --> I[SOH Capacity Fade]
    G --> J[VCU Display Range Estimate]
    H --> K[Motor Controller Torque Limit]
    I --> L[Predictive Maintenance Scheduler]
```

### 4.3 Cell Balancing Flow — Active vs Passive Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BMS Polls All Cell Voltages Every 1 Second] --> B{Vmax minus Vmin greater than 50 mV}
    B -- No --> Z[No Balancing Action]
    B -- Yes --> C{Vehicle in Charge Mode}
    C -- Yes --> D[Active Balancing From High to Low Cells]
    C -- No --> E{Discharge Current less than 0.2 C}
    E -- Yes --> F[Passive Bleeding of Top Cells]
    E -- No --> G[Defer Balancing to Next Rest Period]
```

### 4.4 BMS Safety Fault Tree (ISO 26262 Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[BMS Top Event Catastrophic Pack Failure] --> A1[Thermal Runaway]
    START --> A2[Electrical Fire]
    START --> A3[Loss of Isolation Shock Hazard]

    A1 --> B1[Overcharge]
    A1 --> B2[External Short]
    A1 --> B3[Cell Internal Defect]
    A1 --> B4[Coolant Pump Failure]

    A2 --> C1[Contactor Welded Closed]
    A2 --> C2[Wire Harness Damage]
    A2 --> C3[Ground Fault]

    A3 --> D1[HV Cable Abrasion]
    A3 --> D2[Water Ingress]
    A3 --> D3[Connector Degradation]

    B1 --> SOL1[BMS OV Cutoff plus Pyro Fuse]
    B2 --> SOL2[Pyrotechnic Fuse plus Contactor]
    B3 --> SOL3[Cell Level CID plus BMS Impedance Check]
    B4 --> SOL4[Redundant Pump plus Temperature Sensor]
    C1 --> SOL5[Precharge Resistor plus Welding Detection]
    C3 --> SOL6[Isolation Monitoring Module plus HVIL]
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading Tip:** In the KTU ESE, you may be asked to *redraw* this fault tree from memory. Memorize the **top event → intermediate events → causes → mitigations** structure.

### 4.5 Communication Stack Layer Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer UDS / J1939 / Custom OEM] --> L2[Network Layer ISO 15765]
    L3[Data Link Layer CAN 2.0B / CAN FD] --> L2
    L4[Physical Layer Twisted Pair 120 Ohm Terminated] --> L3
    L2 --> L5[BMS Microcontroller Transceiver TJA1057]
    L5 --> L6[VCU Body Domain Controller]
    L5 --> L7[OBC On Board Charger]
    L5 --> L8[MCU Motor Control Unit]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Battery Management System (BMS). List any four of its key functions in an Electric Vehicle.*

**Model Answer:**

A Battery Management System (BMS) is an electronic control unit responsible for monitoring, managing, and protecting a rechargeable battery pack, ensuring safe, efficient, and reliable operation throughout its service life.

**Key Functions (any four):**
1. **Cell Monitoring** — voltage, current, and temperature acquisition.
2. **State Estimation** — SOC, SOH, SOP, and SOE computation.
3. **Cell Balancing** — equalizing charge across series-connected cells.
4. **Thermal Management** — coordinating coolant/heater with temperature sensors.
5. **Safety Protection** — over-voltage, under-voltage, over-current, and short-circuit cutoffs.
6. **Communication** — CAN-bus interfacing with VCU, OBC, and MCU.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is cell balancing? Why is it necessary in series-connected Li-ion battery packs?*

**Model Answer:**

**Cell balancing** is the process of equalizing the SOC (or voltage) of individual cells within a series-connected battery pack to ensure uniform charge/discharge behavior.

**Necessity:**
- Cells in series experience **capacity mismatch** due to manufacturing tolerances, temperature gradients, and uneven aging.
- The **weakest cell** in the string dictates the usable pack capacity.
- Without balancing, the strongest cell may overcharge while the weakest is still undercharged, leading to **safety hazards** (thermal runaway) and **reduced cycle life**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Explain the architecture of a typical EV Battery Management System with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each block. (7 marks)

*(b)* With necessary equations, describe the methods used for **State of Charge (SOC)** and **State of Health (SOH)** estimation in a Li-ion BMS. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Architecture of BMS — Block Diagram and Role of Each Block**

Refer to the diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The five functional blocks are:

1. **Sensing Layer** — Acquires analog signals (voltage, current, temperature) and converts them to digital form using ADCs. Critical because the **accuracy of all downstream estimates depends on the precision of these sensors**.

2. **State Estimation Layer** — Implements algorithms (coulomb counting, EKF, neural networks) to compute SOC, SOH, SOP. This is the **"brain"** of the BMS.

3. **Protection Subsystem** — Compares sensor data against threshold limits. On violation, it commands the contactors to open, isolating the pack from the load/charger. This is the **safety shield**.

4. **Cell Balancing Subsystem** — Either dissipates excess energy (passive) or redistributes it (active) to keep all cells within a tight SOC window. Improves pack life by up to **30%**.

5. **Communication Layer** — Translates internal BMS data into standardized protocols (CAN 2.0B, UDS) for the rest of the vehicle network and for off-board diagnostics.

**(b) SOC and SOH Estimation Methods**

**SOC via Coulomb Counting** (continuous form):

$$SOC(t) = SOC(t_0) - \frac{1}{Q_{rated}}\int_{t_0}^{t} I(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**SOC via Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) Method:**

$$SOC \approx f^{-1}(OCV_{rest})$$

where $f$ is a chemistry-specific look-up table.

**Hybrid EKF Approach (industry standard):**

State vector: $x = [SOC,\, V_{RC}]^T$. The Kalman filter fuses coulomb counting (process model) with voltage measurement (observation model) to produce a drift-free SOC estimate.

**SOH — Capacity-Based Method:**

$$SOH(\%) = \frac{Q_{max,present}}{Q_{max,new}} \times 100$$

**SOH — Impedance-Based Method:**

$$SOH(\%) = \frac{R_{int,new}}{R_{int,now}} \times 100$$

**Empirical Fade Model:**

$$Q_{max}(N) = Q_0 - \alpha \sqrt{N} - \beta N$$

**Comparison of SOC Methods:**

| Method | Accuracy | Drift | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coulomb Counting | ±2% | High | Baseline |
| OCV Lookup | ±1% | None | Rest |
| EKF | ±1.5% | None | Real-time |
| Neural Net | ±0.8% | Low | Research |

**Conclusion:** No single method is universally optimal. State-of-the-art BMS implements **sensor fusion** combining coulomb counting, OCV correction, and model-based filters (EKF/UKF).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample, 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Compare **passive and active cell balancing** techniques in a BMS. With circuit diagrams, explain the working of a switched-capacitor active balancer. (7 marks)

*(b)* Discuss the **thermal management subsystem** integrated with the BMS. Explain how cell temperature affects performance, safety, and longevity. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Passive vs Active Cell Balancing**

**Passive Balancing:** A high-SOC cell is bled through a resistor controlled by a MOSFET switch. The excess energy is dissipated as heat.

- Advantages: simple, cheap, reliable.
- Disadvantages: **wasteful**, balancing current is low (≈ 100–500 mA), so balancing takes hours.

**Active Balancing:** Energy is **transferred** from high-SOC cells to low-SOC cells via:
- **Capacitive shuttling** (switched-capacitor)
- **Inductive flyback / forward converter**
- **Bidirectional DC-DC converters**

- Advantages: efficient (70–95%), faster balancing, less heat.
- Disadvantages: complex control, higher cost, more failure modes.

**Switched-Capacitor Balancer Working:**

The circuit consists of $N-1$ flying capacitors connecting adjacent cells. In **Phase 1**, switches S1, S3 close, charging $C_1$ to the voltage of Cell 1. In **Phase 2**, switches S2, S4 close, transferring charge to Cell 2 if its voltage is lower. The capacitors shuttle charge until all cells are equalized.

Charge transferred per cycle:

$$\Delta Q = C\,(V_{high} - V_{low})$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Passive | Active (Cap) | Active (Inductor) | Active (DC-DC) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | **0%** | 50–80% | 70–85% | 85–95% |
| Speed | Slow | Medium | Fast | Fastest |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| Heat | High | Low | Medium | Low |
| Application | Entry EVs | Mid-range | Premium | Tesla/Lucid |

**(b) Thermal Management Subsystem**

Lithium-ion cells operate optimally between **15 °C and 35 °C**. Outside this window:

- **Below 0 °C:** Lithium plating occurs during charging → permanent capacity loss, dendrite formation, short-circuit risk.
- **Above 45 °C:** SEI layer growth accelerates, electrolyte decomposition begins, risk of thermal runaway above **60 °C**.

**Thermal Management Architectures:**

1. **Air Cooling** — Fans circulate cabin or ambient air. Low cost, limited to 1C continuous.
2. **Liquid Cooling** — Coolant (50/50 water-glycol) flows through cold plates beneath cells. Maintains ΔT < 5 °C. Used in **Tesla Model 3/Y, Hyundai E-GMP**.
3. **Immersion Cooling** — Cells submerged in dielectric fluid. Best uniformity, used in **Lucid Air, BYD**.
4. **PCM (Phase Change Material)** — Passive thermal buffering.

**Integration with BMS:**

The BMS reads cell temperatures via NTC sensors and:
- Modulates coolant pump speed (PWM control).
- Commands PTC heater activation in winter.
- Derates available power (SOP reduction) when cells are too hot/cold.
- Triggers emergency shutdown if $T_{cell} > 60$ °C.

Heat transfer equation used in BMS thermal model:

$$Q_{gen} = I^2 R_{int} + I T \frac{dOCV}{dT}$$

$$Q_{diss} = h A (T_{cell} - T_{coolant})$$

**Conclusion:** An effective BMS-thermal loop is **co-designed** — neither system can be optimized in isolation.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Read Carefully)**
> 1. **Do not** write generic definitions without naming the specific sensor or algorithm. Mentioning *"NTC thermistor"* and *"Extended Kalman Filter"* earns 2 extra marks over vague statements.
> 2. **Always** annotate diagrams with arrow labels and component names. A bare block diagram without labels scores 50% only.
> 3. **Units are mandatory.** Writing $Q = 60$ without "Ah" loses a mark.
> 4. **Boundary conditions** must be quoted for SOC and SOH.
> 5. **Sign convention** of current (positive on discharge) must be clearly stated.
> 6. Students often confuse **SOP** with **SOC** — clearly differentiate in your answer.
> 7. Avoid using programming code in BMS answers (examiners penalize in non-CS papers).
> 8. Do not skip the **safety standards** — naming ISO 26262 / UN R100 / AIS-156 impresses the examiner.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE] Use this as your **last-page glance** before entering the exam hall.

- [x] **Definition:** BMS = electronic brain of EV battery ensuring safety, longevity, and performance.
- [x] **Five Layers:** Sensing → Estimation → Protection → Balancing → Communication.
- [x] **State Variables:** SOC (fuel gauge), SOH (aging), SOP (power), SOE (energy), SOS (safety).
- [x] **SOC Core Equation:** $SOC[n] = SOC[n-1] - \dfrac{\eta_c I_n \Delta t}{Q_{rated}}$.
- [x] **SOH Core Equation:** $SOH = \dfrac{Q_{max,now}}{Q_{max,new}} \times 100$. EOL = 80%.
- [x] **Cell Balancing:** Passive (resistor, lossy) vs Active (capacitor / inductor / DC-DC, efficient).
- [x] **Switched-Capacitor Balancer:** Two-phase charge shuttling, $\Delta Q = C(V_H - V_L)$.
- [x] **Thermal Window:** Optimal 15–35 °C. Below 0 °C → plating; above 60 °C → thermal runaway.
- [x] **Cooling Types:** Air < Liquid < Immersion < PCM (ranked by ΔT control).
- [x] **Sensor Types:** Shunt/Hall (current), NTC (temperature), ADC (voltage).
- [x] **Communication:** CAN 2.0B / CAN-FD to VCU; UDS for diagnostics; OBD-II for emissions/fault.
- [x] **Safety Standards:** ISO 26262 (functional safety), IEC 62660, UN R100, AIS-156 (India).
- [x] **Architecture Types:** Centralized, Distributed (per-cell slave modules), Modular (mid).
- [x] **Failure Modes:** OV, UV, OC, SC, OT, isolation loss — BMS must handle all.
- [x] **Future Trend:** Wireless BMS (wBMS), cell-to-pack (CTP), cell-to-chassis (CTC).

> **Closing Note:** Module 3 of PEEET522 directly tests BMS architecture, SOC/SOH estimation, and balancing techniques. Mastering the formula sheet in Section 2.4 and the fault-tree diagram in Section 4.4 is the highest-yield 30 minutes of revision you can spend for this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
