topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of EV Numerical Problems

Numerical problems in the context of **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** under Module 3 constitute the quantitative backbone of EV powertrain design, motor sizing, energy management, and performance prediction. These problems translate the **physics of vehicle motion** (vehicle dynamics), **electrochemistry of energy storage** (battery behavior), and **electromechanics of propulsion** (motor characteristics) into solvable engineering equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:**
> Numerical problems in Module 3 of PEEET522 (Electric Vehicles) test the student's ability to compute and verify the following engineering metrics:
> 1. **Tractive Force & Tractive Power** required to propel a vehicle
> 2. **Motor Torque and Speed** sizing for a given drivetrain
> 3. **Battery Pack Sizing** for a target range
> 4. **Energy Consumption** per kilometer (Wh/km)
> 5. **Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery**
> 6. **Acceleration, Gradeability, and Top Speed** performance

The governing principles rely on the **Lagrangian vehicle dynamics model**, the **DC machine / induction machine / PMSM equivalent circuits**, and the **Peukert-corrected battery discharge model**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The EV is a Box on Wheels"**
>
> Imagine pushing a heavy box across a floor. To make it move, you must overcome:
> - **Friction** between the box and the floor (this is the **Rolling Resistance**)
> - **Air pushing back** as you move (this is the **Aerodynamic Drag**)
> - **An uphill slope** if the floor is tilted (this is the **Gradient Force**)
> - The **inertia** of the box itself to get it started or speeded up (this is the **Acceleration Force**)
>
> An EV powertrain designer is essentially asking: *"How hard, how fast, and for how long do I need to push this box using only electricity stored in a battery?"*
>
> The **Numerical Problems** module is the set of mathematical tools that answer this question. They convert a vehicle's mass, drag coefficient, rolling resistance, and desired performance into **Newtons of force, Newton-meters of torque, Watts of power, and Watt-hours of energy**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are used repeatedly in EV numerical problems. These must be memorized verbatim for KTU examinations:

| Symbol | Quantity | Value (SI Units) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $g$ | Acceleration due to gravity | $\mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$ |
| $\rho_{air}$ | Density of air at sea level | $\mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$ |
| $\eta_{motor}$ | Typical motor efficiency | $\mathbf{0.85 \; to \; 0.95}$ |
| $\eta_{transmission}$ | Typical gearbox efficiency | $\mathbf{0.95 \; to \; 0.98}$ |
| $\eta_{inverter}$ | Typical inverter efficiency | $\mathbf{0.95 \; to \; 0.98}$ |
| $C_d$ | Drag coefficient (sedan) | $\mathbf{0.25 \; to \; 0.35}$ |
| $C_r$ | Rolling resistance coefficient | $\mathbf{0.008 \; to \; 0.015}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Convention:** All forces are in **Newtons (N)**, all powers in **Watts (W)** or kilowatts (kW), all energies in **Watt-hours (Wh)** or kiloWatt-hours (kWh), all speeds in **m/s** for calculations and **km/h** for display, and torque in **N·m**.

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of EV Numerical Problems

The numerical problems in this module are unified by **three governing equations**:

**Pillar 1 — Force Balance (Newton's Second Law for the vehicle):**
The sum of all tractive forces equals the vehicle's mass times acceleration.

**Pillar 2 — Power Balance (Energy flow through the drivetrain):**
The power demanded at the wheels must be supplied by the motor, which is fed by the battery through the inverter.

**Pillar 3 — Energy Balance (Capacity vs. Range):**
The total energy stored in the battery (corrected for losses and depth of discharge) must equal the total energy consumed over the target range.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tractive Force Equation — The Heart of EV Dynamics

The total **Tractive Force ($F_t$)** required to propel an EV forward is the vector sum of four opposing forces. Every numerical problem in vehicle performance starts here.

$$F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$$

Where each term is defined below.

### 2.1.1 Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{rr}$)

The friction between the tires and the road. It is proportional to the **normal force** (weight) on the tires.

$$F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

> **Where:**
> - $m$ = total vehicle mass (kg), including payload
> - $g$ = gravitational acceleration (9.81 m/s²)
> - $C_r$ = coefficient of rolling resistance (dimensionless)
> - $\theta$ = road gradient angle (radians or degrees)
> - For small gradients: $\cos(\theta) \approx 1$

### 2.1.2 Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$)

The force of air pushing back against the vehicle. It is proportional to the **square of velocity** (this is why high-speed EVs consume dramatically more energy).

$$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A_f \cdot C_d \cdot v^2$$

> **Where:**
> - $\rho$ = air density (1.225 kg/m³ at sea level)
> - $A_f$ = frontal area of the vehicle (m²)
> - $C_d$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless)
> - $v$ = vehicle velocity (m/s)

### 2.1.3 Hill Climbing (Gradient) Force ($F_{hc}$)

The component of gravitational force pulling the vehicle downhill. It is opposed during uphill motion.

$$F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

> **Where:** $\theta$ = road gradient angle. For a grade expressed as a **percentage** (e.g., 30% grade), the angle is $\theta = \arctan(0.30)$.

### 2.1.4 Linear Acceleration Force ($F_{la}$)

The force needed to overcome the **inertia** of the vehicle's mass and the rotational inertia of the wheels, motor, and gears. This is governed by Newton's Second Law.

$$F_{la} = m \cdot a \cdot (1 + \epsilon)$$

> **Where:**
> - $a$ = linear acceleration (m/s²)
> - $\epsilon$ = mass factor (rotational inertia correction), typically $\mathbf{0.05 \; to \; 0.15}$ for EVs
> - The factor $(1 + \epsilon)$ accounts for the equivalent mass of rotating components

---

## 2.2 Power, Torque, and Speed — The Motor Sizing Triad

### 2.2.1 Tractive Power at the Wheels

The mechanical power required at the tire contact patch.

$$P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v = (F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}) \cdot v$$

### 2.2.2 Motor Power (Including Drivetrain Losses)

The electrical power that must be drawn from the battery.

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{transmission}}$$

### 2.2.3 Torque at the Wheels

$$T_{wheel} = F_t \cdot r_{wheel}$$

> **Where:** $r_{wheel}$ = effective rolling radius of the tire (m)

### 2.2.4 Motor Torque (After Gear Reduction)

The motor must supply higher speed but lower torque compared to the wheels, scaled by the **gear ratio** $G$.

$$T_{motor} = \frac{T_{wheel}}{G \cdot \eta_{transmission}}$$

### 2.2.5 Motor Speed

$$N_{motor} = \frac{v \cdot G \cdot 60}{2 \pi \cdot r_{wheel}}$$

> **Where:** $N_{motor}$ is in **RPM** (revolutions per minute), $v$ is in m/s, $r_{wheel}$ is in meters, and $G$ is the overall gear ratio (dimensionless).

---

## 2.3 Energy Consumption and Range — The Battery Sizing Triad

### 2.3.1 Energy Consumption Rate

The instantaneous power demand at the wheel, divided by the total drivetrain efficiency, gives the rate of energy drawn from the battery.

$$P_{battery} = \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{F_t \cdot v}{\eta_{overall}}$$

### 2.3.2 Specific Energy Consumption (Wh/km)

A key performance metric of an EV is how many Watt-hours it consumes per kilometer. This is what manufacturers advertise.

$$E_{spec} = \frac{E_{total}}{D_{range}} \quad \text{(in Wh/km)}$$

### 2.3.3 Required Battery Capacity (for a Target Range)

$$E_{battery} = \frac{E_{spec} \cdot D_{range}}{DoD \cdot \eta_{charge} \cdot \eta_{discharge}}$$

> **Where:**
> - $E_{battery}$ = required usable energy storage (Wh)
> - $D_{range}$ = target driving range (km)
> - $DoD$ = Depth of Discharge (typically 0.8 for Li-ion, i.e., never discharge below 20% SoC)
> - $\eta_{charge}$, $\eta_{discharge}$ = charge and discharge efficiencies of the battery

### 2.3.4 Range (for a Given Battery Capacity)

$$D_{range} = \frac{E_{battery} \cdot DoD \cdot \eta_{overall}}{E_{spec}}$$

### 2.3.5 Battery Pack in Ampere-Hours

To select a battery, often specified in Ah at a given voltage:

$$\text{Capacity (Ah)} = \frac{E_{battery} \;(\text{Wh})}{V_{pack} \;(\text{V})}$$

---

## 2.4 Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

When the driver brakes, the motor acts as a generator. A fraction $\beta$ of the kinetic energy is recovered.

$$E_{regen} = \beta \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2 \cdot \eta_{regen}$$

> **Where:**
> - $\beta$ = fraction of braking energy that is regenerative (typically 0.3 to 0.7)
> - $\eta_{regen}$ = combined motor-generator + charging efficiency (typically 0.6 to 0.8)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:** Regenerative braking cannot recover 100% of kinetic energy because the friction brakes must always be available for emergency stops. The **ECE R13 / R13-H brake blend regulation** mandates a certain minimum friction brake contribution for safety, which limits the practical value of $\beta$ to around **0.6 to 0.7** in production EVs.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the complete, exam-ready formula sheet for EV numerical problems. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B question on this module.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Rolling Resistance Force | $F_{rr} = m \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | N | Tire friction force |
| 2 | Aerodynamic Drag Force | $F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A_f \cdot C_d \cdot v^2$ | N | Air resistance force |
| 3 | Gradient (Hill) Force | $F_{hc} = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | N | Weight component along slope |
| 4 | Acceleration Force | $F_{la} = m \cdot a \cdot (1 + \epsilon)$ | N | Inertia force |
| 5 | Total Tractive Force | $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$ | N | Sum of all resistance forces |
| 6 | Tractive Power at Wheel | $P_{wheel} = F_t \cdot v$ | W | Mechanical power at tire |
| 7 | Motor Power | $P_{motor} = P_{wheel} / (\eta_m \cdot \eta_g)$ | W | Electrical power into motor |
| 8 | Wheel Torque | $T_{wheel} = F_t \cdot r_{wheel}$ | N·m | Torque at tire contact |
| 9 | Motor Torque | $T_{motor} = T_{wheel} / (G \cdot \eta_g)$ | N·m | Torque at motor shaft |
| 10 | Motor Speed | $N_{motor} = (v \cdot G \cdot 60) / (2 \pi \cdot r_{wheel})$ | RPM | Motor rotational speed |
| 11 | Battery Energy Required | $E_{bat} = E_{spec} \cdot D_{range} / (DoD \cdot \eta)$ | Wh | Energy storage needed |
| 12 | Range | $D_{range} = E_{bat} \cdot DoD \cdot \eta / E_{spec}$ | km | Distance per charge |
| 13 | Regenerative Energy | $E_{regen} = \beta \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2 \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | Wh | Recovered braking energy |
| 14 | Battery Pack Ah | $C_{Ah} = E_{bat} / V_{pack}$ | Ah | Capacity in Ampere-hours |
| 15 | Acceleration Time | $t = (v_f - v_i) / a$ | s | Time to reach speed |
| 16 | Gradeability | $\theta_{max} = \arcsin((F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad}) / (m \cdot g))$ | rad or ° | Maximum climb angle |
| 17 | Specific Energy | $E_{spec} = E_{bat} / D_{range}$ | Wh/km | Energy per km |
| 18 | Frontal Area (approx) | $A_f \approx 0.9 \cdot W \cdot H$ | m² | Approx. from width × height |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

> [!TIP]
> These numerical techniques are not just academic — they are the **daily tools** of EV design engineers in companies like Tata Motors (Tiago EV), Mahindra (eXUV300), Ather Energy, Ola Electric, BYD, Tesla, and Rivian. The complete design cycle of an EV powertrain follows these steps:
> 1. **Mission Profile Definition** — what is the target range, top speed, 0–100 km/h time, and gradeability?
> 2. **Vehicle Dynamics Simulation** — apply the tractive force equation across the drive cycle (UDDS, WLTP, NEDC).
> 3. **Motor Sizing** — from the peak tractive power and torque, select motor kW rating and N·m peak torque.
> 4. **Battery Sizing** — from the total energy consumed in the drive cycle, size the kWh pack and DoD-limited capacity.
> 5. **Thermal Modeling** — verify that battery and motor stay within temperature limits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **To the KTU student:** The five problems below are **worked-out exemplar problems** in the exact KTU valuation style. Each one is structured to mirror a real KTU Part-B 14-mark question. Read each derivation line-by-line — the *narrative* below each equation explains the *physics*, the *unit conversion*, and the *algebraic simplification* being performed.

---

## 3.1 PROBLEM 1: Tractive Force and Power on Level Road

**Problem Statement:**
> A battery electric vehicle has the following specifications:
> - Total mass (including 100 kg battery and 75 kg payload): $m = 1450 \; kg$
> - Rolling resistance coefficient: $C_r = 0.012$
> - Aerodynamic drag coefficient: $C_d = 0.29$
> - Frontal area: $A_f = 2.3 \; m^2$
> - Wheel radius: $r_{wheel} = 0.32 \; m$
> - Road gradient: $\theta = 0°$ (level road)
> - Vehicle velocity: $v = 90 \; km/h$
>
> **Calculate:** (a) The total tractive force required, (b) The tractive power at the wheels, (c) The wheel torque.

### 3.1.1 Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Convert the velocity to SI units (m/s).**

KTU examinations often give speed in km/h. We must convert to m/s for consistency with other SI quantities.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 90 \; \frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} \times \frac{1000 \; \text{m}}{1 \; \text{km}} \times \frac{1 \; \text{h}}{3600 \; \text{s}} \\
v &= 90 \times \frac{1000}{3600} \; \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} \\
v &= 90 \times 0.2778 \; \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}} \\
v &= 25.0 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** The conversion factor from km/h to m/s is the **"5/18" factor** (since 1000/3600 = 5/18 ≈ 0.2778). Memorize this — it appears in **every** EV numerical problem.

**Step 2: Calculate the Rolling Resistance Force ($F_{rr}$).**

Since $\theta = 0°$, we have $\cos(0) = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{rr} &= m \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos(\theta) \\
F_{rr} &= 1450 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 1 \\
F_{rr} &= 170.69 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** We substitute the values into the rolling resistance formula. Notice that the result is in Newtons because all quantities are in base SI units (kg, m/s², dimensionless).

**Step 3: Calculate the Aerodynamic Drag Force ($F_{ad}$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{ad} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A_f \cdot C_d \cdot v^2 \\
F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2.3 \times 0.29 \times (25.0)^2 \\
F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2.3 \times 0.29 \times 625 \\
F_{ad} &= 255.31 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** The drag force scales with the **square of velocity** — at 90 km/h, the dynamic pressure term $\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ produces the dominant aerodynamic resistance. Doubling the speed **quadruples** the drag force.

**Step 4: Calculate the Hill Climbing Force ($F_{hc}$).**

Since $\theta = 0°$, we have $\sin(0) = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{hc} &= m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) \\
F_{hc} &= 1450 \times 9.81 \times \sin(0°) \\
F_{hc} &= 1450 \times 9.81 \times 0 \\
F_{hc} &= 0 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** On a level road, gravity acts perpendicular to the direction of motion, so it contributes **no** component along the driving direction.

**Step 5: Calculate the Linear Acceleration Force ($F_{la}$).**

The vehicle is cruising at constant 90 km/h, so acceleration $a = 0 \; m/s^2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{la} &= m \cdot a \cdot (1 + \epsilon) \\
F_{la} &= 1450 \times 0 \times 1.1 \\
F_{la} &= 0 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** At steady-state cruising, there is no change in velocity, so by Newton's First Law, no net force is needed for acceleration. The motor only needs to overcome the **loss forces** (rolling + drag).

**Step 6: Sum all four forces to get the Total Tractive Force.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la} \\
F_t &= 170.69 + 255.31 + 0 + 0 \\
F_t &= 426.0 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Calculate the Tractive Power at the Wheels.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wheel} &= F_t \cdot v \\
P_{wheel} &= 426.0 \times 25.0 \\
P_{wheel} &= 10650 \; \text{W} \\
P_{wheel} &= 10.65 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** The mechanical power at the wheels is simply force times velocity — a direct application of the work-energy theorem ($P = dW/dt = F \cdot dx/dt = F \cdot v$).

**Step 8: Calculate the Wheel Torque.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wheel} &= F_t \cdot r_{wheel} \\
T_{wheel} &= 426.0 \times 0.32 \\
T_{wheel} &= 136.32 \; \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** Torque is force times the moment arm (the wheel radius, in this case). This is the torque the tire contact patch must deliver to the road to maintain the tractive force.

### 1.4 Final Answer Summary for Problem 1

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rolling Resistance | $F_{rr}$ | 170.69 N |
| Aerodynamic Drag | $F_{ad}$ | 255.31 N |
| Hill Climbing Force | $F_{hc}$ | 0 N |
| Acceleration Force | $F_{la}$ | 0 N |
| **Total Tractive Force** | $F_t$ | **426.0 N** |
| **Tractive Power at Wheels** | $P_{wheel}$ | **10.65 kW** |
| **Wheel Torque** | $T_{wheel}$ | **136.32 N·m** |

---

## 3.2 PROBLEM 2: Motor Sizing Including Drivetrain Losses

**Problem Statement:**
> For the EV in Problem 1, the design team selects a **single-speed gearbox** with overall ratio $G = 8.5$ and efficiencies $\eta_{motor} = 0.92$ and $\eta_{gearbox} = 0.96$.
>
> **Calculate:** (a) The required motor power, (b) The required motor torque, (c) The required motor speed in RPM.

### 3.2.1 Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Recall the wheel-side values from Problem 1.**

- $P_{wheel} = 10650 \; W = 10.65 \; kW$
- $T_{wheel} = 136.32 \; N \cdot m$
- $v = 25.0 \; m/s$
- $r_{wheel} = 0.32 \; m$

**Step 2: Calculate the Required Motor Power.**

The drivetrain absorbs some of the mechanical power as heat (losses). The motor must therefore deliver **more** mechanical power than the wheels receive.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{motor} &= \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gearbox}} \\
P_{motor} &= \frac{10650}{0.92 \times 0.96} \\
P_{motor} &= \frac{10650}{0.8832} \\
P_{motor} &= 12058.4 \; \text{W} \\
P_{motor} &= 12.06 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** The combined efficiency of motor and gearbox is $\eta = 0.92 \times 0.96 = 0.8832$, meaning 11.68% of the input power is lost. The motor must therefore supply $\frac{10.65}{0.8832} \approx 12.06 \; kW$.

**Step 3: Calculate the Required Motor Torque.**

The gearbox **reduces** speed by factor $G$ and **increases** torque by the same factor (minus losses). Thus, the motor torque is *less* than the wheel torque.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{motor} &= \frac{T_{wheel}}{G \cdot \eta_{gearbox}} \\
T_{motor} &= \frac{136.32}{8.5 \times 0.96} \\
T_{motor} &= \frac{136.32}{8.16} \\
T_{motor} &= 16.71 \; \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** The motor is a **high-speed, low-torque** machine. The gearbox multiplies this torque up by factor $G \cdot \eta_g = 8.16$ to deliver $136.32 \; N \cdot m$ at the wheels.

**Step 4: Calculate the Required Motor Speed in RPM.**

The motor must spin faster than the wheels by the gear ratio.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{wheel} &= \frac{v}{r_{wheel}} = \frac{25.0}{0.32} = 78.125 \; \text{rad/s} \\
\omega_{motor} &= G \cdot \omega_{wheel} = 8.5 \times 78.125 = 664.06 \; \text{rad/s} \\
N_{motor} &= \omega_{motor} \times \frac{60}{2\pi} \\
N_{motor} &= 664.06 \times \frac{60}{2 \pi} \\
N_{motor} &= 664.06 \times 9.5493 \\
N_{motor} &= 6341 \; \text{RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** Alternatively, the direct formula $N_{motor} = (v \cdot G \cdot 60) / (2 \pi \cdot r_{wheel})$ gives the same result in one step: $N_{motor} = (25.0 \times 8.5 \times 60) / (2 \pi \times 0.32) = 12750 / 2.0106 = 6341 \; RPM$.

### 3.2.2 Final Answer Summary for Problem 2

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Required Motor Power** | $P_{motor}$ | **12.06 kW** |
| **Required Motor Torque** | $T_{motor}$ | **16.71 N·m** |
| **Required Motor Speed** | $N_{motor}$ | **6341 RPM** |

---

## 3.3 PROBLEM 3: Acceleration Performance — Time to Reach 100 km/h

**Problem Statement:**
> Using the EV from Problems 1 & 2, with additional spec: $G = 8.5$, $\eta_{motor} \eta_{gearbox} = 0.8832$, mass factor $\epsilon = 0.10$. The motor can deliver a **peak power** of $P_{peak} = 90 \; kW$ and a **peak torque** of $T_{peak} = 250 \; N \cdot m$.
>
> **Calculate:** (a) The maximum acceleration from rest at 0 km/h, and (b) The time to accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h assuming average acceleration.

### 3.3.1 Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Convert target velocity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_f &= 100 \; \frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} = 100 \times \frac{5}{18} = 27.78 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the maximum tractive force at low speed using peak torque.**

At low speed, the motor is torque-limited (not power-limited). So we use the peak torque.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wheel, peak} &= T_{peak} \cdot G \cdot \eta_{gearbox} \\
T_{wheel, peak} &= 250 \times 8.5 \times 0.96 \\
T_{wheel, peak} &= 2040 \; \text{N·m} \\
F_{t, peak} &= \frac{T_{wheel, peak}}{r_{wheel}} = \frac{2040}{0.32} \\
F_{t, peak} &= 6375 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the maximum acceleration at very low speed (v ≈ 0).**

At $v \approx 0$, drag and rolling resistance are small. Let's include them for accuracy at v = 5 m/s as a starting approximation, but use v = 0 for peak acceleration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{rr, 0} &= 1450 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 = 170.69 \; \text{N} \\
F_{ad, 0} &= 0 \; \text{N} \; (\text{since } v = 0) \\
F_{net} &= F_{t, peak} - F_{rr, 0} - F_{ad, 0} \\
F_{net} &= 6375 - 170.69 - 0 = 6204.31 \; \text{N} \\
a_{max} &= \frac{F_{net}}{m(1+\epsilon)} = \frac{6204.31}{1450 \times 1.10} \\
a_{max} &= \frac{6204.31}{1595} \\
a_{max} &= 3.89 \; \text{m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** The effective mass is $m(1+\epsilon) = 1450 \times 1.10 = 1595 \; kg$ because the rotating wheels and gears add about 10% equivalent mass.

**Step 4: Estimate the time to accelerate 0 to 100 km/h.**

If we assume roughly constant average acceleration of $a_{avg} \approx a_{max} / 2 = 1.945 \; m/s^2$ (a common engineering approximation because acceleration decreases as drag increases):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{v_f}{a_{avg}} = \frac{27.78}{1.945} \\
t &= 14.28 \; \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** A more rigorous approach integrates $\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{F_{t, peak} - F_{rr} - F_{ad}}{m(1+\epsilon)}$ over the speed range, accounting for the velocity-dependent drag. For KTU, the constant-acceleration approximation is sufficient and commonly used.

### 3.3.2 Final Answer Summary for Problem 3

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Peak wheel-side tractive force | 6375 N |
| **Maximum acceleration at start** | **3.89 m/s²** |
| **Time 0 to 100 km/h (approx.)** | **~14.3 s** |

---

## 3.4 PROBLEM 4: Battery Sizing for a Target Range

**Problem Statement:**
> An EV consumes on average $E_{spec} = 150 \; Wh/km$ over a standard drive cycle. The target range is $D_{range} = 300 \; km$ on a single charge. The Li-ion battery pack has $V_{pack} = 400 \; V$, depth of discharge $DoD = 0.85$, and combined charge-discharge efficiency $\eta_{batt} = 0.90$.
>
> **Calculate:** (a) The total energy required, (b) The required battery pack capacity in kWh, (c) The required capacity in Ah.

### 3.4.1 Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Calculate the total energy consumed by the vehicle over the range.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{consumed} &= E_{spec} \cdot D_{range} \\
E_{consumed} &= 150 \times 300 \\
E_{consumed} &= 45000 \; \text{Wh} = 45 \; \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** This is the energy that must be **delivered** to the motor controller input. It does not account for battery-side losses yet.

**Step 2: Calculate the required battery pack energy (with DoD and efficiency correction).**

The battery must store **more** energy than the vehicle consumes, because:
1. It cannot be discharged below its safe lower SoC limit (the 100% × DoD constraint).
2. Charging and discharging have losses.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{battery} &= \frac{E_{consumed}}{DoD \cdot \eta_{batt}} \\
E_{battery} &= \frac{45000}{0.85 \times 0.90} \\
E_{battery} &= \frac{45000}{0.765} \\
E_{battery} &= 58823.5 \; \text{Wh} \approx 58.82 \; \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** The 23.5% overhead accounts for the energy "trapped" below 15% SoC and the round-trip losses in the battery.

**Step 3: Calculate the required Ah capacity.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Ah} &= \frac{E_{battery}}{V_{pack}} \\
C_{Ah} &= \frac{58823.5 \; \text{Wh}}{400 \; \text{V}} \\
C_{Ah} &= 147.06 \; \text{Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** A 400 V pack with 147 Ah is the standard specification for many production EVs (e.g., Tata Nexon EV Long Range uses a similar architecture).

### 3.4.2 Final Answer Summary for Problem 4

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Energy consumed in 300 km | 45 kWh |
| **Required battery pack energy** | **58.82 kWh** |
| **Required battery pack Ah (at 400 V)** | **147.06 Ah** |

---

## 3.5 PROBLEM 5: Regenerative Braking Energy Recovery

**Problem Statement:**
> An EV of mass 1800 kg (including battery) is travelling at $v = 60 \; km/h$ and brakes to a complete stop. The regenerative braking system recovers a fraction $\beta = 0.65$ of the total braking energy, with a combined regen efficiency $\eta_{regen} = 0.75$.
>
> **Calculate:** (a) The total kinetic energy of the vehicle, (b) The recovered regenerative energy in Wh, (c) The effective increase in range if the EV consumes $E_{spec} = 160 \; Wh/km$.

### 3.5.1 Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Convert velocity to SI units.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 60 \times \frac{5}{18} = 16.67 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the total kinetic energy of the vehicle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KE &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2 \\
KE &= 0.5 \times 1800 \times (16.67)^2 \\
KE &= 0.5 \times 1800 \times 277.89 \\
KE &= 250101 \; \text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Convert KE to Wh and apply the regen fraction and efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
KE_{Wh} &= \frac{250101 \; \text{J}}{3600 \; \text{J/Wh}} = 69.47 \; \text{Wh} \\
E_{regen} &= \beta \cdot KE_{Wh} \cdot \eta_{regen} \\
E_{regen} &= 0.65 \times 69.47 \times 0.75 \\
E_{regen} &= 33.87 \; \text{Wh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the additional range gained.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta D &= \frac{E_{regen}}{E_{spec}} = \frac{33.87}{160} \\
\Delta D &= 0.212 \; \text{km} \approx 212 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5.2 Final Answer Summary for Problem 5

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Total kinetic energy | 250.1 kJ = 69.47 Wh |
| **Recovered regenerative energy** | **33.87 Wh** |
| **Additional range from one regen event** | **~212 m** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A single regen event from 60 km/h adds only about 0.2 km. However, in **city driving** with frequent stops, regenerative braking can extend the urban range by **15–25%** cumulatively.

---

## 3.6 Master Equation Summary Table

The following synthesis table maps every KTU-examined topic to its governing equation and unit:

| Engineering Problem | Governing Equation | Typical KTU Marks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tractive force calculation | $F_t = mgC_r\cos\theta + 0.5 \rho A_f C_d v^2 + mg\sin\theta + ma(1+\epsilon)$ | 5–7 |
| Motor power sizing | $P_{motor} = F_t v / (\eta_m \eta_g)$ | 4–5 |
| Motor torque calculation | $T_{motor} = F_t r_{wheel} / (G \eta_g)$ | 3–4 |
| Motor RPM calculation | $N_{motor} = v G \cdot 60 / (2 \pi r_{wheel})$ | 2–3 |
| Battery energy sizing | $E_{bat} = E_{spec} D_{range} / (DoD \cdot \eta)$ | 5–7 |
| Range calculation | $D_{range} = E_{bat} DoD \eta / E_{spec}$ | 3–4 |
| Battery Ah capacity | $C_{Ah} = E_{bat} / V_{pack}$ | 2–3 |
| Regenerative energy | $E_{regen} = \beta \cdot 0.5 m v^2 \cdot \eta_{regen}$ | 3–4 |
| Acceleration time | $t = (v_f - v_i) / a_{avg}$ | 2–3 |
| Gradeability | $\theta_{max} = \arcsin((F_t - F_{rr} - F_{ad}) / (mg))$ | 4–5 |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: EV Numerical Problem-Solving Workflow

This flowchart represents the **canonical 7-step algorithm** that an engineering student must follow to solve any EV numerical problem on a KTU exam. It is the structural backbone of the entire module.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Problem Statement] --> B[Extract Given Data: m, Cd, Cr, Af, v, theta, eta]
    B --> C{Identify What's Being Asked}
    C --> D1[Tractive Force? F_t]
    C --> D2[Motor Power? P_motor]
    C --> D3[Battery Energy? E_bat]
    C --> D4[Range? D_range]
    C --> D5[Regen Energy? E_regen]
    D1 --> E[Compute F_rr, F_ad, F_hc, F_la separately]
    E --> F[Sum all four forces: F_t = F_rr + F_ad + F_hc + F_la]
    F --> G{Need Motor-side or Battery-side?}
    G --> G1[Divide by eta: P_motor = F_t v / eta_m eta_g]
    G --> G2[Apply DoD and eta_batt: E_bat = E_consumed / DoD eta]
    G1 --> H[Output: F_t, P_motor, T_motor, N_motor]
    G2 --> I[Output: E_bat in kWh and Ah]
    D4 --> J[Use: D_range = E_bat DoD eta / E_spec]
    D5 --> K[Use: E_regen = 0.5 beta m v^2 eta_regen]
    H --> L[Convert units and present final answer with units]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[End: Final Answer Box]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Force Decomposition Schematic

This diagram illustrates how the **four tractive force components** combine to form the total tractive force acting on the EV. It is a high-level functional decomposition.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Mass[Vehicle Mass m] --> M1[Rolling Resistance F_rr]
    Mass --> M2[Gradient Force F_hc]
    Mass --> M3[Acceleration Force F_la]
    Velocity[Velocity v] --> V1[Aerodynamic Drag F_ad]
    M1 --> Sum[Total Tractive Force F_t]
    M2 --> Sum
    M3 --> Sum
    V1 --> Sum
    Sum --> Out1[Motor Power P_motor]
    Sum --> Out2[Wheel Torque T_wheel]
    Out1 --> Bat[Battery Energy E_bat]
    Out2 --> MotorSizing[Motor Torque T_motor]
    Bat --> Range[Range D_range]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Energy Flow Through the EV Drivetrain

This diagram shows how energy flows from the **AC mains (or grid) → Charger → Battery → Inverter → Motor → Wheels → Road**, with losses at each stage. This is the **functional block architecture** representation of an EV's energy system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Grid[AC Grid Supply] --> Chgr[Battery Charger]
    Chgr -->|eta_charge| Batt[High Voltage Battery Pack]
    Batt -->|eta_discharge| Inv[Motor Inverter / Controller]
    Inv -->|eta_inverter| Mot[Electric Motor]
    Mot -->|eta_motor| Gear[Single-Speed Gearbox]
    Gear -->|eta_gearbox| Whl[Wheels and Tires]
    Whl -->|F_t| Road[Road Surface]
    Road -.Kinetic Energy.-> Mot
    Mot -.Regen Mode.-> Batt
    style Grid fill:#E0F7FA
    style Batt fill:#FFF59D
    style Mot fill:#C5E1A5
    style Road fill:#FFAB91
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the path from **Grid → Battery → Motor → Road** for traction power. Follow the path from **Road → Motor → Battery** for regenerative braking power. The **η** (eta) at each arrow represents the efficiency of that stage.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Decision Tree for KTU Numerical Problems

This is the **exam-taking strategy** flowchart. It guides the student on **which formula to use based on what is asked**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Exam Question] --> Q1{Is velocity given?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is gradient given?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3[Use Average / Constant Acceleration Model]
    Q2 -->|Yes, nonzero| Q3a[Use F_hc = mg sin theta]
    Q2 -->|No or zero| Q3b[Set F_hc = 0]
    Q3a --> Q4{Is acceleration given?}
    Q3b --> Q4
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q4a[Use F_la = m a 1+eps]
    Q4 -->|No| Q4b[Set F_la = 0]
    Q4a --> Sum[Compute F_t = F_rr + F_ad + F_hc + F_la]
    Q4b --> Sum
    Sum --> End1[Output: F_t]
    Sum --> End2[Output: P_wheel = F_t v]
    Sum --> End3[Output: T_wheel = F_t r_wheel]
    Q3 --> End4[Compute a = F_t / m 1+eps]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**List and explain the four forces that oppose the motion of an electric vehicle. Write the mathematical expression for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four forces opposing the motion of an EV are:

| # | Force | Formula | Physical Origin |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Rolling Resistance** $F_{rr}$ | $m \cdot g \cdot C_r \cdot \cos\theta$ | Tire deformation and road surface friction |
| 2 | **Aerodynamic Drag** $F_{ad}$ | $0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot A_f \cdot C_d \cdot v^2$ | Air resistance on the vehicle's frontal area |
| 3 | **Hill Climbing** $F_{hc}$ | $m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | Gravitational component along a slope |
| 4 | **Linear Acceleration** $F_{la}$ | $m \cdot a \cdot (1+\epsilon)$ | Newton's second law, with rotational inertia correction |

The **total tractive force** is $F_t = F_{rr} + F_{ad} + F_{hc} + F_{la}$.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define specific energy consumption of an electric vehicle. Why is it a key performance metric?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Specific energy consumption** $E_{spec}$ is defined as the energy consumed by the EV per unit distance travelled, typically expressed in **Wh/km**.

$$E_{spec} = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumed (Wh)}}{\text{Total Distance Travelled (km)}}$$

It is a **key performance metric** because:
1. It directly determines the **battery size** required for a target range.
2. It is the **figure of merit** used by manufacturers to advertise EV efficiency (e.g., Tesla Model 3: ~120 Wh/km, Tata Nexon EV: ~130 Wh/km).
3. It enables **direct comparison** between EVs of different sizes and battery capacities, independent of the actual battery kWh.
4. Lower $E_{spec}$ means **longer range per kWh**, reducing cost-of-ownership.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### **Question B1 — Choice A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

A battery electric three-wheeler (auto-rickshaw) has the following specifications:
- Kerb mass: $m = 450 \; kg$
- Payload: $m_{pay} = 250 \; kg$
- Rolling resistance coefficient: $C_r = 0.015$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient: $C_d = 0.35$
- Frontal area: $A_f = 1.8 \; m^2$
- Wheel radius: $r_{wheel} = 0.25 \; m$
- Maximum velocity: $v_{max} = 45 \; km/h$
- Gear ratio: $G = 6.0$
- Motor efficiency: $\eta_{motor} = 0.90$
- Gearbox efficiency: $\eta_{gear} = 0.95$
- Air density: $\rho = 1.225 \; kg/m^3$

**(a)** Calculate the **total tractive force** required at the maximum velocity on a **level road**.

**(b)** Calculate the **motor power** and **motor torque** required to achieve this performance. Also find the **motor speed in RPM**.

---

#### **Solution to B1(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute the total mass.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{total} &= m_{kerb} + m_{payload} \\
m_{total} &= 450 + 250 \\
m_{total} &= 700 \; \text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Convert velocity to SI units.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{max} &= 45 \times \frac{5}{18} \\
v_{max} &= 12.5 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Rolling Resistance Force.**

Since $\theta = 0°$ (level road), $\cos\theta = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{rr} &= m_{total} \cdot g \cdot C_r \\
F_{rr} &= 700 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \\
F_{rr} &= 103.005 \; \text{N} \approx 103.0 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Aerodynamic Drag Force.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{ad} &= 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot A_f \cdot C_d \cdot v^2 \\
F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1.8 \times 0.35 \times (12.5)^2 \\
F_{ad} &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1.8 \times 0.35 \times 156.25 \\
F_{ad} &= 60.24 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Hill Climbing and Acceleration Forces.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{hc} &= 0 \; \text{N} \; (\text{level road}) \\
F_{la} &= 0 \; \text{N} \; (\text{constant speed})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Sum to get total tractive force.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_t &= 103.0 + 60.24 + 0 + 0 \\
F_t &= 163.24 \; \text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer B1(a):** $\boxed{F_t \approx 163.24 \; N}$

---

#### **Solution to B1(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute tractive power at the wheel.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{wheel} &= F_t \cdot v \\
P_{wheel} &= 163.24 \times 12.5 \\
P_{wheel} &= 2040.5 \; \text{W} \approx 2.04 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute required motor power.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{motor} &= \frac{P_{wheel}}{\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear}} \\
P_{motor} &= \frac{2040.5}{0.90 \times 0.95} \\
P_{motor} &= \frac{2040.5}{0.855} \\
P_{motor} &= 2386.55 \; \text{W} \approx 2.39 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute wheel torque and then motor torque.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wheel} &= F_t \cdot r_{wheel} \\
T_{wheel} &= 163.24 \times 0.25 \\
T_{wheel} &= 40.81 \; \text{N·m} \\
T_{motor} &= \frac{T_{wheel}}{G \cdot \eta_{gear}} \\
T_{motor} &= \frac{40.81}{6.0 \times 0.95} \\
T_{motor} &= \frac{40.81}{5.7} \\
T_{motor} &= 7.16 \; \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute motor speed in RPM.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{motor} &= \frac{v \cdot G \cdot 60}{2 \pi \cdot r_{wheel}} \\
N_{motor} &= \frac{12.5 \times 6.0 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 0.25} \\
N_{motor} &= \frac{4500}{1.5708} \\
N_{motor} &= 2864.79 \; \text{RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer B1(b):**
> - Motor Power: $\boxed{P_{motor} \approx 2.39 \; kW}$
> - Motor Torque: $\boxed{T_{motor} \approx 7.16 \; N \cdot m}$
> - Motor Speed: $\boxed{N_{motor} \approx 2865 \; RPM}$

---

### **Question B1 — Choice B (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

A small electric city car has the following parameters:
- Total mass: $m = 1200 \; kg$
- $C_r = 0.010$, $C_d = 0.30$, $A_f = 2.0 \; m^2$
- Wheel radius: $r_{wheel} = 0.30 \; m$
- Battery pack voltage: $V_{pack} = 350 \; V$
- Specific energy consumption: $E_{spec} = 140 \; Wh/km$
- Target range: $D_{range} = 250 \; km$
- Depth of discharge: $DoD = 0.85$
- Combined battery efficiency: $\eta_{batt} = 0.92$

**(a)** Compute the **total energy required** for the target range and the **required battery pack capacity** in kWh.

**(b)** Find the **battery capacity in Ah**, and verify whether a single regenerative braking event from $v = 50 \; km/h$ to 0 km/h, with $\beta = 0.6$ and $\eta_{regen} = 0.7$, can extend the range by at least 0.15 km.

---

#### **Solution to B1B(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute total energy consumed.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{consumed} &= E_{spec} \cdot D_{range} \\
E_{consumed} &= 140 \times 250 \\
E_{consumed} &= 35000 \; \text{Wh} = 35 \; \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute required battery pack energy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{battery} &= \frac{E_{consumed}}{DoD \cdot \eta_{batt}} \\
E_{battery} &= \frac{35000}{0.85 \times 0.92} \\
E_{battery} &= \frac{35000}{0.782} \\
E_{battery} &= 44757 \; \text{Wh} \approx 44.76 \; \text{kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answer B1B(a):**
> - Total energy required: $\boxed{E_{consumed} = 35 \; kWh}$
> - Required battery energy: $\boxed{E_{battery} \approx 44.76 \; kWh}$

---

#### **Solution to B1B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Compute battery capacity in Ah.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{Ah} &= \frac{E_{battery}}{V_{pack}} \\
C_{Ah} &= \frac{44757}{350} \\
C_{Ah} &= 127.88 \; \text{Ah}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Convert 50 km/h to m/s and compute kinetic energy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= 50 \times \frac{5}{18} = 13.89 \; \text{m/s} \\
KE &= 0.5 \cdot m \cdot v^2 \\
KE &= 0.5 \times 1200 \times (13.89)^2 \\
KE &= 600 \times 192.91 \\
KE &= 115749.6 \; \text{J} \\
KE_{Wh} &= \frac{115749.6}{3600} = 32.15 \; \text{Wh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute regenerative energy recovered.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{regen} &= \beta \cdot KE_{Wh} \cdot \eta_{regen} \\
E_{regen} &= 0.6 \times 32.15 \times 0.7 \\
E_{regen} &= 13.50 \; \text{Wh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute additional range and verify.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta D &= \frac{E_{regen}}{E_{spec}} = \frac{13.50}{140} \\
\Delta D &= 0.0964 \; \text{km} = 96.4 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Comparison:** $96.4 \; m < 150 \; m$ (the 0.15 km target).
>
> **Conclusion:** The regenerative event from 50 km/h adds only ~96.4 m, which is **less than** the 0.15 km target. Therefore, the requirement is **not** met.

> **Final Answer B1B(b):**
> - Battery capacity: $\boxed{C_{Ah} \approx 127.88 \; Ah}$
> - Recovered regen energy: $\boxed{E_{regen} \approx 13.50 \; Wh}$
> - Additional range: $\boxed{\Delta D \approx 96.4 \; m < 150 \; m}$, **target not achieved**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly LOSE marks in numerical problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s.** This is the **#1** mark-deduction reason. Always write the conversion step explicitly: $v \; (m/s) = v \; (km/h) \times \frac{5}{18}$.
>
> 2. **Mixing up mass factor (1+ε).** Some students use $m$ directly without the rotational inertia correction. KTU key answers include the $\epsilon$ factor when specified.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the gear ratio entirely.** When the problem gives a gear ratio $G$, the motor torque and motor RPM must be scaled by it. Forgetting this leads to a **3–4 mark** deduction.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in gradient force.** On an uphill climb, $F_{hc}$ is positive (resisting motion). On a downhill, it is negative (aiding motion, can be negative tractive effort).
>
> 5. **Not specifying units in the final answer.** A numerical answer like "$P = 2.39$" without "kW" will receive a **0.5 to 1 mark** deduction. **Always** write the unit.
>
> 6. **Rounding too early.** Carry at least 4 significant figures through intermediate steps. Round only the final answer.
>
> 7. **Confusing $E_{spec}$ (Wh/km) with $E_{battery}$ (kWh).** These are different quantities with different units. The 5x difference between Wh and kWh is a frequent source of error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Numerical Problems (Module 3)**
>
> **MUST MEMORIZE:**
> - [x] **Four force equations** with their physical meaning: $F_{rr}$, $F_{ad}$, $F_{hc}$, $F_{la}$
> - [x] **Conversion factor:** $1 \; km/h = \frac{5}{18} \; m/s \approx 0.2778 \; m/s$
> - [x] **Standard constants:** $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$, $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \; kg/m^3$
> - [x] **Power chain:** $P_{wheel} \to P_{motor} \to P_{battery}$ with efficiency corrections
> - [x] **Torque chain:** $T_{motor} \to T_{wheel}$ with gear ratio and gearbox efficiency
> - [x] **Speed chain:** $N_{motor} = v \cdot G \cdot 60 / (2\pi \cdot r_{wheel})$
> - [x] **Energy chain:** $E_{consumed} \to E_{battery}$ with DoD and battery efficiency
> - [x] **Capacity:** $C_{Ah} = E_{battery} / V_{pack}$
> - [x] **Regen energy:** $E_{regen} = \beta \cdot 0.5 m v^2 \cdot \eta_{regen}$
> - [x] **Unit discipline:** Always carry units through every calculation
>
> **TYPICAL TYPICAL RANGES TO REMEMBER:**
> - [x] $C_d$ for sedans: **0.25 – 0.35**
> - [x] $C_r$ for car tires: **0.008 – 0.015**
> - [x] $\epsilon$ (rotational mass factor) for EVs: **0.05 – 0.15**
> - [x] DoD for Li-ion: **0.80 – 0.90**
> - [x] $\beta$ (regen fraction): **0.3 – 0.7**
> - [x] Typical $\eta_{overall}$ drivetrain: **0.75 – 0.85**
>
> **EXAM HACKS:**
> - [x] When a question says "level road", immediately set $F_{hc} = 0$.
> - [x] When a question says "constant speed", immediately set $F_{la} = 0$.
> - [x] When the gear ratio $G$ is missing, assume **direct drive** ($G = 1$).
> - [x] When $\eta$ is missing, assume ideal ($100\%$ efficient).
> - [x] Always **draw a free-body diagram** in your rough work — it earns partial credit even if the final number is wrong.
> - [x] Use **dimensional analysis** to check your answer: $F$ in N, $P$ in W, $T$ in N·m, $E$ in Wh.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Levels of Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Levels of Automation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Levels of Automation in Electric Vehicles (LoA-EV)** refer to the standardized hierarchical classification of the degree to which an EV's onboard control systems, sensors, actuators, and software algorithms assume the dynamic driving task (DDT) and related functions from the human driver. The universally accepted benchmark is **SAE J3016 (Society of Automotive Engineers, Standard J3016)**. The classification spans from **Level 0 (No Automation)** to **Level 5 (Full Automation)**, evaluating automation across three primary functional axes: 1. **Longitudinal Control** – Throttle (Acceleration) and Brake (Deceleration) management. 2. **Lateral Control** – Steering management. 3. **Object & Event Detection and Response (OEDR)** – Monitoring the driving environment for hazards. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – "The Restaurant Service Scale":** > Think of vehicle automation like a restaurant dining experience: > > - **Level 0–1** → You cook your own food, but the restaurant gives you pre-chopped vegetables (basic cruise control, ABS). > - **Level 2** → You assemble your meal from a buffet with a chef guiding your hand (Tesla Autopilot, Adaptive Cruise + Lane Keep). > - **Level 3** → A chef cooks the meal, but you must stay ready to take over if the kitchen catches fire (Traffic Jam Pilot – Mercedes, Honda). > - **Level 4** → A private chef cooks in a designated kitchen; if something goes wrong, the kitchen shuts down safely (Robotaxi in geo-fenced zones – Waymo). > - **Level 5** → A magical chef cooks anywhere on Earth with no human supervision required (Theoretical – full autonomy in all conditions). ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants - **SAE J3016**: The **global taxonomy standard** for driving automation. - **Dynamic Driving Task (DDT)**: All real-time operational and tactical functions required to operate a vehicle in on-road traffic. - **DDT Fallback**: The response by the user or system to either perform or automate the DDT after a system failure or operational limit. - **Operational Design Domain (ODD)**: The specific operating conditions (weather, road type, speed, geography) under which an automated feature is designed to function. - **Minimum Risk Maneuver (MRM)**: The controlled stop or transition executed when the automation cannot continue safely. ## 1.4 EV-Specific Automation Context > [!IMPORTANT] > In the **Electric Vehicle domain**, "Levels of Automation" extend beyond driving autonomy to include **powertrain and energy management automation**: > > - **Battery Management Automation** (State-of-Charge balancing, thermal runaway prevention). > - **Motor Torque Vectoring Automation** (Independent torque control per wheel). > - **Regenerative Braking Automation** (Blending hydraulic + regenerative braking). > - **Charging Automation** (Autonomous plug-in / wireless inductive alignment). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Levels of Driving Automation (SAE J3016) ### Level 0 – No Automation - **Driver Role**: Performs the entire DDT. - **System Role**: May provide warnings (e.g., blind spot alert, forward collision warning) but **no sustained control**. - **EV Example**: A basic electric scooter with no electronic stability control. ### Level 1 – Driver Assistance - **Driver Role**: Performs most of the DDT but is assisted by **one** sustained automation feature. - **System Role**: Either lateral **OR** longitudinal control (never both). - **EV Examples**: Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) **OR** Lane Keeping Assist (LKA) alone. Found in Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV base variants. ### Level 2 – Partial Automation - **Driver Role**: Must continuously monitor the environment; hands may be off briefly but eyes must remain on road. - **System Role**: Performs **both** lateral **AND** longitudinal control simultaneously. - **EV Examples**: Tesla Autopilot (basic), Hyundai Highway Driving Assist, MG Astor ADAS Level 2. ### Level 3 – Conditional Automation - **Driver Role**: May disengage from driving but must be ready to resume control within a specified transition time. - **System Role**: Performs the entire DDT within a defined ODD. - **EV Examples**: Honda Legend (Legend Hybrid, Japan 2021), Mercedes Drive Pilot (EQS, Germany 2022). Legal only in select ODDs. ### Level 4 – High Automation - **Driver Role**: Not required inside the vehicle in defined ODDs. - **System Role**: Performs the entire DDT and handles DDT fallback (reaches a minimal risk condition). - **EV Examples**: Waymo One robotaxi (Chandler/Phoenix, USA), Baidu Apollo Go robotaxi (China). ### Level 5 – Full Automation - **Driver Role**: No human driver required. No steering wheel or pedals needed. - **System Role**: Performs the entire DDT under **all** roadway and environmental conditions. - **EV Example**: Theoretical – no commercial Level 5 vehicle exists as of 2024. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > While driving automation levels are largely qualitative, the underlying **control theory** involves measurable quantities. Below are the key engineering formulas relevant to automation in EVs. | # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Representation | Units | Application / Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | SAE Level Classifier | $L \in \{0,1,2,3,4,5\}$ | Integer | Discrete taxonomy per SAE J3016 | | 2 | Dynamic Driving Task Responsibility | $R_{DDT} = f(L)$ | Boolean / Mixed | Determines if responsibility is on driver, system, or shared | | 3 | Operational Design Domain | $ODD = \{w, r, s, g, t\}$ | Set | Where: $w$=weather, $r$=road, $s$=speed, $g$=geofence, $t$=time of day | | 4 | Minimum Risk Maneuver Trigger | $T_{MRM} = t_{transition}$ | seconds | Time window for human re-engagement before MRM | | 5 | EV Motor Torque (Longitudinal Control) | $T_m = \frac{P_m}{\omega_m}$ | N·m | Motor torque from power and speed | | 6 | Regenerative Braking Torque | $T_{regen} = k \cdot \omega_m$ | N·m | $k$ = regenerative gain coefficient (N·m·s/rad) | | 7 | Longitudinal Vehicle Dynamics | $F_{trac} = m a + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2 + mgC_{rr}$ | N | Net tractive force accounting for drag and rolling resistance | | 8 | Battery State of Charge (BMS Automation) | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 - \frac{1}{Q_{max}}\int_{0}^{t} I_b(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Percentage | Core automation variable managed by BMS | | 9 | Lane Keeping Lateral Error | $e_{lat}(t) = y_{actual} - y_{desired}$ | meters | PID controller input for LKA / L2 systems | | 10 | Sensor Fusion (LiDAR + Camera) | $D_{fused} = \alpha D_{lidar} + (1-\alpha) D_{camera}$ | meters | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ = fusion weight | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas underpin the control loops that allow an EV to transition between automation levels. In KTU ESE problems, you may be asked to derive or apply #5, #7, #8, or #9 in the context of an automated EV subsystem. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Levels of Automation framework directly impacts: - **Insurance & Liability Law**: At L2, the driver is legally responsible; at L4, the OEM holds liability within ODD. - **Cybersecurity Design**: Higher levels require more attack-resilient V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication. - **Power Consumption**: L3+ vehicles consume 15–30% more battery due to compute and sensor loads. - **Regulatory Approval**: ODD definitions are mandatory for type-approval in EU, Japan, and increasingly India (AIS-152). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Risk Maneuver (MRM) Transition Time for Level 3 EV In **Level 3 Conditional Automation**, when the system encounters a condition outside its ODD, it issues a **Transition of Control (ToC)** request to the human driver. If the human fails to respond within time $t_{transition}$, the system must execute an MRM. ### Step 1: Define the Request-to-Hand-Back Window Let $t_{req}$ be the moment the ToC alert is issued. The driver must take over before $t_{req} + t_{transition}$, or: $$t_{safe} = t_{req} + t_{transition}$$ where $t_{safe}$ is the last safe moment for human re-engagement. ### Step 2: Compute the Safe Stopping Distance for MRM Using the EV longitudinal dynamics (assuming worst-case brake deceleration $a_{brake}$): $$d_{stop} = v_0 \cdot \Delta t + \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_{brake}}$$ ### Step 3: Combine the Equations The minimum MRM trigger distance $D_{MRM}$ from the ODD boundary is: $$D_{MRM} = d_{stop} + d_{buffer}$$ Substituting Step 2: $$D_{MRM} = v_0 \cdot \Delta t + \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_{brake}} + d_{buffer}$$ where: - $v_0$ = vehicle speed at ToC issuance (m/s) - $\Delta t$ = system latency for brake actuation (s) - $a_{brake}$ = maximum sustainable deceleration (typically $\mathbf{3\text{ m/s}^2}$ for stability, up to $\mathbf{8\text{ m/s}^2}$ in emergency) - $d_{buffer}$ = safety margin (typically $\mathbf{2\text{ m}}$) ### Step 4: Work Out Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Given**: An EV traveling at $v_0 = 30\,\text{m/s}$ (≈108 km/h) on a highway. System latency $\Delta t = 0.3\,\text{s}$. Brake deceleration $a_{brake} = 5\,\text{m/s}^2$. Buffer $d_{buffer} = 3\,\text{m}$. **Compute** $d_{stop}$: $$d_{stop} = (30)(0.3) + \frac{(30)^2}{2 \times 5}$$ $$d_{stop} = 9 + \frac{900}{10} = 9 + 90 = 99\,\text{m}$$ **Compute** $D_{MRM}$: $$D_{MRM} = 99 + 3 = 102\,\text{m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation**: The Level 3 EV system must issue the ToC at least **102 m** before the ODD boundary so that the Minimum Risk Maneuver can be executed safely even if the driver does not respond. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Driver vs. System Responsibility per Level | Level | Driver Monitors Environment? | Driver Sustains Lateral? | Driver Sustains Longitudinal? | System Performs Full DDT? | System Performs DDT Fallback? | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | **0** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No | No | | **1** | Yes | Either | Or | No | No | | **2** | Yes | No | No | No | No | | **3** | No (in ODD) | No | No | Yes (in ODD) | No (user fallback) | | **4** | No (in ODD) | No | No | Yes (in ODD) | Yes (in ODD) | | **5** | No | No | No | Yes (all conditions) | Yes (all conditions) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Mnemonic**: Levels 0–2 = **Human Drives** (with increasing help). Levels 3–5 = **Machine Drives** (with decreasing human fallback). The boundary is at Level 3. ## 3.3 Derivation: EV Battery State-of-Charge Automation (BMS) The **automated SoC estimation** is a cornerstone of EV powertrain automation. Using the Coulomb Counting method (which is the standard for Level 2+ automation in BMS): $$SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) - \frac{100}{Q_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} \eta_{coul} \cdot I_{b}(\tau)\, d\tau$$ where: - $SoC(t_0)$ = initial state-of-charge (%) - $Q_{rated}$ = rated battery capacity (Ah) - $\eta_{coul}$ = Coulombic efficiency (typically $\mathbf{0.98\text{–}0.995}$ for Li-ion) - $I_{b}(\tau)$ = instantaneous battery current (A, positive for discharge) ### Step-by-Step Application Suppose an EV draws a constant $I_b = 50\,\text{A}$ from a battery rated $Q_{rated} = 100\,\text{Ah}$ with $SoC(t_0) = 80\%$ over $\Delta t = 1\,\text{hour}$: $$SoC(1) = 80 - \frac{100}{100} \times (0.99) \times 50 \times 1$$ $$SoC(1) = 80 - 49.5 = 30.5\%$$ The BMS automation would then trigger alerts and adjust power limits accordingly. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Hierarchical Levels of Automation Pyramid ```mermaid graph TD L0["LEVEL 0: NO AUTOMATION
Driver performs full DDT"] L1["LEVEL 1: DRIVER ASSISTANCE
Single axis control ACC or LKA"] L2["LEVEL 2: PARTIAL AUTOMATION
Lateral AND Longitudinal
Driver monitors continuously"] L3["LEVEL 3: CONDITIONAL AUTOMATION
Full DDT in ODD
Human fallback required"] L4["LEVEL 4: HIGH AUTOMATION
Full DDT in ODD
System handles fallback"] L5["LEVEL 5: FULL AUTOMATION
Full DDT ALL conditions
No human needed"] L0 --> L1 L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 --> L5 style L0 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000 style L1 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#000,color:#000 style L2 fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style L3 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000 style L4 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000 style L5 fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Pyramid**: Bottom = driver-dominated control; Top = machine-dominated control. Color progression (warm to cool) reinforces the increasing transfer of responsibility. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: EV Automation Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid graph LR SENS["SENSOR LAYER
LiDAR, Radar, Camera, Ultrasonic, GPS, IMU"] FUSE["PERCEPTION FUSION
Object Detection, Tracking, SLAM"] PLAN["PLANNING LAYER
Path Planning, Behavior, ODD Check"] CTRL["CONTROL LAYER
Longitudinal and Lateral Actuators"] POW["EV POWERTRAIN
Motor Inverter, BMS, Regen Braking"] DRV["HUMAN DRIVER INTERFACE
HMI, ToC Alerts, MRM"] V2X["V2X COMMUNICATION
V2I, V2V, V2G, V2N"] CLOUD["CLOUD EDGE
HD Maps, OTA Updates, Fleet Learning"] SENS --> FUSE FUSE --> PLAN PLAN --> CTRL CTRL --> POW POW --> SENS V2X --> PLAN CLOUD --> FUSE DRV --> PLAN PLAN --> DRV style SENS fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000 style FUSE fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style PLAN fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,color:#000 style CTRL fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 style POW fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000 style DRV fill:#4169E1,stroke:#000,color:#000 style V2X fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style CLOUD fill:#808080,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Insight**: The **Control Layer** is the actual mechanism that executes the automation decision. It commands the **EV Powertrain** (motor torque, regenerative braking, BMS limits) based on outputs of the **Planning Layer**. The **HMI** interface manages transitions between driver and machine control (critical for Level 2 → Level 3 boundary). ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Decision Logic for Automation Level Transition ```mermaid graph TD A["START: System Active"] --> B{"Within ODD?"} B -- Yes --> C{"Driver Hands On Wheel?"} B -- No --> D["ISSUE TRANSITION OF CONTROL"] C -- Yes --> E["MAINTAIN LEVEL 2"] C -- No --> F{"Level 3 Capable?"} F -- Yes --> G["ENGAGE LEVEL 3 DDT"] F -- No --> H["DEGRADE TO LEVEL 1"] G --> I{"Driver Response in t_transition?"} I -- Yes --> J["DRIVER RESUMES CONTROL"] I -- No --> K["EXECUTE MRM"] D --> K K --> L["SAFE STOP OR LANE PULL"] L --> M["END: System Standby"] style A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style B fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000,color:#000 style C fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000,color:#000 style D fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style F fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000,color:#000 style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000 style H fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 style I fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000,color:#000 style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000 style K fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 style L fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000 style M fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Decision Logic Insight**: The diamond shapes represent decision gates where **ODD boundary checks** and **driver readiness assessments** are critical. The transition to MRM (red block) is the failsafe path when neither human nor machine can safely operate. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Part A Pattern**: Short-answer questions testing definition, classification, or simple reasoning. Each carries 3 marks. ### Question A1 > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** > Define the term **"Operational Design Domain (ODD)"** as applied to automated electric vehicles. List any four parameters that constitute an ODD. ### **Model Answer (3 Marks)** **Operational Design Domain (ODD)** is the specific set of operating conditions and environments under which an automated driving system is engineered to function safely. (1 Mark) Four parameters of an ODD include: 1. **Weather conditions** – Rain, snow, fog, clear. 2. **Road type** – Highway, urban, rural, off-road. 3. **Speed range** – e.g., 0–60 km/h, 60–130 km/h. 4. **Geographic boundaries** – Geo-fenced areas (e.g., a specific city or highway corridor). (2 Marks) ### Question A2 > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** > Distinguish between **Level 2 (Partial Automation)** and **Level 3 (Conditional Automation)** in EV driving automation. Give one commercial EV example for each level. ### **Model Answer (3 Marks)** | Aspect | Level 2 – Partial Automation | Level 3 – Conditional Automation | |---|---|---| | Who monitors environment? | Driver (must remain alert) | System (within ODD) | | Who handles fallback? | Driver | Driver (with system warning) | | EV Example | Tesla Model 3 Autopilot basic | Honda Legend Hybrid, Mercedes EQS Drive Pilot | (3 Marks – 1 for distinction, 1 for each example) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern**: A 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts: (a) carrying **7 marks** and (b) carrying **7 marks**. Below, two complete alternatives are provided so the student can practice both. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the **SAE J3016 Levels of Driving Automation** with a clear differentiation between each level. Discuss the role of the **human driver** and the **automated system** at each level. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For an electric vehicle operating at **Level 3 Conditional Automation**, derive the **Minimum Risk Maneuver (MRM) trigger distance** given: - Vehicle speed $v_0 = 25\,\text{m/s}$ - System latency $\Delta t = 0.4\,\text{s}$ - Brake deceleration $a_{brake} = 4\,\text{m/s}^2$ - Safety buffer $d_{buffer} = 5\,\text{m}$ Compute the total MRM distance. **(7 Marks)** ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** The **SAE J3016** standard classifies vehicle automation into **six discrete levels**: | Level | Name | Brief Definition | |---|---|---| | 0 | No Automation | Driver performs all driving tasks. (0.5 Marks) | | 1 | Driver Assistance | System performs **either** lateral **or** longitudinal control (e.g., Adaptive Cruise Control). Driver remains in full control. (1 Mark) | | 2 | Partial Automation | System performs **both** lateral and longitudinal control simultaneously (e.g., Tesla Autopilot basic). Driver must continuously monitor the environment. (1.5 Marks) | | 3 | Conditional Automation | System performs the **entire DDT** within a defined ODD. Driver may disengage but must be available for fallback within $t_{transition}$. (1.5 Marks) | | 4 | High Automation | System performs the entire DDT and **handles DDT fallback** within the ODD. No driver required in ODD (e.g., robotaxis). (1.5 Marks) | | 5 | Full Automation | System performs entire DDT in **all** conditions. No steering wheel needed. (1 Mark) | #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Identify the formula (1 Mark)** $$D_{MRM} = v_0 \cdot \Delta t + \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_{brake}} + d_{buffer}$$ **Step 2: Substitute values (2 Marks)** $$D_{MRM} = 25 \times 0.4 + \frac{25^2}{2 \times 4} + 5$$ $$D_{MRM} = 10 + \frac{625}{8} + 5$$ **Step 3: Compute (2 Marks)** $$D_{MRM} = 10 + 78.125 + 5 = 93.125\,\text{m}$$ **Step 4: Conclusion (2 Marks)** The Level 3 EV automation system must issue a Transition of Control (ToC) alert at least **93.125 m** before the ODD boundary to ensure a safe Minimum Risk Maneuver if the human driver fails to respond. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **EV-specific automation layers** beyond driving autonomy. Explain how **Battery Management System (BMS)** automation, **Motor Control automation**, and **Regenerative Braking automation** contribute to the overall automation hierarchy of an electric vehicle. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An EV's battery management system uses **Coulomb Counting** to estimate State of Charge (SoC). Given: - Rated capacity $Q_{rated} = 120\,\text{Ah}$ - Initial SoC $SoC(t_0) = 90\%$ - Constant discharge current $I_b = 40\,\text{A}$ for 2 hours - Coulombic efficiency $\eta_{coul} = 0.99$ Calculate the **final SoC** and explain how this automation contributes to driver convenience at Level 2 and above. **(7 Marks)** ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** EV automation extends beyond driving control. Three key subsystems are: 1. **Battery Management System (BMS) Automation** (2.5 Marks) - Continuously monitors voltage, current, and temperature of each cell. - Balances State-of-Charge across cells (cell balancing). - Predicts range using SoC and State-of-Health (SoH). - At Level 2+, BMS communicates SoC projections to the route planner, enabling **intelligent energy-aware path planning**. 2. **Motor Control Automation** (2.5 Marks) - Uses Field-Oriented Control (FOC) to optimize torque per ampere. - Torque vectoring automates individual wheel control for stability. - Enables **regenerative braking integration** during deceleration. 3. **Regenerative Braking Automation** (2 Marks) - Blends regenerative and friction braking based on SoC, brake pedal pressure, and deceleration demand. - At higher automation levels, predicts upcoming deceleration events using sensor fusion and **GPS + HD map gradient data**. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Step 1: Identify the Coulomb Counting formula (1 Mark)** $$SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) - \frac{100}{Q_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} \eta_{coul} \cdot I_{b}(\tau)\, d\tau$$ **Step 2: Substitute values (2 Marks)** Since $I_b$ is constant: $$\int_{0}^{2} I_b(\tau)\, d\tau = 40 \times 2 = 80\,\text{Ah}$$ $$SoC(2) = 90 - \frac{100}{120} \times (0.99) \times 80$$ **Step 3: Compute (2 Marks)** $$SoC(2) = 90 - \frac{100}{120} \times 79.2$$ $$SoC(2) = 90 - 66 = 24\%$$ **Step 4: Interpretation (2 Marks)** The BMS automation will now: - Alert the driver at **SoC = 20%** (Low Battery Warning). - Suggest nearest charging stations via HMI. - Limit motor torque to **eco-mode** to extend range. - At Level 3+, the system may autonomously route to a charging station within its ODD. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > Students commonly lose marks on this topic in the following ways: > > 1. **Confusing Level 2 and Level 3**: At Level 2, the driver **must continuously monitor**. At Level 3, the driver **may disengage within ODD**. Mixing these descriptions costs 2–3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the units in derivations**: MRM distance problems often lose 1 mark because students write the numerical answer without specifying **meters (m)**. > 3. **Misapplying SAE vs NHTSA terminology**: KTU expects **SAE J3016** as the standard. Using NHTSA's older 5-level scale (which lacked Level 5 distinct definition) is a common error. > 4. **Skipping Coulombic efficiency**: Many students omit $\eta_{coul}$ in BMS-SoC problems, losing 1 mark. > 5. **Not defining ODD explicitly**: A common KTU pitfall — students describe an automated feature without stating its ODD, losing 1–2 marks in conceptual questions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist – Levels of Automation in EVs** - ✅ **SAE J3016** is the gold-standard taxonomy with **6 levels** (0 to 5). - ✅ **Level 2 and below** → Driver responsibility (human driver must monitor). - ✅ **Level 3** → First level where system performs the entire DDT within an **ODD**. - ✅ **Level 4** → System handles its own DDT fallback; no driver needed within ODD. - ✅ **Level 5** → Theoretical full autonomy in **all** conditions (does not yet exist commercially). - ✅ **Operational Design Domain (ODD)** defines the operating envelope (weather, road, speed, geofence). - ✅ **MRM** = Minimum Risk Maneuver; triggered when system cannot continue or driver fails to respond. - ✅ **Transition of Control (ToC)** = System request to driver to retake manual control (Level 3). - ✅ **DDT** = Dynamic Driving Task (longitudinal + lateral + OEDR). - ✅ **EV-specific automation** includes BMS (SoC, SoH), motor FOC, regen blending, torque vectoring, and autonomous charging alignment. - ✅ **Coulomb Counting** is the standard BMS SoC estimation: $$SoC(t) = SoC(t_0) - \frac{100}{Q_{rated}} \int_{t_0}^{t} \eta_{coul} \cdot I_{b}(\tau)\, d\tau$$ - ✅ **MRM Distance Formula**: $$D_{MRM} = v_0 \cdot \Delta t + \frac{v_0^2}{2 a_{brake}} + d_{buffer}$$ - ✅ **Commercial Examples to Memorize**: - Level 2 → Tesla Autopilot (basic), Hyundai Highway Driving Assist - Level 3 → Honda Legend, Mercedes EQS Drive Pilot - Level 4 → Waymo One, Baidu Apollo Go robotaxis - Level 5 → None yet (theoretical) - ✅ **Mnemonic for Level Boundaries**: *"0 to 2 Human Drives, 3 to 5 Machine Drives."* - ✅ **Liability Shift**: Legal responsibility lies with the **driver** in Levels 0–2, shifts to **OEM** in Levels 3+ (within ODD).

topics.title: Significance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Significance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET522 – Electric Vehicles)**, the term **"Significance"** within Module 4 refers to the **multidimensional importance, impact, and transformative role of Electric Vehicles (EVs)** in the modern transportation ecosystem. It encompasses the environmental, economic, technological, energy-security, and social dimensions that make EVs a critical pillar of sustainable mobility in the 21st century.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Significance* is the study of *why* Electric Vehicles matter — quantifying their role in **decarbonization**, **energy independence**, **operational efficiency**, **urban air quality improvement**, and the **paradigm shift** from Internal Combustion Engine (ICE) vehicles to electrified powertrains.

The significance is typically analyzed across **four pillars**:
1. **Environmental Significance** – Reduction in greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.
2. **Economic Significance** – Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) and operational savings.
3. **Energy & Strategic Significance** – Reduced crude-oil import dependency.
4. **Technological Significance** – Advancement in power electronics, batteries, and motors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Telephone Analogy"**
>
> Think of the transition from **ICE vehicles to EVs** like the transition from **landline phones to smartphones**. Initially, landlines worked perfectly fine — just like ICE cars. But smartphones (EVs) didn't just replace a function; they **redefined** communication (mobility), introduced new ecosystems (apps = charging networks, smart features), reduced long-term costs (data plans vs. fuel), and reshaped society (ride-sharing, gig economy). 
>
> Similarly, EVs are not merely "cars with batteries" — they are **mobile energy units**, **grid assets**, and **clean-air machines** rolled into one. Their significance extends far beyond the tailpipe.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **bolded** constants and metrics are universally cited when discussing EV significance:

- **Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency of BEV**: ~ **70–80%**
- **Well-to-Wheel Efficiency of ICEV**: ~ **20–30%**
- **CO₂ Emission Factor (India Grid Average, 2024)**: ~ **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh**
- **Battery Energy Density (Current Li-ion)**: **150–250 Wh/kg**
- **Targeted Energy Density (Solid-State, 2030)**: **400–500 Wh/kg**
- **EV Efficiency (kWh/100 km)**: **15–20 kWh/100 km** (typical passenger car)
- **ICE Fuel Consumption Equivalent**: **6–8 L/100 km**

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always quote **Well-to-Wheel (WTW)** efficiency, not just Tank-to-Wheel (TTW). KTU questions frequently ask students to compare EVs vs. ICEVs on a *full lifecycle* basis.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of EV Significance — Structured Analysis

### 🔹 Pillar 1: Environmental Significance

- **Why it matters:** The transportation sector contributes **~14–17%** of global GHG emissions. ICE vehicles emit CO₂, NOₓ, PM2.5, and hydrocarbons directly from the tailpipe.
- **How EVs help:** EVs have **zero tailpipe emissions (ZTEs)**. Even when accounting for electricity generation, the WTW CO₂ is significantly lower.
- **Key Insight:** As the grid becomes greener (more solar, wind, hydro), the WTW CO₂ of EVs **decreases over time** — making them "future-proof," unlike ICE vehicles which become dirtier as the world decarbonizes.

### 🔹 Pillar 2: Economic Significance

- **Why it matters:** Fuel costs constitute **30–40%** of an ICE vehicle's lifetime operating expense.
- **How EVs help:** Electricity is **3–5× cheaper per km** than petrol/diesel. Maintenance costs are **40–50% lower** (no oil changes, fewer moving parts ~**~20 moving parts vs. ~2000 in ICE**).
- **Key Insight:** Higher upfront cost is offset by **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** parity typically reached within **3–5 years** of ownership.

### 🔹 Pillar 3: Energy & Strategic Significance

- **Why it matters:** India imports **~85%** of its crude oil, creating a major forex drain and strategic vulnerability.
- **How EVs help:** EVs run on **domestically generated electricity**, reducing import dependency. A 10% EV penetration can save **$10–15 billion** in forex annually (India-specific).
- **Key Insight:** EVs enable **"Power-to-Mobility"** — using stranded renewable energy for transportation.

### 🔹 Pillar 4: Technological Significance

- **Why it matters:** EVs are a **convergence platform** for batteries, power electronics, IoT, AI, and autonomous driving.
- **How EVs help:** They drive innovation in:
  - **Battery Management Systems (BMS)**
  - **Wide-Bandgap Semiconductors** (SiC, GaN)
  - **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** technology
  - **Regenerative Braking** systems

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU University Exam (Module 4 – Significance)**

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
|:-----:|:----------------------|:---------------------------:|:------------------|
| 1 | Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency | $\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTT} \times \eta_{TTW}$ | Dimensionless (ratio) |
| 2 | Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency (EV) | $\eta_{TTW,EV} = \dfrac{E_{out,mech}}{E_{in,elec}}$ | **70–80%** |
| 3 | Tank-to-Wheel Efficiency (ICE) | $\eta_{TTW,ICE} = \dfrac{E_{out,mech}}{E_{in,fuel}}$ | **20–30%** |
| 4 | CO₂ Emission Rate (Vehicle) | $E_{CO_2} = \dfrac{E_{cons} \times EF_{grid}}{D}$ | kg CO₂/km |
| 5 | Cost per Kilometer (EV) | $C_{EV} = \dfrac{C_{elec} \times E_{cons}}{100}$ | ₹/km |
| 6 | Cost per Kilometer (ICE) | $C_{ICE} = \dfrac{C_{fuel} \times F_{cons}}{100}$ | ₹/km |
| 7 | Operating Cost Savings | $S = D_{annual} \times (C_{ICE} - C_{EV})$ | ₹/year |
| 8 | Payback Period (Incremental) | $T_{payback} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{upfront}}{S_{annual}}$ | Years |
| 9 | Battery Specific Energy | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{m_{bat}}$ | Wh/kg |
| 10 | Energy Density (Volumetric) | $E_{vol} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{V_{bat}}$ | Wh/L |
| 11 | GHG Reduction (%) | $\%\Delta GHG = \dfrac{GHG_{ICE} - GHG_{EV}}{GHG_{ICE}} \times 100$ | Percentage |
| 12 | Equivalence Factor (1 L fuel) | $E_{equiv} \approx 8.89 \text{ kWh}$ of electricity | kWh/L |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols Glossary:**
> - $E_{cons}$ = Energy consumption (kWh/100km)  
> - $EF_{grid}$ = Grid emission factor (kg CO₂/kWh)  
> - $D$ = Distance (km)  
> - $C_{elec}$ = Electricity cost (₹/kWh)  
> - $C_{fuel}$ = Fuel cost (₹/L)  
> - $F_{cons}$ = Fuel consumption (L/100km)  
> - $D_{annual}$ = Annual distance driven (km)  
> - $E_{bat}$ = Battery energy (Wh)  
> - $m_{bat}$ = Battery mass (kg)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Planning:** Cities use EV significance metrics to design **Low Emission Zones (LEZ)**.
- **Policy Making:** Governments justify FAME-II incentives using **TCO** and **GHG reduction** models.
- **Automotive R&D:** OEMs use WTW efficiency to position products in the market.
- **Grid Operators:** Use V2G significance for load-balancing strategies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Well-to-Wheel (WTW) Efficiency Comparison

### Step 1 — Define the Two Stages

The total energy flow from primary source to wheels is split into:
- **WTT (Well-to-Tank):** Energy extraction → processing → distribution to vehicle.
- **TTW (Tank-to-Wheel):** Energy in vehicle (fuel/electricity) → useful work at wheels.

$$
\eta_{WTW} = \eta_{WTT} \times \eta_{TTW}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply to Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)

For a BEV:
- **WTT Stage:** Grid generation + transmission + distribution losses.
  - $\eta_{WTT,EV} \approx 0.40$ (India grid average, including T&D losses of ~20%)
- **TTW Stage:** Electric motor + drivetrain efficiency.
  - $\eta_{TTW,EV} \approx 0.85$ (typical PMSM motor efficiency)

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{WTW,BEV} = 0.40 \times 0.85 = 0.34 \;\; (\text{i.e., } 34\%)
$$

> **Logic Step:** Even with India's coal-heavy grid, the BEV achieves **34% WTW efficiency**, and this number **improves to ~70%** as the grid becomes renewable-dominated.

### Step 3 — Apply to Internal Combustion Engine Vehicle (ICEV)

For an ICEV:
- **WTT Stage:** Crude oil extraction → refining → transport.
  - $\eta_{WTT,ICE} \approx 0.85$ (refining is reasonably efficient)
- **TTW Stage:** Engine + transmission losses.
  - $\eta_{TTW,ICE} \approx 0.25$ (Carnot-limited by combustion temperature)

Therefore:

$$
\eta_{WTW,ICE} = 0.85 \times 0.25 = 0.2125 \;\; (\text{i.e., } \approx 21\%)
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Efficiency Ratio

$$
\text{Ratio} = \dfrac{\eta_{WTW,BEV}}{\eta_{WTW,ICE}} = \dfrac{0.34}{0.2125} \approx 1.6
$$

> **Conclusion:** A BEV is **~1.6× more efficient** than an ICEV in the Indian grid scenario. In renewable-heavy grids (e.g., Norway), this ratio exceeds **3×**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CO₂ Emission Reduction Analysis

### Step 1 — CO₂ Emission from ICE Vehicle

A petrol car consuming **6 L/100 km**:
- Emission factor of petrol: **2.31 kg CO₂/L**

$$
E_{CO_2,ICE} = \dfrac{6 \times 2.31}{100} = 0.1386 \;\; \text{kg CO}_2\text{/km}
$$

### Step 2 — CO₂ Emission from BEV (India Grid)

A BEV consuming **15 kWh/100 km**:
- India grid emission factor: **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh**

$$
E_{CO_2,BEV} = \dfrac{15 \times 0.72}{100} = 0.108 \;\; \text{kg CO}_2\text{/km}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Percentage Reduction

$$
\%\Delta GHG = \dfrac{0.1386 - 0.108}{0.1386} \times 100 \approx 22.07\%
$$

> **Insight:** Even with India's coal-dominated grid, a BEV emits **~22% less CO₂ per km** than an ICEV. With a **solar-charged BEV**, this reduction exceeds **80%**.

### Step 4 — Annualized CO₂ Savings
