# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mix-up in $K_t$ and $K_e$:** In SI units $K_t = K_e$ numerically, but units differ (N·m/A vs V·s/rad). Students often write "$K_t = K_e = 0.1$" without units — **deduct 1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing $E_b$ with applied voltage:** $E_b$ is the back-EMF (induced), not the supply. The supply minus $E_b$ drops across $R_a$.
> 3. **Solenoid force vs energy:** Force is $F = dW/dx$ (gradient), not $F = W/x$. Use co-energy for current-controlled systems.
> 4. **Forgetting Lenz's law sign:** Always state that $E_b$ opposes $V_a$.
> 5. **Maximum power point:** Students often claim max power at $T_{stall}$ or at no-load — both are zero-power points. Correct answer is at the **knee of the curve**, i.e., $T = T_{stall}/2$ and $\omega = \omega_0/2$.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**"Define an actuator in the context of Electric Vehicles. List any four actuator applications in a modern EV with one example of the actuator type used."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** An actuator in an EV is a device that converts electrical energy from the control signal into a physical mechanical action (linear or rotary) to perform a vehicle function. It acts as the "muscle" of the EV, executing commands from the VCU.
- **Four applications (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Throttle control** — BLDC motor with reduction gear.
  2. **Brake-by-Wire** — Electrohydraulic actuator (EHA) with BLDC-driven piston pump.
  3. **Steer-by-Wire** — PMSM motor with planetary gear and torque sensor.
  4. **HVAC blower** — BLDC motor driving a centrifugal fan.

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"State and explain the back-EMF equation of a DC motor. Why is it called 'back' EMF?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Equation (1.5 marks):** $E_b = K_e \Phi \omega_m$, where $K_e$ is the EMF constant, $\Phi$ is the flux per pole in Wb, and $\omega_m$ is the mechanical angular speed in rad/s.
- **Explanation (1 mark):** As the armature rotates in the magnetic field, the conductors cut flux lines, inducing an EMF by Faraday's law.
- **"Back" (0.5 mark):** This induced EMF opposes the applied armature voltage $V_a$ (per Lenz's law), thereby reducing the net voltage available to drive current through the armature — hence "back" or "counter" EMF. The voltage equation is $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Students answer ONE full question per module-internal choice.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module-4 Choice-1]

**"With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an Electrohydraulic Brake-By-Wire Actuator used in modern EVs. Derive the expression for the hydraulic power delivered by the actuator and discuss its role in regenerative braking blending."**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the construction and working of an EHA Brake-by-Wire actuator with a block diagram.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. **Brake pedal** with a pedal-travel sensor (potentiometer or Hall sensor) sends the driver's demand to the Brake ECU.
2. **BLDC drive motor** (typically 200–500 W) — electronically commutated, three-phase, with built-in encoder or Hall sensors.
3. **Fixed-displacement axial-piston pump** (e.g., 1–5 cc/rev) — driven by the BLDC motor.
4. **Accumulator** — a small nitrogen-charged bladder that maintains residual pressure (~30–50 bar) for instant response.
5. **Proportional pressure control valve** — modulates the pressure delivered to each wheel caliper.
6. **Brake calipers** with single or dual pistons, integrated with pad wear sensors.
7. **Brake ECU** — closed-loop controller running PID or MPC, communicating over CAN-FD.

**Working (3 marks):**
- The driver presses the brake pedal → pedal sensor reports pedal travel to Brake ECU.
- The Brake ECU calculates the requested brake torque per wheel, factoring in vehicle speed, deceleration, and **regen-blending command** from the VCU.
- The ECU drives the BLDC motor with a controlled PWM signal (using FOC for smooth torque).
- The motor spins the axial-piston pump → pressurizes brake fluid.
- The proportional valve directs the correct pressure to each caliper.
- The pistons clamp the pads onto the rotor → friction torque slows the wheel.
- A **pedal-feedback motor** (a separate small actuator) provides haptic feedback to the driver's foot, simulating the natural feel of a hydraulic pedal.
- Closed-loop: pressure sensors and wheel-speed sensors feed back to the ECU to correct errors within ~5 ms.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** (See Figure 4 in Section 4 — Mermaid block diagram of EHA system.)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the hydraulic power expression and discuss regen-blending.**

**Derivation of Hydraulic Power (3 marks):**

The volumetric flow rate $Q$ delivered by a fixed-displacement pump is:

$$Q = D_m \cdot \omega_p$$

where $D_m$ is the pump displacement in m³/rev and $\omega_p$ is the pump speed in rev/s (or rad/s with appropriate unit conversion of $D_m$).

The hydraulic power delivered at pressure $\Delta p$ across the pump is:

$$P_{hyd} = Q \cdot \Delta p = D_m \cdot \omega_p \cdot \Delta p$$

Substituting the relation between motor speed and pump speed via gear ratio $N_g$ ($\omega_p = \omega_m / N_g$) and motor torque equation $T_m = K_t \Phi I_a$ with pump torque $T_p = T_m \cdot N_g \cdot \eta_g$:

$$P_{hyd} = \frac{D_m}{N_g} \cdot \omega_m \cdot \Delta p \cdot \eta_g$$

**Regenerative Braking Blending (4 marks):**

In an EV, when the driver releases the accelerator or presses the brake:
- The **traction motor** acts as a **generator** (regenerative actuator), converting kinetic energy → electrical energy stored in the battery. This generates a **regenerative torque** $T_{regen}$ that slows the vehicle.
- The total demanded deceleration $a_{req}$ may exceed what regen can safely provide (limited by battery SOC, temperature, and motor limits).
- The **Brake ECU** receives the desired deceleration from the VCU and **blends** the torque:
  $$T_{total} = T_{regen} + T_{friction}$$
- The EHA friction brake is commanded to provide only the **deficit** torque: $T_{friction} = T_{total} - T_{regen}$.
- This maximizes energy recovery (up to 70% in city driving) and **minimizes pad wear**.
- Blending logic uses a **battery SOC limit curve**, **motor speed limit curve**, and **stability/ABS priority** overrides.
- Below ~0.3g deceleration, EHA is mostly disengaged; above 0.3g, both regen and friction act in parallel.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module-4 Choice-2]

**"A 48 V BLDC motor used as an EV cooling-pump actuator has the following specifications:**
- **Armature resistance** $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$
- **Torque constant** $K_t = 0.08 \, \text{N·m/A}$
- **EMF constant** $K_e = 0.08 \, \text{V·s/rad}$ (SI units)
- **Supply voltage** $V_a = 48 \, \text{V}$
- **Flux per pole** $\Phi = 0.01 \, \text{Wb}$
- **No-load current** $I_0 = 0.5 \, \text{A}$

**(a)** Compute the **no-load speed**, **stall torque**, and **maximum mechanical power** of this actuator.
**(b)** If the motor is driving a coolant pump with a load torque $T_L = 0.4 \, \text{N·m}$ and rotational losses of 5 W, compute the **steady-state speed**, **input current**, **input electrical power**, and **overall efficiency**."

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $V_a = 48$ V, $R_a = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $K_t = 0.08$ N·m/A, $K_e = 0.08$ V·s/rad, $\Phi = 0.01$ Wb.

**Step 1 — No-load speed $\omega_0$ (3 marks):**
At no-load, armature current is the no-load current $I_0 = 0.5$ A (which overcomes friction/windage). The back-EMF nearly equals supply voltage:

$$E_{b,0} = V_a - I_0 R_a = 48 - (0.5)(0.5) = 48 - 0.25 = 47.75 \text{ V}$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{E_{b,0}}{K_e \Phi} = \frac{47.75}{(0.08)(0.01)} = \frac{47.75}{0.0008} = 59687.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

Convert to rpm: $N_0 = \dfrac{59687.5 \times 60}{2\pi} \approx 570{,}000$ rpm.

**Alternative simpler approach** (ignoring $I_0 R_a$ for idealization):
$$\omega_0 = \frac{V_a}{K_e \Phi} = \frac{48}{0.0008} = 60{,}000 \text{ rad/s} \approx 9.55 \text{ krpm}$$

(We'll use the **idealized** $I_0 = 0$ form for clarity in the textbook solution.)

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = 60{,}000 \text{ rad/s} \approx 9549 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 2 — Stall torque $T_{stall}$ (2 marks):**
At stall ($\omega = 0$), $E_b = 0$, so $I_{stall} = V_a / R_a = 48 / 0.5 = 96$ A.

$$T_{stall} = K_t \Phi I_{stall} = (0.08)(0.01)(96) = 0.0768 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{stall} = 0.0768 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum mechanical power (2 marks):**

$$P_{max} = \frac{T_{stall} \cdot \omega_0}{4} = \frac{0.0768 \times 60{,}000}{4} = \frac{4608}{4} = 1152 \text{ W}$$

At this point, $\omega = \omega_0/2 = 30{,}000$ rad/s and $T = T_{stall}/2 = 0.0384$ N·m.

$$\boxed{P_{max} = 1152 \text{ W} \approx 1.15 \text{ kW}}$$

**Reality check (commentary):** The computed $\omega_0$ is impractically high for a real pump motor — this indicates the constants chosen are for pedagogical illustration. In practice, $K_t \Phi$ is larger to give realistic speeds (2000–6000 rpm for pumps). **Note this in exam** if asked.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Load torque $T_L = 0.4$ N·m, rotational losses $P_{rot} = 5$ W (friction + windage).

**Step 1 — Required electromagnetic torque $T_e$ (1.5 marks):**
The motor must overcome both load torque and rotational losses (treated as a torque loss $T_{rot} = P_{rot}/\omega$, to be computed at the operating speed). At steady state:

$$T_e = T_L + T_{rot}$$

Since $T_{rot}$ depends on $\omega$, and $\omega$ depends on $T_e$, this is implicit. **Approach:** Use an iterative or numerical solution, OR assume the rotational loss torque is small compared to $T_L$ for a first estimate.

Estimate: at $\omega \approx 30{,}000$ rad/s, $T_{rot} = 5 / 30{,}000 \approx 0.000167$ N·m, which is negligible.

$$T_e \approx T_L + T_{rot} \approx 0.4 + 0.00017 \approx 0.4002 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Required armature current $I_a$ (1.5 marks):**

$$I_a = \frac{T_e}{K_t \Phi} = \frac{0.4002}{(0.08)(0.01)} = \frac{0.4002}{0.0008} = 500.25 \text{ A}$$

**Reality check:** $I_a = 500$ A is far too large for a 48 V pump — this confirms the constants are illustrative. In a real exam, numbers would be consistent. **KTU students should compute mechanically and note the observation.**

**Step 3 — Back-EMF and steady-state speed (2 marks):**

$$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 48 - (500.25)(0.5) = 48 - 250.125 = -202.125 \text{ V}$$

A **negative** $E_b$ is unphysical (the motor would be in generator/braking mode). This means the load torque $T_L = 0.4$ N·m **exceeds** the stall torque $T_{stall} = 0.0768$ N·m — the motor cannot drive the load and will stall.

**Engineering Interpretation:** The motor must be **resized** for the load. For the problem's pedagogical purpose, let's **hypothetically** recompute with a smaller load to demonstrate the method, or simply state:

> "Since $T_L > T_{stall}$, the motor stalls. The student should reselect the motor with lower $K_t\Phi$ or higher supply voltage."

**Step 4 — Methodology demonstration (alternative correct procedure) (2 marks):**

For a load within the motor's capability, the general solution procedure is:
1. $I_a = (T_L + T_{rot}) / (K_t \Phi)$
2. $E_b = V_a - I_a R_a$
3. $\omega = E_b / (K_e \Phi)$
4. $P_{in} = V_a I_a$
5. $P_{out} = T_L \omega$
6. $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in} \times 100\%$

**Substituting sample numerical values** (assuming the motor were matched to a smaller load, e.g., $T_L = 0.02$ N·m, which is within $T_{stall}$):
- $I_a = 0.02 / 0.0008 = 25$ A
- $E_b = 48 - 25(0.5) = 35.5$ V
- $\omega = 35.5 / 0.0008 = 44{,}375$ rad/s
- $P_{in} = 48 \times 25 = 1200$ W
- $P_{out} = 0.02 \times 44{,}375 = 887.5$ W
- $\eta = 887.5/1200 = 73.96\%$

**Conclusion:** EV pump actuators are typically designed at the **maximum-efficiency operating point**, which lies near $P_{max}/2$ of the torque-speed curve, achieving efficiencies of 85–95% in modern BLDC designs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 – Actuators):**

### Core Concepts
- [x] **Actuator** = electrical-input-to-mechanical-output transducer; the "muscle" of the EV.
- [x] **Primary role:** Execute VCU commands for motion, force, position, or flow control.
- [x] **Three main classes:** Electromagnetic, Electrohydraulic (EHA), Electromechanical (EMA).

### Key Equations (Memorize)
- [x] $T_e = K_t \Phi I_a$ — electromagnetic torque
- [x] $E_b = K_e \Phi \omega_m$ — back-EMF
- [x] $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$ — armature voltage equation
- [x] $F_{sol} = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 I^2 A}{2g^2}$ — solenoid force (inversely proportional to $g^2$)
- [x] $P_{hyd} = Q \cdot \Delta p = D_m \omega_p \Delta p$ — hydraulic power
- [x] $P_{max} = \dfrac{T_{stall} \cdot \omega_0}{4}$ — maximum mechanical power at $\omega_0/2$
- [x] $K_t = K_e$ in SI units (numerically equal, but units differ)

### Application Hotspots
- [x] **Throttle:** BLDC motor with gear reduction, 12 V.
- [x] **Brake-by-Wire:** EHA (BLDC + axial-piston pump + proportional valve).
- [x] **Steer-by-Wire:** PMSM with planetary gear and torque feedback (ASIL-D).
- [x] **HVAC & Cooling:** BLDC blowers and pumps on 12 V or HV bus.
- [x] **Active Suspension:** Linear EMA or MR-fluid damper, 48 V.

### Critical Engineering Insights
- [x] Solenoid force is **highly non-linear** in gap (∝ $1/g^2$) → use **PWM soft-start** to prevent impact.
- [x] **Regen-blending:** Brake ECU must coordinate friction + regen torque for smooth deceleration and energy recovery.
- [x] **Functional safety:** Brake and steering actuators are **ASIL-D** under ISO 26262 — must have redundant sensors and fail-safe modes.
- [x] **Power density:** Modern EV actuators target $\geq 5$ kW/kg and $\geq 95\%$ efficiency.
- [x] **Maximum power transfer:** At $\omega = \omega_0/2$, the motor delivers $P_{max}$; this is the design sweet spot for traction motors.

### Common Exam Traps to Avoid
- [x] Don't confuse **stall torque** with maximum operating torque (stall is zero-speed limit, not a recommended operating point).
- [x] Don't forget **Lenz's law** sign in back-EMF (it opposes $V_a$).
- [x] Don't use $K_t = K_e$ in non-SI units (e.g., rpm, oz·in) without conversion factors.
- [x] Don't claim **maximum power at no-load or stall** — both are zero-power endpoints; max is at the knee.

> **End of Module 4 – Actuators Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (PEEET522) — Outcome-Based Education*

topics.title: Path Planning and Effects of Automation in Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Path Planning and Effects of Automation in Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Path Planning — Formal Definition

**Path Planning** in the context of autonomous and electric vehicles is defined as the computational process of determining an optimal, collision-free, and dynamically feasible trajectory from a current state (position, velocity, orientation) to a desired goal state, while satisfying vehicle kinematic/dynamic constraints, environmental obstacles, and traffic regulations.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 4, path planning forms a critical subset of the **Autonomous Driving Stack**, typically positioned between **Perception** (sensor data interpretation) and **Control** (actuator commands).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEEET522 Module 4):**
> Path planning is taught as part of the **Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)** and **Vehicle Automation** units. Students are expected to identify different planning algorithms, their mathematical basis, and their impact on electric vehicle energy management.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are walking through a crowded shopping mall holding a large cup of coffee:

- **Global Path Planning** is the floor-map on your phone telling you *"Take the main corridor, turn left at the fountain, then enter Store 7."* — it knows the entire building layout in advance.
- **Local Path Planning** is the *real-time micro-decisions* you make: *"A child just ran in front of me, I will step right by 30 cm, slow my pace, and keep my coffee from spilling."*

In an autonomous EV, **Global Planning** uses HD maps and route networks (like Google Maps on steroids), while **Local Planning** uses live LiDAR/Camera/Radar data to dodge a pedestrian who just stepped off the curb.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard:** Path planning algorithms for road vehicles are benchmarked using the **SAE J3016 Levels of Driving Automation** (0 to 5) and validated against **ISO 21448 SOTIF** (Safety of the Intended Functionality).

### 1.3 The Two Primary Planning Architectures

| Aspect | Global Path Planning | Local Path Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | HD Map + GPS + Route Request | Real-time sensor data (LiDAR, Radar, Camera) |
| **Horizon** | Long-range (kilometres) | Short-range (10 m – 100 m) |
| **Update Rate** | Low (1 Hz) | High (20 Hz – 100 Hz) |
| **Static/Dynamic** | Mostly static obstacles | Dynamic obstacles (vehicles, pedestrians) |
| **Algorithms** | A*, Dijkstra, RRT*, PRM | DWA, MPC, TEB, Potential Fields |
| **Analogy** | Floor map of the mall | Avoiding that spilled drink on the floor |

### 1.4 Automation in Vehicles — Definition

**Vehicle Automation** refers to the use of sensors, actuators, control algorithms, and artificial intelligence to execute parts of (or the entirety of) the **Dynamic Driving Task (DDT)** that was previously performed by a human driver.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Driving Task (DDT):** All real-time operational and tactical functions required to operate a vehicle on a road, including lateral control (steering), longitudinal control (acceleration/braking), and object/event detection and response (OEDR).

The **SAE J3016** standard classifies automation into **6 levels (0–5)**, ranging from *No Automation* to *Full Automation*.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of the Path Planning Pipeline

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to appreciate that path planning is **not a single algorithm** but a layered pipeline. The following bullets break the concept into KTU-aligned logic:

1. **Mission Planning (Highest Level):**
   - *Why:* Decides the macro goal — e.g., "Drive from Kochi to Bengaluru."
   - *How:* Uses road network graph $G = (V, E)$ where vertices $V$ are intersections and edges $E$ are road segments with weight = travel time or energy.
   - *Output:* A sequence of road segments.

2. **Behavioural Planning (Middle Level):**
   - *Why:* Decides *what driving behaviour* to adopt — e.g., lane change, yielding, overtaking.
   - *How:* Finite State Machines (FSM), Markov Decision Processes (MDP), or Rule-Based systems.
   - *Output:* A high-level intent vector $\mathbf{i}(t)$.

3. **Local / Motion Planning (Lowest Level):**
   - *Why:* Generates a smooth, kinematically feasible trajectory satisfying the intent.
   - *How:* Optimisation-based (Model Predictive Control) or sampling-based (RRT*) algorithms.
   - *Output:* Reference trajectory $(x_{ref}(t), y_{ref}(t), v_{ref}(t))$ for every control tick.

### 2.2 Core Algorithms Explained

#### A. A* (A-Star) Search — The Workhorse

Used primarily for **global path planning** on a discretised grid or road graph.

- **f(n)** = Total estimated cost of node *n*
- **g(n)** = Actual cost from start to *n*
- **h(n)** = Heuristic estimate from *n* to goal

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

**Heuristic functions (h):**
- *Manhattan distance* (for 4-connected grid): $h(n) = \vert x_n - x_{goal} \vert + \vert y_n - y_{goal} \vert$
- *Euclidean distance* (for 8-connected grid): $h(n) = \sqrt{(x_n - x_{goal})^2 + (y_n - y_{goal})^2}$
- *Diagonal distance* (for 8-connected grid): $h(n) = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert) + (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot \min(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Admissibility Rule:** A heuristic is *admissible* if $h(n) \leq$ true cost to goal. This guarantees A* returns an optimal path.

#### B. RRT* (Rapidly-exploring Random Tree Star)

Used for **high-dimensional planning** (e.g., parking, narrow lanes). The algorithm incrementally builds a tree by sampling random states and steering toward them, then rewiring nearby nodes to maintain asymptotic optimality.

**Steering function** (for car-like vehicles with turning radius $r_{min}$):

$$x_{new} = x_{near} + \delta \cdot \left[ \cos(\theta), \sin(\theta), \frac{\tan(\phi)}{L} \right]^T$$

Where:
- $\delta$ = step size
- $\theta$ = heading angle
- $\phi$ = steering angle
- $L$ = wheelbase

#### C. Dynamic Window Approach (DWA)

Used for **local planning** in dynamic environments. It samples velocities $(v, \omega)$ within a reachable window and scores each by:

$$Score = \alpha \cdot heading(v,\omega) + \beta \cdot dist(v,\omega) + \gamma \cdot velocity(v,\omega)$$

Where:
- *heading* = alignment with goal
- *dist* = distance to nearest obstacle
- *velocity* = forward progress

### 2.3 The Six Levels of Vehicle Automation (SAE J3016)

| Level | Name | Steering / Accel. | Monitoring of Environment | Fallback | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **0** | No Automation | Human | Human | Human | Conventional car |
| **1** | Driver Assistance | Human or System (one) | Human | Human | Adaptive Cruise Control |
| **2** | Partial Automation | System (both) | Human | Human | Tesla Autopilot (basic) |
| **3** | Conditional Automation | System | System | Human | Traffic Jam Pilot |
| **4** | High Automation | System | System | System | Robotaxi (geofenced) |
| **5** | Full Automation | System | System | System | Steering-wheel-less pod |

### 2.4 Effects of Automation — A Multi-Dimensional Analysis

The KTU 2024 module emphasises the **systems-level impact** of automation, not just the algorithms:

#### A. Safety Impact
- **Pro:** ~94% of road accidents are attributed to human error (WHO data). Automation eliminates fatigue, distraction, DUI.
- **Con:** New failure modes — *edge cases*, *long-tail distribution* of rare events.

#### B. Energy & EV Synergy
- **Eco-Driving:** Optimised trajectories minimise jerk and unnecessary braking, increasing EV range by **8–15%**.
- **Platooning:** V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle) communication enables trucks to drive 5–10 m apart, reducing aerodynamic drag by **~40%**, slashing energy use.
- **V2G Integration:** Autonomous EVs can self-park at charging stations during off-peak hours.

#### C. Socio-Economic Impact
- **Employment shift:** Decline in driving jobs, growth in AI/robotics/software roles.
- **Urban planning:** Reduced parking demand (cars can drop passengers and self-park far away).
- **Accessibility:** Mobility for elderly, disabled, and minors.

#### D. Legal & Ethical Impact
- **Liability:** Who is at fault — driver, OEM, software provider?
- **Trolley Problem:** How an autonomous car decides in unavoidable crash scenarios.
- **Cybersecurity:** Connected vehicles are vulnerable to remote attacks (e.g., 2015 Jeep Cherokee hack).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| A* evaluation | $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$ | dimensionless or m | $g$ = path cost, $h$ = heuristic |
| Manhattan distance | $h = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ | metres | 4-connected grid |
| Euclidean distance | $h = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | metres | 8-connected grid |
| Curvature of path | $\kappa = \frac{\vert x' y'' - y' x'' \vert}{(x'^2 + y'^2)^{3/2}}$ | m$^{-1}$ | Used in path smoothness |
| Steering radius | $r = \frac{L}{\tan(\phi)}$ | metres | $L$ = wheelbase, $\phi$ = steer angle |
| Path length | $L_{path} = \int_{0}^{1} \sqrt{x'(s)^2 + y'(s)^2} \, ds$ | metres | Arc length parameterised |
| Energy consumption | $E = \int F_{tract} \cdot v \, dt$ | Joules | $F_{tract}$ includes drag, rolling, slope |
| Aerodynamic drag | $F_d = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^2$ | Newtons | $\rho \approx 1.225$ kg/m$^3$ air density |
| DWA score | $S = \alpha h + \beta d + \gamma v$ | normalised | Tune weights per scenario |
| Time-to-Collision | $TTC = \frac{\Delta d}{\Delta v}$ | seconds | Critical for safety layer |
| Platoon drag reduction | $\Delta F_d / F_d \approx 0.4$ at 5 m gap | dimensionless | Truck platooning empirical |
| EV range gain from eco-routing | $\Delta R \approx 0.08 - 0.15$ | fraction | Smooth trajectories |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Path planning is the *brain* of an autonomous EV. Without it, perception sensors are just eyes with no intention. The planner converts raw sensor data into a *story* — where the car should be, when, and at what speed.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Waymo (Google):** Uses a hybrid A* + RRT* + Behaviour planner stack. Has driven **>20 million miles** on public roads.
- **Tesla FSD:** Uses a neural-network-based planner trained on fleet data (end-to-end learning, no HD map in v12).
- **Mobileye / Wayve:** Use rule-based and learning-based planners respectively.
- **Bosch / Continental:** Tier-1 suppliers integrating planning modules with electric drivetrains for OEM partners.
- **Indian Context (KTU Relevance):** IIT Hyderabad's **autonomous car project** and **Tata Elxsi**'s ADAS R&D in Bengaluru.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Curvature-Constrained Path Smoothing

When A* returns a path on a grid, the path is **piecewise-linear** — full of sharp 90° turns that a real car cannot follow. We must smooth the path subject to a maximum curvature constraint $\kappa_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Define the raw A* path.**
Let the A* algorithm return a polyline with vertices $P_0, P_1, P_2, \dots, P_n$ in the 2D plane.

**Step 2 — Parameterise each segment.**
For a cubic Bézier curve between $P_i$ and $P_{i+1}$:

$$B(t) = (1-t)^3 P_i + 3(1-t)^2 t \, C_1 + 3(1-t) t^2 \, C_2 + t^3 P_{i+1}, \quad t \in [0, 1]$$

Where $C_1, C_2$ are control points placed tangentially to the polyline.

**Step 3 — Compute curvature.**
For a curve $B(t) = (x(t), y(t))$:

$$\kappa(t) = \frac{\vert x'(t) y''(t) - y'(t) x''(t) \vert}{(x'(t)^2 + y'(t)^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply curvature constraint.**
We require $\kappa(t) \leq \kappa_{max}$ for all $t$. The minimum turning radius is:

$$r_{min} = \frac{1}{\kappa_{max}}$$

For a car with wheelbase $L$ and max steering angle $\phi_{max}$:

$$r_{min} = \frac{L}{\tan(\phi_{max})}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for feasibility.**
Given a desired corner sharpness, we adjust the Bézier control points $C_1, C_2$ such that the resulting curve fits inside an arc of radius $r_{min}$. If the corner is too tight, the path is *infeasible* and the planner must request a new route or a wider turn.

### 3.2 Worked Example: A* on a 5×5 Grid

Consider a 5×5 grid where the start is at **S = (0, 0)** and the goal is at **G = (4, 4)**. The cell **(2, 2)** is blocked.

**Step 1 — Initialise Open Set.**
Open = $\{(0,0)\}$, Closed = $\emptyset$.

**Step 2 — Expand (0,0).**
Neighbours: (0,1), (1,0). For each, compute $g, h, f$.

- **(0,1):** $g = 1$, $h = \vert 0-4 \vert + \vert 1-4 \vert = 7$, $f = 1 + 7 = 8$
- **(1,0):** $g = 1$, $h = \vert 1-4 \vert + \vert 0-4 \vert = 7$, $f = 1 + 7 = 8$

**Step 3 — Expand the lowest-*f* node.**
Both have $f = 8$. Pick **(0,1)**.

**Step 4 — Continue expansion.**
Following the standard A* loop, the algorithm explores nodes in order of increasing $f$. The expanded matrix of $f$-values is shown below:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Grid of } f(n) = g(n) + h(n): & \\
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
8 & 8 & 10 & 12 & 14 \\
\hline
8 & 10 & \text{X} & 10 & 12 \\
\hline
10 & 12 & 14 & 8 & 10 \\
\hline
12 & 14 & 10 & 8 & 8 \\
\hline
14 & 12 & 10 & 8 & 0 \\
\hline
\end{array} &
\end{aligned}
$$

(Here 'X' marks the blocked cell (2,2) and the bottom-right (4,4) has $f = 0$ because it is the goal.)

**Step 5 — Trace back optimal path.**
The optimal path is reconstructed by following the parent pointers:

$$S(0,0) \to (1,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,0) \to (3,1) \to (3,2) \to (4,2) \to (4,3) \to G(4,4)$$

This gives a path length of **8 grid steps** — the Manhattan lower bound.

**Step 6 — Path length formula.**

$$L_{path} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \sqrt{(x_{i+1} - x_i)^2 + (y_{i+1} - y_i)^2}$$

For unit-grid steps in 4 directions: $L_{path} = n = 8$ units.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Path Planning Algorithms

| Algorithm | Completeness | Optimality | Time Complexity | Use Case | Suitable for EV? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BFS** | Yes (unweighted) | Yes (unweighted) | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Small uniform grids | No (too slow) |
| **Dijkstra** | Yes | Yes | $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | Weighted road networks | Yes (global) |
| **A*** | Yes | Yes (if admissible) | $O(b^d)$, $b$ = branching, $d$ = depth | Gridded urban maps | Yes (global) |
| **RRT** | Probabilistic | No (suboptimal) | $O(n \log n)$ | High-DOF robots | Yes (parking) |
| **RRT*** | Probabilistic | Asymptotically optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | Kinodynamic planning | Yes (racing) |
| **DWA** | Local | Locally optimal | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert \omega \vert)$ | Dynamic obstacles | Yes (local) |
| **MPC** | Constraint-based | Receding horizon | $O(n^3)$ per step | Trajectory optimisation | Yes (control) |

### 3.4 Derivation: Energy-Optimal Velocity Profile (Eco-Driving)

For an EV traversing a planned path, the energy consumed is:

$$E = \int_{0}^{T} \left[ m a(t) v(t) + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v(t)^3 + m g C_r v(t) + m g \sin\theta(t) v(t) \right] dt$$

Where:
- $m$ = vehicle mass
- $a(t)$ = acceleration
- $\rho$ = air density, $C_d$ = drag coefficient, $A$ = frontal area
- $C_r$ = rolling resistance coefficient
- $\theta(t)$ = road slope

**Step 1 — Apply boundary conditions.**
$v(0) = v_0$ (initial speed), $v(T) = v_f$ (final speed), $0 \leq v(t) \leq v_{max}$.

**Step 2 — Apply constraints on $a(t)$.**
$a_{min} \leq a(t) \leq a_{max}$ (no instant teleport!).

**Step 3 — Solve via Pontryagin's Minimum Principle.**
The optimal control minimises the Hamiltonian at every instant:

$$u^*(t) = \arg \min_{u \in U} H(x(t), u(t), \lambda(t))$$

For the energy-minimisation problem with acceleration $u$ as the control input:

$$H = \left( m u v + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^3 + m g C_r v + m g \sin\theta \, v \right) + \lambda v$$

Differentiating and setting $\partial H / \partial u = 0$ leads to the **bang-bang** structure in the unconstrained case and a **coast-at-cruise** solution when the boundary conditions are loose.

**Step 4 — Engineering simplification.**
In practice, planners use a **discrete cost map** instead of continuous optimisation:

$$J_{path} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ w_1 \cdot \text{slope}_i + w_2 \cdot \text{curvature}_i^2 + w_3 \cdot \text{traffic}_i + w_4 \cdot \text{elevation}_i \right]$$

The path with minimum $J_{path}$ is selected. This is the basis of **eco-routing** in modern EVs (e.g., Tesla's navigation that prefers flatter routes to save battery).

### 3.5 Mathematical Model of Automation Impact (Energy Savings)

Let $E_{manual}$ be the energy consumed in a human-driven trip and $E_{auto}$ the autonomous version. The savings ratio is:

$$\eta_{savings} = \frac{E_{manual} - E_{auto}}{E_{manual}}$$

Empirically published values (literature-cited ranges):
- Highway eco-routing: $\eta \approx 0.08$ to $0.12$
- Urban with V2V signal timing: $\eta \approx 0.15$ to $0.20$
- Truck platooning: $\eta \approx 0.25$ to $0.40$

These savings are amplified when combined with **regenerative braking**, where deceleration events are planned to coincide with battery SoC headroom.

### 4.3 A* Search Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Start A Star at Initial Node] --> A1[Add Start to Open Set with f = h]
    A1 --> A2{Is Open Set Empty?}
    A2 -- Yes --> A3[Failure: No Path Exists]
    A2 -- No --> A4[Pop Node n with Lowest f from Open Set]
    A4 --> A5{Is n the Goal Node?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Reconstruct Path by Backtracking Parents]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Move n to Closed Set]
    A7 --> A8[For Each Neighbour m of n]
    A8 --> A9{Is m in Closed Set OR m is Obstacle?}
    A9 -- Yes --> A10[Skip m]
    A9 -- No --> A11[Compute tentative g = g of n + cost n to m]
    A11 --> A12{Is tentative g less than current g of m?}
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Update Parent of m to n, Update g, Compute f = g + h]
    A12 -- No --> A14[Keep Existing Values]
    A13 --> A15[Add m to Open Set if Not Present]
    A14 --> A2
    A15 --> A2
    A6 --> A7F[Return Optimal Path]
    A3 --> A3F[Return Failure Signal]
    A7F --> AEnd[End of A Star]
    A3F --> AEnd
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
Define **path planning** in autonomous vehicles. Differentiate between **global** and **local** path planning with a suitable example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Path Planning** is the computational process of generating a collision-free, kinematically feasible trajectory for a vehicle from its current state to a desired goal state, subject to environmental constraints, traffic rules, and vehicle dynamics.

**Differentiation:**

| Aspect | Global Path Planning | Local Path Planning |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Long-range, road-level | Short-range, lane-level |
| Input | HD Map, GPS, Route | Real-time sensor data |
| Example | "Take NH-66 from Kochi to Kozhikode" | "Steer 0.5 m left to avoid a pothole" |

Global planning uses algorithms like A* or Dijkstra on a road network graph, whereas local planning uses DWA or MPC on live sensor data to handle dynamic obstacles.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for differentiation with example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
List and briefly explain the **SAE Levels of Driving Automation (J3016)**. At which level does the human driver still monitor the driving environment?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The SAE J3016 standard defines **6 levels of automation** (0 to 5):

- **Level 0 — No Automation:** Human controls everything.
- **Level 1 — Driver Assistance:** Either steering OR acceleration is automated (e.g., Adaptive Cruise Control).
- **Level 2 — Partial Automation:** Both steering and acceleration are automated, but **human monitors the environment** (e.g., Tesla Autopilot basic).
- **Level 3 — Conditional Automation:** System monitors the environment; human is fallback-ready.
- **Level 4 — High Automation:** System handles all driving within a geofenced area; no human needed.
- **Level 5 — Full Automation:** No human required under any condition.

**Human monitors the environment up to Level 2.** From Level 3 onwards, the system takes over environment monitoring.

*(Allocation: 2 marks for listing levels + 1 mark for identifying the transition point.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing **path planning** with **motion control**. Planning is *what to do*; control is *how to do it*.
> 2. Forgetting to mention the **admissibility** of the heuristic in A* — this is a frequently asked 2-mark sub-part.
> 3. Writing only the names of algorithms without explaining the **cost function** — examiners specifically test $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$.
> 4. Mixing up **SAE Levels 2 and 3** — the difference is *who monitors the environment* (human vs system).
> 5. For path-smoothing derivations, students often skip the **curvature constraint** — losing 2–3 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **A* algorithm** for global path planning in autonomous electric vehicles. Clearly define the evaluation function and discuss the role of the **heuristic function**, with a comparison of **Manhattan** and **Euclidean** heuristics. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **six levels of vehicle automation** as per SAE J3016. Explain how automation impacts **electric vehicle energy efficiency** through *eco-routing*, *platooning*, and *V2G integration*. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — A* Algorithm (7 marks):**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
A* (A-Star) is a best-first graph search algorithm widely used for global path planning in autonomous vehicles. It uses a **best-first search** strategy and finds the optimal path from a start node to a goal node on a discretised grid or road network, provided an **admissible heuristic** is used.

**2. Evaluation Function (2 marks):**
The A* algorithm evaluates each node *n* using:

$$f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$$

Where:
- $g(n)$ = actual cost from the start node to node *n* (known from prior traversal).
- $h(n)$ = heuristic estimate of the cost from *n* to the goal (estimated, must be admissible).
- $f(n)$ = total estimated cost of the cheapest solution passing through *n*.

The algorithm expands the node in the **Open Set** with the lowest $f(n)$ at each iteration. Once the goal is reached, the path is reconstructed by backtracking parent pointers.

**3. Heuristic Function Comparison (2 marks):**

| Heuristic | Formula | Best Used For | Admissible? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Manhattan | $h = \vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ | 4-connected grid (only 4-direction motion) | Yes |
| Euclidean | $h = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | 8-connected grid (diagonal motion allowed) | Yes |
| Diagonal | $h = \max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert) + (\sqrt{2} - 1) \min(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ | 8-connected grid with mixed cost | Yes |

**Admissibility Rule (1 mark):** A heuristic is *admissible* if it never overestimates the true cost to the goal ($h(n) \leq h^*(n)$). This guarantees A* returns an **optimal** path. If the heuristic is also *consistent* ($h(n) \leq c(n, n') + h(n')$), A* is also optimally efficient.

**4. Pseudocode and Example (1 mark):**

```
OPEN ← {start}; CLOSED ← {}
while OPEN not empty:
    n ← node in OPEN with lowest f(n)
    if n is GOAL: return reconstructed path
    remove n from OPEN; add n to CLOSED
    for each neighbour m of n:
        if m in CLOSED or m is obstacle: continue
        tentative_g = g(n) + dist(n, m)
        if tentative_g < g(m):
            parent(m) ← n
            g(m) ← tentative_g
            f(m) ← g(m) + h(m)
            if m not in OPEN: add m to OPEN
return failure
```

A worked example on a 5×5 grid was illustrated in SECTION 3.2, yielding an 8-step optimal path from (0,0) to (4,4).

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — SAE Levels and EV Energy Impact (7 marks):**

**1. SAE J3016 Levels (4 marks):**
As detailed in SECTION 2.3, the six levels range from Level 0 (No Automation) to Level 5 (Full Automation). The key distinction is **who performs the Dynamic Driving Task (DDT)** and **who is the fallback**:
- Levels 0–2: Human driver is the fallback and (for L0–L2) monitors the environment.
- Level 3: System monitors; human is a backup.
- Levels 4–5: System is the fallback; no human intervention required within the operational design domain (ODD).

**2. Energy Impact on EVs (3 marks):**

- **Eco-Routing:** Path planners in EVs (e.g., Tesla, Lucid) compute routes that minimise energy, not just time. By preferring flatter roads, smoother trajectories (low curvature), and traffic-light-aware velocities, eco-routing can extend range by **8–15%**.
- **Platooning:** Using V2V communication, autonomous trucks drive 5–10 m apart in convoys. Aerodynamic drag is reduced by up to **40%**, yielding significant energy savings for heavy-duty EVs. The savings ratio:

$$\eta_{savings} = \frac{E_{manual} - E_{platoon}}{E_{manual}} \approx 0.25 \text{ to } 0.40$$

- **V2G Integration:** When an autonomous EV is not in use, it can self-drive to a charging station during off-peak hours (low tariff) and later feed stored energy back to the grid during peak hours. This **Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G)** bidirectional flow turns the EV into a distributed energy storage asset, improving grid stability and reducing the user's total cost of ownership.

These automation-driven efficiencies are unique to electric powertrains because of their bidirectional power flow capability (regenerative braking + V2G) and precise torque control, which ICE vehicles cannot match.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style, Module 4]**

**(a)** Describe the **Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT)** algorithm and its optimal variant **RRT***. Explain their application in **autonomous parking scenarios** for electric vehicles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically analyse the **effects of automation on road safety, employment, and the environment**. Mention at least two **ethical** concerns related to autonomous decision-making. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — RRT and RRT* (7 marks):**

**1. RRT Algorithm (3 marks):**
RRT is a **sampling-based** motion planning algorithm particularly suited for high-dimensional configuration spaces, such as autonomous parking where 6–8 state variables (x, y, $\theta$, $v$, gear, etc.) are involved.

**Steps:**
1. Start with a tree $T$ rooted at the start configuration $q_{start}$.
2. Sample a random configuration $q_{rand}$ in the free space.
3. Find the nearest node $q_{near} \in T$ to $q_{rand}$.
4. Steer from $q_{near}$ toward $q_{rand}$ by a step $\delta$ to get $q_{new}$.
5. If the path $q_{near} \to q_{new}$ is collision-free, add $q_{new}$ to $T$.
6. Repeat until $q_{new}$ is within tolerance of $q_{goal}$.

The steering function for a car-like vehicle is:

$$q_{new} = q_{near} + \delta \cdot \left[ \cos\theta, \sin\theta, \frac{\tan\phi}{L} \right]^T$$

**Properties:** RRT is **probabilistically complete** (finds a path if one exists) but does **not** guarantee optimality.

**2. RRT* — Asymptotically Optimal (2 marks):**
RRT* extends RRT by adding a **rewiring** step:
- After adding $q_{new}$, find all nodes in $T$ within a radius $r$ of $q_{new}$.
- Connect $q_{new}$ to the one with the minimum cost (cheapest path).
- For each neighbour $q_{neigh}$ within $r$, check if routing through $q_{new}$ reduces its cost; if yes, rewire.

As the number of samples $n \to \infty$, the cost of the RRT* path converges to the optimal cost. This makes RRT* ideal for **autonomous parking**, where the parking slot is tight and the car must minimise manoeuvres and energy.

**3. Application in EV Parking (2 marks):**
In an autonomous EV, RRT* plans a path that:
- Avoids stationary obstacles (other parked cars, pillars).
- Respects the **minimum turning radius** $r_{min} = L / \tan(\phi_{max})$.
- Minimises energy by avoiding sharp reversals and high-curvature segments.

The computed trajectory is then tracked by a **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** system, which sends steering, throttle, and brake commands at **20–100 Hz**.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Effects of Automation (7 marks):**

**1. Road Safety (2 marks):**
- **Positive:** Automation eliminates human error — the cause of **~94%** of road accidents (WHO statistic). Features like AEB, lane keeping, and blind-spot monitoring reduce crashes.
- **Negative:** New failure modes — *edge cases*, sensor failures, adverse weather, and adversarial attacks can cause novel accidents. The system may be over-confident in scenarios it does not understand.

**2. Employment (2 marks):**
- **Disruption:** Approximately **3–4 million** driving jobs (taxi, truck, delivery) in India could be affected over the next 2 decades.
- **Creation:** New jobs in AI/ML, robotics, sensor calibration, fleet management, and remote vehicle operation emerge. The net effect is debated, but **reskilling** is essential.

**3. Environment (1 mark):**
- **Positive:** Eco-routing, platooning, and smoother driving patterns reduce energy use and emissions. Autonomous EVs can integrate with renewable grids via V2G.
- **Negative:** Higher computational load (data centres) and sensor manufacturing (LiDAR, semiconductors) have a carbon cost.

**4. Ethical Concerns (2 marks):**
- **The Trolley Problem:** When a crash is unavoidable, how should the car decide? Should it prioritise the passenger or the pedestrian? This is a regulatory and philosophical minefield.
- **Liability:** Who is legally responsible for an accident — the human driver (L2/3), the OEM, the software provider, or the fleet operator? Current laws (including Indian Motor Vehicle Amendment Acts) are still catching up.
- **Data Privacy:** Autonomous vehicles collect enormous amounts of location, biometric, and behavioural data. Misuse or breaches raise serious privacy concerns.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Path Planning and Effects of Automation:**

- [x] **Path Planning** = collision-free, feasible trajectory generation. Two types: **Global** (HD map-based, long-range) and **Local** (sensor-based, short-range).
- [x] **A* Algorithm:** evaluation $f(n) = g(n) + h(n)$. Admissible heuristic guarantees optimality.
- [x] **Common Heuristics:** Manhattan ($\vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$) and Euclidean ($\sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$). Use Manhattan for 4-connected grids.
- [x] **RRT / RRT*:** Sampling-based, suited for parking and high-DOF problems. RRT* is asymptotically optimal via rewiring.
- [x] **DWA:** Velocity-space sampling for local planning in dynamic environments. Score = $\alpha h + \beta d + \gamma v$.
- [x] **Curvature:** $\kappa = \frac{\vert x' y'' - y' x'' \vert}{(x'^2 + y'^2)^{3/2}}$. Constraint $\kappa \leq \kappa_{max}$ for feasible car-like paths.
- [x] **Steering radius:** $r = L / \tan\phi_{max}$. Determines minimum turning circle.
- [x] **SAE J3016 Levels 0–5:** Key transition is at **Level 3** (system takes over environment monitoring). Up to Level 2, human monitors.
- [x] **Automation benefits for EVs:** eco-routing (+8–15% range), platooning (−25 to −40% energy), V2G bidirectional charging.
- [x] **Automation risks:** Edge case failures, cybersecurity, legal liability, ethical dilemmas (trolley problem), job displacement.
- [x] **DDT** = Dynamic Driving Task (the operational and tactical functions of driving). OEDR = Object and Event Detection and Response.
- [x] **Eco-Routing cost function:** $J = \sum (w_1 \cdot \text{slope} + w_2 \cdot \text{curvature}^2 + w_3 \cdot \text{traffic} + w_4 \cdot \text{elevation})$.
- [x] **Platooning drag reduction** is ~40% at 5 m inter-vehicle gap; rises sharply with shorter gaps.
- [x] **Real-world systems:** Waymo (rule + learning hybrid), Tesla FSD (end-to-end NN), Mobileye (rule-based), Bosch/Continental (Tier-1 suppliers).
- [x] **Indian Context:** Tata Elxsi (Bengaluru ADAS R&D), IIT Hyderabad autonomous car, ARAI certification of ADAS features.
- [x] **Standards to remember:** SAE J3016 (automation levels), ISO 21448 SOTIF (safety of intended functionality), ISO 26262 (functional safety).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Communication Protocols: Need and Requirements - Functions of Control Pilot (CP) and Proximity Pilot (PP) Pins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Communication Protocols: Need and Requirements - Functions of Control Pilot (CP) and Proximity Pilot (PP) Pins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Vehicle Communication Protocols

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What Are Vehicle Communication Protocols?

In the context of **Electric Vehicles (EVs)**, *vehicle communication protocols* are the standardised, bidirectional exchange of electrical signals between the **Electric Vehicle (EV)** and the **Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment (EVSE)** — commonly known as the charging station. These protocols govern *how* the car and the charger "talk" to each other before, during, and after a charging session, ensuring **safety, interoperability, and optimum energy transfer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim orientation):**
> *Vehicle communication protocols define the rules, signal levels, and pin-level interactions that allow an EV and the off-board EVSE to exchange critical information such as connection status, cable current rating, permissible charging current, ventilation requirements, and fault conditions in compliance with standards like IEC 61851-1 and SAE J1772.*

### 1.2 Why Are They Needed? (The "Need")

Consider what could go *wrong* if a dumb plug-and-charge system existed:

- A 16 A cable could be wrongly connected to a 32 A EVSE — fire hazard.
- The EV could drive away while still tethered — electrocution / mechanical damage.
- The battery management system (BMS) would have no idea when to start/stop charging.
- Indoor charging of a NiMH pack could release explosive hydrogen without ventilation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Pillars of Need (must-learn for KTU 2-marker):**
> 1. **Safety** — prevent shock, fire, and over-current.
> 2. **Interoperability** — any EV with any compliant charger.
> 3. **Charging Level Selection** — match EVSE current to cable/BMS limits.
> 4. **Status & Fault Reporting** — handshake, ventilation, emergency stop.
> 5. **Drive-Away Prevention (Interlock)** — vehicle cannot move while plugged.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> Think of the CP and PP pins as a **telephone conversation between two strangers at a fuel pump**:
> - The **PP pin** is the *handshake* — "Hello, the nozzle is inserted and the hose can carry 32 A."
> - The **CP pin** is the *negotiation* — "I'm ready for 32 A, please open the valve and pump at 1 kHz PWM duty-cycle of 50 %."
> - Without these, neither party knows if it's safe to start the flow.

### 1.4 Pin Overview — SAE J1772 / IEC 61851 Mode 3

The AC slow/fast charging inlet of a typical EV (Type 1 / Type 2) contains **7 terminals**:

| Pin | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| L1 | Line 1 | AC phase 1 |
| L2 / N | Neutral | AC return |
| PE | Protective Earth | Safety ground |
| **CP** | **Control Pilot** | **PWM communication** |
| **PP / CS** | **Proximity Pilot** | **Connection & cable detection** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Control Pilot (CP)** and **Proximity Pilot (PP)** are the *only two* low-power signalling pins — they do not carry the charging current. They are the "data lines" of the EV-EVSE interface.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of the Pilot Circuit

The pilot circuit is a **small-signal, isolated signalling loop** powered by the EVSE. It consists of:

- A **+12 V DC source** and a **−12 V DC source** inside the EVSE.
- A **1 kHz ± 1 % square-wave oscillator (PWM generator)**.
- A **resistor divider chain** ($R_1$ in EVSE, $R_2$ inside the EV) that sets the steady-state voltage seen by the EV controller.
- A **diode bridge** inside the EV to make the EV's pull-up polarity-agnostic.
- A **switch** inside the EV (S2) that connects a resistor $R_3$ to indicate "ventilation required" or "ready."

### 2.2 Operating States of the Control Pilot (CP) Line

The CP line is monitored by the **EVSE controller** through an **analog/digital sense pin**. The voltage on CP identifies the *state* the system is in. According to **SAE J1772 / IEC 61851-1**, the states are:

| State | Nominal CP Voltage | Meaning | PWM? | Contactor? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **State A — Standby** | **+12 V** | EV connected, not ready | No | **Open** |
| **State B — EV Detected** | **+9 V (PWM high) / +6 V (PWM low)** | EV ready, no ventilation | **Yes** | **Closed** |
| **State C — Charging (Ventilation NOT required)** | **+6 V / +3 V** | EV is charging | Yes | Closed |
| **State D — Charging (Ventilation REQUIRED)** | **+3 V / 0 V** | Indoor charging — e.g., NiMH | Yes | Closed |
| **State E / F** | −12 V | **Fault / Error — pilot shorted to ground** | — | **Open immediately** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)?**
> The **duty cycle** of the 1 kHz pilot signal encodes the **maximum allowable charging current** the EVSE can deliver. The EV reads this duty cycle and never draws more than the advertised current.

### 2.3 PWM Duty-Cycle to Current Mapping (SAE J1772)

The standard mapping (for pilots operating between 6 % and 80 % duty cycle) is given by the **piecewise linear relationship** in the formula sheet below. The same current is also encoded by a **5 % pilot current** for **digital communication** in IEC 61851-1 Annex C.

### 2.4 Proximity Pilot (PP) — Resistor Coding

The PP pin is purely a **passive resistor-divider circuit**. When the connector is mated, a specific resistor $R_{cable}$ inside the plug is connected between the PP pin and the Protective Earth (PE) pin. The EV measures the resistance to identify the **current-carrying capacity of the cable** (this is critical when using *portable* chargers like the Mode 2 "IC-CPD" — In-Cable Control and Protection Device).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning / Use | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\boxed{I_{max} = \dfrac{\text{Duty Cycle (\%)}}{0.6}}$ | SAE J1772 mapping for 6 % ≤ D ≤ 80 % | A |
| 2 | $\boxed{I_{max} = 6 \cdot \text{Duty Cycle}}$ (alt.) | Same as above: $D$ in decimal | A |
| 3 | $\boxed{V_{CP,\text{high}} = 12 \text{ V}}$ | State A (Standby) | V |
| 4 | $\boxed{V_{CP,\text{high}} = 9 \text{ V}}$ | State B / C / D (with $R_2$ pull-down) | V |
| 5 | $\boxed{V_{CP,\text{low}} = 6 \text{ V},\, 3 \text{ V},\, 0 \text{ V}}$ | State B, C, D respectively | V |
| 6 | $\boxed{V_{CP} = -12 \text{ V}}$ | State E/F — **PILOT FAULT** | V |
| 7 | $\boxed{f_{PWM} = 1 \text{ kHz} \pm 1\%}$ | SAE J1772 pilot frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $\boxed{R_{cable} \in \{1.5\text{ k}\Omega,\, 680\,\Omega,\, 220\,\Omega,\, 100\,\Omega\}}$ | PP coding → 13 A / 20 A / 32 A / 63 A | Ω |
| 9 | $\boxed{V_{PP} = V_{ref}\dfrac{R_{sense}}{R_{sense} + R_{cable}}}$ | Voltage divider law at PP pin | V |
| 10 | $\boxed{t_{disconnect} \leq 100 \text{ ms}}$ | Required PP-disconnect reaction time | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** These two pins are present in *every* AC charger you see in India (Bharat AC-001 / AC-002 standards adopted from IEC 61851), in Tesla UMC, in commercial wall-boxes, and in public Type-2 AC stations. Understanding CP/PP is the *foundational skill* for any EV systems engineer or charge-station designer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pilot Voltage Levels (States A → D)

The CP circuit is modelled as a **resistor divider + diode + switch**, where the EVSE has a fixed resistor $R_1$ and a fixed +12 V / −12 V supply, and the EV places $R_2$ in parallel with an optional $R_3$ once it is ready.

### Step 1 — Initial Condition (No EV Present)

When no EV is plugged in, the EVSE sees a **clean +12 V** on CP because the only load is a high-impedance sense resistor. This corresponds to **State A**.

$$
V_{CP} = +12 \text{ V} \quad \text{(open circuit)}
$$

### Step 2 — EV Plugged In, S2 Open (Vehicle "Detected" but not yet "Ready")

When the EV's diode + $R_2$ network is connected and switch S2 is open, the divider becomes $R_1$ in series with $R_2$. The CP voltage falls to a *high-side* level:

$$
V_{CP,\text{high}} = 12 \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

Substituting the standard SAE values $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 2.74\,\text{k}\Omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CP,\text{high}} &= 12 \cdot \dfrac{2.74}{1 + 2.74} \\
&= 12 \cdot \dfrac{2.74}{3.74} \\
&= 8.79 \text{ V} \approx 9 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

This ~9 V is **State B — EV Detected**, no charging current yet flowing.

### Step 3 — S2 Closed (Ventilation Not Required)

The EV closes S2, adding $R_3$ in parallel. The effective EV-side resistance drops to $R_2 \parallel R_3$. With $R_3 = 1.3\,\text{k}\Omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{2 \parallel 3} &= \dfrac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \\
&= \dfrac{2.74 \times 1.3}{2.74 + 1.3} \\
&= \dfrac{3.562}{4.04} \\
&= 0.882 \text{ k}\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the new high-side voltage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CP,\text{high}} &= 12 \cdot \dfrac{0.882}{1 + 0.882} \\
&= 12 \cdot 0.469 \\
&= 5.62 \text{ V} \approx 6 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **State C — Charging (no ventilation needed).**

### Step 4 — S2 Closed Differently (Ventilation Required)

If the EV (e.g., a NiMH fleet) requires forced ventilation, it closes a *different* combination, effectively grounding the CP through $R_3$ alone. The voltage at the high level falls to ~3 V and the low level to ~0 V — this is **State D**.

### Step 5 — Fault Condition (Pilot Shorted)

A short to protective earth forces the CP to the EVSE's **−12 V rail**, which the EVSE controller interprets as a fatal error and *trips the contactor within 100 ms*.

$$
V_{CP} = -12 \text{ V} \implies \text{IMMEDIATE SHUTDOWN (State E/F)}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of PWM Duty-Cycle to Current Mapping

The SAE J1772 standard defines that the EV may draw no more than:

$$
I_{max} = \dfrac{\text{Duty Cycle in \%}}{0.6}
$$

**Derivation logic** (specification interpolation):

At duty = 10 % → $I = 16.67$ A
At duty = 50 % → $I = 83.33$ A
At duty = 80 % → $I = 133.33$ A

The line connecting (10 %, 16.67 A) and (80 %, 133.33 A):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{slope} &= \dfrac{133.33 - 16.67}{80 - 10} = \dfrac{116.66}{70} = 1.666 \text{ A/\%} \\
\text{intercept} &= 16.67 - (1.666 \times 10) = 0.01 \approx 0 \\
\therefore I_{max} &\approx 1.666 \cdot D_{\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rewriting in the form $I = D/0.6$:

$$
\boxed{I_{max}(\text{A}) = \dfrac{D(\%)}{0.6}}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

An EVSE broadcasts **D = 30 %** on the CP line. Calculate the maximum charging current the EV is allowed to draw.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{max} &= \dfrac{30}{0.6} \\
&= 50 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the EV's BMS requests 60 A but the EVSE only allows 50 A, the BMS must **clip** its request to 50 A — this prevents the cable from over-heating.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cable Rating vs. PP Resistance

| Cable Current Rating | $R_{cable}$ inside plug | EV measured voltage $V_{PP}$ (with $R_{sense}=1\text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{ref}=5$ V) | EV Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **13 A** | **1.5 kΩ** | $\dfrac{5 \cdot 1.0}{1.0+1.5} = 2.0$ V | Limit to 13 A |
| **20 A** | **680 Ω** | $\dfrac{5 \cdot 1.0}{1.0+0.68} = 2.98$ V | Limit to 20 A |
| **32 A** | **220 Ω** | $\dfrac{5 \cdot 1.0}{1.0+0.22} = 4.10$ V | Limit to 32 A |
| **63 A** | **100 Ω** | $\dfrac{5 \cdot 1.0}{1.0+0.10} = 4.55$ V | Limit to 63 A |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **monotonic increase** of $V_{PP}$ with cable rating — the EV can use a simple ADC to identify the cable. The PP pin does *not* carry PWM, only a steady DC level determined by the resistor.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — EV ↔ EVSE Communication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[EVSE Controller] -->|Reads CP voltage| A2[PWM Oscillator 1 kHz]
    A2 -->|Pilot Signal| A3[CP Pin on Inlet]
    A3 -->|Through diode R2| A4[EV Controller BMS]
    A4 -->|Closes S2 with R3| A5[CP Voltage drops to 6 V or 3 V]
    A5 -->|State C or D detected| A1
    A1 -->|Close contactor| A6[AC Mains Contactor]
    A6 -->|L1, L2, PE current| A7[EV On-board Charger]
    A1 -->|Reads VPP| B1[PP Pin on Inlet]
    B1 -->|Rcable divider| B2[EV PP Sense ADC]
    B2 -->|Identifies cable rating| A4
    A4 -->|Requests I_max within limits| A7
    A1 -.->|Disconnects if VCP goes negative| A8[Fault State E or F]
    A1 -.->|PP open-circuit| A9[Drive-away Interlock Active]
```

## 4.2 CP State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> A: Plug inserted, CP = 12 V
    A --> B: EV pulls CP down to 9 V (R2 detected)
    B --> C: S2 closed, no vent, CP = 6 V
    B --> D: S2 closed, vent required, CP = 3 V
    C --> Charging_AC: Contactor closed, PWM D-cycle monitored
    D --> Charging_AC_Vent: Contactor closed, fan on
    Charging_AC --> B: Disconnect or end of session
    Charging_AC_Vent --> B: Disconnect or end of session
    C --> E: CP shorted to PE, CP = -12 V
    D --> E: CP shorted to PE, CP = -12 V
    E --> [*]: Hard shutdown, lock-out
```

## 4.3 CP/PP Pin Layout on a Type-2 (IEC 62196) Inlet

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inlet[Type 2 Inlet Front View] --> p1[Pin 1 L1]
    inlet --> p2[Pin 2 L2]
    inlet --> p3[Pin 3 L3]
    inlet --> p4[Pin 4 N]
    inlet --> p5[Pin 5 PE]
    inlet --> p6[Pin 6 CP]
    inlet --> p7[Pin 7 PP]
    p6 -. PWM signal .-> cpblk[Control Pilot]
    p7 -. Rcable divider .-> ppblk[Proximity Pilot]
    pe[Pin 5 PE used as PP return] --- ppblk
```

> [!NOTE]
> On a **Type-2 (Mennekes)** connector, the PP pin uses the **PE line as a return path** — there is no separate PP return wire. This is why the PP measurement is *always* referenced to protective earth.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List any three functional requirements of a vehicle communication protocol between an EV and an EVSE.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Connection detection** — The EVSE must positively detect that a connector is physically mated before energising (via PP pin).
2. **Current negotiation** — The EV must communicate or accept the maximum safe charging current advertised by the EVSE (via CP duty cycle).
3. **Fault detection and safe shut-down** — Any pilot-line short, over-current, or loss of ground must cause the contactor to open within 100 ms (via CP negative voltage detection).

*(1 mark each, allocation per CO1: "Understand the need for vehicle communication protocols")*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between the Control Pilot (CP) and Proximity Pilot (PP) pins of an EV charging inlet.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CP Pin | PP Pin |
|---|---|---|
| Signal type | 1 kHz PWM, ±12 V | DC voltage from resistor divider |
| Purpose | Current advertisement, state handshake, fault detection | Connection detection, cable rating identification, drive-away interlock |
| Active element | EVSE oscillator + EV pull-down | Only passive resistor inside cable |
| After full handshake | Carries PWM with duty = current limit | Steady DC level (no PWM) |

*(Table = 2 marks, brief explanation = 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample QP, Module 4]**
> **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **Control Pilot (CP) signalling scheme** used in SAE J1772 / IEC 61851-1 for AC conductive charging. List the four charging states (A, B, C, D) with their corresponding CP voltages.
> **(b)** Derive the **duty-cycle to current mapping** for the CP pilot. An EVSE produces a **30 % duty cycle** on a 1 kHz pilot. Calculate (i) the maximum current the EV may draw, and (ii) the time spent in the "high" state of the PWM in one period.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CP Signalling Scheme — 7 marks**

The CP circuit consists of an EVSE-side oscillator (1 kHz ± 1 %) producing a 1 kHz square wave of amplitude 12 V, fed through resistor $R_1 = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ to the CP pin. Inside the EV, a diode bridge followed by $R_2 = 2.74\,\text{k}\Omega$ pulls the line down, and a switch S2 connects $R_3 = 1.3\,\text{k}\Omega$ in parallel to indicate "ready."

The CP line has three detectable voltage levels corresponding to four states:

- **State A (12 V):** No EV connected (standby).
- **State B (9 V high / 6 V low):** EV detected, ready but not charging.
- **State C (6 V high / 3 V low):** EV charging, no ventilation required.
- **State D (3 V high / 0 V low):** EV charging, ventilation required (e.g., NiMH).

*(Circuit description 4 marks + state table 3 marks = 7 marks.)*

**(b) Duty-Cycle Derivation & Numerical — 7 marks**

The SAE J1772 standard specifies:

$$
\boxed{I_{max}(\text{A}) = \dfrac{D(\%)}{0.6}}
$$

Derivation: The line through (10 %, 16.67 A) and (80 %, 133.33 A) has slope 1.666 A/% and intercept ≈ 0, hence the above linear equation.

**(i) For D = 30 %:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{max} &= \dfrac{30}{0.6} \\
&= 50 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Time in "high" state:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{period} &= \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms} \\
T_{high} &= D \times T_{period} = 0.30 \times 1 \text{ ms} \\
&= 0.30 \text{ ms} = 300 \text{ \mu s}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Formula 2 marks, derivation 2 marks, (i) 1.5 marks, (ii) 1.5 marks.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Sample QP, Module 4]**
> **(a)** Explain the function of the **Proximity Pilot (PP) pin** with reference to cable current rating identification and drive-away interlock. Tabulate the standard PP resistor values for 13 A, 20 A, 32 A and 63 A cables.
> **(b)** For a portable Mode-2 charger with a 32 A cable, the EV measures $V_{PP} = 4.10$ V. If the reference voltage is 5 V and the sense resistor is 1 kΩ, verify the cable rating by calculating $R_{cable}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PP Pin Function — 7 marks**

The Proximity Pilot (PP) is a **passive resistor-coded pin** whose only function is to identify the *current-carrying capacity* of the charging cable and to act as a **drive-away interlock**. When the connector is mated, a precision resistor $R_{cable}$ inside the plug is connected between the PP pin and PE. The EV measures the resulting voltage and clamps its charge current accordingly.

| Cable | $R_{cable}$ |
|---|---|
| 13 A | 1.5 kΩ |
| 20 A | 680 Ω |
| 32 A | 220 Ω |
| 63 A | 100 Ω |

The **drive-away interlock** is realised by the EV monitoring PP continuously — if PP goes open-circuit (connector pulled mid-charge), the EV immediately commands the contactor to open and refuses to allow the vehicle to be shifted out of "Park."

*(Function 3 marks + resistor table 2 marks + interlock explanation 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

**(b) Cable Rating Verification — 7 marks**

Using the voltage divider formula:

$$
V_{PP} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_{sense}}{R_{sense} + R_{cable}}
$$

Substituting $V_{PP} = 4.10$ V, $V_{ref} = 5$ V, $R_{sense} = 1$ kΩ:

$$
\begin{aligned}
4.10 &= 5 \cdot \dfrac{1000}{1000 + R_{cable}} \\
\dfrac{1000}{1000 + R_{cable}} &= 0.82 \\
1000 + R_{cable} &= \dfrac{1000}{0.82} = 1219.51 \\
R_{cable} &= 1219.51 - 1000 \\
R_{cable} &= 219.51 \,\Omega \approx 220 \,\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the standard **220 Ω for a 32 A cable**. Therefore the cable is correctly rated for 32 A charging.

*(Equation 2 marks + substitution 2 marks + solution 2 marks + conclusion 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing CP and PP functions** — PP is *passive* (resistor only); CP is *active* (PWM). Marks lost if you state "PP carries PWM."
> 2. **Wrong voltage for State A** — Many students write +9 V for State A. State A is **+12 V (standby)**, NOT 9 V.
> 3. **Forgetting the −12 V fault level** — When CP is shorted to PE, voltage goes to **−12 V**, not 0 V. This trips the contactor.
> 4. **Mixing up duty-cycle formula** — The current is $D/0.6$ in % form; using $D \times 0.6$ yields 1/10 the correct answer.
> 5. **Neglecting PE as PP return on Type-2** — On Type-2 connectors, PP *uses PE as the return path*. Forgetting this loses 1 mark in circuit diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **CP pin:** 1 kHz PWM, ±12 V, carries *current advertisement* and *state* (A, B, C, D, E/F).
- **PP pin:** Passive resistor divider; identifies *cable rating* and enforces *drive-away interlock*.
- **PWM duty ↔ current:** $I_{max} = D/0.6$ (D in %).
- **Standard PP resistors:** 1.5 kΩ (13 A), 680 Ω (20 A), 220 Ω (32 A), 100 Ω (63 A).
- **State voltages:** 12 V (A), 9/6 V (B), 6/3 V (C), 3/0 V (D), −12 V (E/F fault).
- **Frequency:** 1 kHz ± 1 % (SAE J1772 / IEC 61851-1).
- **Fault reaction time:** Contactor must open within **100 ms** of pilot loss.
- **Standards:** SAE J1772 (USA), IEC 61851-1 (global), IS 17017 (India).
- **Five-pillar need:** Safety, interoperability, level selection, status/fault, interlock.
- **Type-2 caveat:** PP return is through PE — no dedicated PP return wire.
- **Communication is unidirectional current info, but bidirectional for fault** — EV can also pull CP to −12 V via diode in some implementations.
- **Digital extension:** IEC 61851-1 Annex C allows **5 % pilot-current modulation** for high-level digital handshake, in addition to PWM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Communication Protocols - CAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET522 - ELECTRIC VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Communication Protocols - CAN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Controller Area Network (CAN)** is a robust, multi-master, message-oriented **serial communication bus** standardised by **ISO 11898** that allows microcontrollers and Electronic Control Units (ECUs) to communicate with each other *without* a host computer. In Electric Vehicles, CAN forms the **digital nervous system** that interconnects the Battery Management System (BMS), Motor Controller (MCU), Vehicle Control Unit (VCU), On-Board Charger (OBC), DC-DC converter, instrument cluster, and the Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) sensors over a **two-wire differential bus** (CAN_H and CAN_L).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Definition Snapshot**
> *CAN is a vehicle-bus standard, designed to allow microcontrollers and devices to communicate with each other in applications without a host computer. It is a message-based protocol, designed originally for multiplex electrical wiring within automobiles (Bosch, 1986; ISO 11898).*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Conference Call" Model

Imagine an EV as a corporate office with **40+ departments** (ECUs) — Battery, Motor, Brakes, Dashboard, Charger, Air-Cones, Infotainment. They cannot all sit in one room (point-to-point wiring would require thousands of cables). Instead, they share a **single telephone conference line** with these strict rules:

- Every department can **broadcast** a message whenever it has news ("I am the BMS — current state-of-charge is 62 %").
- Every message is **tagged with an ID** (priority/label) so listeners know whether the message is relevant to them.
- If two departments start talking at the exact same instant, the **one with the higher-priority message (lower ID number)** continues, and the other **backs off and retries** — no collisions, no data loss.
- Every receiver checks the message for **errors** using a CRC; if the message is corrupted, it is flagged and discarded.

This single shared "conference line" is the **CAN bus** — it dramatically reduces wiring (weight ↓, cost ↓, reliability ↑), and is the **de-facto standard for in-vehicle networking (IVN)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Layer Recap**
> - **Two wires**: `CAN_H` (high) and `CAN_L` (low), terminated at each end by a **120 Ω resistor** to suppress reflections.
> - **Differential signalling**: `V_diff = V_{CAN_H} - V_{CAN_L}`. Logic '0' (dominant) ≈ **2 V**, Logic '1' (recessive) ≈ **0 V**.
> - **Bit rates**: Classic CAN → up to **1 Mbit/s** (40 m max); CAN FD → up to **5 Mbit/s** (data phase). Low-speed CAN (ISO 11519) → 125 kbit/s.

### 1.3 Why CAN is Critical for an Electric Vehicle

| EV Subsystem | ECU | Typical CAN Messages |
|---|---|---|
| Traction | VCU ↔ Inverter/MCU | Torque request, motor speed, regen level |
| Energy | BMS ↔ VCU | SOC, SOH, pack voltage, cell temp, fault flags |
| Charging | OBC ↔ BMS ↔ VCU | Charging current, state, plug lock, isolation status |
| Thermal | HVAC ↔ VCU ↔ Battery cooler | Coolant pump speed, valve position, fan PWM |
| Body / Safety | BCM, ABS/ESC, EPS | Brake pressure, steering angle, light status |
| Telematics / HMI | Cluster, IVI, T-Box | Speed, SOC%, range, OTA request |

> [!TIP]
> In a KTU 14-mark answer, *always* open with this "CAN is the digital backbone of the EV" framing. It instantly earns the first 2 marks of application-level context (Bloom's Level: *Apply / Understand*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 CAN Protocol Stack (Layered View)

| OSI Layer | CAN Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 7 (Application) | User-defined (e.g., **CANopen, SAE J1939, ISO 15765/UDS**) | Vehicle-specific messages, diagnostics |
| Layer 4 (Transport) | ISO 15765-2 (TP), J1939 TP | Multi-frame message segmentation |
| Layer 3 (Network) | Filtering by **Message ID** | Addressing & bus arbitration |
| Layer 2 (Data Link) | **CAN 2.0A / 2.0B** — Frame format | Framing, bit stuffing, ACK, CRC |
| Layer 1 (Physical) | **ISO 11898-2** (high-speed) | Differential voltage on twisted pair |

### 2.2 CAN Frame Formats (KTU High-Yield)

CAN 2.0 defines **two frame formats** that share the same physical layer:

| Feature | **CAN 2.0A (Standard)** | **CAN 2.0B (Extended)** |
|---|---|---|
| Identifier length | **11 bits** (2 048 IDs) | **29 bits** (537 million IDs) |
| ID field | `ID[10:0]` | `Base ID[10:0] + SRR + IDE + Ext ID[17:0]` |
| Typical use | Body, comfort | Powertrain, ADAS, J1939 commercial vehicles |
| RTR bit | Yes | Yes |
| DLC range | 0–8 bytes | 0–8 bytes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap** — In 2.0B, the IDE bit is **1** (recessive) for extended frames; in 2.0A, IDE = **0** (dominant). The **SRR** bit is always recessive (1) so standard frames always win arbitration against extended frames carrying the same base ID.

### 2.3 Anatomy of a Standard CAN Data Frame (11-bit ID)

The frame is the *atomic unit* KTU examiners love to draw. Field lengths are bit-precise:

| Field | Bits | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **SOF** | 1 | Start Of Frame, always **dominant (0)** synchronises all nodes |
| **Arbitration Field** | 11 + 1 + 1 | ID[10:0] + RTR (1 = remote request) |
| **Control Field** | 6 | IDE (1) + r0 (1) + DLC[3:0] (4) — payload length 0–8 bytes |
| **Data Field** | 0–64 | Actual payload (e.g., SOC %, motor speed) |
| **CRC Field** | 15 + 1 | Cyclic Redundancy Check + delimiter |
| **ACK Field** | 2 | Any receiver overwrites recessive→dominant to confirm |
| **EOF** | 7 | End Of Frame, all recessive bits |
| **IFS** | 3 | Inter-Frame Space before next node may transmit |

> [!NOTE]
> Total bits of a **maximum-length standard data frame** with 8-byte payload = **1 (SOF) + 12 (Arbitration) + 6 (Control) + 64 (Data) + 16 (CRC) + 2 (ACK) + 7 (EOF) + 3 (IFS) = 111 bits**.

### 2.4 The Core "Why" Behind Each Mechanism

**① Non-Destructive Bitwise Arbitration** — How does the bus "decide" who speaks?

CAN is a wired-AND bus. A node transmitting a **dominant (0)** overwrites a node transmitting a **recessive (1)**. All nodes transmit the ID **bit-by-bit from MSB to LSB**, simultaneously monitoring the bus. The first node that tries to send a recessive bit *but reads a dominant bit* on the bus **immediately stops transmitting and becomes a receiver** for that frame — *without destroying any data*. The winning node finishes its transmission untouched.

→ The **lower the numeric ID, the higher the priority** (because it has more leading zeros / dominant bits).

**② Bit Stuffing (NRZ encoding safeguard)** — Why bits must be balanced?

CAN uses **Non-Return-to-Zero (NRZ)** coding which has poor DC-balance and no built-in clock edges. If a long stream of identical bits (e.g., `0000…0`) is transmitted, receivers lose bit-clock synchronisation. To fix this, CAN inserts a **stuff bit of opposite polarity** whenever **five consecutive equal bits** appear in the SOF–CRC sequence. The receiver de-stuffs by removing these bits. This guarantees a clock transition at least every 5 bit times (10 for CAN FD).

**③ Error Detection (5 Mechanisms)** — How is data integrity guaranteed?

| Error Type | Detected By | Flag Bit |
|---|---|---|
| Bit Error | Transmitter monitors bus | Any node |
| Stuff Error | Receiver detects 6 identical bits | Any node |
| CRC Error | Receiver recomputes CRC-15 | Any node |
| Form Error | Fixed-format field violates rules | Any node |
| ACK Error | Transmitter reads recessive in ACK slot | Transmitter only |

When an error is found, the node transmits an **Error Flag** (6 dominant bits — overwrites everything), destroying the frame. The **Transmitter Error Counter (TEC)** and **Receiver Error Counter (REC)** are incremented; nodes can transition through three states: **Error-Active → Error-Passive → Bus-Off**.

**④ Message Filtering (Acceptance Filtering)** — How does the dashboard "ignore" irrelevant chatter?

Every CAN controller has a hardware acceptance filter (mask + code registers). A node sets `ACR = ID pattern it cares about` and `AMR = mask` (1 = "don't care"). A message passes the filter only when `(Received_ID XOR ACR) AND AMR == 0`. Thus the BMS node can ignore the air-conditioning message entirely — saving CPU load.

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Bit-Timing Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The bit timing of CAN is *not* something to memorise numerically for KTU, but the **nominal bit time** and the concept of **segments** is a frequent **2-mark question**.

| Quantity | Symbol | Expression | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Bit Time | $t_{bit}$ | $\dfrac{1}{f_{bit}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ | s |
| Bit Time (segments) | $t_{bit}$ | $t_{SYNC\_SEG} + t_{PROP\_SEG} + t_{PHASE\_SEG1} + t_{PHASE\_SEG2}$ | s |
| Time Quantum | $t_q$ | $\dfrac{1}{f_{CAN\_CLK}}$ (from a prescaler) | s |
| Propagation Delay (line) | $t_{prop}$ | $\dfrac{L \cdot v_p}{c} \cdot k_{safety}$ | s |
| Bus Length (high-speed) | $L_{max}$ | $\approx \dfrac{40 \text{ m} \cdot 1 \text{ Mbit/s}}{f_{bit}}$ | m |
| Bit Stuffing worst case | $N_{stuffed}$ | $N_{data} \cdot \dfrac{5}{4} + 1$ | bits |
| Number of nodes | $N$ | Up to **$2^{11}$ = 2 048** (Std), $2^{29}$ (Ext) | — |
| Standard IDs | — | $2^{11}$ | — |
| Bus termination | $R_T$ | **120 Ω** (split: $2 \times 60\ \Omega$ with capacitor midpoint) | Ω |
| Max stub length | $L_{stub}$ | $\le 0.3\,t_{bit} \cdot v_{prop}$ | m |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard KTU Numerical Pattern** (3 marks): *"If the CAN bus runs at 500 kbit/s and the propagation delay per metre is 5 ns/m, compute the maximum theoretical bus length and the number of time quanta if $t_q = 250$ ns."*
> Use $L_{max} \approx \dfrac{t_{bit}/2}{t_{prop/m}} = \dfrac{1\ \mu s / 2}{5\ \text{ns/m}} = 100$ m (theoretical); practical ≈ 40–60 m.

### 2.6 CAN vs CAN FD vs Automotive Ethernet (Real-World Utility Table)

| Property | Classic CAN 2.0 | CAN FD (ISO 11898-1:2015) | Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Bit Rate | 1 Mbit/s | **5 Mbit/s** (data phase) | 100 Mbit/s |
| Payload | 8 bytes | **64 bytes** | 1500 bytes |
| Arbitration Rate | Same as data | Slower (e.g., 1 Mbit/s arb → 5 Mbit/s data) | N/A (full duplex) |
